THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES
(OFFICIAL REPORT)
IN THE
THIRD SESSION OF THE THIRD PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
APPOINTED TO MEET THE THIRD DAY OF MARCH, IN THE
TWENTY-FIRST YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS
MAJESTY
KING
GEORGE V.
VOLUME XIII.
THE SENATE.

---

Tuesday, 3rd March, 1931.
The Third Session of the Third Parliament of Northern Ireland appointed to
meet on 3rd March, 1931, in the twentyfirst year of the Reign of King George V,
was opened by the Governor (His Grace
the Duke of Abercorn, K.G., K.P.) in
person.
The House met at Twelve of the Clock,
Mr. SPEAKER (the Rt. Hon. Viscount
Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.) in the Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor, being seated.
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is His Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately
in this House.'
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, His Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne:-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
In addressing you at the opening of
the third session of this present
Parliament I deeply regret that the
effect of the world-wide depression in
trade and commerce continues to be
reflected in our main industries.
It is a source of great pride and
satisfaction to me to find employers and
employed so manfully facing the hardships confronting them and continuing
their general practice of adjusting
matters of mutual concern in friendly
conference. Only in rare instances
have industrial disputes resulted in a
stoppage of work and then only for a
comparatively brief period of time.
I am also gratified that the Medical
Benefits Scheme has been established so
speedily and so smoothly. I feel confident that with the passage of time its
great value to the health and well-being
of the community will become increasingly apparent.
of
In pursuance of the policy of
industrial development a Measure will
be laid before you for the co-ordination
and improvement of the existing system
of generation and distribution
Electricity and for the promotion of
schemes designed to ensure supplies
of electrical energy adequate for the
requirements of Northern Ireland, both
for industrial and domestic purposes.
I am glad to learn that the housing
shortage which, for some years past, has
caused general concern has been
A
King's Speech
Papers Presented
THE SENATE
alleviated to a considerable extent.
Something, however, still remains to be
done and, consequently, a Bill will be
laid before you to continue the Government subsidy at a reduced rate for
another year and to enable Local
Authorities to co-operate in connection
with the provision of houses for the
working classes.
A Bill providing for the consolidation
of the Law relating to Joint Stock
Companies will also be submitted for
your consideration.
It was found impracticable last
Session to consider proposals in regard
to the amending and modernising of the
Law in respect of Lunacy, consequently
a Bill dealing with this matter will be
laid before you.
A Measure will be introduced
amending the provisions of the Children
Act, 1908, in regard to the placing out
at nurse of children for reward, with
the object of putting a stop to certain
evils which have come under notice from
time to time in recent years.
I am glad to note the beneficial
results which have already followed
recent legislation dealing with the inspection, grading and packing of
agricultural produce intended for
export. Measures designed further to
improve the marketing of eggs and of
fruit will be introduced during the
coming Session.
Bills dealing with the following
matters will also be submitted for your
consideration:-
Town and Country Planning.
The extension of the Loans
Guarantee Act.
Unemployment Insurance.
Health Insurance.
Purchase and disposal of Government Property.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services
will be laid before you in due course.
In view of the continued grave depression in the industrial situation and
the loss in revenue to be anticipated in
the forthcoming financial year my
Government is taking appropriate steps
to effect certain necessary economies.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
Conditions in Northern Ireland in
regard to Law and Order continue to
be satisfactory. The comparative freedom from serious crime and the gratifying percentage of offenders made
amenable to justice is an indication of
the good feeling generally prevailing
and of the efficient manner in which the
responsible authorities are discharging
their duties.
I pray that under the blessing of
Almighty God the outcome of your
deliberations may advance the happiness and well-being of the people of
Northern Ireland.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.

lie on the Table.
Address in Reply to His Majesty's
Most Gracious Speech.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
Mr. HILL DICKSON (in morning
dress): I beg to move:
--
"That an humble Address be presented
to His Grace in the following words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to your Grace
for the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament."
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dickson]
I feel highly honoured in being permitted to move the Address, and would
ask the Members of this House to extend
to me their accustomed indulgence.
It was during the last Session of Parliament that the tragic death of our late
Speaker took place. The feeling of every
Member of this House was that he had
lost a personal friend. I do not propose
to refer to the great qualities possessed by
the late Lord Dufferin. The sympathy of
the House was expressed when the vote of
condolence was passed, and we shall not
forget the sincere remarks of the Leader
of the Opposition on that occasion. Our
Deputy Leader has retired from the House
with the regret and best wishes of the
Members. The Speaker's chair and the
Deputy Leader's position have been filled
with the approval of all parties, and we
are convinced that the great traditions
of justice and fair play will continue.
The first paragraph of the Gracious
Speech is an expression of regret at the
world-wide trade depression. The fact
that other countries are as badly off, some
of them worse than ours, does not minimise the sympathy we have for the wageearner who cannot find work. In placing
the people of the Six Counties in the same
position as those in Great Britain, the
Government have gone to the utmost limit
of their financial resources in unemployment advances, old age pensions, widows'
pensions and medical benefits. It would
appear that there is not the same inducement to save money when times are good
as there was before these benefits came
into existence. The income tax authorities will look after a quarter of a man's
savings, and death duties will swallow
up a good part of the balance. Notwithstanding this, it is pleasing to note that
the deposits in the savings banks are increasing. When trade depression reaches
the bottom it can go no further. In the
meantime it is satisfactory to know that
employers and employed are adjusting
matters in friendly conference, and disputes have not been the cause of stoppage
of work for any lengthened period.
The health of the community is its
greatest asset, and it is hoped that the
great cost of medical benefits which have
been conferred on the people will be of
lasting value. At any rate, it places the
working man in the same position in this
respect as his fellows in Great Britain.
The Government is assisting and encouraging the distribution of electric
power. The industrial development of
our Province will be dependent on this
great power in the country districts as
well as in the cities, and it will tend to
make our workshops brighter and
healthier.
As pointed out in the Gracious Speech,
the Government intend to continue the
housing subsidy at a reduced rate for
another year. With the enormous number
of new houses erected, and in course of
erection, rents will be bound to come
down, and the working man will have
botter and more suitable surroundings,
and we may hope to see some of the older
slum areas disappear.
It is intended to amend the law relating
to the afflicted classes, and anything done
in that respect that would tend to make
the lot of these unfortunate people better
will, I have no doubt, have the sympathy
of this House.
A Measure will be presented to the
House to look after the welfare of children
of tender years who are placed out to
nurse. Foster mothers in this area,
especially in the country districts, look
after the infants placed in their charge in
a kindly way, but the Government should
have all the powers necessary to see the
child does not suffer for money gain.
The last paragraph in the Gracious
Speech refers to the industry with which I
am connected. Members of this House
will agree with me that if we place our
produce, no matter what it may be, on
the market in an attractive form it will
command the highest price. A few dealers
may suffer from the inspection of their
purchases, but the producer who grades
his produce will command a price according to the quality he exhibits. With the
scientific experiments of the Ministry of
Agriculture at the disposal of farmers the
improvement is within the reach of those
who like to take advantage of them. This
industry is suffering most acutely from the
world-wide depression, but we have to
thank the Government for their generous
treatment in derating agricultural land.
This may be the last occasion on which
the Gracious Speech will be proposed in
this House. In a year's time those of us
who are still members will probably meet
in the new Parliament buildings at Stormont. But those of us who have been
members of this House from the first have
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
3 MARCH 1931
always enjoyed its convenience and the
congenial atmosphere which has always
prevailed here.
The Bills that are foreshadowed will, I
hope, have favourable support when they
come before this House. I beg to move.
Mr. QUIGLEY (in morning dress): I
have pleasure in seconding the motion so
ably proposed by Senator Hill Dickson,
and at the outset I desire to say that I
feel highly honoured at having been
asked to perform this pleasing duty. Of
course, I do not regard the honour in
any personal sense, but as an honour conferred upon the working classes whom I
am privileged to represent in this House.
I am glad that such a compliment has
been paid to them, for there are no more
loyal and stalwart supporters of the
Crown and Constitution, under which
we live, than the working men and
women of Belfast and of Ulster.
our
As has been said by the proposer
of the Address,
miss
we
from
assemblage to-day one who was with us
I refer to the late Lord
a year ago.
Dufferin.
I am only a junior Member of
the Senate, but I was here sufficiently
long to admire the fair and impartial
way in which he acted as Speaker. He
was courteous to everyone, and displayed
the tact, kindness and affability which mean
so much to our smooth and harmonious
working. We shall always keep in
affectionate remembrance his noble life
and inspiring service.
May I be permitted to say that we are
very fortunate in his successor. We all
expected that Lord Bangor with his great
ability, ripe knowledge, and wide experience would make an ideal Speaker,
and we have not been disappointed. We
trust he may live long to occupy that
chair and to direct our deliberations.
With regard to the Gracious Speech,
reference is made first of all to the worldwide depression in trade and industry.
We deeply deplore that depression,
which has caused so much unemployment
and suffering, and it is little satisfaction
to us to know that it is world-wide.
Nevertheless, I think it could be grappled
with if only the right policy were
adopted the policy advocated by those engaged in industry, namely, safeguarding
and protection. Therein lies the remedy.
Why should we allow foreign goods, produced under most unfair conditions, to
be dumped freely into our country without any restriction, when high tariffs.
are placed on the goods which we export to other countries? The thing is
preposterous, and as long as it is permitted we will be in the position of a
muzzled dog, fighting another dog that
can use all its teeth. We cannot strike
back, and are thus severely handicapped.
Of course, we know that our Northern
Government has not the power to impose
tariffs, but it is a downright shame that
our working people should have to suffer
while foreigners receive the benefits.
Our Northern Government has done
much to ease the situation and to provide
work. The Loans Guarantee Acts have
proved a big success, and but for them
there would have been fewer orders,
especially in connection with shipbuilding. Owing to these guarantees the
shipyards have been fairly busy, and one
of them recently recalled some 2,300
hands who were temporarily suspended.
That is encouraging, but more is required. The outlook is not bright, but
we must not be too downcast. We have
surmounted obstacles in the past, and if
we only pull together we can do so
again. I have no doubt, that if given
a fair chance, our captains of industry,
with that enterprise, energy and
severance for which they are noted, and
our workers who have never yet let us
down, will sweep away the dark clouds,
and bring back the sunshine of prosperity.
per-
The Government deserve the warmest
thanks of the whole community for what
they have done in this direction, and I
am sure I will be forgiven if I specially
single out the Minister of Labour, for his
zealous and well-directed efforts, to afford
relief. A more earnest and sympathetic
Minister it would be impossible to find,
and he might truly be described as the
workers' friend. We have heard a great
deal about the campaign across the water
and in America to push Ulster goods. I
only wish that people at home would
realise the importance of buying Ulster
goods, if our industries are to be kept
going.
With regard to medical benefit, it is
gratifying to learn that it is giving satisfaction. It has been an undoubted boon
to the industrial classes, and though
only in operation since October last, it
has been an invaluable factor in the
health of the people.
Members will notice that the Government intends to deal further with the
scheme to provide electricity for Northern
Ireland. Last Session we had a Bill on
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
the subject, and we were then promised
a more comprehensive Measure. I presume we are now to have that larger Bill,
and if the development takes place as outlined, it should have far-reaching effects,
in increasing not only the future prosperity of our industries, but also the everyday amenities of the Province. The
people in outlying districts will not fail
to appreciate abundant supplies of electrical energy for light and power, at prices
within the reach of their pockets.
Reference is made in the Gracious
Speech to the housing question. We
admit that the Government by the subsidies have done much to relieve the
shortage which existed, but I am
satisfied that the working people have been
greatly benefited. The houses erected
were not suitable for them, and the rents
charged were not within their means.
am, therefore, pleased that the Government intend to encourage the building
of houses which will suit the working
classes, and I would suggest that the rents
of these should not be above 6/- or 7/-
a week.
I
A Measure to amend the provisions of
the Children Act should be welcome.
Many evils exist in regard to the boarding
out and nursing of children for reward,
and the sooner a stop is put to them the
better. Too much care cannot be taken
of our child life.
Hon. Senators should be pleased to
learn that further attention is to be paid
to the packing and marketing of agricultural produce. It is essential that our
eggs, fruit, potatoes, and other products,
should be sent across the water in the
best possible condition. That is the only
way in which we can capture the markets.
The legislation already passed on the subject has been very beneficial, and the
Government, therefore, feels that another
step onward should be taken. The Minister of Agriculture is most anxious to help
the farmers, and has aided them considerably to tide over their difficulties.
If
We are badly in need of a Bill dealing
with town and country planning.
such an Act had been in existence we
would not now have some of the badly
laid out streets and unsightly buildings
that we everywhere see. There is no
reason why we should not be artistic, and
try to beautify our cities and towns,
according to some proper method and plan.
Power to deal with this matter is very
necessary.
I am glad to see that the Loans
Guarantee Act is to be extended. As I
have already pointed out, these guarantees
have been most successful in bringing
orders to our city and providing employment. Other Bills dealing with Unemployment Insurance, Health Insurance,
and the disposal of Government property
are to be introduced.
I am much interested in the reference
to economy, and without going into any
discussion I will simply say that I am
satisfied to leave the matter in the hands
of the Government, as the Prime Minister
and the members of the Cabinet are as
eager as anybody else to reduce expenditure, and if they can do so, without impairing efficiency, and without injuring our
social services, I am quite certain they
will not hesitate to act. They have the
best interests of Ulster at heart, and can
be relied upon to do everything possible
for the revival of industry and the wellbeing and happiness of the people.
We are thankful that peace and good
order prevail throughout the Province,
and for that happy state of affairs our
Royal Ulster Constabulary, not forgetting
the Specials, are to be complimented.
Under all the circumstances I think the
Government have managed things well.
They have never borrowed a penny to
balance any one of their Budgets. Few,
if any, Governments could make that
claim. We stand with a clean slate, and
it is up to us to assist and co-operate with
them in maintaining that enviable position. I beg to second.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I wish to sincerely pay the usual Parliamentary
tribute to the hon. Members who have
proposed and seconded the Address in
reply to the King's Speech. If there is
one thing that is pleasing in Parliamentary life, I think it is the fact that
when a political opponent is called
upon to discharge a difficult and impossible task he receives wonderful
sympathy from the whole House. I think
the fact that the two hon. Members proposed and seconded the Address in the
manner in which they did is a great compliment not only to their intelligence and
ability, but also to their moral courage.
(Laughter.) This King's Speech will go
down to history as noteworthy, not for
what it contains but for what it does not
contain. I do not think I exaggerate
when I say that a more futile legislative
programme was never placed before any
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
3 MARCH 1931
serious assembly. When he considers
the depression in Northern Ireland today, I think the most loyal, the most
stout-hearted, and the most blind follower
of the Government could not for one
moment suggest that there is one single
Measure forecasted in that Speech that
will bring any far-reaching benefits to
the people of Northern Ireland.
Can it be seriously suggested that there
is a single Measure referred to in that
Speech that will do anything substantial
towards the regeneration and restoration
of trade and industry in this Province
and the improvement of the present
position. It has been stated that this is
a world-wide depression. I grant that
that is so, but there has been depression
in Ulster for the last ten years, ever
since this Government took office. The
world-wide depression has only lasted a
few months, but it has existed here for
the last ten years, so that the worldwide depression cannot be made any
excuse for the position we find ourselves in
to-day, a position that Members on ali
sides of the House hope will soon come
to an end.
Meeting as I do in social and professional life the farmers of Northern
Ireland, I am shocked beyond words to
think that the only contribution the
Government suggest to ease the agricultural depression is a Bill to improve
the Marketing of Fruit. I grant at
once that the Government of its own
efforts cannot restore the industries of
Northern Ireland to their former prosperity, but I do say it is the duty of the
Government to create and supply the
machinery whereby the people of Northern Ireland may work out their salvation for themselves. There is no
machinery suggested in that Speech
which can possibly assist substantially in
that restoration of trade which we all
desire to see.
On the question of agriculture, several
years ago a Committee was specially
appointed by the Government to investigate three principal questions, one of
which was this: Why is it that the
farmer receives so little for his produce,
and the consumer has to pay so much?
Why does the farmer receive 2/- for
potatoes, while the consumer has to pay
9/- That Committee, after sitting for
a considerable time, brought in a report.
They stated that they were unable to
come to a decision on that particular
point, because they had not sufficient
evidence before them. They also stated
that the great transport companies refused to give evidence, and when
challenged on that question in another
place the Prime Minister said that was
so, but they interviewed him privately
at Stormont Castle. As to the result
of that interview we can draw our own
inference, especially as the sittings of
that Committee have not since been continued. Notwithstanding the fact that
the farmers are receiving 2/- for their
potatoes and the poor people of Belfast
are paying 9/-, no effort has been made
by the Government to find out why that
should be so, and therefore they have
acquiesced in that state of affairs.
Several years ago a Committee was set
up to deal with the question of leaseholders. The omission to deal with this
matter is to my mind one of the greatest
defects in the whole legislative programme.
People can spend hundreds of thousands of
pounds in erecting business premises on
vacant ground on short leases, and when
the lease expires the landlord can come
in and without paying a penny in compensation he can confiscate that property
and take under his own control all the
labour and money that the owner has put
into those premises. The Committee
which was appointed several years ago to
deal with this matter made its report,
but not a single step has been taken by
the Government to put into operation
the findings of that Committee, or to
redress
the grievances of the leaseholders of Northern Ireland. Attention
to such matters as these would have a
substantial and lasting effect, but this is
the type of legislation the Government
has ignored, and that is the reason we
find Northern Ireland in the difficult
position it is in at the present time.
With regard to the speech delivered by
Senator Quigley in seconding the Address,
I think it was a very humiliating speech
for this House to have to listen to, because
he said it was absolutely hopeless to look
for any restoration of trade conditions
unless we had Safeguarding and Protection. But the Government cannot introduce such legislation. They have no
power to do so. Could anything be more
humiliating than for a Parliament responsible for the government of a country to
have to say, "Unless we do what we have
no power to do our country is down and
out." If Senator Quigley's prophecy of
the future is true the outlook for Northern
Ireland must be hopeless indeed.
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
I
The hon. Member also suggested that
the Government, through the police and
the "Specials," was responsible for the
restoration of law and order. I have
great admiration for the ordinary regular
constabulary of Northern Ireland.
think they do their work admirably and
efficiently and show strict impartiality,
but to say that the "Specials" were responsible for the restoration of law and
order is a very daring statement to make,
and a very wrong statement. The present
peaceful state of the country is due to the
sound common sense of the plain people
of Northern Ireland. They have realised
that on hungry stomachs it is a very
foolish thing to run at one another's
throats, and that is the reason there is
peace, and harmony and contentment in
Northern Ireland at the present time. I
do not intend to refer in detail to the
various Bills mentioned in the Speech, but
I do say, in conclusion, that the thinking
people of Northern Ireland, knowing the
position to-day, and looking at the purely
legislative programme that has been forecast, will be very disappointed with the
work that Parliament is to be called upon
to do.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I should like to add
my congratulations to the mover and
seconder of the Address for the capable
manner in which they discharged their
difficult duty. I recognise in Senator Hill
Dickson a member of this assembly who
is not afraid to speak his mind quite freely
at times, and I rejoice when I see him from
time to time on non-party Measures
rise to the full height of his stature and
speak his mind. The hon. Member has
not drawn a very consoling picture for
us this afternoon. He said the income
tax collector takes toll of a man's estate
while he is living, and he takes death
duties when the man is dead. One is
tempted to wonder what he has to work for.
Senator Quigley stated there would never
be any improvement in conditions in Northern Ireland until Protection and Safeguarding came along. I do not know
whether he draws any distinction between
Protection and Safeguarding. I do not
know whether he is very clear in his views
as to where a line is to be drawn between Protection and Safeguarding, but
if he claims to be speaking for the working
class people I would like to ask him how
it is that the overwhelming mass of the
working classes of England are bitterly
opposed to Protection and Safeguarding.
How does it come about that cries
for these two Measures come from
wealthy manufacturers and controllers of
vested interests ? I fear that behind this
Protectionist and Safeguarding plea there
is a very insidious and a very dire threat
of a reduction in the wages of the working
classes. Then when we are told about
Protection being good for the North of
Ireland, I wonder do the cotton operatives
of Lancashire favour Protection for the
linen industry of Northern Ireland. And,
vice-versa, would the linen operatives of
Northern Ireland favour Protection for the
cotton products of Lancashire. I wonder
does the Belfast shipbuilding industry
want Protection? If it does it is the first
time I think in its history that any public
demand for the Protection of the Belfast
shipbuilding industry has ever been made.
When we are talking about Protection
let us think of the position of America
to-day, the finest protected country in the
world, with tariff walls almost reaching
the sky; and yet in America the middle
classes are practically blotted out.
America is becoming a land of 100,000
millionaires and 100,000,000 common
people. That is the fruit of Protection.
Take Australia. Do we envy that land
in its present financial conditions? Why,
I read the other day that so high is Protection in Australia that a consignment of
singlets which left Japan a short time ago
for one of the Australian ports, at the
price of £28, finally changed hands
in Australia for £379. I wonder who
benefits by that. Do the working
classes benefit by it? Nor should we
forget this, that even in this Free
Trade country of ours, with all the
depression in trade, and with an army
of unemployed, the standard of living in
Great Britain or the standard of living
in Northern Ireland is at least 45 per
cent. higher than it is in France or Germany, or any of these protected countries.
It may be the intention that the working
classes should break down from the standard of living which they now exact through
the power of the vote, and descend to the
same level of living for the working classes
as in France, Belgium, and Germany.
Senator Quigley says that the Government has a clean slate. That is practically all the Government has got. That
is one part of his speech I thoroughly
endorse. The Government, so far as useful legislation is concerned, has got
nothing but a clean slate. My Friend,
Senator McAllister, has reminded the
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
3 MARCH 1931
House that a committee was appointed by
the Ministry of Home Affairs two or three
years ago to inquire into the law relating
to landlord and tenant in Northern Ireland towns. That committee reported
over two years ago. The Home Office has
thrust the report into a pigeon-hole, and
there it will rest in cold obscurity and
in cold storage for many a long day. Of
course the Home Office has other things
to think about than relieving the hardships of town tenants. It has more concern, for instance, in extracting every
penny of revenue out of the pockets of the
licensed trade. Town tenants cannot
apply the whip and spur with the same.
skill and the same effectiveness as the
"pussyfoots" of Northern Ireland, who
are now the driving power behind the
Home Office.
When we met this day twelve months
ago over 50,000 were unemployed in Northern Ireland. We meet to-day with
over 70,000 unemployed.
The Ministry
have been simply drifting during these
twelve months, and the unemployed have
been drifting too, no one knows whither.
Of course the existence of this vast army
of workless men and women-workless and
underfed, because the dole, useful as it has
been in many directions, has not given
these people proper sustenance, proper
food, and proper nutriment, does not
break the sleep of Members of the Government. And when one reads of prominent men in this Province saying that all
is well, that this Province is a paradise, a
land flowing with milk and honey-well,
I credit them with combining the imagination of Defoe with the inaccuracy of
Ananias.
The Ministry have been doing something. Four or five months ago I spoke
of the hibernation period then beginning.
It has been going on since, but I notice
originality on the part of at least one
Minister. I notice that recently the
Minister of Agriculture, in the midst of
the economic blizzard that has struck
Ulster, has been recommending farmers to
give fowl cod liver oil in teaspoonfuls.
That is an effective contribution. That is
a step forward. That shows there is originality somewhere in the Northern Government. I congratulate the Minister.
am sure that Ministers of that type will
bring back Ulster's pristine prosperity in
time—but not in our time.
I
Then the Gracious Speech which we
listened to promises no relief in respect of
a grave and urgent problem, the administration of the general public health of this
Province. That administration is at a
miserably low level outside the City of
Belfast. The poor-law medical service
originated in 1851. It is worthy of the year
1851. It is not worthy of the year 1931.
It is 80 years behind the time. The Government in 1923 appointed a local government committee to inquire into this matter. It held many meetings and made
many recommendations on the subject of
poor-law reform and the medical service
and reported in 1926, five years ago.
made very important suggestions and recommendations, but it might as well never
have sat. Nothing has been done to carry
those recommendations into law. It made
one rather striking proposal. It reported
against medical benefits on which my
Friend Senator Quigley has spoken today. That committee reported against
medical benefits, the system now in vogue,
introduced by the Ministry of Labour,
which he claims is a Ministry in excelsis.
It
There is some mention of a Town
Planning Bill. That Bill was circulated
some months ago, and I think it is a very
thin Measure by contrast with the
English Measure. It leaves out the most
important provision of the English Act
as I understand it. The Government
instead of being the subject of eulogy, I
think, deserves pity and deserves censure.
It is in need of a sweeping overhaul of
its methods, machinery, and entire
policy. What has been done in the past.
few months, and what is going to be done
in the next few months to relieve the deplorable problem of unemployment? As
far as one can see it is going to do nothing. It is a Conservative Government.
Conservatives, although divided into
many hostile camps, Empire Crusaders,
and other types of men in the Conservative ranks, are all united on
point, as good Conservatives always
should be on the policy of doing nothing.
one
Now, the last proposition the Government make is to reduce the subsidy on
housing, to cut it down to a fraction of
what it has been heretofore, and that is
done at a time when the Government
has not made a single contribution to
the housing of the more poorly paid working classes. No house has been built for
them not a brick of a house has been
laid on their behalf.
Mr. DORMAN: I join in congratulating the mover and the seconder of the
Address to the Governor. One does not
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
a
[Mr. Dorman]
feel like criticising a Government composed of representatives of a State in
which adult suffrage exists, because one
would be justified in coming to the conclusion that in such circumstances
country has the Government it deserves.
I do not think the Government is facing
up to the problems that confront the
people of Northern Ireland in the
manner in which they should.
I just
wish to refer to one or two Measures
mentioned in the Speech.
In the first place a Bill which is to be
introduced for town and country planning
has been placed in our hands. As
Senator Campbell has said, it is not at
all similar to the Measure passed across
the water, inasmuch as the clause containing financial assistance for the working classes across the water is left out in
this Measure. In addition to that, I do
not think that such a Measure is at all
likely to accomplish the object the Government may have in view unless it is
preceded by some Measure dealing with
the land.
If
Recently the municipality has had
occasion to look out for sites for the
erection of schools inside the borough
area of the city of Belfast. In seeking
the cost of some of these sites they found
that for one in the neighbourhood in
which I live, containing one rood and a
few feet of land, the landlord asked a
price of £2,700 for practically a rood of
land, which means £10,000 per acre.
the Government is not prepared, in the
first instance, to face the foundation
fact underlying slum clearance and
proper town improvement of the housing
of the working classes, then I think all
the Bills they can introduce and pass
through this House and another place,
and place on the Statute Book will be
completely abortive and will not accomplish the object they have in view.
The Province is suffering more probably than any other part of the commonwealth of nations from that unfortunate
problem of unemployment. It seems to
me like a cancer eating into the social
fabric of this particular area, and there
is no sign of improvement. I wonder
how many of the Members and how many
of the people really consider what this
vast army of unemployed means. According to statistics issued by the Ministry
of Labour a few weeks ago, 77,000 persons were on the live register. I think
one would be justified in stating that at
least 10,000 or 15,000 more persons are
unemployed than the number that sign
at the exchanges in this area. That
army of 80,000 unemployed persons exists
at present in Northern Ireland, and
each one of these unemployed persons is
capable of producing on the average £3
of wealth during a week, inclusive of
their own wages.
That would be a very
moderate estimate indeed of the productivity of the unemployed were they
exercised in industry. It means that
the State in Northern Ireland is losing
a sum of practically a quarter of a million
pounds per week. £250,000 of potential
wealth is lost to the community every
week because this vast army of unemployed have not extended to them their
right to work.
I think Senator Quigley in seconding
the Address to the Governor laid stress
upon Safeguarding and Protection as the
only solution of the problem of unemployment. I hold in my hand an
analysis of the revenue returns of Great
Britain and Northern Ireland for the last
ten years.
an
The returns during that ten years for
income tax given by the various classes
conveyed to me at least an overwhelming
reason why we are suffering from this
continuous unemployment. I find from
this return that those who make a return
of their income derived from land have
increased their income from £290,000,000
to £415,000,000,
increase of
£125,000,000. Returns of income from
interest increased from £90,000,000 to
£161,000,000, an increase of £71,000,000.
Those who were engaged in the production of commodities and returned
their income from profits showed
an income of £1,300,000,000, an increase
of £200,000,000, while the cultivators of
the soil recorded a decrease of £49,000,000.
Wage earners subject to income tax returned £335,000,000, as compared with
£863,000,000 ten years ago, or a decrease
of £528,000,000.
I think anyone who examined these
figures would immediately realise that
those who returned an income from the
three classes of land, interest, and profits,
and who derived an increased income of
£396,000,000 from these sources, did not
and could not spend that £396,000,000
in the purchase of commodities from week
to week. The cultivators of the soil and
the wage earners have had a reduction of
£49,000,000 and £528,000,000 respecti-
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
3 MARCH 1931
vely, a total of £577,000,000. Thus
while some classes of the community are clamouring for Protection
and Safeguarding, they are all the
time clamouring for reducing the cost of
production by decreasing the wages of the
working man. We have actually reduced
the wages of the consumers in the home
market by a sum equivalent to the whole
of our foreign trade, and while we go on
clamouring for further and further reduction in the purchasing capacity of the
people at our own doors how can we hope
to solve this great problem of unemployment.
I notice the Government intend bringing in a Measure the object of which, I
presume, is to extend the existing
transitional period. I do not expect it
will contain any solution of the unemployment problem, or give the working classes
the right to work. The working classes
are the chief customers of a country. A
short time ago the Government brought
in a Measure of derating. At that time
they stated that derating was one of the
solutions of the unemployment problem.
Since then unemployment has mounted up
and up, until now it has reached the
figure I have previously mentioned. Until such time as the mass of consumers in
the home market get that consideration
which they ought to receive from the employing class, from those who own and
control their means of livelihood, there can
be no hope of a solution of the problem.
In my opinion, when the working classes
have been deprived of the right to work.
when they are able and willing to work,
when they are deteriorating and becoming
demoralised because they have not the
right to work, when they have no legal
right of access to the means of livelihood,
because it is not profitable to the people
who own and control their means of livelihood, they are justified in calling upon
the Government to give themselves and
their dependents the means of living. If
the Government are not prepared to extend to them the right to work they should
at least take some steps to provide the
means of living for the unemployed from
day to day.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): We find ourselves again at the
beginning of another Session, and my first
duty is to congratulate the mover and
seconder of the Address on their admirable speeches. Mr. Hill Dickson has
been a member of this House since 1921
and his contributions to our debates have
Mr.
always been marked by an independent
judgement and sound common sense.
Quigley has been admitted to our counsels
more recently, but he has already given
us a measure of the first-hand knowledge
which he possesses of everything that pertains to the life of our industrial community. I should like to endorse what
the mover and seconder of the Address
said about our late Speaker, to whose
memory this House has already paid tribute. I also associate myself with their
hope and belief that our present Speaker
will extend the same consideration and
courtesy to every Member as did the late
Marquess of Dufferin.
With regard to the King's Speech, I
need not emphasise the anxieties which
it reflects, and which are ever present to
all those who are concerned with our
principal industries, both in town and
in country. It is the duty of a Government at all times to practise economy,
and with the continuance of the present
depression, which shows as yet no sign of
lightening, it becomes imperative on the
part of our administration that this
economy should be even more stringently
exercised. This is, of course, primarily
a matter for the other House and will
come up for discussion there when the
Estimates are presented, but, as the
question is of vital importance, I think
it would not be out of place to emphasise
it here as well.
It is proposed that our expenditure
for the next financial year should be
scrutinised with the utmost care, and I
am afraid that some sacrifices will have
to be made. In my own department, the
Ministry of Education, the Government
is seriously considering whether some
lightening has not become necessary in
the very heavy cost of teachers' salaries
which falls on the Estimates. This
would, of course, be a disagreeable task,
but it affords at least some consolation to
know that according to the official figures
the cost of living has abated to a not inconsiderable extent in recent monthsan
abatement which automatically reduces the bonus which is received in
addition to basic salary by those public
servants whose remuneration is determined on a sliding scale.
Again, in connection with educational
expenditure it is hoped that the local
authorities will exercise a rigid economy
in the services which they administer,
and particularly in the matter of 'he
burden arising from the erection of new
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
school buildings. I have always regarded
this question of school accommodation as
perhaps the most vital problem to be
dealt with by our local committees, and
most of them have made a very good beginning in tackling what really amounts
to a wholesale re-building or re-conditioning programme for the schools
which are transferred to them from the
old voluntary system; but we all have to
recognise that in times like the present,
the most we can hope to do is to deal
with cases of urgent necessity and to
keep the more normal programme for
another day. I have been in informal
touch with the chairmen of our various
committees, and I am glad to say that
they fully share these views.
The savings made as a result of strict
economy in this matter of new buildings
cannot in the nature of things be a very
large one, so far as immediate relief to
either local or central expenditure is concerned, as the necessary funds are usually
raised by long-term loans; but, at the
same time, I hope that for the next year
or two there may be some measure of
relief from the heavy expenses of our
educational services, without doing any
harm to the health and well-being of our
children, and without causing damage to
the steady progress which has undoubtedly
been achieved in education generally as
a result of the Education Acts. I have
made special mention of the possibilities
of economy in education, but it is not to
be assumed that this branch of the
public services will alone be called on to
bear the brunt. The Government is
anxiously considering the possibility of
savings in every direction in which it
believes that such a course is possible.
With regard to the legislation foreshadowed in the Gracious Speech, hon.
Members will see that the first matter is
in connection with electricity. This
Measure aims at replacing the existing
system of piecemeal distribution of
current by a carefully thought out
system of high tension transmission lines,
from a common point of supply, which
would be designed and equipped to meet
every possible demand for light and
power with the maximum degree of
economy and efficiency. As a result it is
hoped that not only the inhabitants of
our larger towns, but also the people
living in outlying districts, may ultimately have supplies of electrical energy
for light and power at such prices as will
encourage consumption on a much larger
scale than at present. The Bill is to be
introduced at a very early date in another place, and I do not, therefore,
propose to make any more detailed statement, which would only anticipate the
debate.
The next matter on which legislation
is promised is in connection with housing.
As the result of previous Acts passed by
this Parliament it is felt that the housing
shortage is well on the way to being
solved. Up to the end of last month
almost 25,000 houses have been approved
for erection, and almost 20,000 of these
have been completed. The Government
felt therefore, that as the shortage is no
longer acute they would not be justified
in the present financial stringency in
continuing the grant of £100 or 80 to
builders of these small houses. At the
same time, they also felt that as the
shortage had not been entirely met it
would be better not to do away with the
subsidy altogether. Accordingly, it is
intended to introduce a Bill granting a
reduced subsidy of £50.
The next matter is IL regard to the
consolidation of the law relating to Joint
Stock Companies. The House will remember that when we had before us the
recent Companies Bill it was then indicated that a consolidating Measure would
follow, as has actually taken place in
Great Britain. This, it is hoped, will be
introduced this Session.
In accordance with the recommendation of the Local Government Commission, which reported some time ago,
it is proposed to codify the Lunacy Laws
and bring them up to date. The existing
laws are almost one hundred years old
and are, of course, quite unsuitable in
many cases for modern developments,
both in medical science and in the care
and comfort of the sick, particularly of
the mentally defective.
Hon. Members will be immediately
asked to give a First Reading to a Bill
amending the Children Act of 1908 in
certain particulars, so as to secure better
supervision of those children whose parents
do not wish to keep them and who are
taken charge of and brought up by strangers
for a monetary consideration. The Bill is
also intended to cover the case of those
children who are left in charge of others
while their parents are at work. Many
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
3 MARCH 1931
cases have arisen in the past where
children have been lost sight of, and the
Bill is to ensure that some authority exercises supervision over all such cases. It
is felt that the present legislation does not
go far enough, and it is hoped that as a
result of the proposals all of these children
will be more closely supervised.
The next matter in the Gracious Speech
deals with legislation for further improvement in the marketing of eggs and fruit.
Notwithstanding the notable success
already achieved by our marketing legislation for eggs, still higher standards of
quality are demanded to meet the requirements of cross-Channel markets. With
the co-operation of the wholesale trade my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Agriculture, hopes to introduce in the present
session a Measure which is intended to
improve the quality still further by the
extension of education amongst poultry
keepers on such important matters
as marketing and production.
finance this extension of the Ministry's
educational work the licensed egg merchants have generously offered to agree to
an increase in the scale of fees and charges
under the Marketing of Eggs Acts. It
is also proposed to reintroduce the Marketing of Fruit Bill, which was postponed
last session in another place.
To
The next reference in the Speech is to
town and country planning. A Town
Planning Bill was introduced last session
in another place in order to enable all
the interests concerned to examine its proposals and to make any suggestions which
might be considered desirable. A con-
Its
ference has since been held with the
various town surveyors, and I hope that
when the Bill comes before this House it
will meet with general approval.
chief object is to eliminate as far as possible the evils arising from the haphazard
methods which have prevailed in the past
in the erection of buildings and the
laying out of roads. It is hoped that in
future, as the result of this legislation,
before any new works are entered upon
due regard will be had, not only to the
general health and convenience of the community, but also to æsthetic considerations. The new Bill is to be called the
Housing and Planning Bill, and it is
thought desirable that it should extend to
any area which is likely to be developed.
One of the most important features will be
that dealing with the clearance and improvement of slum areas.
The only other Bill which I propose to
mention is that indicated under the title
of Health Insurance. Regulations are at
present in force to prevent certain people
who have been unemployed for a prolonged
period from falling out of insurance and
thereby losing, not only their title to
medical and cash benefits, but also their
contributory pension rights. The object
of the Bill will be to extend for a further
period under certain conditions these privileges, and to transfer the cost from
insurance funds to the Exchequer.
In reply to the Gracious Speech in this
debate, hon. Members of the Opposition
have deplored the inability of the Government to solve the unemployment
problem. You have only to look round
all the States of Europe to see that all
Governments of all shades of political
opinion have failed in the same way. The
fact remains that unemployment primarily
depends upon one thing, the inability or
unwillingness of foreign customers to give
an order. No Government that I know
of can make them do so, and that is the
real consideration on which unemployment rests. The Imperial Government
at present in office, we all remember,
came into office on their promise to cure
unemployment. They had all the wish
in the world to do so, and they got into
office on the strength of that promise,
and with all the will and the cleverness
they undoubtedly possess they have
failed. I do not think it is within the
power of any Government, either in Northern Ireland or anywhere else, to cure
unemployment by their efforts, and such
improvement when it comes will be
found to be based upon the courage and
goodwill and endurance of the people.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved: That a humble address be
presented to his Grace the Governor in
the following words: We, His Majesty's
most dutiful and loyal subjects, the
Senate of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland, beg leave to offer our humble
thanks to your Grace for the Speech
which your Grace has addressed to both
Houses of Parliament.
Ordered: That the said Address be
presented to his Grace by
Councillors of Northern Ireland.
Privy
Adjournment-
Business of the House.
THE SENATE
Children (Amendment) Bill,
"to amend and extend Part I of the
Children Act, 1908, SO as to make
further provision for the protection of
child life," was presented by Mr. Long,
read a First time, ordered to be read a
Second time on Tuesday next, and to be
printed. [Bill No. 1 (S).]
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before I
move the Adjournment I should like to
mention one matter, the question about
the bust of the late Speaker. I think
that every Member of this House has had
a chance of seeing it. It is now in the
smoking room, and you will remember
that Members of the Senate agreed to
purchase it, and I shall be very glad to
receive subscriptions for it at the Ministry of Education. The amount of the
subscription will be left to each individual.
I beg to move "That this House do now
adjourn until Tuesday next," when I hope
to take the Children Bill, Second Reading, and any other business that may
arise.

THE UNEMPLOYMENT FUND.
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House, if he can state the
average number of persons unemployed
each month during the year 1930; the
average amount paid in benefit during
the same period; the average amount of
contributions paid into the fund; and
the amount of the deficiency, if any.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The answer
to the hon. Member's question is in the
form of a statistical table which will be
put in the official report. I am also
sending him a copy.

to:-

THE COMMITTEE ON PUBLIC
EXPENDITURE.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Committee set up by the Ministry of Finance
to inquire into the distribution of public
expenditure between the Exchequer of
Northern Ireland and the rates consists
of one supporter of the Government from
each House of Parliament and five permanent Civil Service Officials; whether any
effect has been given to the protest of the
Council of Municipal Authorities against
the composition of this Committee;
whether the question of a general revolution of rateable property in Northern Ireland is within the scope of the inquiry of
this Committee; and whether it is proposed to give any representation on it to
local authorities.
In line 7 of the question the word
"revolution" is an obvious misprint for
"revaluation."
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
10 MARCH 1931
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
the hon. and learned Member is not concerned in any revolution. I did not think
he was, and I have taken the word as
"revaluation" throughout.
The Committee to which the hon. and
learned Member refers was set up as long
ago as July, 1930, and considerable publicity has been given to its personnel and
terms of reference, which are as follows:
"Having regard to the resources of
Northern Ireland and its taxable and rateable capacity, to consider the present
system of Government Grants in aid of
Local Authorities' expenditure and the
distribution of public expenditure between
the Exchequer and the Rates, and to recommend what changes, if any,
desirable.'
are
My right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Finance, announced his intention of
setting up such a Committee in the course
of his Budget speech last year. He pointed
out the necessity of some such investigation
taking place, in view, amongst other
things, of the effect on the finances of
Government and Local Authorities of the
introduction of the Derating Scheme.
At
that time the Minister intimated that he
had in mind a Committee composed entirely of Civil Servants, but when he came
to choose the personnel he decided that
from the public point of view it would be
more satisfactory that persons representative of wider interests should be included.
Accordingly, members additional to the
Civil Servants were appointed, namely,
Senator Leslie as Chairman of the Committee, a choice with which this House, I
am sure, is in full agreement (hon. Members: Hear, hear)-Captain Chichester-
Clark, who sits in another place and is
also a County Councillor, and Mr. Malcolm Gordon, representing the business
community. I hardly think it necessary for
me to tell the House that the first two of
these Gentlemen are in no sense supporters
of the Government on this Committee.
The reasons for their appointment were
that they were independent persons who
would be able to bring knowledge and
experience to the assistance of the inquiry.
I should like also to dispel any idea that
because there are five Civil Servants on
the Committee their veiws are likely to be
all the same. Two of them represent the
Ministry of Finance, it is true, but of the
remaining three two represent the Ministry
of Home Affairs and one is the permanent
secretary of my own department. I do not
think that there is any fear that they will
not view the various problems presented
with an entirely dispassionate and unbiassed judgment.
Until a letter was received, at the end
of December last, from the Secretary to
the General Council of Municipal Authorities, that is to say, over four months after
the Committee had been set up, there was
no suggestion from any quarter whatsoever that there were objections to the
composition of the Committee, or that it
did not command the confidence of the
Local Authorities.
I may say that correspondence has taken place with the General
Council, and it is hoped that the position
is appreciated, and that there is now no
ground for any possible misunderstanding
on the part of Local Authorities.
In regard to the matter of revaluation,
although the question whether any particular point comes within the terms of
reference is a matter for the decision of
the Chairman, I should imagine that the
question of the basis of valuation of rateable property in Northern Ireland and
any revised valuation must necessarily
come within the scope of the Committee's
inquiry.
I wish on behalf of the Government to
make it clear that any suggestion that the
Committee was to develop some pre-determined line of Government policy is quite
erroneous. The object of the Committee
is to facilitate the collection of evidence
from every point of view and the investigation of the necessarily complicated
details, with a view to the presentation of
a report which will assist the Minister of
Finance and the Government in the formulation of future policy in regard to these
financial questions.
In reply to the last part of the hon. and
learned Member's question, as I have
already pointed out, the Committee was
formed, and has been in session for eight
months, and any addition to its personnel
could not now be made. I am happy to
say, however, that the Association of Local
Authorities concerned have agreed to give
evidence before the Committee, and, by
thus helping to throw light on a difficult
subject, particularly from the point of
view of local finance, I am sure that they
will facilitate the Committee in coming to
proper and, I hope, prompt conclusions
in the presentation of their report.
