REPRESENTATION OF THE PEOPLE
BILL.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether any representation was made by or on behalf of the
Government to the Imperial Government
that the provisions of the Representation
of the People (No. 2) Bill, now before the
Parliament of the United Kingdom, requiring voting at elections to be by the
method of the alternative vote, should not
extend to Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is not in
the public interest to give the information
sought for.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Might I ask the
noble Lord if the Government will communicate to Mr. Clynes its appreciation
of his ready acquiescence in its views?
BILLS PRESENTED.
Coursing of Hares Bill,
"to make better provision with regard to
coursing," presented by Mr. Leslie, read
a First time, and ordered to be printed.
Wild Birds Protection Bill,
"to consolidate, simplify, and amend the
law relating to the protection of wild
birds," presented by Mr. Campbell, supported by Mr. Dorman, Mr. Hill Dickson,
Mr. T. S. McAllister, Mr. Samuel Cunningham, read a First time, and ordered
to be printed.
Children (Amendment) Bill [S].
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The object
of this Bill is to secure better supervision
of those children whose parents do not wish
to keep them and who are taken charge
of and brought up by strangers in consideration of some monetary reward.
At present, under Part I of the Children Act, 1908, it is the duty of the Board
of Guardians to look after these children,
and all persons receiving children for
reward are required to send a notice to
the Board of Guardians within 48 hours
of receiving a child. The Board of
Guardians has the power to appoint
inspectors to visit the homes in which the
children are kept and see that they are
being properly treated. If the home is
unsuitable either for the reason that the
premises are defective or because the person taking the child is negligent or of bad
character, the Guardians may have the
child removed to a more suitable place.
no
All this is very good so far as it goes,
but it will be seen that the powers of the
Guardians do not arise until the child
has actually been placed in the home, and
further, the Act does not apply if the
period for which the child is kept is less
than 48 hours. There is, moreover,
obligation on anyone to ascertain the
name or whereabouts of a child's parents.
My attention has been directed to the
abuses which may arise under the present
arrangements by several Boards of Guardians, and no doubt Members of this
House will, themselves, have seen from
time to time, reports in the newspaper
of cases where children have been brought
into Belfast from outside and handed over
to someone at the Railway Stations to be
given to nurse without any information
whatever as to the name of the child or
its parentage. It is common knowledge
that there are certain persons who make
it their business to act as intermediaries
between the persons desiring to dispose of
a child and persons willing to receive it.
I think you will agree that this is a profession which ought to be discouraged.
The following are some typical examples
of abuses which have arisen. Members
will understand that the names of the
persons concerned are not given
:-
A woman-let us call her Mrs. Xinformed the inspector she could give no
information regarding the child she was
.37

Children Bill-
Second Reading
keeping except that it had been given to
her by some man unknown who had given
her 20/-. Subsequently, however, she
gave the information.
10 MARCH 1931
the Bill under which the words "child"
and "children" are substituted for the
words "infant" and "infants" wherever
they occur. It will be seen by reference
to Section 131 of the Act of 1908 that a
"child" is there defined as a person under
the age of 14 years, so that the substitution proposed has the effect of raising the
limit of age under which children will
have the protection of the Act.
Another woman, who was found keeping an unregistered child, stated that the
of
child was left with her by a Mrs. Y,
Dublin, who gave her £15. The woman
stated that Mrs. Y informed her that she
(Mrs. Y) was the employer of the child's
mother. The child's mother is not
known.
Here is a third case. Mrs. X stated
that she asked a Mrs. Y to get her a
baby girl. Some time later Mrs. Y sent
for her to go to a Nursing Home and she
got a baby, for keeping which she was to
get £10.
Mrs. Y warned her not to let
anyone know she got any payment. This
Mrs. Y figures in a number of other cases,
and evidently acts as intermediary
between mothers wanting to get rid of
children and persons wishing to take
children.
Here is another type of abuse. A
child born in the workhouse was taken
out by its grandmother and placed in the
hands of certain parties. It was given
out by these parties to a woman to nurse,
the woman's name being given as, let us
say, A.B. On inquiry no person of this
name could be traced, and the whereabouts of the child remain unknown,
although the police took the matter up.
I do not think it is necessary to comment
any further on the scandals of which
these are typical.
It is a further defect that the existing
Act only applies to children under 7 years.
of age. While it is particularly necessary
that children of tender years should be
carefully looked after, there is no doubt
that in many cases older children who are
kept by strangers are often neglected and
suffer many hardships, and it appears
desirable to make some provision for their
protection.
The cases of children who are left with
strangers while their mothers are at work
have not hitherto received any special
attention, and it has been thought proper
to make some provision also for their
protection. Accordingly the main proposals contained in the Bill are three in
number.
In the first place it is proposed to extend
the age of the children to whom Part I
of the Act of 1908 applies from 7 years
to 14 years. This is done in Clause 2 of
In the second place it is proposed to
require persons concerned in the handing
over or receiving of children to give notice
beforehand to the Board of Guardians, so
that the latter may be in a position to
determine whether a home is suitable
before a child is handed over. This is done
in Clause 1 of the Bill. This clause takes
the place of the existing Section 1 of the
Act of 1908, and under it the person proposing to hand over a child, as well as the
person receiving it, is required to give 48.
hours' previous notice to the Board of
Guardians and obtain their permission in
writing to the handing over of the child.
In addition to the particulars hitherto
required information will also have to be
given in the notice regarding the parentage of the child. The other sub-sections
of this clause are merely consequential,
and are generally similar to provisions
contained in the original section.
In the third place it is proposed to apply
to children kept for the day while their
mothers are at work the same supervision,
so far as possible, as is exercised in the
case of children placed out permanently
with strangers. The provisions relating
to these children are contained in Clauses
4 to 8 of the Bill. In the case of these
children, however, the same elaborate
machinery of notification is not feasible
and is indeed unnecessary, and it is merely
proposed that persons who make it their
business to keep children for the day shall
apply to the Board of Guardians and obtain a written permit. The Board of
Guardians is to have the power of inspecting
the places where children are kept, and if
it appears to them that the place is unsuitable or that the person in charge is
undesirable, they may refuse the permit,
or where one has been given, withdraw it.
It will be understood, of course, that the
Bill applies only in cases where people
make it their business to keep children for
some monetary consideration, and will not
affect anyone who only takes a child on an
odd occasion to oblige a neighbour. Only
people who make what I might call a
regular business of looking after children
421780
Children Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
will be affected. I might also make it
clear that neither the provisions relating
to children permanently handed over to
strangers nor to children left for the day
apply in the case where the child is left
with a relative, nor do they apply to a
child in any institution established for the
care and protection of children which is
conducted in good faith for religious or
charitable purposes, nor will it apply to a
school. All these exemptions are contained in the existing Act, so that in making them I am not introducing any new
principle.
I trust that these proposals will be of
great benefit in aiding Boards of Guardians
to carry out their duties in relation to
children more effectively, and in particular
will to a great extent prevent the abuses
which have arisen in connection with what
has come to be known as "baby farming."
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: In following the noble
Viscount, and in supporting this Bill,
perhaps I might be allowed to say that
his remarks on the first Clause of the Bill
before us might almost be summarised
by saying that the Bill is intended to
secure that the stable door shall be locked
before the horse is stolen, rather than
afterwards. That, in this particular
connection, is a proposal which will, I
think, commend itself to us all.
If I
remember rightly, the recent report, or
comparatively recent report, of the Local
Government Commission on Poor Law Reform recommended that the age should be
extended in cases where nursing is concerned up to sixteen, but I am afraid I
must confess that I do not for the moment
remember whether that recommendation
was limited to day nursing or whether it
covered both classes of nursing which are
covered by the Bill. In any case, I
imagine the House would not wish to go
so far as sixteen years of age. It would
be inappropriate as regards the day nursing, and I think also that it will be
recognised there are obvious advantages in
confining it to the age which corresponds
to the school attendance age, that of fourteen.
I do not think there is anything more
that is necessary to add as regards what I
might call Part 1 of the Bill, that being,
of course, the first three clauses.
Passing to the second part of the Bill,
dealing with the provisions as to day nursing, I think I should have liked a little
further information as to who it is who
want these provisions; at whose instance
is this part of the Bill brought forward. We know that the principle,
generally referred to as the créche, is one
that has been followed for many years in
divers places, and it may very well require
supervision, but I should like further information as to whether this Bill is
designed more or less to meet conditions
which arise in Belfast, and not in the
country as a whole. I think the matter is
of considerable importance in this way;
that in the smaller and less populated
country districts there is less chance perhaps of the necessity for supervision being
recognised, and less chance that the local
authorities that is, the Boards of
Guardians will avail themselves of the
provisions of the Bill. It seems to me to
be a case in which we ought to be satisfied that there will be a really extensive
publication of the terms of the Measure
in order that its terms and its meaning
may be appreciated throughout the Province.
As regards the Bill itself, one observes
that the wording of the earlier Clauses in
the Act of 1908 have been followed with
the greatest care. I am sure that is a
method which has many advantages, but I
am not quite sure that in one case to which
I shall have to draw attention at a later
date the sequence is not generally, perhaps, a little too closely observed.
In one
case particularly I think that the provision for exemption which applies to the
Clause 1 class is not a principle which
ought to be applied in the case of the day
nursing establishments. That is a matter
which ought, I think, to engage our attention when we come to examine the Bill
more in detail. It does seem to me to be
necessary that a local authority should ensure that a place is kept up properly if it
is to be one exempted from inspection, and
again I must say that experience in the
country districts as regards school attendance officers does not lead one to be unduly sanguine that home inspection will
be carried out with any great enthusiasm
unless driving force is pretty constantly
employed.
It
There remains one further point.
seems to me that the effect of the appointment of the child protection visitor must
be to cause certain expense to be incurred
by someone. The Bill is silent as regards
the fund from which that expense is to
be met. As the local authority is by virtue of the Act of 1908 presumably-in

I
I
Children Bill-
Second Reading.
10 MARCH 1931
-
fact undoubtedly having regard to the last
Clause of the Bill the Board of
Guardians, it must be assumed that any
expense incurred has ultimatly to fall
upon that much enduring fund, the poor
rate, but there is no authority in the Bill
to cast that new expenditure necessitated
by the inspection of daily nursed children
upon that fund, unless it be an implied
authority that it is an expense of the
Board of Guardians. As to that I presume that you, my Lord, are satisfied
there is no indirect indication in the Bill
which takes it outside the competence of
this House. I do suggest that the Bill
ought to say in plain words, although it
may be with some addition of lines in
italics, from what fund any expense incurred by this new service is intended to
be met. I submit this is a matter on
which the position should be made clear.
On the whole I support the Bill, readily
accepting the statement that the supervision of day nursing is desirable and
necessary, and being very whole-heartedly
in support of the earlier Clauses which are
very desirable for the better protection of
child life.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
think this is a Bill that should be supported whole-heartedly by every Member
of this House. Any effort that is made
with the object of stopping or even restricting the traffic in child life is worthy
of every support. Anyone who has studied
this matter must be aware of the
fact that this so-called "baby farming" is carried on to a shameful extent,
especially in Belfast. It is true there
were quite a number of cases of children
brought into the city and handed over to
other persons, and the fact of the matter
is they are lost to everybody. The suffering that must be entailed to these infants
is terrible.
There is just one thing I would like to
say. I do think that the custom
followed by the poor-law institutions helps
to a considerable extent to cause this
traffic that takes place. For instance,
there are girls who make an error for the
first time. They go into these institutions and have a child there, and when
they leave the institution it is quite possible that they may desire to go straight
in the future. Such a girl may have to
work for her living, and the habit of these
institutions, as far as I know, is to compel the unmarried mother to carry the
child out with her. There is an innocent
girl-or, at any rate, a girl who has unintentionally made a slip and she has
to earn a living for herself. How is she
possibly to make a new start in life if
she has to carry out with her the evidence
of her offence? The girl has no other
option but to dispose of the child as
early as possible to any person who can
be got to take it off her hands.
I certainly took notice of the Newtownards case, and I do think the poorlaw institutions should be a little more
humane and consider the case of these
unfortunate girls. If they would only keep
the child and give the girl an opportunity
of trying to walk straight and of making
a living for herself, I believe there would
be less of this. However, I am glad
the Government have introduced this
Bill at the present time to try if possible
to restrict this shameful and terrible
business.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to thank the
right hon. Gentleman, the Leader of the
House, for his able exposition of this.
Measure. But it seems to me the working man may unwittingly be guilty of an
offence under Sub-section (a) of Clause 1.
Notification has to be given 48 hours before the person taking charge of the
infant is really entitled to take such
charge. At the close of the Bill the relatives to whom a person may hand over
a child without having given such information are referred to. There are, no
doubt, numerous instances of workingmen
in a large industrial centre like Belfast who
are strangers to the city and probably
strangers to the Province, and whose
wives may give birth to children. She
may have no relatives such as described
in the latter part of the Bill to take
charge of the child. In such instances
if the notification is not given beforehand-and it is impossible for the man
to give information 48 hours before the
child is born-and if the wife has no relative in the neighbourhood to whom she
can hand over the child, they will be
guilty of an offence if they offer reward
to any person to take charge of the child.
I just wanted to draw attention to that
particular aspect of the Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill. I would
like to ask a question as to the provision
of day nursing in the City of Belfast.
There are quite a number of women
whose husbands earn very small wages,
Censure Motion
Censure Motion
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
and these women have to go out to work.
What I want to know is this: If this
woman has a child and she takes the
child to her mother, that is, the grandmother of the child, will it be necessary
to have permission from the local
authorities for this grandmother?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No. She
is exempted. The relative does not
count. As regards the question of day
nursing which the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) referred to, the provision was asked for by the Belfast Board
of Guardians. But at the same time, I
think, similar circumstances might
easily arise in other large industrial
centres, such as Lisburn and Ballymena.
With regard to Senator Dorman's point,
I think I would like to look into the
matter a little more fully before giving
an answer I will refer to it at a later
stage.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Censure Motion.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this House regrets the failure of
the Government to present to Parliament
any Measures for the reform of the law
of landlord and tenant in towns, for the
utilisation of the labour of the many
thousands of unemployed in useful and
necessary schemes of development, or for
the betterment of the administration of
public health in Northern Ireland."
For two years the Government have
been saying that they have under consideration the amendment of the law of
landlord and tenant in non-agricultural
holdings. That has been said again and
again. The Prime Minister himself more
than a year ago said that he was not
afraid to tackle this question. He has
not tackled it. He has not faced up to
the question. To-day we are, as far as
the leaseholders who hold building leases
are concerned, living under a system that
is a legacy of the old feudal days and old
feudal ideas. Under that system a landlord is enabled to acquire the improvements of his tenants which are the product of their energy, enterprise, outlay,
and sacrifice. Under that system industrious tenants whose leases expire are
left without any security of tenure. The
Government give no promise to reform
the law in this direction.
Of course, one may say, you cannot
expect the Government to make any promise of the kind. It is a landlords'
Government, and a landlords' Government does not desire reform on these
lines. It looks upon tenants' right as
landlords' wrong. It looks upon security
for the tenant as confiscation of the landlords' interests. Therefore, the Government, true to type and true to tradition,
will do nothing. Then again I must confess building leaseholders are themselves
very much to blame. They as a body
will not embarrass the Government.
They will not agitate, and we all know
that any class nowadays which has a
grievance and will not agitate goes to the
wall. But though they do not agitate
they are a very discontented class,
though they may be a very inarticulate
class.
In England the tenants are sheltered
under the Landlord and Tenant Act of
1927 in very important respects. That
Act was passed by Mr. Baldwin's Government, and yet we are told that here
in Northern Ireland we keep step by step
with English legislation. You are a long
way behind the band. You do not keep
step at all. You may waddle along in a
kind of goose step.
Then with regard to the second part of
the Motion, dealing with unemployment,
we seem to have no remedy whatever for
the great army of unemployed in Northern Ireland except the dole. We have
no relief schemes of a large and practical
character. I know there are certain
numbers of unemployed who are engaged
on relief schemes, but they are a small
percentage of the unemployed. They
are a mere drop in the ocean compared
with the vast armies for whom no relief
schemes exist. This is a problem which
and
requires statesmanship
requires
vision, but both these are qualities which
are lamentably lacking.
With regard to the third part of the
Motion, the administration of public
health in Northern Ireland, I said a week
ago and I do not desire to traverse the
same ground this week-that the administration of public health in Northern Ireland outside the City of Belfast
was unworthy of the Province and unfair
to the people. Take the poor-law medical
service inaugurated as far back as 1851.
During the intervening 80 years practically nothing has been done by the State
for the improvement of that service.
Censure Motion
Censure Motion
10 MARCH 1931
Public health is a matter of primary and
fundamental importance in almost every
self-governing community, but apparently
in our area it is a humdrum subject. We
have a long way to catch up on Great
Britain where public health is treated as
a matter of primary importance to the
people.
I submit that in this matter we should
have had long since a uniform system of
administration in connection with local
health services in our Province. Instead
of that we have at present an obsolete,
cumbersome, and over-lapping system.
We should have had a unified system on
a county basis, both in the interests of
economy and efficiency. These things
are talked about. These reforms
promised, but nothing has been done.
The main recommendations of the
Departmental Committee on Local Administration, which reported almost four
years ago, remain recommendations and
nothing else still.
are
If one contrasts the performances of
the Government with their promises, one
is reminded very much of a notorious politician whom, I think, Mr. Lincoln knew,
who walked strictly uprightly and perpendicularly on election day, but when
the elections were over his walk was
anything but perpendicular. It was
called in America "slantindicular."
Nothing of real value has been done by
the Government to heighten the business
efficiency of Northern Ireland; nothing
has been done to enable Ulster industry
to brave the economic storm. I beg to
move.
Mr. DORMAN: I regard this resolution as
a vote of no confidence in the
Government. The Government is very
fond of simple Bible teaching, and I would
take the unusual course of prefacing my
remarks with a quotation of simple Bible
teaching that I think the Government
would be well advised to consider and
meditate upon. It is the statement made
by the patriotic Prophet of Israel, who
said: :-
Let not the wise man glory in his wisdom, neither let the mighty man glory
in his might; let not the rich man glory
in his riches. But let him that glorieth
glory in this; that he understandeth
and knoweth me, that I am the Lord
which exercise loving kindness, judgment
and righteousness in the earth, for in
these things I delight, said the Lord.
I suggest to the right hon. Gentlemen, the
Leader of the House and Minister of Education, that that would be a very good
simple Bible motto to put up in the primary
schools of Northern Ireland. I believe
the Government deserve censure. On
numerous occasions instances of injustices
inflicted by one part of the community
upon another have been brought to the
attention of the Government, who have
promised to give consideration to the
various questions. Some three years ago
I drew attention to the utterances of a
gentleman who is probably best qualified
to give a true expression of opinion as to
the conditions of the people of Northern
Ireland, and one to whose views the Government would be well advised to pay
attention. I refer to the learned Recorder of Belfast. In reviewing the
proceedings in his Court during the past.
year he stated, a few weeks ago, that of
the numerous debtors who came before him
during that
he ascertained that their
year
average wages ranged from 25/- to 35/- a
week, and that the average order for payment he had made was 1/6 per week. I
wonder if the Government ever visualise
the condition of a community in which such
relations between creditor and debtor are
as revealed by the person who is best qualified to judge.
Some three or four years ago the learned
Recorder drew the attention of the Government to the injustice that was being
inflicted upon house-owners and tenants in
Belfast in connection with the cost of
ejectment proceedings. He pointed out
that cases which in pre-war days were adjudicated upon in a court of summary
jurisdiction now had to be brought before
him. The result was that the minimum
expenses in connection with ejectment
proceedings against a working-class tenant
was £3 16s Od, and if the case was defended the costs were nearer £7. That
means that if a working man who has been
unemployed and has got into arrears with
his rent is ejected from his home and is
sufficiently self-respecting to shoulder the
costs of his ejectment he would have to
pay double rent for a period of from
twelve to sixteen weeks after the ejectment
order was obtained. If the tenant defended the action, and the house owner
had to bear the costs of the ejectment he
would have to forego six months' rent of
the incoming tenant to recoup himself for
the loss he had sustained in getting the
tenant ejected from his home. The
learned Recorder drew attention to this
Censure Motion
Censure Motion
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
matter, and on that occasion the Leader
of the House stated that the matter would
be reported to the Minister of Home
Affairs, and that the Government would
give due consideration to the statement
that was made. Therefore, I think the
Government is worthy of censure inasmuch as it has neglected to give justice.
to the people in this particular matter of
the relationship of creditor and debtor.
There is another question that has been
brought to the notice of the Government,
and which the Government promised to
consider with a view to removing the
grievance. When the Government decontrolled the better class houses they were
warned that the result of their action
would be the further oppression of those
who occupied the cheaper houses. The
Government have helped the working
classes to a certain extent by continuing
the control of their houses, but they are
permitting a continuous injustice of a
most grievous and oppressive character to
be imposed upon men engaged in business
in the City of Belfast. I will not occupy
the time of the House by referring to the
many cases I could quote, especially as I
gave many instances in 1928, when the
Leader of the House promised that he
would convey my suggestion to the Minister of Home Affairs, and he added:-
.--
I should like to say that the Government are at present considering the
whole question of control and decontrol.
The
On that occasion I gave numerous instances where the rents of business
premises were jumped 100, 150, and 200
per cent. when the year expired. That
state of affairs is still going on, and I will
quote only one case which came under
my observation a few days ago.
controlled rent of a certain grocer was
£40 a year. During the last ten years
he has spent £152 in putting in a new
front to his shop, £18 in putting up
stairs, £22 in putting in a new floor,
£22 on the roof, and £30 in other reconstruction work. That is a total of
£260 that business man spent on his
premises during the past ten years.
Immediately his premises became decontrolled, and notwithstanding
his
having spent six-and-a-half years' rent
in improving the premises, the landlord
generously demanded an increase of rent
from £40 to £80. The result is that
that grocer will have to pass that additional rent on to his customers by
correspondingly inflating the price of all
the commodities he sells.
My memory goes back over the best
part of the unfortunate disturbances
that were associated with the agrarian
warfare in Ireland. The late Lord
Russell of Killowen, in this book called
"New Views of Ireland," gives statistics
of increases of rent by the landlords, and in no instance during that
long agrarian war was a landlord found
to have increased the rent of a holding
by 100 per cent. Therefore, I think the
Government deserves censure for not
having dealt with this matter and for not
having established a more just relationship
between landlord and tenant.
The Government is out on an economy
stunt. I find that out of a total income
assessable for income-tax of £15,000,000,
Members of the Government and its
officials in Northern Ireland are in
receipt of an income of £1,500,000 per
annum. Suppose the Members of the
Government relinquish 20 per cent of
their salaries, and the officials give up
20 per cent. of their salaries, and that that
reduction is passed on to the lower
paid officers of the Government and is
thereby spent in stimulating the trade of
the country, it would mean only
£3,000 a week being passed on for the
benefit of trade in our area. But if this
economy gesture is the prelude to an
appeal being made to the lower paid
workers, to the 250,000 insured workers
of Northern Ireland, to make a corresponding sacrifice of a penny per hour,
instead of reducing the consuming
capacity of the people to the extent of
£3,000 per week, it would immediately
reduce it by a sum of £50,000 per week.
How fallacious, then, is the effect of this
offer to sacrifice a certain percentage of
the salaries of the better-paid officers of
the Government.
Suppose the trader makes a 20 per
cent. profit on that £50,000, it would
mean a demand for an additional
£40,000 worth of goods from the wholesaler. If the wholesaler made a profit
of 10 per cent. it would mean an additional £35,000 worth of manufactures
from the producer of the commodity who,
in order to cope with the increased demand, would have to absorb some of the
vast army of individuals who are now
walking about the North of Ireland
begging all and sundry to give them the
Censure Motion
Censure Motion
10 MARCH 1931
right to work. I believe the Government is worthy of censure for not having
paid attention to these things, and, in
conclusion, I would again commend to
their serious consideration the piece of
simple Bible teaching with which I
began my remarks.
a
on
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
associate myself in a very few words
with the Motion that has been proposed
by Senator Campbell. Some years ago,
by a Motion in another place, I initiated
discussion the question of the
amendment of the law of landlord and
tenant. In the course of that discussion facts and figures were cited
which rather shocked all right-thinking
men. An agitation sprang up in some
parts of the country and, fearing that
it was going to develop to a larger extent,
the Government adopted the usual
scheme under such circumstances of
setting up a Committee to inquire into
the whole question. They do, no doubt,
fulfil the purpose the Government intended. They stop the outcry of the
people against these injustices. They
think when a Committee is sitting and
functioning that the Government intend
to put their recommendations into
operation and to use the services and the
work that Committee performed a year
ago.
Some time ago, the Prime Minister at
a social function forecasted the immediate
passing of legislation to ease the burden
and assuage the wrongs of leaseholders in
Northern Ireland. I was struck by his
remarks at that time. He stressed in
line after line of his speech the question
of the sanctity of contracts. The inference to be drawn from that was that
if that speech went down with the leaseholders of Northern Ireland he would be
justified in bringing in a Measure which
would be of no benefit to the short leaseholders of Northern Ireland.
Every right thinking man looks upon
free, genuine contracts as absolutely sacrosanct. In the case of the short leaseholders any honest man would say there
is nothing in the nature of a contract at
all. The tenant has no bargaining
powers. The landlord can impose his will
upon the tenant at any time he likes.
There is no question of the sanctity of
contract. If this question of the sanctity
of contract put an absolute bar on legislation of this description we would have
had no land laws in Northern Ireland; we
would have had no Rent Restriction Acts;
we would not have had the 1925 Land
Purchase Act, because prior to these there
were contracts of a freer kind than those
imposed on the short leaseholders of Northern Ireland by many landlords.
We all recognise that there are fair, just
and equitable landlords in Northern Ireland, but the people must be saved from
the extortionists. I think if one were to
pass through the towns of Northern Ireland you would see written in the houses,
buildings, state of repair and the state of
development the character of the landlord.
I think no one can justify the proposition
that if a man takes a vacant plot of ground
and spends three or four thousand pounds
on it in building a residence, or business
place, or factory, or whatever it may be,
on a lease of 61 years-there are many of
these building leases in Northern Ireland
at the present time at the end of that
period the landlord should be allowed to
come in and confiscate the whole work,
and take over the building without a
single penny of compensation to the man
who, perhaps, expended all his money and
the greater part of his life in developing
his business. I think that is a perfectly
indefensible proposition.
I know that in the City of Belfast the
necessity is not so vital, and for this
reason: You have many landlords in Belfast. They are competing with one
another. In the provincial towns of Northern Ireland, in nearly every instance,
one landlord controls the whole town, its
business sites, and its residential sites, and
has the state of development and general
outlook of the people in his hands.
With regard to the second part of the
Motion, I only want to say a word or two.
I do say if this great mass of unemployed
all these years had been doing some productive and constructive work we would
not have the unemployment fund in the
state of insolvency in which it is to-day.
Reference has been made by the hon.
Senator behind me to the married
woman who through want of means is
compelled to work. I do not know the
state of affairs in Belfast, but I do know
that in one provincial town within the
last ten days a wholesale attack has been
made upon these women. They have been
deprived of unemployment benefit wholesale. There is something radically wrong
there. If this money had been properly
employed that state of affairs could not
exist.
Censure Motion
Censure Motion
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
I mention one case to bring home to the
House this state of affairs. A woman
came to me who is the mother of two
children. Her husband is in America.
Her mother is an old-age pensioner.
Her brother is unemployed and receiving
no benefit. There are five of them
house.
living in one
She was the
one member earning when she was working. Her eldest child is six years old.
During these entire six years she worked
day and daily when work was available.
Her mother looked after the child. Factory work became short, and when she
went for unemployment benefit this week
she was told she could not get it because,
being a married woman, she was not available for work. Yet her mother looked
after the child for the last six years,
and
is prepared to look after it. This woman
came to me with tears in her eyes and
told me that all they had to live on was
the ten shillings a week, her mother's oldage pension. Surely there should be something done with the unemployment fund
to prevent a case of that sort occurring
in Northern Ireland.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
surprised to see this Resolution put forward by hon. Members. When one
considers the amount of beneficial legislation that has been passed by this
Government of ours, one comes to the
conclusion that there is very small gratitude to be expected from the Benches
that the Resolution comes from. It is
quite true that there is very much need
for the amendment of the law with
regard to landlord and tenant in regard
to town tenants. I understand the
Government intend to deal with it in the
course of time. But so much has been
made out of the unemployment question
I feel very much inclined to ask the hon.
Member-and this is not the first time
he has brought the Resolution forward
-could he not make some suggestion as to
how the unemployment trouble could be
dealt with. It is not in Belfast alone
or Northern Ireland. This is something
which is giving the whole world trouble.
Every Government in every country in
the world is, I think, almost as bad as
we are ourselves.
Surely it must be admitted that our
Northern Government has done remarkably well to deal with the unemployment problem in our own particular area.
There has been a considerable amount of
money spent on building houses for the
working classes to give employment to
the workers. The Government has given
a very liberal subsidy to encourage that.
There has been a considerable amount
of road making throughout Northern
Ireland. There has been a considerable
amount of money spent on that and
bridge building. It cannot be said that
the Government are careless regarding
this matter. Furthermore, it must be
admitted that the Loans Guarantee Acts
that were put into operation by our
Government and are kept still in
existence by the Northern Government
have done a considerable amount to bring
trade to the shipbuilding yards of Belfast. Furthermore, it went some little
extent to assist the artificial silk at
Ballymena. If the hon. Member would
think he could hardly blame the Government for being careless or indifferent.
Then this Government have not confined
themselves to efforts at home. They have
spent a considerable amount of money in
advertising the linen industry throughout America and Canada, all for the
purpose of trying to create employment
for the workers of Northern Ireland. I
do think Members appear to be very ungrateful if they do not recognise that a
little has been done in respect of unemployment. So far as the payment of
unemployment benefit is concerned, I
think I have as good knowledge of that
matter as any other Member of the
House. I am continuously attending
the Courts of Referees. It is being
given very sympathetically not only to
men but to married women. Indeed,
I must admit that a large number of
married women who are, I think, not
entitled to draw benefit have been getting
it up to the present. One cannot blame
the authorities or those who are responsible for unemployment benefit if
those who are not entitled to benefit are
put off as soon as possible, so that benefit is reserved for those better entitled
to it.
I do not think much more could be
done by the Government, and if that
motto of Mr. Dorman's were painted on
the gables at every street corner I do
not think it would help unemployment
in the least. If hon. Members who put
down the Motion would only make
suggestions of what the Government
could do more than they have done,
then we might press the Government to
carry them out if at all feasible.
Censure Motion
Censure Motion
10 MARCH 1931
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will take
the three parts of this Motion of Censure
in the order they are given on the Paper.
With regard to the question of the reform of the law of landlord and tenant in
towns, it is a complicated and comprehensive
subject, which was some little time ago
referred for inquiry and report to a Commission. The report of the Commission
has been the subject of careful investigation by the Government, but in view of
the complexity of the matter, the Government felt that it would be most improper
to attempt to rush legislation, until all
the various points at issue have been
carefully considered and cleared up. The
matter is still receiving careful consideration and it is hoped that it will be
possible in the near future to frame the
heads of the Bill dealing with the matter
for submission to Parliament, but of
course I cannot give any specific pledge as
to when the Government will be in a
position to do this, for the reasons already
given.
The next matter in the Motion is concerned with the utilisation of the labour,
or rather the failure to utilise the labour,
of our unemployed in development
schemes. I do not think that the Government can be accused of a lack of enterprise
in this direction within its limited means.
It has continued and proposes to continue
the policy of giving financial assistance to
local authorities and other public bodies
who initiate and accelerate works of public
utility in order to provide increased employment. Since Parliament was prorogued in November last, 27 such schemes
of which the total cost is estimated at
£146,000 have been approved. Moreover,
since that time several schemes which had
been approved at an earlier date have been
commenced, the most outstanding being
the L.M.S. Railway Loop Line, near
Greenisland, which is estimated to cost
£200,000; the Bangor Sewerage Scheme,
£100,000; Altnaheglish Dam, in connection with the new Londonderry Water
Supply Scheme, £176,000, and a water
and sewerage scheme for Moy, £11,000.
There are 2,300 men directly engaged on
the schemes at present being carried out.
As regards the question of the administration of public health services in Northern Ireland and the delay in introducing
the Bill dealing with local government reform, I may point out that while the Local
Government Commission submitted its
report some three or four years ago, that
report dealt with the whole subject of
"Local Government," including lunacy,
poor relief, public health, local government institutions, medical benefits,
financial control, etc. Following the
submission of the report, each element in
it was subjected to careful scrutiny, and
it appears that in certain respects at any
rate the recommendation made by the
Committee might involve additional expense not only to the rates, but also to the
State. Accordingly, in order to avoid
such an undesirable contingency, steps
were taken to see how, if at all, it would
be possible to give effect to the more important recommendations without involving additional expenditure either to the
ratepayer or the taxpayer.
In the meantime, however, the principle
of derating has been introduced and
established in our area, and this principle,
involving as it does such a burden on the
Government in connection with local expenditure, altered the whole circumstances
of
local government administration.
Practically simultaneously the principle of
medical benefits was also introduced, which
affected to a marked degree the subject of dispensary medical relief, thus
rendering it necessary to review the Commission's reconmendations in the light of
the new circumstances.
At the same time the question of finance
has, during the past couple of years,
assumed still greater dimensions, and, as
the hon. and learned Member is aware, it
has been found necessary by my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, to set up
a
Committee to investigate the whole
question of the financial relations between
the Government and the local authorities
in connection with local government expenditure. Until this matter has been
cleared up, it is impossible for the Government to consider the question of
bringing before Parliament any comprehensive proposals for local government
reform, but it is to be remembered that
the Government has taken up one aspect
of the Local Government Commission's
Report, namely, that dealing with lunacy
reform, and that a Bill will shortly be
introduced to the House dealing with this
matter.
In the meantime, however, every
possible step is being taken, under
existing laws, for the improvement of
Public Health Administration in Nor-
Adjournment-
Business of the House.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
thern Ireland. I hope I have shown
to the House that the Government
have taken all the steps to deal with the
present unhappy condition of affairs that
it is possible for any Government with
limited powers to undertake.
Question put, "That this Motion be
agreed to.
The House divided: Contents 4, Not
Contents 16.
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Barclay, Mr. W.
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dorman, Mr. R.
