Dickson, Mr. J. Hill
Dixon, Sir Thomas
Glendinning, Mr. J. C.
Leslie, Mr. J. G.
Charlemont, The Viscount
Clark, Sir George S.
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Cunningham, Rt. Hon. Samuel
Adjournment.
Long, Mr. J. A.
McCorkell, Mr. D. E. B.
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday next." On that day
there will be some business in connection
with Divorce Bills which it would be a
hardship to postpone until the following
week. I would like to take the Committee stage of the Children (Amendment) Bill on that day also, but as we
have taken the Second Reading only
to-day I am not sure that hon. Members
might not think it was too soon to embark upon the Committee stage on
Thursday.
Mr. LESLIE: As regards taking the
Committee stage of the Children Bill on
Thursday, I shall myself have a few
Amendments to direct attention to, but
McCullagh, Sir Crawford
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
Sinclair, Col. T.
they are of such a trival character that I
do not think there would be any objection to taking the Committee stage of
the Bill on Thursday, especially having
regard to the fact that those in charge
of the Bill have been advised of the
character of my Amendments.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think the feeling
of the House would be to pass the Bill as
speedily as possible.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Then we
will take the Committee stage of the Bill
on Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes before
Five o'clock until Thursday next.
Papers Presented.
Oral Answer
12 MARCH 1931
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 12th March, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs :
Report on the Administration
Local Government Services, 1929-30.
Ministry of Commerce:
of
Annual Report of the Registrar of
Friendly Societies for the year ended
31st December, 1930, Part A.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
The Incorporated Belfast Maternity
Hospital and the Royal Victoria
Hospital Bill.
Donaghadee (County Down) Water
and Improvement Bill.
Londonderry Corporation Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons and read a First time.
Solomon's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second Time."
McCleane's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second Time.”
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT
(Leader
of the House): I beg to move, "That
Standing Order No. XXV. be suspended
for this day's sitting for the purpose
of taking the remaining stages of the
Children (Amendment) Bill."
From the time-table point of view the
suspension of the Standing Order is unnecessary, because we have really got
plenty of time. On the other hand, I
personally would like to have Part 1 of
the Bill in operation as soon as possible.
However, I do not want to press the
Motion on the House if any hon. Member has any objection to it.
Mr. LESLIE: I venture to suggest,
following a conversation a few evenings
ago, that it would be better for the
House to accept the Motion, because I
think we are all very much in accord
with the desirability of the immediate
introduction of what the Noble Viscount
called Part 1 of this Bill. As regards
Part 2, the day nursing business, about
which questions may perhaps arise in so
far as it affects country districts, it
seems better that the Bill should leave
us and go to another place as speedily
as possible in order that those more
directly representative of the people
concerned should have an opportunity
of expressing their views.
Mr. McALLISTER: I would like to
say, in case this should be taken as a
precedent-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No, no.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
ELEMENTARY SCHOOL BOOKS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Dorman:
To ask the Leader of the House what
proportion of the books supplied to the
children in the Public Elementary
Schools are printed and published in
Northern Ireland.
Mr. DORMAN: In asking this question, I should like to thank the noble
Viscount for supplying me with an answer
to the question I put on Tuesday last.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As far as
my Ministry is aware, there is only one
Northern Ireland firm which publishes to
any considerable extent books for use in
Public Elementary Schools. The Ministry
has no information as to the arrangements
of this firm in regard to printing.
It is not possible to give any estimate
of the proportion of the total number of
school books used by the children in the
schools throughout the area which are the
publications of this local company. The
Ministry exercises a general supervision
over books for reading, history, citizenship, economics, and Irish which are used
in the schools with a view to securing that
no books which are unsuitable for anv
reason are permitted, and lists of such
text-books as are approved are supplied to
the schools. It is open to the school
authorities to select books issued by any
publisher which appear on these approved
lists.
Committee

Children Bill-
THE SENATE
Children (Amendment) Bill [S.]
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved, "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Sanction required by and
notice to be given by persons receiving
children for reward).
*
*
*
(4) The notice to be given under subsections (1), (2) and (3) of this section
shall state-
(a) the name, sex, and date and
place of birth of the child;
(b) the names of the parents and,
if they are living, their addresses;
(c) If the parents are dead, the
date and place of death;
(d) the dwelling within which the
child is to be kept;
and in addition, the notice required by
sub-section (1) shall state the name and
address of the person from whom the
child is to be received; the notice required by sub-section (2) shall state the
name and address of the person by whom
the child is to be received; and the
notice required by sub-section (3) shall
state the name and address of the person from whom such child was received.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 1, page 2, line 23, after
the word "death" ("the date and place of
death") to insert the words, "if known.'
This Amendment is intended to meet
to a certain extent the difficulty hinted
at in Senator Leslie's suggested amendment to Clause 1, that is, that in some
cases a person may not be in a position
to give the information asked for in the
notices. It is not desirable to leave any
opening for evading the requirements of
the Bill, which would be the case if any
general proviso were inserted limiting the
particulars to be furnished to such as
were within the knowledge of the persons
required to furnish the notices.
It does not appear to be any hardship
to require a person proposing to accept a
child to make the inquiries necessary to
ascertain the information asked for in
Clause 4 of the Bill, but it may be that
the particulars relating to the death of the
parents might not be easy to obtain, and,
accordingly, I move the Amendment
standing in may name.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
noble Viscount for looking into this
matter. I am inclined to think if the
Committee
the
accepts
Amendment
there will be no necessity for me to
move the next Amendment on the
Paper which stands in my name. There
is still some slight apparent difficulty as
to the names of the parents and, if they
are living, their addresses. It is going
just a little bit further than the
original Act of 1908 in requiring full
information, and it might be there will
be the same difficulty as regards the
address of the parents as about the date
and place of death of such parents.
However, I am inclined to think the
words proposed to be inserted are
sufficient to indicate to the local
authority the extent to which they will
be required to pursue their inquiries,
and I think the matter might be left
there. We do not want to do anything that will weaken what we intend
to strengthen, and for that reason, I
think, we should be safe in leaving the
matter as has been suggested. At the
same time, if at any later stage it should
appear on further consideration that a
little further change might be made there
will be plenty of opportunity for doing it.
I shall support the Amendment, and I
do not propose to move the next Amendment on the Paper in my name.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
Page 2, line 33, at end insert, "Provided that where any fact required
under this Sub-section to be stated in any
Notice is not within the knowledge of the
person giving the notice, it shall be
sufficient for such person to state in such
notice that such fact is not within his
knowledge."
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Amendment of Part 1 of
8 Edw. 7, c. 67).
Children Bill-
Committee
12 MARCH 1931
For the words "infant" and "infants," where these words occur in the
provisions of Part I of the principal
Act, there shall be substituted the
words "child" and "children" respectively, and the words "under the
age of seven years" in sub-section (6)
of section two and in section four of
the principal Act are hereby repealed.
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 2, page 3, line 34, to leave out the
word "these" (these words occur), and to
insert instead thereof the word
"those.'
This is simply a grammatical Amendment.
Viscount CHARI,EMONT: As Minister
of Education I am always pleased to accept
any thing that is a grammatical improvement on a Bill as drafted. Therefore, I
have pleasure in accepting the Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Does the noble Lord,
as Minister of Education, say that the
word "these" is ungrammatical ?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I did not
suggest that.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 3 (Saving for certain
policies of life insurance), and 4 (Restriction upon undertakings of day
nursing of children), ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Appointment of visitors
and inspection of premises).
*
*
*
*
If in the district of any local
authority, any persons are found who
undertake the day nursing of children,
the local authority shall appoint one or
more persons of either sex to be child
protection visitors, whose duty it shall
be to visit any premises in which day
nursing is carried on in order to satisfy
themselves as to the proper care and
attention of the children and the suitability of the premises in which they
are kept.
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
line 33, after the word "visit" (to visit any
premises) to insert the words "from time
to time." The words I propose to insert
do occur in a Section of the principal Act
of 1908, although of course they do not
there relate to day nursing, because there
was none in that Act. I think it would
be better to put the words in because it
serves as an indication to the local
authority and the child protection visitors
that they are expected to make their visits
effective, and that, therefore, they should
repeat them and not be content with one
possibly perfunctory visit and then let the
matter rest. I think we are sufficiently
justified by the precedent to which I have
referred to insert these words, although
I must admit they are not absolutely
essential from the legal point of view.
I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
5, after Sub-section (2), to insert as a new
Sub-Section:
or
"(3) If any person undertaking the
day nursing of any child or children refuses to allow any such visitor
authorised person to visit or examine
the children or the premises in which
they are kept, such person shall be
guilty of an offence under Part 1 of the
principal Act."
This is a somewhat different matter.
Again in the principal Act, in regard to
the particular subject of examining a
child put out to nurse, a sub-clause
similar to the sub-clause I am suggesting
does occur. That is, it is made an offence
for any person in charge of a home, whatever class of a home it may be, to refuse
to allow an authorised person to come
into the premises to examine a child. It
seems to me that it is perhaps desirable
that we should give the same power under
the day nursing part of the Bill as we
give in the other and more material part
of the Measure. I am aware that the
noble Viscount has another Amendment
on the Paper which it might be thought
will meet the case in a rather different
way.
However, the question seems to me to
be of some importance, since the ill-treatment of a child might very well take place
and cannot be prevented if the person
visiting were denied access at such time
as for some reason or other he desired to
examine the child. I am inclined to move
if only to get an explanation as to the
view the Government entertain, and as
to how far the proposal is really practicable.
Children Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is not
thought necessary to make it an offence
to refuse to allow inspection, as the refusal
or withdrawal of a permit to keep children
would meet the case. As, however, there
is some doubt in the Bill, as drafted,
whether the local authority would have
power to withdraw a licence
on
this
ground, I am proposing to move an
Amendment in Clause 7 (2), making the
refusal to allow inspection a reason for
withdrawing a licence, in addition to the
other grounds therein set out. Therefore I cannot accept this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am inclined to think
that, having regard to the extreme improbability of any serious difficulty arising, the Amendment suggested by the
noble Viscount will meet the case. I
therefore ask leave to withdraw my
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
5, page 5, to leave out Sub-Section (4).
I am not sure that this Amendment is, in
the circumstances, of great importance
either. It deals with a matter I mentioned on the Second Reading. The point
was that if a local authority does exempt
from being visited any particular premises
it seems to me that that party will have
a power of control that seems to me to be
unassailable from the theoretical point of
view. But from the practical point of
view we have got to consider that we are
now only dealing with day nursing; that
is, the temporary attendance of children
while their parents are employed, and we
are presumably only dealing with children
of school age, who for some reason or other
are not at school. Whether it is really
necessary that the local authority should
be restricted or not seems to be a point
on which opinions might very reasonably
differ, but on the face of things I am inclined to say that a local authority if given
power to require visiting, ought to stick
to it, and they should not be allowed to
escape their responsibilities in a general
way if for any reason they do not think
it necessary to inspect particular premises.
It seems to me that all that is required
could be done by instructions to child protection visitors, but I do not like the
suggestion that the power of the local
authority should be permanently parted
with. Accordingly, I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is considered desirable that the local authority
should have a certain amount of discretion
in carrying out their duties of inspection.
There will be cases of well conducted
premises, such as charitable institutions,
run by responsible persons who are not
exempt under Clause 8 of the Bill, but
who will not require close supervision,
and where it would not be desirable that
the supervision of the local authority
should be too obtrusive. I am afraid,
therefore, I am unable to accept this
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the noble
Viscount has shown us the way out. He
has indicated the kind of premises it is
proposed to exempt, and in that sense,
and having regard to the rules given to
the local authority, I do not think there
is any occasion to persist in the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Local authority may fix
number of Children which may be kept),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Withdrawal of sanction in
certain cases).
or
(2) If at any time day nursing is
carried on by any person who, by
reason of negligence, ignorance, inebriety, immorality, criminal conduct,
other similar cause, is, in the
opinion of the local authority, unfit to
day nursing, the local
authority may withdraw any sanction
granted to that person to carry on day
nursing.
carry on
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
7, page 5, to leave out from "who" in
line 24 to "is" in line 26 (by reason of
negligence, ignorance, inebriety, immorality, criminal conduct, or other
similar cause). Here again is another
apparent difficulty of a small character.
None of these matters are, I think, of
first importance. The text before us, as
I am sure hon. Members will recognise,
is simply a reproduction of paragraph (b)
of Sub-section (1) of Section 5 of the
principal Act, and that does not apply to
day nursing. It will be noticed that the
reasons we give as guides to a local
authority withdrawing their sanction are
negligence, ignorance, and so on, but we
do not specify either an accident to or
Children Bill-
Committee
12 MARCH 1931
an illness of the person who receives the
sanction. I am bound to say that I do
not quite see with which of the things
specified the other causes are supposed to
be similar. That is an expression we
borrow from the earlier Act and for which
Northern Ireland has no particular responsibility.
But it does occur to me as regards this
withdrawal of sanction that we ought to
leave the local authority in the same
position as they were in regarding the
original grant of the licence. It must be
presumed that the local authority would
satisfy itself before giving any sanction
that there was no existing negligence,
ignorance, immorality, and so on, but I
do not see why they should be restricted
further as regards withdrawing sanction,
because if a local authority does withdraw
its sanction it can always re-issue such
sanction on good cause being shown.
Therefore, I am inclined to think it
might be better in practice to leave the
authority unrestricted, and that would be
the effect of omitting the words I am moving
to leave out. It is really not a matter of
the greatest importance, although I will
move the Amendment.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The diffi-
Bills
culty in
local
dealing with
authorities is to allow them sufficient
discretion and yet not too much.
effect of this Amendment would be to
leave the withdrawal of a permit entirely
to the discretion of the local authority.
It would seem, however, better that the
authority should be required to state
some ground for withdrawing a permit,
and the grounds set out appear to cover
very fully every case in which a permit
be
ought to
these
withdrawn. For
reasons I am unable to accept this
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think the
Amendment ought to be pressed.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
after
move, in Clause 7, page 5, line 27,
the word "nursing" (carry on day
nursing), to insert:-
Or if any person by whom day
nursing is carried on refuses to allow
any child protection visitor or other
duly authorised person to visit the premises for the purpose of exercising the
powers of a child protection visitor
under this Act.
The object of this Amendment is to
secure that the local authority shall have
the right to withdraw a permit for the
day nursing of children if the person by
whom the day nursing is carried on refuses to allow inspection. It will be seen
that certain grounds are specified in
Clause 7 which entitle the local authority
to withdraw a permit, and unless refusal
to inspect is specifically included as one
of these grounds they would apparently
be unable to withdraw a permit on this
ground. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Exemptions).
(1) The provisions of this Act with
respect to day nursing shall not extend
to any relative of a child who undertakes
the day nursing of the child, or to
institutions established for the protection and care of children and conducted
in good faith for religious or charitable
purposes, or to any school.
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
8, page 5, line 35, at end of Sub-Section
(1) to insert:
:-
"Provided that any number fixed
under the provisions of Section Six of
this Act shall not be exceeded by virtue
of this Section."
At
I have to confess to the Committee that
I am not in the least sure that the Amendment I have put on the Paper is really
required to meet any difficulty that is
likely to arise. The difficulty is, as the
Committee will see, that we have given
a local authority a definite power under
Clause 6 to fix the number of children to
be kept in any premises. That is
obviously a necessary power, and the local
authority presumably will fix that number. It may be up to twenty, but it is
much more likely to be six or seven.
all events, there is power to fix the number. Then in Clause 8 we go on to make
a very desirable and necessary provision
that the Clauses regarding day nursing
shall not be held to cover relatives, but
there is a possible difficulty in the production of an unreasonable number of
relatives' children. That is to say, there
might be an unreasonable number of
grandchildren, neices, and nephews taken
into premises, and so it might happen that
your number, whether it be twenty or
thirty or only six or seven, may be rather
seriously exceeded.
B
Children Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
! Mr. Leslie]
Exactly how it would be proposed that
the local authority should meet that case
unless we put some such words as
these in the Bill I am not altogether clear.
We do not want a Bill that will make a
nuisance of itself, but we do want a Bill
that will give us what we require, namely,
the protection and supervision of the
children concerned. The houses used for
this purpose are likely to be very small,
and the people using them are likely to be
in humble circumstances, and the number
might be exceeded for the reason I have
mentioned. Therefore, if only to obtain
the considered view of the Government,
I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I understand that the object of this Amendment
is to provide for a case where the local
authority, in accordance with Clause 6
of the Bill, have fixed the number of
children who may be kept on any premises,
and where the person occupying the
premises brings any other children related
to him within the meaning of Clause 8 (2)
and therefore exempt from supervision. I
may point out, however, that in such a
case it is open to the local authority to
revise the number originally fixed, having
regard to the altered circumstances. The
Amendment, therefore, does not appear to
be necessary.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I think that is
the answer. Let the local authority in
such cases review the number. The local
authority must take some interest in the
matter. I will not press the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Short title, construction,
and citation) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
PREAMBLE and TITLE agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported with
Amendments to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendments.
Moved, "That this Report be now received." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving,
"That this Bill be now read the Third
time," I should like to give an explanation
on certain matters that were raised on the
Second Reading. As regards expenses
under this Bill, it is not considered necessary to insert any Clause providing for
the expenses of the local authority. The
Bill is to be construed as one with Part 1
of the Act of 1908, and Section 10 of that
Act provided for the expenses of the local
authority.
Senator Dorman raised a matter which
I should have explained when Clause 1
was before the Committee, but, unfortunately, I forgot. He said, I think,
that a case of emergency might arise
where it would not be possible for a person who was undertaking the nursing of
a child to give the 48 hours' notice required under Sub-section (1) (a) of this
Clause and to obtain the necessary
sanction of the local authority. For example, the mother of a child might die
in child-birth and the father might have
no relative to whom he could entrust the
infant. He would, accordingly, be
obliged to get someone who was not a
relative, and so not covered by the exemption provided later, in the Bill, to
undertake immediately to look after the
baby and would naturally remunerate
them for doing so. I quite appreciate
this point, but it is hardly possible to
draft a proviso which would not leave too
wide an opening for the evasion of the
Act. If hard or exceptional cases arise
it seems best to leave them to the good
sense of the local authority.
I have just been asked what will be the
powers of child protection visitors. They
will have power to visit a child in its
home, but they will not have power to
act. They can only report to the local
authority which will take the necessary
action.
Mr. DORMAN: I should like to
suggest to the noble Viscount that these
child protection visitors should have regard to the cubic space of the dwellings
in which children are placed. Unfortunately, there is a good deal of congestion in the houses of the working
classes in Belfast, especially in the
kitchen type of house, and these visitors
should be instructed to see that the premises contain sufficient cubic space for the
children.
Adjournment
Children Bill-

12 MARCH 1931
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before we part
with this Measure may I
satisfacexpress
tion at the clear phraseology of the Bill,
and its freedom from legislation by
reference.
Mr. LESLIE: I take due note of the
intimation given us by the noble
Viscount to the effect that the expenses
incurred, if any, will fall upon the common fund of the local authority, and that
we have given that power by implication.
I pass from that to the question of the
congestion to which Senator Dorman has
just referred. I think I may take it that
the intention of inserting paragraph (d)
in one of the earlier sub-clauses of the
first Clause, referring to a dwelling in
which a child is to be kept, is to be construed as a direct intimation to the local
authority that they shall satisfy themselves in those matters before they give
the required sanction. That is as I
understand it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The premises must be suitable.
Mr. LESLIE: Exactly. Now I come to
the question of day nursing, on which I
spoke for a second or two on the Second
Reading, and its application to the country
districts. If we could find a rural union
which consisted entirely of rurality, I
suggest to the House that we should have
made the second part of this Bill entirely
adoptive on the part of local unions, but
I do not think there is such a union in
Northern Ireland. I cannot think of any
union which does not include one or more
urban districts, or small towns approaching that status. But I do think there is
some risk of our putting too strict regulations in force in a purely rural district,
where the practice is for people who work
in a mill to leave perhaps one or two
children in someone else's house, whether
for payment or not, and, if for payment,
for a very small payment. I think there
is some risk of our being unduly stringent
in that case. It does seem to me that if
any case can be made for an alteration,
as I have indicated, by making the provisions of the day nursing part of the Bill
adoptive rather than mandatory, that
can better be suggested elsewhere where
there are a large number of representatives
from such localities as those I have mentioned. I think it is quite possible that
we may be asked to make our rules just a
little less stringent, particularly in the
country, than we have thought it
necessary to do in this House
I should like to say a word or two as
regards its effect, or lack of effect, on the
question of adoption. It will be remembered that since the Local Government Commission's Report dealt with these matters
we have passed an Adoption of Children
Act, and there is some likelihood of misapprehension in the country as to how
far the provisions of this Bill will affect the
provision of the Adoption of Children Bill.
I need scarcely say that the broad
difference between the two Bills is that
we are now dealing here with children put
out for nursing and maintenance for reward, as opposed to the permanent adoption of children. That is a point of view
to which I should rather like the attention
of local authorities directed, not so much
for their own information as for the information of those who come under their
notice in connection with matters of that
kind. I believe the Bill as a whole will
be found to be of great use, although I
shall not be surprised if we are asked to
make some slight modification on the lines
I have mentioned.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read the Third time) agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass.'
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to carry down the said Bill and
desire their concurrence thereto.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes before Four o'clock,
until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint.
Oral Answers
Government and Agriculture
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 25th March, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Education:
Salaries and Capitation Grants (Public)
Elementary Schools) Interim Regulations,
1931.
Building and Improvement Grants
(Voluntary Schools) Regulations, 1930,
Amending Regulations No. 1, 1931.
Ministry of Finance:
Schedule of Ministry of Agriculture
documents, the destruction of which is
approved by the Inspecting Officers
appointed under Rule 2 of the Schedule
appended to Council Order, Northern
Ireland, 20th January, 1925.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Marketing of Dairy Produce (Surprise Inspections of Butter) Rules (Northern Ireland), 1931.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Question to Minister.
WOODEN STRUCTURES ON COAST
ROADS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the Government will
take steps to preserve the scenic beauty
of the coasts by securing more effective
public control over the building of
wooden structures along leading coast
roads.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The answer
to the question of the hon. and learned
Member is in the affirmative. As he is
doubtless aware, local authorities have
power to make building by-laws, although
I am sorry to say that all the authorities
have not availed themselves of these
powers. Under building by-laws certain
standards are prescribed which would
prevent the erection of the great majority
of the wooden bungalows which have
sprung up in the last few years.
In 1919
it was felt necessary, owing to the acute
shortage of accommodation, to enable
local authorities to relax their building
by-laws temporarily, and this provision
was continued from year to year in the
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill until
last year.
The hon. and learned Member will, perhaps, recollect that this matter
was raised in this House on two or three
occasions.
In the Town Planning Bill, introduced
at the end of last session in another place,
and shortly to be re-introduced, provision
was included making it compulsory for
local sanitary authorities to promulgate
building by-laws, and there was also contained in the first part of that Bill, dealing with planning, a number of provisions which would enable local authorities to deal effectively with the matter
referred to in the question.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that, may
I inquire whether it is the intention of
the Government to limit itself to dealing
with towns under the Town Planning
Bill. I rather thought the noble Viscount
would like to explain that, and tell us
that it will apply both to the town and
the country.
Viscount CHARLEMONT': I think I
should like to give a more comprehensive
answer than "yes" or "not" to that
question, and, therefore, I will ask for a
little more notice.
Government and Agriculture.
Mr. McALLISTER: I beg to move:-
That this House regrets that no comprehensive steps have been taken by the
Government to restore the farming industry of Northern Ireland to its former
prosperity.
In moving this Motion I ask the House
to accept my assurance that I have put it
on the Paper in order to centre attention
on what I believe to be undoubtedly the
most vital question to the welfare and
security of the people of Northern Ireland. By every test the farming industry
is the most important industry in Northern Ireland. In the first place it circulates the most money. In the second
place a greater number of people are
employed in it than in any other industry,
and as the Government's chief interest is
centred in the social and business welfarc
73 Government and Agriculture

25 MARCH 1931
Government and Agriculture
of Belfast I venture to suggest to the
House that this industry has not received
the care and attention it is undoubtedly
entitled to by reason of its importance.
I go so far as to say that the very existence of the Six Counties rests upon the
success of the farming industry. I feel
that in the past this question has been
approached from an entirely wrong angle
by the Ministry of Agriculture and the
Government. The one hope for the redemption of farmers in this Province is to
re-organise and revolutionise the whole
system of the marketing of their produce.
It is an impossible position when the
entire farming community are not in competition with the purchasers, but are in
competition with one another to sell to
the skilled, well-trained, and experienced
purchasers in that market. I suggest
that the only remedy is for proper methods of organisation to be brought into
being by the assistance of the Government, who should create machinery for the
sale and marketing of agricultural produce on collective lines, and for purchase
upon the same lines. The present system
creates the greatest number and the most
varied markets for the purchaser, and the
individual farmer has no facilities for
realising the real position of the markets.
The skilled and experienced buyer has all
the facilities, all the trade records, and all
the material necessary to guide him when
he into the markets, and 1 do suggest
goes
that it is only on this principle that the
farming industry of Northern Ireland can
work out an economic existence.
It seems to me that there are only two
methods by which the present system can
be changed. One is compulsion by legislation, and the other is education.
I do
not intend to advocate compulsion at the
present time, although I noticed that
some time ago the Prime Minister of
England stated definitely and emphatically that if compulsion were necessary he
was prepared to introduce a compulsory
Measure to organise the farmers of England in regard to collective marketing and
purchasing. I do say-and I put this
forward as my own humble opinion,
gained from interviews with many practical and experienced farmers-that the
only hope for agriculture is a system of
education and agitation amongst the
farmers themselves, organised by the
Government upon the most intensive
lines possible, whereby they may be taught
to see the fallacy of the present system and
the advantages to be obtained by the
system in vogue in Denmark and other
countries.
Fifty years ago Denmark was in a
worse position than Northern Ireland is
to-day, but they revolutionised, amongst
other things, their system of marketing,
and, to-day, they are probably the most
progressive agricultural community in the
world. They have no facilities or advantages that we here do not possess.
We
have an equally good climate and a better
soil, and I think we have farmers capable
of carrying out all that is required of
them if they are well and efficiently
guided. I think it is a very damning fact
that in a purely agricultural community
the children who will in the future pursue
an agricultural career are taught absolutely nothing either of the theory or
practise of farming. I do suggest that at
at an early age these children in the rural
districts should be taught the theory, and
on small plots of ground the practice of
farming, so that they may be given an
interest in their future avocation. I also
think the system could be extended so as
to educate and interest these children in
the whole question of co-operative buying
and selling. That, I think, would be one
of the surest ways of improving agriculture in our Province.
Again, I think that something should
be done to encourage the
and more
young
progressive amongst our farming community, whereby those young farmers who
prove themselves to be promising and
ambitious should be afforded an opportunity of studying advantageous methods.
of farming in Denmark and other countries, and, on their return, give the young
farmers of Northern Ireland the result of
their inquiries and the benefit of their
experience. Thus our farmers could be
induced to apply to farming here those
methods which had proved so beneficial to
other agricultural communities.
present system is very extravagant from
the producers point of view. Take, for
instance, the question of linen. There is
one thing I never could understand, and
that is how the linen industry could stand
so many profits and pass through so many
hands from the time the seed was sown
until the linen reached the purchaser
through the shops. I have calculated
that it passes through no fewer than ten
hands in the course of its manufacture
and sale.
The
Potatoes are sold by the farmers at 2/-,
and they are purchased at 9/-. I do not
Government and Agriculture
Government and Agriculture
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
consumer.
say there is any extravagant profiteering,
but I do say that under the present system
the producer cannot afford to allow his
produce to pass through the hands of four
or five individuals before it reaches the
Such a system of marketing
could not possibly be economic. At a
time when less land than ever is under
tillage and prices are falling, it should be
our earnest duty to concentrate all our
resources upon trying to restore and reestablish the most important industry in
Northern Ireland. I do not think the
most sincere friend of the Government
could say that since its establishment the
Government has passed any legislation
which has had any real or far-reaching
benefits to the farming industry in Northern Ireland. In the present state of the
industry something very substantial would
have to be done to revive the industry and
make its future secure.
With regard to the important question.
of transport, surely it is a damning fact
that produce can be brought from America
to the markets in England more cheaply
than it can be taken from the markets in
Northern Ireland to England.
There are
I
sufficient farmers in Northern Ireland,
provided they are properly organised, to
prevent such a state of affairs as that
continuing. There is sufficient produce
to be sent across the water, and there are
enough feeding stuffs and artificial
manures to be brought back home, to
justify the farmers providing their own
transport if the present transport companies cannot carry their produce at fair
and economic rates. I suppose that in his
reply the Leader of the House will refer
to the alleged benefits of derating.
will anticipate it by saying that the
Derating Act was one of the greatest
failures in the whole legislative programme
of the Government. Undoubtedly the
large farmers, who represent only a small
percentage of the agricultural community,
have obtained some benefit from the Act,
but to the small farmers it has been of
absolutely no benefit at all. The pound
or two the small farmer has received he
has paid away in the increased cost of
transport.
Derating has confused the whole financial arrangements of the local authorities,
and it has prevented them carrying into
effect projects and enterprises which they
had in view. Instead of being of any
advantage to the agricultural community
derating has proved to be one of the most
retrograde steps the Government have
ever taken, owing to the manner in which
it has tied the hands of local authorities in
the matter of carrying out administrative
reforms. I do not wish to detain the
House unduly, but I hope Members will
give me credit for having by this Resolution made a bona-fide effort, free from any
party bias, to try to induce the Government to make a concerted effort to
emancipate the greatest in number, and,
perhaps, the most deserving people engaged in the oldest industry of Northern
Ireland.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I am in agreement with a great many things the previous speaker has said. He referred to
the fact that agriculture was the largest
industry in this Province, and to the necessity for co-operation on the part of
farmers. We all agree with those two
statements. The only question is whether
it is the duty of the Government to form
co-operative societies. We must remember that the Ministry of Agriculture is
not a trading society. As has been pointed
out, there is co-operative trading in Denmark, but there are not the same political
difficulties in Denmark as we here have to
contend with. The children of Denmark
are not educated in two different classes
of schools, and they have not the same
difficulty of bringing the people together.
I would like to say to Senator McAllister
that I do not think there is in any country
a Ministry of Agriculture that has done
so much for agriculture as has the
Ministry of Agriculture in Northern Ireland. I would invite him to come to the
Royal Ulster Show at Balmoral, at the
end of May, where he will see the Ministry's exhibit. There the results of years
of experiments in agriculture are placed
before the people, but the unfortunate
thing is that so few of our farmers take
advantage of that research work which is
placed at their disposal.
The best class of horses in the world are
also placed at their disposal for service,
and yet we find them sending unsound
mares to these horses. They also have at
their service the best class bulls, sheep,
and pigs, and the increase in the milk of
our cows has been very remarkable.
agree with the hon. Member's remarks as
to the difficulties of transport, and the
difference in the price the producer gets
and the consumer pays. The Ministry
may show us the best way to grow potatoes
I
Government and Agriculture
Government and Agriculture
25 MARCH 1931
and produce eggs, but they cannot regulate the prices. That can only be
done, as Senator McAllister has suggested,
by a system of co-operation, but I am
afraid that that is not the job of the
Government.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I hope the
House will allow me to intervene in this
debate for a few moments. I think the
mover of the Motion should have done
something more than attack the present
Government. He should have gone back
to the real root of the evil, which is the
system of taxation under which we live.
Think what used to happen in the old
days before such taxes as estate duty and
surtax were put on. Last year a sum of
£200,000,000 was taken in surtax and
death duties. In the old days a large
portion of that went back into agriculture
and industry, but now that huge sum is
entirely used by the present Government
as a means of income and not capital. If
you deprive agriculture of any possible
capital, of course you are taking away
that on which agriculture stands.
The
fact that you put capital into a farm
means that you can make money, because
you can buy cattle, sheep, and fertilisers;
but if you have no capital how can you
put money into a farm. The primary
curse of agriculture is the system under
which we live, and until we get a Government in office which will re-organise the
system of taxation agriculture will continue to suffer.
CO-
Members have talked about
operation. I am a great believer in cooperation if it can be brought about, but
what is really bringing down the price of
agricultural produce in this country is
the fact that other countries have cooperated and rationalised their industries.
I do not think there is any chance of the
price of our produce rising, because
Canadian and other farmers are producing at a cheaper rate than we can. There
is only one cure, and that is for the
Government to take power to stop the
dumping of cheap produce into this
country. Unfortunately we in this
Province have no power to do that.
Before we can put a tariff on any class of
agricultural produce we have got to take
into consideration the voting power in
Great Britain. The voting power in
Great Britain is about 90 per cent. urban,
and, therefore, you will have this position.
The urban voter will say to himself, "I
want my food cheap, and I am not going
to put a tax on agricultural produce that
will make my food dearer.". "He does not
care a twopenny damn for the farmer.
I agree with Senator Hill Dickson that
the present Government have done a very
great deal for agriculture. They have,
undoubtedly, introduced into the farming
community the idea of co-operation,
through the medium of the various Marketing Acts they have passed. Therefore,
I consider the Government have done a
great deal for the farming industry of
Northern Ireland, and I shall vote against
the Motion.
We
Mr. CAMPBELL: If I may adopt a
phrase of the last speaker, judging by the
tenor of the speeches I doubt if the Government care a twopenny damn about
agriculture in Northern Ireland.
have had a panegyric of the Ministry of
Agriculture, but by its fruits ye shall
know it. Where are the fruits of the
There is not
Ministry of Agriculture?
the fields of
a ripple of prosperity over
Northern Ireland.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Or anywhere
else.
Mr. CAMPBELL: "Sufficient unto the
day is the evil thereof." It is poor consolation to a Northern Ireland farmer to
know that the Russian farmer is as badly
off as he himself is. It does not put one
penny more into his pocket. I venture to
suggest that Senator McAllister made
a very temperate speech. It contained a
number of very valuable suggestions which
I think the Government would be well
advised to consider. The tenor of his
speech was that the Government are making no honest bona-fide effort to grapple
with this problem of the organisation of
the farming industry. They have done
so successfully in Denmark, Germany,
France, and Canada, and although our
area is so small we seem quite powerless
to follow in the footsteps of the Governments of those other countries. Senator
McAllister has voiced the views of a great
many members of the farming community
of Northern Ireland, irrespective of
politics. I do not intend to follow the
hon. and gallant Member (Colonel
Pakenham) in going into the question of
Protection and Free Trade and the surtax. I wonder how many farmers in
Northern Ireland pay surtax. Would
there be more than five hundred?
79 Government and Agriculture
Government and Agriculture 80
THE SENATE
Colonel PAKENHAM: I said the surtax that ought to have gone to the
farmers was now being used as income.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I thought the hon.
and gallant Member was referring to the
surtax imposed on the farming community as well as on other sections of the
population. In any event, all this is part
of the price we pay for our Imperial connection. If we want to move step-by-step
we must pay these duties. I do not intend to go into the question of dumping,
but I often wonder what dumping really
means. It is a very metaphorical phrase.
I would like to see a definition of the
word in an Act of Parliament. I do not
intend to detain the House longer by
crossing the t's and dotting the i's of the
speech of Senator McAllister, but I support his Motion.
Mr. PORTER-PORTER: In his speech
I think Senator McAllister supported the
Ministry of Agriculture as far as he possibly could, and if he will agree to delete
the words, "no comprehensive steps" from
his Motion I will support it. If there is
one doctrine the Ministry of Agriculture
have pushed it is that of compulsion and
education. We all know that the Ministry
have been abused more for their methods
of compulsion than for anything else they
have done. There is only one thing left
to improve the state of agriculture in this
Province, and that is for the farmers to
farm in a different way from what they
have been doing, and to improve their
methods. If we have a fortnight more
of the present weather there will be much
less talk amongst the farmers of the
It is the weather more than
country.
anything else that has been responsible
for the bad state of agriculture during
the past ten years. I with a great
agree
deal that was said by Senator M'Allister,
but he forgot the most important thing.
That is, that someone must control agriculture, and, perhaps, the present
Minister is as good as anyone else who
Icould be found.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I am not a
farmer, but if co-operation is of such
great importance to the farming community why do farmers not co-operate?
The rich farmer will have nothing to do
with his poorer neighbour. If farmers
could agree to co-operate to keep up the
prices of their produce they could easily
put an end to the middlemen, and they
could exert sufficient influence to induce
the shipping companies to bring down.
their rates. Senator McAllister advocated
the sending of young farmers abroad to
learn their business. I really think that
is going a little bit too far. If farmers
are to be sent abroad to learn their
business why should not members of other
trades be sent abroad to learn their
various trades. Although I have the
greatest sympathy with the farmers, and
would do anything in my power to help
the industry, yet at the same time I am
disposed to think that the improvement
of the industry is a matter for the farthemselves, and that they are
largely to blame for the present position.
mers
Mr. BARCLAY: The thing I cannot
understand is that there should be so
much poverty amongst the farmers. On
several occasions in this House the cry of
the poor farmer has been raised. I
would not like to go round the country
calling upon farmers and tell them they
were poor. They would not be very
pleased. A short time ago I had a trip
through parts of County Derry, County
Tyrone, and County Armagh, and I was
greatly surprised at the prosperity of the
farmers. The old thatched cottage is
disappearing, slated buildings are being
put up, and the old tumble-down shacks
the farmers used to house their cattle in
are being displaced by decent buildings.
I came to the conclusion, from what I
saw, that farming must have greatly
improved compared with the time I was
brought up on a farm. It strikes me
that the farmers are a discontented lot
of people like the Socialists. The more
you do for them the more they expect
you to do.
I cannot support the Motion, although
I believe that some of the suggestions
Senator McAllister has made will come
about as time goes on, when the farmers
get a little bit more wisdom. In some
parts of the country there is co-operation
so far as the egg industry is concerned.
In the district I was reared in they have
a branch of a co-operative society. When
I was at home some time ago I inquired
whether the Marketing of Eggs Act had
been of any benefit to the farmers, and
a sister told me they got the same price
for ten eggs as they used to get for a
dozen-that is selling by weight. The
Ministry of Agriculture have done a
great deal to improve the stock of horses,
cattle, sheep, pigs, and poultry, and it
is by a continuance of this policy that
agriculture in the Six Counties will
prosper in the future.
Government and Agriculture 82
Government and Agriculture
25 MARCH 1931
Mr. ARMSTRONG : I think every
Member of the Senate is of the same
opinion regarding two matters. One is
the desirability of co-operation, and the
other is the wish that farmers should
have better facilities for transport, but
I cannot exactly see what more the
Government is to do in these matters.
Denmark has been referred to as a
country where co-operation prevails, but
I have not heard it stated that that was
due to the action of the Government. I
think that perhaps we are rather in a
hurry in regard to this question of cooperation. The facilities for education
amongst the farmers have been in
existence now for a considerable number
of years, and perhaps the older farmers
were not very easy to convert to the new
system. The next generation will probably accept these ideas, and I think we
shall find that not only does farming
improve in consequence of this education,
but that, in addition, there will be a
readiness to combine, which has not
existed up to the moment.
Farming is, no doubt, a disappointing
occupation. I have been more or less
engaged in it for a number of years, and
what with the climate and the uncertainty of prices it is very difficult to make
a success of it. It requires great activity
of mind, and farmers who take things
for granted cannot succeed. I know
farmers who constantly watch the prices
in the markets and are active in mind,
and, in consequence, they are doing very
well indeed. I think that as time goes
on that tendency will increase.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I am
sure that all the Members of this House
would be only too pleased to see an
improvement in agriculture in the "Six
Counties. With regard to the question
of co-operation, I remember that where
I lived in my young days there was no
such thing as delivery of produce to one's
house. We had to bring everything to
the house, including potatoes, beef, and
bread. Now there is not a house in Belfast at which a boy or someone else does
not call every morning to take an order
for a red herring or something else. I
think there is a great deal of money unnecessarily spent in distributing small
orders around the towns. The cost of
a stone or hundredweight of potatoes a
farmer sells must be trebled by the time
it is delivered at the house of the purchaser. I think the question of better
and more economic selling will have to
be taken up by the farmer and the consumer direct. If the farmer would
make his potatoes and other produce up
into smaller quantities he could have
customers of his own to whom he could
deliver direct and get a better price for
them.
I should be very sorry to see the Government interfere in the buying and selling of agricultural produce. We had a
recent experience of that in America.
The American Government thought they
would stabilise the price of wheat, and
their action has proved a curse to America
and the whole world. I hope the Northern Government will never be so misguided as to follow in the footsteps of
America in that direction. We are ali
anxious to see the farming industry in a
more prosperous condition, but I think
it will have to be brought about by cooperation on the part of consumers and
farmers.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sure
that no
one in this House wishes to
minimise the difficulties which have faced
our farming community. We could all
quote figures showing the low level of
prices for agricultural produce, and it is
small consolation to know that this depression is general throughout the whole.
world. At the same time, I propose to
show that we are not at the very bottom
of the trough of the wave. I have a lot
of facts and figures here showing the sur
plus of wheat in other countries, but they
are too long to quote. At a time when
agricultural production throughout the
world is maintained at a high level
economic changes have curtailed the purchasing power of the population and
have led to depressed prices. I believe
it is true to say that in Northern Ireland
the farming community has probably
withstood the existing wave of depression
better than in any other part of the
United Kingdom or, possibly, than most
foreign countries. This is because our
farmers have concentrated upon the production of live stock and live stock products, and our Ministry of Agriculture
is entitled to some credit for its guidance
in that direction. We have no depressed
area of cereal growers such as exists in
East Anglia, in England, or in the
Lothians of Scotland. Approximately 80
per cent. of our output is sold off our
farms in the form of live stock or live
stock products. Undoubtedly the live
Government and Agriculture
Government and Agriculture
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
stock branch of the agricultural industry
is the least depressed at the present time;
indeed, prices of cattle during 1930 were
slightly higher than in 1929.
The emphasis which has been laid by
the Ministry of Agriculture upon live
stock breeding and the policy of that
Department in devoting as much money
as possible to awarding premiums to highgrade sires throughout the country and,
in passing the Live Stock Breeding Act
of 1923, has been abundantly justified.
It has enabled our farmers to get through
a period of world-wide agricultural depression with the minimum of loss and
with far less suffering than in any other
part of the United Kingdom. The very
depression which has hit arable farmers
in other parts of the world has, in fact,
been an ill-wind which has blown a positive good to our live stock farmers in
Northern Ireland by cheapening the cost
of animal-feeding stuffs, such as oats and
maize meal. In saying this I do not
overlook the low prices which have been
obtained by our flax growers during the
past season, nor the poor prices for
potatoes which prevailed throughout 1930.
In the case of both these crops, however,
we are largely affected by the production
in other countries. This is especially the
case in connection with flax, for a large
part of the fibre used in our linen factories is obtained from the Baltic
countries.
In the case of potatoes, I would remind
hon. Members that in both 1929 and 1930
the production of this crop in Great
Britain was exceptionally large, and it is
a tribute to the value of the Marketing
of Potatoes Act, which was passed in
1928, that in face of the quite abnormal
surplus of potatoes which has existed in
Great Britain during the past two years
we succeeded in exporting no less than
208,835 tons of potatoes in 1929 and 1930.
Indeed, thanks to the high reputation
enjoyed by our ware potatoes as a result
of the Marketing of Potatoes Act, we
actually succeeded in selling Northern
Ireland potatoes on the Lancashire market
in preference to local supplies.
A very large proportion of the produce
raised on Ulster farms is exported to
Great Britain, and has there to compete,
not only with home produce grown in
England and Scotland, but with produce
from overseas. For Great Britain is the
one great open market for agricultural
produce in the world to-day, and produce
In
from Northern Ireland has thus to be sold
on what is probably the most competitive,
and least sheltered, market for agricultural produce that is in existence.
order to secure our fair share of that market, it is essential that our produce should
bear comparsion with the very best that
is imported from Denmark or Holland, or
that is produced on the home farms of
Great Britain. It is for this reason that
our Ministry of Agriculture has devoted
so much attention during the past seven
or eight years to the quality, standardisation, and grading of the produce sent
from Northern Ireland to Great Britain.
When it is remembered that we have
100,000 farmers in Northern Ireland-a
third more than in the whole of Scotland
-and that practically every one of these
farmers is producing some small surplus
of eggs, butter, fruit, potatoes, sheep,
cattle, or pigs for sale, the task of ensuring that these small lots of produce are
assembled into consignments, which are
of uniform good quality, and graded to
definite standards is, obviously, one of
great difficulty. Only a few years ago
any one of our 100,000 farmers couldand often did-export his produce in any
condition he liked. The odium and disrepute which, in Great Britain, was coming to be attached to Northern Ireland
agricultural produce as a consequence of
these haphazard methods of marketing
are well known. As a result of the legislation in connection with the marketing
of our agricultural produce, which has
been placed on the Statute Book in recent
years, however, there has been a complete
change in the attitude with which merchants and consumers in Great Britain
now regard our produce.
are
The Live Stock Breeding Act of 1923
has led to such an improvement in our
cattle population that our stores
eagerly sought after in the cattle-feeding
districts of Great Britain, and in both
England and Scotland the Departments
of Agriculture are looking forward to the
day when they will be in a position to
administer similar legislation in regard to
the cattle population of their countres to
that which we have in the Six Counties.
Our eggs command higher prices on the
English market than any other class of
eggs with
the exception of English
national mark eggs. I have already referred to the reputation enjoyed by our
ware potatoes, thanks to the Marketing
of Potatoes Act. I am confident that
the effect of the Marketing of Dairy
85 Government and Agriculture

25 MARCH 1931
Government and Agriculture
Produce Act and the Agricultural
Produce (Meat Inspection) Act will
improve the reputation of our dairy produce, meat, bacon and hams in the same
manner, while the Marketing of Fruit
Bill, which is at present before Parliament, is designed to safeguard the standards and reputation of our fruit exports.
Northern Ireland is essentially an exporting area so far as large classes of
agricultural produce are concerned. Our
great market-indeed in many instances
our only market for this surplus produce
-is Great Britain. At all costs the reputation of our produce in that market
must be maintained at the highest possible
level, and this has been furthered by the
marketing legislation which I have outlined. With a recovery in economic conditions in Great Britain, the Government
is confident that we may anticipate an
increased demand for our produce, the
bulk of which has now achieved a deservedly high reputation for quality with
the British consumer. It is impossible
for any Government to raise the level of
agricultural prices, much less our Government in Northern Ireland. The low
level of agricultural prices, I must repeat, is due to general world causes, and
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dorman, Mr. R.
any efforts upon our part to interfere
with the course of those prices would be
illusory. But we can direct our farmers into
those fields of farming activity in which
there is least depression. That course we
have adopted with a substantial measure
of success, while the high reputation
which we have created for Northern Ireland produce in Great Britain means that
whenever a revival of demand occurs our
produce will be the first to benefit from
the improved purchasing power of the
British consuming public.
At
The speech of Senator McAllister was
listened to with great interest. It was
as free as could have been expected from
any political bias, and I am sure his suggestions will receive consideration.
the same time, I agree with other hon.
Members that co-operation, while a very
desirable thing, is primarily a matter for
the farmers themselves.
Question put, "That this House regrets
that no comprehensive steps have been
taken by the Government to restore the
farming industry of Northern Ireland to
its former prosperity."
The House divided: Contents, 4;
Not Contents, 14.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be read a
Second Time to-morrow.
Coursing of Hares Bill [S].
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LESLIE: In rising this afternoon
to move the Second Reading of this Bill,
which has been some time before hon.
Members of this House, I feel I must
claim, perhaps, a larger share of that indulgence of which I am always in need,
and which, as a rule, this House is so
good as to afford me, because the subject
with which I have to deal is one which
presents a good deal of difficulty. I venture to say it presents a very great deal
more difficulty than those who deal with
it lightly and airily, by letter to the papers
and by casual remark, are aware of.
It will be within the recollection of
this House that last November when
speaking on a Bill dealing with coursing,
a Bill which, as you will remember, this
House did me the honour to read a Second
time, and subsequently passed through
Committee without amendment, I said
then that that Bill, which I knew could
not become law owing to the period of
the session-that that Bill was to be regarded rather as a contribution to the
discussion than anything else.
And I also said that I thought that
during the interval betweeen the sessions
we ought to be able to arrive at something
humane, reasonable and workable. That
particular Bill simply proposed the suspension of the operation of a paragraph
of a Sub-section of the well-known Protection of Animals Act. It was objected
to as a Bill, and the Bill now before the
House has been objected to, and will be
objected to by two classes of persons.
There is one class which said the Bill
went too far. They said it would stop
coursing altogether. I am not quite sure
that some of those people recognise that
making that criticism involves the admission that the sport is attended with
cruelty. I think that possibly most of
the critics made their criticism without
reading the Act of Parliament that I have
referred to. That was one class.
The other class of critic said the Bill
did not go far enough. They wanted
complete prohibition, not only of coursing
in enclosed places, commonly called park
coursing. I am not sure how much
more they do want. They certainly want
that. There will be the same criticism of
the Bill now before the House. In fact
I may say, judging from a hasty glance
at this morning's papers, something of
that kind has already begun. Passing
from this to other criticism, to which I
attach much importance, I come to the
third, the more effective criticism that
such a Bill as the first one would not provide for uniformity of administration, and
therefore, would probably fail as other
Bills have failed. The Bill then really
-that is, last November's Bill-only accomplished this. It did serve as notice
to the public that this Parliament intended to do something-that something
was going to be done and that that something was going to be done by the Parliament sitting here in this place; the Parliament authorised to deal with these matters, and was not going to be done by
some-shall we say less responsible
assembly sitting, perhaps, in Dungannon;
perhaps, in High Street, or possibly in the
office of a newspaper in Belfast. The
intention was that Parliament should do
something.
Since November I have been in constant consultation and correspondence
with various people who, I thought,
could afford me information on this subject. Amongst these people were Members of Parliament, Members of both
Houses, and even Members of the Government. It seemed plain that there was
still a distinct demand on the part of the
public either for complete prohibition or
for strict regulation. Now, as regards
the first complete prohibition-I have
to say quite plainly that, although I do
not course myself and do not propose to,
I am not satisfied that there is any
sufficient demand throughout the Province
for complete prohibition. Until there is
such a demand before Parliament, Parliament ought not, in my judgment, to
move in that direction.
In support of the agitation for complete prohibition certain well-meaning
people, for whom I have great respect,
for I know they have studied this
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Second Reading
25 MARCH 1931
question and have prepared and circulated
extensively a petition for signature and
presentation. I am not aware that the
response to that agitation has been such
as to lead us to think there is really a
strong public demand for Parliament to
go so far. The question of regulation
remains, and I think the feeling is rather
in favour of strict regulation.
I
Now, besides correspondence and conference, I thought it right to attend, in
response to an invitation kindly issued
to myself and others, a more or less
private demonstration of enclosed coursing in a recently established stadium,
perhaps I may call it, near Belfast.
subsequently went to an actual meeting
where coursing was carried on there.
Those of us who went there were
received in the most candid manner, and
with something more than courtesy, and
generally in a very friendly spirit. We
were received as honest seekers after
truth, not as enemies of sport. All the
same I came away with the conclusion
that something ought still to be done by
Parliament.
I was not alone in my investigations.
Amongst others, the Minister of Home
Affairs has, to my knowledge, given his
personal attention to this subject, and
I think I am not going too far if I say
that before I really put this Bill in draft,
I had very good reason to believe that
the Minister was not prepared to resent
the suggestion that regulations should be
introduced into Parliament and adopted
by Parliament. That being so, one's
task became very much easier. One did
not worry very much therefore on the
subject of prohibition, because that could
very well be left over until, if ever,
the public demand I have spoken of
really does arise. One could give one's
attention to the production of a workable
Bill. That task is not easy, as I have
indicated. It is rather complicated, because the law on the subject is very
complicated, indeed, much more so than
appears at first sight.
The necessity, as I think, for Parliament to intervene in this matter arises
really from the modern tendency to try
to reproduce under artificial conditions
an ancient and, as is claimed, an honourable sport. Old-time coursing conducted
in the open was one thing, and park
coursing is almost necessarily another.
It is only fair to say again, that there
are coursing meetings in Northern Ireland in which the utmost endeavours are
made to approach as near the real thing
as it is possible to do. That is so, and
in asking the House to deal with this
matter it would not be right that I
should refrain from making that remark.
There is certainly no reason whatever to
think that people who
course
are
naturally more cruel than other people.
Nor is there any reason to believe that
they want to kill a large number of
hares. For one thing, when a hare is
killed it costs a certain number of
shillings to get another. For another,
as regards the competition of dogs a mere
kill counts a relatively small number of
points. We may abandon all that and
come back to the question of difficulties.
Parliament as a rule does not like to
interfere with sport. But it has done
so, and the example that occurs to everybody is the prohibition of the shooting of
captive pigeons. Well, I have not to
argue that. It has been settled. There
is this essential difference, and it is one
of the reasons why dealing with this
matter becomes difficult. There is an
essential difference between shooting captive pigeons and coursing hares. In the
case of shooting pigeons a man does the
shooting. In the case of coursing hares
it is dogs that do the coursing. Really
that is fundamentally the difference that
gives rise to so much technical difficulty.
But if coursing in enclosed spaces is not
like shooting captive pigeons it has perhaps some resemblance to cockfighting, or
bull baiting, both of which used to be
carried on in very much enclosed spaces,
and, of course, not directly by the agency
of man.
Let me digress to suggest to the House
that there are several abstruse legal
problems that arise in considering
this matter of coursing as now practised.
I am not at all sure myself that some of
the coursing meetings in the North of
Ireland as sometimes conducted-I am
not speaking generally-what I am going
to say does not apply to all meetings, and
not necessarily to the same meeting at all
times but I am rather doubtful as to
whether some of these meetings, at all
events, are not at present unlawful, quite
irrespective of the question of the escape
provided for hares; whether they are not
unlawful perhaps in common law in the
same way as keeping a cockpit was held
to be unlawful some 150 years ago, for a
reason apart from any question of cruelty.
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Papers Presented
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
But it is not very profitable to pursue
that line of argument. The remedy would
be too cumbersome.
Then, when we come to statute, we
have to consider what sort of an animal
in the eyes of the law it is that is used
for coursing. There seems to be a popular impression that the hare is a captive
hare. In popular language so it is. But
speaking at a luncheon, I think, given at
a recent coursing meeting, a gentleman
who occupied a somewhat prominent
position in that club gave his opinion.
definitely to the effect that the hares used
were not captive animals at all.
It may
be they are, and it may be they are not,
because the hare used is undoubtedly captive at one time. But is it captive when
released? That, again, is not a very
profitable subject. Let us look a little.
further.
Some years ago, following England,
we passed an Act dealing with the training and keeping for exhibition of animals.
trained for public exhibition. It is not
perhaps altogether unfruitful to ask ourselves whether if you take a hare from
the wild country and shut it up in a
large cage, exercise it by running it up
and down for coursing-it is rather a
question whether you are not training
that animal for public exhibition. There
was a loophole in that statute which,
perhaps, might be relied upon.
There remains another possibility, and
that is whether the hare put in a cage
and fed regularly-and these hares are
treated remarkably well-whether a
hare treated in that manner, exercised
and trained-a hare has got to be
trained, otherwise it will not know its
way to the escape and not give a good
course whether a hare treated in that
way has not been promoted or degraded,
whichever you like, to the status of a
domestic animal within the meaning of
the Act of Parliament. A domestic
animal under the Act of 1911 is not only
the sheep, pig or pigeon, and so on,
specially named, but any other animal
whatever which has been or is being
sufficienty tamed to serve some purpose
for the use of man, and it is to my mind
a very difficult question whether such a
hare, which is to be used for testing the
comparative merits of greyhounds, has
not been tamed or trained to serve some
purpose for the use of man. Still these
points are rather difficult and they have.
not yet been settled by the courts. Of
course they might be at any moment
brought before the court. But they
are not touched in any way by the Bill
before the House.
I come back to the question as to
whether the
the regulation of coursing
establishments ought not to be considered
rather in relation to cock-fighting than
anything else. But cock-fighting, the
House will remember, is absolutely unlawful in itself, and the Bill before you
proposes not to make coursing universally
unlawful, not to touch open coursing or
harriers at all, not to make even park
coursing universally unlawful, but to
allow it to continue under certain conditions. For that purpose I think you
must have regard to the necessity for
licence or permit or certificate, call it
what you will, and you must attach that
licence or certificate to some premises or
place, using the cock-fighting form of
words again, rather after the manner
adopted in England in 1879 when dealing
with the question of licensing metropolitan suburban race courses, a statute
with which I think some Members of
this House will probably be familiar;
and the more familiar they are with that
statute the more they will understand
why I have not followed it completely
in the present Bill.
It follows then if you adopt that part
of the Act of 1879 and go in for licensing, you must decide to what authority
you will entrust the power to issue
licences or permits. That is a matter
which gave me very great trouble. One
had to look round and see what precedents
there were and how far they were
applicable. One thought, perhaps in
the first instance, of giving the power
of issuing licences to the Minister of
Home Affairs, but rejected the idea.
It really is not his business. One
thought of the magistrates in
in the
same way as some other licences are
granted, and of the county council, but
I came back to the question of the
police. It seemed to me that perhaps a
superior officer of the police, something
on the analogy of the firearm permit and
motor race matters, was as good an
authority as you will find.
One was bound to recognise that it
was a pure question of administration
and the last word as to what ought to
be the proper licensing authority must
rest with the Minister of Home Affairs
93 Coursing of Hares Bill-

25 MARCH 1931
Second Reading
in whose ambit this matter is really contained. Therefore, although I drafted
the Bill on the lines of making the
county inspector the licensing authority,
I recognised that it was extremely likely
it would be said that the right course
was to follow the more recent precedent
of 1928 in the Act commonly known as
the Totalisator Act, and give the
authority to a board of control. Whether
the Minister of Home Affairs would
prefer a board of control to the county
inspector seems to me a question for the
Government to advise the House upon.
If the Government advise the House that
some other authority should be substituted
for the county inspector, I need scarcely
say, so far as I am concerned, I will most
readily acquiesce. It would make comparatively little difference to the Bill.
The alterations and ancillary alterations
would not be very serious. When I
drafted the Bill I knew there might be
some difficulty of that sort, and I endeavoured to provide that little more
than a change of name would be required.
But whatever authority is decided on,
there are certain matters which I think
Parliament ought to take on its own
shoulders. One of the things I have
provided for is a strong advisory boarda board to be subordinate to the Ministry
but not under its control. It may be
that in the development of this discussion
we shall find that the advisory board
of the Bill and the controlling board, if
that is suggested, will merge into each
other and will give no serious difficulty.
Now I pass to the Bill and will run
rapidly through the clauses. My first
clause provides for the issue of a permit
and for conforming to such regulations
as will be made. I should like to lay
particular stress on paragraph (d) of
this Clause, that is the paragraph dealing with the admission of a child under
fourteen years of to places where
age
coursing is in progress. I owe to my
right hon. Friend in front of me (Mr.
Samuel Cunningham) the suggestion
that a word or two ought to be added
there, and if the House honours me by
giving the Bill a Second Reading, I will
accept that suggestion, that is, that
such a child should not be permitted to
remain. It is thought that there might
be some difficulty of a child getting in
"unbeknownst, as the saying is, and
the child ought to be excluded.
As regards the general question of excluding a child of school age, a child
under fourteen, I put it to the House
