in this way. It is important, and I
attach importance to it. Will any Member of this House get up and say that in
his opinion a child under fourteen ought
to be admitted to a place of this sort
and brought into contact with sights and
sounds which occur, shouts and screams,
not entirely screams of a possibly
wounded hare? There are other screams
to be heard at a coursing meeting.
There are scenes and sights, as I say,
that a child of tender years ought not
to be exposed to, and I hope if there is
one provision of the Bill more than
another, which will receive the unanimous
support, not only of this House but of
those who sit elsewhere, it is that particular paragraph providing for the exclusion of a child under fourteen years
of age.
re-
The second Clause is simply one
lating to offences and it is borrowed
from the cock-fighting paragraph of the
Act I have referred to, but I thought
it necessary to add a few words specially
applicable to hares.
Clause 3 deals with permits, drafted of
course on the lines that the permit will
be issued by the county inspector. But
by whatever authority the permit is
issued the bulk of the Clause will, I
think, provide the machinery. In that
Clause will be found the provision of the
necessary fee. That is a matter of some
difficulty. It might be thought that one.
should say £20 or such lesser sum, or
perhaps such greater sum, as might be
hereafter decided. The difficulty arises
in this way.
It is obvious that certain
expenses must be incurred in ensuring
that the regulations are complied with.
It is equally certain that the public
ought not to be asked to pay any share
of the expense. I have given, as you will
see, the right to refuse to issue a permit
and the right of appeal against such a
refusal. I have provided for the application of the fees received.
Clause 4 gives the necessary powers of
entry and inspection-such powers as
are given I think in the Act dealing with
the training and exhibition of animals,
to which I have referred, but not
universally given under the more general
Act of 1911.
The next Clause deals with the power
to make regulations and gives an indi-
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
cation of the lines which those regulations
are expected by Parliament to follow.
I might draw particular attention to
paragraphs (e) and (f) of the Bill,
especially (e), because one of the causes
of cruelty at these meetings has undoubtedly been the coursing of hares,
perhaps let out of a bag or basket that
very afternoon, or the coursing of a
hare not accustomed to its surroundings.
It is not a practice which any honest
courser would think of following. I use
the expression "honest courser" as meaning a man who follows coursing as
sport. Also there is the question of the
length of slip. The other one is simply
the case of killing at once any hare that
may be injured. There have been one
or two horrible instances reported of a
hare being taken from the dogs and
thrown while still living over the rails.
I do not think that is a matter which
this House would wish to allow to be
repeated.
a
Then I come to Clause 6, establishing
a board which is intended to be helpful to
the controlling authority, and not intended to take responsibility off Parliament. That particular Clause might
necessarily require alteration if it is decided to make an alteration in the
licensing authority. It should give no
particular difficulty.
In Clause 7 I have provided the same
penalties that may be incurred under the
Act of 1911. I have not gone very far.
They are not unduly severe. My hope is
that whatever they are they will never
have to be inflicted, because I am hoping
that a regulating Bill will be accepted by
those concerned, and that those concerned
will administer it properly, and, therefore,
there will be no need to put the punitive
powers into force.
Clause 8 is one of great latitude.
Whatever powers the board have, whether
of advice or control, it is necessary they
should be kept advised of anything that
may happen. Therefore, any conviction
ought to be reported to that board
directly or indirectly. It does not matter.
whether it comes direct or is passed
through the Minister, but they ought to
be advised in order that they may advise
or decide whether or not a permit, licence
or certificate, whatever you may call it,
ought to be withdrawn.
Then under Clause 9 I give the necessary saving of existing Acts.
Under Clause 10 I give definitions.
The definition which gave me more trouble
than anything else was that of the coursing period. In the question of coursing
it is essential to provide against coursing
of immature hares, and it seems to me
that the dates I have inserted are, on the
whole, fair enough. Three and a half
months are provided for such coursing
meetings Then follows Clause 11, and
the Schedule.
Now that is the Bill. I have brought
it forward, admittedly, in the hope that
it will be accepted, and that the demand
for prohibition will not be pressed. I do
not think that the Bill is too harsh.
There are some people who may say it
is too strong. Those, presumably, will
be the people concerned with coursing.
To those I would say it is not this Bill
that stands to-day at the bar of public
opinion. It is those coursing organisations under which it was possible for
some of those atrocities to occur which
have disgraced Northern Ireland in the
not far distant past. It is for Parliament
to see that these things do not occur
again. To the others, those who say that
the Bill does not go far enough, I would
venture very respectfully to commend the
words of Bacon: "A man should so carry
the notions of his mind as if he cannot
have that which he seeketh in the best
degree, yet may he have it to the second
or third." I beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
congratulate the hon. and learned Member on his industry, and on the lucid
manner in which he has introduced this
Bill. When he was moving the Second
Reading I could not help being struck
with the intimate knowledge he seemed
to have of all these alleged mal-practices
at coursing meetings, and I was also surprised at the very definite information
he had about the illegal game of cockfighting. The thought at once ran
through my mind about the converted
poacher becoming game-keeper. (Laughter.) I oppose this Bill on various broad
and general principles. In the first place,
I oppose it because it is a Bill that is
absolutely unnecessary to an over-legislated people. In the second place I
oppose it on the ground that there is no
demand for the Bill.
No evidence has been produced here to
show there is any demand for the Measure.
Very often the class of people who pursue
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Second Reading
25 MARCH 1931
to be introduced if Parliament is to be
consistent.
the policy enunciated by the hon. Member are very vocal, but their numbers are
very few.
The fact that a few people are
more vocal than the overwhelming
majority of people is no justification for
the introduction of legislation that will
infringe the liberties and freedom of the
great majority of people. I speak as one
who has no interest in coursing and one
who has never followed that sport. I
have not discussed this Bill with anyone,
and I have not been approached by anyone; but I do say that this class of legislation that is introduced in this House,
session after session, infringing the liberties of the subject, must be very irritating
and irksome to the public.
There is another side to this Bill which
is even more serious. I have never heard
of a Bill which has been SO much
criticised as being class legislation as this
Bill has. Whether we like it or not we
must admit that the great majority of the
followers of coursing are democratic
people, and they naturally ask, and very
reasonably ask, why the sport which their
limited pocket enables them to participate in should be attacked, and the sport
of the rich allowed to go on unchecked.
That is the feeling abroad in regard to
this Bill, and that is one of the most
dangerous outlooks that can be cultivated
in respect of a Parliament. Nothing could
do more harm to a Parliament than for
the people to come to the conclusion that
it would stand for and sponsor class legislation of this description.
If this legislation is to continue, what
is to happen? If a man goes out to shoot
wild birds, such as pheasants and partridges, and leaves a wounded bird lying
for two or three days before it is found,
is not that greater cruelty than letting a
hare escape which is killed almost instantly. If this Bill is passed the hon.
and learned Senator, if he is consistent,
is bound to introduce a Bill to prevent
the shooting of game. I hate shooting of
all descriptions. I hate guns in any
shape or form, but is that any reason why
I should interfere with the liberty of a
man who wishes to go out for a day's
shooting. What is to happen to hunting
-the best sport in the world. We all
know that hares are killed in the course
of hunting, and yet it is considered to be
the best and most manly sport in the
world. Are we going to introduce legislation to curtail the sport of the man who
hunts? That is not the poor man's
sport, but he will expect such legislation
I do not intend to go into the details
of the Bill at this stage, but there is one
detail to which I would like to direct
attention. That is, that the whole control of coursing is to be placed in the
hands of an individual county inspector.
I have said previously in this House and
in other places that I have nothing to
disapprove of in the administration of the
law by the Royal Ulster Constabulary.
They are efficient and fair, so far as I
know, but to place the whole control of
coursing at the whim of an individual
county inspector, who may be in one
county to-day and another to-morrow, is
perfectly absurd. In one year you may
get a county inspector who is in favour
of the sport, who may take a broad view
and grant a permit. He may be succeeded the next year by a county
inspector who may not have such a broad
outlook and he may prohibit coursing.
Therefore, you may have coursing permitted in one county by a district
inspector in one year and the next year
it may be prohibited by another district.
inspector. That is too absurd for words.
There is also the question of regulations. The hon. and learned Member
has joined his voice with mine on many
occasions in condemning the practice of
empowering Ministries to make these
wide and far-reaching regulations, and
that he of all the Members of the House
should now sponsor a Bill giving almost
unlimited powers to the Ministry of
Home Affairs in this respect astonishes
me. I should have thought he would
have been the last person to write with
his own hand a clause of that kind. As
I said, I am opposed to the Bill on
various broad principles.
Mr. SAMUEL
CUNNINGHAM :
After the events that have taken place
in some provincial towns I think something was required to be done regarding
this sport, and the Bill before the House
is, I think, a very fair effort to deal with
the matter. At the same time, I think
it would improve the Bill if the Clause
dealing with the admission to coursing
meetings of boys under fourteen years of
age was either omitted or changed.
once took one of my own boys to a shoot.
We shot hares and pheasants and, therefore, it would be hyprocisy for me to turn
round and say a boy should not attend
a coursing meeting. According to the
I
Coursing of Hares Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Samuel Cunningham]
Clause on page 5, if the gatekeeper at a
coursing meeting admits a boy under
fourteen years of age he will be committing an offence, but if the boy is over
fourteen there will be no offence. I know
the difficulty that will arise in that case.
I will tell the House of an experience
One of my boys was
I actually had.
accustomed to go to school in a tram with
me. He was pretty big and I usually
paid the full fare for him. The day before he was twelve years old I decided to
pay the reduced fare, and I handed the
conductor the fare. He looked at me
with a good deal of suspicion. I said "I
am all right," but he still looked at me,
although he did not say anything. Going
out of the tram I said to the conductor,
"I will not do it again. The boy will be
twelve to-morrow, and I just wanted to
keep his last half-fare ticket."
what the position of a gatekeeper at a
coursing meeting will be if this particular
Clause passes.
If he admits a boy a day
before his fourteenth birthday he will be
committing an offence, but if he is a day
older than fourteen he will be all right. It
means that a father taking his boy to s
coursing meeting will have to carry in
his pocket the birth certificate and photograph of his boy. Therefore, I hope the
Clause will be amended in the interests of
the gate-keepers. In all other respects,
I think the Bill is a good one, and I hope
it will result in cruelty at coursing meetings being abolished.
Think
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The principle of the Bill may be taken as being
accepted by the Government in so far
as the Bill is designed with the object of
removing as far as possible the element
of cruelty from the sport of coursing without, at the same time, putting a stop for
all time to the sport. I thought the
speech of Senator McAllister was dictated
by the idea that this Bill would put a
stop to coursing for all time, but it is
merely designed to put a stop to unnecessary cruelty. Unnecessary cruelty is no
proper element of any kind of sport. It
is now generally recognised that one of
the coursing meetings held last year under
improper and cruel conditions was mainly
responsible for the concerted agitation
which has recently been observable in
opposition to coursing and for the protests
made by the Society for the Prevention of
Cruelty to Animals. I should remind
the House that I am president of that
society, but I am now speaking only as
the Government spokesman.
Inquiries have been made, and it is
understood that the members of the Irish
Coursing Club are no less concerned in
connection with the manner in which that
particular meeting was conducted, and it
is understood that the Irish Coursing Club
has decided to take such steps as may be
necessary to prevent a repetition of the
unpleasant scenes and incidents which
took place at the meeting in question. At
the same time, the Government feel that
there is some justification for the argument that some stronger guarantee is
needed, and that Parliament should now
step in and impose such further limitations on the sport of coursing as will remove all possible sources of cruelty.
Accordingly, the Bill in so far as its
principle is concerned, may be accepted,
but while accepting the principle and
while allowing the House to vote freely on
it, the Government must take strong exception to certain of the details of the
Bill, and I should like to make clear to
the hon. and learned Member, who is
promoting the Bill, that unless drastic
alterations are made by him in the Committee stage the Government's acquiescence cannot be relied upon.
The most important point to which
exception must be taken is the principle
underlying the Bill, under which no
coursing meetings can be held except on
the basis of a permit granted by a county
inspector of the Royal Ulster Constabulary. At first sight this principle might
appear to be a harmless and, perhaps, a
very proper one, as it would undoubtedly
ensure that coursing meetings in future
would be held under proper auspices. On
further reflection and consideration,
however, it would appear that this principle of adopted would, in effect, establish
a Department of the Government and the
officers responsible to the Department as
a sports-controlling department.
un-
It is admitted that in some continental
countries the Government has taken certain responsibility for sporting activities,
but that principle has not been accepted
in English-speaking countries, and it is
one that if it were accepted would,
doubtedly, lead to further developments.
It is a principle which is contrary to the
spirit of British people, and it may be
said that the success of sport in the United
Kingdom has been due, to a large extent,
to the fact that there has been no Government interference.
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Second Reading
25 MARCH 1931
At the same time, it is felt by the Government that there is some justification
for the issue of permits for the holding
of coursing meetings, and it is suggested
for the consideration of the hon. and
learned Member who has introduced the
Bill that the permits should be issued by
Statutory Committee-the Coursing
Committee of Northern Ireland, and
following this suggestion he may see fit to
make an alteration substituting a Coursing Committee for the Advisory Committee, which appears in his Bill, and to
give to the Coursing Committee the
powers of issuing permits, thus eliminating
any reference to the County
Inspectors of Police in connection with
such procedure.
The Government also have some doubt
as to the expediency of one or two other
minor points contemplated by the Bill,
and in this connection reference might
in particular be made to the amount of
the fee proposed to be charged for the
issue of permits, the penalty for admitting
children under fourteen and the dates
prescribed for coursing.
In regard to the first of these matters
-the question of the fee it will be
apparent that in the conditions under
which coursing is carried out in Northern Ireland it is extremely difficult to
exclude spectators, as the grounds are
mostly open and spectators who do not
wish to pay for admission are enabled to
gain entrance to the grounds by crossing
over fences. Then, again, it should be
apparent to the hon. and learned Member that under such circumstances it is
somewhat drastic to impose penalties on
the promoters if children under fourteen
years of age are discovered by the police
amongst the spectators. The difficulty,
of course, has been alluded to by the
right hon. Senator who spoke
last.
Thirdly, as regards the dates during
which coursing can be carried out, it has
been represented that the dates prescribed in the Act are somewhat too
narrow, and that a more extended period
should be allowed. This is a technical
matter and one that might very properly
be left to the Coursing Committee which
is to be established under the Bill, and
possibly this suggestion may commend
itself to the hon. and learned Member.
Subject to the above remarks and to
an assurance from the hon. and learned
Member that the matters will be taken
into consideration before Committee
stage, it may be taken for granted that,
so far as the Government is concerned,
all proper
facilities will be accorded for
the passing of the Bill. At the same
time, in connection with the alterations
of the Bill on the lines indicated above,
every possible facility will be accorded to
the hon. and learned Member and his
friends by the responsible officers of the
Government.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like if I may
to remind the hon. Senator whom I do
not see in his place, who spoke about
pheasant shooting and of leaving wounded
birds to linger three or four days-I
should like to say that I have myself
attended a certain number of pheasant
shootings during my perhaps misspent
youth, and I have never yet been at one
where there was not at least one competent retriever nor where at the conclusion of the shoot the owner did not
walk along the lines, not for the purpose
of counting the slain, but for seeing
that no wounded birds were overlooked.
I have never been at a place where on
the morning following the shoot every
keeper and every retriever was not out
searching for wounded birds or cripples.
I see the hon. Senator objects to powers
being given to the county inspectors, and
his objection is reinforced by the noble.
Viscount. As I indicated, I have not
the least objection to substituting another authority. I take due note that
the right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Samuel
Cunningham) in the interval of dealing
with tramcars and tramways intimated
that he would like, say, to take his son to
see a coursing meeting. Passing to the
noble Viscount, I am quite prepared to
give an assurance as regards the substitution of another authority for the
county inspector. I am equally prepared to make the necessary ancillary
alterations in the Bill. As I indicated,
they will not cause any serious trouble
or difficulty. I am very much surprised
if the adviser of the noble Viscount
could not produce what is required in
half an hour.
As regards the other matters, I think
we must feel that the question of the
fee proposed is a problem. The question
of the child is very difficult. There is
the risk of demoralisation on the one
hand. There is very little use in the
noble Viscount seeing, as I know he does
see, that lectures are given in establishments under his control on kindness to
Wild Birds Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
animals and so forth if the holidays are
spent under these conditions. I should like
to thank the House for the consideration
given. However, we have concluded the
discussion of this matter in something
under fifty minutes.
Question put: "That the Bill be now
read a Second time."
The House divided: Content, 12; Not
Content, 4.
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Barclay, Mr. W.
Charlemont, Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
CONTENTS
Cunnignham, Rt. Hon. Samuel
Dickson, Mr. J. Hill
Dorman, Mr. R.
Leslie, Mr. J. G.
Long, Mr. J. A.
MeCorkell, Mr. D. E. B.
Pakenham, Colonel H. A.
Quigley, Mr. Andrew
NOT CONTENTS
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
Wild Birds Protection Bill (S.).
Order for Second Reading read.
To
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read a Second time." In
moving this Bill I do not lay claim to expert knowledge of the subject. I have
no knowledge beyond that of the ordinary
lover of birds. Everybody in Northern
Ireland has a real love of birds. Its bird
life is one of the charms of Ulster.
large and increasing numbers birds are a
source of wonder and delight. The songs
of birds have a peculiar charm for men
and women of all ranks, especially from
April to June, when all the song birds
are in full song. Who has not enjoyed
one of those spring days when you caught
the first preludes of the lark, those days
which set the hedges musical with song?
The songs of the robin and the wren one
can hear all the year round amid all the
vagaries of our climate, for these birds
pour forth their songs in scorching
sun heat and with even more vivacity
when the earth is snow-clad.
I was
lately much struck by a thought of Lord
Grey of Falloden. Lord Grey, in his
"Falloden Papers," has the arresting
thought that the songs of the birds in
England to-day must be the same songs
that were heard by the inhabitants of
England before the 400 years of the sway
of Imperial Rome in England, that the
songs of the birds come down unchanged
through great antiquity, and that we are
listening to-day in whatever part of the
world we may be to songs which must
have been familiar to races of men of
which history has no record and no knowledge. We can all enjoy these songs.
We can all enjoy outdoor nature.
as boundless as the sea in the exceeding
It is
McHugh, Mr. John
Porter-Porter, Mr. John
plenty of its riches. We can enjoy it
without the least tinge of envy of anyone
else's enjoyment. We can draw without
stint upon its inexhaustible funds.
There is perhaps a certain fitness in
thinking of wild bird protection just now,
because tremendous armies of birds returning home northwards from their
winter quarters have been caught by the
Arctic weather prevailing in Central
Europe this March, and have perished in
tens of thousands from cold and
exhaustion, so that probably much less
than the average of these ever welcome
visitors will reach our shores this
year. As regards bird life, we are all
Protectionists. There are, I think, about
180 species of birds which nest in Northern Ireland, of which about 35 are
migrants or summer visitors only. That
leaves about 145 species native of the soil
all the year round. Of these a few have
become rather rare, and every effort will
be required to preserve them. Some
species are on the increase. The starling
is one of the birds on the increase.
This Bill is in broad outline the result
of the labours of the informal Committee,
representative of the various interests concerned, appointed in April, 1927, by the
Minister of Home Affairs to consider the
general protection of wild birds in Northern Ireland, having regard in particular
to the conditions of bird life as it affects
agriculture. It was a Committee of
weight and authority. Some of its
members were life students of this subject. I believe that the organisations interested in birds favoured the principle of
the Bill, and the county councils also.
The Bill goes on the principle that all
birds have a right to protection, with few
exceptions, some of which might grow so
Wild Birds Bill-
Second Reading
25 MARCH 1931
numerous as to be a nuisance to agriculture or to the community. Unprotected
birds may be put on the protected list
at any time by an order of the Ministry
of Home Affairs, when they need protection. The Bill is a declaratory Bill
in a large degree. It seeks to clarify and
simplify the law. It repeals six Acts.
Hardly anybody knows what is the law
at present. No one is anxious to put
obscure laws into force. Here we find
well illustrated "the lawless science of our
law." The enforcement of the law being
difficult is necessarily sporadic. I hope
this Bill will have the backing of public
opinion, that it will have a good chance
of being enforced, and that it will give
more protection to wild birds than the
long chain of Acts and Orders which
appear to give them protection, but really
do not. The birds suffer from too much
legislation-too many Acts, too many
local Orders, too many lists of birds which
have been protected in some areas and
outlawed in adjoining areas.
The first
Wild Birds Protection Act was passed in
the 1880. The second, passed in the
year
year 1881, bore the significant title, "An
Act to explain the Wild Birds Protection
Act, 1880." A succession of protective
Acts followed. I will now give a short
sketch of the Clauses of the Bill.
Clause 1 prohibits cruel methods of
taking or killing birds, say by means
of a hook. A like provision is found in
the Wild Birds Protection Act, 1908.
The Clause prohibits the killing or taking
of birds by means of motor-driven boats
or aircraft. It is poor sportsmanship to
make use of a swift motor-boat to hunt
down wild birds, but such are used, I
learn, by hunters armed with guns in
parts of the United States. Elsewhere,
motor-cars are used to run down gazelles.
The idea of aircraft so used may seem
far-fetched, but in Southern Spain, the
bustard, which used to be native of these
Islands, was run down by aeroplanes until
it was tired out and fell to the ground,
whereupon the aeroplane landed and the
bird was captured alive.
Clause 2 makes unlawful the selling, or
having for sale for human consumption,
or having for human consumption any
bird during the period between the 15th
day after the beginning of the close season
for that bird and the end of the season.
Clause 3 makes it illegal to use as a
decoy any caged live bird, or any blind
or injured bird, or to use bird lime for
the purpose of taking birds, and prohibits captive birds being confined in
cages too small to permit the birds freely
to stretch their wings. This Clause is a
replica of Sections 7 and 8 of the Protection of Animals (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, 1926, which two sections
this Bill accordingly repeals. Few
sights are sadder to a friend of birds than
to see a skylark, the brightest of birds,
with that peal of bells in its throat,
capable of making what Shelley calls
"harmonious madness," whose home for
song is the boundless sky, battering its
frail body in frenzy against the bars of
a too small cage.
Clause 4 requires any person who takes
birds for sale alive to obtain a certificate
from the District Inspector of Constabulary. This Clause would put some check
on the commercialism of amateurs who
I am
capture wild birds to make £ s. d.
not particularly keen on the retention of
that Clause as it stands, having regard to
what the noble Viscount, the Leader of
the House, said to-day as to the objections
to putting too much administrative control
in the hands of the police.
Clause 5 makes it unlawful to kill or
take birds or wilfully disturb nests on
Sunday. This provision would tend to
secure one close day in the week for birds,
one day on which their nests will be safe
from depredation, and it should promote
the enjoyment of many, particularly of
the working classes, who stroll into the
country on that day and are not free
to go there on any other day. I do not
think that many wish to kill or capture
birds on Sunday.
Clause 6 prohibits the shooting of wild.
birds at meetings, competitions, or exhibitions, and is a reproduction of Sections
9 and 10 of the Protection of Animals
(Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, 1926.
Clause 7 is the first of the Clauses providing special protection for all wild
birds, except the fourteen species which
are on the black list, but which can be
put on the protected list at any time by
an Order of the Ministry of Home Affairs.
The excepted birds are quite common.
There is no danger of their becoming
exterminated. The Clause provides for
the classification of birds in three categories.
Under Clause 8 almost all wild birds
will be specially protected at all times
against being shot or taken. They will
have complete protection. The eggs and
nests of birds in all three categories will
Wild Birds Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
be specially protected at all times against
being taken. The Parliament at Westminster in 1928 passed an Act for the
special behoof of the lapwing, intituled
the Protection of Lapwings Act. This
Bill does as much for this most beneficent
bird that destroys insects and worms
inimical to the production of food.
Clause 9 prohibits the liberation of imported birds, with certain exceptions,
without the leave of the Minister of Home
Affairs. The little owl was introduced
into England and has proved a destructive little bird. Leave would be required
for the introduction of any such bird here.
Clause 10 empowers the Minister to
grant licences exempting holders from the
provisions regarding the killing or taking
of birds and the taking of eggs where
exemptions would be desirable in the
cause of science or for the protection of
property.
Clause 11 requires taxidermists to keep
registers of the protected birds, and the
skins, eggs, and nests of such birds received by them. At present there are, I
am informed, only two of that trade in
our area. This Clause will not become
operative until a similar Clause becomes
operative in Great Britain.
Clause 12 provides for the creation of
bird sanctuaries. I believe we have only
one in Northern Ireland-in the grounds
of Government House at Hillsborough.
Bird sanctuaries are springing up all over
England. Hyde Park in the heart of
London has one. In time birds will
realise where these sanctuaries are, in
which they can go on singing their glad
songs and enjoying their joyous lives with
safety. I should like to see these
sanctuaries tax-free and rate-free. They
will not exist for profit. But birds of a
harmful species of the hawk tribe, saymight enter there. One could not keep
such birds out, so it is wise to give power
to put outside the pale of protection any
bird which might prey upon the birds
around it there and make the place a
slaughter house instead of a sanctuary.
Clauses 13, 14 and 15 deal with the
question of offences, penalties and legal
proceedings and require no observations
of mine, except that Sub-Clause 4, of
Clause 15, refers to offences committed
near the coasts within the jurisdiction of
the Admiralty. That Sub-Clause is put
in with great diffidence and doubt, because I fear that it is not competent for
this Parliament to legislate on this matter,
and I invite particular attention to this
point.
Clause 16 defines the powers and duties
of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, to
whom the execution of the Act will be
entrusted. I think the powers will be
found reasonable and necessary, and
prove effective. Clause 17 enables a Wild
Birds Advisory Committee to be set up,
a body which should be representative
of the various interests concerned with
bird life.
Clause 18 deals with the jurisdiction of
the Ministry to make orders generally, or
as regards a specific district. There will
be general sympathy with the suggestion
that the administration of an Act of this
kind should be centralised and coordinated. The area is very small, smaller
than Yorkshire. I believe the county
councils approve of co-ordination. Bird
life varies little from county to county in
Northern Ireland. The conflicting
jurisdiction which is sometimes vested in
county councils has led to confusion, birds
being protected in one county, and unprotected in an adjoining county. I heard
of one case at a bridge near Belfast-
Shaw's Bridge-which is a dividing line
between one county and another. On one
side it was illegal to shoot a certain bird but
on the other side it was absolutely safe.
Elasticity in working the Act will be
obtained by the powers given to the
Ministry to make orders.
Clause 20 makes it the duty of the
Minister to exhibit Orders made under the
Bill in such manner as he may deem advisable. Clause 21 is the definition Clause
and there may be considerable controversy
over that, especially over the period of
the close season, which is prescribed as the
period from 1st March to 12th August.
That is the period recommended by the
informal committee which met in connection with this matter. I understand
that a prolonged close season is the surest
and simplest way to preserve the stock.
I noticed quite recently that a distinguished statesman, Mr. Churchill, suggested a close season of five or six months
for Parliament and for politicians.
I am sorry for taking up so much time.
I venture to claim that the Bill is free
from perplexing references to earlier Acts,
and from technical terminology. It aims
at preserving natural species of great
charm and interest without in any way
Wild Birds Bill-
Second Reading
25 MARCH 1931
conflicting with the interests of agriculture, science, or sport, or the maintenance
of the food supply. Its language can
be understood by a school child. Many
would be glad to see interest in birds
stimulated in the schools here
the schools of the United
as in
States and
Germany. In Hungary they have an
annual bird day in the
schools.
When writers of a certain class of
tales used to describe a very bad boy
they usually called him "a robber of
birds' nests." The growing youth of
Ulster look upon it as a cruel and
cowardly act wantonly to destroy the eggs
or nests of birds. The skill exhibited in
choosing a spot and building a nest, and
the contrast between the delicate tint of
the eggs and the materials composing the
nests are a source of pleasure to countless
bird lovers as to Wordsworth, on whom
". . . . the chance-discovered sight,
Gleamed like a vision of delight."
Ulster youth should be taught to take
an intelligent interest in birds, and to
cultivate a habit of observation of their
wonderful lives and ways, and to appreciate their charm and value. I acknowledge
the courtesy and kindly'spirit in which the
House has listened to me. Some will say
that Parliament could be better employed
on bread and butter problems, but this
Bill will not take more than perhaps onethirtieth of the time of the Session.
Among the few pleasures of the workers
are the songs of bords and the
workers will be the last to demur to the
brief time spent on a Bill directed against
cruelty to birds, and against unnecessary
wastage of bird life, due, in part, to the
inventions of the age. I ask the House
to lead in this humane movement, and
to pass the Second Reading by a unanimous vote.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I congratulate
the hon. and learned Senator on the introduction of this Bill. I am glad to see
that he is a lover of birds, but, on the
other hand, he voted against the hare.
I am very much in favour of this Bill.
I think the hon. and learned Senator's
suggestion of bird sanctuaries deserves
the interest of people who care for birds.
There are a good many places where these
could be set up, and they would prove
a great blessing to the birds and a great
lesson to the people. I am, in addition,
grateful for this Bill because of my friend,
the wild duck, which I tried to get introduced into the Game Preservation Act a
couple of Sessions ago. Under this Bill
the wild duck will be immune from the
1st March to the 12th August. The man
who shoots a wild duck between these
dates is no sport. Round about the 12th
July in this country men and boys go
out with guns and destroy the flapper.
That is of no benefit to themselves or to
the flapper. Give him to the 12th
August and he has some chance against
the gunner, and he gives the gunner great
pleasure, and a greater pleasure to eat.
I am, therefore, much pleased that the
wild duck is put into Category No. 3 by
the hon. and learned Senator. There will
thus be much more chance of bird life increasing in this country.
I shall support
the Second Reading of this Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: After the
interesting explanatory statement which
the hon. and learned Member introducing
this Bill has made, it only remains for
me to say that I welcome the introduction of this Measure. There are, however, some Amendments which I think
it will be necessary to make on the Committee stage, and perhaps I should just
mention two of these now.
In the first place, I think that Clause
4 might be omitted, because I do not
like the idea of putting the onus of deciding who is a suitable person to receive
the certificate mentioned in that Clause
on a District-Inspector of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary, for it will be his
duty, regardless of whether or not there is
such a certificate, to ensure that all birds
caught for sale alive are caught in accordance with the provisions of this Bill. In
other words, if we omit this Clause, it
will mean that any person wishing to
take birds alive for sale, can only take
those birds which are excepted from protection under Category I of the First
Schedule, and, further, he must, in doing
so, not contravene any of the provisions
of this Bill as to cruel methods of capture.
Again the bird, when it has been
captured, must be kept in a cage which
complies with the provisions of Section 3
of this Bill. In the circumstances I
think that it is better not to deal with
this matter by certificates, but to allow the
police to enforce the ordinary law, and
in any event the sale of birds in cages is
not a very large industry.
Again, I am advised that Clause
15, Sub-Clause 4, which deals with
Admiralty jurisdiction, is a matter
which we cannot re-enact in this Bill.
I think it would be well to omit this
Sub-Clause, as Sub-Section (6) of the
Wild Birds Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Act of 1880 only deals with a very few
offences and it is hardly worth while reenacting this provision. There are, in
addition, one or two minor Amendments
to which I am sure the hon. and learned
Member will agree, as they are more
drafting Amendments than anything else
and not matters about which there need
be any contention. Following as it does
the recommendation of the informal
Committee appointed by the Minister of
Home Affairs to consider the general
aspect of protection of wild birds in Northern Ireland, I feel sure that the Bill
will meet with the approval of all persons, whether they be ornithologists or
not, who are interested in protecting
bird life from the many evils from which
it may easily suffer.
I feel that this Bill, altering as it does
the whole system which has applied to
the protection of birds since 1880, is an
excellent one, for it is much easier for
the general public and also for those
whose duty it will be to enforce the provisions of the Bill to do so efficiently,
when they know that all birds are protected except some fourteen well-known
species, which are mentioned in the First
Part of the First Schedule, than it has
been in the past when various grades of
protection were given to long lists of
birds which appeared in the Schedules of
the various wild Birds Protection Acts,
many of which birds had hardly ever been
heard of and certainly never
seen in
Northern Ireland.
The position in the past has been
rather ludicrous inasmuch as adjoining
counties might be, and were in fact, protecting entirely different species of birds
and entirely different species of eggs, and
I am afraid that in some instances in
considering what species of birds should
be protected, the views of those who
were best in a position to know were not
always consulted. The informal Committee, to which I have previously referred, was composed of representatives
of all those interests who are or might
be affected by bird life, and I think that
in their recommendations and in this Bill
we find a very real advancement towards
legislation which may be both easily
understood and efficiently enforced.
This Measure has to my mind .one
great advantage, and that is that it consolidates the law in connection with the
protection of wild birds, and that we will,
in Northern Ireland, in the future have
only the one code to govern this matter
and in that code we find a complete
absence of references to preceding
statutes. I do not anticipate that this
Bill will find much opposition in any
quarter of the House, and I would like
to congratulate the hon. and learned
Member on the trouble to which he has
gone in drafting and consolidating the
law on this matter followed by the introduction of this Measure to-day. I
may say that the first legislation of this
kind of which I have got any information was introduced by Moses, as may be
read by the hon. and learned Senator
in Leviticus, where he lays down precise
terms as to how many eggs a robber of
nests may take and how many he must
leave behind. (Laughter).
Mr. LESLIE: Like the noble Viscount
I congratulate the hon. and learned
Senator on his very valuable contribution
to a matter in which all of us take a
considerable interest and which enlists
our sympathy. The hon. and learned
Senator has, if I may say so, laid before
us his view of the report of the Committee to which he has referred, and he
has consolidated some six or seven Acts
of Parliament. It seems to me that the
hon. and learned Senator's Bill has departed very seriously from some of these
statutes, and that it would take
further than I myself would wish to go.
I believe he has gone beyond what the
Committee did, in fact, recommend.
us
The main difference, as it seems to me,
is this. The Bill recommends a practically universal close season from 1st
January to 31st December for all birds
which are not excepted as being on the
black list or by some special provisions
in Category II, or those birds which are
given a close season to themselves, birds
not game birds but analogous to game
birds, such as wild duck, for which, I
am glad to say, there is now a chance of
getting more protection than there was
before. I do not think it can really be
the intention of the hon. and learned
Member that this universal close season
should extend against the whole world,
even including the owner of the
perty. He cannot surely mean that a
man shooting some wild birds or other in
his own demesne outside the ordinary
close season should be subject to the
apparition of a Royal Ulster constable
putting his head over a hedge and crying out, "Who killed cock robin?" That
cannot be the intention of the hon. and
learned Senator.
pro-
atte


Second Reading
Wild Birds Bill-
25 MARCH 1931

D.c
ding
I
If his Bill is enacted exactly as it
comes before us to-day it seems to me
that a man cannot catch a thrush in his
strawberry net, and a man cannot get
out of bed and shoot a corncrake that is
disturbing his rest. It also seems that
it includes our little friend the canary,
which is to be deprived of its statutory
right to a cage of suitable dimensions.
I am sure that is not what is in the hon.
and learned Senator's mind, but I am
afraid that is what he has done. He
has taken away from his canary the right
to have a proper-sized cage. Then a man
who has a trout stream or hatchery is
not to be able to shoot a heron if he
wants to. None of these things can be
done without first going to the Ministry
for a special certificate at a cost of 5s.
That surely cannot be the intention. All
we have to do is to define a little more
clearly what is intended by a close time.
Certainly I should not propose that
the Bill be rejected, very much to the
contrary, but I think it will require
very serious consideration in Committee.
When we get the Bill in Committee we
shall have to consider not only the question of a universal close time, good
against the owner of the land and everybody else, but we shall have to consider
such smaller matters as the fitting of an
Evinrude attachment to a boat going up
and down a river, the question of birds
taken out of cold storage, the importation
of birds for food, and the question of
casting on a man the duty of proving
his innocence, and so reversing the
usual practice. We shall have to deal
perhaps, though this is a very small
matter with such subtle suggestions as
that an officer of the Royal Ulster Constabulary is not a member of the Force,
and we shall have to pay particular
attention to that Advisory Committee,
because I am afraid as the Bill comes
before us the Advisory Committee proposed is one which would be set up to
control the Minister and would come between the Minister and Parliament.
However, those are matters which will
occupy our attention later on, but in
the meantime I should like to say a word
or two about that Admiralty Clause.
The hon. and learned Senator expressed
his willingness to amend the Clause as
being one not within the competence of
this Parliament, and I rather think the
noble Viscount agreed with him. I
venture to suggest that a little further
inquiry into that matter might be made.
I think perhaps the Miscellaneous Provisions (Northern Ireland) Act, 1928,
might be found to go some way in
smoothing over the difficulties experienced
by the hon. and learned Senator. It will
be remembered there are powers given
in that Act of Parliament of Northern
Ireland to repeal and re-enact Measures
which apart from that would not be
within our competence. I rather think
that perhaps if we can we ought to keep
Section 6 of the Act of 1880 alive, but
we must be careful we do not go beyond
it. There is a word or two in the Bill
which will have to come out.
Passing from all that, I should like
again to express my appreciation of a
Bill which consolidates, which avoids
legislation by reference, and which
gives us a uniform authority in these
matters for the Six Counties. I only
regret that the Title of the Bill prevents
me from directing further attention to
the question of snipe and woodcock
which were dealt with some time ago.
I support the Second Reading.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I would just like
to express my indebtedness to the hon.
Members who have spoken, particularly
to the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) who has just sat down, and to
the Leader of the House. I can assure
them that the very fullest consideration
will be given to the points raised on the
Committee stage.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn.
Perhaps I may say a word as to the business of the House. We have two Money
Bills for to-morrow-the Loans Guarantee
Bill and the Consolidated Fund Billwhich it is necessary to get through this
week. I do not like to jump a fence
before that fence is obviously near, but
the question will be to-morrow whether
we suspend Standing Orders and take
them all in one day, that is sitting on
Thursday only, or whether we sit on
Thursday and Friday. In any case it
shall be exactly as the House may wish.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five minutes before Six
o'clock.
Sittings of the House
Housing (Grants) Bill
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 26th March, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Commerce:
The Belfast Electricity (Extension)
Order, 1931.
Laid before the House (Pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
The following Motion stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Viscount
Charlemont :-
"That Standing Order No. XXV be
suspended for this day's sitting for the
purpose of taking the remaining stages
of the Loans Guarantee Bill and the Consolidated Fund Bill."
Viscount
CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): When the Motion on the
Paper was put down I had intended to
incur a certain amount of unpopularity
by moving it to-day. However, I would
much
Housing (Grants) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The two
main points in this Bill are, firstly, an
extension for a further year of the
housing subsidy payable by the Ministry
of Home Affairs on houses of smaller
dimensions which are suitable for the
working classes, and, secondly, a reduction in the amount of that subsidy to
£50.
over
In regard to the first of these matters,
hon. Members may recollect that when
the corresponding Bill which is now
about to expire was before this House
a year ago I gave particulars of
building operations which had taken
place up to that time and I said that
while those figures were highly satisfactory, there was evidence that the
need was still pressing and that the continuance of the subsidy for another year
would appear to be justified. Since
that time the number of such houses
which have been completed has been, I
am glad to say, very considerable. For
the year which ended 31st March, 1930,
it amounted to almost 5,000, and for
the year which ends this month there
will probably be about a similar total.
This is very gratifying, and there are
many indications that the shortage has
nearly been met.
At the same time the Government,
after full consideration of the matter, is
of the opinion that an extension of their
further year is
one
arrangement for
rather incur unpopularity tomorrow than to-day, and for that reason,
and as I think we shall have the Unemployment Insurance Bill from another
place in a few minutes, I will withdraw
the Motion to-day. Unfortunately, I
have to announce that I shall have to
ask the House to pass this Motion tomorrow in order to take the Unemployment Insurance Bill through all its
stages at to-morrow's sitting.
Solomon's Divorce Bill.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now
read the Third time."
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill, as
reported from the Joint Committee,
without Amendment.
justified and should help finally to solve
what has been a very difficult problem.
Another gratifying feature is that for
some little time past there has been
a tendency for rents to come down and
an additional supply of houses, such as
is hoped for through the continuance of
the subsidy to next year, should hasten
that very desirable object.
In regard to the second point, namely,
the reduction of the subsidy to £50,
there is the very good reason that the
present financial position does not admit
of a larger measure of generosity. It
has also to be remembered that there has
recently been a fall in the price of building materials, and after a full consideration of the whole matter the Government
is quite definitely of the opinion that it
would not be justified in asking for a
larger subsidy than this reduced amount
of £50.
26 MARCH 1931
Housing (Grants) Bill-

Second Reading
manner as
was £140 profit on each house that this
man built.
The second Sub-Clause in Clause 1 of
the Bill continues the powers of local
authorities to supplement the Government subsidy in the same
under the previous legislation. I hope
that this brief statement will satisfy hon.
Members that the policy of the Government is suitable both to the needs of our
working classes in the matter of housing
and also to the exigencies of the
financial situation. I beg to move.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill, but I may
say that I have not the same enthusiasm
for it as I had for the Housing Bill of
last year..
Some remarks made by
Senator Hill Dickson last year caused me
to make inquiries during the past twelve
months. The hon. Member made a statement to the effect that those people whom
the Government intended to derive
benefit from this housing subsidy did not
receive the benefit. In consequence of
that statement I kept my eyes and ears
open, and I discovered that the statement
was perfectly true. The Government intended that this subsidy should be used
for the building of good houses for the
working-class people, cheap houses that
the working classes could afford to live in
comfortably. The working-class people
were supposed to get the benefit of the
subsidy, but what did I find? I found
that they did not receive the benefit the
Government expected and intended them
to get. A great number of speculative
builders came into existence, and they
built houses and sold them at an actual
profit on the building of the houses, in
addition to which they had the full benefit of the subsidy.
Some time ago I was in conversation
with one of these builders, and the question arose of the criticism regarding the
high prices they were receiving for their
houses. This builder began to calculate
what it had actually cost him to build one
of these subsidy houses. He was selling
them at £370 each, and he reckoned up
a total cost of £360 each. He said, "I
have only £10 for my own profit." I asked
"During the time you were erecting these
houses you were receiving weekly wages,"
and he said he was. I then said, "You
forgot to mention the subsidy of £140-
£100 from the Government and £40 from
the Corporation." "Well," he replied,
"the Government and the Corporation
have given that to the builders as a kind
of bonus for us to build houses." There
Last year, when a similar Bill was before the House, I drew attention to the
question of the supervision exercised in
the building of subsidy houses. Outside
Belfast the supervision is very poor, and
a builder can put up any kind of a house
he likes. Some of the houses that have been
erected will be falling down in the course
of the next ten years. I know of some
houses that were built within the last
twelve months, and a few weeks ago the
ceilings fell to the floor and had to be
I think that when the taxrepaired.
payers and the ratepayers are paying
money in the form of a subsidy to assist
in the building of houses they should get
good value for that money, which they
are compelled by Act of Parliament to
contribute.
Another matter that has come before
my notice is the sewerage and drainage
of some of these subsidy houses. I, personally, know of four semi-detached villas
all drained into one six-inch drain from
the houses to the main sewer in the road.
There is a very poor fall, with the result
that the man-hole is always filled up and
drain rods have to be used. There should
be closer supervision over the drainage of
houses erected in country districts.
Another house of the large subsidy type
that was built several years ago was
erected on low lying ground. The sewer
in the road is too high for this house, in
fact, it is practically on a level with the
house. When heavy rain falls and the
water comes down the hill it drives all
the sewerage out of the grid, which is
burst open and the sewerage is sent over
the garden. Such a thing should not be
possible if there was proper supervision
during the erection of these houses so as
to ensure that the ground was raised high
enough for the drains to have a proper
fall into the main sewer.
One of the objects of the subsidy was
to provide work for the uenmployed, but
as a matter of fact it has not found very
much employment. This is because a
great proportion of the materials used in
the construction of houses has been imported into Belfast. Doors, gates, windows, and other such articles are coming
in from Sweden, and not long ago I saw
from the newspapers that building
materials were being imported from
Russia, where the wages paid are much
lower than they are here.
Housing Grants (Money)-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
one
[Mr. Barclay]
are
Then we have cement coming in from
Belgium and France, and inferior
cement. The fact of the matter is,
windowsills which
made of this
cement have the corners falling off before the houses are finished. Foundations
are put in of this cement, when we have
finest
of the
manufactories of
cement in Great Britain, down at
Magheramourne, and I am informed the
workers there are working half time. If
we are taxing our people, why not see
that the windows and doors and the
cement and that kind of stuff are produced at home, when we can produce
them. If we cannot produce them we
could go to a foreign country for them.
But when we can we should produce at
home and find work and wages for these
people who are living on the dole.
I hope in this coming year the local
authorities as well as the Government
inspector when he goes round inspecting
these buildings will take more interest
in the houses in future than in the past.
To my mind it is not enough for the
Government inspector to go round and
put a tape from sidewall to sidewall and
from gable to gable to see that there is
the specified number of superficial feet.
There should be proper supervision to
see that the houses are in proper condition. There are some places with one
down spout for four or five houses, with
the result that in the upper two houses
the water is running over the walls.
The fact of the matter is, a gentleman was telling me the other day that
he took a house built within the last
twelve months and he had to leave it for
the water was coming in at the back and
going out at the front.
These are things that should be looked
after. The country is paying this and
the country should get value for the
money. If these speculative builders
and profiteers get away with this kind.
of business the poor working man has got
to pay the penalty in years to come. I
support the Bill, but not SO wholeheartedly as I did in the past.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The hon. Senator
who has just sat down has made a very
challenging speech with regard to the
Bill, giving very good reasons, to my
mind at least, why this Motion should be
rejected, and if he takes the logical
course, which I think is to take a
division of the House on this Bill, he can
met.
The
perhaps count on my support.
noble Lord who introduced the Bill
speaks of the shortage of houses in Northern Ireland as having been very nearly
I venture to think that is a very
optimistic view. I wonder what figures
the noble Lord relies upon which enable
him to put forward a statement so clear
as that statment is. He also states that
the tendency of rents is to come down.
Again I wonder has he any general
figures for this Province which would
give any real foundation for that opinion
hazarded by the noble Lord.
This Bill means that the Government
is coming to journey's end as builders or
part builders of houses in Northern Ireland, and you are doing so at a time
when very great numbers of the better
paid working classes have not yet been
properly housed. I venture to say that
one of the great English cities has built
as many subsidy houses as have been built
in the length and breadth of Northern
Ireland. We know perfectly well that
building by private enterprise at the present time is utterly impossible as an
economic proposition. It cannot be done.
Decent comfortable houses fit for human
beings to live in cannot be built at the
present time, and may not be built for
a long time by builders in the ordinary
way of making profit. And yet the
State is settling down and going at half
speed. I take it this Bill is only a preliminary to another Bill this time twelve
months by which the subsidy will be discontinued altogether.
The tragedy is that the class of
the community that most need the subsidy houses and most deserve the subsidy
houses have never been touched at all by
this housing scheme. They remain to-day
as they were before the war. They are
out in the slums. The subsidy is going
to cease and they are to remain in the
slums. That is the be-all and end-all of
the housing policy of the Government.
Mr. McCORKELL: I rise to support
the Bill, but there is one remark of an
hon. Senator that I cannot allow to pass
without challenge. The remark was
that outside the city of Belfast the supervision of these houses was poor. That
is not so in my experience. In the city
of Derry supervision has been excellent,
with the result that this Act has been
a great boon and done a great deal of
good, and so far as supervision is concerned we have a supervision second to
121 Housing (Grants) Bill-

26 MARCH 1931
Second Reading
none. Undoubtedly in some country
districts it is bad, but I think there is
a certain remedy which I need not waste
time by labouring.
Mr. LESLIE: Like others, I rise to support the Second Reading of the Bill, and
in doing so I cannot but agree that the
Bill is in strict conformity with the policy
developed generally of supplying houses
for the poorer classes, which was not only
in the earlier Housing Acts, but also in
the method by which rents have been decontrolled. It seems to me that the continuance of the policy of a subsidy, even
at a reduced amount, is exactly on the
same footing as keeping the smaller houses
later as regards decontrol.
The question of the supervision of houses
has been referred to by my hon. Friend
on my left, and I think I am right in
saying that that question has been raised
before in this House on the earlier
Housing Bills. I should like to be sure
on this occasion that supervision will not
begin when the house is completed, not
even begin when the house is begun, but
that something will be done with respect
to the inspection of the site before building is commenced. That is a new point
put forward by the hon. Member. Then
there is no compulsion on the local
authorities whatever to give any consideration to these houses, either by way of
additional subsidy, loan, or exemption for
a period from rates. The local authorities
have all these powers, but they are not
compelled to exercise them. And, therefore, if they wish they can avoid expense
falling upon the ratepayers in that connection.
If that is so, if the local authorities
have power to give these further concessions the local authority has, as it seems
to me, where the need arises, the right of
doing something towards meeting another
point raised by my hon. Friend, and that
is providing for the use of local or semilocal material. It seems to me there
should be no great difficulty. Perhaps I
had better not mention a name of a town
in Antrim providing for the use of Antrim
cement, and so on.
There is yet another point. I presume
I am correct in imagining that the provisions of this Bill are in a way without
limit, that is to say, Parliament is not
providing for any definite sum for the
purpose of this Bill. Any house, as I
understand it, that will comply with the
statutory conditions will qualify for the
subsidy, whether it be in Belfast or
whether it be in the country districts. It
is rather important at the moment to
make sure that no undue share of the subsidy money goes to the larger cities of
Belfast and Londonderry. I do not suggest favouritism or anything of that sort.
I say no undue share to the towns as
opposed to the country, having regard to
the fact that it is for reasons of economy
the declared policy of the Government to
suspend for three years the operation of
the Labourers Act, which provide assistance in the matter of providing houses
for agricultural labourers. I think it is
fair to ask for some little assurance upon
that, not necessarily now, but at all events
before we finally part with the Measure.
I think we ought to be told something
about that. On the whole, I support the
Bill, which continues the good work which
has already been begun.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise with very great
pleasure to support the Bill, I think with
greater pleasure than my hon. Friend on
my left (Mr. Barclay). By the Housing
Act which the Government introduced
some time ago the people undoubtedly
benefited. Builders have been referred
here to-day by Senator Barclay
all alike.
if they were
Now, I
think that is not fair. There are honest
builders in Belfast, and I can give a name,
the builder who built a house for me not
very long ago. There would be no necessity to visit that house from the first brick
was laid until it was finished.
to
as
Mr. BARCLAY: I said supervision
outside Belfast.
Mr. QUIGLEY: You meant outside
Belfast, but you spoke generally.
Well,
even outside Belfast I am sure there must
be honest builders. In regard to the Bill
before us, what pleases me most of all is
the fact that the Government is going to
do now what they have neglected all along
-to provide houses for the working-class
people. That has really been neglected,
and I think it is a great shame that it
has been. The houses which have been
erected are not within the limit of the
ordinary working-class man at all. But
now I am pleased to see that they are to
be "the kitchen or small parlour type
with a minimum of five hundred superficial feet in rural districts, and five hundred and fifty superficial feet elsewhere."
I hope close supervision will be given to
these houses and that they will be of the
Housing (Grants) Bill-
Second Reading
'HE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
best type for the working people entitled
to them. I also hope that the rents will
not be such that the working-class cannot pay. I would say that the rents of
the houses should not be above six or
seven shillings a week, because the working man cannot pay more with the wages
he is paid.
The Government should see that only
this class of house is built, and that the
builders who contract to build the houses
should show no delay, because there are
hundreds of people in Belfast and elsewhere in need of such houses for themselves. In a great many cases there are
people living in rooms, and they are only
living there because they cannot afford to
pay the rents demanded for houses. Of
course, there are some people in rooms
and you could not possibly get them out
of them. People will undoubtedly get
the benefit of the subsidy if a reasonable
rent is asked. That is an important point
in connection with this Bill-that the
rents of these houses should not be more
than 6s or 7s per week. I am satisfied,
as I have already said, that we have
builders who will erect decent and
satisfactory houses. The builder in Belfast to whom I referred has put up quite
I have great
a lot of subsidy houses.
pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of the Bill.
Mr. McHUGH: I think the contractors and builders in Belfast are
entitled to shake the hand of the hon.
Senator who has just sat down. It is the
first time I have heard of the contractors
and builders in Belfast being so honest
and upright. For many years they have
been tainted, and at last we have found
one honest builder.
Mr. QUIGLEY: He deserves it.
Mr. McHUGH: The previous speaker
(Mr. Barclay) has shifted his position as
regards the question of jerrybuilding in
Belfast by stating that all this was in
the country.
Mr. BARCLAY: Outside Belfast.
Mr. McHUGH: That means the
country. My experience is that proper
and well-built houses are being erected
in the rural districts. Proper sanitary
arrangements are provided in accordance
with the specification for each house.
Mr. BARCLAY: That is in Fermanagh.
Mr. McHUGH: Just in Fermanagh.
Mr. BARCLAY: There are five other
counties.
Mr. McHUGH: I am not speaking of
any place except that which has come
under my observation. It is very
pleasing to be able to state that there is
one county in which honest people live.
The principal reason why I rose to speak
was with reference to the 500 superficial
feet which relates to the rural districts.
These houses are only little box houses.
The parlour is one of those places you
would almost prefer to be out of when
a friend comes. There is no room in the
small bedrooms. It would be impossible
to have proper health in them. The
Government went so far last year as to
allow 600 superficial feet.
Mr. BARCLAY: 700.
Mr. McHUGH: 600. The maximum
is 750 feet. While they compel the rural
districts to build a house of 500 feet they
allow 550 feet elsewhere.
Are persons
in the rural districts allowed to build
houses of 550 superficial feet?
Viscount CHARLEMONT indicated
assent.
Mr. McHUGH: If that is so that
clears the air, because it states distinctly
here 500 feet for the rural district
and 550 feet elsewhere. The rural
people should have the same liberty to
build the same class of house as in the
city. If they can then I have no objection whatever. I quite agree with the
previous speakers that these subsidy
houses are a good thing. It is a scheme
that encourages people to build. What
is really wrong about the whole thing is
that the Government has not courage
enough to say, "You shall not charge
more than so much per week for any of
these houses built under the subsidy
scheme." They should allow no man to
charge outrageous rents. The hon.
Senator (Mr. Barclay) mentioned one
case where a man made £140 profit out
of a single house. All this thing could
be made right by the Government
having courage enough to insert a small
section that no person shall charge any
125 Housing (Grants) Bill-

26 MARCH 1931
Second Reading
rent beyond a certain figure. That would
remove all the grievances we have heard
this evening. Personally, I have great
pleasure in supporting the Bill. If the
Clause I have referred to is inserted it
would be much better.
Mr. DORMAN: I feel placed under
rather awkward circumstances with regard to this particuar Measure. I am all
out for the purpose of supporting the
Government in every legitimate effort they
can make for the purpose of housing the
working classes, but I do not think that
the system at present carried on is such
as ought to be supported in the interests
of the life, health and well-being of the
working classes, mainly owing to the limitation of the cubic space set apart for the
dwellings for which the subsidy is proposed to be given by the Government and
local authorities. I think I am right
in saying that if these houses were
properly measured as to cubic space,
and compared with the minimum already
set out as necessary under the Public
Health Acts, it would probably be found
that four-fifths of the working classes with
families who go to reside in any of these
smaller houses of 500 superficial feet would
necessarily be forced to live in violation of
the provisions of the Public Health Acts.
I think the Government will find that the
amount of space made compulsory for
those housed in our gaols is 50 per cent.
greater than is provided in most of these
houses.
The primary object of all production is
to feed, clothe, and house the community.
I therefore take the opportunity of congratulating the Government, as I did previously in relation to several Measures in
Northern Ireland, on their having been
forced to acknowledge the absolute failure
of individualistic effort and competition to
supply the primary needs of the community from day to day. This muchlauded competitive system, which the Government and its supporters support, has
absolutely failed to satisfy these primary
requirements of the community.
They
must necessarily now step in and grant
State aid to the individualistic system that
has failed in providing necessaries for the
people.
On the principle that a half loaf is
better than no bread I feel compelled to
support this Measure, but I do think that
the necessary minimum cubic space ought
to be set apart. I really believe from
this point of view if a scientific examination were made of the working-class dwellings of Belfast-especially amongst the
numerous one-room dwellers, who have,
through the Government's decontrol of
the more valuable property, been forced
down to live under conditions and to pay
rents for single apartments that I know
of, and to which I drew the attention of
this House before. In numerous instances
inside the borough of Belfast there are
five, six, and seven human beings living
inside the four walls of single apartments in which they have to carry on all
the necessary work of the home-cooking,
washing, and drying of clothes, and so
on-and as I say I believe that if a scientific
examination of the homes of the working
classes were made to ascertain what proportion are sleeping in apartments which
constitute a violation of the Public Health
Acts it would be a revelation to the citizens
of Northern Ireland, and certainly the
work would be pushed on more rapidly
in order to house the people of the area
properly.
The
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I intend to support the Second Reading of
this Bill. At the same time, I do feel
sorry that the Government have found
it necessary to reduce the subsidy. By
that subsidy there is no doubt that local
authorities have been able to provide our
working-class people with houses.
Belfast Corporation, I believe, have been
successful in producing what is the best
and most suitable house for the workers
up to the present at the lowest possible
rent. Under the last contract completed
under the supervision of the Corporation
they have been able to let the housesin some cases, indeed, not only did they
let them, but the tenants become proprietors of those houses at the end of twentyfive years by paying a rent of 8/6 a week.
Those houses are built in a very healthy
locality with any amount of air space,
both at back and front, and the Corporation have had demands for that class of
house far in excess of the number they
are able to provide.
If it had been possible for the Government to have continued the larger subsidy
of £100 I believe the Corporation of the
City of Belfast could have met the demand, but with the reduced amount I am
afraid they will not be able to do so. I
happen to be somewhat interested now in
the Estates Committee of the Corporation.
We asked our supervisor the other day to
see if it would be possible to erect and to
Housing (Grants) Bill-
Loans Guarantee Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
let houses at a similar rent under the reduced subsidy, and we were told it would
be impossible, so that that scheme will
have to be stopped. That is one of the
reasons why I feel sorry that the Government were not able to continue the larger
subsidy for a further period. We recognise that economy is necessary, and on
that ground I suppose we have no reason
to complain. The Government know
exactly what the position is, and the local
authorities are similarly situated, but, as
far as the housing of the working-class
people of the city of Belfast is concerned,
I believe that the Corporation have gone
a considerable length to meet the demands.
I understand that the Government intend to promote a Bill in the very near
future for slum clearance and also for
accommodation in
providing suitable
dwellings for those people who are disturbed. For the present, at any rate, I
must say that this subsidy has served its
purpose, and has relieved the terrible
congestion and the tremendous want which
the workers would have felt if it had not
been for the Government stepping in and
giving the subsidy which they did to enable the local authorities to provide suitable houses. I certainly intend to support
the Second Reading of the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is a
matter of regret to the Government that
financial exigencies compel the reduction
of the subsidy to £50. At the same time,
I should like to point out that since the
subsidy was instituted some 22,000 houses
have been built with its assistance, and of
these nearly 13,000 have been of the
small type. It is not possible, I think,
for any Government to give a subsidy to
builders, and then to dictate what rents
they should charge. That would really
be control of rents, and I do not think
at the present juncture such a thing would
be possible. At the same time, I can
legitimately hope that with the addition
of 22,000 houses-and possibly more in
the next year or two-economic conditions
will force a reduction of rents and produce more houses for the working classes
to live in. I have been asked two definite
questions with regard to the operation of
this Bill, and I would like to give the
information asked for by the hon.
Senators concerned at a later stage.
On Question, Motion, "That this Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Unemployment Insurance Bill, to which
they desire the concurrence of this House.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read
a First Time"--(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Loans Guarantee Bill,
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second Time."
The main object of this Bill is to continue for a further year the facilities
which have hitherto been afforded under
this legislation.
assistance.
The principal advantages secured by
our Loans Guarantee Acts, as hon. Members are aware, have been to obtain
orders for our shipyards, and in this they
have certainly been of the greatest
There has been some talk in
another place, and outside, as to loans
which were guaranteed to certain shipowning companies and which were not
met on the dates on which they were due.
It does not follow, however, that our
Government is going to be at any
eventual loss in this respect, and it is
considered that the arrangements for continuing these facilities for a further year
are fully justified.
There are also in the Bill some modifications of the financial arrangements for
the guarantees. Altogether the Measure
is entirely a financial one and, of course,
money details are not subject to amendment by this House. Therefore, without further ado, I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I seems to me that this
Bill if passed-and the House will undoubtedly pass it-will have the effect
of leaving permanently at the disposal of
the responsible authorities of the Government the sum of £13,000,000 or rather
the power to guarantee a sum of
£13,000,000, which is not quite the same
thing, from this time forth and for evermore. The Bill, as I understand it,
seems to depart a good deal from earlier
practice, which was to limit the amount
of the guarantee and not to allow the
money that has once been met-the suit-
129 Loans Guarantee Bill-

26 MARCH 1931
Second Reading
LG

able expression will be found in the Bill
to be money in respect of which the
liability of the Ministry of Finance has
ceased to be let out again. If I understand the matter correctly that is the
effect of the Bill before us. There will
be a distinct provision that, in the future
at any time, there may be a guarantee
of £13,000,000. In the past there has
only been power for a maximum guarantee of some sum less than £13,000,000,
decreased by the expiration of guarantees.
I do not know whether the noble
Viscount will wish to make any further
statement on the matter, but the decision
come to earlier this afternoon will afford
him ample opportunity of expanding the
information which has been given.
On the more general question of some
loss being entailed by this guarantee, I
think I am right in saying that up to
