date only one loss has actually been
experienced and that that was a very small
matter. I am not sure that the figure
I am going to mention is not even beyond
the facts, but it has been suggested that
there has been a loss of a sum of £40,000.
The House can work out for itself what
percentage £40,000 represents of the
money which Parliament has given the
Government power to guarantee. I think
they will find it considerably less than
one half of one per cent., and they can
set on the credit side, so to speak, the
amount of wages which were paid in the
district concerned during the period of
that guarantee, and the amount which is
indirectly saved to the Unemployment
Fund by reason of the work made available in consequence of that guarantee.
If that is the only case that can be citedand I believe it is-where there is real
reason to believe that there will be a loss,
I think it cannot be said that during the
years that this system has been in
operation it has worked otherwise than
with extreme advantage to the Province.
Therefore, I do not think that, in spite
of the alterations which I have suggested
as having taken place in the system, the
House will wish to refuse its consent to
the passing of the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will give
the necessary explanation for which the
hon. and learned Senator has asked at a
later stage.
On Question, Motion, "That this Bill
be now read a Second Time," put, and
agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill be now committed" (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion negatived.

Housing (Grants) Bill-
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Friday, 27th March, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. HILL DICKSON
(Deputy-Speaker) in the Chair.
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That
Standing Order No. XXV. be suspended
for this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking the remaining stages of the Unemployment Insurance Bill."
In moving this Motion I should like to
say that, contrary to general feeling, I
am not in favour, as a general rule, of
the suspension of Standing Orders, but in
the present case I think the advantages
outweigh the disadvantages. The Bill
does not introduce any new principle of
unemployment insurance, and it is purely
a Money Bill. Hon. Members will have
learned from the speech on the Second
Reading in another place that it is desirable the passage of the Bill should be
facilitated because of the shortage of the
funds in hand, and it is important that
the Royal Assent should be obtained as
soon as possible. Therefore, I think it is
desirable the Measure should pass through
all its stages in this House to-day.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not like the
suspension of Standing Orders, because I
think that such a proceeding is scarcely in
conformity with the dignity of this House,
unless it is a case of some grave or particular emergency. I very respectfully
enter my protest against such an abuse of
the privileges of this House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
M'Cleane's Divorce Bill.

Committee was agreed to without Amendment.

133 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

27 MARCH 1931
Third Reading
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The objects
of this Bill are to raise to £2,500,000
the borrowing powers of the Northern
Ireland Unemployment Fund, and to
extend for six months the period for the
payment of transitional benefit.
Under the Reinsurance Agreement with
Great Britain, the Unemployment Fund
of Northern Ireland has to carry a deficit
amounting to its insured population proportion-about 2.2 per cent.-of the de-
The
ficit of the Great Britain Fund.
increase of the deficit on the Great
Britain Fund has recently led to its borrowing powers being increased from
£70,000,000 to £90,000,000, and as the
parity deficit of the Northern Ireland
Fund has continued to increase proportionately with the deficit in Great Britain,
it has become necessary to extend the borrowing powers of the Northern Ireland
Fund.
These powers at present are limited to
£1,750,000, an amount which will last
only until the middle of April, 1931.
The extension of the borrowing powers to
£2,500,000, proposed by the Bill, will
enable the Fund to be financed until early
in November, 1931.
commences
The extension by six months of the
period for the payment of transitional
benefit is designed to keep the position.
for the payment of such benefit unchanged
until the Royal Commission on Unemployment Insurance has reported on the
matter and opportunity has been afforded
for consideration of its recommendations.
Under existing legislation transitional
benefit
18th
to expire on
April, 1931, and comes entirely to an end
on 17th April, 1932. The Bill provides
for the postponement of each of these
dates by six months. The cost of this extension, including the administrative cost
of transitional benefit, is estimated at
£237,000, of which £167,000 will fall in
the year 1931/32, and the balance of
£70,000 in 1932/33. This cost continues
to be borne by the Exchequer.
Under the Bill the administrative cost
of this additional transitional benefit,
amounting to £15,000, will be included in
the total amount which the Fund pays to
the Exchequer as an appropriation-in-aid
of the administration expenses of Unemployment Insurance. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committed."(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: I had proposed on the
Second Reading to ask the noble Viscount
for a little more information as to the
finance of this Measure. As I have been
able to pick up the facts, we are raising
the limit by £750,000 in order to meet an
expenditure of, I think, £267,000. At all
events, the actual figure does not much
matter. What I did want a little
information about was where the money
was coming from to meet this amount,
whatever it might be; how much we might
expect to recover under the agreement
with Great Britain which is referred to in
the first Clause, and how much we might
expect to fall as a direct burden on our
own taxpayers. I should be glad of some
further information on that point, although it is not a matter for which
responsibility, in the first instance at all
events, rests on this House. Still, the
constant drain on our resources of these
various forms of benefit, whether they are
called transitional or whatever other name
may be thought appropriate, is a matter
which is causing great concern in the
Province owing to the fact that the
Province does not, unhappily, possess a
bottomless purse.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not in
a position, I am afraid, to give the information asked by the hon. and learned
Per-
Senator at this particular moment.
haps he would kindly get over the difficulty by putting down a question on the
Paper on the matter, which I could answer
at a future date.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
"Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Questions to Minister.
PROCEEDINGS AGAINST DEFAULT-
ING TENANTS.
Mr. DORMAN asked the Leader of the
House whether the Government will consider the advisability of introducing
legislation immediately to allow of proceedings against defaulting
tenants
whose rent is Ten Shillings per week or
under being taken in a Court of Summary Jurisdiction, and thereby save the
tenants and houseowners the unnecessary
expense of having to take such cases into
a higher court.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I may say
at once that this matter is one which
has been under consideration for some
considerable time, and has indeed been
discussed in this House on more than cne
occasion. The desirability of effecting
a change of jurisdiction in the direction
indicated by the hon. Member is not,
obvious as would
however, quite so
appear at first sight, and it is open to
question if the increased facilities for
ejectment, which would be afforded by
such an arrangement would not ultimately be found to prove a source of
greater hardship
hardship than the existing
system. Any alteration in the law,
which might possibly have such a result,
naturally requires very careful consideration.
The point is one, however, which has
been dealt with by the Departmental
Committee which sat to consider the
Landlord and Tenant Acts generally,
and that Committee has recommended
that, subject to jurisdiction in such cases
being confined to resident magistrates,
it should be increased to the extent re-
The
ferred to by the hon. Member.
question of the jurisdiction of justices is
one in regard to which we all know there
is a great diversity of opinion. Apart
from other considerations, it is clear that
any such arrangement would throw a
considerable amount of additional work
upon the resident magistrates, possibly
necessitating an increase in the establishment, and the present moment, when
every effort is being made to effect
economies, is hardly a favourable
occasion for proposals which might have
the result of increasing expenditure.
I can, however, assure the hon. Member that the question which he has
raised will receive the most careful consideration in connection with the recommendations of the Departmental Committee to which I have referred, but I
am not at the moment in a position to
anticipate the decision of the Government in regard to this particular recommendation.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think Senator
Dorman has again rendered service
by drawing attention to this matter.
The noble Leader of the House is,
I think, slightly in error. Doubtless the
Departmental Committee he referred to
unanimously recommended an increase in
the jurisdiction of the Petty Sessions
Courts to deal with these cases. I
happen myself to have been a member of
that committee, and what the committee
did recommend was, that in the case of
Oral Answers
Housing (Grants) Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
poorer dwellings, the landlord should
be free to pursue his remedy in the
Petty Sessions Court in all cases where
the rent did not exceed 10/- a week. The
committee differed in their views as to
whether the Petty Sessions Court should
be constituted as an ordinary Petty
Sessions Court, or solely of resident
magistrates. The majority of the committee recommended that the court
should be constituted of resident magistrates, while the minority took the view
that the ordinary justices would be quite
competent to deal with cases of this kind.
Apart from that question, the law, as
it at present exists, bears very heavily on
poor tenants and also on landlords who,
when they seek remedies in County Courts,
get their decrees for possession and get an
order for costs, only to find often it is not
worth the paper it is written on. The
costs may amount to £6 or even £7 in
some cases, and the Petty Sessions
Courts, which are our cheapest tribunals,
would have this merit: that there would
be a very great saving of expense to
both sides in the majority of cases to
which Senator Dorman has referred.
hope this question will not be lost sight.
of, and I regret that this particular part
of the recommendations of the Departmental Committee, that the jurisdiction
of Petty Sessions Courts should be enlarged to include ejectment cases where
the rent did not exceed 10/- a week, has
not already been given effect to.
COURT OF REFEREES
REGULATIONS.
I
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that the regulations for a Court of
Referees under the Unemployment Insurance Acts prescribed that, when the
tribunal proceeds to deliberate as to
whether or not an unemployed claimant
has established a claim to benefit, it may
order all persons not being members of
the court to withdraw; whether in any
instances a Court of Referees has ordered
all persons, including the claimant's representative, to leave, but has allowed the
appeals officer of the Ministry of Labour
to remain during its deliberations, and,
if so, whether the Ministry considers this
procedure consistent with due observance
of the forms of the administration of
the law and a compliance with the
regulations.
I wish to say that I do not put this
question by way of criticism in the least
degree, but simply with a view to
strengthening public confidence in the
administration of the Unemployment
Insurance Acts.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The answer
to the first and second parts of the
question is in the affirmative. In regard
to the third part, the Minister of Labour
has been advised by the Attorney-General
that the procedure mentioned is proper,
and in accordance with the provisions of
the regulations.

Housing (Grants) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before I
formally move the Third Reading of this
Bill there were some matters which were
raised in the Second Reading debate, and
to which I now desire to reply. Senator
Barclay raised the question of supervision over the building of subsidy
houses. All houses in urban districts
are erected subject to the supervision of
the local authority's surveyor, and have
to comply with the local by-laws. In
rural areas where by-laws are in
operation similar supervision is exercised
by the surveyor. In areas where no
by-laws are in operation surveyors have
141 Housing (Grants) Bill-

31 MARCH 1931
Third Reading

been specially asked to exercise supervision. In all cases the drains, sanitary
work, and plumbing work, are to be laid
out and constructed to the approval of
the local authority.
It will be understood that it is
impossible for
for the inspectors of the
Ministry of Home Affairs to make
frequent inspections of any one house,
に having regard to the fact that houses are
being completed at the rate of nearly 500
per month, and in many cases are widely
scattered, involving a considerable amount
of travelling. The inspectors, however,
examine every house before it is certified
for payment, and draw attention to any
defects which may be discovered. It
may be added that, having regard to the
very large number of houses constructed,
the number of complaints received by the
Ministry has been comparatively few.
As regards sites, the Ministry's rules
require that the site must be suitable,
reasonably dry and free from liability to
flooding, and the local authority's surveyor is required to satisfy himself that
it is in all respects a suitable one before
he issues a certificate authorising the
building. In rural districts a certificate
is also required from the Medical Officer
of Health stating that he is satisfied that
the site is suitable for erection of
dwelling-houses, and that the proposed
arrangements for sanitation and water
supply are satisfactory.
a
The hon. Member also mentioned the
question of the use of local materials.
The Government is in full sympathy with
the principle of requiring the use of
home material or home manufactured
goods wherever possible, and, in
circular issued to local authorities, it has
already indicated that it is open to them
to give a preference to British articles.
It is, however, much more difficult to
control the operations of private builders,
but the Ministry of Home Affairs has
undertaken to draw the attention of
builders to the desirability of using home
manufactured goods to as great an extent
as possible.
Another matter which was raised was
the question of the rents charged for
these houses. In the Government's view
a reduction in the rents of dwellinghouses can only be brought about permanently by building a sufficient number
of houses to reduce the extreme competition for accommodation which has
existed since the war. In accordance
with this view the Government, from the
as
the
start, adopted the principle of placing as
few restrictions possible on
builders of subsidy houses, recognising
that restrictions-particularly as regards
the terms on which the houses might be
sold or let-would be likely to discourage
people from building.
The course of events has justified the
Government, as there is no doubt that
rents-although still too high-have
now reached a level considerably lower
than that at which they stood five or six
years ago. In Belfast, houses can now
be had at rents varying from 8/6 to 14/-
per week, according to the type and
situation of the house, and in many cases
these rents cover the purchase of the
house. In rural areas houses may be had
at rents varying from 3/6 to 6/6 a week,
according to the accommodation provided. If the rate of building continues
for another year, as it is hoped it will,
there should be a further fall in the
rents.
The final point is that mentioned by
Senator Leslie, who wished to make sure
that no undue share of the subsidy
money goes to the larger cities. I may
give him the assurance that he asks for,
that the country is not being treated less
favourably than the town in the matter
of housing. The subsidy under the Bill
is paid under exactly the same conditions whether a house is erected in the
country or in a town. I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read the Third
Time."
a
Mr. McHUGH: I am sorry the noble
Viscount has omitted to answer one
question that
on
was put to him
That was
previous stage of the Bill.
whether the people in the rural districts
are allowed to build houses up to 700
feet superficial area. It seems to be distinctly laid down in the Bill that they
are allowed to build houses of only 500
or 550 superficial feet.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think there is any difference between
town and country as far as this Act is
concerned.
Mr. McHUGH: There is no difference.
Mr. DICKSON: If I may intervene,
I think it clearly states that the minimum in the towns is 550 feet and the minimum in the country is 500 feet. But the
maximum in either town or country is
700 feet.
Sittings of the House
Adjournment-

THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
no distinction.
There is
On Question, Motion agreed to.

-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the

Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such time as the Speaker shall
direct."
Mr. LESLIE: May I ask whether the
noble Lord can give us any sort of idea
as to the duration of our holiday. After
all, this is the 31st of March and we
would rather like to know when we are
likely to meet again, whether the first,
second, or third week in April.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
be certain. I should think-of course I
do not like to bet-but I think we meet
again on the 21st. But on the other
hand, another place meets on the 14t
and it is possible that some business
might arise which would necessitate our
meeting on the 14th. I think myself
that the 21st is somewhat more likely.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

time as the Speaker shall
direct.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Belfast Electricity Extension Order,
1931.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That the
Special Order made by the Electricity
Commissioners for Northern Ireland under
the Electricity (Supply) Acts, 1882 to
1919, and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce under the Electricity (Supply)
Act, 1919, in respect of certain Townlands in the Rural Districts of Belfast and
Lisburn, in the County of Antrim, and
in the Rural Districts of Castlereagh,
Hillsborough, and Newtownards, in the
County of Down, be hereby confirmed."
This Special Order, granted by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce, will confer upon the Belfast
Corporation statutory authority to supply
electrical energy for public and private
purposes in certain townlands in the rural
districts of Belfast and Lisburn, in the
County of Antrim, and in the rural
districts of Castlereagh, Hillsborough and
Newtownards, in the County of Down.
The rules of procedure have been complied with and the Order, which follows
the usual form, is not opposed.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Coursing of Hares Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, “That
the House do resolve itself into Committee on the said Bill." In doing so,
perhaps, I may be permitted to add that
the Amendments on the Paper are
brought forward in consequence of what
was adumbrated on the Second Reading
stage. I hope it will be agreed that I
have kept my part of such bargain as
there was. I should like to say that I
have taken full advantage of the opportunity kindly given to consult certain
persons, and I have received the greatest
assistance, for which I return my
acknowledgments. I said that the conference would take thirty minutes. I
was unduly sanguine; it occupied nearly
forty. I should like with your permission.
to follow the precedent of the Criminal
Evidence Bill, 1923, and SO far as
possible take the consequential Amendments en bloc. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Certain premises to
be deemed to be kept for unlawful
coursing):
Any premises or place shall be
deemed to be kept, managed and used
for the purpose of unlawful coursing,
if it is proved that in, or in respect of
such premises or place, on any one
occasion since the commencement of
this Act-
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
(a) coursing has been conducted
without a permit under this Act
issued by the county inspector and in
force on the occasion of such coursing; or
(b) coursing has been conducted in
contravention of any regulation made
under this Act, or of any condition
under which a permit has been
issued by the county inspector; or
(c) coursing has been conducted on
any day not within the coursing
period, or
(d) any child under the age of
fourteen years has been admitted
while coursing was, or was about to
be, in progress.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
1, line 7, to leave out from "proved"
("If it is proved that") to the end of
line 22, and to insert "in respect of such
premises or place that,
that, on any one
occasion after the commencement of this
Act-
(a) coursing has been conducted.
without a certificate issued by the
Coursing Control Board under this
Act and in force on the occasion of
such coursing; or
(b) coursing has been conducted in
contravention of any regulation made
under this Act or of any condition
under which a certificate has been
issued by the Coursing Control Board."
The alterations proposed in
this
Clause are to substitute the Coursing
Control Board for the county inspector
as
the licensing authority with the
necessary alteration of the word "certificate" for the word "permit." Further,
the opportunity is being taken to dispose
of paragraph (c) which is not really
necessary, and to relegate the question of
the admission of children to the sphere
of regulations where, perhaps, it is more
appropriate. We shall deal with the
appointment of the Control Board at
later date. Meantime, I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Offences connected with
unlawful coursing), ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Permits for coursing in
enclosed spaces):
(1) If any person desires to obtain
a permit under this Act such person
must apply in writing to the county
inspector on or before the thirteenth
day of September next preceding the
coursing period for which the permit
is required, and must send with such
application a full description of the
premises or place in respect of which
the application is made, and an application fee of twenty pounds.
(2) The county inspector shall consider any such application and, if
satisfied that the premises or place
to which the application relates
are, or are about to be, laid
out and arranged in such manner
as to be suitable for coursing in conformity with the regulations made
under this Act, and if also satisfied
that it is in fact intended that any
coursing conducted in such premises or
place shall be conducted according to
law, shall issue a permit, subject to
such conditions, if any, as may seem
to him to be required. And where the
county inspector is not satisfied
aforesaid he shall refuse to issue any
permit.
as
(5) A permit issued under this Act
shall, in respect of coursing, have
effect during the coursing period next
ensuing and no longer:
Provided that no premises or place
in respect of which a permit under this
Act has been in force shall be deemed
to be kept, managed or used for unlawful coursing only because such premises or place are being kept up in
such manner as to be in readiness for
the coursing period next succeeding.
(7) Where an application for a permit under this Act has been refused or
has been withdrawn by the applicant
the Ministry may repay to the applicant for the permit such proportion of
the application fee, not exceeding onehalf, as the Ministry, having regard to
the circumstances of the case, may
consider fair and reasonable.
Mr. LESLIE: With your permission I
will take the first three Amendments to
Clause 3 together. They are purely consequential. I beg to move that the alterations to lines 15, 17, and 19 as on the
Paper be made.
Page 2, line 15, leave out "permit" and
insert "certificate."
149 Coursing of Hares Bill-

16 APRIL 1931
Committee
Page 2, line 17, leave out "county inspector" and insert "Coursing Control
Board."
Page 2, line 19, leave out "permit" and
insert "certificate."
Amendments agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, on page
2, line 21, to leave out from "and" ("is
made, and an") to the end of line 22,
and to insert "at the same time pay to
the Secretary of the Board the prescribed
application fee."
(2) The prescribed application fee in
respect of the first coursing period after
the passing of this Act shall be twenty
pounds, and in respect of subsequent
coursing periods such amount not exceeding twenty pounds as the Coursing
Control Board may prescribe in respect
of each such period.
The Amendment deals with the prescribed application fee. It was thought
on the Second Reading that the provisions
should be made a little more elastic, so
I am suggesting that in respect of
subsequent coursing meetings the amount
should be such sum as the Coursing Control Board may prescribe. It is impossible to fix a lower figure than £20 on the
first occasion. There is reason to hope
it may be possible to reduce the sum
at a later date. Hon. Members will
recognise that it is impossible to say what
custom, so to speak, will come to the
Coursing Control Board. It is difficult to
forecast the payment to the secretary, and
possibly an inspector, and so on. Visiting
the place will necessarily cost a certain
amount. I hope £20 will be found
sufficient. I beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I think no data
or evidence has been given to justify the
Committee in sanctioning the sum of £20
either for the first certificate or for any
subsequent certificate. After all, if we
compare this with other sports. Take
point-to-point meetings run by the Hunts
Club, which has been hunting hares
all year. So far as I know there
is no compulsion on them to deposit
with any authority the sum of £20 in
order to carry on point-to-point meetings.
There may be a reason for fixing the sum
of £20, but certainly no data or reasons
have been given to the Committee why
this sport should be, as I suggest,
penalised by that sum, because these clubs
are poor. They are all run or promoted
by poor and democratic men. I do not
see why they should be called upon to
pay down the sum of £20, unless we know
for what reason and how it is going to be
applied. I think some facts should be
given to the Committee.
Mr. LESLIE: I think if the hon.
Senator will look a little further on on
the Amendment Sheet he will see what
expenses will fall upon the Coursing Control Board. He will see that the Board
shall apply the application fees received
to the payment of expenses, and may retain and accumulate the balance year by
year. He will see that the Coursing Control Board may pay the members' and the
secretary's travelling expenses, and other
expenses reasonably incurred by members
of the Board. They also will pay any
expenses incurred by the constabulary,
and, further, a secretary and inspector
may be appointed and, of course, paid.
I mentioned when moving the Amendment that I did not think it would be
prudent or safe to suggest a lower figure.
Full power has been given to charge a
reduced sum on subsequent occasions.
Mr. McALLISTER: I am opposed to
this system which is analogous to minimum
fines. I think, in the first instance, the
amount should be fixed as a sum not exceeding £20, and not a hard and fast
sum of £20 as a minimum.
I suggest the
hon. and learned Senator should reconsider the question and not be penalising
sport.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to back up hon. Senator McAllister's remarks. I think a sum not exceeding £20
would meet the case.
Mr. LESLIE: If there is a general
feeling I do not see any objection to
making the alteration in the Amendment.
It will then read:
The prescribed application fee in respect of the first coursing period after
the passing of this Act shall be a sum
not exceeding twenty pounds, and in respect of subsequent coursing periods such
amounts not exceeding twenty pounds
as the Coursing Control Board shall
prescribe in respect of each such period.
Amendment, as amended, agreed to.

the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-

CLAUSE 4 (Powers of entry and
inspection).
(1) For the purposes of this Act any
officer or constable of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary or any person authorised
in writing by the Ministry or by the
county inspector may enter at all
reasonable times any premises or place
in respect of which an application for
a permit has been made, or a permit
has been issued under this Act, and
inspect such premises or place, and any
animal or thing therein.
(2) If any person obstructs or wilfully delays any such officer, constable
or person authorised as aforesaid in
the execution of any power as to entry
and inspection or conceals any animal
or thing with a view to avoiding such
inspection, such person shall be guilty
of an offence under this Act.
The following Amendments stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie:
Page 3, line 29, leave out from the
first "the" in line 29 to "may" in line
30, and insert "Coursing Control Board."
Page 3, line 32, leave out "permit"
and insert certificate.'
""
Page 3, line 32, leave out "permit"
and insert certificate.
""
Mr. LESLIE: These three small
Amendments are all consequential. I
beg to move.
Amendments agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Power to make regulations).
(1) The Minister of Home Affairs,
after consultation with the Advisory
Board, shall make regulations for the
purposes of this Act and, in particular:
(a) with respect to the issue of permits by county inspectors and for
attaching appropriate conditions to
such permits;
(b) with respect to the laying out,
arrangement and general management of any premises or place to be
used for coursing or any purpose in
connection therewith;
(c) for the exhibition of suitable
notices at the entrances
to any
premises or place used for coursing
indicating, amongst other things, that
a permit is in force;
Committee
Coursing of Hares Bill-

16 APRIL 1931
(d) for the maintenance of order
within such premises or place;
(e) for providing a reasonable interval between the importation of any
hare into such premises or place and
the coursing of such hare therein for
the provision of a reasonable length
of slip, and for sufficient and suitable
outlets for the escape of hares;
(f) for the humane destruction of
any injured hare; and
(g) for the keeping, and inspection,
of such records relating to any coursing in such premises or place as to the
Minister may seem fit.
this
(2) Regulations made under
section shall be laid before both Houses
of Parliament as soon as may be after
they are made.
Provided that if an address is presented to the Governor of Northern Ireland by either House within the
statutory period next after any regulations are laid before such House
praying that the regulations may be
annulled, the regulations shall thenceforth be void, without prejudice to the
validity of anything previously done
thereunder.
The following Amendments stood on
the Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Leslie :-
Page 4, line 1, leave out from "The"
to the end of line 6, and insert "Coursing
Control Board shall make general regulations for the purposes of this Act, and,
in particular:-"
Page 4 line 14, leave out "permit" and
insert "certificate."
Mr. LESLIE: Again these two Amendments are strictly consequential and relate to the same substitution as before.
I beg to move.
Amendments agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
4, line 23, after "for" insert "preventing
the wilful injury, molestation or subsequent coursing during the same coursing
period of any hare which has passed
through an outlet provided for escape;
and for."
This is a new Amendment, although it
has been in my mind for some time owing
to the very strong public sentiment in
favour of a hare which has once escaped
not being allowed to be coursed again.
There were difficulties in making the proposal in the original Bill, but since the
Bill received the Second Reading I have
received representations from more than
one quarter, particularly perhaps from a
quarter in which coursing is well understood, pointing out that it was a perfectly
practicable suggestion. Therefore, I have
put it on the Paper, and I can only say
that if the Committee is pleased to accept
this proposal I can imagine nothing in
the passage of this Bill that will give me
greater personal pleasure. One thing
everybody hates is the thought that a hare
that has run the gauntlet once and got
away should be recaptured and coursed
again. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
4, line 24, to leave out the word "and"
("hare; and").
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 4, line 27, leave out from "place"
to the end of the line, and insert:-"and
to the purchase of hares for such coursing
as the Coursing Control Board may seem
fit, and
(h) for preventing, so far as may be
practicable, the presence of any child
under fourteen years of age at any coursing in such premises or place."
Mr. LESLIE: I must move this
Amendment in two parts, because I think
some hon. Members desire to speak on
the second part. I therefore move in
the first place to leave out from "place"
to the end of line 27, and to insert, "and
to the purchase of hares for such coursing
as the Coursing Control Board may seem
fit, and;". The idea is that if we can
make regulations as to the purchase of
hares we shall be putting into our hands
a great weapon for the prevention of
what I may call pre-coursing cruelty to
the unfortunate hare. If we know where
the hares come from we can do, perhaps,
a good deal more. I therefore beg to
move the first part of the Amendment
up to the words, "fit; and."
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I now beg to move the
insertion of the second part of the
Coursing of Hares Bill-
THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
Amendment, namely "(h) for preventing,
as far as may be practicable, the presence of any child under fourteen years
of age at any coursing in such premises
or place." This refers to the question
of the admission of the child. It will be
remembered that when the Bill was first
introduced I tried to be rather more definite and to refuse permission for a
child to attend a coursing meeting at all.
That was objected to by the right hon.
Senator in front of me (Mr. Samuel
Cunningham), who gave the House reasons
for thinking if I do not misrepresent him
-that a child under fourteen ought to
be taken to a coursing meeting.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: Not
ought to, but might be.
Mr. LESLIE: Not ought to, but
might be. It therefore seemed to me
that it would be better to put the matter
with the regulations and to qualify it by
introducing the words, "so far as may be
practicable." I understand that coursing
often goes on in places from which you
could not very conveniently exclude a
child. You might refuse children admission at the turnstiles, if there are any,
but they might come through a gap in
the hedge. I think we ought to do something if we can to protect children under
fourteen from the associations which are
apparently inseparable from coursing
meetings, and if this proposal is objected
to the responsibility will not rest on me.
I beg to move.
Colonel PAKENHAM: We are dealing in this Bill with cruelty to hares,
and I think we would be going out of our
way to deal with children if we passed
this Amendment. I think we can quite
safely leave the question of the presence
or absence of children at coursing meetings to the parents of those children, and
I am going to vote against the Amendment.
Mr. PORTER-PORTER: If it would
be of any assistance to the Committee
I should like to say that I have been to
several of these country coursing meetings and I have seen only one child under
fourteen present.
Mr. LESLIE: As it would seem to be
the wish of the Committee that this
Amendment should be withdrawn, I will
not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
4, line 29, after the word "laid" to insert
"by the Minister of Home Affairs." As
the regulations are to be laid before
Parliament it is necessary to say who
shall lay them. The Minister of Home
Affairs will not now do it automatically.
I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
4, line 30, after "made" insert:
-
"If either House, within the statutory
period after any regulation made by the
Coursing Control Board under this Act
is laid before such House, resolves that the
regulation ought to be annulled, the regulation shall, after the date of the resolution, be of no effect without prejudice
to the validity of anything done in the
meantime thereunder or to the making
of a new regulation."
This raises a rather different question.
It is purely a formal matter. It is not
the practice when regulations are made
by some authority other than the Minister
to use the procedure of address to the
King for any correction Parliament may
desire to make. The important point is
to retain for Parliament such power of
review that it may wish to exercise, and
that is done by the arrangement before
us. Had the original proposal in the
Bill stood, there would have been no
necessity for this alteration. I beg to
move.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
page
4, line 31, to leave out the last paragraph
of sub-clause (1) (lines 31 to 37 inclusive).
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Advisory Board).
For the purpose of advising the Minister of Home Affairs on matters relating
to coursing there shall be established
an Advisory Board consisting of not less
than seven and not more than nine members appointed by the Minister of Home
Affairs and constituted as provided in
the schedule to this Act.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Committee
Coursing of Hares Bill-

16 APRIL 1931
Page 5, line 7, leave out from the beginning of the line to the end of line 12,
and insert :-
:-
For the purposes of this Act there
shall be established a Coursing Control
Board which shall consist of a chairman
and eight other members appointed by
the Governor of Northern Ireland.
(2) Such number of persons, representative of organisations established
for the promotion of the sport of coursing hares, whether in the open country
or otherwise, as the Governor of Northern Ireland may see fit, shall be appointed members of the Board.
Provided that the maximum number
of the Board shall not be increased by
virtue of anything contained in this
sub-section.
Mr. LESLIE: This is a more serious
matter. For the purposes of this Act it
was originally proposed that the Minister
of Home Affairs should appoint an Advisory Board. After what happened on
the Second Reading it was obvious that
that would require alteration, as I warned.
the House at the time. The proposal now
is that there shall be established a Coursing Control Board appointed by the
Governor of Northern Ireland and not by
the Minister, and therefore the necessary
provisions have to be made respecting
such a Board. Those provisions are taken
almost word for word from what is commonly called the Totalisator Act of 1928.
I brought the Act with me in case anybody should ask any question about it.
The details are of the most ordinary
character. It will be observed that in the
forefront of the battle, so to speak, it is
required that this Board is to include
such members of coursing organisations as
the Governor may see fit to appoint.
That means that instead of some political
body, which was first proposed, the bulk,
at all events, of this Board will presumably be composed of people who understand their business. That I think,
from what I can hear, is what meets with
most general approval, and it will commend itself to the general common-sense.
The Members of the Board are limited.
They must pay expenses. We have dealt
with that before. They must have a secretary. Some body must convene the first
meeting. They must deal with their fees,
and finally they shall annually make and
furnish a report of proceedings and
accounts, and that is laid before Parliament. That, I may remind the Committee again, follows the procedure in the
case I have quoted. I do not suppose
anybody desires that I should read the
actual extracts from the Act I am quoting.
I beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: My only objection
to this Amendment would be on the following grounds. After all, the first
constituted Advisory Board in the original
Bill established some safeguard. But this
committee will be appointed on the advice
of the Government, and Governments are
susceptible to influence and public pres.
sure. I should think that as the opponents
of coursing are sufficiently vocal they
could elect on this body a number of
members who will not facilitate or encourage coursing, and perhaps have a
crippling influence on the whole sport.
That is the objection to that Clause.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I think if the
hon. Senator reads on, in sub-clause 2 he
will see such number of persons, representative of organisations established for the
promotion of the sport of coursing. They
cannot be anti-coursing if they are promotors of coursing. I think sub-clause (2)
states plainly who are to be appointed.
Mr. McALLISTER: I do not think
that puts the position any further, because
it is such number as are approved by the
Governor. At the instance of the Government they can appoint a minority of one
or two really representative of coursing.
What influence would one or two have in
a committee of eight? I do not think
there is any protection at all.
remarks.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid, like my
hon. and gallant Friend, I rather fail to
appreciate the point of the hon. Senator's
I thought it was fairly clear
the Coursing Board is to consist of a
chairman and eight other members. The
Governor is to appoint the nine. Amongst
that nine there is a proviso that there
are to be such number of persons representing coursing organisations as the
Governor may think fit. You must
leave the Governor full discretion on the
matter. That is the idea in the Clause.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
5, line 12 (end of Clause) to insert:-
"(2) The Coursing Control Board
may pay to the Chairman, other members of the Board and the Secretary any
travelling and other expenses reason-
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
ably incurred by them as such members
or Secretary, and, as part of their
expenses, the Board may pay any expenses of any officer or constable of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary incurred by
such officer or constable in carrying out
any provision of this Act.
(3) The Board may appoint any
person to act as their secretary or as
an inspector on their behalf and may
prescribe the duties and remuneration
of any person so appointed.
(4) The first meeting of the Board
shall be convened by the Chairman.
(5) Subject to the provisions of the
Schedule to this Act, the Board may
regulate their own procedure.
(6) The Board shall apply the application fees received under this Act to
the payment of their expenses under
this Act and may retain and accumulate any balance accruing from year to
year and apply any such balance or a
competent part thereof in aid of the
expenses of any later year.
(7) The Board shall annually make
and furnish to the Minister of Home
Affairs a report of their proceedings,
together with an account of the moneys
received and expended by them during
the year, and such report and account
shall be laid by the said Minister before both Houses of Parliament."
This is really part of the preceding
Amendment. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 7 (Penalties).
(1) Any person guilty of an offence
under this Act shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding
twenty-five pounds, or alternatively, or
in addition thereto, to imprisonment
for any
term not exceeding three
months.
Act
(2) All offences under this
shall be prosecuted and all fines thereunder shall be recovered in manner
provided by the Summary Jurisdiction
(Ireland) Acts, and a person aggrieved
by a conviction or order made by a
court of summary jurisdiction on determining any information or complaint under this Act may appeal in
manner provided by the said Summary
Jurisdiction Acts.
(3) In any prosecution for an offence
under paragraph (d) of section one of
this Act the burden of proof that the
child was in fact over fourteen years
of age on the day on which the offence
was alleged to have been committed
shall rest on the person charged.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
5, line 25, to leave out lines 25 to 29
inclusive (sub-section (3)).
The matter of children having been
disposed of, these special provisions are
no longer required.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Particulars of conviction
or complaint to be specially reported).
(1) Particulars of any conviction
under this Act shall, and particulars of
any complaint not resulting in conviction may, be forwarded to the Ministry
by any officer of the Royal Ulster Constabulary who has knowledge, or has
received notice of, such conviction or
complaint, and the Minister may refer
such particulars to the Advisory Board
for consideration and report.
(2) The Advisory Board shall consider such particulars so referred to
them and after giving, if they see fit,
any person who appears to them to be
concerned an opportunity of being
heard, shall report to the Minister of
Home Affairs and shall state their
opinion as to whether or not any permit
issued under this Act ought to be withdrawn:
Provided that the Advisory Board
shall not report that any permit ought
to be withdrawn unless the person on
whose application such permit was
issued has been given an opportunity of
being heard before the Board.
(3) If the Advisory Board advises
him that any permit ought to be withdrawn the Minister of Home Affairs
shall have power to withdraw such permit and any permit so withdrawn shall
cease to be in force.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 5,
line 32, to leave out "Ministry" ("to the

from "complaint" ("conviction or complaint") to "Board" in line 37, and insert (2) the Coursing Control."

These are really again the same thing.

Mr. LESLIE: In page 5, line 38, I beg

Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, page 5,
line 40, to leave out from "heard" ("being
heard, shall") to the end of page 6,
line 10, and insert:-"may withdraw any
certificate issued under this Act.
Provided that a certificate shall not be withdrawn under this section unless the person
on whose application the certificate was
issued has been given an opportunity of
being heard before the Board.'
This again is consequential. The duty
of considering whether a certificate shall
be withdrawn now rests solely with the
Board. Originally the Board had to advise the Ministry. The necessary alteration is, I think, sufficiently indicated by
the proposal on the Paper.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I think this
Amendment would require some further
elucidation. The wording seems very
peculiar to me to put into a legal
Statute. It provides that the certificate
shall not be withdrawn unless the person
on whose application the certificate was
issued has been given an opportunity of
being heard before the Board. Heard in
what way? For what purpose? The
word "heard" conveys nothing. It gives
no direction to the Committee.
It is an
unusual expression in an Act of Parliament, and I think it is rather ambiguous.
It does not approach legal phraseology.
trouble.
Mr. LESLIE: I think if the hon. Member will look a little further back to
section 2 he will see that the distinction
arises in this way. The Coursing Control
Board may like to summon the inspector
or anybody else. That is a matter for
them. They may not put them to the
But they shall not withdraw a
certificate unless the person on whose
application the certificate was issued has
actually had an opportunity of coming before them. I think it is quite sufficient,
and I think the expression, with all due
deference to the hon. Gentleman, is perfectly appropriate. It is not heard by a
court, but by a Board. There is wording
in the Petty Sessions Act about courts
having heard, and so on. I venture to
suggest that the intention is sufficiently
plain, and that the words are appropriate.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Savings), ordered to

CLAUSE 10 (Interpretation).
In this Act where the context does
not otherwise require, the following expressions have the meaning hereby
respectively assigned to them, that is
to say: :-
"County Inspector" means the
county inspector assigned to the
county wherein the premises or place
in respect of which an application
for a permit has been made or a
question under this Act has arisen,
are situate, and, in the case of a
county borough, includes a commissioner or town inspector;
"Coursing" means the coursing or
hunting in any enclosed place, of
any hare wilfully driven, loosed, or
urged into, or liberated in such place
for the purpose of being therein
coursed or hunted;
"Coursing Period" includes any
day other than a Sunday or Christmas
Day, after the fourteenth (or in the
case of private trials the first) day of
November in any year and before
the first day of March next
following;
"Enclosed Space" means any premises or place enclosed, or partly
enclosed in such manner as to prevent
or hinder the escape therefrom of
any hare;
"Hare" includes "Rabbit";
"Unlawful Coursing" includes
coursing in contravention of any
provision of this Act or of any regulation made, or condition imposed,
by virtue of this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
6, line 20, to leave out lines 20 to 25
inclusive. That is, the definition of a

Amendment agreed to.
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Wild Birds Bill
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
6, line 31, to leave out from "the" in
line 31 to "first" in line 32.
This alteration is moved to meet representations that were made on the Second
Reading and outside this House.
It was
represented that the coursing period was,
perhaps, unduly short, and it was also
represented that, after all, there was no
great protection from the hare's point of
view in allowing trials to take place a
fortnight before meetings. Accordingly,
I am giving back to the applicants their
eleven days. It is only eleven days, because there will be two Sundays in it.
There will also be Armistice Day, which
I hope will not again be used for the purpose of coursing. It is a matter left to
decent feeling and not put in an Act of
Parliament. I think allowing coursing to
begin at the beginning of November is.
the proper date. It corresponds with the
date selected by harriers for commencing
operations. I think that is so. The
coursing meetings which attracted attention last year were both held before
November or 15th October. It was admitted in another place two days ago,
when discussing another Bill, that hares
were softer in October than later in the
year. I think it will be agreed that in
making this concession this House is going
as far as can reasonably be expected. I
beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 11 (Short title and citation)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
SCHEDULE (Constitution of Advisory
Board).
Mr. LESLIE: It is, first of all, necessary to amend the title of the Schedule.
Instead of constitution of Advisory Board
it becomes "Provisions as to the Coursing Control Board." I beg to move to
leave out lines 2 to 9, and insert "Provisions as to Coursing Control Board."
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 19,
to leave out "three" and insert "four."
Perhaps it will be thought that four members of the Board ought to form a quorum
instead of three, as originally fixed.
beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.

Wild Birds Protection Bill (S).
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this House do now resolve itself
into Committee on the said Bill." In
doing so I wish to say that many of the
Amendments on the Order Paper are
purely verbal and consequential, and I
ask leave to deal with them in the same
way as on the previous Bill. A great
many of the Amendments are due to compromises that have been made in conference to meet the objections of interested
parties.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Before Clause 1 to insert the following
new Clause :-
(1) Subject to the provisions of this Act
it shall be unlawful for
person:
any
(a) to kill, injure or take any bird
at a time when it is protected
under this Act; or
(b) except in the course of ordinary
agricultural operations or the like
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
16 APRIL 1931
to take or wilfully disturb the egg
or nest of any bird at any time
when such bird is so protected; or
(c) to kill, injure or take any bird or
take or wilfully disturb any egg
or nest on any Sunday; or
(d) knowingly to sell, or expose or
offer for sale, or have in his
possession any bird, or the skin or
plumage of any bird killed,
injured or taken, or any egg or
nest taken in contravention of this
section.
(2) For the purposes of this section:-
(a) all birds, other than those respectively specified in Part I or
Part II of the first schedule to this
Act, and the eggs and nests of
such birds shall be deemed to be
protected at all times; and
(b) the birds specified in Part II of
the said schedule, and the eggs and
nests of such birds shall be deemed
to be protected throughout the
close season.
(3) (i) Nothing in sub-sections (1) and
(2) of this section, other than the
provisions of paragraph (d) of subsection (1), shall be so construed as
to make unlawful any act or thing
done by the occupier of any land in
respect of any bird, egg or nest upon
such land:-
(a) on any day (other than Sunday)
after the eleventh day of August
in any year and before the first
day of March next thereafter followin; or
(b) with a view to the protection of
any person or property, at any
time whatever.
(ii) For the purposes of this subsection:-
(a) the expression "occupier" includes any member of the household, or servant of the occupier
and any person acting with the
written permission, and for the
purpose of protecting the property,
of the occupier; and
(b) the expression "property" shall
be deemed to include birds, crops,
eggs, fish, fruit, game, vegetables
and wildfowl.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to approach, and
to ask the Committee to approach, the
consideration of what I am glad to think
is the only point on which the hon. and
learned Member who has introduced this
Bill and myself are not in agreement. I
am glad that the accident of circumstances allows me to bring forward a new
Clause at the very commencement of
our discussion, because that will be found
to be most convenient, for whatever
happens to my proposed Clause, whether
it be accepted or rejected, there will be
a very considerable repercussion on the
Amendments on the Paper. Some in
one way and some in another will become
unnecessary, but I think there is a little
extra advantage in taking this Clause at
the beginning. The Bill claims to be a
Bill "to consolidate, simplify, and amend
the law relating to the protection of wild
birds," a fairly general title, and I think
perhaps it is more appropriate that we
should begin with a very general Clause
rather than start off on the side track
of the illegality of erecting a pole trap.
((
I venture to suggest that the hon. and
learned Senator has allowed himself to
be rather tied up and restricted by the
actual wording of the Report of the
Informal Committee which was, I understand, appointed somewhere about April,
1927, and that he has tied himself up a
little further in the terms of the Bill
which was once, and for all I know, now
still is before the English Parliament, but
has not yet become law. Then he has
added to his difficulties by introducing
the expression specially protected "
throughout the Bill. I am going to ask
the Committee, if they accept.
accept this
Amendment, to get clear of little difficulties of that kind. That, however, is
for the moment a side issue. I am going
to suggest to the Committee that the
alterations proposed in the Bill allow too
big a change to be made in a Bill which
attempts to combine Amendment with
consolidation, two things which do not
run well together in double harness.
that old maxim this is a good example.
Of
At the moment the occupier of land
has certain rights. He can kill at any
from
time throughout the year,
1st
January to 31st December, any bird
whatever that is not a game bird, and
which is not included in the lengthy Schedule to the 1880 Act. He can also kill
any bird whatever, whether in the Schedule or not, outside the close season. The
effect of the Bill would now be to require
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
a man to entertain on his property any
number of feathered visitors that might
come to him whether he wanted them or
whether he did not. It would allow him
to kill the blackbird which was damaging
the fruit trees and currants in his garden,
because the blackbird is placed in category 2, and can thus be taken by the
owner of the garden. But the owner of
the garden is prohibited from taking the
nest or eggs of the blackbird, even though
it be in his garden. He must wait until
the eggs are hatched out and the little
blackbirds appear, and then he may kill
them.
The Bill also gives to the water hen
more protection than is given to the swan.
It prevents the man who owns a trout
fishery destroying the heron unless he had
previously obtained a permit from the
Ministry of Home Affairs. A shepherd
could not shoot a raven preying on his
lambs, and, although a good deal is said
in favour of the raven, the books do still
teach that ravens do attack young lambs.
The occupier of land is prevented from
destroying the little owl if that bird
appears, and the Committee will remember
that it was to some extent the little owl
on which the hon. and learned Member
relied when moving Clause 9 of his Bill,
to the effect that foreign birds must not be
liberated without the consent of the
Minister of Home Affairs. If it should
happen again that the eagle or other large
bird of prey appeared in Northern Ireland
-and I am afraid we cannot accept as an
impossibility the entire cessation of the
migration of large birds of prey, even if
there are none in Northern Ireland at the
moment-but if the should happen to
appear and do damage they could not be
destroyed until they had got into a special
category under the Act.
A further effect of the Bill would be that
the Minister of Home Affairs would have
to establish an ornithological department
in order to deal with the numerous applications he would receive for exemption
under the Act, or for variations in the
categories. The effect of the Amendment
is to save the rights of owners, rights
which, I may venture to suggest, he does
not exercise in nine hundred and ninetynine cases out of a thousand, and does not
want to exercise as long as cartridges remain at their present price. But he has
got that right, and the proposal in the
Bill is to take it away from him, and the
proposal is made without any particular
support. It can only cause discontent.
I understand from a circular I received
this morning that the informal Committee
to which I have referred did intend in
their report-a report which I must remind the Committee has not been laid before Parliament, and has not been circulated to the public, so that the ordinary
person in Northern Ireland has no knowledge of the report-to protect universally,
subject to small exceptions, from 1st
January to 31st December.
I did not at first think that that was
what they did mean, because the report is
somewhat loosely drawn up, and we find
such expressions as "complete protection,"
"unprotected all the year round," and
"protected all the year round," without
any very clear distinction. We also find
a proposal that the lapwing-a bird, I
imagine, we are all anxious to protectshould get more protection than it does
in England. They are given complete
protection all the year round, and that
being so it is rather impossible to make
out from the report what it really did
mean. But it seemed fair to assume that
it did not mean to interfere with what
has been the law for so many years,
namely, that outside the close season an
occupier could do what he liked. I do
not think the acceptance of this Amendment will really make any difference to
the birds, but I do think it will avoid discontent. A man does not like to be deprived of a right without being heard.
It is not quite fair. The effect of the
Amendment also will be to abolish the
categories and to substitute what, if I may
say so, will be a far more simple and easily
followed Schedule. We shall get rid of
that "special" distinction, and we shall
not have to define the expression "specially
protected." We shall be able to get rid
of Clauses 5, 7 and 8, and we shall in short
make a simpler Bill, and we shall not be
passing a Bill which we shall not be able
to enforce and which would not command
support.
I have said the report has not been
published. I must go further and say
that no case has been made out by the
hon. and learned Senator for asking
Parliament to go so far as he has done.
He has not told us, and there is nothing
in the course of that interesting speech of
his in our reports to suggest that any of
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee.
16 APRIL 1931
the ordinary birds are in danger of extinction. There is nothing to suggest
there are not quite as many of them as
we want, and I am afraid that until the
public is satisfied there is really some
danger of the extinction of birds the
public will not be prepared to go blindly
beyond the protection which is already
given. The protection that is already
given is the old Act of 1880, reinforced by
subsequent legislation, and the possibility
of making orders. That possibility will
have to continue, and I think that, perhaps, that will be sufficient.
I do not
think that the acceptance of this Amendment would do other than improve the
Bill, but I do think that the rejection of
the Amendment on these lines, particularly as regards the necessity of getting
permission from the Home Office before
you can do certain things, would seriously
imperil the Bill. Therefore, I suggest to
the hon. and learned Senator and to the
Committee that it would be well to insert
this new Clause, which I now beg to
move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The acceptance of
the Amendment moved by Senator Leslie
would mean, I fear, the torpedoing of
the Bill. It would mean transforming
the Bill from a Wild Birds Protection Bill
to a Game Birds Protection Bill. Let me
say in a very few sentences what the
Measure does. It provides general protection in Northern Ireland for all birds.
It
provides also special protection in Northern
Ireland for about eight per cent. of those
birds.
I mentioned when moving that the
House go into Committee that most of the
Amendments were tabled as the result of
conferences and compromise between
people who approached the question from
different angles. The desire was to be all
things to all men. I have consulted many
authorities.
The early Clauses give protection to all
wild birds at all times against all persons,
against cruel methods of capture or killing, and protection to all captive birds
against cruel caging Clause 5 protects
all birds of every class against capture or
killing on Sunday. Clause 6 protects all
captive birds against shooting in competitions or exhibitions. General protection
for all birds, wild or captive, is in the
forefront of the Bill. Then come the
Clauses giving to most birds special protection, either all the year round or
during the close season, against being
killed, taken, or injured.
Senator Leslie's Amendment, in the
main, would leave free every landowner
and every occupier of land, and every
person having shooting rights over land
and their household, servants and agents
to kill or take any bird on their land on
any day except Sunday during the open
season-from 12th August to 1st March,
and leave them free to kill or take any
bird during the close season also when
necessary for the protection of person or
property from injury. That is a very remarkable departure, even from the existing law. I fear if we adopt that we
would be going back in respect of the
protection of wild birds. The Bill itself
protects the eggs and nests of all birds
throughout the year and protects all birds.
except 14 species.
The Amendment leaves the nests and
eggs of ten species unprotected in the
open season. In the case of the blackbird, chaffinch and greenfinch the Bill
sanctions their killing or capture where
necessary by owners or occupiers of land
at any time for the protection of crops
or fruit. This Amendment very much
narrows the scope of protection, and
would give to landowners a privilege
denied to others. It would expose the
great majority of birds to killing or capture in rural Ulster for 6 months of the
year and leave the public parks the only
places of shelter for birds during that
period. The risk of damage to property
from the protected birds is very remote.
The Wild Birds Protection Act, 1880,
protected all birds, even sparrows, from
1st March to 1st August, and scheduled
over 80 species which the owner or occupier of land could not kill or take on
his land on any plea or pretext whatever
during the close season. This Amendment would allow all species to be killed
or taken by an owner or occupier during
the close season on a plea of the protection
of property, and would put back the
hands of the clock beyond 1880. That
is, birds protected by the Imperial Statute
will not receive similar protection in
Northern Ireland.
The Committee which recommended an
all-round protection in all seasons of all
but a few birds, consisted of representatives of the Ulster Farmers' Union, the
Wildfowl, Game and Fish Protection
Association of Ulster, the Ulster Association of County Councils, the Inspector-
General of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, the Ulster Society for the Protection of Birds, the Ministries of Agriculture
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
and Commerce and eminent and practical
ornithologists. They considered the
question from the standpoints of the protection of bird life and agriculture.
Under the Game Preservation Act,
1930, no one-no owner, no occupier-is
allowed to take or kill woodcock or snipe
between 15th February and 12th August
-or to destroy the eggs or nests of a
woodcock at any time. More protection
is to be given to game than to skylarks,
which, I believe, are protected in England
throughout the year against all folk. I
understand that in Glasgow-with a population almost equal to the population of
Northern Ireland-bird life is protected
absolutely all the year round-bird, eggs
and nest-and Glasgow has a large rural
area within its boundaries. As I have
said, this Bill specifies 27 species which
may be killed or captured unconditionally or conditionally. That list can be
added to at any time. If it is thought
that any bird should not receive protection
there is ample power under the Bill to
transfer it from one list to another or to
have it put on the black list. Full elasticity
is given. As I said, the owner can at any
time kill or take fourteen black-listed
distinctive birds. He can kill or take
ten other species in the open season. May
I submit, in view of the remarks of the
hon. and learned Senator about the rights
of property, that the owner of the land
does not own the birds that fly over his
land.
Mr. LESLIE: He does.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not think he
owns the birds that flit about over his land
or sing in his trees and hedges. They
may be on the land of adjoining occupiers
to-morrow. Wild birds are not a subject
of property in law until they are killed or
caught. Then for the first time they become property. A wild bird may be on
the Cave Hill one month and on the
Matoppo Hills the next month. No man
has a right of property in any wild bird
until it is captured or killed.
The great majority of birds here are, if
not beneficent, at any rate harmless. Very
few occupiers will kill such birds. It
is not their way or wont. They seek
victims more worthy of their guns. I
think the hon. Senator's grievance is sentimental rather than practical. I think in
999 cases out of a thousand, as he says,
the owners would not exercise the right he
asserts. There are several other aspects
of the Amendment I might have dealt
with, but these are the main points. These
are the broad issues on which I think it is
impossible to accept the Amendment of
the hon. and learned Member.
It may be suggested that property should
at least include fruit. I very carefully
considered that, and I drafted an Amendment at the very end in which property
is said to include fruit and vegetables.
Therefore, I think wherever protection is
needed it is given. I do not think we
should bring in a class of property that
may not be property at all according to the
law at the present time.
For these reasons I ask the Committee
not to agree to the Amendment proposed.
Mr. McCORKELL: I rise to support
the Amendment, and the reason I do so
is because I feel that the Bill is thoroughly
unwieldy. If anything can be done to
simplify and improve it it should be done.
At the same time I would like to congratulate the hon. and learned Member
on bringing forward a Wild Birds Protection Bill, because I am a lover of birds.
It is my opinion, however, that the Bill
in its present form is far too unwieldy,
and that it has been very loosely constructed. The hon. and learned Member quoted the names of several organisations which were represented on the Informal Committee, on whose recommendations this Measure is based. Amongst
them were county councils. I have been
a member of a county council, and the
amount of wild bird lore that comes before a county council is very small indeed.
I have also been a member of the
Farmers' Union, which was represented
on the Informal Committee, and while
farmers know their own job very well,
they know very little about wild bird life
and lore. For instance, in this Bill we
have the chaffinch put on the semi-protected list, but anyone who knows anything about the chaffinch knows that he
is comparatively harmless, whereas a few
great tits can strip a row of peas in a night.
Then we have all swans protected. I have
counted 420 mute swans in one flock, and
they destroy the spawn of trout as fast as
they can.
Under this Bill any small boy who
takes a bird, a nest, or eggs becomes a
Committee

Wild Birds Bill-
16 APRIL 1931
malefactor. I think the hon. and learned
Member suggested that the youth of the
country should be encouraged to take an
interest in the bird life of the Province,
but it is impossible for anyone to take an
interest in birds if they do not actually
handle birds and their eggs. If people
are made malefactors and not allowed to
have anything to do with birds their
interest in them will soon cease. It is
too late in the day now to attack the Bill
as a whole, but it would have been much
better, in my judgment, and in the judgment of other people, if the Bill had
simply sought to protect those birds.
which are becoming extinct. I think
there are some eighty birds protected in
the old Act.
I will support any Amendment which
will tend to simplify this Bill and make
it one that the public can understand.
As it is, I object to this Measure on
acount of the loose way in which it has
been drafted, and also because of the
extraordinary way in which the Schedules
are dealt with. For instance, the shag
is put on the black list, although it is
very uncommon in Northern Ireland.
Not one person in Northern Ireland will
see a shag here. I welcome the
Amendment, and I will support it as
strongly as I can.
ever
Mr. LONG: The proposed new Clause
would make a very considerable alteration in the Bill, and in the opinion of the
Minister of Home Affairs it would, if
accepted, to a very large extent make the
whole Measure unworkable. Under the
Bill as at present drafted all birds, with
the exception of the 14 in category 1, are
protected all the year round, and with the
further exception of the three species in
category 2, which may be shot to prevent
damage by the owner or occupier of land,
and, of course, with the exception of the
quasi-game birds in category 3, which
may be shot in the open season.
Senator
Leslie's Amendment cuts at the very root
of the Bill in this respect as, under the
Amendment, owners and occupiers of land
could shoot on the land which they own
or occupy any of the birds which the Committee recommends should be protected-
(a) from the 11th August to the 1st
March, without any excuse at all; (b) at
any time for the protection of their
property.
As hon. Members are aware, this Bill is
based entirely on and strictly follows the
recommendations of the Informal Committee appointed by the Minister.
Senator Leslie's Amendment is so completely contrary to the Committee's recommendations that if it is passed it will
be necessary to re-consider the matter
before the Government can afford further
facilities for the progress of the Bill. If
Senator Leslie thinks that any particular
bird should be removed to another category it is for him to make a suggestion to
the Advisory Committee, which will carefully consider the matter, and the alteration can be made if necessary.
The whole idea of having the Advisory
Committee is to have an expert body who
can balance up the relative merits and
demerits of any bird so that a decision
can be arrived at as to whether or not
it should be protected. If owners of
I
land are to be the judges of what is
proper in this respect you might as well
have no Advisory Committee. Senator
Leslie made a statement that the report
of the Informal Committee had not been
laid before Parliament or published.
think the hon. and learned Senator is
under a misapprehension in that respect,
because the Report of the Committee has
been laid before both Houses of Parliament. For the reasons I have given
I am opposed to the Amendment.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
was in some difficulty in considering this
Amendment, but what weighs with me
is the fact that this Bill is intended as a
protection Bill, whereas the Amendment
would leave a considerable loop-hole, and
would change the objects of the Measure.
Wild birds are always distributed in the
property of someone. There are comparatively few in the cities, and if every
person who has property is allowed to
shoot them there would be no protection
for these birds, and the chief object of
the Bill would be defeated. The Amendment may make for simplicity, but it
would mean the defeat of the objects of
the Bill, and I shall vote against the
Amendment on that account.
Mr. LESLIE: In the first place I
think I should apologise for misleading
the Committee. The copy of the Report
in my possession gives no intimation that
it was laid before Parliament, but, of
course, I accept the statement that it
was. The hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell) attacked rather severely
the suggestion that paragraph (b) of Sub-
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
clause (3) of my new Clause gave a man
a right to destroy birds on his property
which he had not got before. I cannot
agree with him about that, because there
is no bird in the Schedule to the Act of
1880 which, so far as I can see, can do
any damage to anybody whatever. But
the other birds that can do damage
are not in the Schedule, and therefore
they can be destroyed at the moment.
The Amendment does not narrow down
anything. It does not give anybody
anything they have not got now.
The defender of the Bill says that the
chance of damage is remote. He does
not tell us what compensating advantage
there is to be for incurring even remote
risk of damage. He speaks of birds as
though there were no such thing as a
qualified right to take and kill, but that
is a small matter. He seems also to have
Londonderry, The Mayor of
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast.
Barclay, Mr. W.
Campbell, Mr. T J.
Cunningham, Mr. J.
overlooked the fact that the exemptions
I propose are for the occupier only. I
cannot but think there will be those who
will say the Bill, even with this Amendment, goes further than the public either
require or desire. However, opinions
will differ about that. The question is,
are birds to be maintained at somebody
else's expense at the wish of a Committee,
or is the law to remain practically as it
is with some consolidation and a little
further strengthening such as this
Amendment will give it. That is the
point, as briefly as I can put it. The Bill
will take away something from the
occupier of land and will give him
nothing, and he will not be consulted.
Question put, "That the new Clause
be there inserted."
The Committee divided: Contents, 6;
Not Contents, 11.

CLAUSE 1 (Prohibition of certain.
methods of taking or killing birds)—
1. It shall not be lawful for any
person-
R
*
