Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 1, line 6, to leave out
the words "not be lawful" and to insert
instead the words "be unlawful." The
object of this Amendment is not to draw
attention to the difference between two
expressions, but simply because, if the
hon. and learned Member will turn to
Clause 13, he has there attached
penalty to things which are declared to
be unlawful. It is, therefore, desirable
to use the same words in this Clause.
accordingly beg to move.
a
I
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think this matter
is purely verbal. The expression "it shall
not be lawful" is very common phraseology in an Act of Parliament. It
occurs in the Game Preservation Act of
Northern Ireland, and it occurs again and
again in this Bill, and it is hardly worth
our while to change it when it means
exactly the same thing.
that
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry
my extreme brevity failed to bring home to the
hon. and learned Senator what I was
driving at. If we leave the words as they
stand it shall not be lawful for any
person, "there is no sanction attached to
the crime which we are creating.
If we turn, as I venture to suggest the
hon. and learned Senator should turn to
what I referred him to Clause 13-I was
speaking from memory, but it appears
to be the correct Clause-if we turn to
Clause 13 we see that any person committing any act declared by this Act to
be unlawful "these are the words I
propose to put in-shall be guilty of an
offence under this Act, and if we look
further on we shall know what is to
happen. But nowhere are we told what
is to happen if he does something declared
"not to be lawful." I am left in complete doubt as to why the hon. and
learned Senator went out of his way to
alter the wording of the Act of Parliament from which he took paragraph (a).
If he had stuck closely to the Act-
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
16 APRIL 1931
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think it will probably facilitate the passage of the rest
of the Bill if I accept, and with that
object I do accept.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: Before we leave Clause
1-I do not move any Amendment for the
moment as I have not given notice-I
wish to direct attention to paragraph (d).
Mention was made on Second Reading of
a motor-boat used on rivers.
I do not,
know how much motor attachment it takes
to make a mechanically-propelled boat.
It does occur to me that a mechanicallypropelled boat is only likely to be used.
in Lough Erne or Lough Neagh, where you
might find it difficult to enforce the law,
or in tidal waters. What I want the
hon. and learned Senator to consider is
whether we are empowered to insert a
provision of that sort which may have a
very serious bearing on the law relating
to tidal waters. While speaking of that
paragraph I should like to direct further
attention to the fact that we are apparently prohibiting the use of aircraft for
the purpose of pursuing seagulls. I think
the hon. and learned Senator should consider that.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Sale for human consumption).
If any person during the period between the fifteenth day after the commencement of the close season prescribed
in respect of any bird to the end of such
season, sells, or has in his possession
for the purpose of sale, for human
consumption, or has in his possession
for human consumption, any such bird
or any egg of any such bird, he shall be
guilty of an offence under this Act.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
page 2, line 1, to leave out from "period"
to the end of line 3 ("close season") and
insert "when a bird is specially protected
under this Act." This is a consequential
and verbal Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, on
page 2, line 7, after "Act" to insert:
"Provided that the Minister may by
Order direct that the provisions of this
section shall not apply in respect of any
bird specified in such Order during the
period of fifteen days next after the beginning of the close season for such bird."
The section deals with the sale of birds
for human consumption, and it is
thought right in the case of merchants
having a quantity of birds in store that
they should have a period of grace of 15
days after the close of the season for disposing of them. This is to give latitude
to the Minister. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: Before we part from
Clause 2, I would like the hon. and learned
Senator to take into consideration later
on what is going to be done, if anything,
about birds killed outside Northern Ireland and imported here for the purpose
of being eaten.
Mr. CAMPBELL: That matter will be
attended to. In fact it is under consideration.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Illegal method of taking
or confining birds):
(1) If any person uses as a decoy any
live bird which is tethered or is confined in a cage or other receptacle, or
is secured by means of braces or other
similar appliances, or which is blind,
maimed or injured, or uses bird-lime or
any substance of a like nature for the
purpose of taking or capturing alive, or
attempting to take or capture alive,
any wild bird, he shall be guilty of an
offence under this Act.
(2) Where any person is convicted of
any offence under this section, the Court
may, in addition to any penalty that
may be imposed, order any live decoy
bird used by such person for the purpose of taking or attempting to take or
capture alive any wild bird to be forfeited and disposed of as the Court may
direct.
(3) If any person keeps or confines a
bird in any cage or other receptacle
which does not measure sufficient in
height, length, and breadth to permit
the bird freely to stretch its wings, he
shall be guilty of an offence under this
Act.
This sub-section shall not apply (1)
to domestic fowls, turkeys, geese, ducks,
guinea-fowls and pigeons, or (2) to any
bird kept or confined in a cage or other
receptacle which does not comply with
the conditions mentioned in this subsection-

(a) whilst being conveyed by land
or water; or
(b) whilst being shown for the
purpose of public exhibition or competition;

the Act from which this is taken-I think

confined to public exhibition. I have
taken the opportunity to arrange the
names in alphabetical order. I beg to
if the time during which it is so kept or
confined does not in the whole exceed
seventy-two hours.

move.

Clause, as amended, ordered to stand

CLAUSE 4 (Taking of birds for sale.
alive).
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to leave

CLAUSE 5 (Unlawful to kill or take
birds on Sundays).
or
It shall not be lawful for any person
to kill or take any bird, or take
knowingly and wilfully disturb any
nest or egg, on Sundays, except for the
purpose of protecting property, crops,
or fisheries from immediate damage.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The Amendments

the Committee. I beg to move.
Page 3, line 27, leave out "any nest or

Page 3, line 27, to leave out "Sundays"
and insert "Sunday."
Page 3, line 28, after "crops" insert
"game."
Page 3, line 28, to leave out "immediate.'
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

16 APRIL 1931
(1) If any person promotes, arranges,
conducts, assists in, receives money for,
or takes part in any meeting, competition, exhibition, pastime, practice, display or other event whatever at or in
the course of which captive birds are
liberated by hand or by trap, contrivance or other means, for the purpose
of being shot at the time of their

occupier, or person in charge of any
premises permits his premises or property or any part thereof to be used

of an offence under this Act.
or

conviction to a fine not exceeding

in addition thereto, to be imprisoned.
with or without hard labour for any
term not exceeding three months.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
6, sub-section (2) to leave out "with or
without hard labour." As the Committee will remember, we do not usually
insert these words.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I accept.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Classification of birds for
purposes of the Act).
Subject as hereinafter provided, for
the purposes of this Act the birds specified in Part I, Part II and Part III of
the First Schedule to this Act are
classified respectively as being in Category I, Category II and Category III;
and references in this Act to those Categories shall be construed accordingly.
Provided that the Minister of Home
Affairs may, subject to the provisions of
this Act relating to the making of
orders, by order alter the classification
of any bird from one Category to
another, or direct that this Act shall
apply to any bird excepted from classification for special protection under
this Act as if such bird were included
for such classification, and specify the
Category under which such bird shall
be classified; and on the making of any
such order this Act shall have effect
accordingly.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The Amendments
to Clause 7 are purely consequential. I
beg to move on page 4, line 19, after
"another" ("category to another, or")
to insert "or except any bird from
classification in any category," and in
line 20, after "from" ("from classification') to insert "such."
Amendments agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Leave out Clause 7.
Amendment not moved.
The CHAIRMAN: I may say in
explanation there seems to be a misprint
here, because Clause 8 comes in after these
two lines, although the Amendments on
the Paper are apparently to Clause 7.
You will see, if
you look at the Bill, that
Clause 8 begins at line 26.
CLAUSE 8 (Protection of birds according to categories)—
(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, it shall not be lawful for any
person-
(a) to kill, injure or take, or to
attempt to kill, injure or take, or
to use any means for the purpose
of killing, injuring or taking, any
bird at a time when it is protected :
Provided that as respects any bird
excepted from classification in Category I., but which may hereafter
be classified for special protection
by an order of the Minister of Home
Affairs made under this Act, the
foregoing provision shall not apply
to the owner or occupier of any land
upon which any such bird is for the
time being, or to any person belonging to his household or in his service,
or authorised in writing signed by
him to act on his behalf; or
(b) except in the ordinary course.
of farming or forestry operations, to
take or knowingly and wilfully to disturb any nest or egg at a time when
it is protected; or
(c) to sell, or expose or offer for
sale, or to have in his control or
183 .

Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
possession, or to transport knowingly,
any bird, or the skin or plumage of
any part of any bird killed or taken,
or any egg or nest taken, in contravention of this section.
(2) For the purposes of this section-
(a) birds classified under this Act
as being in Category I and the eggs
and nests of such birds shall be
deemed to be protected at all times;
and
(b) birds classified under this Act
as being in Category II shall be
deemed to be protected at all times,
but it shall be lawful for the owner
or occupier of any land or other
person belonging to his household or
in his service or authorised in writing
signed by him to act on his behalf, to
kill or take any such birds on his
land when necessary for the protection of his crops or property from
immediate damage. The eggs and
nests of such birds shall be deemed
to be protected at all times; and
(c) birds classified under this Act
as being in Category III shall be
deemed to be protected during the
close season, and the eggs and nests
of such birds shall be deemed to be
protected at all times.

I' and insert "by Part I of the First

Page 4, line 36, leave out "made under

Page 5, line 8, leave out "nest or egg"

Page 5, line 28, after "crops" insert
"game, fisheries."
Page 5, line 29, leave out "immediate."
Amendments agreed to.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Leave out Clause 8.
Amendment not moved.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Liberation of imported
birds).
It shall not be lawful for any person
without the leave of the Minister of
Home Affairs knowingly to liberate imported birds of any species, but this
provision shall not apply to pheasants,
partridges, grouse, heath or moor game
or black game.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
9, page 5, line 37, to leave out "not be
lawful" and to insert "be unlawful."
This is the same point again, and I presume the hon. and learned Member will
agree to it as before.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Quite so.
Amendment agreed to.
""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page 6,
line 1, to leave out from "to" to the end
of line 2, and to insert "game.' This is
an Amendment which I think Senator
Campbell will agree to, because it saves a
great deal of unnecessary verbiage. We
use the word "game" throughout the Act,
and it is unnecessary to repeat the names
of the various species. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I would be very
glad to agree to this Amendment, if I
could, but I think the Clause as it stands
is simple and clear. If the Amendment
was inserted we could not tell from Clause
9 what game is, and we would have to go
to the definition Clause at the end of the
Bill to find out what it is. The words in
the Bill are taken from the Game Preservation Act, 1831, and I think it would be
better from the point of view of the lay
reader to leave them as they are.
Amendment negatived.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Licences to kill and take
birds, etc.).
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
16 APRIL 1931
(1) The Minister of Home Affairs
shall, subject to the provisions of this
Act, have power to grant a licence to
any person exempting the holder of the
licence from any of the provisions of
this Act relating to the killing or taking
of birds of any Category, or to the
taking or disturbing of the nests or
eggs of birds of any Category, for any
scientific purpose, or for the purpose of
the protection of property, crops or
fisheries, or for any other special purpose approved by the Minister.
(2) Any applicant for a licence under
this section may be required to enter
into a bond with or without securities
for the payment of a sum not exceeding
fifty pounds in case any condition
attached to the licence is infringed.
(3) A licence under this section may
be granted for such areas, and subject
to such conditions as may be specified
in the licence.
(4) There shall be payable on the
grant of a licence under this section a
fee of five shillings.
(5) A licence granted by the Minister
of Home Affairs under this section may
be signed either by the Minister of Home
Affairs or by any person authorised by
him in that behalf.
The following Amendment stood on the

CLAUSE 11 (Register to be kept by the
taxidermists).
(1) It shall be the duty of every
person carrying on business as a taxidermist, or otherwise as a dealer in stuffed
birds, or the eggs of birds, to keep a
register of all birds classified under this
Act as being in Categories I, II and III,
and of the skins, eggs and nests of all
such birds received by him after the
commencement of this Act, showing details in each case of the locality where
they were taken and of the person from
whom they were procured.
(2) Every register kept in accordance
with the requirements of this section
shall be open to inspection at all reasonable times by any officer or member of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary, or any
person duly authorised in writing by
the Minister of Home Affairs.
(3) This section shall not come into
operation until a day to be appointed by
the Minister of Home Affairs, which shall
be a date not earlier than that on which
a similar enactment shall come into
operation in Great Britain.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 6, line 29, leave out from "birds"
to the first "and" in line 30, and insert
"protected under this Act."
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
6, line 33, after the first "the" to insert
"date and." It seemed to us that in the
keeping of this register the record of the
date was an important factor as well as
the locality. Accordingly, I beg to
move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I assent.
Amendment agreed to.
The following Amendments on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:
Page 6, line 37, leave out "member"
and insert "constable."
Page 6, line 38, leave out from "Constabulary" to the end of line 39.
Amendments agreed to.

Mr. CAMPBELL: This is an Amendment which Senator Leslie and I agreed
to, but it has since been represented to
me that it would be unwise to alter the
Clause as it stands, and for this reason.
There is at the present time a Bill dealing
only with Scottish wild birds before the
Imperial Parliament, and it might be injudicious to make the alteration suggested
in this Clause at the present time.
Mr. LESLIE: This is one of the more
interesting of the minor Amendments. If
I understand the hon. and learned Member correctly, he proposes to wait until a
similar enactment is in operation over
the whole of Great Britain.

to the hon. and learned Member that the

Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not move the

move?

Clause, as amended, ordered to stand

CLAUSE 12 (Creation of bird sanctuaries).
(1) With a view to the creation of bird
sanctuaries the Minister of Home
Affairs may, subject to the provisions
of this Act relating to the making of
orders, by order direct that in any district defined by the order all birds,
with or without any specified exceptions, shall in that district be deemed
to be classified in Category I, and on
the making of any such order this Act
shall in that district have effect accordingly:
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
16 APRIL 1931
Provided that no such order shall be
made except with the consent of the
owner and occupier of any land comprised in the district to which the order
relates.
(2) Any order made under this section may provide for the classification
of any birds excepted by the order from
classification in Category I, as respects
the district to which the order relates.
(3) The provisions of this section
shall be without prejudice to the generality of the provisions of this Act
empowering the Minister of Home
Affairs to alter the classification of any
bird.

and."

Mr. CAMPBELL: I think it would be

Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 13 (Offences)-
Subject to the provisions of this Act
any person who-
(a) commits any act declared by
this Act to be unlawful, or incites
any person to commit such act; or
(b) acts in contravention of or fails
to comply with any regulations made
under this Act; or
(c) being a person in whose case
a certificate or licence granted under
this Act has been revoked or cancelled
applies for a new certificate or licence
and conceals the fact that his certicate or licence has been so revoked
or cancelled; or
(d) during any period in which a
certificate or licence granted under
this Act to him is suspended, applies
for a new certificate or licence and
conceals the fact that his certificate
or licence has been so suspended; or
(e) being a person carrying on
business as a taxidermist or otherwise
as a dealer in stuffed birds or the
eggs of birds, fails to keep a register
in pursuance of the provisions of this
Act, or fails to make in such a
register any entry which he is required by this Act to make, or makes
in any such register an entry which
is false in any particular, or refuses to
submit any such register for the inspection of any officer or member of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary or
other person entitled under this Act
to inspect it; or
(f) being the holder of a licence
granted under this Act infringes
any of the conditions attached to the
licence;
shall be guilty of an offence under this
Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in paragraph (a) after "commits" to insert "or
attempts to commit." It is better to
cover the attempt as well.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I concur.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
paragraph (a) to leave out "or incites any
person to commit such act." This is
amply provided for under Section 22 of
the Petty Sessions (Ireland) Act, 1851.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in paragraph (b) after acts to insert
attempts to act." This is the same
Amendment as before.
Amendment agreed to.

CLAUSE 14 (Penalties).
*

(2) In addition to any other penalty
which a court has power to inflict for
offences under this Act the court before which a person is convicted of any
such offence shall order that any bird,
skin, egg, or nest, in respect of which
the offence was committed shall be forfeited, and, if they think fit, deposited
in a public museum, and shall order
the liberation of any bird illegally
taken, and may order that any gun or
other instrument in respect of or by
means of which the offence was committed shall be forfeited or otherwise
dealt with as the court may direct,
and may also order that any certificate
or licence under this Act, held by the
person convicted, shall be cancelled, or
shall be suspended for such period as
may be specified in the order, and
where any certificate or licence under
this Act has been so cancelled, or
during any period when a certificate
or licence is so suspended, no other
certificate or licence granted under
this Act to that person shall be valid
(3) Upon the conviction of the
holder of a licence granted under this
Act for the offence of infringing any
of the conditions attached to the
licence. the court shall, in addition to
any other penalty which it has power
to inflict, have power to enforce any
bond entered into in accordance with
the provisions of this Act as though it
were a recognizance, and the Minister
of Home Affairs shall have power to
revoke the licence.

CLAUSE 15 (Legal Proceedings).
(1) Where any person sells or has in
his possesion for purposes of sale any
bird, or the plumage or skin or any
part of any bird classified in Categorv
I, Category II. or Category III, or
the egg or nest of any bird classified
in Category I or Category II or Category III, it shall be deemed to have
been killed or taken, as the case may be,
in contravention of this Act, unless he
satisfies the court that it was not, or
that he had reasonable grounds for
believing that it was not, so killed or
taken.
(2) Where any person is found in
control or possession of any bird, or the
plumage or skin or any part of any
bird or the nest or egg of any bird,
before
and it appears to the court
which any proceedings are taken in
respect thereof that there is reasonable
ground for believing that the bird.
nest or egg was killed or taken at a
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
16 APRIL 1931
are
time when it was protected under this
Act, the bird, egg or nest, as the case
may be, shall, unless the person
against whom the proceedings
taken satisfies the court that it was
not, or that he had reasonable grounds
for believing that it was not, so killed
or taken, be deemed to have been
killed or taken in contravention of
this Act-
(a) in the case of a bird classified in
Category I, or the egg or nest of a
bird classified in Category I or
Category II or Category III, if it
is so found at any time;
(b) in the case of a bird classified in
Category II, if it is so found at
any time in the possession of any
person not being the owner or occupier of the land on which the
bird was killed or taken or a person
belonging to the owner's or occupier's household, or in his service,
or authorised in writing. signed by
him to act on his behalf;
(c) in the case of a bird classified in
Category III, if it is so found on
any day between the fifteenth day
after the beginning and the last
day of the close season for that
bird;
(d) in the case of a bird excepted from
classification in Category I, or the
egg or nest of such bird, if it is so
found on any day between the
fifteenth day after the beginning
and the last day of the close season
for that bird prescribed by any
order of the Minister of Home
Affairs, in the possession of any
person not being the owner or occupier of the land on which the
bird was killed or taken, or the egg
or nest taken, or a person belonging to the owner's or occupier's
household, or in his service, or
authorised in writing signed by him.
to act on his behalf.
(3) In any proceedings under this
Act, the doing of any act which may be
done under a certificate or licence
granted under this Act or by leave of
the Minister of Home Affairs shall be
deemed to have been done under such
certificate or licence or by such leave,
if the person who did the act proves
that he was the holder of a certificate
or licence issued under this Act or that
he has obtained the leave of the
Minister of Home Affairs, as the case
may be, authorising the doing of the
act in question, but not otherwise.
(4) All offences under this Act committed within the jurisdiction of the
Admiralty or on the sea coast or at sea
beyond the ordinary jurisdiction of any
court of summary jurisdiction shall be
dealt with in all respects as if committed in the county or county borough
nearest adjacent to the place where the
offence was committed; and in any prosecution or conviction for any such
offence the offence may be averred to
have been committed "on the high
seas."
(5) Where any offence under this Act
is committed in or upon any waters running between or forming the boundary
between any two counties or petty sessions districts, such offence may be prosecuted before justices in either of such
counties or districts.
(6) All offences under this Act shall
be prosecuted and all fines thereunder
shall be recovered in manner provided
by the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland)
Acts, and a person aggrieved by the
making of a conviction or order, or the
refusal to make a conviction or order,
by a Court of Summary Jurisdiction on
determining any information or complaint under this Act, may appeal in
manner provided by the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts.
(7) A court of quarter sessions shall
on an appeal under this Act have power
to make any order which a court of summary jurisdiction could have made
under this Act, and to confirm, quash
or vary an order which has been appealed against:
Provided that no appeal shall lie
against an order made under this Act
for the disposal by destruction of a live
decoy bird.
(8) Where any person under the age
of sixteen is convicted of an offence
under this Act, the parent or guardian
of the offender shall be liable for any
pecuniary penalty incurred.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to
leave out sub-clause 1.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
sub-clause (2), after "was" (was protected
under this Act) to insert "specially."
This is purely consequential.
Amendment agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: On a point of Order.
My Amendment and the first five of Mr.
Campbell's produce the same result.
Perhaps Mr. Campbell will move the
Amendments.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
page 10, line 32, to leave out from "a" to
"authorising" in line 39, and insert:-
"licence granted under this Act shall be

are moving Mr. Leslie's Amendment as

Mr. LESLIE: Yes.

Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not move the

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to

that it is not competent for this Parliament to deal with this matter of jurisdiction on the high seas beyond our Province.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move on
page 11, line 13, to leave out "shall" and
insert "may." This is purely consequential.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, after

an officer or constable of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary or by a member of the
public." That is giving to any member
of the public the right to initiate proceedings under this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to suggest

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to
insert the following new sub-section at the
end of line 29:-
"(8) In all cases of prosecutions for
offences under section eight of this Act the
complaint shall be made within twelve
months from the time when the cause of
complaint has arisen."
As the clause stands complaint must be
made within a period of six months. It
might be advisable in the case of birds for
various reasons to extend the limit of
prosecution to a period of 12 months.

Mr. LESLIE: The hon. and learned
Senator was good enough to inform me
that he purposed moving this Amendment,
and I undertook to support it. I still
support the principle of the Amendment,
but I am not quite sure whether the wording is right. I am rather afraid that if
we leave the words as they are we are
just running counter to sub-clause (6),
which says offences shall be prosecuted, and
so on. I am not quite certain that you
ought not to say instead of the words on
the Paper, "notwithstanding anything
contained in the Summary Jurisdiction
Acts," and so on.
Mr. CAMPBELL: To save any trouble
I will add the words: "notwithstanding
anything contained in the Summary
Jurisdiction Acts, with just one variation
in the Amendment, instead of 'shall' to
insert 'may' ('may be made')." That
will make it right.
Amendment, as amended, agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to
leave out lines 30 to 33 (sub-section 8).
This provision is already covered.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 16 (Powers and duties of
officers or members of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary)-
(1) Where a person is seen or found
offending or is reasonably suspected of
offending against this Act, on a highway or in a public place, an officer or
member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary may, without warrant, stop, detain and search him, and if his name
and address are not known to the officer
or member, and such person fails to
give them to the satisfaction of the
officer or member, the officer or member
may, without warrant, apprehend him;
and the officer or member may, whether
so stopping or detaining or apprehending the person or not, detain and
examine any bird, nest, egg, gun, or
other instrument, vehicle, boat, or
thing to which the offence or suspected
offence relates, and may seize and,
pending prosecution, retain any such
bird, egg or nest.
(2) If any person obstructs or impedes or assists to obstruct or impede
an officer or member in the execution
of his duty under this Act the officer
or member may, without warrant,
apprehend the offender.
(3) When an officer or member takes
and detains in his custody under the
authority of this Act any bird, egg or
nest, he shall as soon as conveniently
may be
(a) take such steps as may be
proper to have the person guilty, or
believed to be guilty, of the offence
committed, or believed to have been
committed, in relation to such bird,
egg, or nest brought before a court
of summary jurisdiction, and
(b) produce such bird, egg, or nest
who may
peace,
to a justice of the
order the same to be sold or
destroyed.
Where a justice of the peace orders
such bird, egg or nest to be sold, and
the person from whom it has been taken
is convicted of any offence in relation
to such bird, egg, or nest, the proceeds
of such sale shall be added to and dealt
with in the same manner as any penalty
imposed for such offence. If no conviction takes place, the bird, or the
proceeds of the sale thereof (when such
sale has been authorised by a justice
of the peace) shall be restored to the
person from whom the bird, egg, or
nest was seized, and no officer or member who, by direction of a justice in
writing, sells or destroys any bird, egg,
or nest so seized, shall be liable to any
legal proceedings for such sale or destruction or for any loss caused by such
sale or destruction.
(4) No officer or member shall be
liable for any loss or damage occasioned
by or in the course of the exercise
of any of the powers conferred on him
by this Act, unless such loss or damage
was caused by such officer or member,
wantonly or maliciously.
(5) Every person who obstructs or
impedes any officer or member in the
exercise of any of the powers conferred
on him by this Act, or refuses to give
his name and address, or gives an untrue name and address when such
name and address are demanded
under the powers conferred on an officer
or member under this Act, shall be
guilty of an offence under this Act.
(6) An officer or member or any person authorised by the Minister of Home
Affairs shall, if he has reasonable
cause to suspect that any contravention of the provisions of this Act is
being or is about to be committed on
Wild Birds Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
any land, have power to enter on the
land without the consent of the owner
or occupier, and any person who by
himself, or by any person in his
employment or acting with his knowledge or consent, refuses, fails, or unreasonably delays, to admit such officer
or member, or authorised person shall
be guilty of an offence under this Act.
(7) An officer or member or any
person authorised by the Minister of
Home Affairs, shall, if he has reasonable cause to suspect that any contravention of the provisions of this Act
is being or is about to be committed
in any house or other building, have
power, under a warrant issued to him
for that purpose by a justice of the
peace, to enter and search such house
or other building and any person who
by himself or by any person in his
employment or acting with his knowledge or consent, refuses fails or unreasonably delays, to admit such officer
or member or authorised person or
hinders him in such search, shall be
guilty of an offence under this Act.

CLAUSE 17 (Wild Birds Advisory
Committee).
17. (1) There shall be established
a Wild Birds Advisory Committee for
Northern Ireland consisting of such
members as may be appointed by the
Minister of Home Affairs.
(2) The duties of the Committee
shall be to advise the Minister of Home
Affairs upon any questions which he
may refer to the Committee in connection with the administration of this
Act, (or with projected schemes for the
protection of wild birds, either in
Northern Ireland or internationally.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
page 13, lines 39 and 40, to leave out
"wild birds either in Northern Ireland
or internationally" and insert "birds."
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 18 (Orders of Minister of
Home Affairs.)
(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, orders made under this Act by
the Minister of Home Affairs may be
so made as to extend generally or as
respects any district specified in the
order.
(2) All orders made by the Minister
of Home Affairs under this Act shall
be published in the Belfast Gazette,
and in such other manner as
Minister may direct.
the
(3) At least one month before making under this Act any order which is
to extend generally, the Minister of
Home Affairs shall cause notice of the
proposal to make the order and of the
purport thereof to be published in the
Belfast Gazette and in such other manner as appears to him to be desirable,
and shall consider any objections to
the proposal made within such time as
may be prescribed in the notice.
(4) The Minister of Home Affairs
may on the representation of the Wild
Birds Advisory Committee for Northern
Ireland make, for the purpose of the
protection of the eggs of birds, such
orders as he may deem advisable.
(5) The power to prescribe by order
the close season for any species of birds
not prescribed by this Act shall be
solely vested in the Minister of Home
Affairs on the representation of the
Wild Birds Advisory Committee for
Northern Ireland.
(6) Any order made under this Act
may be varied or revoked by a subsequent order made in like manner.

Tuesday, 21st April, 1931.

Dobbs's Divorce Bill.

Anderson's Divorce Bill.

Joint Standing Orders Committee.
The following report was made from
the Joint Standing Orders Committee:
That the Standing Orders not complied.
with in respect of the additional provisions in the Donaghadee (County Down)
Water and Improvement Bill ought to
be dispensed with, and leave given to the
Committee on the Bill to insert the additional Provisions.
Donaghadee (County Down) Water
and Improvement Bill.
Moved, "That the Senators following be
named of the Joint Opposed Committee
on the Donaghadee (County Down) Water
and Improvement Bill-
Mr. Armstrong.
Major Dobbs.
Mr. Dorman."
-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Londonderry Corporation Bill.
Moved, "That the Senators following be
named of the Joint Opposed Committee

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I wonder
if this House would have any objection
to my taking the National Health Insurance Bill out of its proper order and
reading it a Second time forthwith. The
objections to such a course are that a
Senator might see the Order Paper and
might delay his coming into the Senate
until a later time in order to speak on it,
and we might catch him out by this
method. I am not doing it for that
reason, and I do not think there is much
risk in it.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The main
purpose of the present Bill is to retain
certain classes of insured persons in the
same rights and privileges as they would
enjoy if they were living in Great Britain.
These are people who, owing to trade depression, have had little or no insurable
employment for some years, and would
lose their title to health insurance benefits
and contributory pensions unless special
provision is made for them. When the
National Health Insurance Act, 1924,
was amended in 1928, it was foreseen that
elderly persons were apt to drop out of
employment through no fault of their own,
and thus lose their right to the pension
at 65 years of age, towards which they
had contributed for possibly many years.
The 1928 Act, therefore, provided that
persons over 60 years of age who had been
at least ten years in insurance could have
their insurance prolonged from year to
year on proof of genuine unemployment;
this preserved their right to pension at
the age of 65, and to treatment benefits
in the meanwhile, but not to ordinary.
cash benefits. Persons under 60 years of
age, however, had no such right of indefinite prolongation. They got a "free
period," averaging about 21 months from
the date of their last contribution, during
which they remained entitled to all
benefits; and a further "extended year"
during which they kept their right to pension and treatment benefits only; but,
after that all such rights ceased.
Power was given by the 1928 Act to
effect by regulation the transition from
the old conditions to the new. These
regulations expired at the end of last
year, and the Imperial Parliament
passed, late in December, 1930, the Prolongation of Insurance Act, 1930, to make
provision for the insured persons affected
by the cessation of the regulations, who
would otherwise go out of insurance.
207 Health Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Wild Birds Bill-
[Viscount Charlemont]
The Parliament of Northern Ireland was
not then sitting so that a corresponding
Act could not be passed before the existing regulations lapsed. The immediate
difficulty was met by a Regulation which,
in effect, continued the existing regulations at the expense of the approved
societies, who consented to this course
being adopted on the understanding that
a Bill would be introduced at an early
date to recoup them for the expenditure
they had incurred. The present Bill,
therefore, conforms to the Act of the Imperial Parliament with retrospective effect
to 1st January last.
The third Clause of the Bill deals with
a special matter. Under the Health Insurance Acts as they stand at present,
the only power of regular inspection of
approved societies is that given to the
auditor. This is confined to purely
financial matters; and yet it is evident
(and the Ministry of Labour's experience
of several societies has proved it to be
true) that the books of a society may be
accurately kept and yet its general administration may be such as to be contrary to the interests of its members and
dangerous to its very existence. But unless the Ministry of Labour has actual
evidence of maladministration (and such
evidence rarely comes to light except by
accident), they have no right to intervene.
The powers given to the Ministry come
into force only after definite acts of maladministration have been committed and
have come to light; and by that time the
evil has generally reached a point at
which drastic action must be taken in the
interests of the members. The intention
of this Clause is that the Ministry may
gain earlier knowledge of the state of a
society's affairs and so help it to avoid
mistakes rather than punish it for committing them. The experience of the
Ministry, gained from many societies,
will be at the disposal of any society that
needs it; and it is hoped that timely
advice, instruction or warning will prevent the recurrence of some disasters that
have recently overtaken a few of these
bodies and thus tended to discredit
approved societies in general-the majority
of which, I believe, are discharging
their responsible duties faithfully and
efficiently. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly,
and committed to a Committee of the
whole House.
Coursing of Hares Bill (S.).

Moved, "That this Report be now
received." (Mr. Leslie.)

CLAUSE 20 (Definitions) —
In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires, the following expressions
have the meanings hereby assigned to
them respectively, that is to say:-
"Bird" means any wild bird (including wild fowl), except grouse,
partridges, pheasants, heath or moor
game, black game, snipe, and woodcock;
"Close season" means the period
from the first day of March to the
twelfth day of August:
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move
to leave out "any wild bird (including
wild fowl)" and insert "any wild bird or
wild fowl." The intention is to make
the definition of wild birds as simple and
at the same time as exhaustive as possible.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line
36 to leave out from "except" to the
end of line 37 ("woodcock;") and to insert "any game bird." We are about in
in a few minutes to define game, and
that will render the repetition unnecessary.
Mr. CAMPBELL: If the Bill has any
claim to merit whatever I think its merit
is in trying to be as plain and simple
as possible. In the definition of birds

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in

'specially protected' means protected

Amendment is purely explanatory.
in the Clause we have included all birds
which are called game birds in the Act
of 1831, with one exception. Game birds
in the Act of 1831 are defined as pheasants, partridges, grouse, heath or moor
game, black game and bustards. All these
are specified one by one in the definition
Clause as it stands at the present
moment, with the exception of bustards,
which are not now natives of these islands
and may not be in our time. Then in
addition to those game birds which are
specified in the Act of 1831, we have
added on snipe and woodcock, which are
birds specially protected in the Game
Preservation Act of 1928. Therefore, in
my opinion, the Clause includes all birds
which are included in the Act of 1831,
and also the local Act of 1928, with the
one exception of bustards. I think it
would be simpler to allow the definition
to stand as it is at the present moment.
Mr. LESLIE: The difficulty is, and I
am afraid the hon. and learned Senator
has left us in the difficulty, what is he
going to do about defining game. The
word is used several times, and unless we
have a definition we shall be left in a
difficulty. The definition referred to in
the Act of 1831, as the House may remember, is limited to the Act in which
it occurs. It was adopted as a basic
principle in the Act of 1928, but it was,
if I remember aright, so done as amended
by subsequent enactment, and by that
Act. I cannot help thinking we shall
be wise to adopt this Amendment of mine
and the following one, but I recognise
that if we do, we shall have to go back
later on to an earlier stage of the Bill
when the hon. and learned Member refused to accept somewhat similar
Amendment with a view to brevity.
There is no particular reason for insisting
on this Amendment if the hon. and
learned Member objects to it, because we
can put the matter right at a later stage.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
15, to leave out lines 12 and 13 and to

This is perhaps a little more involved
than some of the other Amendments. It
appears to me to be necessary to insert
some of the other provisions here or hereabouts, and that being so, it is a little
awkward to have the citation paragraph
at the beginning, where it is now.
mally the citation and operation clauses
come at the end of things, and that is
where I propose to put them. The lines
I want to leave out are perfectly all right,
but they are not quite in their right place.
It would not have mattered if I had not
Norto
insert other
wanted
provisions.
Passing to the things I want to insert, I
think Sub-Clause (1) is necessary, because
the Protection of Animals Act, as the
Committee will remember, applies to any
captive animal, and "animal" includes
"bird." You may have a bird which is
a captive bird, and therefore it should not
lose the protection given to it by that
Act. I think that a sort of fence, as
it is sometimes called, should be erected
in this Bill to show that the provisions
of the other Act are not affected.
The second Sub-Clause is simply a replacement of one that occurs in the Bill
later on, and now we come to the third
Sub-Clause. I think it is necessary that
some words of this sort should be inserted,
otherwise, if ever this Bill becomes an
Act, all the existing Orders relating to
birds will carry on in spite of the new
enactment. I do not think anybody
knows exactly what valid orders relating
to birds and eggs are still in existence.
It is possible, and only possible, that the
Ministry of Home Affairs may know, but
nobody else knows, and therefore I think
we must take some steps. For that purpose I think it is necessary to make the
re-arrangement I have mentioned.

Amendment.

Wild Birds Bill-
Committee.
21 APRIL 1931
Now we begin to clear up matters.
First of all, it is necessary under any
circumstances to make an alteration in
the commencement Clause.
We must get
in the words, "save where otherwise provided," because, as the Committee will
remember, in the Clause relating to
taxidermists we provide that our Bill
shall not come into operation until something else happens, and therefore we
cannot leave the date definite as it
originally was. Then we come to the
question of whether we can bring the Bill
into operation on the 31st July this year
in order to save the wild duck this year
instead of putting it off until next year.
If it is at all possible I think the Committee will wish to do that. The second
sub-section is the citation Clause, which
is merely transferred. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I accept the
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
FIRST SCHEDULE.
*
The following birds shall be specially
protected at all times, except when it
is necessary for the owner or occupier
of any land, or a person belonging to
his household or in his service or
authorised in writing signed by him to
act on his behalf, for the purpose of
protecting the owners' or occupiers'
crops and property from immediate
damage, to kill or take these birds on
the land of the owner or occupier; and
the eggs and nests of these birds shall
be specially protected at all times:-
Blackbird,
Chaffinch, and
Greenfinch.
PART III.
BIRDS IN CATEGORY III.
The following birds shall be specially
protected during the close season,
that
is, from 1st March to 12th August, and
their eggs and nests shall be specially
protected at all times:-
Swans (all species),
Geese (all species)
Ducks (all species),
Golden Plover,
Grey Plover,
Common Redshank,
Common Curlew,
Whimbrel,
Rock Dove, and
Stock Dove.

Mr. LESLIE: I do not like these
Amendments at all, but it seems to me
that we are in a difficulty. The purpose
of the Bill is to deal only with wild geese
and ducks, but I am very much afraid
that as the Bill stands we have not got
the protection in the context. We have
said, "The following birds shall be protected," and we have said elsewhere,
"birds" mean "wild birds." It seems to
me very questionable whether introducing
the words, "or species," does not take it
out of the context and give it a little
context of its own. I do not know what
the hon. and learned Senator thinks, but
there is a difficulty.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think the introduction of the word "wild" is unnecessary. I think birds specially protected,
including swans and geese, specifically
refer to wild swans and wild geese, and
if the word "wild" were inserted here it
would be practically the only part of the
Bill in which that word appears.
Mr. LESLIE: I am rather glad the
hon. and learned Member does take that
view. I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendments.
Amendments, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, on page
17, column 3, line 19, to leave out "9."
This is another matter which may or may
not prove troublesome. I had better tell
the Committee that Section 9 of the Protection of Animals Act, which I suggest
for the moment to leave out, is reproduced
in our Bill as Clause 6, that is to say,
the Clause dealing with the shooting of
captive birds.
I am afraid I have got to
say that if we leave Clause 6 as it stands
in the Bill the repeal of Section 9, which
we are doing now, of the Protection of
Animals Act would re-establish the sport
of shooting captive pigeons, which is the
The
last thing any of us intend to do.
difficulty is, of course, that birds have
now been defined as wild birds, and the
pigeons used in shooting are not invariably
wild pigeons. They were very frequently
tame pigeons imported for the purpose.
Having got as far as that the Committee will see it raises the question
whether all the provisions we have taken
from Part 2 of the Protection of Animals
Act ought to be included in this Bill or
not, whether they ought not to be excluded as being applicable just as much.
to birds which are not wild birds. If the
Committee should come to that conclusion
it would be necessary to make another
alteration. We should have to alter the
schedule to Section 7 and the subsequent
one of Section 8, and, of course, leave out
9 and 10. It may be thought desirable
to keep in as much as we can of the Protection of Animals Act in order to make
the consolidation as perfect as possible.
There is another method of meeting the
difficulty, which could be taken on report.
Perhaps the hon. and learned Senator will
tell the Committee if this is the course
he prefers. For the moment, I move to
leave out the reference to Section 9.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I venture to suggest to the hon. and learned Senator that
the Amendment with regard to Section
9 of the Act of 1926 is unnecessary.
On
the Report Stage I will frame a set of
words which will make it clear that Section
6 extends to all captive birds whatever.
Mr. LESLIE: I think, subject to
seeing the set of words, that that will
probably be the most convenient method.
I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

for country people.
I do not think the Bill will take us very

be nothing more to be said, but if it is
desired to get the Bill earlier there is a
course which the noble Viscount hates to
suggest to the House.

a horrible day to sit?
Sir E. HERDMAN: It is a fast day.

admit that the course the hon. and
learned Senator suggests occurred to me,
but I thought it disagreeable and I did
wish, as I do with other disagreeable
things, to put it off as long as possible.

Thursday, 23rd April, 1931.

of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the

Minute dissolving the Committee for the

The Swine Fever (Belfast) Order, 1931.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That Standing Order No. XXV be suspended for
this day's sitting for the purpose of taking
the remaining stages of the National
Health Insurance Bill." I have pointed
out before that we could not have had
this Bill sooner, and it would be a great
convenience to the Ministry of Labour if
it was passed by this day week.
certain number of days must necessarily
be allotted to secure the Royal Assent I
do not think the House will object to my
proposal, particularly as I do not think
the Bill is in any way contentious.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Questions to Minister.
IMPRISONMENT FOR DEBT.
As a
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state how many
persons were imprisoned last year in Northern Ireland under County Court orders
following non-payment of debts due under
County Court decrees; and how many were
imprisoned under Petty Sessions warrants
following non-payment of rates and other
moneys due under Petty Sessions orders.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would be
very much obliged if the hon. and learned
Member would put down the Question
again in about a week's time, as the information that is being collected is not yet
available.
ULSTER IMPORTS AND EXPORTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House, if he will state the figures for
the year 1924 showing the volume of all
imports into Northern Ireland from
British and Foreign ports, and from the
Irish Free State, and the volume of all
exports from Northern Ireland to British
and Foreign ports, and to the Irish Free
State; and the relative figures for the year
1930.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would remind the hon. Senator that complete
separate Customs statistics of Northern
Ireland's trade are not compiled by the
Imperial Board of Customs. In these
circumstances I can only give him estimates
of the value of the imports and exports
based on the Returns furnished to the
Ministry of Commerce by the various
Northern Ireland Harbour authorities.
The estimated value of goods imported
into Northern Ireland from all countries
in 1924 was approximately £69,000,000.
The figures of the imports from the Irish
Free State in 1930 are not yet available,
but the estimated value of the goods imported into Northern Ireland from all
countries, excluding the Irish Free State,
during this latter year was, approximately,
£52,000,000. The estimated value of
goods exported from Northern Ireland to
all countries in 1924 was, approximately,
£66,000,000. The goods exported in 1930
to all countries excluding the Irish Free
State, for which the figures, as in the case
of imports, are not yet available, were
valued at, approximately, £49,000,000.
I need hardly add that as these figures
are estimates they can only be used with
a certain amount of reserve.
WAGES OF WEEKLY WAGE
EARNERS.
Mr. DORMAN asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the
changes in the wages of the weekly wage
earners of Northern Ireland during the
years 1921 to 1930 inclusive, and the
approximate number of persons whose
wages were increased or decreased during
each year.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Such
general information concerning changes
in the rates of wages as is available
appears in the "Labour Gazette,"

CLAUSES 1 (Prolongation of period
of insurance under s. 3 (3) of principal
Act), 2 (Financial provision), 3 (Administration of approved societies), 4
(Short title and construction), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.

without Amendment to the House.

resume.

the Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont).

-(Viscount Charlemont).

Coursing of Hares Bill (S.).
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. LESLIE: On rising to move,
"That this Bill be now read the Third
time," I desire in the first place to express to you, Mr. Speaker, and to the
House my deep sense of the courtesy and
consideration which have been extended to
this Bill, and, I may add, to the author
of the Bill. I am aware that this Bill
will not satisfy those holding what I will
call the more extreme view. I am not
aware that that more extreme view is the
view which obtains generally in our area.
On the contrary, evidence has been furnished from another place, since this Bill
received a Second Reading, which points
very distinctly, as I think, in another
direction. I do not claim that this Bill
Coursing of Hares Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
will prohibit coursing. It was never the
intention of this House that the Bill
should take that form. But I do claim
that the Bill legalises nothing which is
at present unlawful, and I claim that it
will so operate as to make it easier than
it is at present to give full effect to that
spirit of our law which requires the
humane treatment of every creature committed to the dominion of man.
I say
the Bill does that without any violent
attempt to compel one section of our community to conform to the views of the
other. I presume to hope that the Bill
will not provoke any very hostile criticism
amongst those who sit elsewhere. I expect full weight will be given to that repeated decision of this House, which will,
I believe, be arrived at again in a few
moments. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I did not intervene
at any stage of the Bill, but before it
passes away from the House I should
like to say a few words expressive of the
minds of a good many people in this Northern community with regard to this
Measure. The Bill is rather an exceptional one, in the sense that I do not
recollect any parallel Measure, either in
the Imperial Statutes or our Statutes here,
under which any form of sport is put in administrative fetters. That, in brief, is the
effect of this Bill. We are dealing with
one class of sport only, and we cannot
disguise from ourselves the fact that in
particular aspects it is a poor man's sport.
There are other sports within our area
which are not poor men's sports, and I
think the criticism will be levelled against
the Bill that it is a Measure directed more
or less against the sporting activities of
a certain poorer class of the community,
while their more favoured brethren are
allowed to practise pastimes equally cruel,
and perhaps in some degree more cruel.
We have here in Northern Ireland, as
a form of sport, stag hunting. It exists
all over England. It is a cruel sport, a crueller sport even than coursing hares. We do
not intend in any way to control or fetter
it. I read quite recently in papers of
high respectability in England of an incident in the week before Christmas concerning the hunting of a stag in Somerset,
where the hunted stag, after a chase of 24
miles a very long chase compared with
coursing of a hare a few yards between
the moment of liberation and the moment
of death-the hunted stag, after a chase
of 24 miles, leaped the back wall of an
inn in a suburb of Taunton, sought refuge
there, and was there despatched. The
hunted stag was actually killed in the bar
parlour of the hotel, where it was held till
the hunters arrived. A remarkable thing
about it was that three lady hunters
watched the final scene.
In
The
A
I read also recently in the Belfast papers
how another Somerset hunt ended.
that case a hind was hunted by the Somerset and Devonshire hounds at Minehead.
The hind, after a prolonged hunt, sped
along a path leading towards the centre of
the town, burst through the back garden
of a house and tried to hide there.
hind was caught and a kill ensued.
remarkable fact is that a hind has not
been caught in that country since 1926.
That hind was killed at the quay, after
having been chased in the sea by motor
boats. That is a form of sport which I
think can be very cruel. The agony of a
stag, hunted through a distance of 24
miles, must have been agony intense and
of prolonged duration, compared especially
with the death crisis of a hare which is
killed in coursing in a matter of a very few
seconds. I have not read any instance in
our area as repulsive to humanity as those,
and yet the sport exists. Such things may
happen.
I quite agree that in this case the hon.
and learned Senator has effected a kind
of modus vivendi between opposing
parties. There is, as we know, a strong
party which wants to abolish hare
coursing root and branch. There is an
equally strong party that thinks the law
as it is, if properly enforced, is quite
sufficient. We know that under the present law a hare cannot be coursed unless
it is liberated in a fresh and fit state.
It cannot be coursed unless there are
reasonable means of escape. I do not
know if Senator Leslie's Bill gives any
greater protection. In fact, I think, it
gives less in some cases. But the Senator
has come in between two opposing
parties, and we know what the fate of
one who tries to strike a middle course,
especially in Ireland, very often is.
I submit that coursing is not a sport
to be decried. I do not know the particular driving force there is behind the
Bill of the hon. and learned Senator. 1
do not know the particular demand there
has been for it. I think I have heard
very strong criticism of the Bill itself
coming from divergent quarters. It is
really the result of an incident which
has, doubtless, been exaggerated. We
225 Coursing of Hares Bill-

23 APRIL 1931
Third Reading
all know that the Irish sporting
authorities are as deeply concerned over
that incident as any man in the Province.
They have taken very strong disciplinary
action. They are humane men.
So are
the authorities who control the Waterloo
Cup. There is no outcry against the
Waterloo Cup. Anyone who knows the
course knows the percentage of kills is
very high there. The hunted hare has no
great prospect of escape from the hounds.
The Bill may, I
am afraid, have
an unintended result of abolishing
coursing in this Northern area. I venture
to believe that many coursing men will not
care to submit themselves to the strict
regulations which are prescribed. Being
sportsmen they will pursue the sport,
and if they cannot get it in our area
they will try to get it in the Border
counties of Monaghan, Cavan, Donegal.
I do not know if this result is intended.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast.
The Mayor of Londonderry
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
I rather think it is not. It strikes me
as a very probable result of this Measure.
Mr. LESLIE: In reply to the hon.

to take advantage of this occasion to

if I remind the hon. and learned Senator
that the geographical boundaries of Northern Ireland do not include some of the
places he referred to, and if I further
refer him to the title of the Bill before
the House.

The prescribed application fee in

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the
