Marketing of Fruit Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Wild Birds Protection Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I regret that I am
not in a position to proceed. I ask that
the Report shall remain on the Paper.
Marketing of Fruit Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before I
formally move the Second Reading of this
Bill, I should like to inform the House
that, unfortunately, there is a mistake in
the amount of the fines on page 11 in the
copy of the Bill as first circulated to
Members. The copies now on the Table
are correct.
This Bill, to which I ask the House to
give a Second Reading, is a further instalment of the marketing legislation which
has been promoted in recent years with the
object of ensuring that certain of our agricultural exports reached the British
market in satisfactory condition. Parliament has already given its consent to
Measures dealing with eggs, potatoes and
dairy produce. As a result of the Marketing of Eggs Act, Northern Ireland eggs
now fetch higher prices in Great Britain.
than those of any of our highly efficient
foreign competitors. It can safely be said
that without this legislation Ulster eggs
would by now have largely disappeared
from the British market.
At present our potatoes are bringing
higher prices in the Liverpool market than
those from Lancashire. In the south of England they are fetching much higher prices.
than those from Germany. That, in brief,
is what the Marketing of Potatoes Act has
helped to achieve. The Dairy Produce
Act has, as yet, been operative for only a
brief period, but equally beneficial results
are anticipated from that Measure.
With regard to fruit-the subject under
consideration in the present Bill-it may
be observed that for a considerable time
public bodies, representing the agricultural
and fruit-growing interests, and also trade
representatives have been impressing upon
the Ministry the need for such legislation
if the fruit industry of Northern Ireland
is to be preserved from extinction. It is
true that the area under fruit in Northern Ireland is comparatively small.
Nevertheless our exports of fruit, mainly
apples, to Great Britain and the Irish
Free State were in 1929 valued at approximately £100,000. It is, however, indisputable that this export trade could be
considerably expanded and made more
profitable if the fruit were properly graded
and packed. That would mean more
money for the growers, and it might
reasonably be expected that if better
financial results were forthcoming commercial fruit growing would be taken up
seriously by many farmers who now regard fruit culture as of little or
economic importance.
no
With modern methods the growing of
fruit is no longer a gamble. Experiments
conducted by the Ministry and county horticultural staffs from 1922 onwards have
demonstrated that by improved methods of
cultivation satisfactory crops of apples can
be grown almost every year-not one year
out of two or three, as formerly. It is
satisfactory to note that the better systems advocated by the Ministry, as a result
of the experiments referred to, have now
been taken up with successful results by
progressive growers. It will be the business
of the horticultural education service to
see that the improved processes of cultivation are brought to the notice of all
growers, and on this work the staffs concerned are now concentrating. However,
if our growers are to receive maximum
profits, efficient production must be accompanied by efficient marketing, and that
means, in the first place, grading. It has
become more and more clear that, with
present marketing methods, Northern Ireland fruit is being driven off even the home
market by the overseas product. A glance
round the fruit shops in Belfast, or any
other of our cities and towns, will illustrate this because, invariably, the fruit
which is properly graded and which fetches
the best price is imported.
Broadly speaking, the present Bill falls
into two divisions. Firstly, as to fruit for
export, it is proposed that all merchants
who export fruit should be licensed and
should grade their fruit up to certain
standards. The Bill, of course, only refers
to fruit produced in Northern Ireland.
Licensed merchants will pay an annual fee
of £1. Permits, free of charge, will be
given to growers to export fruit grown by
them. In such cases grading will be required. It is provided, however, that
permits may also be given for fruit which
is being exported to bottling, canning or
similar establishments. In this case
grading may be dispensed with. It is intended that the Act shall not apply in the
following cases:
---
Marketing of Fruit Bill-
Second Reading
23 APRIL 1931
!
(a) To fruit not grown in Northern
Ireland but which is merely conveyed
across Northern Ireland for export.
(b) To fruit consigned to the Irish
Free State, except to such extent
as may
be found necessary and
practicable. This is
is following the
system established by the Marketing
of Potatoes Act, which has worked well.
(c) To small consignments.
as
Secondly, in regard to trade within
Northern Ireland, it is desired to emphasise that, so far as the home trade is
concerned, the compulsory provisions of
the Bill will not apply except in one
respect, and that is that steps will be
taken to prevent what is known
"topping.' At present, owing to this
nefarious practice, the man who grades
his fruit and packs it honestly does not
get his proper reward, for the simple
reason that the buyers have to regulate
their prices generally to cover losses due
to "topping." It is accordingly provided
that fruit sold or exposed for sale in a
container shall be packed in such a manner
that any fruit exposed to view fairly
represents in size, maturity, condition.
and variety, the entire contents of the
container. It is also provided that
types of containers may be prescribed for
particular classes of fruit. The remaining provisions of the Measure which
apply to the home trade are purely
voluntary, and are on the lines of the
National Mark system in force in Great
Britain. Briefly, persons who grade
their fruit for the home market up to
certain standards will be authorised to
use a grade designation mark which will
be a guarantee of quality.
As hon. Members are aware, there was
at one time very considerable opposition
to this Measure. That opposition was,
however, largely due to a misconception
of the provisions of the Bill. The
Measure has been very fully discussed in
the Press and elsewhere, with the result
that misunderstandings have been removed, so that it can be claimed without hesitation that the Bill now comes
before this House with the strong support of both the fruit-growing and
trading interests. It may be observed,
in conclusion, that all regulations to be
made under the Act will be drawn up in
close consultation with an Advisory Committee on which the growers will be
adequately represented. This will ensure that nothing unreasonable or impracticable will be attempted. I beg to
move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
say a few words on the Second Reading
of this Bill. While I am opposed in the
most hostile manner possible to the
principle involved in this Bill, at the
same time I think we must recognise
that in the very unsatisfactory state of
affairs that exist at the present time, an
inefficient and unjustifiable means of
remedying that state of affairs is better
than no means at all. It is upon that
principle that I do not intend to vote
against the Bill. The noble Lord had,
unhappily, referred to what he called the
success of the Marketing of Potatoes
Act, and he suggested more or less that
that was a justification for the introduction of this Bill. I think his remarks
were hardly justified, because so far as
the farmers are concerned, up to a few
weeks ago the Marketing of Potatoes Bill
had affected them very detrimentally
in the price of their produce. I hope
that when the inspectors are administering this Bill they will do SO in a
reasonable way and will not be too
drastic in their requirements.
""
I am sure Members on both sides of
the House will agree that nothing could
justify this nefarious practice of "topping,
and although I hate the principle involved in this Bill I am not prepared to
oppose it, because it will do something
to put a stop to "topping." With regard
to the regulations to be made under the
Bill, it is some consolation to know that
a small Advisory Committee is to be
appointed and that this Committee will
be consulted in formulating the regulations. At the same time, I do not think
that is sufficient. It is all very well to say
that these regulations will be laid on the
Table of the House, but I suggest that
steps should be taken to see that regulations made under any Bill should be
posted to Members of this House. When
I have been called upon in my professional capacity to look up regulations
I have been astounded at the drastic
character of some of them, particularly
those made under the Motor Acts. They
were more comprehensive and more farreaching than the Act itself was ever intended to be. Therefore, I think, that
in order to safeguard the public and to
facilitate the Members of this House in
the discharge of their duties an under-
Marketing of Fruit Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
taking should be given that these regulations will be posted to Members, so
that they will have an opportunity of
opposing them if they are contrary to
the principle of the Act.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise to support this Bill, and I hope the Ministry
of Agriculture will find their reward in
an improvement in the price of our fruit
overseas. There is one factor in connection with our fruit industry that should
not be overlooked, and that is that we
do not get sufficient sun to grow such
good fruit as they are able to grow in
other countries. However much we may
grade our fruit we shall still find that
fruit grown in sunnier climes will still
beat ours, and no regulations made by
the Ministry will alter that fact. I
would like to suggest to the Minister of
Agriculture, whom I am very glad to see
in this House, that those who have
hitherto so strongly objected to this Bill
should be put on the Advisory Committee. If they were, I am sure, they
would change their ideas and would become very useful members of society. 1
think that is a suggestion worthy of the
It is
Ministry's consideration.
sometimes as well to have our opponents as
well as our friends on a Committee such
as this. Either you will convince them
or they will convince you, and if you had
these people on your Committee they
would be able to put forward the
objections that certain people have to
this Measure.
In the matter of the apple trade inside
the country, I am glad one is not to be
tied down to strict regulations. I am a
seller of apples, and I trembled to think
of the regulations that might be made
under this Bill. However, I understand
that I can sell apples with black spots on
them inside this country, and that fact
will cheer a large number of farmers.
There are certain grades of apples that
will develop black spot. My Blenheim.
apples are very good, but they have been
affected with black spots and I should be
very sorry I could not dispose of them,
even if they have black spots. I was
approached by farmer friends on this
very point and they asked: "Are we not
to be allowed to sell our apples inside the
country if they have black spots?" I
hope the Minister of Agriculture will
answer in such a way as to allay the fears
of a great many of these apple growers.
Otherwise, I have pleasure in supporting
the Bill, and I cannot agree with the
views of Senator McAllister, who
practically called it a damnable Bill.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: There seems to be
general approval of this Bill, and perhaps
it is unnecessary for me to add my own
approval of it. But as I come from the
county that is sometimes called the orchard
county I should like to say a few words.
I took some trouble to find out what was
the right course to pursue in regard to
this Bill, because there was considerable
difference of opinion when the Measure
was first introduced. Charges of one kind
and another were made against those who
were for and against the Bill, but I pass
that by. I think the Bill is of considerable importance to this Province, and more
particularly to the county I come from,
because though the area is not very large
in comparison to the area of the whole
Province it is very considerable, and there
can be no doubt that if grading and packing were adopted, as they are in other
countries which compete with us, our
produce might be very much improved in
price. The condition of the apples in
Northern Ireland has been remarkably
improved by continual spraying, and the
pests, including the woolly aphis, capsid
bug and apple scab have practically
disappeared as a result of that continual
spraying. As other countries
adopted regulations to enforce packing
and grading I think we have no alternative
but to do the same, or else retire from the
business or accept lower prices for our
produce.
have
In the old days what was called the
laissez faire policy was adopted, and it was
said that if we let it alone the people who
graded their apples would command such
a good price that those who did not would
follow suit. That is a very slow process.
and by the time they had followed the
example of the successful growers their
market had disappeared. Competition is
extraordinarily keen, and it requires a
Bill of this kind to enable our apple
growers to compete successfully with the
produce of other countries. I understand
that at the present time there are 8,906
acres in the Province under fruit. That
is in 1930. The export of apples
was 5,543 tons in the same year. That is
notwithstanding the disadvantages under
which this business is conducted. Then
in County Armagh there are 5,000 acres
under apples, or rather were in 1930, and
from that 15,000 tons is the estimated
Marketing of Fruit Bill-
Second Reading
23 APRIL 1931
am sure.
This
yield, and £120,000 the value.
might have been very much increased, I
From January to May, in 1930,
the average price for Bramley seedlings in
Belfast market was only 12/- the barrel,
while American graded and packed apples
-packed under regulations-was 25/6. In
the period between August and December,
1930, the average price of Irish Bramley
seedlings in Belfast was 10/8 the barrel.
It had sunk to that. The American
apples, packed and graded under Government regulations, were 30/-. In the face
of figures of this sort something must really
be done.
I think what is proposed in this Bill will
really meet the difficulty, and will
probably raise apple culture to the same.
position as egg production and other
things that have been done by the
Measures the Government introduced and
carried through. I think we have every
reason to be grateful to the Government
for the action they have taken. I think
as time goes on, and this Advisory Committee sits, all these objections, bona fide
objections, to the provisions will disappear.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I just wish to
say a word or two in support of the Bill.
The hon. Gentleman who has just sat
down, coming from Armagh, the applegrowing county, is very much better
acquainted with the subject than I am,
and he has given us very interesting figures.
as to the results of the acreage and the
prices and so on. I should like to point
out that 80 per cent of the fruit in Northern Ireland is imported. My right hon.
and gallant Friend opposite speaks of not
being able to grow apples in this country.
I would agree with him so far as dessert
apples go, but for Bramleys or fruit of that
kind I think our country can grow as
good apples as any other. We have a fine
market across the Channel for fruit, and
if the apples were graded and in proper
condition we have a good market for our
fruit-growing industry in this country.
The great difficulty has been this
topping business, which applies to potatoes
as well as to apples. This Bill will prevent that, not only for shipments, but
for home-grown produce and for our markets at home. It will not press hard in
any way on the honest grower of fruit.
There is no unfair provision in regard to
the matter to which the hon. Senator
referred. I have no doubt the inspectors
will look after the growing in a reasonable
manner, and give us a mark similar to
what we have for eggs. The Bill does not
increase the growers' costs in any way,
and I certainly will be very glad to give
my support to the Second Reading.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to support this
Measure. I think it is a Measure that is
long overdue in the interests of the
masses of the people in our own area. I
have taken some little trouble since the
introduction of this Measure to go round
the vendors of fruit, and ascertain
whether there was any justification for
the general accusation about this nefarious system of topping; and without
exception every vendor complained that
they were continuously subject to this
nefarious system. It was making it
practically impossible for them to estimate when purchasing apples exposed for
sale in the markets, whether or not they
might make a reasonable profit as the
result of their labour of vending such
fruit.
A personal friend of my own, about
four weeks ago, bought a barrel of apples
in the Belfast market. It was late when
he bought the barrel, and he had no
time to examine more than the top row.
He asked the man from whom he got
them to deliver them at his store. They
were delivered at his store when the
market had closed. When he took the
first two or three rows from the top he
found the fruit below was of an inferior
quality. He, therefore, refused to accept
them from the fruit-grower who sold
them to him. The man, instead of
taking them away, entered into a long
controversy with him, pointing out that
the market had closed and that he would
not now take them back. I need not
tell
where
you
my friend told him to
take them.
I was also speaking to a gentleman
extensively engaged in this industry in
the Free State. He happens to be a
gardener in charge of a fairly large
garden, and selling a large quantity of
fruit in the Dublin market. When he
took charge of that orchard he immediately instituted a system of grading
of fruit which had not been done by his
predecessor. He informed me that in a
period of two years he increased from 50
to 100 per cent. the price commanded in
the market. He established a record in
the quality of the apples that were
bought, so that his apples were the first
235 Marketing of Fruit Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Dorman]
bought in the market and put up for
auction. He could demand and obtain
50 per cent. more than the apples which
were imported from Armagh into the
Dublin market.
There is another aspect of this matter,
and that is, that this nefarious system of
topping is inflicting far greater injustice
on the poorer working classes. If a man
goes to the market and purchases apples
for the purpose of vending them and the
apples are topped, he takes them to
his shop and he must necessarily obtain
some profit over and above what he paid
for them as remuneration for his labour.
If this system of topping is continued,
it is passed on in an unjust price to the
purchaser of the poorer grade of apples
at the bottom of the barrel.
I think the fruit growers will derive
an enormous advantage from the introduction of this Measure. As I read the
Bill, it will give the honest fruit growers
an opportunity of shaking themselves
loose from the middlemen, who are at
the present moment making exorbitant
profits as intermediary between
growers and the purchasers in the external market. I hope the House will
unanimously support the Measure.
the
Mr. BARCLAY: I support the Second
Reading of this Bill. I do so because I
believe it will be a great benefit to the
fruit growers and to the fruit industry
here in Northern Ireland. As most of
the hon. Members here to-day know
pretty well, there has been a great deal
of injustice to the consumer as far as this
trade is concerned. But now I am glad
to say by this Bill this will be removed
to a certain extent.
There is one thing that has just come
before my mind, and I would like to ask
the Minister of Agriculture what he intends to do in this respect. That is the
question of windfalls and apples that fall
while they are pulling the tree and thus
get damaged. What is going to be done
with those? I understand the regulations
are very strict as far as the exportation
of apples is concerned. But as far as
the home market goes they are not so
strict, and it has suddenly dawned on
me that probably these windfalls and the
apples that fall while they are pulling
them may be gathered and sold by
hawkers to the poor of the City of Belfast and other places. I would be very
pleased if the Minister of Agriculture.
would take this question into consideration and prevent in some way these
apples going into the city to be sold at a
cheaper rate. I know that for the home
market there will be a certain mark for
apples, but, as has been said in this House,
you can always drive a motor-car through
practically every Act of Parliament. We
want to stop a motor-car going through
this Act as far as we possibly can.
have great pleasure in supporting the
Second Reading of the Bill.
I
Major DOBBS: I think this Bill is a
very valuable aid to the marketing of
fruit from the North of Ireland. I was
buying some pears about two years ago
a few miles from London. After a few
weeks I had the curiosity, because these
pears were always very good and always
just ripe, to ask where they came from.
I suggested that they came from Essex,
but I was told "no," that they came from
the other side of the Atlantic. They came
in boxes, and in these boxes if you took
one pear out you knew exactly what the
others were in size, ripeness, and flavour.
It was a wonderful object lesson of what
can be done by method and organisation.
It appealed very much to the retailer,
because if he took one pear out he knew
exactly what they were and that the customer would be pleased. There is one
thing which answers the point made by
the hon. Member on my right, that is
with regard to the sun. The flavour of all
these pears was not as good as the flavour
of our own pears.
I think we may beat
the Americans.
There is another point I want to ask,
and that is in regard to Clause 13, Section
2, where the inspectors are given power
to go through the books of the sales of
fruit. Would it not be wise to put a
limit on that, and only allow them to go
through the books of that season's fruit,
and not the previous season's?
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir E. Archdale): I am very grateful to
the Members of the Senate for the
favourable reception they have accorded
to this Bill. I will endeavour to reply
to the several questions that were put by
hon. Members. Senator McAllister referred to the regulations that are to be
laid on the Table of the House, and
asked that they should be posted to
Senators. The present practice is that
adopted in Great Britain, but I will consider whether it is possible to send the
regulations to each individual Member.
237 Marketing of Fruit Bill

23 APRIL 1931
Adjournment
The hon. and gallant Senator (Colonel
Pakenham) suggested that we should put
on the Advisory Committee those growers
who had opposed the Measure in the first
instance. I think those growers were entirely mistaken in their opposition to the
Bill. They seem to have taken the Bill
the wrong way about. They talked as if
they were going to be put under all sorts
of difficulties in the conduct of their business, and that they would have to pay
heavy fees, instead of which they will have
nothing to pay. The only thing they
must not do is to top the apples they sell.
When the Bill has received the Royal
Assent we intend to put on the Advisory
Committee representatives of every section of the apple growers, opponents as
well as supporters of the Bill, because I
know quite well that when people have to
draw up regulations themselves it makes
a great deal of difference to their attitude.
The hon. and gallant Senator also referred
to the question of apples with black spots
being retailed for sale inside Northern
Ireland. If the barrel is properly topped
there will be apples with spots on the top,
and therefore there can be no complaint.
Senator Barclay spoke about windfalls.
Such apples can be sent to a canning or
jam factory, but if they are, the barrel
must be the same from top to bottom.
grower cannot put windfalls in the centre
or bottom and have good apples at the
top so that the buyer would be deceived.
We allow a man to send to the canning or
jam factory apples that have been bruised
or are badly shaped, but they must all be
of the same kind.
A
With regard to home-grown fruit not
being so sweet as imported fruit that has
had the benefit of more sun, while that
may be so, we can still beat the world in
cooking apples. It has been proved at the
London show and elsewhere that Northern
Ireland can grow better cooking apples
than any other part of Great Britain.
Every part of the Dominions grades and
packs the fruit sent to Great Britain in a
way that we hope Northern Ireland people
will grade and pack theirs. Senator
McAllister said we were a year behind in
starting this scheme. We might have
started it sooner, except that we would
have been told we were going too rapidly.
It is a very good motto to make haste
slowly when you are trying to deal with
people who are as conservative as some of
our apple growers are. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). I am very grateful to the
Senate for the support and help they have
given in connection with this Measure.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill be
now read a Second time') agreed to.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until next week." I think one day next
week will be sufficient to dispose of our
business, and that will probably be on
Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty Minutes after
Four o'clock, until such
date as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint.

Questions to Minister.
WAGES OF WEEKLY WAGE
EARNERS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the
of Mr.
Dorman:
ww
name
To ask the Leader of the House if the
Government will arrange in future to
publish the separate figures relating to
the changes in wages of weekly wage
earners in Northern Ireland.
Mr. DORMAN: In asking that this
question should be placed upon the Order
Paper, I had in view the summary of
the changes in the wages in Great
Britain and Northern Ireland from the
year 1921 to the year 1930, as given in
the Ministry of Labour Gazette for this
month. I think it is desirable, and
necessary, that the figures should be
given in such a manner that students
would be able to differentiate as between
Great Britain and Northern Ireland. I
find by the summary that during the
years 1921, 1922 and 1923, the increase of weekly wages is given as
£194,000 per week, and that during the
same three
years the reductions in
weekly wages was £10,681,100 per week.
After deducting the £194,000 from the
total decrease there would be a net decrease of £10,297,050 per week, which
multipied by fifty-two would be
£525,446,600 per annum.
This means
that during the seven succeeding years
the efficient demand for goods in the
Home Markets of Great Britain and Northern Ireland has been reduced by the
gigantic sum of £3,678,126,200, as the
inevitable result of the three years reductions.
30 APRIL 1931
Oral Answers

Oral Answers.
borough in Northern Ireland during
1930; the number of persons killed or injured in each such area during that period;
and the figures for 1929.
am
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I
afraid that I am not able to give the
figures which the hon. Member asks for
in the exact form set out in the Question.
I propose, however, with his permission,
to circulate with the Official Report two
tabular statements which will give him
the statistics available.
As Northern Ireland has suffered more
from unemployment than Great Britain
during the last ten years, I think it is
desirable that the figures as to comparative decrease in wages should be set
out separately, especially as our industrial workers in Northern Ireland are
largely engaged in the production of goods
for the world markets. It would be
helpful in enabling economists, in their
investigations, as to how far the enormous
sacrifices made by the weekly wage earners
has helped in the restoration of our
foreign trade, and what has been the
effects of lowering wages on the volume
of unemployment. In addition, it would
give us a permanent record of these
essential figures, from the date of the
establishment of our Government in
Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The Government will communicate with the Imperial Ministry to
see what can be done to meet the hon.
Member's wishes in the matter.
MOTOR ACCIDENTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the number of road accidents in connection with
motor vehicles in each county and county
The first table sets out by counties the
number of accidents in which vehicles of
all descriptions were involved, whether
motor vehicles, horse-drawn vehicles, or
such vehicles as steam waggons. With a
slight correction for the accidents caused
by horse-drawn vehicles, steam waggons,
and tramcars, these would be the statistics which the hon. Member requires.
The second statement may also interest
the hon. Member. It sets out the number of accidents in which investigation and
analysis of the reports showed that the
driver of the motor vehicle was to blame.
The accidents emunerated in the second
table are also, of course, included in the
first table.

Oral Answers.
Education Bill-
THE SENATE
RENTS OF SUBSIDY HOUSES.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the attention of the Government has been
drawn to a statement made in the Imperial Parliament by a Minister of the
Crown that it is now possible, under the
Imperial Housing Act of 1930, to build
houses in urban areas in England and let
them at rents of £12 a year, or four
shillings and eightpence a week; and
whether he can state the highest and
lowest rents of subsidy houses in urban
areas in Northern Ireland.
Mr.
CAMPBELL: The Imperial
Minister referred to is the Lord Privy
Seal (Mr. Johnston).
Viscount CHARLEMONT: While I
cannot trace the particular statement referred to in the Question, I am aware
that the Minister of Health, when introducing his Housing Bill in April last
year, expressed the opinion that in connection with re-housing schemes carried
out under the Bill it would be possible
to let some of the houses at any rate at
rents as low as 5/- a week, exclusive
of rates.
I have not information, however, to the
effect that any houses have yet been built
and let at those rents, as I cannot find
any reference in recent official statements
issued or uttered by the Minister of Health
to houses erected or let under the new
Bill.
On the other hand, I find it officially
stated that the rents of the smallest type
of houses built in the larger towns in
Great Britain under the Imperial Housing
Acts range from 6/4 to 9/6 a week,
exclusive of rates.
So far as Northern Ireland is concerned,
the jurisdiction of my right hon. Friend,
the Minister of Home Affairs, does not
extend to the approving of the fixation
of rents for subsidy houses, whether built
by local authorities or private individuals.
At the same time, however, information
is received from time to time as to rents
charged by local authorities. According to
that information the rents appear to vary
considerably according to the size and
nature of the houses and to the localities
in which they are erected. The lowest
rent charged for a kitchen type of house
by an urban authority is 5/- a week, and
the highest is 15/- a week for a more
superior type of house. The average rent
charged throughout the Northern Ireland
area is approximately 8/6 a week.

Education Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
I
The MINISTER OF EDUCATION
(Viscount Charlemont): In this House
somewhat less than a year ago I expressed a pious hope that I might never
have anything more to do with an
Amending Education Bill, or any Education Bill. However, this pious hope has
been frustrated in a very short time.
can only say that I do not think this Bill
is controversial. It is unlikely to cause
any apprehension to any Member of a
Regional Committee, and it will not even
quicken the heart of a Minister of any
religion. This is a short Bill, its object
being to remedy a flaw which has been.
found in that part of the educational
legislation dealing with compulsory school
attendance.
In the Education Act of 1923 there are
provisions in part VI.-which deal with
school attendance. The obligation is
laid on the parents of children between
the statutory limits of age to cause them
to attend school. If they do not do so,
it is enacted that the education authority
shall summon the parent before them,
and, if he does not appear or if his explanation is not satisfactory, a school
attendance order is made by the authority.
If this does not produce the desired result a summons for non-compliance with
the order is brought at petty sessions.
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
30 APRIL 1931
Later on in another part of the Actin Section 88 of the Act of 1923—it is
prescribed that if after inspection of any
school it is found that efficient or suitable
secular instruction is not being provided
the Ministry may notify it as inefficient
or unsuitable, and if the matters complained of are not remedied, it shall
thereafter be no defence in any prosecution that may be instituted for nonattendance that the child was actually
attending such a school. This, it was
thought, satisfactorily covered the cases.
of private schools. The Ministry had no
direct powers or jurisdiction over them,
but it ensured that they were reasonably
satisfactory by the indirect method
whereby the parents of children in
attendance at a school which was found
to be inefficient were made subject to the
same penalties as apply to cases of nonattendance.
This, as I have said, was considered to
be sound law as well as sound commonsense. The King's Bench Division, however, delivered judgment in Belfast last
November on a case stated by the
magistrates before whom a parent had
been summoned by an education authority.
The child had been in attendance at a
school which was not an ordinary school
recognised by my Ministry, and which
had been declared by the Ministry to be
inefficient. The learned judges held that
notwithstanding the later Section in the
Act of 1923 to which I have referred-
Section 88-no attendance order and no
prosecution could lie against such a person as the word "school" in the earlier
sections must be taken, in the absence of
closer definition, as meaning any institution, efficient or otherwise, which
could in ordinary parlance be called a
school. This is then the proper interpretation of the law as it stands
present, but it obviously results in an
unsatisfactory state of affairs and it is to
remedy this and to restore what was, I
am certain, the intention of the framers
of the Act that this Bill is now being
introduced.
at
The Bill imposes no hardship on any
one and my Ministry will not be called
on to exercise any more extensive or
stringent supervision over private schools
than it has been doing in the past. We
have been working in co-operation with
the various education authorities, and the
Ministry's inspectors have paid visits to
private schools which were known to
receive pupils within the compulsory
limits of age. These inspections were
merely to ensure that the instruction was
reasonably efficient for school attendance
purposes. Actually about seventy such
schools have been visited, and I think the
visits were welcomed by those in charge
of the schools. It is only right that this
power and this safeguard should continue. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: If the noble Lord
entertained the pious hope that he had
heard the last word on education in Northern Ireland-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No; the
last amending Act.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Or the last Act
likely to be passed by this Parliament
I think he must be an optimist of a very
sanguine temperament. It is perfectly
clear that neither the 1923 Act nor the
1930 Act defined what the noble Lord
thinks anyone of common sense should
know-what a school is. If the Education
Act ought to be clear on anything at all
it ought to be clear as to the definition
of a school. That was left in ambiguity,
and the noble Lord's views of what is
sound law and common sense differ from
those of another authority.
Here we have an amending Bill which
I think is disappointing in some vital respects. There are other sections and other
parts of the 1930 Act which require
amendment of far more drastic and urgent
character than the mere detail that this
Bill covers. Up to October, 1930, about
£750,000 of public money had been.
expended on the provision and equipment
of new provided schools and the reconstruction and equipment of transferred
schools. Up to the same month the
Ministry of Education had in fulfilment
of obligations taken over from the former Commissioners of National Education
made grants amounting to £21,000 for the
erection and equipment of new voluntary
schools under Catholic management, and
£5,000 for the reconstruction and equipment of existing Catholic voluntary
schools. I think this expenditure on
Catholic schools was prior to the 1923
Act. The ratio of public expenditure on
the provided and transferred schools to
public expenditure on the Catholic voluntary schools was about 30 to 1.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt the hon. and learned Senator, but
Second Reading
Education Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Speaker]
I think he is rather wandering from the
subject of the Bill under discussion at
the present time, and making a general
statement.
Mr. CAMPBELL: On the Second
Reading I was anxious to point out that
a matter of this kind was a thing that
would require immediate attention by the
Ministry far more than the Bill brought
forward, an amending Measure confined
to one petty detail. But if your Lordship thinks I am not in order I will not
for a moment persist. I think, however,
on the Second Reading of an amending
Bill I am entitled to raise the points which
are left unnoticed by the Bill, and which
I think ought to be noticed. My remarks
were nearly completed. There is one
matter that ought to have been dealt with
by the Bill, and that is the repairs to
voluntary schools. That is a matter left
untouched by the last Education Act, and
is a matter of recurring discontent. I ask
the noble Lord whether this Bill could
not in some way be made to deal with
this question. We know how the law
stands under the 1923 Act.
The

The provided and transferred schools
are kept in repair wholly at the public
charge. The schools under Four-and-Two
Committees are, as to one-half of the cost,
repaired also at the public charge.
Catholic voluntary schools-over
strong-have to bear every penny of the
cost of repairs, a vexatious burden on
these schools. It is not unreasonable to say
that when the Government were bringing
in this amending Bill at least this question of repairs ought to have been kept
in mind and an attempt made to remove a
recurring cause of discontent.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill before us,
which relates solely to amending the law
relating to compulsory school attendance,
is, if I may say so, a rather interesting
little Bill, in spite of the fact that it marks
the administration of some slight check
to the optimism of the noble Viscount on
a matter which I am sure he has the sympathy of all of us. The Bill is interesting
in another way. It is the first Bill that
has come before us drawing attention to
a flaw in the wording of our Education
Act, and that wording as viewed by the
courts. It is, I believe, although I cannot speak quite definitely, the first instance in which we have had to propose to
amend the law in consequence of a de-
I venture to say
cision of the courts.
those are matters on which we can congratulate ourselves, and I should like to
go so far as to remind those Members of
this House not with us in 1923 that it
is an extraordinary tribute to the skill of
those responsible for the Act of 1923, that
so very little alteration on those lines
should have been required, especially
when it is borne in mind that the Education Bill of 1923 and the Intoxicating
Liquor Bill of the same year, which is a
very complicated Measure, were both run
through Parliament at once. I think
that the public as a whole would do well
to reflect, when passing criticism on this
Parliament and everybody connected with
it-it would do them no harm to reflect
a little on these matters.
Again, the Bill is interesting in another way.
It reminds us of the fact
that our Acts both good and bad do come
constantly before the courts of law, and
that the courts of law will not be prone
to let through anything that is not expressed in the clearest possible language;
and the effect of that is to afford justification for the most careful examination,
not only in this House, but if I may say
so, in another place, of every detail of
every Bill that is presented.
The Bill itself will not of course
arouse, I imagine, the smallest opposition. We are all anxious that schools
should be efficient schools in order that
the object of the Act should be attained.
I shall suggest to the noble Viscount in
Committee a trival addition. Those who
heard the noble Viscount read out the
terms of Section 88 of the Act will have
observed that that Section states that if
as a result of inspection of the school it
is found that the education is inefficient,
but it does not tell us who is to make the
discovery. I am quite sure the intention
of the Act must have been that the
matter should be settled by the Ministry
on the report of the inspector, but as it
is, it is possible to suggest that it was the
education authorities and not the Ministry on whom the responsibility was cast.
I would suggest that we should add a few
words to the Section to make it clear.
All we have to do, it seems to me, is to
leave out the words "is found" and substitute the words
words "appears
"appears to the
Ministry." In the meantime I support
the Second Reading of the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I shall be
glad to consider the alteration in Com-
249 Marketing of Fruit Bill-
Committee

30 APRIL 1931
!
learned
it
mittee which the hon. and
Senator has suggested. Of course
means inspection by the Ministry's inspector. With regard to the other points.
፡ on the whole matter of education raised
by the hon. and learned Senator Campbell, I do not think that on the Second
Reading of what he quite rightly called
a trivial Bill, I can go into the general
matter of Protestant and Roman Catholic
authorities. If, on the other hand, on
some future occasion he would like to put
down a question to have a debate on the
subject I shall be only too pleased to
answer.

Marketing of Fruit Bill.
Order read for the House to be put
into Committee on the said Bill.
Moved: "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Restriction on sending
fruit to Great Britain, Irish Free State,
and Isle of Man).
Except as otherwise provided by this
Act, a person shall not send or attempt
to send fruit from Northern Ireland to
Great Britain, the Irish Free State or
the Isle of Man, unless he holds a
licence for that purpose under this
Part of this Act.
""
""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, after the word " licence," to
insert or permit. It seems to me
that the two words I suggest to insert are
required in order to form a connecting
link between Clause 1 of the Bill and the
subsequent Clauses 4 and 5, and when we
form a connecting link in that way it will
also enable us to disconnect the class
which definitely requires a licence from
the class which may be dealt with under
the provisions of Clauses 4 and 5. I think
if the House looks closely into the matter
they will see that this permit has been
treated rather as the Cinderella of the
Bill. The licence has been brought upon
the stage with an overture of the whole
band, but the permit is left out of it
until we come to Clause 4. It does not
really seem to me that the two things
are sufficiently connected. It may be the
intention of the Bill that where a permit
is given a licence will not be required.
But the Bill does leave us in doubt as to
whether or not in the case to which a
permit applies a licence is required in
addition. If we insert the two words
everything falls into its place. That is
really the important point. Is the permit to take the place of the licence in a
limited class of cases to which it applies,
or are permits and licences supplementary
to each other. I beg to move.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: The
permit is altogether different from a
licence. The licence is a much greater
thing. It is for the exporter. The permit is for small parcels of fruit going
across. It refers only to small parcels,
and I cannot see how the hon. Senator
can be disturbed about it. I am afraid
we cannot accept the Amendment.
Clause 4 makes it quite clear.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I must
enter into the question a little. more
carefully. What Clause one of the Bill
does quite definitely is to prohibit the
sending of fruit from Northern Ireland
to Great Britain. It does offer us a
kind of exception "except as otherwise.
provided." Now these words "except as
otherwise provided" may very well be
taken to be required as, in fact, they
are required for the purpose of Clause 9
of the Bill. It does not follow that such
virtue as they possess is exhausted by that
application, but they do not go quite far
enough.
We leave Clause 1 with a definite prohibition on sending fruit to Great Britain
and so on, unless under licence. We come
on to Clause 4, and we find that the Minister may upon such conditions as he thinks
fit grant permits authorising certain
consignments to be sent away, but we are
Marketing of Fruit Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
not told definitely whether that permit
is to be in addition to or in substitution
for the licence. We are not told whether
it is to be issued to the original consignee
or merely to the carrier, and the person
who acts on that permit is not given
We
definite protection for doing so.
might get over all that were it not for
the wording of Clause 5. Clause 5, Sub-
Clause (1), treats the licence and the permit as wholly separate entities, as they
are intended to be, but Sub-Clause (2)
goes on to speak about circumstances in
which a permit is required by virtue of
There are no cirthis part of this Act.
cumstances in which a permit is, in so
many words, required by this Act. They
are not there, although they may be intended to be there, and we can put them
there if on reconsideration the Government feels able to accept this Amendment.
I do not myself think the Clause is at all
clear without these words, and this Bill
ought not to be allowed to go to the public with an obscurity of this sort in it.
Sir E. ARCHDALE: The Advisory
Committee, and those present at eight
meetings all over the Six Counties were
quite clear about the object and effect of
this Clause. They had not the same difficulty as the hon. and learned Member
seems to have in deciding when a permit
is required and when a licence is necessary. I think the works "except as otherwise provided by the Act" make the
matter quite clear.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very sorry, but I
am quite unable to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 2 (Power of Ministry of
Agriculture to grant, revoke and suspend
licences), 3 (Standards of quality, etc.,
for fruit consigned under licences), 4 (Permits for consignments of fruit in certain
cases), 5 (Surrender and production of
licences, etc., and giving of information),
6 (Power to enter premises and examine
consignments, etc.), 7 (Seizure and detention of consignments), 8 (Contravention of
provisions of Part I of Act), 9 (Savings),
10 (Prescription and use of grade designations), 11 (Grade designation marks),
12 (Provisions as to sale of fruit in containers), 13 (Exemption of commission
salesmen under 17 & 18 Geo. 5, c. 6), 14
(Power of entry for purposes of Part II.
of Act), and 15 (Power of Ministry of
Agriculture to make rules) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 16 (Offences, prosecutions,
and application of sums).
(1) Any person guilty of an offence
under this Act for which no special
penalty is provided shall be liable on
conviction-
(a) in the case of a first offence to
a fine of one pound or any greater
sum not exceeding ten pounds;
(b) in the case of a second offence
to a fine of three pounds or any
greater sum not exceeding twenty
pounds;
(c) in the case of a subsequent
offence, to a fine of five pounds or any
greater sum not exceeding fifty
pounds, or, if in the opinion of the
court the offence was committed by
the personal act, default or negligence
of the person accused, and the court
is of opinion that a fine will not meet
the circumstances of the case, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding
three months.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
16, page 11, line 13, to leave out from
"fine" to "not" in line 14 (of one pound
or any greater sum). This is my old
enemy as, I am sorry to say, it is the old
friend of the Minister of Agriculture, and
that is the minimum fine. There is very
little fresh to be said about it. We
approach the consideration of the question
this afternoon fortified to an extent we
have not hitherto been fortified, and that
is by the fact that hon. Members in
another place, or some of them, have at
last become articulate upon this subject.
I think I am correct in saying that when
this Bill secondly saw the light of day,
and possibly firstly, but at any rate the
original Bill proposed a greater minimum
fine.
They have now come down to the
usual pound, but representations were, as
I understand, made suggesting that it
would be better if the minimum fine disappeared altogether. Some of us are constantly pressing that view in this House,
and I am afraid those of us who take what
I must describe as the normal modern view
as regards minimum penalties—that is,
that they should not be inserted in Bills
-the only thing we can do is to draw
Marketing of Fruit Bill-
30 APRIL 1931
Committee
!
attention to every occasion that it is
sought to make use of them, and put the
House to the trouble of dividing on the
point. For the moment, all that is necessary is to move that certain words be left
out, and that will make the penalty in
the case of a first offence a fine not exceeding £10. I beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I have spoken so
often against this principle of the
minimum fine that I do not intend to go
into the question in detail again to-day.
I do not think the Government are
honest in introducing this Clause, and I
am quite satisfied that it is done in
order that the collective effect of all
these minimum fines is to contribute to
the revenue of Northern Ireland. I am
quite sure that no responsible legal
adviser would advise any Government to
adopt this principle of the minimum fine
in any Act of Parliament. It is a great
impediment to the administration of
justice, it limits the discretion of any
tribunal, and it is contrary to the most
elementary principles of the administration of the law. On that ground, and
also on the ground that it is not an
honest way of raising revenue, I am
strongly opposed to the principle.
Sir E. ARCHDALE: Senator Leslie
seeks once more to bring out his pet
fancy. In this particular case it will be
observed that the minimum fine is prescribed only for attempts to export fruit
illegally. Offences of that kind are of
very serious character, involving the
export of fruit without a licence or permit, or the export of ungraded fruit in
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dobbs, Major A. F.
Dorman, Mr. Robert
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
The Mayor of Londonderry
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Barclay, Mr. W.
Charlemont, Viset.
circumstances where grading would be
required. For all other offences the
penalty is left entirely with the
magistrates. Very considerable concessions have been made in the amount of
the minimum fine, and it is regretted that
the Amendment cannot be accepted. We
have incorporated the minimum fine in
several other Acts, and we find it is very
necessary.
Mr. LESLIE: I have only to add that
the court is perhaps the best judge of
the proper amount of penalty to be inflicted. A regards the reference to cases
where the minimum fine has been inserted
in other Acts, I can only say that as the
minimum fine was in those other Acts
it is quite impossible to say what would
have happened if it had not been.
There might have been smaller or larger
penalties, we do not know. There
18 a point in the remarks of Senator
McAllister that this is a method of
raising revenue, and the revenue is
apparently to be appropriated in aid of
the expenses of the Ministry. There is,
therefore, apparently some incentive to
insist on a fine, but I maintain, as I
always do, that it is for the court to
settle these matters, because the court
has heard the case. It is not for Parliament to endeavour to forecast everything
that may possibly happen. I cannot, of
course, withdraw the Amendment.
Question put: "That those words be
left out."

Not Contents, 15.

Provided that such consent shall not
be necessary where proceedings are required to be taken under the provisions
of Part I. of this Act with respect to

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, at the
end of Sub-Section (5) of Clause 16 to
Marketing of Fruit Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
add, "or in any case arising under Sub-
Section (6) of Section 6 of this Act." I
imagine that this Amendment is really
one which carries out the intention of the
Bill, but I cannot suppose for a moment
that it is intended that if it should
happen, as is suggested as a possibilityundoubtedly a remote possibility in Sub-
Clause 6 of Clause 6-that an authorised
officer should disclose any information unlawfully and thereby render himself liable
to a penalty of £50, I cannot imagine it
is the intention that the prosecution of
that officer should not be undertaken
without the consent of his own superiors,
the Ministry of Agriculture.
Sir E. ARCHDALE: I will accept the
Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 17 (Application of Act):-
The provisions of this Act shall apply
to fruit grown or produced in Northern
Ireland and not to any other fruit; and
if, in a prosecution for an offence under
this Act in relation to any fruit, the
question arises whether such fruit was
grown or produced in Northern Ireland,
the burden of proving that such fruit
was not so grown or produced shall rest
upon the person charged with the
offence.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
17, to leave out from the word "fruit" in
line 28 to the end of the Clause. Here
again there is a slight difficulty. The
objection to the words I am moving to
leave out is partly, of course, that it is
rather unusual to require a man who is
charged to prove his innocence, instead of
leaving it to the prosecution to prove he
is guilty. That is one objection. The
second and more important objection is
that the wording of the Clause requires
him to prove a negative in order to establish his innocence. Accordingly I suggest
leaving out the words dealing with that.
But if the Minister thinks something of
this nature would be of assistance to him
in carrying out the Act there would be a
possibility which I personally would agree
to-there would be a possiblity instead of
leaving out the words I am suggesting, of
inserting after the word "that" in line 31
the words "that he had reasonable grounds
for believing," because if a man establishes that he had reasonable grounds for
believing that would be sufficient to ensure
his acquital. So if the Ministry are prepared to accept the other, I would ask
leave to withdraw and move this in
substitution.
Mr. McALLISTER: The hon. Senator
who has just spoken has said that this
Bill is contrary to the general principle of
the administration of the law, and I think
that no grounds have been shown why in
a Bill like this the general principle of law
should be departed from. It is entirely
contrary to the principle that a man is
presumed to be innocent until he is proved
guilty. I think it is a very bad principle
that a man should be called upon to prove
his own innocence, instead of the Crown,
with all their resources behind them, being
called upon to prove the guilt of the
person charged. I would earnestly appeal
to the House on the question of the principle of legal administration and of justice
to support the Amendment moved by the
hon. and learned Senator.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: Clause
13 provides that the Act shall apply only
to fruit grown in Northern Ireland, and
it further provides that if in legal proceedings a question arises as to whether the
fruit was grown in Northern Ireland or
not, the burden of proof that the fruit was
not so grown shall rest upon the defendant. A very similar provision appears in
other legislation already passed by Parliament, the Livestock Breeding Act and the
Horse Breeding Act. In this particular
case the arrangement is both reasonable
and necessary for the proper administration of the Act. Suppose, for example, a
person is exporting fruit from Northern
Ireland without a licence or permit, and
that he is brought into court. He asserts
that the fruit in question originated outside Northern Ireland, say, America or
Australia, and is not therefore within the
scope of the Act. It is surely perfectly
fair to ask such a person to produce some
proof that his contention is correct. Such
proof should be readily available to him.
He will know from whom he purchased the
fruit, how it was invoiced, and so on. In
the circumstances I regret very much that
I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman has just made himself an excellent
case for the substitution. He is asking
that the man is to prove that the fruit
was not so grown. He cannot do that,
257 Marketing of Fruit Bill-

30 APRIL 1931
Committee
but he can give proof that he has reasonable grounds for believing. If the right
hon. Gentleman will accept that I will
ask leave to withdraw Clause 17 down to
the word "that" can be put and then
I can move the other. A man may very
well be asked to produce evidence that he
had reasonable grounds for believing that
the fruit was not grown in Northern
Ireland.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I am sorry to
intervene again, but the speech of the
Minister of Agriculture has made me
more hostile to the Clause as it stands
than I was before. His justification for
a departure from this principle-almost
a Magna Charta-is the statement that
the Ministry have been in the habit of
introducing legislation of this description.
As one associated with the administration of the law I have some idea of what
this means. I say that if we as a House
pass a Bill without any justification or
special cause shown, without any attempt
to show why the general principle should
be departed from we will be passing a
most reactionary Bill and legislating in a
most reactionary manner.
I can
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE:
see no difficulty in a man who is sending
fruit, saying where he got it. It is very
different for the Ministry to do that, but
with a man sending fruit it is easy for
him to say where he got it.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: With regard to
this question of asking a man to prove a
negative, it seems to me that if the word
"elsewhere" were substituted it would
remove the difficulty. That is where it
is suggested that the burden of proving
that such fruit was grown or produced
in Northern Ireland, it would read that
the fruit was grown or produced elsewhere.
He would not have to prove a negative.
Mr. LESLIE: The point is that a
man cannot prove whether it was or was
not so grown.
The most he can do is to
prove that he has reasonable grounds for
believing that it was grown outside. That
is possible, and that is the real point.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: I would
accept Senator Armstrong's Amendment.
Mr. DORMAN: I think the Government should accept the words "reason-
""
able grounds for believing.' Assuming
I
obtained
had purchased fruit, or
from a third party a quantity of fruit,
and having a licence to export it across
the water, reasonable belief that that
fruit has been grown in Northern Ireland, I think, should be sufficient for me
to prove.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: Where
would that come in?
Mr. LESLIE: The substitution I suggested was that the words in the Clause
as far as the word "that" in line 31 should
stand, and after the word "that" we
should insert "had reasonable grounds for
believing that." That will put the burden
of proof on defendant that he had reasonable grounds for believing.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: It does
not seem to me to be correct. I am
afraid I cannot accept the Amendment of
the Senator opposite (Mr. Leslie). I
accept Senator Armstrong's Amendment.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I move that-
Mr. SPEAKER: Senator Leslie's
Amendment is before the House.
Mr. LESLIE: In that case, and for
that purpose I would be prepared to withdraw my Amendment, on the understanding that the Minister is prepared
to introduce the word "elsewhere.'
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I beg to move in
line 31 to leave out the words "not so."
If that is accepted I will move the insertion of the other word.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I beg to move in
line 32, after the word "produced" to
insert the word "elsewhere."
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to explain
that I am joining in the acceptance of
the Amendment on the grounds that it
will be for the court to determine the
amount of proof of the fruit being grown
elsewhere that will satisfy it. That matter is to be left in the hands of the court.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move "That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday." I should like to say
that I think the days on which business
will be transacted next week will be
Tuesday and Thursday.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not know if there is
any use in reminding the noble Viscount
that Tuesday will be a rather inconvenient
day for at least three Members of this
House, owing to the fact that three of us
are likely to be engaged in Committee on
a Private Bill, which can scarcely be completed on the morning of Tuesday. It will
involve an adjournment. The Committee
is expected to take all Tuesday and possibly
Wednesday. I do not know if it is possible to do all the necessary business on
Thursday.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It would
not be possible to do the necessary business
on one day, except by having recourse to
an expedient that I tremble to mention.
I am also informed that the Royal Assent
to a Bill has to be given on Tuesday.
As the Committee to which the hon. and
learned Senator refers is sitting on both
Tuesday and Wednesday, I think this
House must sit on Tuesday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty minutes after Four
o'clock, until Tuesday
next.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 5th May, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
The Royal Ulster Constabulary Allowances (Consolidation) (Amending) Order,
1931.
The Royal Ulster Constabulary Pay
(Amending) Order, 1931.
Presented to the House, pursuant to
Act, and ordered to lie on the Table.
Questions to Minister.
BETTING HOUSES IN NORTHERN
IRELAND.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to ask the
Leader of the House, if he can state how
many Open Betting Houses there are in
Belfast and the other towns of Northern
Ireland; how many of the owners of such
houses were prosecuted on behalf of the
Crown during the year 1930; what were
the amount of fines imposed on the owners
of such houses; and if any of the owners
of such houses have ever been sentenced
to imprisonment for repeated violation
of the Law.
When I visit the libraries in Belfast I
find that the Corporation has done all
that can be done by a public body to discountenance this habit of betting in the
community. But when I come out of
the libraries I find that within a hundred
yards of it there are no fewer than three
or four open betting houses presenting
temptation to the public. Only this
morning I happened with a friend to go
to one of the libraries, and across the
street, standing in an entry, was a child
of about six years of age. He came over
and asked us if we were looking for the
"bookie," and he went round the corner
and showed us where the "bookie" was,
that we did not see or know about.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not
clear as to what exactly is meant by the
term "open betting houses" mentioned
by the hon. Member in this question, but
if this refers to premises on which betting
is known to have been carried on, an
exhaustive and extensive enquiry would
be necessary in order to ascertain the required particulars in regard to the
various towns in Northern Ireland.
Some time ago, however, the numbers
of such premises in Belfast and Derry
were estimated to be 218 and 12 respectively, and perhaps this information.
will meet the requirements of the hon.
Member.
Particulars are not available as to the
number of cases in which the owners of
such premises have been prosecuted, but
the total number of prosecutions for
offences against the betting laws during
1930 was 1,121, and the fines imposed
amounted to £4,741.
I am unable to say whether any owner
of such premises has ever been sentenced
to imprisonment for repeated violations
of the Betting Acts, but I have ascertained that no such case has occurred
during the last three years at any rate.
Mr. DORMAN: May I ask the right
hon. Gentleman if the Government will
consider the advisability of taking proceedings against the landlord of such
premises?
CHARLEMONT: I must
Viscount
have notice of that.
IMPRISONMENT FOR NON-PAY-
MENT OF DEBTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if he can state how
many persons were imprisoned last year
in Northern Ireland under county court
orders following non-payment of debts
of a civil nature due under county court
decrees; and how many were imprisoned
under petty sessions warrants following
non-payment of debts of a like nature due
under petty sessions orders, but exclusive
in either case of persons imprisoned for
non-payment of fines.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Five persons were imprisoned last year in Northern
Ireland under county court orders following non-payment of debts of a civil nature
due under county court decrees, and fortyfive were imprisoned under petty sessions
warrants following non-payment of debts
of a like nature due under petty sessions
orders. These figures do not include the
cases of any persons imprisoned for nonpayment of fines.

Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional

Marketing of Eggs Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not in

this Bill to-day, and wish to defer it to
