Electricity Supply Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: My hon.
Friend (Mr. Long), when moving the
Second Reading, in November last, of the
brief Measure which was the forerunner
of this Bill which is now before the House,
gave some account of the general lines of
development which we propose should be
taken in regard to the distribution of
electricity throughout the Province.
need only recall that we had the advantage
of the services of a very well known consultant, Mr. J. M. Kennedy, who drew
up a very able report on the whole subject.
This report has been in the hands of hon.
Members for some time, and has also been
available to the general public, so that a
full opportunity has been afforded for considering its recommendations. The present Bill goes into full detail in regard to
the constitution of the Electricity Board,
and the way in which it will operate. For
the present the work will be confined to
what is called the south-eastern area,
which was the subject of Mr. Kennedy's
first report.
In regard to the board itself, I do not
imagine that there can be much criticism
of that part of the Bill which regulates its
constitution and general powers and
duties. An important matter to notice is
that an advisory committee may also be
established, for the purpose of giving the
Minister of Commerce general advice and
assistance on the question of improvement
and development of electricity supply, and
will be principally representative of
the local authorities concerned and the
chambers of commerce
The next part of the Bill—that is,
Part II-deals with development areas for
which schemes are required. There is
also dealt with in this part of the Bill
the important question of the effect which
the proposals will have on existing undertakings. It may be desirable, first of all,
to explain the difference between the terms
"statutory undertakers" and "non-statutory undertakers". The former may be
a local authority or company who have
been authorised by Parliament in a special
Act or Order made under the Electricity
Acts to supply electricity for all purposes
in a specified area of supply. The Acts
or Orders usually prescribe maximum
prices to be charged, and impose on the
undertakers the obligation to supply all
consumers on the same terms under like
circumstances. The latter may be any
company or person providing a public
supply of electricity without statutory
authority. Such companies or persons
have no defined area of supply and are
under no obligations or restrictions as
to the manner in which the supply will be
given or the prices charged.
The Board are required from time to
time to prepare and submit for the approval of the Ministry of Commerce, the
development schemes to which I have referred, and the area covered by each
scheme is referred to in the Bill as a
"development area". It is proposed
that
the recommendations made in
Mr Kennedy's report for the south-eastern areas shall be deemed to be a development scheme for that particular area.
Under such scheme the Board will have
power to distribute electricity to consumers in all districts included in a development area, which are not within the
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading
5 MAY 1931
E

area of supply of existing statutory undertakers. The Board will have power to
acquire compulsorily all the undertakings
in a development area, whether statutory
or non-statutory. The Bill, however, provides that as soon as the Board constructs
or acquires a main transmission line in
close proximity to any undertaking, the
Board shall exercise its compulsory powers
within three years, except where an agreement to the contrary is entered into with
the undertakers, or where the Ministry of
Commerce see good reason for directing
otherwise. This has the effect of limiting
the element of uncertainty which would
be detrimental to the development of
existing undertakings.
These provisions apply alike to all
statutory and non-statutory undertakings. On the passage of the Bill through
another place, however, an Amendment
was inserted providing that in the case
of a statutory undertaking owned by a
company the Board shall not exercise its
powers to acquire the undertaking in any
case where the Ministry of Commerce, on
the representation of the undertakers
and after holding a local inquiry,
determines that the acquisition of the
undertaking would not be in the interests
of the consumers in the undertaker's
area of supply. The object of this
Amendment was to secure that where an
area is being supplied by private enterprise in a satisfactory way the undertaker
may be allowed to carry on.
Outside development areas the Board
has power to acquire existing statutory
undertakings by agreement, and also to
apply to the Ministry of Commerce for
orders authorising the Board to become
statutory undertakers in any area.
In
regard to the terms of acquisition of existing undertakings; in the case of a local
authority undertaking no price will be
paid, but the Board will take over the
existing debts and liabilities of the undertaking and carry on. In the case of
other undertakings, where the Board and
the undertaker are unable to come to
terms, the matter will be determined by
arbitration, and the arbitrator is required by the Act to have regard to the
following considerations:-
Firstly, the market value of the works,
plant, etc.;
Secondly, the value of the goodwill as
indicated by the trading results of the
undertaking;
Thirdly, the reasonableness of the rates
of charge made for the supply of electricity by the undertakers;
Fourthly, the provisions of any Act of
Parliament of Special Order which provide for the undertaking being acquired
compulsorily by the local authority.
I hope I have made clear what the Bill
does to protect the interests of existing
undertakings. I think that everything
reasonable has been done in this connection.
are
The financial provisions of the Bill
also deserving of a brief mention.
The Electricity Board will have power to
borrow the necessary capital and to issue
stock, and provision is made for a Government guarantee at the beginning as to
the principal and interest of any loans
which the board may raise. After five
years' time any such guarantee will cease,
when it may be anticipated that the
board will have consolidated its position
and will have reached the normal
revenue-earning stage. I may point out
in this connection that the local rates will
not be called on to carry any of the
financial responsibility, and that the
board will be required so to carry on its
operations as to balance receipts and
expenditure.
The remaining parts of the Bill are
largely concerned with matters of detail,
such as are necessary in a proposal of this
kind, and I need not enter into them
separately. I think that the Bill should
commend itself to the House as being a
genuine effort on the part of the Government to introduce an up-to-date system
of electricity distribution throughout the
country, and in this matter we are only
following the lead of all progressive
countries elsewhere. Quite apart from
the immediate employment which will be
occasioned by the constructional work,
the successful inauguration of the scheme
will lead to more economical production
and to the development of industry outside the larger centres of population. I
beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I should like at
the outset to say that I am in favour of
the principle of this Bill on the ground
that we are all anxious for the future development of Northern Ireland from an
electrical or any other point of view.
the same time I do not think this is a
helpful Bill. It is not a Bill that inspires
any confidence so far as I am concerned.
It is vague and indefinite, and so far as
At
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
power
manage
this
I can see all that it does is to give wholesale
that should be confined to the
legislature, to this Electricity Board and
the Ministry of Commerce, who will
with untrammelled
sway
whole question of electrical development.
I have grave fears and misgivings as to
what will be the result of this Bill. I
have no real confidence that the Bill will
effectively do what it is hoped by the
Government it will do. If it fails in that
respect it will have a very detrimental
effect instead of a beneficial one, because,
after a little more experience is gained,
and we have acquired more knowledge of
this subject, the load that will have to be
carried, the legacy left by this Bill will,
in my judgment, seriously and prejudicially affect the efficiency of any new
and up-to-date Measure that may have to
be introduced to remedy the defects of
the present Measure.
Of course, in a highly technical Bill of
this description the Government have the
advantage over private Members, because
private Members have no facilities by
which they can consult experts to examine
the work of the experts on whose recommendations the Government have brought
in this Bill. For that reason I suggest
to the noble Lord, the Leader of the
House, that if the experts, technical and
commercial, upon whose advice this Bill
was introduced, have been unanimous in
recommending it, then he should give that
information to the House. On the other
hand, if they have not been unanimous,
this is such a big and far-reaching question, one beyond party politics entirely,
that the House is entitled to know why
the experts did not come to the same conclusion. If any of these experts are
against any of the provisions of this Bill
I think Members of this House are entitled to know upon what their opinion
is based, in order that we may have an
opportunity of judging impartially between the two sets of opinions.
I would also like to know what opportunities these experts had of going
thoroughly into this very big question.
I do not know where they came from, but
if they came from Picardy I would like
to know what opportunity they had of
studying the requirements of the agricultural community of Northern Ireland,
and of the people in the small provincial
towns in Fermanagh and Tyrone. I do
suggest very deliberately to the House
that if this Bill has been drawn up upon
the recommendations of experts who have
come to Northern Ireland from London,
Manchester, Glasgow, or anywhere else,
experts who are accustomed to deal with
matters of this kind in these large cities,
if they have been here for only a few
days, and were given a map of Northern
Ireland, and told the population according to the census, I suggest most seriously
that that is not sufficient data and knowledge upon which to deal with this vital
For
and far-reaching question.
that
reason I would in all earnestness ask the
noble Leader of the House to communicate
this information to the House before
asking Members to finally pass the
Measure.
From another point of view I think
this Bill is founded upon an entirely
wrong basis. It is a piecemeal Bill.
There are going to be several centres, and
I understand from those who have a knowledge of this matter, that the greatest
advantage to be got from electrical output is derived from unified control by
which you will have one set of head
charges instead of a number. Under this
Bill you are going to have, in all the
counties that are affected by it, different
offices with separate staffs and separate
sets of employees, so that the head charges
are bound to be five, six, seven, or eight
times what they would be if the whole
output was unified and worked from one
central control point. That is what has
been done in connection with the Shannon
scheme, and in other large schemes elsewhere. After our experience of the last
few years I think the time has now
arrived when we should take a broader
and wider view, when the parochial outlook should not be allowed to prevail,
especially when large and important
interests are at stake. I think the
Government might have ascertained if it
were possible to obtain a supply of electricity from the Shannon scheme, and
upon what terms, and if the terms were
more beneficial than they are likely to be
under this Bill then, in the present state
of affairs-on which I am not going to
dwell now-it was the bounden duty of the
Government, as the trustees of the best
interests of the people of Northern Ireland, to get their supply from the best,
the cheapest, and the most economical
source.
I notice that throughout this Bill
everything the Electricity Board has to
do is subject to the confirmation of the
Ministry of Commerce. I think I am
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading.
5 MAY 1931
R
entitled to give to the House some evidence of what the Government have
recently done as regards the confirmation
of electricity schemes, and I hope that
before this Bill reaches the Statute Book
the House will take steps to see that such
mismanagement will not occur under this
Bill, because it would be fatal to the
whole electrical development of Northern
Ireland. Within the past year or so, in
my own town of Ballymena, application
was made to the Government for power
to take over the electricity supply of the
town. In the first place I should think
it would be an elementary principle in
the purchase of any electricity or other
undertaking to have as few profits as
possible intervening between the output
stage and the time the article reached the
consumer. But what did the Government confirm in the case of Ballymena?
At least three profits have to be paid by
the consumer in Ballymena from the time
the electrical energy is generated until it
reaches the consumer. It is generated
by B, sold to C, sold again to D, and then
to the consumer. I respectfully submit
to the House that that was a violation of
the most elementary business principle,
and I will give the House the financial
results of that policy in a moment.
The point is this. If the experts
who advised the Government to confirm
that arrangement in the case of Ballymena are the same gentlemen who are
advising the Government in connection
with this particular Bill, then I say there
is a hopeless and futile outlook for the
present Bill. Under the Ballymena
arrangement, which was confirmed by the
Government, the Ballymena Urban Council borrowed £25,000. The Government
refused to lend this sum, and I understand the Council had no arrangement
made as to where the money was to come
from when the contract was entered into,
That £25,000 was to be repaid on a short
term loan of fifteen years. There was a
fixed annual payment of £500 to the
Antrim Electricity Supply Company for
twenty-one years, plus 3 per cent. loss on
transmission, and 10 per cent. for use of
transformer. That does not include
overhead charges and local expenses of
supplying and putting in electrical
appliances. The net result is that the
ratepayers of Ballymena under
arrangement confirmed by the Government have to pay this year £500 in the
first instance to the Antrim Supply
Company; interest on the loan, £1,250;
first year's repayment of the loan,
£1,600, a total of £3,350, plus 3 per cent.
loss on transmission, and 10 per cent. for
the use of the transformer. With regard
to the income, there are 80 subscribers
at the present time, but if you take the
number as one hundred, at £10 a year
each, you have a gross income of £1,000
a year. That is an electricity scheme
recently confirmed by the Government,
presumably on the advce of the same experts as will advise the Government in
connection with this Bill.
Under the Ballymena scheme there is
an annual loss of £2,250, which, I understand, means an extra rate of 2s in the
£. I contend that had the Government
made an arrangement to take a direct
supply from those who generate it, such
as the Belfast Corporation, it would have
proved very much cheaper and more
economical. I am not going to throw
bouquets at the Belfast Corporation, but
it is an admitted fact that they have
made a first class job of their electricity
undertaking. Private manufacturers
are generating electricity at a very much
smaller rate per unit, both for lighting
and heating, than the local undertaking
in Ballymena can do. In the Free State
the cost of electricity is 4d per unit,
including all the loss in transmission over
a very wide range of country running
to many, many miles. In Belfast it is
5d. a unit; in Ballymena it is 8d., and
Ballymena is the latest of the lot. With
these facts-for they are facts-before us
I would ask the House to be very careful
before handing over full control, because
that is what this Bill does, of the future
development of the electrical industry in
Northern Ireland. I think it is not a
hopeful outlook. To my mind this Bill
is very indefinite and very vague, and
although I hope and trust it will be successful beyond the most optimistic ideas
of the Government, still I have the
gravest misgivings as to what is going to
take place.
There is just one little matter I would
like to stress before sitting down, and that
is, in this area of County Antrim if one
passes along one can see these electrical
standards right in the centre of small
fields of an acre or two acres. The ordinary small farmer obviously is not considered or his convenience consulted when
these poles are put up. I think with
very little inconvenience and probably no
additional cost it would be quite as easy to
put these poles in a corner of the field,
Electricity Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
which would not prevent the farmer to
the same extent labouring or tilling his
land.
Mr. GLENDINNING: Whatever the
reception by the House of this Bill may
prove to be, it cannot be denied that
electricity is one of those phenomena or
products of nature that are becoming as
necessary to mankind as the air we breathe
or the water we drink. It is satisfactory
to know that the Government are keeping
pace with Great Britain and the rest of
Ireland in providing for the use of electricity in the cheapest form by the general
community. I myself heartily trust that
the industry in the Province will increase
to a degree to demonstrate, if not the
necessity, at least the advisability of this
Measure. I am sure, with regard to any
risk incurred by the Bill, the Electricity
Board will not put any of the development schemes in any area into operation
until they have reliable data which will
show a certainty of profitable work.
With regard to any co-ordination or
clashing of interests of the Electricity
Board and the Corporations of the county
boroughs, particularly with respect to the
important generating stations in Belfast
and Londonderry, I can see no reason
why the Board should be empowered to
sell electrical fittings while a similar power
is withheld from the large municipal
undertakings. A question will also arise
under part 5, which refuses any compensation on acquiring undertakings in respect of goodwill, even when the profits.
of the undertaking had been applied in
relief of rates. I myself am prepared to
support the Bill. I believe in moving
with the times, and I shall certainly
follow the progress of the Bill through
the rest of its stages with great interest.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House; the House to be in Committee on Thursday.
Education Bill.
Order for the House to be put into

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this House do now resolve
itself into Committe on the said Bill.
On Question, Motion agred to.
In Committee accordingly.
(Viscount BANGOR in the Chair).
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of provisions
of 13 and 14 Geo. 5, c. 21, as to school
attendance, 20 and 21 Geo. 5, c. 14).
*

(2) In section eighty-eight of the
principal Act the following words shall
inserted after the word
be
cerned": :-
"con-
"and a parent may be summoned
to appear before the education
authority, and a school attendance
order may be made upon a parent,
under Part VI. of this Act, notwithstanding that his child is in attendance at such school."
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, Sub-Clause (2), after "Act to insert
"for the words 'is found' there shall be
substituted the words 'appears to the
Ministry' and." As I mentioned on
Second Reading, in the Section of the 1927
Act, which it is proposed to amend, it
struck me there is a possible ambiguity.
At the commencement of Section 28, it
seems to me that it may be argued that
the finding of the inspector might be intended to be acted on by the education
authority or even by the school committee,
whereas the intention, I am sure, was that
it should be the Ministry that should be
responsible for acting on the report of
their own inspector. It might therefore
be worth while, as we are amending the
Section, to make the slight change there.
The necessity or desirability for the
change seems to me to gather force from
the words the noble Viscount is proposing
to add to the Section, because the effect
of the alteration which I am suggesting
will be to put, so to speak, the whole
weight of the Ministry behind the notification sent to the school authority to the
effect that if the school does not mend its
ways and give proper and efficient education, the school will cease to be recognised
as a school, and that parents may be summoned before the school attendance committee, and if they are recalcitrant they
may be prosecuted. The full weight of
the Ministry will be behind all these proceedings. For that reason I suggest we
should make the addition which stands on
the Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am quite
willing to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Wild Birds Bill-

5 MAY 1931

Bill reported, with Amendment, to the

Wild Birds Protection Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Moved: "That this Report be now received." (Mr. Campbell).
I
Colonel SINCLAIR: At this stage of
the progress of the Bill I desire to make
a few observations of a general character.
The hon. and learned Senator who introduced this Bill has discharged, in my judgment, a very important public duty.
think he has earned the gratitude not
merely of bird lovers, but of the community. When the community comes to
understand its full significance I think it
will itself recognise the importance of this
Bill. To have previous Bills codified and
harmonised and presented to the public
in a way the public can understand, and
the authorities who have to administer the
Bill can understand, is, I think, a matter
of great utility. And I should imagine
we can look forward with confidence to the
enforcement by the authorities of this important and unified Measure in a way
that will secure the end the hon. and
learned Senator had in view in introducing
it, and that great benefits will result
from its administration. The previous
Measures were not, on the whole, very
much enforced, and people did very much
as they liked in regard to birds, but this
Bill will, I think, improve matters.
I am making these observations in an endeavour to meet the criticisms levelled
against this Bill outside this Chamber,
that the Senate had very little to do in
devoting so many days and so much time
in debating and dealing with a Measure of
this kind, regarded by many people as
more or less trivial, if not sentimental.
May I point out that in recent years the
sciences of entomology and epidemiology
have made vast strides, and the discovery
that a great many diseases of human
beings, of trees, plants, and fruits, and so
on, to a large extent depend upon the
ravages of noxious insects and their larvae,
makes it quite clear that anything that
helps in the repression or destruction of
these noxious insects is a very great advantage not merely to agriculture, horticulture, but to humanity itself.
The knowledge of the habits and breeding and propagation of these insects has
been elucidated greatly in recent times,
and our information as to the habits of
their natural enemies, birds, has also been
advanced, but it has not exactly filtered
down to the people yet, and they do not
know how much they owe to the birds in
the extermination of insects that do so
much harm. So many people regard these
birds as more or less ornamental and picturesque, that they are apt to overlook the
solemn fact that birds may conceivably be
able to do without humanity,
humanity, but
humanity has not been able to do without
birds. Therefore if what I may call the
useful type of birds specified in this Bill
are preserved and fostered, we should hope
that a great deal of the ravages of these
noxious and morbitic agents may be reduced in future with great benefit not
only to agriculture and horticulture, but
to humanity itself.
I hope, therefore, I have made clear
from this standpoint that this Measure
will be a very useful one indeed and if
I may, before sitting down and without
impertinence, I would congratulate the
hon. and learned Senator on the way he
has presented this Bill. Now that it has,
with the assistance of the hon. and
learned Senator Leslie, been battered into
shape and use in its present form I trust
it will commend itself not only to this
House but to another place, and before
we are much older this beneficent Bill
will have found its way to the Statute
Book in time to operate in the direction
in which it is aimed during the current
year.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 1 (Illegal methods of exposing to injury, taking, or killing birds).
*
*
*
*
*
*
(c) to take or attempt to take any
bird by means of a hook or other
similar instrument; or
(d) to use for the purpose of killing
or taking birds any mechanically-propelled boat or any aircraft.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 1, line 15, paragraph (c) to leave
Wild Birds Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
""
[Mr. Leslie]
out the words, or attempt to take."
These words have become unnecessary
owing to an alteration made on the Committee stage as a result of which the
actual doing of, or attempting to do such
a thing was declared to be unlawful.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am quite prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 1, to leave out paragraph (d).
That is the paragraph relating to the use
of any mechanically-propelled boat or any
aircraft for the purpose of killing or
taking birds. There are various difficulties in allowing the words in the
paragraph to remain as they stand.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I cannot accept the
Amendment, because the point is covered
to some extent by the next Amendment I
intend to move making more definite
and more direct what the offence aimed
at is, namely, the direct pursuit of birds
by means of a motor boat or aircraft for
the purpose of killing, injuring, or taking
such birds.
Mr. LESLIE: I must point out to the
hon. and learned Senator that, before we
can discuss the substitute Amendment he
himself has on the Paper, we must get
rid of paragraph (d) in its present form.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am quite prepared
to get rid of it.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to
insert the following new paragraph:-
-
"(d) to pursue in any mechanicallypropelled boat or in any aircraft any
bird for the purpose of killing, injuring
or taking such bird."
That makes clear and definite what the
offence aimed at is. It is to use actually a
motor boat or aircraft for the purpose of
killing, injuring or taking such a bird. It
is an undoubted fact that birds are pursued
by the means specified in America and in
Southern Spain. The bustard, which
used to be a bird native to these islands,
is now actually being pursued in the air.
When it is exhausted it falls to the
ground, the aircraft descends, and the
bird is taken in a wearied and exhausted
condition. Such a thing will not arise
under this Bill at present, but it may
arise hereafter.
Mr. McCORKELL: I am quite in
sympathy with the idea which I know
underlies this Amendment, but I am not
quite sure that it will have the desired
effect. I think that what the hon. and
learned Senator aims at is this. He does
not want birds to be run down by a
motor boat or aeroplane, but if that is
put in the Bill in the form of this Amendment it will have this result; that a man,
for instance a puntman, employing even
an outboard motor boat for the purpose
of propelling himself in one way or another would be prohibited from doing
that. Again, a private sportsman might
be prohibited in the winter time from
crossing a number of estuaries in a motor
boat to get from point to point, because
he might be held to be pursuing birds for
the purpose
them. While
of killing them.
sympathising with the hon. and learned
Senator's aim that a bird should not be
run down by superior speed, I cannot
agree that a man should be prohibited.
from going from place to place in his own
motor boat. As I have said before,
while I welcome the principle of the
Amendment I think it is going rather far,
because it might prevent certain persons
from following their legitimate occupation,
and unless I receive an assurance that this
will not be the effect of this particuular
Amendment I shall vote against it.
haps the hon. and learned Senator would
agree to make his Amendment a little
more water-tight, so that it may attain
the object he has in view without penalising the class of persons I have referred to.
Per-
Mr. CAMPBELL: I should be very
glad to meet the views of the hon.
Senator if I could, because we have both
the same object in view. At the same
time, I do not think that the phrase "to
pursue in any mechanically-propelled
boat or in any aircraft any bird for the
purpose of killing, injuring or taking such
bird" could have the effect the hon.
Senator conceives it might have.
I cannot imagine any better set of words to
cover the inention I have in view.
Question put: "That these words be
there inserted.'
The House divided: Contents, 15; Not
Contents, 7.

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, at
the end of Clause 1, to insert the following new paragraph:-
"(2) If any person is guilty of an
offence under the last preceding subsection he shall be liable on summary
conviction to a fine not exceeding
twenty-five pounds or alternatively or
in addition thereto to imprisonment for
any term not exceeding three months."
This penalty is taken from the Protection of Animals Act (Northern Ireland), 1926. I thought it was wise not
to divert from the penalty laid down in
that Act, which is the same as prescribed
in other clauses.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 2 (Illegal methods of taking
or confining birds).
(1) If any person uses as a decoy any
live bird which is tethered or is confined in a cage or other receptacle, or
is secured by means of braces or other
similar appliances, or which is blind,
maimed or injured, or uses bird-lime or
any substance of a like nature for the
purpose of taking, or attempting to
take, any bird, he shall be guilty of
an offence under this Act.
(2) Where any person is convicted of
any offence under this section, the Court
may, in addition to any penalty that
may be imposed, order any live decoy
bird used by such person for the purpose of taking, or attempting to take,
any bird to be forfeited and disposed of
as the Court may direct.
(3) If any person keeps or confines any
bird whatsoever in any cage or other receptacle which does not measure
sufficient in height, length, and breadth
to permit the bird freely to stretch its
wings, he shall be guilty of an offence
under this Act.
This sub-section shall not apply to
domestic fowls, ducks, geese, guinea
Leslie, Mr. J. G.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Sinclair, Col. T.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Long, Mr. J. A.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
McHugh, Mr. J.
Quigley, Mr. A.
fowl, pigeons and turkeys, or to
any other bird, kept or confined in a
cage or other receptacle which does not
comply with the conditions specified in
this sub-section :-
:-
(a) whilst being conveyed by land
or water; or
(b) whilst being shown for the
purpose of public exhibition or competition, if the time during which it
is so kept or confined does not in the
whole exceed seventy-two hours.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
2, page 2, line 1, to leave out the words
"wild bird" and to insert instead thereof
the words "bird whatsoever." This
Amendment is to meet the case I mentioned in Committee in order to make the
wording of the Bill apply to any bird,
whether it be a wild bird or not.
If we
leave "bird" by itself, owing to the
definition Clause we confine it to wild
birds and exclude others. I am afraid
it may be necessary at a later stage to
consider still further whether we ought to
incorporate in this Bill any of these
Clauses which belong to all birds, whether
wild or otherwise. However, that is not
before us at the moment. It might be
thought that in trying to consolidate the
law we were, as we say, poaching on the
general law relating to the protection of
all kinds of animals, which include, of
course, birds. The present point is to
insert these words in order to save those
birds which may not otherwise be caught
by the Clause.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I will accept the
Amendment if the hon. and learned
Senator will agree to add to it the words,
"wild or domestic," to make it clear that
that the Clause embraces both.
Mr. LESLIE: I am certainly prepared
to agree to that.
Amendment, as amended, agreed to.
Wild Birds Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
The following Amendments stood on

Mr. CAMPBELL: These Amendments
are purely consequential.
Amendments agreed to.
CLAUSE 3 (Unlawful to kill or take
birds on Sunday).
It shall not be lawful for any person
to kill or take any bird, or take or
knowingly and wilfully disturb the nest
or egg of any bird on Sunday, except
for the purpose of protecting property,
crops, game or fisheries from damage.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 3, to leave out "not be lawful"
and insert "be unlawful." This is purely
verbal.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 4 (Shooting captive birds).
(1) If any person promotes, arranges,
conducts, assists in, receives money for,
or takes part in any meeting, competition, exhibition, pastime, practice, display or other event whatever at or in
the course of which captive birds are
liberated by hand or by trap, contrivance
or other means, for the purpose of being
shot at the time of their liberation, or
being the owner, or occupier, or person
in charge of any premises, permits his
premises or property or any part thereof to be used for any such purpose, he
shall be guilty of an offence under this
Act.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 4, page 2, lines 37 and 38, to leave
out "captive birds are" and insert "any
captive bird whatsoever, wild or domestic,
is." This is consequential on an Amendment already accepted.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
"Page 2, lines 37 and 38, to leave out
"captive birds are" and insert "any
bird whatsoever is."
Mr. CAMPBELL: I would ask leave to
adopt the hon. and learned Senator's
suggestion, leaving out "captive" and inserting the phrase "any bird whatsoever,
wild or domestic is."
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
3, line 2, to leave out "their" ("time of
their liberation.")
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 6 (Protection of birds according to categories).
(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, it shall not be lawful for any
person-
*
(a) to kill, injure or take, or to
attempt to kill, injure or take, or to
use any means for the purpose of
killing, injuring or taking, any bird
at a time when it is protected:
Provided that as respects any bird
excepted from classification by Part I.
of the First Schedule to this Act, but
which may hereafter be classified for
protection under this section by an
order of the Minister of Home Affairs,
the foregoing provision shall not
apply to the owner or occupier of any
land upon which any such bird is for
the time being, or to any person belonging to his household or in his
service, or authorised in writing
signed by him to act on his behalf;
or
*
*
*
*
*
(c) to sell, or expose or offer for sale,
or to have in his control or possession,
or to transport knowingly, any bird, or
the skin or plumage of any part of any
bird killed or taken, or any egg or nest

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 6, to leave out "not be lawful"
and insert "be unlawful." This is purely
verbal.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out "or attempt to kill, injure or take.
This is another omission. The matter is

Wild Birds Bill-

5 MAY 1931
Mr. CAMPBELL: I accept.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to
leave out the proviso. The proviso now
appears to be unnecessary. The Ministry
may transfer from the unprotected black
list to a protected class. In all probability
these birds will be transferred if transferred to category 2. When they are
classified in category 2 the owner
Occupier will have under Clause 6, Sub-
Clause (2) ((1), paragraph (b), the same
power as is given by this proviso. It is in

Amendment agreed to.
or
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in

of any''), and insert "or." This is purely

CLAUSE 7 (Sale for human consumption).
If any person during the period when
a bird is specially protected under this
Act sells, or has in his possession for the
purpose of sale, for human consumption,
or has in his possession for human consumption, any such bird or any egg of
any such bird, he shall be guilty of an
offence under this Act:
Provided that the Minister may by
Order direct that the provisions of this
section shall not apply in respect of any
bird specified in such Order during the
period of fifteen days next after the
beginning of the close season for such
bird.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 7, at the end of line 6 ("under
this Act :"), to insert "and shall be liable
on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding twenty-five pounds, or alternatively, or in addition thereto,
imprisonment for any term not exceeding
three months."
to
This again is the question of penalty,
which the House has already considered.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out from "that" ("Provided that the
Minister") to the end of the proviso, and
insert "a person shall not be liable to be
convicted under this section if he satisfies
the Court either:
:-
(a) that the killing or taking of such
bird or egg was lawful, having regard
to the time and place of such killing or
taking; or
(b) that the bird was killed or taken,
or the egg taken, in some place to which
this Act does not extend; or
(c) that he had reasonable grounds.
for believing that the bird or egg was
taken under any such conditions
aforesaid."
as
This is the Clause on which we had some
little difficulty. It seems to me that we
shall meet the requirements if we follow
the Act of 1881 as closely as possible and
insert the words which are on the Paper.
To do that we must first get rid of the
words after "that," so that the new proviso will read, "Provided that a person
shall not be liable to conviction,' if he
is able to show that he took or killed the
egg or bird at a time or place where it
was lawful, or at a place, at all events, that
this Act did not extend to, or that he
had reasonable grounds for believing that
the bird or egg was so taken. I think
that is what is required, and I beg to move
accordingly. Still I must say I am not
quite sure it goes quite as far as the hon.
and learned Senator wants as regards
another matter.
Mr. CAMPBELL: It would be unfair,
I submit, to some traders in Northern
Ireland to drop the proviso, and if we
adopt the Amendment we are dropping the
proviso. The offence is selling for human
consuniption, or having in possession
for human consumption any bird or egg
during the protected period. That seems
perfectly reasonable. Then there is the
proviso. At the end of the protected
season merchants in Belfast or Londonderry very often may have some birds over
that were taken during the permitted time
and may not be able to dispose of them.
It may take a week or ten days to get
rid of the stock. In other wild bird Acts of
Parliament we find the period of grace
of
15 days allowed them to sell. The proviso
aims exactly at this object. It allows a
period of grace of 15 days next after the
close season begins for the merchant
to get rid of his stock. My hon.
and
learned Friend's Amendment
really to some extent unnecessary.
do not think a court would convict any
person who proved to the court's satisfaction that the killing or taking of the
bird was done at a lawful time or place.
is
I
Wild Birds Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
I think the law at present is an absolute
barrier and safeguard against any such
injustice.
Mr. LESLIE: The difference between
us is that the hon. and learned Senator
insists on 15 days, and I have insisted on
no period whatever. I have protected
everybody who has not done anything unlawful. I do not insist.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
line 7 (first line of proviso) after
"Minister" to insert "of Home Affairs."
This is purely verbal.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move at
the end of the proviso to add :—
"Provided also that the Minister of
Home Affairs may by Order direct that
any of the provisions of this section or
the last preceding section shall not
apply in respect of any specified bird
killed or taken outside Great Britain
or the Irish Free State and imported
into Northern Ireland, or any egg of
any such bird."
The proviso is intended to cover the
case of a bird like the quail. The quail,
I believe, was more or less a constant
visitor to the shores of Belfast Lough
years ago. I have heard a high court
judge tell with glee how as a boy he shot
quail on the present site of the Belfast
shipyards. Dead quail does come in considerable numbers from Egypt to this part
of the world. I think it right in the case
of a bird of that kind killed and imported
into Northern Ireland for human consumption that there should be means by
which a person selling a bird of that kind,
if the proper authorities allow, should
have the right to sell it without infringement of the law.
Mr. LESLIE: I must say I am a little
doubtful about the propriety of inserting
these words. It seems to me, as the law
stands, if a person wants to import quail
into Northern Ireland he can do so and
this Parliament cannot stop him. I do
not think we should insert anything like
that. The position at the moment is that
we cannot interfere with trade from outside.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think there is a
misconception. It is with regard to selling
in our territory which may be an offence.
There is no question of interfering with
importing. The last preceding Section
deals with sale. There is no question of
importing.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not mind.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 8 (Liberation of imported
birds).
It shall be unlawful for any person
without the leave of the Minister of
Home Affairs knowingly to liberate imported birds of any species, but this provision shall not apply to pheasants,
partridges, grouse, heath or moor game
or black game.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to
leave out "or black game,
"" and to insert
"black game, snipe, or woodcock."
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 10 (Register to be kept by
taxidermists).
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move to
leave out Clause 10. This is the Clause
which compels taxidermists to keep a
register. I understand there is only
one taxidermist in Northern Ireland, and
it is provided that the Clause shall not
be put into operation until a similar enactment comes into operation in Great
Britain. We cannot say when a similar
Act will come into operation in Great
Britain. They have been struggling with
a Bird's Bill there for a number of years,
and at the present time there is an independent Scottish Bill going through the
Imperial House. As the Clause stands it
might be years before it will be put into
operation, and then it may affect only one
trader. Therefore, I think, the House
would be justified in dropping the Clause.
Amendment agreed to.

*

(2) Any order made under this
section may provide for the classification
of any birds excepted by the order from
classification in Category I., as respects

CLAUSE 14 (Penalties).
*
*
*
*
Provided that where an offence under
this Act is in respect of more than one
bird which is classified under this Act
as being in Category I. or in Category
II. or in Category III., or in respect.
of more than one skin, egg, or nest of
any such bird, the maximum fine which
may be imposed under this Act shall
be determined as if the person convicted
had been convicted of a separate offence
in respect of every bird, skin, egg and
nest.
(2) In addition to any other penalty
which a court has power to inflict for
an offence under this Act the court before which a person is convicted of any
such offence shall order that any bird,
skin, egg, or nest, in respect of which
the offence was committed shall be forfeited, and, if they think fit, deposited
in a public museum, and shall order
the liberation of any bird illegally taken,
and may order that any gun or other
instrument in respect of or by means
of which the offence was committed
shall be forfeited or otherwise dealt with
as the court may direct, and may also
order that any licence under this Act,
held by the person convicted, shall be
cancelled, or shall be suspended for such
period as may be specified in the order,
and where any licence under this Act
has been so cancelled, or during any
period when a licence is so suspended,
no other licence granted under this Act
to that person shall be valid.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in paragraph (4) to leave out "the making of a
conviction or order," and to insert "a conviction or order made." We do not make
a conviction, we do it the other way,
accordingly I suggest the Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am quite content.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
paragraph (4), to leave out "or the refusal
to make a conviction or order." This is
a matter of considerable importance, because I admit it is rather an innovation
in our law that there should be an appeal
from a refusal to make a conviction or
order. There is a rule allowing an appeal
against a conviction, but not against a
refusal to make a conviction or order. It
has been suggested that as the paragraph
now stands it might mean that a man
might defend himself successfully in the
court of first instance, and then have to
fight the case a second time on appeal
against a refusal to make a conviction or
order each time at his own cost.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in

or nest." This is a consequential Amendment, as the words in the paragraph cut
slightly across an Amendment made in

Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
paragraph (6), to leave out "eight" and
insert six. This is consequent on the

""

CLAUSE 16 (Powers and duties of
officers or constables of the Royal Ulster

(2) If any person obstructs or impedes

nest, he shall as soon as conveniently
may be
Wild Birds Bill-

(a) take such steps as
may be

or

Where a justice of the peace orders
such bird, egg or nest to be sold, and
the person from whom it has been
taken is convicted of any offence in
relation to such bird, egg, or nest, the
proceeds of such sale shall be added to
and dealt with in the same manner as
any penalty imposed for such offence.
If no conviction takes place, the bird,
or the proceeds of the sale thereof
(when such sale has been authorised by
a justice of the peace) shall be restored
to the person from whom the bird, egg
or nest was seized, and no officer or
constable who, by direction of a justice.
in writing, sells or destroys any bird,
egg or nest so seized, shall be liable to
any legal proceedings for such sale or
destruction or for any loss caused by
such sale or destruction.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out Sub-Section (6).I do not think this
may
Sub-Section ought to be allowed to stand.
It seems to me that the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) has got all he
ought to get in Sub-Section (7). That
is to say, before an officer or constable
can go roaming over other people's lands
he must have his warrant. It
be remembered there was a good deal of discussion on the Preservation of Game Act
as to the proper powers to be given to the
police, and there is certainly nothing in
that Act which comes anywhere near the
It has
proposal contained in this Bill.
been in the Bill all the time, but it may
be remembered that the discussion on it
was rather left over to the alteration in
the succeeding Clause which Senator
Campbell took in hand. We now have
Sub-Clause (7) which provides that if a
justice of the peace is satisfied there is
reasonable grounds for suspecting an
offence he may issue his warrant, and on
that an officer or constable can act, but
we cannot allow an officer or constable to
do anything on people's land unless he is
furnished with a warrant.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I appreciate the
reason for the Amendment, but if we
look at it in another light I think it will
be realised that the Sub-Section is
reasonable. This power is given only to
an officer or constable, it is not given to
any other person. In some other Acts
this power is given to other persons
authorised by the Ministry. Then before
an officer or constable can act he must
have reasonable grounds for acting. He
cannot act from mere caprice. He must
suspect that an offence has been or is
about to be committed, and
to
power
enter is given only in respect of land.
Take the City of Belfast, around which
there are farms. If a constable saw an
offence being committed on certain land,
or had reasonable grounds for suspecting
that an offence was being committed,
Iwould it be reasonable that the constable
should be prevented from interfering or
hampered in any way because he had not
a warrant? Obviously he should have the
power to enter, especially in the case of a
large town like Belfast and urban centres.
Very often it is there the trouble is.
is entirely a matter for the good sense of
the House. I am quite content to leave
it to the judgment of the House. I will
remind the House that in recent Acts of
Parliament that this House adopted this
right of entry has been granted. Take
the Marketing of Potatoes Act, 1928.
There the right of entry is given.
It
Wild Birds Bill-
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
Take again the Marketing of Dairy
Produce Bill, 1929. Again the right is
given. It is also given in the Agricultural Produce Act of 1930. I am not
quite sure that in some Bills pending
before the House at the present time the
corresponding right is not given.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I think it will
do away with a great deal of the effect of
this Act if that Clause is left out. If
a policeman must wait until he gets a
warrant, where on earth would he get
a conviction? I think it is the most vital
part of the Act that a policeman should
have that power.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I rise to
support the last speaker, and also the hon.
and learned Senator who introduced the
Bill. I think if a police constable who
had reason to suspect that an offence was
going to be committed had to get a warrant the utility of the Act would fall to
the ground, unless a police constable was
furnished with a Medusa's head, which
turned persons who disagreed with him
into stone. But the present Ministry
looks upon the employment of that means
with dislike.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 18 (Duty of Minister of
Home Affairs to exhibit orders).
The Minister of Home Affairs shall
cause to be kept exhibited any order
made by him under this Act in such
manner as he may direct, with a view to
bringing the contents thereof to the
notice of the public.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, to
leave out "shall" and insert "may."
Amendment agreed to.
""
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, to
leave out "kept.' That is not the real
intention of the Clause. It means a temporary exhibition.
Amendment agreed to.

In this Act unless the context otherwise requires, the following expressions
have the meanings hereby assigned to
them respectively, that is to say:-
"Bird" means any wild bird or
wild fowl, except grouse, partridges,
pheasants, heath
or moor game,
black game, snipe, and woodcock;
"Specially protected" means protected under section eight of this
Act;

vegetables;

CLAUSE 21 (Short title and commencement).
(1) This Act may be cited as the
Wild Birds Protection Act (Northern
Ireland), 1931.
(2) This Act save where otherwise
therein provided shall come into operation on the thirty-first day of July,
nineteen hundred and thirty-one.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in

of the Clause which provided that the

Marketing of Fruit Bill

5 MAY 1931
Marketing of Fruit Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, "That this report be now received.'
""
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I now beg to moveand I am sorry I had not time to give
the noble Viscount notice that this Bill
be referred back to the Committee, the
point being that rather unlucky Clause 17.
It will be remembered that we amended
Clause 17 in Committee. I am afraid
that it requires further examination. The
point is that that Clause like a good many
other things has got both a head and a
tail. It was only the tail that we dealt
with, and I am not sure that we made
a very good job of it. Perhaps we would
like to reconsider that. The real difficulty about the Clause seems to me to be
the head. The Clause runs like this:
The provision of this Act shall apply
to fruit grown or produced in Northern Ireland, and not to any other
fruit.
I think the House will see on looking
back that that does not agree very well
with Clause 9, in Part 1. In fact we
may go so far as to say that that Clause
makes the qualifications complete. I
think we might like to reconsider that.
Also it appears that that part of the
Clause would have a most extraordinary
effect on Clause 12, the "topping"
Clause. I understand that one of the
primary purposes of this Bill is to stop
what is called "the nefarious practice of
topping." Topping is made as bad as
treason, arson, or anything else. At all
events, however nefarious the attempt at
topping the case of apples from Armagh
-it may be much worse than topping a
box of chocolates, packets of dates or figs,
or cases of oranges-but I cannot see
that this is any worse than topping boxes
of apples coming from Monaghan.
I do not think that can be the intention
of the Bill, but if we leave Clause 17 as
it stands the provisions of the Act will
not apply to any fruit grown outside
Northern Ireland.
I do not think that is the intention,
and therefore, I think we should send this
Clause back to the Committee to reconsider that question. It is a simpler
way to do that than to move something
after Third Reading. It might be some
other alteration of the Bill might be considered necessary.
I do not think we
want the provision of that Clause at all,
because we are covered fully by Clause 9
in the other part of the Bill.
As no
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
notice has been given on this question.
I cannot answer anything definite on the
matter. I should have thought myself
that it would have been easier to introduce a discussion on Third Reading than
to go back. Of course, it could be done
by Amendment in precisely the same way
as in Committee, and it would not interfere with the progress of the Bill in
another place. If we re-commit the Bill
we shall have to go on with Report and
so on and delay matters. I think if we
considered this on Third Reading it
would be simpler. I would like to hear
what the hon. and learned Senator says.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much in the hands
of the noble Viscount, because I have not
given notice. It seems to me that the
difficulty in dealing with the matter on
Third Reading is that if one puts down
an Amendment which is not generally
accepted it might be desirable to make
some other alteration, and then we would
have at that stage to send the Bill back
to Committee. I am inclined to think
that this is probably quicker. It gives
an opportunity of examining any consequential alteration.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have got
no objection to the course proposed.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill accordingly recommitted to a committee of the Whole House, the House
to be in Committee on Thursday.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday, when this Bill will be
again considered in Committee."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five Minutes before Five,
until Thursday next.

Brought from the House of Commons
and read a First time
Questions to Minister.
COST OF LIVING FIGURES.
Mr. BARCLAY asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the
comparative figures for the cost of living
in Great Britain and Northern Ireland
for the last ten years.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): The combined cost of
living index figure for Great Britain
and Northern Ireland is published
monthly in the Imperial Ministry of
Labour Gazette. A separate index figure
is not computed for Northern Ireland as
the Cost of Living Committee set up by
the Minister of Labour in September, 1922,
recommended, after a very exhaustive inquiry, that no attempt should be made to
publish separate figures for Northern Ireland, their opinion being that no substantial difference existed between
Northern Ireland and Great Britain in
this respect. The hon. Member will
appreciate that there is no more reason
for publishing separate figures in the case
of Northern Ireland than there would be
in the case of, for example, Scotland or
Wales.
HABITUAL CRIMINALS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the Government consider that any changes are desirable in
the existing methods of dealing with persistent offenders, including habitual
offenders, who are liable to sentences of
preventive detention, and to other
classes of offenders who return to prison
often.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Habitual
criminals are, as the learned Senator is
aware, at present dealt with under the
provisions of Part II of the Prevention of
Crime Act, 1908, but, as a matter of fact,
the Courts in Northern Ireland have not,
during the past few years, given any
sentences of preventive detention, and
probably for this reason there has been
no suggestion that any immediate alteration in the law is called for.
I am informed that a Departmental
Committee has been appointed in England
to consider the whole question of the best
way of dealing with the habitual criminal,
and I am sure that its report will be
awaited with interest, and will receive
most careful consideration by the Departments concerned.
321 Planning and Housing Bill-

12 MAY 1931
Second Reading
Planning and Housing Bill.
Order for Second reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have
little doubt that this Bill, the Second
reading of which I have the honour to
move, will meet with the approval of
hon. Members in all parts of the House.
I do not, therefore, propose to dwell on
these objects at length, but rather to deal
in a general way with the machinery
which the Bill proposes to set up in order
to attain them.
The Bill, as hon. Members will have
seen, is a fairly long one. Some of its
provisions are of no little complexity, and,
so far at least as Part I is concerned, the
subject matter of the Bill is new to this
House, as we have had no legislation in
Northern Ireland dealing with what is
popularly known as Town Planning.
I
shall, therefore, have to take up a considerable portion of the time of this
House, but I do not apologise for this,
as the Bill is one which merits very careful consideration. I can promise hon.
Members, however, that I shall relegate
all matters of detail to a later stage and
confine myself for the present to the main.
principles upon which the Bill is based.
I would, at the outset, draw the attention of hon. Members to the fact that we
have deliberately dropped the well-known
expression "Town Planning Schemes"
and substituted "Planning Schemes."
The reason for this is that we desire to
make it clear that Planning Schemes are
not to be confined to urban areas. Under
the Bill any area in Northern Ireland,
wherever situated, and to whatever use
it has been put, may be included in the
plan.
It might prevent confusion at a later.
stage if I were to indicate now the local
authorities who are to be responsible for
the preparation and adoption of planning
schemes. In county boroughs, boroughs,
and urban districts these will be councils
of these areas. In rural areas the local
authority will be the county council, but
provision has been made whereby the
county council may hand over all or any
of its powers to a rural district council
if the latter so desires. The reason for
this arrangement will be manifest to hon
Members. Some of the matters with
which Planning Schemes will deal, for
example, roads, building lines, and the
control of advertisements, are in rural
areas, the concern of the county council,
whereas for other matters, such as the
regulation of buildings, sewerage and
water supply, the rural district council is
responsible. It would, therefore, be
impracticable to empower either local
authority to make planning schemes without reference to the other. We believe
that only through the co-operation of the
local authorities concerned will it he possible in rural areas to draw up a proper
scheme, and feel sure
that the
arrangement which I have indicated will
achieve this happy result
we
This question of the co-operation of
local authorities leads me naturally to the
consideration of what are known as
Regional schemes. A Regional scheme is
one embracing the areas of more than one
local authority. It is often necessary for
a planning scheme thus to override Local
Government boundaries. For example, in
order to plan effectively an area on the
outskirts of an urban district, it be
may
essential to include the adjoining rural
We have, therefore, provided in
Clause 3 for the constitution of joint committees of local authorities who desire to cooperate in the making of regional schemes.
Where a local authority whose co-operation is necessary for the efficiency of a
scheme refuses to join in, the Ministry
may itself, on the application of any local
authority interested, constitute a joint
committee including representatives of
the recalcitrant local authority.
area.
I shall in a moment proceed to indicate
as concisely as I can some of the matters
with which a planning scheme may deal,
but, before doing so, I would draw the
attention of hon. Members to the general
objects towards which every provision for
a planning scheme must be directed if it
is to be within the powers of the Bili.
The general objects of planning schemes
are three in number, and they are set out
in Sub-Clause (1) of Clause 1. I need
hardly dwell upon the importance of the
first of these objects, namely, proper
sanitary conditions, since the protection
of the public health has always been a
matter of concern to the legislature.
The second object is the preservation of
amenity. I have no doubt that any
measure which will tend to preserve the
beauties of the country-side will meet
with the approval of hon. Members. The
third object is convenience, which relates
particularly to easy methods of communication. I need not remind hon. Members
F
323 Planning and Housing Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
of the importance to the community of
the provision of wide and convenient roads
suitable to modern traffic conditions. I
would also like to draw the attention of
hon. Members to a special type of planning scheme referred to in Sub-Clause (2)
of Clause 1. The object of these schemes
is the preservation of the existing
character of localities of special historical
or artistic interest.
I would now ask hon. Members to turn
to the Second Schedule of the Bill. Here
they will find set forth the various matters
with which a properly drawn planning
scheme should deal. It will occur to
many hon. Members that local authorities
already possess extensive powers in regard
to many of the matters included in the
Schedule. This is quite true. The advantage conferred by the Bill, however,
will be that local authorities will be able
to utilise the powers which they already
possess in a more concerted manner than
heretofore as a result of their being able
to make their plans as regards roads,
sewerage, etc., well in advance of development, and not, as very often at present,
at a time when it is too late for them to
exercise their powers in the most economical manner and to the best advantage.
Take, for instance roads and streets
which form item 1 of the Schedule. Local
authorities who propose to make a new
road or to widen an existing road at some
time in the future will be able to reserve
sufficient land both for the making of
the road itself and also for the provision
of proper verges. They will thus be able
to prevent the land required for the road
from being built upon or used in the
meantime for any purpose which would
make the construction of the road more
difficult or more expensive when the time
for making the road came.
The second item on the list is buildings,
and in this connection I would draw
attention to Clause 5 (2) of the Bill,
which indicates in more detail the matters
which may be dealt with under this head.
It is to be hoped that local authorities
will take full advantage of their powers
in this connection and that they will en
deavour to prevent the erection in their
areas of buildings without any unity of
design and concentrated in so small a
space as to prevent the access of light and
air. Factories and such like should also,
as far as possible, be excluded from areas
which are mainly residential.
Next we come to the provision of open
spaces. The importance of providing such
spaces, whether they be in the nature of
parks, playing fields or play-grounds for
children, need not be emphasised. Hon.
Members will, I am sure, agree that in
these days, when the traffic conditions
make the streets dangerous for children
to play in, the provision of proper playgrounds can no longer be regarded as a
kind of luxury. The "preservation of
objects of historical interest or natural
beauty," which forms item 4 in the
Schedule, covers such matters as the protection of ancient churches and such
objects as trees which add to the beauty
of the landscape.
is
The "control of advertisements
another matter which local authorities
must deal with if the beauty of the
countryside is to be preserved. Sewerage.
lighting and water supply are matters
wth which local authorities have already
extensive powers. Unfortunately, however, it is often difficult for them to
exercise these powers advantageously and
at a reasonable cost owing to the sporadic
manner in which development very often
takes place.
The first step to be taken by a local
authority which wishes to make a planning scheme is to pass a resolution deciding to prepare a scheme and determining
the area to which the scheme will apply.
The passing of the resolution does not give
the local authority immediate power to
enforce the planning scheme but it does,
however, affect land owners and prospective builders in that any buildings or
works undertaken after the date of the
notice of the resolution without permission under an Interim Development Order
may be pulled down, removed or altered
without compensation if they contravene
the scheme as finally approved.
I now come to the question of compensation. The general rule is that any person whose property is injuriously affected
will receive compensation. To this rule
there is, as I have just pointed out, an
important exception. Where any works
or buildings were undertaken after the
date of the notice of the resolution of the
local authority without permission, they
may be removed without compensation.
There is one other apparent exception,
which dealt with in Clause 10. This
Clause deals with certain cases in which
a planning scheme may restrict the full
rights of ownership but where it is not
325 Planning and Housing Bill-

12 MAY 1931
Second Reading
14 F 29
considered reasonable that the Owner
should receive compensation. The reason
for this is that in these cases the property
cannot be regarded as being injuriously
affected. To take, for example, provisions relating to density, an owner
should not desire or expect to be allowed
to erect an excessive number of dwellinghouses on a given area, and if he is prevented from doing so under a scheme he
cannot reasonably expect to obtain compensation.
I now come to the provisions relating
to what is known as betterment. I
have just pointed out that where an
owner's property is injured by reason of
a planning scheme he is entitled to compensation. It may happen that so far
from property being injured it may
actually be increased in value as a result
of the coming into operation of a planning
scheme. For instance, the value of a
building site may be considerably increased by reason of adjoining land being
reserved under a planning scheme as a
permanent open space.
It would appear
to be only reasonable that the owner of
such a site should make some contribution
towards the cost of the scheme which will
benefit him so considerably, and we accordingly provide for the payment to the local
authority by the owner of one-half of the
increase in value as determined by an
arbitrator.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Why one-half?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Well, why
not? Perhaps you will ask the question
later. So long as the land which has
increased in value is used as agricultural
land the payment of betterment is not
enforceable, as the benefit of the scheme
will not accrue until the land has been
developed.
I have now dealt with the salient
features of the provisions relating to
planning schemes, and it only remains for
me to mention one other matter which
may be exercising the minds of hon. Members. This is the question of the publicity to be given to planning schemes.
It is essential that owners of land should
have adequate notice of the making of
any scheme which is likely to affect them
and that they should have ample opportunity of submitting any obiections which
they may desire to offer. Hon. Members
mav. I think. rest assured that proper
provision has been made in all these matters.
Under Clause 6 the Ministry of
Home Affairs is required to make regulations with regard to the giving of
notices to all persons interested. Clause
12 makes special provision in regard to
the service of notices in urban areas in
which that Clause is put into operation,
and under Clause 19 the Ministry of
Home Affairs is required to cause a public
local inquiry to be held in the case of
every scheme to which objection is made.
Before I leave this part of the Bill I
wish to refer to one important Clause,
namely, Clause 13. This Clause enables
local authorities to require the removal
or rendering sightly of "unsightly and
dilapidated structures." Such buildings
not only detract from the general
appearance of the countryside, but also
give an air of shabbiness and decay, which
creates a most unfavourable impression in
the minds of visitors to this part of the
country.
I now turn to Part II of the Bill, which
deals with the clearance and improvement
of unhealthy areas and the demolition and
the repair of individual and unsanitary
dwelling-houses. This is what is popularly called "Slum Clearance."
A
I would like to point out, in the first
place, that town planning and slum.
clearance are two distinct operations, although they may be and often are carried
out in conjunction with one another.
local authority may make a planning
scheme without carrying out any slum
clearance, or, alternatively may carry
out a scheme for the clearance of an unhealthy area without adopting any planning scheme. Much of the value of a
clearance scheme is, however, lost unless
the clearance scheme be followed up by a
planning scheme.
There are two points of general interest
which I wish to make in connection with
Part II of the Bill. The first relates to
the kind of buildings which may be demolished, and the second to the question
of compensation. As regards the first
point, I would like to make it clear that
the only buildings which can be demolished under a clearance order are (1)
dwelling-houses which are unfit for human
habitation or are dangerous or injurious.
to the health of the inhabitants of the
area, and (2) other buildings which are
dangerous or injurious to health. Local
authorities are, under the existing law,
empowered to secure the demolition of
buildings coming within these categories.
327 Planning and Housing Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
The object of the provisions of Part II
of the Bill is to provide machinery whereby the demolition, etc., of such buildings
can be dealt with more effectively and
expeditiously.
Secondly, no question of compensation.
arises in connection with the demolition
of property of the kind which I have indicated. It is a recognised principle
under the existing law that such property
may be demolished without compensation,
and hon. Members will not, I think, expect me to make any apology for this
principle. Hon. Members may take it
then that, generally speaking, the changes
in the law made by this part of the Bill
are not so much changes in principle as
changes in method and machinery.
Clauses 22 to 24 deal with the clearance
of unhealthy areas. Under these Clauses
an urban sanitary authority, if satisfied
that the dwelling-houses in any area are
by reason of disrepair or sanitary defects.
unfit for human habitation or are, by
reason of their bad arrangement or the
narrowness or bad arrangement of the
streets, dangerous or injurious to the
health of the inhabitants of the area and
that the other buildings in the area are
for a like reason dangerous or injurious,
and where they are convinced that the
only satisfactory method of dealing with
the conditions in the area is the demolition
of all the buildings in it, they are empowered to make a clearance order requiring all the buildings and dwellinghouses to be demolished. Before doing
so, however, they must satisfy themselves
that accommodation is available for the
persons who will be displaced by the
clearance of the area.
It may occur to hon. Members that.
although a certain area may consist for
the most part of unsanitary houses, there
may be islands of sound and useful property within the area. These islands
cannot be included in the clearance area.
Clauses 25 to 27 deal with some minor
amendments of the law relating to improvement schemes under Part I of the
Housing of the Working Classes Act,
1890.
We now leave the question of dealing
with areas or blocks of houses and come
to the provisions under which local
authorities may deal with individual unsanitary houses. In the first place, we
require local authorities-in this case
urban and rural sanitary authorities-to
have inspections of their districts made
from time to time with a view to ascertaining whether there are any dwellinghouses therein which are unfit for human
habitation. On receiving these reports
the local authority will consider whether
each house reported to be unsanitary is
or is not capable of being repaired at a
reasonable cost. If they decide that it
is not so capable, they must deal with it
under Clause 29, while, if the house is
in their opinion worth repairing they are
required to deal with it under Clause 30.
Where the local authority is satisfied
that an unsanitary house cannot be repaired except at a prohibitive cost, they
are required to serve notice on the owner
to the effect that they will consult with
him as to whether the house should be
demolished, or whether he is prepared to
incur the necessary expense for carrying
out such repairs as will, in the opinion
of the authority, render the house fit for
human habitation, or whether he will
close the house and use it for some other
purpose, such as a store. Within seven
days after the consultation the owner may
agree not to use the house for human
habitation until it has been put into
proper repair. He may then either use
the house for some purpose other than a
dwelling-house, or he may take the opportunity of having the house repaired while
it is empty. One of the advantages of
this new procedure is that it will enable
owners to carry out improvements and
repairs which could not be carried out
while the tenants were in the house.
Clause 30 deals with the second type of
house to which I have referred, namely,
those which, in the opinion of the local
authority, are capable of being rendered
fit for human habitation at a reasonable
cost. In these cases the local authority
are required to serve a notice specifying
the repairs which in their opinion must
be carried out if the house is to be rendered fit for human habitation. Here,
again, the owner has several alternatives.
He may carry out the repairs forthwith
or he may agree to close the house for
human habitation. In the latter case he
may utilise it for some other purpose (such
as a store) or he may take the opportunity
of carrying out the necessary repairs when
the house has been vacated. If the repairs are not carried out or an undertaking given, the local authority may
themselves carry out the repairs and recover the cost from the owner.
329 Planning and Housing Bill-

12 MAY 1931
Second Reading
The rest of the Clauses in this part of
the Bill relate to the procedure to be
adopted in connection with the enforcement of demolition and repair orders and
other matters incidental to the carrying
out of such orders. Clause 34, I would
point out, provides for appeals to the
county court against demolition repair
orders, etc.
em-
Part III of the Bill which we have now
reached is the shortest of the four divisions into which the Bill is divided, but
it is at least as important as any of the
others. Under the existing law, urban
and rural sanitary authorities are
powered to make By-Laws regulating the
erection of new buildings. Such By-Laws
deal with the sites of buildings, the structure of buildings, and quality of the substances used in their construction. They
make provision for securing stability, the
prevention of fires, and for purposes of
health generally.
There is, however, no obligation upon
local authorities to make building By-
Laws, and it is to be regretted that a
considerable proportion of the local
authorities in Northern Ireland have
neglected to do so. The result has been
that new buildings have been erected in
many places which do not conform to a
proper standard of construction. They
are often objectionable, not only from the
public health point of view, but also from
the point of view of the amenity of the district.
I have no doubt that the House
will agree that proper By-Laws should be
in operation in every district, and it is
accordingly proposed to make it compulsory on local authorities to formulate
building By-Laws.
As regards Part IV of the Bill, I propose to refer to one Clause only, namely,
Clause 45. This Clause provides means
whereby the validity of planning schemes
and clearance orders can be tested in the
Supreme Court. This provision is designed to prevent either local authorities
or the Ministry of Home Affairs from
acting in excess of the powers conferred
upon them by the Bill. In conclusion, I
desire to commend the Bill to the earnest
consideration of the House. I beg to
move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: When one of the
great English parties is in opposition I
understand that it is its practice to
have what is called a shadow Cabinet,
which has no existence in law. I
think we are drifting into the position
of producing what I may call shadow
legislation. This Bill now before the
House is modelled in part on the Imperial Housing Act of 1930 and the
Imperial Town Planning Bill of 1931.
In England they keep these matters of
housing and town planning distinct and
separate. They deal with housing in one
Bill and planning in another. They
apparently act on the principle that you
must house your people decently before
you plan your towns, and housing precedes
planning in the English legislation. In
