the Bill now before this House housing
is telescoped into planning, and the result is a clash. This Bill has jettisoned
some of the most essential provisions of
the English Housing Act of 1930, and of
the Town Planning Bill now before the
Imperial House of Commons. Take,
for instance, the Housing Act of 1930.
It deals to a large extent with matters
which this Bill deals with, but there is
in the Imperial Act of 1930 a most important provision totally omitted from
this Bill.
Under that provision the Imperial
Government make contributions in aid of
local authorities in respect of the clearance
and improvement of unhealthy areas, and
also in respect of the demolition or repair
of unsanitary dwelling-houses. Those are
two of the objects aimed at in the Bill now
before this House. In order to attain
those ends the Imperial Government have
included in their Act an Important provision omitted from this Bill. For every
person up to a certain number displaced
from a house in an agricultural parish
the Imperial Government give a contribution of £2 10s Od per year for forty
years, if suitable accommodation in a new
house has been rendered available for such
person. If a family of five is thus accommodated the State contribution
£12 10s Od a year for the forty years.
The corresponding contribution in respect
of houses in non-agricultural parishes is
£2 5s Od. We have no such provision in
our Measure, although it seems to me to
be a very necessary and a very sound
provision.
is
It takes account of the rent of a new
house, and it makes the rent more
bearable. It also takes into account the
size of a family. I remember very well
that in this House a matter of two years
ago some of us suggested that in dealing
with the rents of subsidy houses some
account should be taken of the size of
the family. That suggestion was rather
331 Planning and Housing Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Campbell]
pooh-poohed as a puerile idea. Yet to-day
in that great English Act this very idea
is in full operation. This central idea is
enshrined there. I do submit to the
House that this Bill, having no such provision as that in the English Measure, is
more or less doomed to failure from its
inception. That provision is the mainspring of the English Act. This Bill has
no such mainspring, and therefore the
rest of the machinery will not work.
Contrast our Bill with the English
Planning Bill. There again we differ in
a very vital matter. Where the indi-

vidual owner of land has his property
bettered by a public planning scheme.
which is the result of the combination,
energy, and enterprise of the whole community, this Bill proposes to take from
the community one-half of the result of
their energy and enterprise and give it
to that private individual.
individual. When I
asked the noble Lord during the course
of his speech why one-half." he
replied, "Why not." Does he suggest
that a private individual should be entitled to the whole of the result of the
enterprise and energy of the community
at large. There is no such provision in
the English Act at all. It contains a
provision of a very different character.
Under that Bill payments are to be made
by owners of property, except agricultural land, which has been increased
in value by planning schemes to the
public authority to the full extent of such
increase. Under this Bill the public can
recover only one-half.
Why should we depart from the
English principle? Is there any reason
why a private owner here should be
treated differently to a private land
owner in England? I can see none. I
submit that under this Bill we are taking
from the community one-half of the result of its enterprise and energy and
giving it to the private owner. This increased value was never the property of
the private individual. It is not due to
his interest, his energy, his enterprise, his
work, or his outlay, and yet we say that
wherever in town or country property is
increased in value as a result of an improvement or planning scheme we will
take from the community at least fifty
per cent. of the result of its own energy,
effort, and enterprise. It is a very remarkable deviation from the corresponding legislation in England. Why should
the community at large not get the
entire benefit of the increased value when
the community alone created the entire
increased value?
Then, again, if a local authority will
not put this Bill into operation what is
going to be done? I see no provision in
the Bill under which any compulsion can
be applied to a local authority which
fails to put the Bill into operation. Will
you mark time? You provide no remedy
for such a breakdown of the machinery
at the very outset. It must be obvious
that local authorities are given no incentive to work this Bill. Unlike their
English compeers, they are destitute of
any financial spur or stimulus. I know it
will be said that you provide a remedy
in the case of a local authority which has
prepared a scheme and failed to carry
out its subsequent stages. In such a case
you will go to the High Court and seek
a mandamus and, if necessary, you will
put the members of that local authority
in gaol. That is a very strange remedy
to adopt in the present day. Would it
not be much better to tell them that if
they did not do their duty and act under
the provisions of the Act you would act
for them, and thus avoid the ignominy of
sending them to prison for what may
at the most amount to a mistaken sense
of public duty.
But where a local authority refuses at
the very beginning to put the provisions.
of the Act into operation you will simply
rest on your oars and take no steps whatever, because there is no power given in
the Bill to enable you to compel a local
authority to put a planning scheme into
operation. Under the English Bill the
Government can compel a local authority
to present a scheme within a specified
period, and if it fails to do so the Ministry concerned can take the matter into
its own hands and act in place of the
local authority. We have not adopted
that provision in our Bill. All we say
is that where a local authority have
adopted a scheme and then become stubborn and fail to take the necessary subsidiary steps to carry it out, the
Government will go to the court for a
mandamus to compel them to do their
duty.
A good planning Bill ought to be a
check on land speculators and jerrybuilders, and they are not unknown in
Belfast. They do not see any farther
than their noses, and never understand
their job. This Bill fails to give local
333 Planning and Housing Bill-

12 MAY 1931
Second Reading
authorities the earliest opportunity of
protecting their areas from spoliation by
bad development by such people. A Bill
of this character ought to set a standard
of amenity and seemliness in landdevelopment schemes. Anyone who cares
for Ulster must be interested in town and
country planning, and we who are as interested in the welfare of Ulster as any
hon. Member on the Government benches,
we who were born and bred in Ulster, and
who are living in Ulster, and are proud of
Ulster, would rejoice to see the provision
of a comprehensive and compulsory development scheme for the Province as a
whole.
I am very glad to know that this Bill
does one thing, and that is, it gives ample
facilities for the creation of sanctuaries
for children. It enables local authorities
to deal with the problem of excluding
motor traffic from certain streets which
have an over-flow of children from small
houses and are only third-rate thoroughfares. I am sure everyone realises that
that is a very great source of trouble in
our towns. Even the most ruthless
champion of motorists' rights would
hesitate to insist too strongly upon the
child's duties and responsibilities as a
pedestrian. The child's irresponsibilities
must be accepted as a normal hazard of
the streets. I am glad that this Bill gives
an opportunity for creating playgrounds
of streets free from traffic, streets from
which traffic is excluded at certain times
on certain days. Such a thing will be
good for the children, the public, and
the motorists themselves. There may be
other opportunities under the Bill for
creating further facilities, but on the
whole I must confess that, in my humble
judgment, the Bill is disappointing.
Mr. DORMAN: The hon. and learned
Senator, who has just spoken, has practically covered all the omissions it would
be necessary to rectify in order to bring
this Measure into line with that in Great
Britain. I think it is hopeless to expect
any of the municipal authorities in Northern Ireland to succeed in carrying out
schemes for clearing slum areas under the
provisions of this Bill. One of the
principal reasons is that the cost of the
necessary land for the re-housing of the
people removed from the slum areas is
so exorbitant that it is hopeless to expect
any public authority to undertake the
burden without running into debt to an
enormous extent. Under the English
Bill there is provided out of State funds
a sum of £12 10s Od for certain areas,
and £11 5s Od in respect of other areas
for housing an ordinary family for a
period of forty years.
The Belfast Corporation at present
finds it impossible to erect houses to let
at a rent reasonably meeting the requirements of the wages of the working classes
of Belfast. It will be necessary for the
Corporation to offer some inducement to
that part of our community which, from
economic poverty, is compelled at present
to reside in the cheapest form of human
habitation in the slum. We cannot
expect that the slum dwellers of Belfast
will be able at any period during the
next forty years to pay the rent of the
houses erected at the present time owing
to the cost of building material and the
cost of land, etcetera, unless the State
steps in and assists the local authority in
its effort to re-house those displaced by
the slum-clearing schemes. And, therefore, while welcoming the Measure
and feeling the necessity for town
planning and slum clearance, I think the
Measure is not calculated to be a success
unless the Government see the importance
of giving State aid to the various corporations and local bodies, so that they
may re-house at an economic rent those
whom they will be compelled to displace.
by the provisions of this Act coming into
operation.
a
Mr. McALLISTER: I am wholeheartedly in favour of the principle of
this Bill but, I think, it is open to
criticism from at least three major viewpoints. In the first place, it is
calamity in my judgment that the
Measure has been so long delayed. It
is surprising that in these last few years
subsidies have been given on 22,000 or
23,000 houses, costing the State something like £2,500,000, and these buildings have been erected at that cost
without town-planning schemes. It is a
terrible calamity.
The next point is that the Bill is not
compulsory. I have no hope that a Bill
left for voluntary operation in the hands
of local councils will be carried into effect
in anything like an efficient manner. We
all know that some urban councils have
no plans for a water supply, no plans for
sewerage, and no plans for gas. It is to
improve that sort of thing, and thereby
improve the health of the people, that
this Bill is required. I think it should
be compulsory. I may say I do not entirely agree with my colleague (Mr.
335 Planning and Housing Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. McAllister]
Campbell) in his views in regard to the
application of the increased letting value.
I do think owners have done something
towards increasing the letting value, and
they are entitled in my judgment to some
proportion, whatever that proportion.
may be, of the increased letting value.
On the other hand, all that the local
authorities have done perhaps is to make
the street after the houses were built.
I think in all fairness the owners of
property are entitled equally to the increase in value. I speak for myself, but
that is my view of what is actual justice
on the subject.
no
The other fault has been also dealt
with, that this Bill has no financial
structure. The Bill is far-reaching and
very comprehensive, but there is
finance provided by which to carry it
out. I say that is a tremendous fault.
I have not the slightest hesitation in saying that the local authorities will not
make the Bill function so long as it is
voluntary, and so long as there is no
financial aid from the Government.
There is one matter that has not been
touched upon. When you come to interfere with private rights of individuais
and with property, I think care should be
taken that the individuals should have
the fullest and most adequate notice. So
far as I understand there is no notice
provided by statute. I think it is the
duty of this House or any legislative
assembly to protect owners of property
in that way.
In the Bill itself it is
stated the Ministry may make regulations.
about notice, but I say the length of
notice should be specifically and expressly
stated in the Bill. It is the duty of this
House to see that that is done in order
to protect these people. Another reason
why I support the principle of this Bill
so much is this. I am sure when we are
driving about the beauty spots of Northern Ireland-and I claim to live in the
most beautiful county of a very beautiful
country-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Tyrone is
better.
Mr. McALLISTER: I knew I should
raise dissension-I know it is a terrible
eyesore to see some of these shacks that
are built round these coasts. For that
reason I am whole-heartedly in favour of
this Bill to prevent vandalism of that
sort taking place in the rural and urban
areas. In the English Bill it is stated
that a man whose property is taken away
in slum areas receives compensation of
something like £12 a year for forty years
I think a man whose property is taken
from him for whatever reason should
receive compensation. The plans were
approved of, the houses were built, they
have gone into decay from no particular
fault of his, and I think in these instances
it would be an injustice to take away any
man's property without paying reasonable
and fair compensation. With those
views I commend the principle of the Bill
and I hope it will function very much
more satisfactorily than I am of opinion
it will.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise to draw
attention to two points, one of which my
hon. Friend, Mr. McAllister, has referred to. That is the question of the
beauty spots of this country. We do
claim that in County Antrim we have
got a very fine coast road from Larne,
one of the most beautiful, but at the
present moment it is being developed in
the most ferocious style you can possibly
imagine. You have got a noble road leading round a noble coast. You have on
each side of that road from Larne the
most violent type of bungalow not only
placed on the left-hand side, going north,
but on the sea side, so that the view of
the ocean is interrupted for people going
along.
In the course of a few years' time that
road will be a continuous development
of ferociously bad houses, and while I am
naturally in favour of the rights of property I believe property has also responsibilities. I do believe the people
that own the land on the sea side of that
road should not be allowed to build these
dreadful edifices. I should like to see in
this Bill compulsion to take those buildings down, because in a few years time
the whole of that road will be entirely
ruined. I think we should take much
stronger steps, and when a local authority
adopts a planning scheme we should
appeal to the local authority to have
these things removed.
The
That also applies to Clause 13 to
which the noble Viscount referred.
local authority may destroy or take away
or cause to be taken away unsightly
things such as ruinous cottages with the
walls standing about the country. On
my way to Belfast every day I come
straight from the edge of Lough Neagh
by the road through Ligoniel. On my
road from my home to Belfast there are
337 Planning and Housing Bill-

12 MAY 1931
Second Reading
seventeen buildings of no possible use to
anybody. They have no roofs, nothing
but just jumbles of stone. I have spoken,
and a much more important person has
spoken on the matter, because the Prime
Minister two years ago in addressing the
Ulster Development Association pleaded
for all these things to be removed.
I have spoken to those in the country
and they will not do anything. The
word "may" should be changed into
"shall" and I am going to take steps to
have it done, but I am afraid the Government will not accept. I will press this
House to back me up. These things have
no business there at all. If we leave
these buildings as they are they will be
there for ages and people coming from
other countries will say, "What a woeful
country you have that you cannot take
old walls and things down."
stones might do service on the roads, because they are no use where they are.
These
Then, as regards Mr. McAllister's remark about the want of financial backing,
I am afraid I agree with him. I do
think there ought to be some sort of aid.
I know we are not rich, but I hope that
at some future time the Government will
be able when better off to come to the
help of the local authorities in this matter.
The local authorities do depend on the
rates, and we are all rated more than we
like.
Therefore, I think, it is a matter
for the central Government to try to help
this matter on. I support the Bill. We
have long wanted town planning and
long wanted country planning. I welcome the idea of slum clearance under
this Bill.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would also like to express my pleasure
at seeing this Bill introduced. We can
all express our pride in our country, and
our regret that this Bill was so long delayed. Nevertheless, there is
every
possibility of making a considerable improvement. It is never too late to mend,
and we have seen what can be done with
some kind of regulation and control.
Therefore the Bill is absolutely necessary
now. I do agree that there have been a
considerable number of buildings put up
all round our coasts which have destroyed
the beauty of one of the prettiest spots in
the British Isles. Everybody admits the
beauty of our natural scenery. It is a
pity we did not take care of it before
this. However, I am satisfied that this
Bill is bound to bring about a change.
The provision of breathing spaces and
playing grounds and parks in cities is
also very necessary in some of our towns
and cities. There are districts, I am
sure, that may be neglected of these
interests.
With reference to the housing scheme,
I, too, feel somewhat sorry that the
Government have not been able to put
down some definite financial assistance to
local authorities. After all when we
consider the financial condition of the
country to-day it is not in the least
surprising that our Government, although
they adopt the Bill passed by the Imperial Parliament, should be particularly
careful regarding expenditure. The Imperial Government certainly passed their
Bill allowing £2 10s 0d in some cases and
£2 5s Od in other cases for persons disturbed in slum dwellings. Probably
that may be one of the reasons why the
Chancellor of the Exchequer called upon
the House of Commons and also upon the
country to be a little more economical
and to make some sacrifices.
It is perfectly proper then for our Government
when they come to deal with the same
Bill to be a little more careful.
I am perfectly satisfied that the Corporations of Belfast and of Derry and
these other towns will not be deterred
from making improvements and removing
slum dwellings as early as possible.
Belfast has already made some contribution of that kind. They have erected
a considerable number of houses for the
working-class people and of the small
class likely to induce the slum dweller to
remove to them. I am satisfied that the
Ministry of Home Affairs, when the
matter is carefully considered, will give
the necessary assistance. I happened to
be one of a deputation that appeared
before the Minister with reference to this
Bill from the Estates Committee of the
Belfast Corporation, and the Minister
pointed out that they had no money to
squander carelessly, but when the Estates
Committee of the Belfast Corporation had
drawn up planning schemes, then the
Government would come along and decide
what assistance they would give.
That was perfectly satisfactory as far
as I was concerned, and I believe every
other member of the deputation was satisfied with that statement. We quite
realised that the Government must be
very careful in the present financial state
of the Province. Any savings it is pos-
339 Planning and Housing Bill-
THE SENATE

Second Reading
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
sible to make should be made, and any
expenditure that can be delayed should
be delayed for the purpose of encouraging
the restoration of industry. Senator
Campbell said that
said that the Government
should have dealt with housing first and
town planning afterwards. I think he
was rather mistaken, because had there
been planning schemes in operation before
there were so many houses built there
would have been no trouble. It has been
the tremendous number of houses that
have been erected that has shown the
necessity for planning schemes. Therefore, even if it is rather late, we welcome
this Bill in the hope and belief that it
will effect great improvements in the
future.
Mr. LESLIE: The chief criticism on
the Bill appears to be that there is nothing in it in the way of provision for State
aid. Another criticism-and I am not
quite sure which of the two is intended
to be ancilliary to the other is that it
is a permissive Bill and not a compulsory
Bill. It appears to me that the two
things are connected. Unless you had
State aid you could not have compulsion,
and if you are not prepared to give State
aid, then you must make your Bill a
permissive Bill. It seems to me on the
whole that there is no immediate prospect
that I know of of any shower of gold
descending on the Province, and it would
have been very inept indeed to have further postponed the introduction of a
Measure of this class until means for subsidising it were forthcoming. It seems to
me that, on the whole, the Bill is a well
thought out scheme for providing, so far
as possible, that in the future, at all
events, whatever may have happened in
the past, planning shall, as the hon.
Senator on my left (Mr. J. Cunningham)
suggested, precede housing.
I am inclined to suggest-although I
have not yet studied the Bill as closely as I
had hoped to do that there is very little
wrong with the Measure, but that which is
wrong with it is in regard to the very small,
in more senses than one, urban district
council which, as has been pointed out,
has not yet provided itself with schemes.
for answering the ordinary needs of
civilised life. But is it to be expected
that that small-minded and perhaps a
small endowed urban district council is
fit to be trusted with such powers as it
might obtain under this Bill. That does
seem to me to be a doubtful question,
and I think we shall have to endeavour
to devote attention to it when we get
the Bill into Committee. I am reserving,
of course, an open mind on all questions
of detail, and I do not propose to deal
with matters of detail this afternoon. I
am hoping that a more than usual ample
allowance of time will be given between
this stage and the next stage of the Bill.
It is a Bill of great importance and some
complexity. It really needs very close
study and a good many hours ought to be
devoted to it. It is not easy to find many
hours to devote to one Bill during a
crowded portion of the Session.
We shall have to consider such matters,
for instance as notice, to which one hon.
Senator alluded. If hon. Members will
turn to Clause 10, which I think the
noble Viscount drew attention to, and
which deals with the question of not
giving compensation for diminution in the
density of houses allowed to the acre, on
that Clause-I am only mentioning it as
an instance the question will arise, what
is to happen if a man is, so to speak,
limited to so many houses, and plans are
made accordingly. If it should turn out
that nobody comes forward to take houses
of that class on that piece of turf, this
man will turn round and say, "If you
will allow me to build smaller houses I
can find a sale for them." Those are the
sort of questions we have to bear in the
back of our minds, and with which we
ought to be prepared to deal when the
time comes. But before we can get the
Bill into Committee we must give it a
Second Reading, and that, I imagine,
the House is about to do.
It
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
that on the whole the principles of this
Bill have been favourably received.
is, as Senator Leslie has said, a long and
very complicated Bill, and I think it is
at least as important as many Bills which
have aroused a great deal of interest outside, Bills of a contentious nature. This
Bill, if properly carried out, is likely to
greatly improve the amenities of Ulster
and the pleasure of living in this Province
long after those other Bills are forgotten.
I shall, therefore, be very pleased to allow
as much time as possible before taking
the Committee stage, which I do not intend to take until next week. Then I
think it would be possible to devote one
whole day to the full consideration of the
Bill if the Senate will agree to sit
sufficiently late. The Bill has undoubtedly
341 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

12 MAY 1931
Committee
taken some time to produce, but it must
be remembered that all the local authorities have had to be consulted, and that
takes a great deal of time, as anybody
who consults local authorities knows that
matters cannot be hurried.
As regards the question of compelling
local authorities to give effect to the Bill,
although there are no compulsory Clauses.
in the Bill it has to be remembered that
local authorities have asked for the
Measure. Therefore, I think it is likely
that most of them will take advantage of
it, and it is to be hoped that in the case
of those who do not the pressure of public
opinion will compel them to act. As
Senator Leslie has stated, unless you give
State aid you cannot very well have compulsion, and in the present financial state
of the Province I am afraid that any
State aid on generous lines would not be
possible.
The hon. and gallant Senator (Colonel
Pakenham) spoke about ribbon development in certain areas. I would point out
that a local authority under a scheme may
prohibit building on unsuitable sites.
Senator Campbell referred to jerrybuilding, and I think one part of the Bill
lays down that local authorities can formulate by-laws and building laws, which,
of course, would prohibit jerrybuilding
entirely. Senator Campbell also referred
to the fact that under the Bill fifty per
cent. of the increased value of any property was to be granted to the owner.
The reason for that is that we hope by
this means owners may be induced to cooperate with local authorities, which they
certainly would not do if they were deprived of all financial gain from property
which was improved in value in consequence of what the local authority had
done. The other points that were referred to in the course of the debate can
more conveniently be dealt with on the
Committee stage, which, as I have already
stated, will take place next week.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second Time'") agreed to.
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved, "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair]
CLAUSES 1 (Amendment of s. 1 of 14
& 15 Geo. 5, c. 15, as to licensing of wholesale dealers in eggs), 2 (Keeping of records
and returns by Class A licence-holders),
3 (Registration of wholesale dealers'
premises), 4 (Removal and detention of
eggs in transit, etc.), 5 (Restriction on
sending eggs to Great Britain, Irish Free
State and Isle of Man), ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Provisions as to appeals
and prosecutions).
(1) Where a court of summary jurisdiction makes an order dismissing,
either upon the merits or without prejudice, any information or complaint
made under the provisions of the principal Act, the prosecutor shall have a
right of appeal to a court of quarter
sessions against the order of dismissal,
and the provisions of section twentyfour of the Petty Sessions (Ireland)
Act, 1851, relating to appeals (except
the provisions as to recognizances) shall
apply accordingly.
(2) A court of quarter sessions, when
hearing and determining any appeal
under this section or from any conviction for an offence against the principal
Act, shall be constituted of the county
court judge or recorder sitting as the
sole judge.
(3) Proceedings for an offence against
the principal Act shall not be instituted.
except by or with the consent of the
Ministry or by a district inspector or
other officer of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, or by an inspector or other
officer of the Ministry authorised in
that behalf by general or special directions of the Ministry, and any such inspector or officer of the Ministry may,
although not a counsel or solicitor,
prosecute or conduct before a court of
summary jurisdiction any proceedings
such offence.
for
any
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
the Amendment which stands in my name
I will move the omission of the two subsections and the insertion of the new subsection together.
Mr. LESLIE: The omission is one
thing and the insertion is another. I
must point out that there is an alternative
insertion on the Paper.
343 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Then I will
formally move that sub-sections (1) and
(2) of Clause 6 be deleted.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I beg to
move, at the beginning of Clause 6, to insert:-
(1) A court of summary jurisdiction
when trying any offence against the
principal Act shall be constituted of a
resident magistrate, sitting alone, or
with other resident magistrates, and a
court of quarter sessions when hearing
and determining an appeal from a conviction for any such offence shall be
constituted of a county court judge or
recorder sitting alone.
The object of this Amendment is to
overcome certain objections to Clause 6
raised in another place. The Minister of
Agriculture had experienced considerable
difficulty in pursuing prosecutions for
offences under the Marketing of Eggs Act
in cases where the defendant has been an
influential licence holder. Instances have
arisen where a local bench has refused to
convict, although the evidence has been
of a character which in other instances
has been amply sufficient to secure a conviction, especially when the unpaid justices have been presided over and guided
by resident magistrates. In other instances cases have been dismissed by a
majority of the magistrates with the resident magistrate who presided over the
court dissenting.
It is essential, therefore, that our Marketing of Eggs Act
should be strictly enforced. Any failure
to enforce the Acts will eventually result
in the high reputation for our eggs built
up in Great Britain as a result of our
standard of quality being in danger. For
that reason the Minister originally sought
to have the right of appeal from a dismiss
by a court of summary jurisdiction
to the county court, with a county
court judge or recorder sitting alone.
In view of the objection to this
course taken in another place, where it
was pointed out that the effect would be
that a man might be tried twice for the
same offence, the Government propose to
confine the trial for offences against the
Marketing of Eggs code to resident magistrates
Mr. LESLIE: I hope I need scarcely
say that I am not prepared to consent to
the proposal of the noble Viscount. I
have more than once spoken on this subject, and I had rather hoped that I should
not have to speak on this subject again.
It has before been pointed out that proposals of this class give the greatest
possible offence to the unpaid magistracy,
a body of men who may be presumed to
take some pride in their commission, and
also that proposals of this sort should be
made in one of our legislative chambers
puts, I think, the Members of these
chambers in something of a difficulty.
Either the House is to say that it has
confidence in some branch of the judiciary
or it has not. In any case whenever these
proposals are made it can scarcely be
wondered at that those of us who have
held his Majesty's commission for some
years and who do, as I say, take some
pride are bound to rise in defence of our
order, and when an attack is made upon
it, to ask for or even demand the fullest
particulars of the statements that have
been made on the opposing side.
a
I have just said that I did not expect
to have to speak again on this subject.
That is because I understood that
deputation of the recently formed Magistrates' Association had been received by
the noble Viscount, the Prime Minister,
and a definte undertaking had been given
that there should be no further infringement of the prerogatives of the ordinary
bench. Now, whether that deputation
did or did not wait on the Prime Minister, and whether the Prime Minister
did or did not make the statement which
I have just said has been reported, is a
simple question of fact, and as to that
the House can be satisfied one way or
another. The opinion I have suggested
appears to be widely held, for the newspapers-I was going to say are persecuted
by having correspondence addressed to
them on the subject. There was one
letter this morning from somebody writing
under an apostolic designation although
of a minor description, drawing attention
to this particular matter I have alluded
to
I will pass from that, for this is a very
much more serious subject. We have
been told, and in fact we know, that the
original proposal of the Bill when introduced in another place was a proposal to
give an appeal from the court of summary
jurisdiction in the case of the prosecution.
Well, those who sit elsewhere could not
stand that, and, therefore, we have not
to decide that. It was then apparently
suggested that we should take the course
which is now proposed. In fact the
345 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

12 MAY 1931
Committee.
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of
Agriculture, spoke on the subject when
he was with us during the Second reading
stage in respect of that provision..
According to the official report, which
hold in my hand, the right hon. Gentleman said:
Provision is made for appeals, because at the present time the Ministry
know of hundreds of cases where persecutions have been brought, and the
magistrate who has most power on the
bench is most likely himself a producer
of eggs. Very often decisions are given
which are certainly not borne out by
the facts.-(Official Report, vol. 13, 7th
May, 1931, col. 306.)
Now, the right hon. Gentleman spoke
of hundreds of cases, and I should think
we are entitled to ask for some support
for that statement. I cannot help thinking that the right hon. Gentleman is
badly misinformed and badly misled by
those who should have kept him straight.
If there are, in fact, or have been
hundreds of cases of prosecutions under
the Marketing of Eggs Acts it seems, I
would suggest, that these Acts have not
been the success we hoped, if they have
given rise to hundreds of cases of prosecutions, and if the suggestion is made
that very often in the hundreds of cases
brought justice has not been done, that
suggestion ought to be substantiated.
the suggestion that there have been
hundreds of cases cannot be substantiated
the suggestion ought to be unreservedly
withdrawn. And if that suggestion is
unreservedly withdrawn, then any argument which is sought to be founded upon
it ought to fail also.
As it stands a most serious charge has
been made against the magistracy of
Ulster as a whole. It is said that hundreds
of cases have been dealt with inequitably,
and these hundreds of cases must have
arisen within the last seven years, for the
first Marketing of Eggs Act is only seven
years old. No doubt the right hon. Gentleman will tell us something more in
support of that, if he can. As regards
the fact of the change to the resident
magistracy it does not seem necessary to
say much. It has been before pointed
out that there are not enough resident
magistrates, and that great inconvenience
has been caused by the present practice
of holding amalgamated courts, a practice
of somewhat doubtful legality.
I pointed out myself when speaking on
the minimum fine in one of the Agriculture Acts that if you make a distinction
hetween trying a case under the Marketing of Eggs Act you will find yourself in
a difficulty as regards the ordinary Sale
of Food and Drugs Act. I mentioned
that before. The difficulty would seem
to arise principally under the Act of
1926. The cases it is proposed to transfer
from the ordinary jurisdiction to special
jurisdiction, I presume, in the hope of
getting a tribunal more favourable to the
Ministry, that is to say, in the hope of
being able to pack by statute. Packing
not by statute was invariably objected
to, and rightly objected to.
At all events, what are these cases it is
the resident
proposed to transfer to
magistrate? Are they not simply cases
which anybody could deal with-cases of
whether or not eggs are dirty, of whether
or not a man did use registered wholesale
premises; whether he did or did not
obstruct some officer seeking to enter
those premises. They are plain cases
which the ordinary magistrates could
decide. There is no justification at all
for the suggestion that some special procedure is necessary, and I regret very
much the proposal made. We are all at
the moment endeavouring to-"boost" is
the common expression, Ulster products,
the Ulster egg, the Ulster cabbage, or
what not. Why does it not occur to us
sometimes to say something about our
primary product the Ulster man? Why
do we hold up the magistracy of Ulster
as men who cannot be trusted? That
certainly attracts attention outside. Why
cannot the Ulster magistrate do his duty
as well as the English magistrate? He,
being an independent person, is going to
do what he conceives to be his duty and
is not going to sit on the bench to carry
out the decrees of a Government Department. That is not what he is for.
However, the right hon. Gentleman
made another statement besides the statement about hundreds of cases to which I
have alluded. He said the magistrate
himself was most likely a producer of eggs.
That is not unlikely. We discussed the
question of who was a producer of eggs
on the Second reading, and we decided,
I think, that we may refer to the man
who owns the hen as the producer of
eggs. However, this is a question of a
magistrate who is himself interested in
eggs. I have put on the Paper an alternative suggestion which might, I think,
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
be accepted, that is, that while not intertering with jurisdiction, while not depriving the ordinary magistrate of this
jurisdiction, you might confine your
jurisdiction to magistrates who do not
carry on the business of producing or
For that there is ample
selling eggs.
precedent, as the Committee is probably
aware?
So far back as George the Third a short
Act was passed in England dealing with
the hat trade, and people engaged in the
hat trade were prohibited when cases arose
between journeymen in the hat trade. A
far stronger case was the Bread Act
passed in 1834, which only applied to
England. That Act definitely prohibited
any miller, baker, or anybody in any way
concerned in that kind of thing from sitting on any case of that sort. But that
Act, as I say, did not apply to Ireland.
Later you get Trades Union Acts of 1871
which enacted, I think, for the first time,
that masters should not sit in cases where
they might be prejudiced, and finally,
you have got the Truck Act of, I think,
1887, which was far wider. No person
engaged in the same trade as the person
prosecuted could sit on the bench.
venture to suggest that that is a principle
not unsound in itself, and not offensive.
I
There is no suggestion made that a particular magistrate who happened to be a
miller, a hatter, or engaged in any form.
of occupation, as in the Truck Act, is
biassed, but there is the recognition that
people might think they were biassed, or
inclined to be biassed, and that is not good
for the administration of justice. If you
adopt that suggestion-in the event of
the first Amendment being disposed of
I shall be able to move it--you will not
do violence to anybody, and you will,
perhaps, give the right hon. Gentleman
some assistance. I do not know how
many cases there really have been.
the other hand if we accept the Motion
on the Paper we shall not give satisfaction
to, I believe, a large body of very worthy
men, and we shall be doing violence to
established principles. What will
happen as regards the undertaking said.
to be given I do not know. I expect to
be told. In the meantime I venture to
hope that the Amendment before the
House will be withdrawn or disposed of,
and if that be so, then, the Amendment
standing in my name, can, perhaps, be
thought to be what is required.
On
a
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to support
shortly, but no less sincerely, the views
expressed by the hon. and learned
Senator. I always think it strange, considering my position in this House, and
holding the political opinions I do,
that I should be charged with defending the honour and integrity of
bench of men, the vast majority of
whom are entirely opposed to my political convictions. I have a good deal
of experience of practising before these
benches, and I say to put down that
Amendment is a libel, and a gross libel,
upon the men whose integrity and honour
1 have never had any reason to find fault
with.
I have now passing in my mind at the
moment one bench to which the learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) gives much of his
time and knowledge, and if I were
defeated by that bench in any case I
would not come here and say that bench
was wrong and want the law amended.
I would say they were wrong merely from
an error of judgment.
I think it is very wrong indeed for the
Minister of Agriculture to come into this
House and say there are hundreds of
cases of mal-administration of justice by
these benches, and that the Ministry has
found great difficulty in getting convictions in these egg cases. I think it is
greatly unfair to make such a statement unless the right hon. Gentleman is
prepared to give details of such cases.
We all know the official mind. We know
that when they prosecute they prosecute
to secure a conviction at all costs. When
the court decides in favour of the defendants the official mind steps in and
says deliberately that the court has betrayed and perjured itself and has done.
an injustice. That is what really took
place in the cases to which the Minister of
Agriculture and Viscount Charlemont have
referred. Surely it is an extraordinary
thing that these magistrates can be
trusted in a murder case where life is at
stake and where a wrong question may
result in a man being hanged, and yet
these same magistrates are not considered
capable and trustworthy enough to determine a small case under the Marketing of
Eggs Acts. It is an indefensible proposition, and one that cannot be justified.
I very earnestly and very sincerely
support the attitude of the hon. and
349 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

12 MAY 1931
Committee
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) on this
question. Local magistrates are men of
honour and integrity, possessing a proper
civic sense of duty. I have every respect
for resident magistrates, but at the same
time local magistrates are equally to be
trusted and very often more so. I know
resident magistrates who have some particular pet hobby and they run it to
death. In a particular case you would
say, "I have no chance with so and so;
I know his convictions.' In the case of a
bench of magistrates the pet hobby is
counter-balanced by that of another and
you are far more sure of getting a just
decision.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I support the
Amendment. In every instance where
an agricultural Bill has come before this
House I have favoured the trial of offences
being confined to resident magistrates. I
think the Minister of Agriculture was
perfectly justified in his statement that
hundreds of cases were not fairly dealt
with by local magistrates. In County
Fermanagh sheep-dipping cases had to be
dropped altogether because in practically
every case the fine imposed was twopence
per head. In my own county and in
every county where the Committee of
Agriculture brought prosecutions they
were made invalid by the small fines imposed. This was due to the fact that the
men who were brought before them were
their own immediate neighbours and
friends. I cannot think that this Bill
would be of any use at all if offences
under it were to be decided by local
magistrates. Senator Leslie referred to
his own Amendment under which any
magistrate who was an egg dealer was
prohibited from adjudicating in
brought under the Marketing of Eggs
Acts. I would ask him if there is a
magistrate in the Six Counties who is
not in some way or other engaged in the
egg trade. If his Amendment was
adopted I do not know where he would
get a magistrate to adjudicate in these
cases.
cases
Mr. LESLIE: The expression on the
Paper is, "a person who carries on the
business of purchasing or selling eggs."
Mr. McCORKELL: I should like very
briefly to support the Amendment.
Senator Leslie stated that the effect of
the Amendment is to suggest no confidence in some branches of the judiciary.
I would not put it as high as that. I
i
would say it means that there is perhaps
more confidence in some branches than in
others. I speak as a justice of the peace,
and I think it would be better if cases
under these Egg Acts came before
resident magistrates. The Amendment
which Senator Leslie has on the Paper
seems to me to be more insulting to local
magistrates than the Government Amendment. I am quite open to admit that a
bench of magistrates would be capable of
trying cases of this sort, but I think it
is most insulting to say that if you either
buy or sell eggs your integrity is so
slight that you are not fit to try these
cases. Even if Senator Leslie's Amendment was adopted it would be difficult to
find magistrates in the rural districts who
did not sell eggs. I think it is safe to
say that the majority would be farmers
and would be engaged in the business of
selling eggs. I do not think any insult
is contained in the Amendment moved
by the Government. On the other hand,
it should relieve magistrates of the difficulty of sitting day after day trying his
next-door neighbours and friends.
these reasons I support the Amendment.
For
The
Mr. CAMPBELL: If some of the
arguments we have listened to carry conviction there ought to be an end of all
local magistrates in Northern Ireland.
There is no other logical method, no via
media, and it would be far simpler to
abolish magistrates instead of beating
about the bush and having long discussions
on this subject on different Bills.
Government should introduce a short Bill
putting an end to all justices of the peace,
because the argument to-day as disclosed
by this Amendment is that they are not
fit to be trusted to try egg cases.
I respectfully ask the Senate to say whether
Ulster justices, taken as a whole body,
are not fit to try egg cases. We are
"boosting" Ulster eggs, but we are
certainly not "boosting' Ulster J.P.'s.
It strikes me as being very remarkable
that this Amendment did not come before
the House of Commons. Here in Ulster
we have had men of the highest eminence
trying Petty Sessions cases, men like
Lord Macnaghten who one day presided
at one of the local courts and the next
day sat in the highest court in the land.
It is an insult to say that gentlemen of
that kind are not to be trusted to try
egg cases. When an egg case comes
along they are to step off the bench in
favour of some solicitor or barrister of
six years' standing. It seems very like
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
reducing the law to an absurdity. An
old woman with a basket of eggs comes
into the court and when her case comes
on she sees the magistrates on the bench,
local men whom she knows and respects,
step off the bench and another legal
gentleman steps on to try her egg case.
Very often in these egg cases it is
simply a question of who is telling the
truth and who is an honest witness. I
never knew a Government Department
whose representative did not come into
court and say in effect, "There is a perfectly clear case, and I am dumbfounded
that it has been dismissed." He reports
to his Department accordingly. Now
the Minister of Agriculture says that
hundreds of cases of injustice to his
Ministry have taken place in Northern
Ireland. I think experience shows that
Department officials are as vain as a
prima donna or a prominent politician so
far as these matters are concerned.
should imagine that an egg merchant on
the bench is as much a danger to a person
charged with an offence under the
Marketing of Eggs Acts as any other
magistrate. They say that two of a trade
do not as a rule agree, and if you find
one of a trade on the bench and another
of the same trade charged before him
he would be likely to fare worse than he
would before another magistrate.
I
Mr. BARCLAY: I support the Amendment on the ground that it would be a
farce to leave such cases as these in the
hands of ordinary magistrates. Very
strong reasons have been given why these
egg cases should be confined to resident
magistrates. I have a great deal of
respect for the unpaid magistrates, and,
no doubt, there are amongst them a great
many intelligent and educated men who
are well up in the law. On the other
hand, there are a certain number who are
not so well qualified and who do not
determine cases from a legal but from a
sentimental point of view. Cases such as
these should not be decided on sentiment.
A magistrate I have in mind who alwavs
decided cases on sentiment I would like
to describe to the House. He was a
cooper by trade, a very industrious man,
and he saved some money and started to
buy potatoes. He had no education
whatever, and travelling in the train between Magherafelt and Maghera he
bought newspapers, put on his glasses,
and read them upside down.
a magistrate, but how
was
That man
could you
expect to get justice from a man like
that. I have great pleasure in supporting the Amendment.
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir E. Archdale): Although Senator
Leslie spoke very strongly about me defaming local magistrates he himself
defamed a class of magistrates who are
supposed to be more learned than the
ordinary magistrate.
Mr. LESLIE: I did
resident magistrates.
not defame
Sir E. ARCHDALE: The hon. and
learned Senator referred to the practice
of confining cases to resident magistrates
as being equivalent to packing the bench,
and if he does not call that defaming
resident magistrates I do not know what
he means by defaming. It is the same
as my saying that a magistrate friend of
a defendant is much more likely to fine
him a penny than a larger fine. It is
common knowledge in the country that in
any prosecutions for dirty eggs or offences
of that kind, the case is either dismissed
or a fine of sixpence imposed.
magistrates have told me they would not
carry out the provisions of the Marketing
of Eggs Acts at all, and that if anyone
appeared before them charged with an
offence under the Act they would not
inflict any penalty at all. I do not know
that I could for seven years state all the
cases which have come to my knowledge,
but anyone who has been reading the
country papers in the last seven years
will know. Our papers are filled with
them. Certainly there are not so many
lately.
Many
We are determined that our egg trade
shall not go down and the foreign egg
trade come in. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). We cannot see that proper eggs
are brought in for packing and put on
the market without strong measures.
That is the reason we have taken this
power. In the other place when the Bill
was brought in the only alternative we
had to what we are proposing-we did
not want to have a man tried twice for
the same offence, as it is in the Bill-the
only alternative we had was to remove
his licence. Having a man up and getting
a small penalty is far better than removing the licence. That puts him out of
the trade altogether. It upsets the collection of eggs in the district, and the
men themselves are entirely in favour of
what we propose here.
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Committee
12 MAY 1931
FM 25 F-1
M:
N
IT
The hon. and learned Members opposite
are both of course naturally against the
Amendment. But I do not agree with
my hon. and learned Friend when he says
that I belittle the magistrates. I consider
the magistrates fit to try a murder case,
but when a man is up for a simple thing
like a dirty egg they would be much more
inclined to let him off. It is a different
affair. I might trust a man to hear the
first stage of a murder case, but I would
not trust him to hear a case when his
neighbours' wives are up for bringing in
dirty eggs and selling them in the market.
That is quite a different case.
The hon. and learned Member (Mr.
Campbell) is, I think, rather hard on
the R.M.'s for their youth. I hardly
know of any R.M. who has not served a
considerable time as a solicitor or as an
Army officer or something else. I do not
know a young resident magistrate myself.
I think they are very well able to hold
the position on the bench they do, and I
believe the country person coming up before them for dirty eggs or anything else
would much sooner have them on the bench
than some of the other magistrates. I have
been some time a magistrate, and I have
many times had a man in the country coming up to me to know would I go on the
bench to see that justice was done as a
neighbour was bringing a case of trespass
against him. I am pretty sure the hon.
Member has had that sort of case himself.
Mr. LESLIE: No.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: I cannot
understand my hon. and learned Friend
from Ballymoney being on the bench during all that time and not having many,
many cases of the same sort. He has a
good deal of drainage to do about there,
and I am perfectly certain they come to
him about opening drains or their neighbours stopping up a sewer that was open.
Belfast, Lord Mayor of
Londonderry, Mayor of
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Leslie, Mr. J. G.
Mc Allister, Mr. T. S.
I think these were the principal objections brought up to-day. I can only say
that when we brought this in in another
place the learned Attorney-General told
us that many cases have been sent up to
him-so many cases that he does not like
to go into them all-for insufficient fines
and wrong sentences. He was the man
who really proposed this Amendment, and
I think all our legal Members in another
place are in favour of it, and are strongly
in favour of the resident magistrate having the power. I do believe that, taking
them all round, the majority of the people
in the country would sooner have the
resident magistrate hearing these cases
than some of the magistrates who they
think might have a spite against them
or something of that sort.
Mr. LESLIE: Before this goes to the
Committee there is one point I think
ought to be cleared up. The right hon.
Gentleman did not tell us much in respect
of the hundreds of cases. He did speak
about fines of 6d. I was under the impression that there was a minimum fine
in operation in these marketing cases.
Perhaps I am wrong. At all events, it
ought not to go to the Committee in cases
where the minimum fine is in operation
unless it is a fact, as it is suggested, that
in spite of the provisions of the Act fines
of only 6d are imposed.
Sir EDWARD ARCHDALE: I was
talking about when the Act first came in.
I have known many cases of 6d. The
magistrates told me they would not impose a fine of more than 2d. They were
all annoyed at the time it came in, until
they found out the benefit of the Act.
Their fines recently have been better.
Question put, "That those words be
there inserted.'
The Committtee divided: Content,
17; Not Content, 5.

CLAUSE 1 (Constitution of the Electricity Board)
(7) The Board shall have power to

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
1, Sub-Clause (7), at the end to add,
12 MAY 1931
Electricity Bill-

Committee.
a little too far. It is quite reasonable to
say that a man who sits on the Board
should not be the holder of stocks, shares,
and so on, in any company engagel in
the generation, distribution, or supply of
electricity in Northern Ireland, but, I
think, we ought to stop there. I think
it is quite reasonable to say he shall not
be engaged in the manufacture of, or
deal in electrical fittings anywhere, because the manufacture and sale of
electrical fittings is world-wide, and it is
a reasonable restriction. But I think it
is going too far to say that because a man
is a member of this Board he must not
hold any shares in a company which is
engaged in the manufacture, distribution,
or supply of electricity in India, Canada,
Australasia, or even in London. There
are a very large number of corporations
and bodies, and companies engaged in
works of that kind, and they offer a not
unattractive field of investment. The
proposal in the Bill seems rather unreasonable.
"and to fix the salaries and allowances
to be paid to any person so appointed."
The question is whether the Amendment
is or is not necessary, or whether the
power already given is sufficient. If the
Committee will look at Clause 25 of the
Bill they will see that a fund is provided
out of which the Board may pay salaries,
fees, allowances, superannuation allowances, gratuities, and so forth. If the
Committee will look further at Clause 51
they will see that a scheme may be established for the payment of superannuation
allowances and gratuities, and if the Committee remembers that in the provision of
Sub-Section (9), of Clause 1 of the English Act of 1926, where it was specially
provided that such fees might be fixed
by the Ministry of Transport, they will,
I think, know all about this that it is
necessary to know. The question is
whether it is necessary to insert these
words, and whether whoever is in charge
of the Bill is satisfied that there is
sufficient power already in existence.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am advised that when Sub-Clause (7) is read
in conjunction with Clause 25 of the
Measure before the House the Amendment suggested is unnecessary, as the
power of fixing salaries and allowances is
already conferred upon the Board.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not press it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

prejudice or affect any legal right or
claim of any person against any
authorized undertakers."
It
It seems to me that, perhaps, there is
a little difficulty here. It cannot be a
matter that can arise very often, but it
does seem to me that the possible rights
of creditors might be unjustly affected,
and that cannot be the intention.
says that if any authorised undertaker
sells his generating station, and so on,
which he may possibly only do because
his financial condition is not quite all he
would desire. Then it goes on to say that
the proceeds should be applied in accordance with Acts or Orders, or if such Acts
or Orders do not contain such a provision,
then as the Ministry of Commerce may
approve. It seems to me there is just a
little chance of the allegation being made
that legal rights have not been properly
protected. Accordingly I draw attention
to the matter, and beg to move.
to
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I hardly
think this Amendment is necessary. All
that happens is that power is given to
authorised undertakers
sell their
generating station, or machinery, or
plant where such generating station or
generating plant has ceased to function
under the provisions of the Clause. The
authorised undertaker is under obligation to utilise the proceeds of the sale in
accordance with the special Acts
Orders under which he is operating, and
if these Acts or Orders do not contain
provisions covering the case, then the
Ministry of Commerce approves how the
proceeds are to be dealt with.
or
I suggest to the hon. Member that the
fear he may have at the back of his mind
would be amply met by the provisions I
have enumerated. Perhaps it may clarify
matters if I remind the hon. Member
that his Amendment can only refer to
authorised undertakings in the hands of
companies. The only ones affected,
therefore, are Ballycastle, Cookstown,
Enniskillen, Lisburn, Portadown, and
Warrenpoint.

Questions to Minister.
USE OF PREMISES FOR ILLEGAL
BETTING.
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House, whether in view of
the fact that the increase of prosecutions
under the Betting Acts from 679 in 1927,
to 1,121 in 1930, and the increase of the
fines imposed from £4,210 to £4,741 respectively have not diminished the
offences under these Acts, the Government will take steps to institute proceedings against the landlords who knowingly
let, or continue the letting of, their
premises for the carrying on of this
illegal business.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I venture
to express the hope that the conclusions
drawn by the hon. Member from the
figures which he quotes are not borne
out in fact, and suggest that on the other
hand the offences of the character referred
to are being satisfactorily kept in check.
With reference to the suggestion he
puts forward that owners of premises
where illegal betting is carried on should
be proceeded against, I may point out
that in the cases of owners the offence
under the existing law is, knowingly and
wilfully permitting illegal betting to be
carried on.
In any case where sufficient evidence to
substantiate a charge under this provision
is forthcoming, the hon. Member may
rest assured that the necessary steps to
institute a prosecution will be taken by
the police authorities.
YIELD OF LAND TAX IN
NORTHERN IRELAND.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if he can state the
probable yield per year of a tax of one
penny in the pound on land in Northern
Ireland having a ripened or ripening
building value over and above cultivation
or agricultural value-the tax being levied
on that excess value.
I
am
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
afraid it is quite impossible to furnish
any useful estimate at the moment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Or undesirable?
Viscount
impossible.
CHARLEMONT:
I said
RENTS OF SUBSIDY HOUSES.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can state
which are the urban areas referred to in
his recent speech where subsidy houses are
let at rents of five shillings a week, and
the valuation of such houses; whether it
is a fact that in the Carnmoney and Glengormley districts some subsidy houses of
£4 valuation are let at rents of ten
shillings and ninepence, and eleven
shillings and sixpence a week; whether in
various streets in Belfast subsidy houses
of £6 10s. valuation are let at rents of
eleven shillings to twelve shillings and
sixpence a week, while rent-controlled
non-subsidy houses of the same valuation
on the other side of the street continue
to be let at five shillings and tenpence a
week.
The following question also stood on
the Order Paper in Mr. Campbell's
name:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is a fact that heavy increases of rent
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
14 MAY 1931
are often demanded from incoming tenants on changes of tenancy of small Belfast dwelling houses of over £8 valuation
on the houses becoming decontrolled
owing to such changes; and whether the
Government will consider taking steps to
protect the poorer classes of tenants from
increases of rent of 60 per cent. to 100
per cent.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I propose
to take together the two questions which
are on the Paper in the name of the hon.
and learned Member.
The
At the time that I answered a previous
question on the same subject, put by the
hon. and learned Member, the subsidy
houses owned by the Tandragee Urban
Council were let at 5/- a week.
Council have since raised the rents by 6d
per week.
In Newry some of the subsidy
houses owned by the Council are let at
5/10 a week. The valuations are £5 in
each case.
As I pointed out in a previous question
put by the hon. and learned Member, the
jurisdiction of my right hon. Friend the
Minister of Home Affairs does not extend
to the fixing of rents on subsidy houses,
and, therefore, he has no official information on the subject. He is aware that
the rents charged in the case of some subsidy houses are as high as those indicated
in the question, but, on the other hand,
there are many subsidy houses let at
lower rents.
The rents charged cannot properly be
compared with those in force under the
Rent Restriction Acts in respect of houses
coming within the scope of those Acts.
because the controlled houses were built
when the costs of building were very much
less than at the present day.
The discrepancy in the rents will right
itself automatically when the supply of
houses equals the demand, and the Government is of opinion that an extension
of artificial control would postpone indefinitely the date when this state of
affairs will be reached, and would not be
in the interests of the tenants themselves.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Can the noble
Lord say whether the rents in Newry and
Tandragee are exclusive of rates?
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I
afraid I must have notice of that.
am
Mr. QUIGLEY: Arising out of that
answer of the noble Viscount in connection with houses and the rents quoted
in the question, I should like to say it
is a fact that in the City of Belfast men
of the working classes have to pay a
great deal more than 12/6 for a house.
Mr. DORMAN: For a single room.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I am now speaking
of houses-12/6, and a great deal more.
Now, I cannot understand it, as up to
March, 1931, the subsidy amounted to
£140. In Comber, which is only some.
eight miles from the city, subsidy houses
have been built which are let at 4/9 per
week. On the ground floor the kitchen
is 15 feet by 15 feet, a room on the
ground floor 10 feet by 8 feet, and a
scullery. There are two bedrooms 15
feet by 15 feet and 15 feet by 8 feet, a
quarter of an acre of a garden, and the
rent is 4/9. How is it that in the city
of Belfast we cannot build houses to let
to the working classes at a less rent than
from 10/- to 17/6 per week, when this
can be done within eight miles from
Belfast.
There are also a number of houses,
I understand, in Coagh built and rented
at less than 5/-, and also in Dungannon.
The working-class people have really, in
my opinion, received practically no benefit from the subsidy. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). These people cannot be
expected to pay 15/- or 17/6 per week:
in rent. Undoubtedly the builders have
had the advantage and, up to the present
time, the working classes have had no
advantage. I was very pleased at the
commencement of this Session when I
heard that the working classes were now
going to get the benefit, but certainly
I have seen no improvement. I feel sure
that they could build houses like what we
have in some parts of the city, rows of
houses down a street, even in the east
end, where some people have fine houses
at 5/6 a week. I sincerely hope the
Government will take a greater interest
in this matter, for it is very necessary.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Without
for one moment defending the imposition
of excessive rents anywhere, I should like
to point out to the hon. Member who
has just spoken that there must be a
considerable difference between the expense of building houses in an urban
area and the expense of building them'
in a country area. I do not know where
the houses are in Dungannon, whether
Electricity Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
they are in the urban area or outside it.
There is, of course, a considerable difference in the expense. Also in regard to
the houses mentioned in Comber, I do
not know whether these houses were built
by a private individual or by the local.
authority, as he did not mention that,
but it is always possible where a private.
individual builds houses either in Comber
or Coagh that he may be content for his
own reasons to accept a rent which an
urban authority could not charge. He
may be content with less than what may
be called an economic rent. I should like
to point out these facts. Of course the
Government will have the matter under
consideration.
Mr. DICKSON: I would just like to
inform the Leader of the House and the
Members that the houses in Comber were
built by the local authority, of which I
happen to be chairman, in Newtownards.
They are let at 14/- per month.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I did not refer to
those houses.

CLAUSE 17 (Duty of the Board to
supply electricity to power users).
(4) The Board shall not be under an
obligation to supply electricity under
this section to a power user in any area
or part of an area, until the Board
have notified, as respects that area
or part thereof, that they are in a
position to supply electricity.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 17, Sub-Clause (4), to leave out
the word "notified," and insert "notified
in such manner as the Ministry of Commerce may prescribe." When I put this
proposal on the Paper it occurred to me
that the insertion of these words would
supply an apparent gap. But I am not
very certain that there is any great necessity to qualify the notification referred
to. The only point was, it struck me,
that the public generally ought to be in
a position to know whether definite notification had been given that the Board
was in a position to supply electricity or
not. Therefore, if the noble Viscount
prefers the text of the Bill as it is I will
not put the Committee to the trouble of
a division.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and learned Member may have observed
that in another place, and also on the
Second Reading of this Measure, certain
criticisms were directed to the effect that
the Ministry of Commerce was intervening too much in the affairs of the Board,
and therefore I do not like the Amendment as proposed. If the hon. and
learned Senator would like to alter it and
delete the word "notified" and insert
"given public notice," I think that might
meet his personal objections without
infringing upon what has been done before. If that change can be made I
would be willing to accept.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the Committee
will perhaps give me leave to withdraw
the first Amendment, and then I will
move another in its place.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in line
19, to leave out the word "notified," and
to insert instead thereof the words, "given
public notice."
CHARLEMONT: I will
Viscount
accept that Amendment.

Electricity Bill-

14 MAY 1931
that
score of
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me
Clause 19 is as convenient a Clause as any
other to raise the general question of the
expression "local
"local authority," which
occurs in the Bill in about a
places. What I really want to know is
whether the noble Viscount is satisfied,
either that the words are used throughcut in the same sense, or that they are
always used in such a way as to be readily
understood. As the Committee knows, the
expression "local authority" is one of
our standing little difficulties, because
sometimes it is defined and very often it
is not. For electrical purposes it began
life in the Schedule to the Act of 1882,
where it was defined as "an urban or
rural sanitary authority, within the meaning of the Public Health Act of 1878,"
A great deal of water has flown under
the local government bridges since then,
including more or less the torrential
stream of 1898, when the Local Government Bill was passed.
Although we are not really concerned
to-day with the childhood of the expression we ought to be satisfied now that
it is used in the same sense throughout
the Bill, and that we understand it. For
instance, in Clause 40, page 37, line 39,
it is perfectly clear what sort of local
authority is meant, because we are referred to streets which are repairable by
the local authority, but there are passages
in the Bill where in default of explanation I think perhaps matters are open
to misunderstanding. In fact, I shall
direct attention to one of them in a few
moments. In the meantime, if the noble
Viscount can give us an assurance on one
of two points, either that the expression
is used throughout in the same sense, or
always in a self-explanatory sense, it
would assist not only this Clause, but
others.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
advised that the definition of "local
authority" contained in Section 31 of the
Electric Lighting Act, 1882, is ample to
cover all cases with the one exception
dealt with in Clause 40. Under existing
electricity laws a county council is not
a local authority as defined in Section
31 of the 1882 Act. As in Northern
Ireland practically all the roads in rural
districts are repairable by the county
council they are not, therefore, for the
purposes of existing electricity legislation,
repairable by the local authority. Consequently when it is desired to obtain
powers to break up a road in a rural
district, it has been necessary hitherto to
have the county roads specially scheduled
in the Special Order.
The provisions of Clause 40 will remove that anomaly and place county
roads in the same category as roads repairable by an urban district council,
or a borough or county borough council,
and the county council will have all the
protection extended by the Acts to urban,
borough and county borough councils.
The Clause, it will be observed, also
provides for Amendments in the Acts of
1899 and 1919 consequential on this
change in existing law. I hope this explanation will satisfy the hon. and
learned Member.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The only definition
of local authority is contained in Part II
of the First Schedule. There the definition of local authority is that contained
in Section 31 of the Act of 1882, but it
is strictly limited in its context to that
particular part of the Schedule.
For
Mr. LESLIE: I was going to draw
attention to the same point, and to the
fact that it occurs in the second part of
the Schedule without a definition.
myself I do not see any great necessity
for the definition in the second part of
the First Schedule, having regard to what
the noble Lord has told us. He has referred us to the definition in Section 31
of the Act of 1882, which recurs in the
Schedule of that Act which we are
quoting. As that definition occurs in the
1882 Act it automatically appears in this
Bill also, because this Bill is going to be
cited and construed with the Act of 1882,
so that any definition in the 1882 Act will
come in here automatically. Therefore,
I think, everything is quite all right.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
advised that that is so.

(2) The exercise of the powers of

Electricity Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
*
(a) The Board shall not sell electric
fittings except-
(i) to a consumer or a person who
intends to be a consumer of electricity
supplied by them, or
(ii) to a person who, being engaged
in the business of selling and installing electric fittings, requires
such fittings to enable him to supply
them to a person who is or intends
to be a consumer of electricity
supplied by the Board.
*
*
*
*
*
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Major
Dobbs :-
Clause 21, page 17, line 30, after
"them" insert "and then only such
fittings as shall be necessary for that
consumption."
Major DOBBS: When I put down this
Amendment I had in my mind the idea
of protecting the small retailers, but on
further consideration I came to the conclusion that they were sufficiently safeguarded under paragraph (c). I also
understand that there would be considerable difficulty in carrying out my
suggestion in practice. Therefore, I do
not propose to move the Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
Major DOBBS: I beg to move, in
paragraph (a) (ii), at the end to insert
the following new paragraph:-
(b) No fitting sold or supplied shall
be of foreign manufacture.
I should have preferred to have put
this Amendment down in a more confirmatory manner, such as, "All fittings
provided, sold, or let on hire shall be of
British manufacture," but the words
"British manufacture" might rule out
fittings manufactured in the North of
Ireland. I am afraid that even if this
Amendment is passed it will not do much
to help employment, but at any rate it
will be a gesture in the right direction.
I think the agents of the Board who go
around showing electric fittings would be
met most sympathetically and enthusiastically if they were able to say, "Yes,
these are all of British manufacture." I
do not quite understand what the word
"manufacture" in the third line of the
Clause really means. The Board are not
able to manufacture, but I do not know
whether they would be able to ask a firm
to
ur
in Wolverhampton or Manchester or any
other places manufacture certain
articles for them in order to enable them
some American
to compete with
German fitting. At any rate I do think
this Amendment, if adopted, would be
of real help in enabling British manufacturers to compete with foreign fittings.
I would like to point out that it does not
prevent a user from buying foreign stuff,
so that the Free Traders bug-bear of a
tariff is absent from this Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am in
full sympathy with the object the hon.
and gallant Member has in view, but I
am afraid I cannot accept his Amendment because, I think, it is extremely
doubtful if this Parliament has power to
insert in a Bill a provision in restraint
of trade on the lines suggested.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

(4) The maximum amount which may
be borrowed by the Board, otherwise
than for the purpose of paying off loans
previously raised, shall be one million
pounds:
*
*
*
Major DOBBS:
*
*
I beg to move, in
Clause 27, sub-section (4) after the word
"raised" to insert "before the passing of
this Act." I am afraid I am not very
clear about what this sub-clause means.
I quite realise I may have stumbled on a
mare's nest, but it does seem to me that it
might be possible for the Board, if this
Amendment is not accepted, to raise a
million pounds one year, the next year
to raise a million pounds to pay off the
previous million, and then to raise another
million pounds to work with, which would
become two perpetual borrowings. I beg
to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
this Amendment is based on a misappre-
Electricity Bill-
Committee
14 MAY 1931
hension of the position. No loans will
be raised prior to the passing of the Act,
and the only object of the sub-section is
that where the Board have raised a loan,
say of £600,000 for a short term, say five
years, and at the end of five years the
Board decide to raise a loan of say
£1,200,000, one-half whereof would be
used to liquidate the first loan, then the
limit of £1,000,000 will not apply. In
effect the object of the sub-clause is to
restrict to £1,000,000 the Board's indebtedness at any time on the borrowings.
The Amendment would defeat that object,
and therefore, I cannot accept it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

issue stock).
*
*
*
*
