(3) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, any stock created by the Board
under the powers of this Act shall be
issued, transferred, dealt with and redeemed according to regulations made.
by the Ministry of Commerce with the
approval of the Ministry of Finance,
and any such regulations may apply
for the purpose of this section, with or
without modifications, any provisions
of the Government Loans and Exchequer Provisions Act (Northern Ireland), 1925, the Public Health Acts
Amendment Act, 1890, and the enactments amending those Acts, and of any
Act relating to stock issued by a local
authority.
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 28, sub-section (3), line 14, to leave
out from "Acts" to the end of the subclause. The words I suggest to leave out
here are found, of course, in the English
Act of 1926 in exactly the same subclause of section 28 of that Act. It has
nothing to do with me whether these
words were applicable to Great Britain,
but I suggest to the Committee that they
are not applicable to Northern Ireland,
and that we ought not to put them in.
The Bill is giving power to the Board to
issue, transfer, deal in, and redeem its
stock according to regulations made by
the Ministry of Commerce with the
approval of the Ministry of Finance, and
the regulations may apply, with or without modification, any provisions of the
Government Loans and Exchequer Provisions Act, as to which I do not know
whether the noble Viscount has a short
résumé for the convenience of the Committee also the Public Health (Amendment) Act of 1896, which deals with the
matter very briefly, and does not make
much difference. We add to that "and
the enactments amending those Acts,"
but then we go on to say "and of any Act
relating to stock issued by a local
authority."
That seems to me a rather dangerous
leap in the dark. I do not think it would
be very much use to us. I am not sure
whether there is any such stock issued in
Northern Ireland outside the Belfast Corporation. But of course some local
authority, using the expression in the
sense which we are told it is to bear
might, and they might have some extraordinary conditions we might not like to
see incorporated. Or again it might be
that these words would include provisions
relating to stock in England which would
be a very wide matter. I venture to
suggest on the whole that the words are
of no great value, and there is a possibility of some difficulty arising in
Therefore, I think, we should
do as I suggest and stop Clause 28 at the
word "Acts" in line 14. I beg to move.
future.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In addition
to the Acts specifically
specifically mentioned
in the sub-clause, which are the main
enactments governing borrowing by
Local Authorities, a number of subsequent enactments have been passed, for
example, the Public Authorities and
Bodies Loans Act, 1916, and the Housing
Additional Powers Act, 1919, which confer certain additional borrowing powers on
Local Authorities, and it is, accordingly,
desirable that the Ministry of Commerce
in making regulations should have the
power to apply the provisions of these
further Acts to the transactions of the
Board in connection with the issue,
transfer and redemption of its stock. I
am therefore unable to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: On that explanation,
which is not quite clear, it seems to me
that the right thing would be to ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment and bring up
a slightly different form of words on Report.
The intention now is to limit the effects
of these additional words to public enactments applying generally to stocks issued
by local authorities. That is what I
Electricity Bill-

gather from the noble Viscount's explanation, though I certainly have not quite
grasped it. I venture to suggest it is not
quite clear. If the Committee allow me

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

CLAUSE 37 (Wayleaves).

*
*
*
(c) If the owner or occupier of the
land refuses or within the said fourteen
days fails to give his consent as aforesaid the undertakers may, with the
consent of the Ministry of Commerce
but not otherwise, place the line across
the land in the position and manner
stated in the notice.
or
(d) A notice under this sub-section
may be served upon the owner
occupier of any land by delivering it
to him, or by leaving it or forwarding
it by post addressed to him at his usual
or last known place of abode and may
be addressed to the "owner"
"occupier" of the lands, naming them)
without further name or description.
*
*
*
*
*
*
or
Major DOBBS: I beg to move in
Clause 37, Sub-Clause (2), paragraph (c),
after "or" in the first line to insert
"the." My only object in putting
this forward is to make it quite certain
that both the owner and the occupier are
able to refuse their consent. It seems to
me that as the words stand we might have
the owner refusing and the occupier not
having a chance of refusing when the
owner's refusal had been cleared up.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
advised that this Amendment is unnecessary. I may point out that the
phraseology "the owner or occupier" used
in the sub-section in question follows
that which has been in existence in connection with wayleaves since 1919.
I am,
therefore, unable to accept.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Major DOBBS: I beg to move, in paragraph (d), to leave out the word "abode"
to the end of the paragraph. I see no
reason why the authorised undertaker
should not take the trouble to find out
who are the owner and occupier of the
lands they want to run their lines
through. I do not see why this work
should be done by the General Post Office.
I am also most anxious that the owner
or occupier should have an opportunity to
refuse. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am unable to accept this Amendment. I should
explain that Clause 37 re-enacts the existing provisions of the Electricity (Supply)
Acts relating to wayleaves, with certain
modifications which experience has proved
to be necessary. The Sub-Clause in
question re-enacts without any modification Sub-Section 6, of Section 22, of
the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, which
is already in force in Northern Ireland.
The Amendment, if accepted, might have
the effect of holding up the construction
of a line indefinitely owing to difficulties
in tracing the owner or occupier.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Major DOBBS: I beg to move at the
end of paragraph (d) to insert the following new paragraph:
(e) In the placing of an electric line
across any land due regard shall be had
to the amenities of the countryside.
I greatly fear the country may be seriously
disfigured by the lines, standards, and
pylons, and so on, put up unless the
greatest care is taken. It is a terrible
thing to see in the outskirts of London
what has happened. I must say the lines
and standards are not very serious, but
they add to the disfigurement of the
country.
The hon. Senator, Mr McAllister, on
the Second Reading pointed out that a lot
of these pylons and standards were placed
in the middle of fields. I think there
ought to be an axiom that the standards
should be put in the hedges. I do
not mean that the line should be
along the hedges. Of course,
that is quite out of the
run
question,
Committee
Electricity Bill-

14 MAY 1931
but at any rate, as far as possible, the
standards should be put in the hedges.
That would reduce the number in the
field. In a very small field there should
be no necessity to have a standard in the
centre. I quite realise that the engineer
must put the standards exactly 200 yards
apart or whatever the distance is, and
23 that if the posts were 199 yards apart
the current would not flow. I still cannot
see why he should not be required to take
care to put as few standards in the fields
themselves as possible.
P
We are giving power to the owner and
to the occupier to refuse leave, and you
are not taking power that other people
in the neighbourhood should have a say in
the matter. I have got here a cutting
from "The Times' of Tuesday about the
pylons of Surrey. It is a letter from the
chairman of the Bookham Parish Council.
I will not read the whole of it, but I
would like to read one or two points.
He starts off by saying he wishes to call
attention to what appears to be the very
unnecessary spoiling of one of the loveliest
views in Surrey, and he goes on to say that
the County Council in the erection of
pylons have been doing it. They have
selected spots thirty or forty feet from the
high road, standing right up against the
skyline, and completely spoiling the view.
I do not know whether any of the
Members know that part of the country,
but it is a wonderful piece of country,
and has some very fine beauty spots.
it?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Where is
Major DOBBS: Bookham, fifty miles
east of Guildford. We might have the
leave of the owner and occupier for the
line to be put up, and it might become
a terrible obstacle and terribly spoil the
beauty of the whole scenery.
The only
protection appears to be in the Ministry
of Commerce. Of course at the present
time I realise we could not have better
protection, but in years to come we might
not have that protection. I feel very
strongly on this matter. I think the
mere fact that the authorised undertakers the sound of the word itself is
enough—at any rate I am afraid I would
not trust them. I know a case of very
much the same sort happened some years
ago.
The General Post Office wanted to put
up a line of posts to carry a line up a
certain road. The owner, who was my
father, did not like it being on a
particular side of the road, and suggested
that it should be put on the other side.
I went up to see the chief man in Belfast
of the National Telephones to explain the
matter to him. I said that both sides of
the road belonged to my father, that he
strongly objected to having the line of
posts up on a certain side, but had no
objection to the other side being used.
No one else was affected at all. Amongst
other things, I pointed out that it was
demesne land. But the gentleman could
not see eye to
with me.
eye
He said there
were a lot of trees, and we must take care
of them. I said, "The Post Office are
always extremely careful in the way of
lopping trees, and I am pepared to give
sanction for the lopping of these trees,
and, if necessary, you may cut them all
down. Further, if necessary, we will
cut them down ourselves. But I said
my father strongly objects to having the
posts on this side of the road." He told
me that of course the Postmaster-General
had complete power in the matter and
could put the poles where he liked, and
he was afraid he could not alter what had
been done. I got up and said: "I give
you 48 hours' notice in which to take
down all the posts on a certain road,
because most of them are trespassing on
my father's ground."
I then went out, but he called me back.
A bell was rung, and a special engineer
was sent for, and he was asked this
question: "Is there any reason why the
posts should be put on one side of the
road, the east side, and not on the west ?"
The answer was: "No, sir. No reason
at all." It does make me
feel very
strongly on the matter. We want some
special protection for the countryside.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Again I
find myself in full sympathy with the
object that the hon. Member has in
mind. I do not think, however, it would
be practicable to incorporate this Amendment in the Bill. The hon. Member will
observe that already in sub-clause 8 of
Clause 37 the Ministry of Commerce, before giving its consent to the placing or
retention of an electric line across any
land, is to have regard, among other considerations, to the effect, if any, on the
amenities or the value of the land of the
placing of the line in the position and
manner proposed. I may explain that
the Ministry is required by Statute to
Electricity Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
refer to the local authority all applications for permission to construct an overhead line, and this gives an opportunity
for raising the question of interference
with the general amenities of the countryside. The adoption of the Amendment
would tend to prove embarrassing without.
really giving any opportunities additional
to those which exist to-day to objectors to
the construction of an overhead line.
With regard to the question of placing
pylons in hedges, and having consideration for the owners of property, experience
of the working of these provisions under the
Act of 1919 has shown that there is not
any great difficulty in obtaining the cooperation and consent of the farmers in
the placing of overhead lines across their
lands. In the case of the existing line
between Belfast and Lisburn only one
case came before the Ministry for decision,
and when the line was extended as far as
Lurgan last year wayleaves were obtained.
in every case without an appeal to the
Ministry. The Ministry is at present
negotiating wayleaves for the lines between
Lurgan and Armagh, and between Belfast
and Newtownards, and, so far, no difficulties have arisen. Steps are taken to meet
the wishes of the landowners as far as
possible, and, while it is not possible to
place every pole in a hedge, the greatest
care is taken to secure that the lines when
erected will cause the minimum of interference with agricultural operations.
am afraid I cannot accept the Amendment.
I

(4) The authorised undertakers shall
issue instructions to their officers and
servants with a view to securing that
trees and hedges shall be lopped or cut
in a woodmanlike manner, and so as to
do as little damage as may be to trees,
fences, hedges and growing crops, and
shall cause the boughs lopped to be removed in accordance with the directions
of the owner or occupier, and shall make
good any damage done to the land.
*
*
*
*
*
Major DOBBS: I beg to move, in
Clause 39, sub-section (4), to leave out
the word "woodmanlike" and insert
"workmanlike." I have no objection at
all to the word "woodmanlike," but I do
not think it is an English word-I cannot
find it in the dictionary-and I suggest
the word "workmanlike," which we all
know, should be used instead.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think it
is a most deplorable thing that any
Senator should wish to
to deprive any
Government department of the honour
that accrues to them for enriching the
English language with a new word.
(Laughter.) I admit that when I read it
for the first time I thought it was an odd
word, and I think so still, but I would
like to remind this House that the word
"regional" was invented by the Minister
of Pensions, and my own Ministry,
although it did not invent it, produced
the word "educable." (Laughter.) If
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Commerce, likes the word "woodmanlike”
I think it is very unfortunate that any
criticism should be made. I am rather
sorry that the honour of inventing this
word is not his, because it is used in the
Electricity Supply Act of 1926. At least,
this Clause is similar in all respects to the
Clause in the English Act, although I do
not know whether the word "woodmanlike" is included in the English Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: It is.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Therefore
the honour is not ours. The hon. and
gallant Member would like to deprive
us of the use of this word, but I
would point out to him that it is
used designedly so that the lopping
of branches can be done in a way that
will cause no ill effects to the tree itself.
Electricity Bill-
Committee.
14 MAY 1931
If you tell a carpenter to deal with a
tree in a workmanlike manner he might
cut it down altogether, whereas if he did
it in a woodmanlike way he would only
lop it.
Therefore I cannot accept the
Amendment.
Major DOBBS: I have very great
pleasure in asking leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSES 40 (Amendments of law as
to breaking up of streets, railways, etc.),
41 (Use of electricity supply for purposes
of railways, etc.), and 42 (Stand-up supplies of electricity), ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Joseph
Cunningham:-
After Clause 42 insert the following
new Clause:
-
Power of Local Authority to maintain Showrooms and hold Exhibitions,
etc.-"A local authority, being authorised undertakers, may maintain showrooms for the display of electric lines,
fittings, apparatus and appliances for
lighting, heating and motive power,
and for all other purposes for which
electricity can or may be used (in this
section called 'electric fittings'), and
may conduct displays, exhibitions, and
demonstrations of electric fittings, and
generally do all things (including advertising) incidental to the promotion
and encouragement of the use of
electricity."
new
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
Electricity Committee of the Belfast Corporation have taken a considerable
interest in this Bill, and I believe they
have had several of their Amendments
adopted in another place. The
Clause I have put on the Paper is one
that these authorised undertakers consider reasonable, and they think it should
be inserted in the Bill. The cost of a
generating station is enormous, and it is
necessary by advertisements and shows to
do everything possible to increase the consumption of electricity. By that means
the undertakers can reduce the cost to the
consumers, and therefore it is very
necessary that they should have the power
to establish showrooms for the display of
electrical fittings and appliances. At the
present time the Electricity Committee
of the Belfast Corporation have showrooms at their Bridge End premises, and
I know it has been the means of inducing
many people to adopt the use of electric
stoves, heaters, and appliances of that
kind.
I might also point out that the Gas
Committee of the Belfast Corporation
already have this power to maintain showrooms. There is no doubt that these
showrooms have been the means of considerably increasing the use of gas, because they exhibit to housewives the great
advantages of cooking and heating stoves,
and of a good light. I think it is advisable that
that the Electricity Committee
should have the same power. I would
point out that there is no desire on the
part of the Committee to acquire power
to sell fittings; they ask only for power
to advertise them and bring them to the
notice of the public by means of showrooms. I do not anticipate that there
will be any objection to this Amendment
either from the Government or Members
of the Committee, because I think it is a
very reasonable request.
Mr. McCORKELL: I strongly support.
this Amendment, and I am only
sorry it does not go further. I think
it would be only logical if the Belfast Corporation asked for power to
sell electric fittings and appliances, and
for this reason. Under Clause 21 of
the Bill the Electricity Board has
power not only to show and exhibit
electric fittings, but to sell them, and in
the English Act there is a Section which
permits any local authority to display
electric fittings and also to sell them. To
bring the matter a little nearer home, in
my own City of Derry the Corporation
has power not only to display but to sell,
and yet that same power is apparently
not vested in the Corporation of the City
of Belfast. The Electricity Board has
that power, and I am sure they as well
as the general public will welcome it The
reason some people think a local authority
should not have this power is that the
traders might object to it. If that was
so they would object even more strongly
to the Electricity Board having that
privilege, because the Board is really not
in touch with the traders.
It sits up
above them, and can trade in any way it
likes.
On the other hand, a local authority
should be in constant and friendly relationship with the traders in their area.
Electricity Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McCorkell]
Take a concrete case.
Recently in Derry
City showrooms were opened, in which it
is permissible to sell electric fittings. But
the first thing one notices on entering the
showrooms is a notice which says: "These
things are merely displayed for information and are not for sale." I think the
notice also says the articles displayed may
be obtained locally. That is a help and
an encouragement to the local trader,
because the people see these things in the
showrooms, and then go round to the
local shops. If they are not stocked, it
is the duty of the local authority to stock
them and keep up-to-date.
Two things that have been associated
in our minds for a very long time are law
and logic. It is illogical to give this power
to one local authority and withhold it from
another, but the position is still more
illogical when there is a third party in the
form of the Electricity Board who are
given this power. Tradesmen cannot
argue with the Board. I am sorry the
Amendment does not go further and seek
power to sell electric fittings, because a
trader can come into contact with his local
authority in a way that is not possible in
the case of the Electricity Board if, as
I expect, it sets itself on a plane of
superiority over the rest of us.
I do not
see why one local authority should have
an advantage over another in this respect,
and why the Electricity Board should have
an advantage over both. I think it would
be in the interests of the traders themselves that the local authority should
possess this power.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
would be inclined to go a little further
than the Amendment does, in the direction of allowing the Electricity Board to
give instruction in the use of electrical
apparatus. The Gas Light and Coke
Company of London was almost wiped
out by electricity, but they appointed a
number of ladies to visit the housewives
in their district, and these ladies showed
them how to cook with and use gas for
various purposes.
The result is that that
company is in a better position to-day
than it ever was before. Therefore. I
think that in addition to having showrooms, authorised undertakers should also
have power to appoint instructors to teach
people how to use electricity in various
ways. I got an electric cooker installed
in my house, and we found it very difficult at first to use it to the best advantage.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to support
this new Clause. It seems to me that
what we are aiming at in this Bill is to
do what I may call a really big thing
in a really big way, and I can imagine
few things more calculated to assist us
in promoting the use of electricity than
giving power to a large local authority,
such as the City of Belfast, to, in the
very words of the Clause," generally do
all things (including advertising) incidental to the promotion and encouragement of the use of electricity." I quite
agree with the Mayor of Derry that there
are few things you can do which will act
more effectively in encouraging the use
of electricity than to have showrooms exhibiting the uses to which electricity can
be applied. Shows of that kind are
popular more or less at different times
across the water, and they always attract
a crowd. People do learn from them,
and they go away and become more imbued than they were before with a desire
to install electricity and make use of it,
and also I should be quite willing for
my own part to permit the local authority
to sell small fittings on the ground that
the local authorities can deal with these
matters also in a large way. They can
obtain their supplies at a low cost, I
think, and can sell them at a low cost
also. And there again they popularise
electricity by putting the necessary fittings at the disposal of the ratepayers
and public at a low rate. I do not myself
see that that would be embarking on a
new undertaking to which the ordinary
objections of the local trade apply with
anything like their ordinary force. However, we are not upon that point at present. We are on allowing the maintenance of showrooms. On that point I
heartily support the Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the Amendment. With
regard to what was said about strengthening or going rather further in the interests
of Belfast Corporation, I think some time
ago they promoted a private Bill, and
while the Bill was under discussion the
limited powers to sell now enjoyed by
the Corporation were agreed upon between
them and the local traders. Of course,
if they wished to extend the powers at
any time it would be open for them to
proceed by means of a private Bill.
Amendment agreed to.

FIFTH SCHEDULE. (Provisions
consequential upon abolition of Electicity
Commissioners).
*
*
*
*
*
1. The Ministry of Commerce shall,
subject to the approval of the Ministry of
Finance, at the beginning of each financial
year prepare an estimate of the amounts
payable out of moneys provided by Parliament in respect of the expenses incurred
by the Ministry of Commerce in the said.
financial year in the exercise and performance of the duties transferred to it from
the Electricity Commissioners for Northern Ireland, and the said Ministry shall
also prepare an estimate of receipts payable to the said Ministry in the exercise
and performance of the said powers and
duties.
*
*
*
*
*
5. Any sums due from any authorised
undertakers under this section shall be
payable on demand and shall be recoverable, together with interest under Article
6 of this Part of this Schedule, by the
Ministry of Commerce as a debt due to
that Ministry from the undertakers.
*
*
*
*
*
*
7. Section 29 of the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, and section one of the
Electricity and Gas (Expenses) Act (Northern Ireland), 1923, shall cease to have
effect:
Provided that any sum remaining due
from authorised undertakers under those
enactments at the day appointed for the
abolition of the said Electricity Commissioners shall as from that day be payable to, and may be recovered by, the
Ministry of Commerce as if it were due
under this Part of this Schedule.
*
*
*
*
*
*
8. The proviso to section twenty-six of
the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, and
section thirt-five of, and the Schedule to,
that Act shall cease to have effect.
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after
"expenses" (in paragraph 1), to insert
"to be." On reading this through it
seemed to me there was a slight ambiguity
as to what the Estimates were related to.
The Ministry is to prepare an estimate of
the amounts payable out of moneys provided by Parliament in respect of the
expenses incurred by them in the said
financial year. Without the insertion of the
words I suggest it might be understood as
relating solely to what is called "ascertained commitments," which were known
definitely at the beginning of the year.
The intention, I presume, is that the
estimate should be made not only of the
ascertained sums to which the Ministry
is definitely committed, but also what we
might describe as the ordinary running
expenses. I presume the intention is that
the estimate should be as wide and have
as reasonable latitude as possible. Therefore it seemed to me it might be desirable
G
Committee
Electricity Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
to try to clear things up a little by inserting the two words "to be" after the word
"expenses,' in line 10. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am advised that this Amendment is unnecessary,
for if the hon. Member will examine the
section as a whole, I think he will agree
that the phrase "the expenses incurred by
the Ministry of Commerce" is a phrase
descriptive of its activities in the exercise
and performance of the duties transferred
to it from the Electricity Commissioners.
Mr. LESLIE: If that is the explanation, and it is perfectly clear that the two
Ministries concerned thoroughly understand the situation, I do not think I need
trouble.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I think something is not
quite right here (in paragraph 5), but I
am not quite certain what we ought to do.
First of all the word "section" occurs in
line 44. It is not clear. It is in an
article really, and as there are not any
sums due to any authorised undertakers
under this, the only way it seems to me
we can make sense of the word "section"
is by assuming that it means section
55. Really this fifth schedule is not so
much a schedule of the Bill as it is an
appendix to Clause 55. It deals with
various things to happen when the Electricity Commissioners are abolished.
A little further on in article 7 we find
that certain sums may be recovered as due
under this part of the schedule. Therefore I am inclined to think that what we
ought really to do is to leave out the word
"section" and insert in place of it "part
of this schedule" and make it read "in this
part of this schedule." It will be noticed
that no reference is given at the head of the
schedule to a section of the Bill to which
it relates, and, as the Committee knows,
that is usually done. I gather it has
been omitted here for a particular purpose, because some parts of the proposed
schedule are not particularly related to
Clause 55. If the reference had been
there, or if it could have been put there,
perhaps it might have made things a little
easier. As it is, I am inclined to think
everything will be in order if we just
leave out the word "section" in article 5,
and insert "part of this schedule." Accordingly that is what I move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept that.
Amendment agreed to.
.-

for
Mr. LESLIE: Is it really necessary
us to sit to-morrow? We have only to
give the Marketing of Eggs Bill a Third
Reading. We shall have to sit next week,
I take it. We are taking, I think, the
Planning and Housing Bill next week.
Can we possibly take that next week?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I suppose
on Thursday it would be possible to take
it. However, next week can take care
As
of itself for the time being. I agree
about the Marketing of Eggs Bill.
the hon. Senator knows, it has been
amended, and it is wanted in another
place, and as we cannot take it to-day we
must take it to-morrow. It is only for
that purpose.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
half-past Four o'clock.
Business of the House
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Monday, 18th May, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Business of the House.
The following Motion stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Viscount
Charlemont: -
-
"That Standing Order No. XXV be
suspended for this day's sitting for the
purpose of taking the remaining stages of
the Electricity (Supply) Bill, the Incorporated Belfast Maternity Hospital and
the Royal Victoria Hospital Bill, and the
Donaghadee Urban District Council Bill,"
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I should like to say that this
Motion does not apply to the last-named
Bill, the Donaghadee Urban District
Council Bill. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Incorporated Belfast Maternity
Hospital and the Royal Victoria
Hospital Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee to the said Bill were considered
and agreed to.
Bill read the Third time and passed.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the Amendments
made by the Joint Committee without any
Amendment.
Question to Minister.
NORTHERN GOVERNMENT'S
POWERS OF TAXATION.
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether, in view of
the various reports appearing in the
Press as
to the powers of the Government of Northern Ireland with
regard to taxation, he will state
what these powers are, particularly
with respect to the taxation of land values
and super-tax.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: The
Government of Ireland Act legislates in
two directions in respect of taxation.
Firstly, it states that there is a general
power inherent in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland to impose taxation,
then follow certain exceptions. Secondly,
it states what powers of taxation are reserved to the Imperial Parliament. The
Section dealing with the reserved powers
is not merely a recital of what is excluded
from this Parliament's powers by the
earlier Section, but the exclusions are
wider than the list of reserved taxes.
This is owing to the fact that in addition
to the exclusions there are direct prohibitions against this Parliament levying
certain types of tax. One of these prohibited taxes is "any tax of the nature of
a general tax on capital, not being a tax
substantially the same in character as a
tax in existence" in 1920. This caveat
was, I believe, introduced so as to ensure
that we have power to levy death duties.
Having said this, I can perhaps now
illustrate more clearly what the position
is as regards individual types of taxes.
Taking the reserved taxes first, we find
that Section 22 of the Act of 1920 reserved to the Imperial Parliament the
levying of Customs and Excise Duties, the
power of imposing Income Tax-including
super-tax, which is one of the items specificially mentioned in the Senator's question-Excess Profits Duty, Corporation
Profits Tax, and any other tax on profits.
I would like Senators carefully to observe
those words, "any other tax on profits,'
because they are of importance in considering the question in which, I imagine,
this House is more immediately interested at the moment. In Section 21 of
the Act, as is natural, these types of duties.
and taxes are again rehearsed as being
excluded from the Northern Ireland's
powers. There is, however, added to the
exclusions the following, namely, "any
tax substantially the same in character as
any of those duties or taxes." This
addition was, no doubt, designed as a
prohibition against
against Northern Ireland
levying a tax exactly the same in
character as a reserve tax, but calling it
by some other name.
The final prohibition in Section 21 is
against this Parliament imposing any tax
"of the nature of a general tax upon
capital." I have already, in my preliminary remarks, referred to this prohibition.
It only remains to decide whether a tax
on land values similar to the tax proposed
to be levied by legislation in Great Britain
comes within either of the exceptions, so
far as Northern Ireland is concerned,
Electricity Bill-
Report Stage.
18 MAY 1931
namely, "a tax on profits" or "a general
In the opinion of
tax upon capital."
the advisers of this Government such a
tax would not fall within either of these
exceptions. It follows, therefore, that
such a tax could be imposed by the
Parliament of Northern Ireland.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I hope the hon.
Senator (Mr. Dorman) is not dazzled with
the flood of light shed on him by the
answer of the noble Lord. May I ask,
now that we have ascertained that the
Parliament of Northern Ireland has
power to impose taxation on land values,
whether there is the least possibility that
the Government will suggest to Parliament the imposition of such a tax?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The land
tax has not been passed in England at
the present moment, and, therefore, it
would clearly be outside my jurisdiction.
to give any reply that could possibly add
any enlightenment to the answer.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Mr. Gladstone
could not have said it better.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
great compliment.
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
That is a

Moved, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass.'

Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.

The said Amendments were accordingly

CLAUSE 5 (Advisory Committee).
*
*
*
*
*
*
(2) The advisory committee shall be
constituted of the following persons
appointed by the Minister of Commerce:-
One person nominated by the
council of each county in any part
of which the Board are distributing
or propose to distribute electricity;
One person nominated by the
council of each borough and urban
district within which the Board are
distributing or propose to distribute
electricity, and being a borough or
district the population of which,
according to the census for the time
being last ascertained, exceeds 10,000;
Four persons nominated by the
Association of Municipal Authorities
of Northern Ireland, being representative of areas within which the
Board are distributing or propose to
distribute electricity;
Four persons nominated by the
Association of Rural District Councils
in Northern Ireland, being representative of areas within which the
Board are distributing or propose to
distribute electricity;
Two persons representative of the
Associated Chambers of Commerce in
Northern Ireland;
Such additional persons (not exceeding four in number) as the
Minister of Commerce may think fit
to appoint from time to time.
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to move, in
Clause 5, Sub-clause (2) at the end of
line 29, to insert-
One person nominated by the
organised workers engaged in the production and distribution of electricity
under the Board.
According to the Bill, the purpose of
this Advisory Committee is to advise the
Ministry of Commerce and assist them on
such matters connected with the general
improvement and development of the
supply of electricity as may be referred
to the Committee by the Ministry. I find
that almost every interest is represented
on this Advisory Committee, but no provision is made for any representation of
the large staff that will necessary be ultimately employed in the development of
electricity in this area. It is well known
that in private businesses and municipal
undertakings, where an opportunity is
afforded to those who are actually in con-
Electricity Bill—
Report Stage
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
tact with the plant, and where encouragement is given to them to pass on suggestions in the interests of the undertaking to those who have control of the
management, such undertakings have been
remarkably successful, and have developed
out of all proportions to similar undertakings in which such facilities for passing
on suggestions have not been afforded to
the employees. I am sure we all desire
to assist the Ministry and the Government in making this great undertaking
a success in the future, but it appears
to me that if the Government shut out
from representation on the Advisory Committee the large number of individuals
who will necessarily have an extensive
knowledge of the actual working of the
plant and so forth they will be depriving
themselves of one of the most important
means for securing the future success and
development of the undertaking.
I have personally studied the development of this mysterious power for over
fifty-two years.
I have seen it advance
scientifically and industrially, and I know
that many of the improvements that have
taken place in the development of electrical power have emanated from men
occupying what were considered to be inferior positions, and who passed on
valuable suggestions to those above them.
I have in mind an electrical undertaking
in a certain town in this country. Thirty
years ago some of the subordinates connected with the plant made suggestions
to the management with regard to the
treatment of the accumulators and
storage batteries, but the manager was
not in favour of receiving suggestions
from inferiors. One day a gentleman
happened to be sitting at a lunch table in
a hotel fifty miles away. The name of
the town I am referring to happened to
be mentioned and this gentleman asked
another man if he took any interest in
this town, and he said he did. The other
gentleman then said, "Iam on my holidays,
and I have been inspecting the electrical
undertakings of this country. I occupy
the position of electrical adviser to many
of the large municipalities across the
water, and I want to tell you that unless
attention is immediately paid to the
storage batteries in your town disaster will
overtake it."
On his return to his own town that man
immediately conveyed this information to
a member of the Electricity Committee
and it was found that from £3,000 to
£5,000 worth of damage had already been
done to the plant so far as the storage
power was concerned, and it would have
been absolutely derelict in a few years,
not having lasted for one-tenth of the
ordinary duration of a plant of that
character. I mention this to show the
House that from the ranks of the actual
workers there may come very useful suggestions which would assist the Ministry
of Commerce in the management and development of this great concern. Therefore for these reasons I have every confidence that the House will support the
Amendment which I move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to support
the Amendment, and on one ground particularly. On looking over the Bill I see
it is laid down that on this Advisory Committee there are to be two representatives
of the Associated Chambers of Commerce
in Northern Ireland. I think that when
the Government give recognition to this
body, the general body of the people of
Northern Ireland, who form by far the
great majority, should have at least one
representative on this Committes, as is
very reasonably suggested in the Amendment. I think the House will agree that
it is the wish of everyone that this Bill
should secure the whole-hearted co-operation and enthusiastic support of every
section and every class of the community,
and that nothing should be done by the
Bill that will cause suspicion, but that,
on the other hand, everything should be
done to allay fears and spread confidence.
I think the fact that the Associated
Chambers of Commerce have two representatives on this Committee justifies the
Amendment on the Paper, and it would
be in the best interests of the Bill that it
should be accepted. After all, if this
Bill is going to function it will have to
secure the enthusiastic support of all
sections of the community, and I think
it is a reasonable demand on the part of
those whom Senator Dorman represents
that they should have one representative
on this Advisory Committee. The
workers would then feel that they had a
representative to whom they could make
their suggestions direct, and if he did not
carry out their wishes I would not like
to be in his place.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I also would like
to commend the Amendment to the goodwill of the House. Clause 5 enables the
Minister of Commerce to appoint a large
number of representatives on this Advisory Committee. I do not know the exact
Electricity Bill-
Report Stage.
18 MAY 1931
workers are
number, but it cannot be less than twenty,
when you take into account representation for each county and borough and
urban district, the Association of Municipal Authorities of Northern Ireland, the
Association of Rural District Councils of
Northern Ireland, the Associated
Chambers of Commerce in Northern Ireland, and such additional persons, not exceeding four in number, as may be
appointed by the Minister of Commerce.
The request made by Senator Dorman is
a very modest one. It simply asks for
the appointment of one representative of
the organised workers. These organised
men of great practical
experience in the working and development of electricity undertakings. They
are men who will be interested in the wellbeing and progress of this electricity
scheme. Besides in a place like this I
think it is a good thing to encourage, as
far as we can, the co-operation of
employers and employees, and a great
public undertaking of this character
should set an example in a matter of this
kind. It is a very modest demand and
one that is eminently fair. It will be
productive of good, and it will give the
workers a very direct interest in making
the scheme the success which we all desire
it should be.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: While
I have no objection to the Amendment, I
do not envy the task of the Minister of
Commerce in selecting one representative
of the organised workers to serve on this
Advisory Committee. The workers
engaged in the production and distribution of electricity form a very large body.
You have the firemen at the boilers, the
engineers who make the turbines, the
electricians who deal with the wiring, and
many other skilled and semi-skilled
workers who are engaged in various
branches of this great industry. I hope
Senator Dorman will not be dissatisfied
when the Minister of Commerce comes to
make his selection. I know it will be a
very difficult task.
Mr. DORMAN: We do not ask the
Minister to make the selection. The intention is that the representative should
be nominated by the organised workers
themselves.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
sub-section says:—
The Advisory Committee shall be
constituted of the following persons
appointed by the Minister of Commerce:-
It goes on to state the different bodies
to be represented, and to these Senator
Dorman wants to add one representative
to be nominated by the organised workers
engaged in the production and distribution of electricity under the Board. The
Minister of Commerce will have to ask
these organised workers to nominate a
representative. As I have said, there are
a very large number of workers engaged
in this industry, and it will be very
difficult to satisfy all of them by selecting
one of their number.
It
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I find myself rather in disagreement with the mover
of this Amendment. As I read the whole
Clause it does not seem to me that the
purpose for which this Advisory Committee is to be constituted and appointed
is altogether a purpose in which the assistance of a person nominated by any
organised body concerned in the detail of
carrying out the purpose of the Board
would be of any great assistance.
seems to me that if there is any case in
which there is any one person who happens to be connected with the organised
workers engaged in the production and
distribution of electricity, which is a
pretty wide phrase, if there is any person
of that sort who is for some very special
reason a desirable member of the Advisory
Committee, the proper door for him to
enter that Committee is to be found in
line 30 on page 4. He should be one of
those additional persons, not exceeding
four in number, whom the Minister may
think fit to appoint. I think that is the
way to get the outside expert if his assistance is desired, rather than to introduce
what I cannot but regard as a new class
altogether into the Advisory Committee.
On the whole, I think it would not serve
any very good purpose if this Amendment
were accepted.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: The
mover gave as his reason for this Amendment that it would enable the workers to
convey suggestions to the management
of the electricity scheme. My experience
is that an intellectual tradesman, such as
an engineer, would prefer to go direct to
the management rather than convey his
suggestions through a nominated representative. If a tradesman invents something that is likely to be a success he will
give it to the management. I know of
Electricity Bill—
Report Stage.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Samuel Cunningham]
one successful firm in Scotland who made
a practice of encouraging their men to
come to them with suggestions. That
method has worked well and advantageously to the firm and the employees,
and the firm have received a good many
useful hints from their workpeople. I am
afraid that if the men engaged in the
electricity industry are told that they
must put forward all suggestions of this
kind through a nominated representative,
a workman who might make a useful
discovery or invention might be slow to
pass it on through that source. He would
probably prefer to go to the management
direct. For this reason I do not think
the Amendment would be of any advantage to the Electricity Board. On the
other hand, it might prove to be a disadvantage from the point of view of
developing an efficient electricity scheme.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to back up the Amendment moved by
Senator Dorman. I think it would be a
very commonsense thing to put one representative of the men employed on this
Advisory Committee. I will not quarrel
with Senator Leslie as to what particular
part of the Sub-Section he should come
in under, but I think if the Amendment
is adopted as it is it will do a great deal
of good and will create much more interest on the part of the men employed
under the Board. I shall certainly vote
for the Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
explain for the information of the hon.
Member that the main object of this
Clause is to set up a Council representative of consumers. Membership is therefore mainly drawn from the elected bodies
within whose areas the Board will operate.
No provision is made for the special representation of sectional interests, but
through the local authority representation
the organised workers in the electrical
trades will have their interests safeguarded just as in the case of other members of the community. With the explanation I have given, the hon. Member
will appreciate that the Amendment is not
acceptable.
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dorman, Mr. R.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
The Mayor of Londonderry.
Armstrong, Mr. H. B.
Barclay, Mr. W.
Charlemont, Viscount
Mr. DORMAN: The purpose of the
Amendment would be fulfilled if the
Leader of the House would consent to the
suggestion of Senator Leslie. I do not
desire that this nominee should have
power to interfere in the management of
the concern, but merely that through his
representation the workers engaged in the
undertaking should be brought into direct
contact with the Board. If the noble
Lord would agree that the four additional
members of the Board to be nominated
by the Minister of Commerce should include a representative of the organised
workers engaged in the production and
distribution of electricity under the Board
I should be satisfied. I know the engineers are anxious that some representation
of this kind should be given in the interests of the undertaking as a whole. As
the Bill stands at present there is no
skilled engineer represented
on the
Advisory Committee, and it seems to me
there would be no difficulty whatever in
the Government asking the organised
workers to nominate a representative,
who would act as an intermediary between
the workers and the Board. I do not
suggest that he should have any right to
interfere in the terms and conditions of
labour.
Mr. LESLIE: I must not endeavour to
forecast what the noble Viscount may be
going to say, but I think I ought to
explain that such suggestion as I made
was merely intended to draw attention to
a possibility. It was not intended that
it should be taken as an indication of the
feeling of the Committee. I did not
argue it on those lines at all.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
give any undertaking on the matter.
can only promise that the Minister of
Commerce will consider the suggestion to
include a representative of the organised
workers in the four additional members
to be nominated by him.
Question put, "That those words be
there inserted."

CLAUSE 28 (Power of the Board to
issue stock).
*
*
*
(3) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, any stock created by the Board
under the powers of this Act shall be
issued, transferred, dealt with and
redeemed according to regulations made
by the Ministry of Commerce with the
approval of the Ministry of Finance,
and any such regulations may apply
for the purpose of this section, with or
without modifications, any provisions
of the Government Loans and Exchequer Provisions Act (Northern Ireland),
1925, the Public Health Acts Amendment Act, 1890, and the enactments
amending those Acts, and of any Act
relating to stock issued by a local
authority.
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 28, sub-section (3), page 22, line
14, to leave out from 'Acts" to the end
of line 15, and insert "or of any Act
relating to the issue of stock by local
authorities." The House may remember
that when we had this Clause in Committee we had some little difficulty in incorporating the words as they stood in
the original text. I myself put up in
Committee the suggestion that the power
proposed to be conferred on the two departments was perhaps rather too wide,
and that it would be open to incorporate
the special provisions of some individual
local Bill. That, I was informed, was
not the intention, but a little conference
behind your Chair, Mr. Speaker, revealed
that it would make things easier for the
ordinary man to understand if we added
the words which are now on the Paper,
having first got rid of certain words.
If
we insert these words it will make it perfectly clear that what we are aiming at
is to permit the incorporation of any
general Act relating generally to the issue
of stock by local authorities generally.
Accordingly I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
FIRST SCHEDULE. (PART 11).
The expressions "local authority"
and "district" mean respectively a
local authority and a district as defined
by section thirty-one of the Electric
Lighting Act, 1882.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the
First Schedule, page 49, to leave out lines
38 to 40 inclusive. It may be remembered that when we were discussing
the meaning of the phrase "local authority" we were a little at a loss as to
whether the insertion of the particular
definition in this somewhat remote paragraph would not run counter to or cause
difficulty when thinking of the more
general definition given in Section 31 of
the Act of 1882. Accordingly it was
suggested, in very much the same way as
in the case of the previous Amendment,
that it would be better and would make
the Bill simpler if we left out the particular words on page 49. The effect of
that will be to leave the expression "local
authority" throughout the Bill to be defined in the ordinary manner under the
Act of 1882, with which, as mentioned
the other day, this Bill will be
incorporated.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass.'
-(Viscount Charlemont).

Anderson's Divorce Bill.

Macartney's Divorce Bill.

Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving,
"That this House do now adjourn until
Wednesday," perhaps it would be well if
I gave the House a forecast of the business. On Wednesday of this week we
shall have the Appropriation Bill.
wish the Senate to consider the Planning
and Housing Bill in Committee, and the
I
Adjournment
Business of the House
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
question is what would be the earliest
time hon. Members would like to take it.
There will be the Royal Assent to Bills
on Tuesday next week, and I should like
to know if it would be convenient to meet
in the afternoon and go on with the Committee stage of the Bill. Members might
prefer that it should be postponed until
a later date, although personally I think
that if we took it on Tuesday next week
sufficient time would have elapsed since
the Second Reading.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I have put
some Amendments on the Paper, and as
far as I am concerned Tuesday next week
will suit me admirably.
Mr. LESLIE: Am I to take it that the
question of what is to happen to the Planning and Housing Bill has not yet been
settled?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Senate
will go into Committee on the Bill. We
are not affected by the Adjournment of
another place. Whether or not it will
be advisable to take all the subsequent
stages of the Bill and finish with it during this part of the Session, or content
ourselves with taking the Committee stage
only before the recess, is a matter for the
House to decide later.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Four o'clock until Wednesday next.
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers
20 MAY 1931
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 20th May, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Business of the House.
Moved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking all stages of the
Appropriation Bill and the remaining
stages of Divorce and Local Bills."-
(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Anderson's Divorce Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time.”
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill, as reported from the Joint Committee, without Amendment.
Macartney's Divorce Bill.
The Amendment made by the Joint
Committee to the said Bill was agreed to
without Amendment.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the Amendment
made by the Joint Committee without
Amendment.
Questions to Minister.
JURISDICTION OF MAGISTRATES.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that, by reason of the denial to honorary
magistrates of jurisdiction to hear charges
of summary offences created by the
successive Acts of this Parliament, many
magistrates have ceased attending petty
sessions; whether inconvenience and loss
to the public and delay in the working of
these courts have resulted; and whether
the Government propose any means for
the restoration of the speedy course of
summary justice.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I have no reason to believe
that the suggestion contained in the first
part of the hon. and learned Senator's
question is borne out by the facts. The
adjournment of a court of petty sessions
by reason of the non-attendance
of
justices of the peace is, I am glad to say,
a very rare occurrence, and, consequently,
I cannot agree that there are any grounds
for supposing that litigants are inconvenienced or that delay exists in
disposing of cases coming before the
justices. The Government is satisfied
that the work of the summary courts is
progressing normally, and, accordingly,
there would appear to be no reason to take
such action as is proposed in the last part
of the question.
the
I am aware, from reading the newspapers, that certain magistrates have
from time to time expressed their disagreement with the provisions of certain
Acts of Parliament under which
jurisdiction is confined to resident
magistrates, but I am satisfied that the
point of view of some magistrates will not
deter the majority from carrying out
their duties as justices of the peace.
Should the fears of the hon. and learned
Senator be realised at a later date, I can
assure him that the Government will give
the whole question its careful consideration and take such action as is necessary
to ensure that the administration of
justice is not delayed.
I would like to add that the Government have no statistics showing the average attendance of magistrates at petty
sessions, but if it would be of interest
to the hon. and learned Senator I would
be very pleased to give him the average
attendance of magistrates in the county
over which I have control as far as magistrates are concerned.
Mr. CAMPBELL:
much obliged.
I would be very
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
do it until the returns come in, which will
not be for some two months yet.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I say that the
professional men attending these courts
are complaining in regard to delays and
adjournments?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Is
the Leader of the House aware that considerable inconvenience is caused in
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
working-class districts owing to the
considerable reduction in the number of
justices of the peace? A good number of
these magistrates resided in working-class
districts, and were able to sign such papers
as were required by the people. During
the past few years there has been such a
reduction owing to death and other
causes that it is causing great inconvenience to the poor people who have to
travel long distances in search of someone
who can sign these papers.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
know of any specific cases of the kind the
hon. Senator has referred to, as this is
more the duty of his Majesty's Lieutenants than of the Government. I can
only speak about my own county.
should think that in a case like that where
there is a shortage of magistrates, the
best thing would be to approach either
the Minister of Home Affairs or the Lieutenant for the county. That is the procedure that would be adopted so far as
County Tyrone is concerned.
Mr. BARCLAY: I think Senator
Joseph Cunningham was referring to Belfast. I know from experience that Belfast is short of magistrates in consequence
of so much unemployment and sickness.
When people make application for benefit
they have to have their papers signed by
a magistrate, and undoubtedly they have
a great deal of running about sometimes
before they can find a magistrate. The
magistrates who are available tell me they
are over-run, and have to neglect their
own business sometimes in order to sign
the papers of unemployed persons making
application for benefit.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Is it not a fact
that when a bookie's case comes up at the
Belfast courts about 20 or 25 magistrates
turn up to adjudicate?
Mr. McALLISTER: Is it not a fact
that if magistrates were appointed simply
for the purpose of signing unemployment
benefit forms, and then denied responsibility for adjudicating upon the Bench,
a very unsatisfactory set of persons will
be appointed to such a position?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
quite agree with what the last hon.
Senator has said. When the petty
sessions courts were first instituted-I do
not like to make statements without being
certain of my dates, but I will say a considerable number of years ago—they had
to try the same kind of cases they are
allowed to try nowadays. The reason for
the reduction in the number of cases in
which they can adjudicate is really due to
the more complicated legislation of
modern times. In the old days they only
had to try simple cases, and they try those
cases still. I do not think the fact that
their jurisdiction has been taken away in
certain cases will have any reference to
the class of magistrates selected or the
willingness of a great number of people
to be made magistrates. Since the reduction in the kind of legislation they can
adjudicate on has come into practice, I
cannot say, speaking as Vice-Lieutenant,
that there has been any reduction in the
number of applicants for the honour of
being justices of the peace. On the contrary,
numbers have remained
absolutely stationary during the five or
six years I have held that office. As regards the alleged shortage of magistrates
in Belfast, I can only promise to bring the
matter to the consideration of my right
hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs.
the
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask the noble
Lord whether it would not be more logical
and sensible, instead of nibbling away
their jurisdiction, to introduce a Bill
abolishing the jurisdiction of magistrates
root and branch?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That is a
matter of opinion.
JOINT STOCK COMPANY LAW.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House whether it is the intention
of the Government to introduce before
the Recess the Bill relating to Joint
Stock Company (Consolidation) referred
to in the Gracious Speech.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The preparation of a Measure for the consolidation of the statutes relating to Joint Stock
Companies is now in hand, but is not
sufficiently far advanced to enable the Bill
to be tabled before the Recess. It is,
however, intended to have the Consolidation Bill introduced in the Autumn
Session.

Appropriation Bill.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second time."
As is customary in the case of a Money
Bill, I shall not bore the House with
details.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I quite appreciate
the fact that this is a Money Bill. Still
it falls under the purview of the House.
The Bill provides for grants out of the
Consolidated Fund of Northern Ireland
to the extent of about 8 millions for the
service of the present year. This is only
a sum of £300,000 short of the figure of
the year before. The reduction seems
remarkably slight and unimportant. The
Bill itself shows that the stream of public
money flows as regards expenditure in
certain broad defined channels. For instance, the Royal Ulster Constabulary,
according to the Bill before the House,
absorbs about three-quarters of a million
for the current year. There are, I believe, some 2,800 effective members of the
force one for every 475 of the population. One is tempted to ask is such a
large number-a brigade or four battalions
-required for such a comparatively peaceful population as the population in this
Province.
The grants to local authorities and in
relief of local rates take over 1 million
pounds. Contributions towards unemployment relief account for 1 million
pounds. Old-age pensions, widows' and
orphans' pensions and blind persons' pensions demand 1 million pounds. The
administration of these pensions is also
becoming an increasingly heavy burden on
the Province. We have now some 1,850
civil servants; we had in 1923-24, 1,230
-an increase of about 600 in a period of
a little over six years. The expenditure
onl education is almost two million
pounds in the year. One remark I would
make is that I trust that the amounts
appropriated for the building and reconstruction of voluntary schools under the
Education Act, 1930, will be reasonable
and adequate for the purpose, and that
the working of the Act in this respect
will be neither dilatory nor tantalising.
There is an impression abroad that the
concessions in the Act may be whittled
down to zero, and I fervently hope the
impression will not prove well founded.
The expenses of the Ministry of Agriculture are over £200,000. Is the Province
getting any return of real permanent
utility for this vast outlay? Among the
sums voted for the expenses of the
Ministry of Finance are sums for the administration of the Loans Guarantee Acts.
Under the first Act-that of 1922-the
limit of the capital amount of these loans
that the Government was to guarantee
was fixed at £2,000,000. One Act after
Appropriation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
a
[Mr. Campbell]
another raised the limit until the Act
of 1929 raised the limit to £14,000,000.
That was staggering sum for
a
small area, a staggering increase in
seven years.
The amount guaranteed by
the Government to one gigantic combination-the Kylsant group of shipbuilding
or shipowning concerns-was about 71
millions. Of this sum over 5 millions is
now outstanding. For how much of this
total will the Ulster taxpayers have to foot
the bill? Of this amount sums totalling
close on three million pounds fell due in
the year ended September, 1930, and were
not paid. True, the interest was duly
met. Was the security for the capital
ample? Have the Government exercised
the acumen of ordinary prudent business
men in these transactions? Have they
been circumspect and cautious, as they
ought to have been, in the exercise of their
powers as guarantors? Why did the
Government put practically all their eggs
in one basket?
These are questions that are agitating
the minds of Ulster taxpayers. Shipbuilding has for a long span of yearssince the close of the war-been suffering
from very deep, unbroken depression.
Much of this money may be eventually
levied on many sick industries for the
benefit of one very sick industry. The
economies, which have been so much
vaunted of late, look shrunk and petty
compared with the sum at stake in this
matter. The Government ought to review its position with a very great degree
of care.
Mr. LESLIE: I rather welcome the
intervention of the hon. and learned
Senator on a question of this sort, because
I think it is rather undesirable that we
should let the Appropriation Bill go
through the House without any form of
discussion whatever. As we know, we
are expressly prohibited from amending
this Bill and Bills of this sort. Of
course, we also know we have a sufficient
reserved power to make an Amendment
in the Bill if it so happened that we
really wanted to do it. The method-the
rather roundabout method-by which
that could be put into operation need not,
I think, be alluded to any further. What
the hon. and learned Senator complains
of is the scantiness of the reduction in
expenditure for the coming year
opposed to the last year. I think that is
the general burden of his complaint.
as

economy.
dealt at some little length with the

It
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is rather
difficult to reply to questions on this particular Measure without facts and figures

is difficult to say anything that will be

the education service is one of the greatest

With regard to agriculture, I think

produce have proved that the Ministry of

another place to the fact that the number

knowledge on the subject, but I think that

We hope in future years that that increase

With regard to that Loans Guarantee

am sorry that the right hon. Gentleman
the Minister of Commerce (Mr. Barbour)

it was
been able to give a great deal more information on the subject than I am able to
give. The Government has been criticised
in that they did not know five years ago
or more the state of affairs into which
this big shipping company would get, but
Governments, unfortunately, are not composed of supernormal people. They are
only ordinary people trying to do their
work as well as they can. The gift of
prophecy is denied to them as
denied to shareholders of that company
and many other people concerned in the
administration. No one foresaw in those
years that shipping companies would
suffer the decline in value that they have.
I think that the Government acted in
accordance with the advice they received.
I can only say that if the full extent of
our possible losses were incurred to-morrow
I think it could be proved that had we
not given the guarantee referred to in
this particular case we should have expended far more in unemployment benefit.

Questions to Minister.
THE DEATH PENALTY.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Governhave had under consideration the Report
of the Select Committee of the Imperial
House of Commons recommending that a
Bill be passed during the present Session
providing for the abolition of the death
penalty for an experimental period of five
years in cases tried by civil courts in peace
time; and, if so, whether the Government
consider taking any action in Northern
Ireland.
I wish to make a short statement with
regard to the matter of the question before the House. It concerns capital
punishment in peace time inflicted by the
civil courts, and an incident of the past
week brought that subject in a very acute
manner before the public mind. A man
in England was taken from the condemned cell in Liverpool to the Court of
Criminal Appeal in London. He there
had the verdict of the jury and the
sentence which the judge passed upon him.
set aside. Had there been no appeal then
the man's life would in all probability
have been forfeit to the law. One cause of
uneasiness on a subject of this kind is that
what the law does in a charge of murder,
taking the life of a man, is beyond recall.
The Committee of the Imperial House of
Commons which investigated this matter
was not unanimous in its findings. The
six or seven Conservative Members of the
Committee withdrew from the last deliberations of the Committee, and the report, therefore, is in some way minimised
both in authority and weight. It represents the conclusions of the Liberal and
Labour Members of the Committee only.
Of course it would be quite impossible to
expect unanimity of feeling in a matter of
that kind, when one considers that almost
every change in the criminal code for
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
26 MAY 1931
many years past has been a subject of acute
controversy. The Committee had many
sittings, and it heard no fewer than 44
witnesses quite a number from foreign
countries and the conclusion was arrived
at and formulated in the report, after
prolonged examination of the situation
in foreign countries had increasingly confirmed it in the assurance that capital
punishment might be abolished in Great
Britain without endangering life
property.
""
or
The Committee recommended as an
experiment the abolition of the penalty
for five years, after which stock could be
taken as to whether or not the experiment
worked for good or evil. It may be asked,
and the question is a perfectly fair one,
whether the experiment would be a rash
and hazardous one. Well, the Report
says, "no," and gives a reason.
It says
"the death bell is no longer tolled, the
black flag is no longer raised, and if we
take away the scaffold as well it is because we feel it is not needed in this day
of social rebuilding.' An argument
against any change in the law in this
direction is that this terrible punishment
deters offenders. The reply is that
though the punishment is a terrible one
to the condemned man, whose hours and
minutes are numbered, and are being
ticked off one by one by the clock, it is
of no avail at all at the moment of the
commission of the crime.
In many European and American
countries the punishment of death is no
longer imposed. The law there ceases to
exact the old toll of an eye for an eye
and a tooth for a tooth. I learn public
opinion is satisfied in those countries that
human life is not made less secure through
human law foregoing to take the lives of
a few wretched beings every year.
It is
also felt that convictions in proper cases
are more certain than they would be if
the penalty was death. I put the question in order to elicit the opinion of the
Government on a point which is agitating
the minds of many people and exercising a
great deal of attention across the water
consequent on this report from the Imperial Committee.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is, of
course, a very debatable subject, and
without any great personal knowledge on
the matter I think it would be best if I
merely replied to the question on the.
Paper to the effect that the Department
concerned has received a copy of the Report of the Select Committee referred to
by the hon. and learned Senator, the main
recommendations of which are set out in
the first part of the question. I would
remind the hon. Senator that the Report
does not represent by any means the views
of the Committee as a whole. While the
Government are prepared to consider the
terms of the Report, they can give no
undertaking that they are prepared to
adopt the recommendations contained
therein.
FIRE ESCAPES IN HOTELS.
Colonel PAKENHAM: In asking the
question that stands in my name, I may
say that the reference in the question is
to no particular hotel, but to hotels all over
County Antrim, and I am prompted to ask
the question by the fact that a great
number of people have lately expressed
the opinion that there seems to be a great
deal of risk in some cases in these hotels
not being furnished with proper means of
escape. It was exercising the minds of
the public some two years ago when the
Association of District Councils brought
to the notice of the Ministry of Home
Affairs that these methods of egress from
hotels are not provided in a great many
places. The reply to that was made in
May of that year, and was that the
question would be taken into consideration when local government affairs were
being dealt with. There was nothing
done until 1930 when Antrim Rural District Council wrote again to the Ministry
drawing attention to this previous communication. To that no answer was
vouchsafed.
Now, it is a fact that hotels in Antrim
do cater for a large tourist traffic which
comes in during the summer. In particular in the town of Antrim several
hotels cater for this, and there are one
or two hotels that in the estimation of the
people of Antrim are not furnished with
proper escapes. One hotel has bedroom
accommodation for 200 people and only
one means of egress through a door 2 feet
6 inches wide. It does seem dangerous, and for that reason I put the
question to ascertain the views of the
Government. I beg to ask the Leader of
the House whether the Government will
consider taking action in the near future
to ensure that hotels in Antrim will be
provided with adequate exits and fire
escapes.
419 Planning and Housing Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The matter
has been carefully noted and will be dealt
with when a suitable opportunity occurs
But I would remind this House that dealing with it in such a way would require
legislation.
Mr. McALLISTER: I take it the intention of the Government would be in
dealing with this matter to have one
universal scheme for all the Six Counties,
and not to direct attention to any particular area. I think it would be unfair
and unjust to differentiate between any
particular parts of these Six Counties, or
that special legislation should take place
with regard to any particular towns and
not be applied similarly to every town in
the area.
Mr. LESLIE: Might I ask the noble
Viscount whether it is not a fact that
powers to deal with matters of this kind
are not uncommonly taken by urban
councils and, perhaps, by corporations
also? I may remind the House that the
town of Antrim has not got an urban
council. As regards urban councils generally, is it not a fact that powers to deal
with the provision of methods of escape
from fire from hotels, and so on, are very
commonly taken by means of private Bills,
really local Bills?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am afraid
without expert advice on the subject I
cannot give an actual categorical assurance
to the hon. and learned Senator who has
just spoken, but I would say that in
common sense it would certainly be unjust
on the part of the Government to single
out Antrim as the one place where there
must be protection against risks of fire.
Planning and Housing Bill.
