CLAUSE 1 (Planning schemes).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: On Clause 1, I would
like to take the opportunity of asking the
noble Viscount what is the ultimate
sanction for requiring compliance with a
planning scheme or, perhaps putting it in
another way, what is the penalty for noncompliance with a planning scheme, or for
doing something directly in opposition to
it. I have not had time to study this
important Bil! as closely as I could wish,
and on this particular point all I have
been able to find as regards a planning
scheme is a rather obscure reference in
Sub-Clause (3) of Clause 7. If the Committee will turn to that they will see that
if any question arises as to whether or not
any building or work contravenes
planning scheme, the matter can be determined by an arbitrator appointed by the
Ministry under article 29 of the schedule
to the Local Government (Application of
Enactments) Order. Well, that article.
29, so far as I can make out, appears to
apply the regulations of the Railways Act
of 1868 which, in turn, incorporates the
Railway Arbitration Act.
a
By this somewhat circuitous route one
arrives at the power of the courts to enforce what is intended to be done.
Whether there is anything more than that
in the Bill as regards town planning
schemes, I would like to be informed. If
I may pass for the moment to a somewhat cognate question, which is a question
that does not arise on the Clause before
us, the Committee will see that if they
turn to Clause 23, Sub-Clause (6), there
is direct power to proceed in a summary
way under certain conditions. But that,
if I may say so, is more or less of a side
show. The main point is what I should
like to be assured of.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The question is how are people to be prevented
from disregarding or contravening the
provisions of a scheme?
In this connection reference might be
made to Clause 7, which enables the local
authority to remove, pull down, or alter
any building or other work in the area
included in the scheme which is such as
to contravene the scheme, or in the erection or carrying out of which any provision of the scheme has not been complied
with, or, on the other hand, to execute
any work which it is the duty of any
person to execute under the scheme and
any expense incurred in doing these
things may be recovered from the person
421 Planning and Housing Bill-

26 MAY 1931
Committee
in default. If, for instance, a scheme
said that in a certain area there should
not be more than 20 houses to the acre
and an owner builds 30, it would be open
to the local authority to remove the excess
number at the owner's expense. This
provision seems to place a sufficient deterrent on any one contravening the
provisions of a scheme.
Mr. LESLIE: It is rather a case of
locking the door when the horse has been
stolen. I wonder if, at a later stage, the
Government might not propose to give us
something a little more direct.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I shall be
pleased to consider it at a later stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
5, sub-clause (3), paragraph (b) to leave
out from the word "authority" in line 34
Ito the end of line 35. I am afraid we must
admit, if we look at the text, that this particular reference is not introduced with our
accustomed neatness. Having said that,
I think the Committee will readily and
rightly infer that Northern Ireland is not
responsible for the form of words that is
here adopted. The whole passage is taken
direct from paragraph (b) of sub-clause
(2) of Section 5 of the English Act of
1925. I think it cannot be denied that
as the words stand the expression "responsible authority" must relate to the
local authority in the line before it. But
to arrive at that conclusion would
necessitate reliance not on a comma, but
on the absence of a comṛṇa. The intention is plain, namely, that for the responsible authority we have to refer back
to the authority to be responsible in line
30. The question for the Committee to
consider is whether it is necessary for us
to take any steps to make things a little
plainer, or whether, with the English precedent before us, we can leave the
expression as it is, while not altogether
approving of it, on the ground that no
possible difficulty can result. If it is
thought that difficulty might result, the
Amendment on the Paper might be
thought to meet the case. It may be we
shall be told that the opinion is that,
taking the Bill as a whole, the position is
sufficiently clear, but I hope it will always
be remembered that we ourselves are not
responsible for this form of words.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This and
the next Amendment, in the name of
Senator Leslie, relate to a matter of drafting, and the suggestions put forward by
the hon. and learned Senator have been
carefully considered by the draftsman.
I am advised that possibly there is some
ambiguity and that the matter might perhaps be more elegantly expressed,
although it is a matter of some difficulty
to find the precise form of elegance. If
the hon. and learned Senator will withdraw his Amendments the draftsman will
re-examine the Clause with a view, if
possible, of finding some formula which
will meet the difficulty to which he has
drawn attention.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that will be
much the best way to deal with the
matter, and I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment. I do not move the next
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

an

authority defined under the provisions of this section."
Amendment not moved.

:-

CLAUSE 10 (Exclusion or limitation
of compensation in certain cases).
Question proposed: "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: I made some remarks on
this Clause on the Second Reading as regards the protection that might be given.
under certain circumstances. Since then
I have further examined the Bill and it
seems to me that the protection given in
an earlier Clause, a Clause we have
passed, is likely to be sufficient for the
purpose. Therefore there is no necessity
to make any change as regards that. The
point was as to what was to happen if
land is allotted for a certain number of
houses only, and it became apparent in
the course of time that nobody was coming forward to build the appropriate
number of houses. I accordingly suggested that the question ought to be
reconsidered, but it now seems to me that
there is sufficient power by means of revoking or modifying the provisions of the
scheme. Therefore I have not put down
any Amendment to this Clause.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

modify provisions of scheme), and 12
(Registration of ownership of land and

CLAUSE 13 (Power to require removal
of structures).
(1) Where any unsightly and dilapidated structure, not being an occupied
dwelling-house, exists on
any land
whether or not included in a planning
scheme), the local authority may serve
a notice, in the prescribed manner,
upon the owner of the structure, and
upon such other persons as may be prescribed, requiring that the structure
shall be either removed or rendered
sightly within the time specified in the
notice, which shall not be less than two
months.
*
*
*
*
*
Colonel PAKENHAM: I beg to move,
in Clause 13, page 13, sub-clause (1) to
leave out "may" in line 43, and insert
instead the word "shall." On the Secord
Reading of this Measure I tried very hard
to interest the Government in this question of the removal of these obnoxious old
buildings. The sub-clause reads:-
Where any unsightly and dilapidated
structure, not being an occupied
dwelling-house, exists on any land
(whether or not included in a planning
scheme), the local authority may serve
a notice
The object of my Amendment is to substitute the word "shall" for "may."
The word "shall" occurs frequently
throughout this Bill. It is in Clauses 23
and 43 and other Clauses, and therefore
it is not a forbidden word.
I should very
much like to see it adopted in this Clause,
because in putting down this Amendment
I am really following the lead given by
our Prime Minister two or three years
ago. Speaking at a meeting of the Ulster
Tourist Development Association, Lord
Craigavon pleaded for the destruction of
all these unsightly buildings on our roads,
but so far as my own part of the country
is concerned I have not noticed that a
single unsightly building has been removed. These old ruins are of no use to
anyone, whereas if they were pulled down
the stones might be useful for various
purposes. As it is they are very unsightly
and the cost of their removal would not
be very much. I do earnestly plead that
we should back up the plea made by our
Prime Minister and get rid of these unsightly buildings by making their removal
compulsory.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have the
utmost sympathy with all that the hon.
and gallant Member has said, but I regret
that I cannot accept his Amendment.
The Government, after very careful consideration, came to the conclusion that
this Bill should be in the nature of an
enabling Measure and that there should
be as little compulsion in it as possible.
The Clause which it is proposed to
amend goes considerably further in the
matters with which it deals than corresponding legislation in operation in Great
Britain, where nothing can be done except under a planning scheme. The
Clause, as hon. Members will observe,
makes a definite advance on the existing
law here in Northern Ireland, as hitherto
structures of the kind to which the Clause
425 Planning and Housing Bill-

26 MAY 1931
Committee
to
relates could only be dealt with if they
constituted a nuisance from the public
health point of view. For these reasons
and also because there may often be a
legitimate difference of opinion as
whether any particular structure is or is
not unsightly, the Government considers
that local authorities should have a discretion in the exercise of the powers
conferred by this Clause.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

(4) Any expenses incurred by a local
authority under the last preceding subsection, after giving credit for any
amount realised by the sale of materials,
may be recovered by them as a simple.
contract debt in the county court within

determine to be just and equitable.

I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, in Sub-Clause (4), line 27, to leave

CLAUSE 14 (Duty of local bodies to
have regard to amenities of locality, etc.),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Colonel
Pakenham:-
After Clause 14, insert the following
new Clause:-
Where it appears to the local authority
that any buildings in their district are
of such a character as to interfere with
the scenic amenities, or the beauty of
the neighbourhood, it shall be lawful
for such local authority to require such
structure to be removed, on payment of
compensation to the owner of such
structure.
Colonel PAKENHAM: This Amendment deals particularly with the Antrim
coast road. Without taking any narrow
view I suppose this road is really one of
the greatest assets Northern Ireland possesses. I am not belittling the beauty
spots of other counties in the Province
when I make the claim for the Antrim
coast road that it is one of the finest scenic
roads that can be found anywhere, and
I do contend that it is the duty of the
Government to preserve the amenities of
that road, which is the property of the
Province as
a whole. I do not think
there is any power given in this Bill to
compel those responsible to keep this road
in the way it should be kept. Members
of this House who have lately driven along
the road must have seen that on either
side, and especially on the sea side, some
very unsightly structures have been put
The bungalows of the class that
have been erected are really nothing more
than tin-pot bungalows, and they have no
business to be there at all. They are
probably extremely unsanitary, and they
are certainly very ugly. As we are the
custodians of the beauty spots of the
country we ought to look after them and
preserve their amenities.
up.
I have put down this Amendment in
order to invite the Committee to express
its opinion that these bungalows ought
to be removed so that this road may revert
to its natural state, and that its beauty may
be preserved. As to the compensation to
be paid to the owners of bungalows I
would point out that an owner has his
responsibilities as well as his rights, and
I think a man who had erected an ugly
bungalow of this type ought to lose money
instead of make it, and, therefore, I think
he ought to get as little compensation as
possible. I beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I support the
Amendment on general grounds, but, at
the same time, I think it ought to provide the method by which compensation
is to be arrived at. Another matter that
should be considered is this. The people
who have built these bungalows are comparatively poor, and I understand the
ground on which the bungalows are
erected has been taken on lease. Legislation would, therefore, require to be introduced to relieve them of their obligation
to pay rent for ninety years, or as long
as their lease lasts. I support the
principle of the Amendment, and I
endorse the grounds on which it has been
introduced.
427 Planning and Housing Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
If
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
this Amendment is passed I am afraid it
will affect very unfairly the poor people
who have erected bungalows along the
seashore. One must recognise that there
are a great many poor people who are
confined to cities in the winter time, and
who gladly avail themselves of the opportunity to erect a small bungalow at the
seaside where they can sleep at night in
the summer time and return to the city
each day. I agree that it would be desirable that in future these small bungalows
or huts should not be erected in such places
as the Antrim coast road, but to order
those that at present exist to be razed to
the ground would be very unfair to the
owners, and would make it appear that
this House had no sympathy with the
poor people who have built them to obtain
the benefit of the seaside air and scenery.
Mr. LESLIE: I rarely before have desired to make an anti-landlord speech, but
I cannot refrain from pointing out that
it seems to me that the direct responsibility for the evils to which the hon. and
gallant Senator (Colonel Pakenham) has
referred, and which do undoubtedly exist,
rests rather on the landlord, that is, the
original owner of the land, than on the
poor person who has built this little shack
to serve as a retreat for the week-ends.
I think that if anything could be done
under this Bill to make it a little more
difficult to let land for such a purpose it
would be a good thing. It may be that
the general tenor of the Bill will result in
producing that desirable result, but still
we all know a certain number of these
excrescences which it would be difficult to
get rid of, and if we could get rid of them
I am afraid I should not resent very
greatly putting part of the cost, at all
events, on the man who let the land for
that purpose either deliberately or carelessly. I hesitate to say that I support
the Amendment. I think I must say
that I support the principle of the
Amendment, reserving judgment
other matters. I should like this matter
dealt with before Parliament finally parts
with this Bilī.
on
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
draw the attention of the hon. and
gallant Senator (Colonel Pakenham) to
Clause 13 of the Bill, which would appear
to cover a very large proportion of the
buildings to which his Amendment refers.
As regards buildings which are not
dilapidated, I may say that the Government, after very careful consideration of
the whole matter, is not satisfied that the
time is yet ripe for empowering local
authorities generally, except in pursuance
of a planning scheme, to demolish buildings which are serving some useful
purpose, such as a dwelling-house, on the
sole ground that the buildings are in the
opinion of the local authority unsightly.
There is little doubt that any arbitrary
action on the part of a local authority in
such a case would create such opposition
to the exercise of planning powers generally that the objects of the Bill might be
defeated.
Attention, however, is also drawn to
Clause 7 of the Bill, which provides that
a local authority in enforcing a planning
scheme may demolish buildings, the removal of which is necessary for the proper
carrying out of the scheme. This Clause
would cover many other cases to which
the Amendment is designed to apply.
I regret that this Amendment cannct
be accepted.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 15.-(Advisory committees
for local authorities).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: Clause 15, like every
other Clause, seeks to empower the local
authorities to do something. In this case
the Clause seeks to empower the local
authority to appoint an advisory Committee, but, I am afraid, it is silent and
necessarily silent as to providing any
means whereby there may be produced a
supply of men competent to serve a local
authority in the capacity of members of
this Advisory Committee. The reason I
draw attention to the matter is this, that
I want to ask whether there is any idea
that the Ministry would undertake to
direct the minds of local authorities,
either officially or semi-officially, towards
the necessity for drawing to their counsels
people who perhaps do not reside within
the sphere of the local authority in
question, but people who are members of
that somewhat small class of persons who
have some knowledge of these matters and
whose assistance would be of great value,
but who may very easily escape the
attention of the local authority owing to
the fact that they do not live within their
sphere of influence, and whose intrusion
might, perhaps, at first be rather resented.
429 Planning and Housing Bill-

26 MAY 1931
Committee
It might be that a little kindly and helpful suggestion could be made on this
point, and I should like to know whether
the Ministry would do anything of the
sort.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Would the
hon. and learned Senator bring this
question forward at a later stage of the
Bill? I cannot give an answer on the
matter at the present moment, but I
would remind him of the old proverb that
the hour brings forth the man, or the
men.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 16 (Provisions as to local
authorities and their expenses) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move as
a New Clause :-
The council of every borough and
urban district containing a population
according to the last census of more
than five thousand shall before the first
day of January, nineteen hundred and
thirty-four, nor such later date before
the thirty-first day of December,
nineteen hundred and thirty-eight, as
the Ministry may in any case allow,
prepare and submit to the Ministry a
Scheme in respect of all land within
their district in respect of which a
Scheme may be made under this Act.
The intention of this proposed New
Clause is to bring the Bill into line with
the corresponding Measure now before the
Imperial Parliament. I do not know at
the moment of any sound logical reason
or any principle why the Bill should depart in this very vital matter from this
provision in the English Measure.
The
Bill as it stands is a purely permissive
Bill. It leaves the working of the
Measure altogether in the hands of the
local authorities; it exercises no control
or coercion and imposes no obligation
upon the local authorities; it provides no
spur or stimulus; it leaves it a matter of
discretion to the local authorities to make
the Measure a dead-letter or to make it
a real live Act of Parliament.
The object of the Bill is one that commends itself to us all; it is to make the
very best possible use of land in Northern
Ireland, having regard to the legitimate
rights of the owners of land and the
amenities of the situation. In this matter
we have departed from the English corresponding legislation. We have departed
in other important respects which were
dealt with during the Second Reading of
the Bill. We have given no encouragement to the clearance of slums which is
a vital matter in the great city of Belfast.
We have given no encouragement whatever to the Corporation of Belfast, or to
any other local authority to clear slums,
such as they have given in England by a
handsome liberal allowance for a period
of many years in respect of every person
who is dispossessed by a clearance.
Unless we adopt a Clause of this kind,
making it mandatory on the local
authorities to take some steps to bring
this Bill into operation, the Bill will become a dead-letter. How can the local
authorities be expected to work this Bill?
How can they be expected to house people
in decent homes if that is to be done altogether at the expense of the local ratepayers, if the burden of making
improvements of this kind falls on local
shoulders? The local shoulders are not
able to bear the burden, and the burden
will fall to the ground. The local
authorities will not take the matter up in
these circumstances, and I respectfully
ask the Committee to give this Bill a
chance of becoming a real Act of Parliament by adopting this Amendment.
This Amendment makes it mandatory
on the local authority to prepare a scheme
under the Bill. The period is a very extended one; it is either before the 1st
January, 1934, which is 2 years hence,
or not later than the 31st December, 1938,
which is 7 years hence. That, I submit
to the Committee, is a long period which
the local authorities will have in which
to prepare a scheme under this Bill. In
the very next Clause-Clause 17-we find
that
Where the Ministry has refused to
approve a planning scheme prepared or
adopted by a local authority, except
with certain modifications or subject to
certain conditions, and the Ministry on
any representation is satisfied that the
local authority have unreasonably refused to consent to the modifications or
conditions so imposed by the Ministry.
the Ministry may order the local
authority to consent to the modifications
or conditions so imposed.
That applies to a case where the local
authority have started a scheme and are
delaying unreasonably in carrying it into
execution; if they are doing things in a
half-hearted way the Ministry may step in.
I submit that this is a rather unsatisfac-
431 Planning and Housing Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Campbell]
tory state in which to leave this Bill, and
that if we wish to make the Measure a
thorough and effective one we should incorporate this New Clause.
The population limit I have taken is different from
that named in the English Measure.
Question proposed: "That those words.
be there inserted.'
Mr. LESLIE: The hon. and learned
Senator at the conclusion of his remarks
drew attention to a matter which I was
about to bring before the Committee. He
reminded the Committee of the difference
between the population suggested by his
Amendment and the population dealt
with in the English Act. I presume I
am right in thinking he was referring to
Section 3 of the Act of 1925, which takes
a population of 20,000. One cannot refrain from pointing out the very material
difference between a population of an urban
district of 20,000 in Great Britain, and
a population of an urban district of 5,000
in Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
point out that slum clearances are referred to in Part II of the Bill, and that
this proposed new clause has no connection with that subject. I should also like
to point out that one of the fundamental
principles of this Bill is that it is an enabling Bill.
The Bill, I may point out, has been
introduced at the request of certain local
authorities, and the various local authorities consulted in connection with its drafting have expressed general approval of
its aims. The Government, therefore,
has no reason to believe that local
authorities will not take full advantage
of the powers which the Bill will confer
upon them.
The new Clause proposed by the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) is
based upon a Section of the Town Planning Act, 1925, but it is observed that
in that Act compulsion in this matter
applies only to urban areas having a
population of 20,000 and upwards, instead
of 5,000, as is suggested in the proposed
new Clause. The Government believes
that in many of the smaller urban areas
where the local authority has had in recent
years to strike a heavy rate in order to
provide essential public health services, it
would be unfair to exercise compulsion
and to compel local authorities to adopt a
scheme which might involve heavy
expenditure.
I regret this Amendment cannot be
accepted.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I state in
regard to the point of population
mentioned by the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) that in Northern
Ireland we have only two urban areas
with a population of over 20,000, the city
of Belfast and the city of Londonderry.
Amendment negatived.
The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Campbell:
After Clause 16 insert a new Clause:
If the Ministry is satisfied after the
holding of a local inquiry that a scheme
ought to be prepared by a local authority as respects any land it may by
order require the authority to prepare
a scheme and to take such other steps
as may be necessary for bringing it into
operation, and the order of the Ministry
shall have the same effect as a resolution
of the authority to prepare a scheme
for the area to which the order relates,
and the Ministry may act in the place
and at the expense of the authority in
the execution of such a scheme.
Mr. CAMPBELL: After the decision
on the last Amendment it would not be
right on my part to detain the Committee
by moving the proposed new Clause. I,
therefore, ask leave to withdraw it.
Amendment not moved.

433 Planning and Housing Bill-

Committees
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
23, page 23, line 10, to leave out "two"
("a penalty not exceeding two pounds")
and to insert instead thereof "ten."
our
1 put this Amendment and its twin
brother and successor on the Paper really
more in order to get an explanation than
with any very definite purpose. The
penalties obviously are not excessive, and
there is no nonsense about a minimum
fine. They are, in fact, the ordinary
kind of penalty. But it is rather unusual to put what I may call the little
penalty first and the heavier penalty
afterwards. It is more usually
practice to impose the larger penalty for
the first infraction of the law, and to
have it followed by the smaller penalty.
In this case we have reversed that order.
We are making the penalty for the
original misdemeanour one not exceeding
two pounds, and are following it with a
further penalty not exceeding ten pounds
in respect of each day during which the
misdemeanour continues. I should like
to know if that is the deliberate intention
of the Government and, if so, what is the
explanation of this departure from the
ordinary custom? In the meantime, and
in order to elicit that information, I beg
to move the first Amendment, and if
necessary I will follow it up with the
second one.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I agree
that it is unusual, where provision is made
for an initial fine and continuing penalty,
to find that the continuing penalty is
greater than the initial fine, and on
further consideration the Government
think that there are cases where the
initial fine of £2 might not be adequate
and, accordingly, I am prepared to accept
the first part of the Amendment increasing the maximum fine from £2 to £10,
leaving the Justices to inflict a suitable
penalty within that limit, if that would
meet the wishes of the hon. and learned
Senator. The second part of the Amendment is not under consideration at the
present moment.
Mr. LESLIE: Not at the moment.
The effect of that is that the first Amendment to leave out "two" and to insert
"ten" is acceptable to the Government.
Viscount CHARLEMONT indicated
assent.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
23, page 23, line 11, to leave out the word
"ten" ("a further penalty not exceeding
ten pounds') and to insert instead thereof the word "two."
"?

I am
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
afraid that I cannot agree to the second

Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that would

Mr. CAMPBELL: Is that so? The

435 Planning and Housing Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Campbell]
without unduly hastening the passage of
the Bill, what the probable effect of the
Bill will be, taken as a whole, and having
regard to the shape it has taken in this
House, and especially with regard to the
marked divergencies from the corresponding English Act and the Bill now before
the Imperial Parliament. The matter is
further complicated by the fact that since
this Bill was introduced in Northern
Ireland a Budget has been presented in
England under which there is to be taxation of land values, a tax of 1d in the
pound on the capital value of land having
increment value. We do not know how that
will work out in England, or whether it will
be adopted in Northern Ireland because
Northern Ireland is expressly excepted
from the Finance Bill. In my view,
therefore, it would be wise not to carry
this Bill further at the present stage.
There is no immediate urgency for the
Bill. There is no necessity for its early
legislation, and between this and the reassembly of the House in October there
will be ample time for the Government
to make up their minds on the various
points, and, perhaps, they would also
have the benefit of the experience of the
working of the English Planning Bill
during three or four months.
In order
the better to shape the Bill and to put it
into such order as would be of best advantage to the country, I beg to move that the
Committee be now adjourned.
I am en-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
tirely in the hands of the Committee, but
I would put forward a reverse opinion to
that of the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell). I do not think the Government are likely to modify their point of
view regarding the Bill, subject, of course,
to the passing of Amendments or the proposal of other Amendments either at this
The Bill,
stage or on the Report Stage.
I
of course, is not entirely the same as the
Bill before the Imperial House of Commons, but it is not to be presumed that
every bit of legislation which we pass here
is to be entirely in accordance with that
passed by the Imperial Parliament, any
more than the reverse is the case.
would, therefore, oppose the proposal to

which to consider the remaining Clauses
of the Bill before next week I am entirely

Mr. LESLIE: Assuming that the intention is to follow the ordinary coursethat is to say that if we complete the
Committee Stage this afternoon we will
take the Report Stage on one day and the
Third Reading on another day with a
reasonable interval between the two-it
seems to me that there is absolutely no
use in adjourning now. The remaining
Amendments on the Paper are, if I may
say so, of a conversational rather than of
a controversial order. There is nothing
on the Paper which would make any
serious difference, and the House has
already given its approval to the general
principle of the Bill on the Second
Reading. I think it would be better to
go on with the Committee Stage to-day.
We shall finish at no very late hour, and
it would be better to go on to-day, and
then take the Report Stage and the Third
Reading in the ordinary course.
cannot put out of our minds the possibility that, although this House may
adjourn to a definite day, there will be
the usual qualification that we may be
called together if certain circumstances
arise. It might be that some opportunity
would arise whereby those who sit elsewhere
might assemble and might then deal with
our Amendments, and so the Bill might
get through. I think the general idea of
the local authorities is that they would
like the Bill on the Statute Book as soon
as it is reasonably practicable. For that
reason I think it would be undesirable
that the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) should pursue his proposal.
We
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
say that the ordinary time for the consideration of this Bill will be given both
on the Report Stage and the Third Reading. There is no immediate hurry.
On Question, Motion ("That this
House do now resume") negatived.

"The expression 'Act of Parliament'
in section seven of the Telegraph Act,
1878 (which makes provision as to work

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 48, page 41, line 22, to
leave out the word "the" ("in pursuance
of the Acts of Parliament').
This is purely a drafting Amendment.
The object is to make it clear that the
reference to Acts of Parliament in line 22
applies to such Acts generally and not to
any particular class of Acts.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it is necessary
to point out that if we accept this Amendment-as of course we shall-it will involve altering the word "involves" in the
next line to "involved." I imagine,
however, that that will be done automatically and it will not be necessary to move
an Amendment.
It is so
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
small it has escaped the human eye.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes, quite so.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand

CLAUSE 49 (Interpretation) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 50 (Short title):
This Act may be cited as the Planning and Housing Act (Northern Ireland), 1931.
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, in page 41, line 32, at end insert
the following new Sub-section:
"(2) Sections twenty-eight to thirtyfour, inclusive, and Section thirty-eight
of this Act shall come into operation on
the first day of January, nineteen hundred and thirty-two, but save as aforesaid this Act shall come into operation
on the passing thereof."
The object of this Amendment is to
provide for the lapse of a certain space
of time between the passing of the Bill
and the coming into operation of those
provisions which relate to the repair, demolition and closing of individual insanitary houses. This space of time is
necessary for the purpose of setting up
certain machinery in connection with
these provisions, which is necessary if the
provisions are to be brought into effect,
e.g., rules of court governing appeals
must be made and forms of notice, etc.,
drafted, printed, and made available to
the local authorities. This machinery
cannot be set up until the Bill has received its final shape.
Hon. Members will have observed that
when Clauses 28 and 30 have come into
operation local authorities will no longer
be able to deal with insanitary houses and
with certain types of nuisance under Part
II of the Housing of the Working Classes
Act, 1890, and the Public Health Acts,
but must deal with them under this Act.
It is, therefore, essential in the Government's opinion that these Clauses should
not come into operation until such time
as steps have been taken to provide the
necessary machinery, as otherwise danger
might arise to the public health owing to
there being no means available whereby
cases of the kind to which I have referred
could be properly dealt with.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

Committee.
FIRST, SECOND and
SCHEDULES agreed to.
THIRD
FOURTH SCHEDULE (Restrictions
on operation of certain provisions of Act).
1. Nothing in Part I of this Act shall
authorise the acquisition for the purposes
of any planning scheme of any land which
is the site of an ancient monument or
other object of archæological interest.
2. Nothing in Part I of this Act shall
apply to any lands or buildings belonging
to any religious body and used by them
for religious or ecclesiastical purposes,
without the consent of the governing
body of the religious body concerned.
3. Nothing in Part I of this Act or in
sub-section (1) of section twenty-six in
Part II thereof shall authorise the
acquisition of any land which is the property of anv local authority, or has been
acquired, or is used, or in through or
over which any easement cr right is enjoyed, by any corporation, company or
person for the purposes of a railway, dock,
canal, water or other public undertaking,
without
the consent of such local
authority, corporation, company or person:
Provided that such consent shall not be
unreasonably withheld, and any question
whether or not such consent has been
unreasonably withheld shall be determined by the Ministry.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
45, line 6, at end ("of archælogical interest") add "without the consent of the
Ministry of Finance.”
This follows exactly the corresponding
passage in the Imperial Act, 1925, but so
far as I know the law in Great Britain
relating to ancient monuments is not
quite the same as our own. I think that
urban districts in Great Britain have
greater power than this Parliament saw
fit to confer upon them in dealing with
ancient monuments three or four years
ago. It seems to me that the Bill is quite
right in closing the door as is done in the
article under discussion but I am not sure
that it is quite wise for us to bolt it, and
therefore I suggest that we should put in
a little saving Clause, by permitting the
acquisition of ancient monuments if the
Ministry of Finance consents. The
Ministry of Finance are the natural
guardians of ancient monuments-we
made them so except in those cases
where county councils retain or acquire
sites. I think that perhaps we might
find it convenient to put in a saving
Clause, as I am suggesting, so that it will
be possible for land which is the site of
ancient monuments to be acquired for the
purpose of any planning scheme if the
Ministry of Finance consent. Of course,
before it does consent it will take such
steps as are reasonably required.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
45, line 14, after "by" ("by any corporation'), insert "a local authority or
by."
This is getting a little more difficult.
The Schedule is really a rather important
part of the Bill in this case.
This particular article has, if we may say so, a
somewhat chequered history. It does not
appear here this afternoon in the form
in which it first saw the light in another
place, I suppose, somewhere about this time
last year.
The difficulty which I am trying to meet by inserting "a local authority
or by" is that it would appear that
we are protecting the property of
local authorities, but we are not protecting the easements acquired by local
authorities. That is what it would
appear, but as we know things are not
always what they seem. We have got
the expression "land" in relation to local
authorities. I am not certain whether
in the English Bill the expression,
"land" would not cover all such easements and so on as are here referred to.
For our own purposes and for the purpurposes of this Bill, in this particular we
have no right to take land further than
the Interpretation Act, and that includes
messuages, tenements and hereditaments.
We might then have an interminable argument as to whether some of these easements
and rights are hereditaments or not. Therefore it would seem to me we want to put
in here the expression, "a local authority
or by" in order to make sure that where
a local authority have acquired easements
or rights they should have some protection as if they had property. I think
the best way out of the difficulty is to
adopt the suggestion I have made.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
441 Planning and Housing Bill-

Adjournment
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
45, line 15, after "water" ("water or
other public undertaking') insert
electricity supply.”
Here again we are going back to past
history. When this Bill first came in,
the Electricity (Supply) Bill, which has
now received the Royal Assent, had not
passed, and I am in great trouble in my
mind as to whether we ought to insert
the words, "electricity supply," here after
"water,' so as to give the electricity
supply corporation, company, or person
the same power that we have given to the
dock, canal, or water undertaking. We
have to consider first of all, I think,
whether we want to give that protection
to the electricity people, because it is
rather a larger problem than water pipes.
These electricity cables will stretch over
a far greater expanse of country than
would water pipes or anything of that
sort. We have also to consider, I think,
whether the electricity people, as I have
mentioned, are not there already under
a company or a person engaged in a public
undertaking. There is not a very clear
definition of a public undertaking. I
really am in considerable doubt about the
matter, and therefore have brought it before the attention of the Committee.
am not a bit sure about it.
I
Unfortu-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
nately I am not very certain about it
either. What the hon. Senator has in
mind would surely be covered by the expression "other public undertaking." If
we are to specify electricity why not also
specify gas undertakings, and, possibly,
there are other things that should also be
included. It appears to us to be better
to leave the Clause as it is so that they
may all be covered by the expression I
have mentioned. I prefer not to accept
the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think it better that
the Amendment be withdrawn.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Oral Answers.
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 2nd June, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Questions to Minister.
DOUBLE-DECK PUBLIC SERVICE
VEHICLES.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to ask the first
question on the Paper, although part
of it I am afraid is rather out of date--
whether it has been decided to allow
"double-deck" public service vehicles to
operate on the roads of Northern Ireland,
and, if so, what conditions, if any, have
been imposed as regards weight, pace and
the prevention of obstruction at corners.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No general
approval has been given to the use of
double-decker omnibuses on the roads in
Northern Ireland, but permission has
been given in certain specified cases where
it has been proved to the satisfaction of
the Ministry of Home Affairs that the use
of the vehicles in question will not prejudice the public safety having regard to
the width, the surface and camber of the
authorised roads, the height of the
bridges, and taking into account other
features such as over-hanging trees, buildings, etc. Each application is dealt with
on its merits and no general rules can be
laid down regarding the routes over which
these types of vehicles will be allowed to
run, but as a general principle no doubledeck omnibus is allowed on any road or
section of road where the use of such
vehicle might tend to prejudice the safety
of either the passengers or other users of
the road or where it would appear that
the road in itself is not suitable for such
vehicles.
CASES TRIABLE BY RESIDENT
MAGISTRATES SITTING ALONE.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Government have received representations from
magistrates regarding cases of summary
jurisdiction triable only by a resident
magistrate sitting alone as to the inconvenience caused by these cases being heard
concurrently with ordinary petty sessions.
cases before the ordinary bench, but in a
different part of the courthouse; and
whether arrangements will be made to
obviate the resulting inconvenience.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government has received representations in
connection with the matters included in
the hon. and learned Member's question,
and I can inform him that my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Home Affairs, has
at present this matter under consideration
in order to ascertain how best the difficulties mentioned may be overcome.
RESIDENT MAGISTRATES'
TENURE OF OFFICE.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Government contemplates legislation making
resident magistrates irrremovable during
their tenure of office except on an address
to the Crown from both Houses of Parliament
By way of explanation perhaps I might
say that courts of petty sessions in Ireland have been superseded, as far as the
Irish Free State is concerned, by district
justices' courts. In Northern Ireland
there have been in recent years very remarkable accessions to jurisdiction in the
case of resident magistrates. Take, for
instance, the administration of the licensing Acts. In almost every case of a
charge followed by a conviction there is
an endorsement of the publican's licence,
and an endorsement of a publican's
licence is a matter of very great importance to the particular offender. I am
not quite sure whether it is accurate or
not, but it is said that one endorsement
means a depreciation of the property by
one-third and a second endorsement by
two-thirds, and if a third endorsement
follows inside a period of five years the
licence is annulled and the premises are
disqualified for all future time for a grant
or a renewal of the licence. So we see
from that fact alone the enormous power
now vested in resident magistrates, a
power that in some respects is not often
exercised by judges of the High Court.
Then, again, there is an increasing
tendency to deliver over to resident
magistrates the entire jurisdiction of
departmental offences, offences in which
one or other of the Ministries is the complainant on one side and a member of the
public the defendant on the other. That
tendency is becoming more marked and it
Planning and Housing Bill 446
Oral Answers
2 JUNE 1931
is of vital importance if any alterations.
are in progress regarding the jurisdiction.
of petty sessions in Northern Irelandand there is an impression that we are
drifting in the direction of the Irish Free
State model-if that is the case, I submit
it would be wise to secure, as far as possible, the complete independence of every
branch of the judiciary from the Executive
Departments. Nothing is more important, and I think it is doubly important
in a small community.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government does not contemplate the introduction of legislation on the lines
suggested by the hon. and learned
Member.
re-
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to support
the viewpoint expressed by my Colleague
who sits beside me. I think in the interests of justice it is essential that the
most complete confidence should be placed
in the tribunal by the public, and
if resident magistrates, with the extended
jurisdiction to which my Friend
ferred, are to be more or less subject
to and under the control of the Government, and not to exercise the same independence as the ordinary courts,
naturally the administration of justice is
bound to be more suspect where the
executive are concerned in cases before
them than if they were free and independent as the judges undoubtedly should be.
I very seriously and very earnestly support the viewpoint expressed by my
Colleague. I think it is absolutely
essential that the public and practitioners
should have confidence and for that they
must feel that the magistrate is in the
same position as the judges, free from all
Government influence and control.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that,
would it not almost seem that the logical
course is to take the original appointments
of these magistrates out of the hands at
present controlling them and place them
in the hands of the Lord Chief Justice?
That would more completely separate the
judiciary from the executive, so to speak.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House,
what objection he sees in principle to
giving the resident magistrates the same
tenure of office as the district judges in
the Free State-tenure for life, subject
to certain statutory conditions.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid any answer I gave on those lines
would not square with the answer I have
given that the Government do not contemplate the introduction of legislation on
the lines mentioned. I think the confidence of the public in the decision of
the resident magistrates is complete. I
never heard the matter of their decisions
being questioned on the grounds that that
decision has been altered or amended or
controlled by Government interference.
There is an appeal from their decision,
but I do not think it is often exercised.
Mr. CAMPBELL: There is no reflection whatever expressed on the resident.
magistrates.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No. But
they might be mistaken.
Planning and Housing Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Moved, "That this Report be now received."(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.

of paragraph (b) to
authority so defined is," I suggest to the

be remembered that paragraph (b) gave

of the purposes of the scheme;"
That, I venture to suggest, will completely connect the somewhat troublesome

5 which is now before us. Also, it is
perhaps better to use the word "specify"
rather than "define." I beg to move,
first the omission of paragraph (b), and
secondly the substitution of the paragraph I have read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept this Amendment, both

on the Paper.

HOUSING OF WORKING CLASSES.
BLIND PERSONS' PENSIONS
ADMINISTRATION.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Thursday next."
Mr. CAMPBELL: This House is now
almost on the eve of adjourning for a
period of four months, and before we
separate I think we ought to take notice
of a very urgent problem which the Government has not solved. It is a problem
which I think the Government have
ignored, but which the public do not forget, and that is the absence of wholesome
housing for the low-paid classes of the
community. The stream of subsidies for
the building of dwelling-houses is about
to run dry. So the Government has
decreed. The middle classes and the
well-paid workmen are the people most
Housing of Working Classes 450
Adjournment-
2 JUNE 1931
benefited by the erection of State subsidy dwellings. Subsidy houses have
gone to people who ought never to have
had them. The poorer classes in Belfast
have as much chance of occupying one
of these houses as of occupying a lordly
demesne. How can they pay rents in
Belfast from 10/- to 17/6 a week? The
average rent of a Belfast subsidy house
is 12/- a week, while the slum dweller
What
pays 4/- or 5/- a week for rent.
the State has done for his more fortunate
brother it would not do for the poorer
tenant. He is worse off than before the
war. His children are the chief sufferers.
The broad aim of the Housing Acts
should have been to house the poor who
cannot house themselves. These Acts
will be a monument of futility if these
deserving and helpless folk are not aided.
to obtain houses worth living in.
only dimly realises the hard conditions
under which the masses subsist. We are
told the Housing Acts are to come to an
end next year, and then there will be no
more subsidies. Instead the Government
offer the people a starveling Planning
and Housing Bill which will accomplish
neither good planning nor good housing,
and is but a shadow and simulacrum of
English Measures. Ulster Tories go step
by step with Englishmen in the distribution of the "dole" because they dare
not do otherwise, but whenever they can
drop out of step they drop comically out
of step with English reforms and prove
themselves the unprogressive Tories their
party is by tradition and sympathy.
ingly, slums

When will that be? Speaking in the

What credulity! Where is the supply
of houses for these poor people?
demand is there. The demand is beyond

authority.
Private enterprise cannot with

tightening its purse strings it has remembered that to him that hath shall be
given, and from him that hath not shall
be taken even the little that he hath. It
is a crying injustice that these unfortuleft
people are
deserted and

Pharaoh-like has steeled its heart against

Housing Act of 1930, with its yearly

for 40 years for every man, woman and
child taken from a slum home by the local
authority and put into a decent house is
a real bona-fide Housing Act.
nate

have a Government out of touch with the

common masses,

Blind Persons' Pensions 452
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
Here, again, the Government say laissez
faire is the only way that matters
will right themselves in time. The overrented tenants must be patient and "dree
their weird." It is a policy of donothing, of drift--a nerveless policy.
The Government has scoffed at what is
called the "children rent rebate," by
which local authorities can charge a lower
rent for a subsidy house according to the
number of children in the family. That
principle is enshrined in the English
Housing Act of 1930. I suppose the
principle was too progressive for this
area.
Mr. McALLISTER: I would like
very shortly to draw the attention of the
Government to a small but very deserving class of the community who are denied rights to which
Member
every
of this House will agree they are entitled
to. I refer to the number of people in
Northern Ireland who are so blind that
they cannot obtain employment, but yet
are not so blind that they are brought
within the provisions of the present
Blind Persons' Pensions Act. I desire to make it perfectly clear that I
do not for one moment reflect on the
administration of the blind persons pensions scheme. I know it is administered
in the most broad and generous way by
those responsible, but legislation prevents
them doing justice to this small but very
deserving class of the community.
As the law stands at the present time,
in order to come within the four corners
of the pensions Act a person must be,
I think the words are, "so blind as to
be unable to do work for which eyesight
is essential." I suggest that legislation
should be introduced, and it can be introduced at very small cost, to bring
within the Blind Persons Pensions Act
those persons who are not so blind as to
be unable to do work for which eyesight is
essential, but who are so blind that they
really cannot get work.
Take the case
of a skilled man in the engineering or
any other trade who has spent all his life
up to sixty years of age in this particular
employment. Those who administer the
Pensions Act might find that although
he is too blind to follow his own employment, the employment to which he has
been accustomed all his life, he is not so
blind as to be unable to do any class of
work for which eyesight is essential.
Again, take the case of a woman who up
to sixty years of age has earned her
livelihood at sewing. Her eyesight may
be practically gone, but those who administer the Act cannot find as a fact
that she is so blind that she cannot do
any work for which eyesight is essential.
She cannot continue the work to which
she has been used all her life, but we all
know that if this skilled engineer or this
woman to whom I have referred go to
look for ordinary manual employment no
one will employ them. In the first
place they would be unable to do the
work, and in the second place employers
would be afraid to employ them owing
to their defective eyesight. As I say,
this must be an infinitesimal part of the
community, and I would ask the House
sympathetically to urge upon the Government between now and the next meeting
of Parliament the case of this highly
deserving class. I am perfectly sure
every Member of the House has the
greatest possible sympathy with them,
and to provide for them would necessarily
be a comparatively small additional
charge upon the revenue. It is a case
that will appeal to every Member of this
House, and I believe they will support the
suggested legislation.
Mr. DORMAN: I am very glad the
hon. and learned Senator, Mr. Campbell, has drawn the attention of the Government to this question. There is a
deplorable state of affairs existing in
Northern Ireland amongst the class to
which the hon. and learned Senator
referred, the lowest paid working class
in our community. Over 50,000 such
families
are living under conditions
which mean there are three persons per
apartment in the dwelling, and, I speak
from memory, there are in Belfast over
10,000 of the working class living under
conditions which mean three persons per
apartment. In Belfast there is street
after street composed of small kitchen
houses, and any hon. Member who would
take the trouble of visiting these workingclass dwellings would find that the cubic
space of each is not more than that of
two or three ceils in our prison on the
Crumlin Road, and he will recognise that
the tenants are living under conditions
which mean three persons in each compartment. In a kitchen house there
would be two sleeping apartments, which
would mean there would be six persons
in a room in such a house.
I think it is absolutely impossible even
for the employing class to depend upon
2 JUNE 1931
Adjournment—

Blind Persons' Pensions
who knows one job and is getting on in
years, but cannot do his job now because
of possibly increasing blindness, if he
has to find other work other than that
to which he is accustomed, is thrown with
a hopeless disability upon an already
over-crowded labour market. This is the
line to which I myself should like attention directed to see if the existing law
cannot if necessary be stretched and
given a somewhat wider application.
the individuals who emanate from homes
of that kind to give the value they ought
to give in return for the expenditure the
employers make in the form of wages
because of the physical deterioration resulting from the housing conditions
prevailing in Belfast and Northern Ireland. I hope the Government will
concentrate its attention on bringing in
in the near future a House Planning Bill
which will bring the law into line with
Great Britain, where they have introduced a scheme which will apparently
obliterate slums within 40 years.
there is evidently no provision, no looking forward to the removing of these sad
and lamentable conditions that prevail
amongst the class of the community to
which the hon. and learned Senator has
drawn the attention of the Government,
and though he has done so even at this
almost the last sitting of the Senate, I
hope he will be successful in concentrating the attention of the Government
upon the importance of legislating upon
these lines in the near future.
Here
Mr. LESLIE: There seem to be two
questions before us for discussion at the
moment, the first, raised by the hon. and
learned Senator dealing with housing,
with which I do not propose to deal, and
the second by the hon. Senator who sits
beside him dealing with the sad cases of
the comparatively small number of persons who, if not totally, are at least parthe
tially blind. I, myself, have
greatest sympathy with those who are
partially blind, and I think the whole
House is inclined to urge that any steps
that can be taken to ameliorate their condition should be taken, but the opportunity of which we are at present availing ourselves is one rather for discussing
the administration of the existing law
perhaps than of suggesting an alteration,
and the lines to which I, myself, would
like to see attention directed are whether
there is not perhaps some undue harshness in interpreting the existing Blind
Persons' Pensions Act.
The fault, if it be a fault, as it seems
to me, is that in considering that question to which the hon. Senator referred,
the question of work for which eyesight
is essential, somewhat too narrow an interpretation is placed upon it and it is
left quite general. It seems to me the
man who is partially blind, admittedly
so, found on examination-and there are
repeated examinations in these cases-
Mr. QUIGLEY: I would like in dealing with the question which Senator
Campbell has brought before the House
in regard to housing to say I feel deeply
interested in that matter. I am in
agreement, but not thoroughly in agreement, with all he said as I feel the
working people up to the present have
not received the benefit they should have
received from the Housing Acts. The
hon. and learned Senator has stated that
many have benefited through this subsidy who should not have benefited. I
am in agreement with that. It is a great
pity that up to the present time the
poorer class of people cannot possibly
get a house at a less rent than from 12/-
to 17/6 per week. On a former occasion
when I spoke on this matter I gave some
particulars in regard to houses in Comber, the measurements of the different
apartments, etc., which were rented at
4/9 per week, and also of houses built in
Coagh, County Tyrone, let at less than
5/- a week, and I understand the same
applies to Dungannon. I realise that
in building houses in Comber and Coagh
the ground rents and other things are
much cheaper than in Belfast, but why
should the rents in the city be three or
almost four times those of the houses I
have mentioned ? Regarding the hon.
and learned Senator's reference to what
could be done in England, I wish the
party Mr. Campbell represents had
thought of the goodness of England 25 or
50 years ago.
Reference has been made by Senator
McAllister to the blind. I am
thoroughly in agreement with him in regard to that matter. There is no doubt
the law on the subject is that the person
must be totally blind-
Mr. HILL DICKSON: No.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Well, practically
blind. I had the case of a man who
came to see me, and after taking his par-
Adjournment-
Blind Persons' Pensions
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
ticulars I interested myself in him.
I
had to lead him out of the office into
He is
which he came to see me. I put forward
his claim, but the decision went against
him. It would be an utter impossibility
for that man to get employment.
one of the cases the hon. Senator quoted
of men working until they are 60 years
of age.
Where is the employer who is
going to engage a person almost blind?
Would he take one into a factory or workshop? Would he take one anywhere?
It is impossible to get a man or woman
to employ the partially blind.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: What the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) really requires when he spoke about
housing would necessitate an enormous
financial outlay in order to provide
houses at non-economic rents for the class
for which he speaks. This would be
taken as dealing sympathetically with the
subject even if a Tory Government introduced it, but the Tory mind must have
a certain amount of reference to the facts,
and the finances of this Government
would not stand such an outlay as would
be involved. The only thing we can do
is to give subsidies. That has been done.
A very large sum for a small area has
been expended in this direction, nearly
£2,000,000 to date. I am afraid
it would be impossible to relieve the
housing shortage, which is undoubtedly
great, by any other method than by continuing this subsidy, even in its reduced
form. Though there has been some
diminution in the number of houses
built under this reduced subsidy building
is still going on. I think this represents a sympathetic attitude on the part
of the Government in dealing with this
undoubtedly very great problem.
With regard to the question of the
blind persons, which Senator McAllister
has raised, there would be administrative difficulties in the course he suggests,
but I am willing to promise on behalf of
the Government that they will consider
sympathetically what he has said, and explore the avenues to better the situation
which undoubtedly causes
such great
hardship.
On Question, Motion ("That this
House do now adjourn until Thursday
next") agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Four
o'clock, until Thursday next.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
4 JUNE 1931
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 4th June, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Returns of Local Taxation in Northern
Ireland for the year 1927-1928.
Presented (by Command) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Questions to Minister.
LOANS GUARANTEE ACTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can
state the aggregate loans guaranteed by
the Ministry of Finance under the Loans
Guarantee Acts in respect of shipowning
and shipbuilding concerns; the aggregate
of such loans guaranteed in respect of the
linen and farming industries; whether
the Ministry will present to Parliament
the papers in connection with its guarantee of 53 millions in respect of guarantees
given to the Kylsant group of companies;
and whether it is the intention of the
Ministry to give further guarantees of
loans until the maximum of £13,000,000
is reached; and what is the longest period
allowed to guaranteed persons for their
repayment to the Government of moneys
for which the Ministry may become
liable in fulfilment of guarantees under
these Acts.
The contingent liability of the taxpayers of Northern Ireland under
guarantees of vast amounts given by the
Ministry of Finance under the Loans
Guarantee Acts is naturally a matter of
solicitude to the taxpayers. The history
of these guarantees is very much of a
mystery to the man in the street. The
relation of the Government to these loans
is very much of a mystery to him. The
Ministry act under a series of Loans
Guarantee Acts modelled on the Trades
Facilities Acts of the Westminster Parliament. The last of the British Trade
The
Facilities Acts was passed in 1926.
ink is hardly dry on the last of our
Guarantee Acts. The British Government ceased giving such guarantees in
1927. Our Government may go on giving guarantees up to 1932. The maximum limit to guarantees by the British
Government was £75,000,000. The
maximum limit to our Government's
guarantees was £14,000,000, reduced
to £13,000,000 under the Act of 1930.
That is about one-sixth of the maximum
permitted to the British Government. Is
the ratio not striking? Has the Government been flying its kite too high? The
population of Great Britain is 35 times
ours, and Britain has a very long purse
and Ulster a very short purse. Yet for
each £5 Britain could guarantee our
Government took power to guarantee £1.
The loans which the Government
guarantee are of course secured by
mortgages and other means. Are these
securities appreciating or depreciating?
The interest on these loans has been paid.
So far so good. Has there been an extension of time in respect of three millions of
loan capital which fell due last year?
What the Ulster taxpayers are thinking
of is what money they may have to pay
eventually on foot of the Ministry's
guarantees. The administration of these
Acts calls for clear sight and long sight.
Shipbuilding has been in the depth of
depression for years. Shipbuilding has
been spoon-fed as a delicate foster-child
of the Government under these Acts.
How much has been guaranteed in respect of shipbuilding since 1927, when
the British guarantees were wisely
brought to a close? How long will the
Government continue to play the role of
guarantor? Will it now
Doubtless it will claim that the guarancry halt?
tees relieved unemployment, but could
unemployment have been any worse during these past few years? Is there an
industry or trade in the Province which
would not welcome Government guarantees to tide it over its own difficulties?
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The aggregate loans guaranteed by the Ministry of Finance under the
Loans Guarantee Acts in respect of ship
construction amount to £11,326,587, of
which £2,029,190 has been repaid, leaving £9,297,397 outstanding; no guarantees have been issued in respect of loans
for capital construction in connection
with the linen and farming industries.
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
As regards the suggestion that the
papers dealing with the guarantees given
to the Kylsant group of companies should
be made available to Parliament, I think
it must be obvious to the hon. and learned
Member that such a proposal could not
be acceded to as the papers contain a
great deal of confidential matter supplied
to the Loans Guarantee Committee by
the Companies concerned, by the
Treasury, and from other sources. I am
informed, however, by my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, that all
information sought by the Comptroller
and Auditor-General on behalf of the
House of Commons in the ordinary discharge of his audit duties has been made
available to him. Statistical information
in regard to guaranteed loans is, of
course, supplied to this House in the
quarterly returns presented by the Ministry of Finance.
Applications for guarantees within the
existing statutory limit will continue to
be considered by the Advisory Committee
and by the Ministry on their merits.
The period of the guarantee depends in
each case upon the character of the
transaction and the method of finance
employed. The longest period guaranteed under these Acts is one of twenty
years, being in respect of a loan raised
by means of a public issue.
Mr. McALLISTER: I would like to
say a word or two with regard to the
figures that have been given by the noble
Leader of the House. I have always held
the view, which I have stated in this
House on more than one occasion, that
Belfast receives preferential treatment
compared with the urban and rural areas.
I think the urban and rural areas will
be rather staggered when they learn that
for one industry alone in Belfast the
Government have guaranteed loans
amounting to nine and a quarter million
pounds, while for the great farming industry of the Province, which employs
more people and is the most important
industry in Northern Ireland, there are
no guaranteed loans at all. Therefore I
think I am justified when I criticise the
Government on the ground that it shows
a partial administration in favour of the
City of Belfast, where the social, business, and other interests of the Government are mainly concentrated. I have
always maintained, and I think the
figures quoted by the noble Leader of the
House bear me out, that the urban and
rural districts have been very much
neglected by the Government, and as a
representative, more or less, of a country
district I desire to make the
vigorous protest possible against onesided administration of this type.
most
Mr. BARCLAY: In my opinion the
urban and rural districts benefit just as
much from these guaranteed loans as do
the people in the City of Belfast. These
loans were guaranteed to assist shipbuilding, which enabled the workers of the
city to be employed in larger numbers
and more continuously than they otherwise would have been. The wages they
received went to buy farm produce for
which the farmers in the country were
paid. Therefore, if the people in the
country did not benefit directly from
these guaranteed loans they received
benefit indirectly through the workingclass people of Belfast spending their
wages in the purchase of the food stuffs
they required from day to day. I cannot see what the non. Senator has got
to complain about considering that the
money goes round and that farmers
throughout the country got their share
just as well as the workers in the City
of Belfast.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Senator
Barclay has stated what is a fact. The
city cannot exist without the country any
more that the country can exist without
the city, and the enormous amount of
unemployment which would have existed
in the City of Belfast had it not been
for these Loans Guarantee Acts would
have reflected on the country districts.
TRADE WITH IRISH FREE STATE.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can
state the value of imports into Northern
Ireland from the Irish Free State for
the year 1930, and of exports from Northern Ireland into the Irish Free State
for the same year; the corresponding
figures for the year 1924; and the number of customs posts maintained on each
side of the Boundary between the two
areas.
During the present Session of Parliament the noble Lord, the Leader of the
House, informed the House that the
estimated imports into Northern Ireland
Planning and Housing Bill 462
Oral Answers.
4 JUNE 1931
from all sources in 1924 was approximately £69,000,000, and that in 1930 the
value of imports from all countries,
except the Irish Free State, was
approximately £52,000,000, a fall of
£17,000,000. According to the noble
Lord's statement the estimated value of
exports from Northern Ireland to all
countries in 1924 was approximately
£66,000,000, and the estimated value of
exports in 1930 to all countries except
the Irish Free State was a matter of
That is a fall again of
£49,000,000.
£17,000,000, that is to say, the combined decline in trade outwards and
inwards in a period of six years, contrasting 1924 with 1930, was £34,000,000, a
drop of from about £135,000,000 to
about £100,000,000. These figures give
food for thought.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In reply
to the first part of the hon. and learned
Member's question, I have to inform him
that the only figures available showing
the value of imports into Northern Ireland from the Irish Free State or value
of exports from Northern Ireland into
the Irish Free State for the year 1930,
are those contained in the Irish Free
State official publication as the Imperial
figures are not yet published. In order
that the figures for the two years may be
comparable, I am therefore quoting the
official Irish Free State figures for both
1924 and 1930. In considering these
figures the hon. Member will doubtless
bear in mind that values of exports from
the Irish Free State are free on board,
whereas the imports into the Irish Free
State are cost, insurance, and freight.
The value of exports from the Irish Free
State to Northern Ireland was £7,456,992
and £4,863,575 in the years 1924 and
1930 respectively. Imports into the
Irish Free State from Northern Ireland
in
the same years were valued at
£7,853,319 and £5,760,048.
In reply to the second part of the hon.
and learned Member's question, there
are 16 boundary posts on the Northern
Irish side of the boundary and 7 customs
stations. I have no official information
in regard to the number of boundary
posts and customs stations on the Free
State side of the boundary, but, according to a table compiled by the Automobile Association previous to the approval
of the Pettigo Kesh-Enniskillen road
and the setting up of the Pettigo
boundary post, there were then 16 Irish
T
!
Free State boundary posts and 13 customs stations. It seems likely, therefore, that there are now 17 Free State
boundary posts and 13 or 14 customs
stations.
Planning and Housing Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time." (Viscount Charlemont).
Mr. CAMPBELL: In bidding goodbye to this Bill I wish to say that I think
it is regrettable that the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, did not see his way
to accept some of the Amendments proposed in the Committee stage, the aim
of which was to stimulate the local
authorities in bringing the beneficial
Clauses of the Bill into operation. I am
sure we all wish the Bill well, but many
of us fear that in the absence of all spur
in the Bill to stimulate the local authorities the Bill will remain on the Statute
Book a curiosity, I am afraid, of ineffective, inefficient, and impotent legislation. I hope it will be otherwise.
hope it will work well, but I am afraid
that without the co-operation and
activity on the part of the local authorities the Bill is very likely to remain a
dead letter.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot help thinking that although the Bill is without the
spur to which the hon. and learned
Senator referred, perhaps it is not unfair
to point out that such spur could scarcely
be provided in this House. But although
the Bill does lack the incentive of subsidy I think there is reason for hoping
that those local authorities who have
asked for the Bill will make an endeavour
to use it now that they have, or are about
very shortly to get it. I think we may
have some hope that the time and trouble
bestowed on this Measure not only by the
two chambers of Parliament but by conferences outside over a space of I think
almost two years will not be altogether
wasted.
There is a small matter of administration to which I should like to draw
attention. Hon. Senators who are
familiar with Clause 9 of the Bill, the
Clause mainly directed to the payment of
463 Planning and Housing Bill-
Adjournment
THE SENATE

[Mr. Leslie]
compensation in respect of property
injuriously affected and which contains
the power of the local authority to recover one-half of the betterment due to
the planning scheme. In both of these
connections it will be remembered a time
has to be set in the planning scheme
within which the claims, whether on one
count or the other, have to be made.
There is a minimum provided in the Bill
of three months, that is to say, three
months at least must be allowed-again
to one side or the other-to put forward
its claim. But although that is the minimum limit there is no maximum limit.
I should rather like it to be borne in
mind that it is probably in the public
interest that the maximum limit which
will come into being in the scheme itself
should not be too wide. You do not
want the question of these claims to stand
over for an unduly long period. That is,
I think, a pure matter of administration.
I did not make any suggestion at any
stage of the progress of the Bill, because
the wording of the Bill follows the wording of the corresponding Section of the
English Act. I think it is a point worthy
of consideration as a matter of administration, that the upward limit should not
be too long.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will
bring that matter to the notice of my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs. I think it is very desirable that
the upward limit should not be too long.
Of course, as the local authorities have
asked for the Bill I think it is unlikely
the Bill will become a dead letter. I
sincerely hope it will not. But I should
like to point out with regard to the
absence of compulsion, the absence of the
spur, I think this criticism only applies
to the first part of the Bill, that is, the
more interesting part, the town-planning
part. In Part 2, it is made the duty of
local authorities to deal with unhealthy
areas and sanitary housing within the
local area. Clause 22 says the local
authority "shall." I think the criticism that there is no spur contained in
the Bill has not really very much
foundation.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass.
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill, with
some Amendments, to which they desire
their concurrence.
Adjournment.
Moved, "That this House do now adjourn until such date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint."--(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty minutes before
Four o'clock until such
date as Mr. Speaker
shall appoint.
