On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a First time accordingly and
ordered to be read a Second time tomorrow .
Adjournment.
PREVENTION OF PROFITEERING.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, That this
House do now adjourn until to-morrow,
at 3-15, when we propose to take all the
remaining stages of the Economy Bill.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Will
the Deputy Leader of the House give us
any information as to how we will be fixed
next week-whether we are likely to be
called?
Mr. LONG: There will be a formal
Sitting to receive the Royal Assent on
Tuesday.
Mr. CAMPBELL: On this the first
occasion we have met since the Recess,
I think it would be desirable in the
public interest if the Deputy Leader of
the House would inform us whether or
not the Minister of Home Affairs
during the present emergency will instruct the proper officers to keep a close
watch on the trend of prices of foodstuffs in Northern Ireland during the
present crisis, and whether the Government will take whatever steps may be
necessary to deal sternly with persons
attempting to profiteer.
Mr. MCALLISTER:
Before the Deputy Leader of the House answers
that question I would like to ask him
to indicate what power the Government
have to prevent profiteering. Is he
satisfied that the Government have
sufficient powers to prevent it, and if
the Government are not satisfied that
they have sufficient power, is it the intention of the Government to seek such
powers from the House?
Mr. LONG: I am glad of the opportunity which the hon. and learned
Senator has afforded of stating that the
Government is fully alive to the necessity
of protecting the community from any
attempt on the part of unscrupulous
persons to take advantage of the conditions at present existing by increasing
the price of foodstuffs above a level that
would be justified by the circumstances.
The Government will, therefore, continue to watch the trend of such prices
and will take whatever steps may seem
necessary or advisable to prevent any
such attempt being made.
In reply to hon. Senator McAllister,
I should say that there is legislation in existence dealing with certain
emergencies, and if there are not
sufficient for the purpose, which we are
considering, the Government would not
hesitate to seek the necessary powers
from Parliament to deal with the
matter. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty minutes before
Seven o'clock.
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
25 SEPTEMBER 1931
THE SENATE.

Friday, 25th September, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. Speaker in the
Chair.
JOURNAL OF THE SENATE.
Mr. SPEAKER laid upon the Table the
General Index to the Journal of the
Senate, beginning with Volume one
(1921) and ending with Volume eleven
(1930).
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move,
"That
Standing Order No. XXV be suspended
for this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking the remaining stages of the
Economy Bill."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Economy Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
the Second Reading of this Bill I need
not labour the details of the financial
situation which has made measures of
economy imperative both in Great
Britain and in our own area. I think
that our Government here are entitled to
a little credit for having already taken
steps to secure retrenchment in the local
services which are separate and under our
own control at the beginning of the
present financial year. Hon. Members
will recollect that we had to stand a very
considerable amount of criticism-I do
not say in this House-for the disagreeable economies which we then considered
it necessary to make.
The present Bill, as the Schedule
shows, is concerned with two of our
social services-National Health Insurance and Unemployment Insurancein which our arrangements are closely
bound up
with those which are in force
in Great Britain, and in which as a
consequence the proposed alterations
follow necessarily those which are being
made across the Channel.

The Bill itself adopts the somewhat
unusual method of adopting the
machinery of Orders in Council for
carrying out the details of the various
changes which are to be made in these
two services. This procedure is similar
to that which is being applied in Great
Britain, and the justification for it is
to be found in the necessity for immediate
action at the present time of crisis.
There is, however, a White Paper which
has been made available to hon. Members and in which is given a summary
of the various measures which are proposed under the general powers given in
the Bill. The intended changes in
National Health Insurance are comparatively simple. A deduction of one
shilling is to be made in the capitation
rate now payable to insurance doctors
under the scheme, and the amounts received by chemists for the supply of
drugs to insured persons will be reduced
in a similar proportion. There is also
some adjustment proposed in the Central
Fund itself.
In regard to Unemployment Insurance,
which is a much larger question, it will
be seen from the Paper that a number
of important changes are intended.
rates of benefits are to be altered in
conformity with the changes in the English rates; the weekly contributions in
respect of insured workers are increased;
there is a "limitation" in the period of
ordinary benefit enjoyed by those who
become unemployed; a needs case is to
be applied to the transitional benefit
cases, that is, to those drawing benefit
after the ordinary benefit has become
exhausted; certain anomalies in the
present system are to be removed; and
borrowing for the current account of our
local Fund is to cease as soon as the total
reaches the sum which is equivalent, on a
pro rata basis, to the English figure
which has been fixed as their limit.
everyone knows, this borrowing for the
Unemployment Insurance Fund has been
a potent factor in the financial crisis
which has arisen. The deficiency in the
Fund will, as soon as this borrowing
limit has been reached, have to be met
by Exchequer grants. Hon. Members
will find on the last page of the Paper
a table showing the effect of these
proposals. It is hardly necessary to
remind the House that in the
matter of unemployment insurance we
are bound up with the English arrangements under the Re-insurance Agreement.
As
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Economy measures are, as I have said,
always disagreeable, but when they must
come, it is the duty of any Government to
try to ensure that they call for an allround sacrifice from members of the community. It may be urged that in this
Bill we are injuring the poorer classes,
but no one can deny that the measures
already taken and the additional taxation
which has been imposed is putting a very
heavy burden on other sections of our
people.
We are very glad to be able to welcome
here to-day the Minister of Labour, who
will reply to any points raised in the
course of the debate. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The effect of the
proposals in this Bill will be to lay an
unfair burden on the poorest and most
helpless of the people. It is an attempt
to deal with the economic situation on
an altogether wrong basis.
There are
fairer and more equitable ways and
means of effecting retrenchment. Retrenchment is undoubtedly necessary,
sacrifice is necessary, but these unemployment allowances were made expressly
to keep the unemployed worker and his
dependants in a state of physical fitness.
To lower that standard is bad economics
for which the Province will have to pay
heavily in the long run. The 72,000
unemployed men and women in this
area, making with their dependants
probably 300,000 people, the poorest in
the land, are called on to lower their
standard of life, already miserably low.
A man with a wife and two children,
heretofore, got 30/- a week.
week. Under
the Bill he will get 27/3. In all fairness can a family of six-father, mother,
and four children-to whom the existing scales give 34/- a week-live on
31/3 a week? The rent will probably
be 10/- a week in Belfast. This would
leave for food, fire, clothing, boots and
other necessaries for the six, 21/3, or
3/6 a week for each, or 7d a day for
each. Can it possibly be maintained
that an individual, whether man,
woman, or child, can live in health at
the present time on 7d a day. If money
is wanted for the Insurance Fund it should
be provided by those who are more
fortunately placed, and the burden
should be placed on the shoulders best
able to bear it.
There is no real equality of sacrifice
in these proposals. It occurs to my
on
wages.
mind that these cuts are forerunners of
a general attack
To cut
wages will reduce still further the purchasing power of the people, make trade
worse, and increase unemployment. All
this will re-act on revenue. It is not an
all-round sacrifice, but a sectional sacrifice that the Government demand.
Contrast these cuts in meagre family
allowances with the average yearly cost,
as I read recently, of a cadet, say from
Northern Ireland, at some of the great
colleges across the water. I read, and
I have no reason to doubt its accuracy,
that the average cost per year of a
cadet at Woolwich Royal Military
Academy is £572, at the Royal Naval
College, £415; at the Royal Air Force
College at Cranwell, £529, and at the
Staff College, £650 per head. Are the
latter figures not capable of drastic reductions? Is economy to be practised
solely on those least able to bear it?
see
Behind these proposals one can
stalking through the Province poverty
and wretchedness. In many poor homes
there will be a grim struggle to keep
the wolf from the door. The workers
are begging for work or bread. The Government is giving them stones. They
are begging for fish. The Government is
giving them scorpions. The standard of
living of the working classes is 45 per
cent, above the standard in France. Are
the Government going to haul down the
standard below the French standard?
Are they going to haul down the British
Flag as it were?
At the same time the middle classes
are being ground between the upper millstone of Imperial taxation, and the lower
millstone of Ulster taxation. I read
over a flesher's shop last week the
notice, "If you are not properly butchered
already, step inside." (Laughter.) That
will soon be the invitation of the Minister of Finance (Mr. Pollock) to the
Ulsterman after Mr. Snowden has
finished his major operation on the Ulster
taxpayer. The present position in Northern Ireland, as across the water, is
bad, but the prospect is worse.
Unemployment exists mainly in the industries
in these islands which produce predominantly for export. The depressions
in these basic export industries is deepening. Competing nations overseas
exploiting their own great natural resources and are content with lower wages
are
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
25 SEPTEMBER 1931
and lower standards of living. The proofs
of a serious and continuous slowing down
of British productivity are overwhelming.
This is a far-reaching, probably a permanent change. What will be the end
of it all? People are saving less and
spending more than before the war. The
balance of trade is surging relentlessly
adverse. They may balance finance.
Can they balance trade? Is the taxpayer of Ulster to be inexorably bound
like one of the fabled figures of
ancient mythology Ixion to the wheel of
British taxation? The strain of British
and Ulster taxation is nearing the
breaking point. The total public expenditure of the United Kingdom in the
financial year before the
£197,000,000. The estimated British
expenditure this year is £885,000,000.
war was
There is one part of the Bill I regret
to see. The machinery of Orders in
Council proposed in the Bill is ultra-
Parliamentary and savours of a dictatorship. It might be pardonable in a grave
emergency in time of war. It is a dangerous precedent in time of peace, and
should not be followed on a future
occasion. Then, again, the working
classes will feel that they are being
branded as paupers by the power to assess
the needs test for transitional benefits
being vested in Boards of Guardians.
Honest poverty-and there is such a
thing even now-will hang its head in
shame at the idea of being associated
with the stigma of pauperism and workhouse relief. In many Ulster homes it
used to be the boast that not one of
their name ever was in the workhouse
or took workhouse relief. I think that
sense of pride ought to be cherished and
cultivated in our midst. That home-bred
honest sense of self-respect and independence is to the credit of our people, and
we should not do anything at all to wear
it down. I do submit that it would be
a wise thing, now that we have the Minister of Labour here, that steps should be
taken as far as can be to guard our
workmen from actual contact with workhouse surroundings.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
cannot say it is with any pleasure that
I rise to support the Second Reading of
this Bill. Indeed, it is with very great
regret I find the position so bad that
such legislation is necessary.
But we
have got to admit, although we have been
looking forward month after month and
year after year to a greater improvement
in the trade and business of the country,
we are, it appears, now down to the very
lowest and very worst position possible.
A great deal has been said about the
cut in the unemployment benefit, but
even the trade unions have been compelled to cut the benefit off from some
of the most necessitous cases. There is
one very old trade union which, without
any warning whatever, sent instructions
to the secretaries to cease the payment
of superannuation benefit. What could
be more drastic than that? A man who
has paid 40 years' contributions for oldage pension or superannuation from the
society is to be denied it and left in actual
want in many cases. But I do not blame
the society. Their funds are expended;
they are in such a position they have got
to do something. Another very important trade union, the engineering union,
have recently deprived members of the
low benefit, that is, the reduced benefit,
and have put 6d per week on the contributions of every member of the union.
That goes to prove that the state of the
country is terrible, and it is not the Government of the country only that is
taking action. These trade unions have
been compelled to try to save themselves
from being completely submerged and
put out of existence.
Unfortunately, at the present time we
have no alternative. No alternative has
been put forward unless we are prepared
to drift irrespective of what the consequences may be, and so far as I can see
the consequences would be very serious,
if Northern Ireland were to allow the
matter to go irrespective of the action
of the Imperial Parliament. In the first
place, it would break the agreement that
exists between the Imperial Government
and us, that is, the Re-insurance Agreзment. I know that when the Minister
of Labour was able to make that arrangement he was applauded all over the Six
Counties. Nobody recognised more than
the workers that he had done something
wonderfully good for the benefit of the
working classes. It would be disastrous
for us to say we should allow the matter
to drift and separate ourselves from the
Imperial Parliament.
Then another thing you have got to
remember is that the industrial position
here is very much linked up with that of
the United Kingdom, and it would be
Economy Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
almost impossible to separate.
All our
trade unions are controlled and governed
from London, Manchester, and Newcastle. We are all under the same rules
and regulations and the safe benefits and,
therefore, it would be disastrous to make
any change in the unemployment benefit.
I do not think any person would be sane
who would recommend separation at the
present moment, because it would only
leave the workers in a worse position. It
is only for that reason, I assure you, I
intend to support the Second Reading of
the Bill, because, while it is a severe blow
to the unemployed worker, at the same
time it might prevent him being much
worse. That is something to look forward to.
a
I recognise as well as anyone else that
the reduction of 2/9 a week in a married
man's unemployment benefit means
great loss to him. I have some experience.
I have worked for small wages.
I have
gone out at five to six in the morning and
looked for a job for a few weeks. I have
had to depend on unemployment benefit.
I know what it is. I know that 2/9 will
be a tremendous sacrifice for the unemployed worker to make. But as I said
before, it is better to lose 2/9 than to
find in the course of a short time-
Mr. BARCLAY: It is 1/9.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It is
2s 9d for a married person. There is one
shilling reduction on the wife's allowance.
It is 2s 9d for the married man. We
have all along kept pace and step by step
with Great Britain. When they improved the benefits and removed the
restrictions I am very pleased to say the
Northern Government immediately took
the same step. When the family income
restriction was there they paid benefit
only when the test would allow it. The
Northern Government removed that
restriction immediately the Imperial
Government did it.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: It was a great
mistake.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
When the not-genuinely-seeking-work
restriction was removed by the Imperial
Government, the Northern Government
removed it also. They have seen that
the workers in Northern Ireland are in
no way worse on account of their residence
in Northern Ireland than they would
have been had they been in Great
Britain. Now, unfortunately, England
is in a very bad position, but I think it
would be most unwise and injudicious to
attempt to separate the unemployment
benefit and to say we will go our own
way. I am perfectly sure that the
workers here will say, "We are prepared
to do our bit and suffer in the hope that
times will soon improve and be better."
I am well aware that many delegates
connected with the trade unions to-day
are opposed to this action being taken by
our Government, but they have never
yet said how it will be possible to improve the financial position or get over
the difficulty, although I have seen it
stated that men preaching at street
corners put themselves forward as being
more competent to deal with this financial
crisis than even our Government and
their financial experts. I am satisfied
that our Government
are taking the
matter carefully and thoroughly into consideration. I am sure they have had
consultations with their financial advisers
to discover if there are any other means
out of the difficulty.
We have to recognise this fact that even
in the Imperial Parliament they could.
find no other way out of the difficulty.
Mr. Ramsay MacDonald, a man who
spent a lifetime in building up the Labour
party, separated himself from his
colleagues, and said there was no alternative only that adopted by the Imperial
Parliament, and I certainly would be
very much inclined to be guided by him.
and those who advised him in preference
to those preaching at the street corners.
I also recognise men like Jim Thomas, a
man who spent his lifetime in the interests of the railway workers, and who
obtained for that class the best conditions
of all workers. He, too, has had to
separate himself from his colleagues
simply because he was bound conscientiously to advocate a reduction of unemployment benefit for the country in this
terrible crisis. If any guide is required,
even for the street orator, these men in
my opinion should be taken into consideration.
I feel very sorry when I find some of
those who are associated with trade
unions endeavouring to persuade the
workers to revolt against the attitude
adopted by the Government, and to take
some drastic steps. I am satisfied in so
doing they are not advising the men wisely
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
25 SEPTEMBER 1931
or correctly. When I was connected
with a trade union, and had to give a
report in regard to any delegation I may
have been on, I always felt it my duty to
give a truthful and correct statement,
and not by any means an exaggerated one.
In the present case we find these men
exaggerating the position in such a way
that they try to convey to the workers
the impression that the Government are
their enemies. I am as satisfied to-day
as ever I was that the Northern Government are as much concerned about the
welfare of the workers as anyone, and I
am perfectly sure that the best will be
done by our Government.
Reference has been made to the question of administration being placed in the
hands of the Poor-law Guardians. I do
not feel that there is any danger there
such as has been represented by some of
those who have spoken on the subject.
It has to be admitted that even the Poorlaw Guardians are democratically elected
representatives of the people. They have
had a very unpleasant duty to perform
for a number of years past in giving
out poor-law relief, and in trying to
decide who were genuine and who were
impostors. Notwithstanding that, they
have come in for a great deal of abuse
and criticism. At the same time I am
sure that when they come to administer
the transitional benefit, they will conduct
and carry out the business in a strictly
honest and honourable way. I know a
number of members on the Poor-law Board
in Belfast, and several of them are also
members of the City Council. The people
thought so much of these men that they
sent them as their representatives on both
bodies. Surely these men are going to
have as much respect for the poor as you
or I, or even those at the street corner?
I sincerely hope that they will treat the
claimants as liberally as they possibly
can.
There is only one clause in the Bill to
which I would draw the attention of Mr.
Andrews. It is certainly causing a great
deal of uneasiness amongst local authorities. There is a clause in the Bill which
states:
-
"any such Order may make provision
for imposing duties and conferring powers on local authorities in
connection with the administration of
any such service, for the performance
or exercise of any such duties or powers
by committees, sub-committees and
officers of local authorities."
For
So far as I can understand "local
authorities' in Belfast, and all over the
North of Ireland, mean the local authorities in any particular locality.
instance, in Belfast the City Council is
the local authority. The Water Board
is a local authority. The Poor-law Board
is also a local authority. The clause is
very vague. Those connected with the
City Council are very much afraid owing
to the phraseology of the sub-section that
it may be open for the Governor to commandeer the services of the officers of
local authorities, or instruct committees
and sub-committees to perform the duties
which he may require done. I would be
very glad if Mr. Andrews would make it
perfectly clear whether it is intended to
apply to the City Council. If it is it
will be necessary to put down an Amendment to make it perfectly clear. I am
glad to see the Minister here, and I am
sure he will be able satisfactorily to reply
to any queries put to him.
Mr. DORMAN: The hon. Member who
has just spoken seems to have street
orators on the brain. I would respectfully
remind him that the great men of the
past who have made their mark and footprints on human history are the men who
began at the street corner, and that the
founder of the common religion which we
all profess was pre-eminently distinguished
in propagating His glorious good news at
the street corners of His native land.
even fifty years' study of this particular
question authorises an individual to stand
up in any assembly and express an opinion
then I claim to be capable of expressing
an opinion upon the question which is
here before us to-day. There is a crisis.
What is this crisis? Who has caused this
crisis? How have they caused this crisis?
And why have they caused this crisis?
If
Those are questions I would put to the
right hon. Gentleman who will reply to
the criticisms that will be offered to this
Measure this afternoon. I have been endeavouring to ascertain from the public
Press what this crisis is and how it has
arisen, and I have come to the conclusion
that it is practically impossible for any
ordinary citizen to get a clear perception
of what it is. In my own belief it has
arisen from the fact that the nations of
the world have permitted willy-nilly their
medium of exchange and standard of
value to be controlled and manipulated
in the interests of a certain section of the
community. They control and manipulate that which is necessary in a civilisa-
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
tion such as we have, where the multiplicity of products necessary to supply home
needs from day to day is so great that it
is utterly impossible for us to exchange
them by swopping or bartering, and so
we must have some medium to enable us
to carry on in a civilised country from
day to day. All the governments of the
world have allowed the shareholders in
limited liability companies to manipulate
that medium of exchange in their own
interests, not giving due consideration to
the needs of the industrialists, producers
and consumers. It was reported in the
Press that in ten days thirty-two millions
of gold was withdrawn from the Bank of
England, and that depleted the reserves
of gold so much that a crisis was confronting us, and the value of the pound was
bound to drop.
Seeing that the Bank of England were
using their deposits for the purpose of
grant credit on long term loans at
more per cent. than the bank gave, depositors who had placed their money on
deposit with the bank naturally said,
"These people are getting five per cent.
profit on our deposits though they guaranteed to pay us on demand, and we will
demand our money back." They demanded it back, and according to the
Press the funds of the bank were depleted
by 32 millions in about ten days. Those
who controlled our medium of exchange
were using that medium and credit of
long-term loans for the benefit of their
former enemies, the Germans, and other
countries that were our enemies in the
Great War. Because of that maladministration of our medium of exchange a crisis
confronts the people, and the poorest of
the poor are asked to make sacrifices
which before I have finished I will show
are entirely unreasonable, unjust and
uncalled for on the part of this or any
other Government.
Now what has happened? In face of
this crisis across the water an National
Government is established. I would call
it a conglomerate Government, the most
heterogeneous collection of statesmen that
has ever been called together for the purpose of governing this or any other
country, since we evolved from a state of
primitive savagery. It is made up of
Liberals and Tories, Free Traders and
Protectionists, Prohibitionists and Anti-
Prohibitionists, Socialists and Trade
Unionists, and men of every political
opinion who cannot cohere any more than
oil and water can cohere. There was some
jusitfication for the calling of a National
Government across the water when the
Government saw that any measure they
recommended to the people's representatives might not be approved of, but I say
there was no justification for putting
these extraordinary and unconstitutional
measures into the hands of an irresponsible body, away from the elected representatives of the people in the Parliament of this particular area. I am surprised that a Government backed up by
those who always claimed to be constitutionalists and lovers of civil and religious
freedom, that a Government of that
character, with an overwhelming majority
in both Houses of Parliament should find
it necessary to relinquish their Parliamentary rights to the Privy Council with
the Governor at its head, seeing that when
a crisis arose in the past within two or
three hours both Houses backed up the
Government in the request they made to
preserve order in the area.
Much has been said about sacrifices,
and I am going to quote not the wild
statement of a man from the street corner
but the Revenue Blue Book, and I am
going to show that the trend of affairs
for the last six years docs not justify this
Government or the Government in Great
Britain asking the poor, and especially
the unemployed, to make a sacrifice of any
percentage of their meagre allowances.
I find from that year book that during the
year 1920-1921 the salaries assessable for
income tax, including the Free State,
amounted to £429,000,000, and that the
income of the wage earners assessable for
income tax was £863,000,000. In the
process of the succeeding four years, leaving out the Free State, the income of the
salaried portion of the community was inflated to £710,000,000; in other words
those who paid income tax on salaries
during the four years succeeding 1920-
1921 have had an increase of £281,000,000,
while the weekly wage earners in the
year 1920-1921 paid income tax on
£863,000,000, and in the year 1924-1925
on £311,000,000. This means they had
been subject to a decrease in the income
of their homes of £552,000,000 in four
years.
These are not the statements of a street
corner agitator, but they are extracts
from the Government Revenue Blue
Book. All this information has been col-
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
25 SEPTEMBER 1931
lected by income-tax officials, who make a
scrupulous investigation into the income
of every individual subject to income tax.
Apart from that, the figures of the
"Labour Gazette" go to prove that during the last eight or nine years the weekly
wage-earners have had their income
reduced by a sum of over £700,000,000 a
year, and now, because these men and
women cannot get an opportunity of
selling their labour power, the sale of
which enables them to live from day to
day, they must willy-nilly be asked to
submit to a further reduction in their
meagre income.
Then this crisis is said to be connected
with unemployment. I do not wish, seeing the various stages of this Bill have to
be passed through this House to-day, to
occupy the time to the House to any
considerable length, but it is necessary, I
think, that these facts should be brought
before the minds of hon. Members so
that they should be convinced that there
is no justification for asking a corresponding sacrifice from the low-paid workers of
our community in view of the figures I
have already quoted from the Revenue
Blue Book. But what is unemployment?
Unemployment is an inherent part of
our present system of society. I will not
attempt to occupy the time of the House
by describing how that comes further than
to quote from a man of international
reputation, whose findings I do not think
any reasonable man will question. I am
quoting from Sir Charles Booth, the celebrated social investigator, and I am quoting his expression made in the year 1896
with regard to unemployment. These
are the words that learned and humane
man made use of:
"Our modern system will not work
without some unemployed margin, some
reserve of labour. Thus it will be seen
that Capital actually flourishes on the
unemployed; for wealth is rooted in
Labour's poverty, and the workers are
mere counters in Mammon's game of
greed. Capital, therefore, has need of
the unemployed to maintain its supremacy over Labour."
There is another aspect of this Measure
I wish to refer to, and that is the stigma
of pauperism which, whether hon. Members recognise it or not, will operate in
the minds of the vast mass of the people
of this area. I have followed with close
attention from the early 'nineties of last
century, when the "Labour Gazette" was
first published, the returns of pauperism
per 10,000 of the population in Great
Britain and Ireland, and I was struck
from year to year with the fact that the
lowest percentage practically throughout
the whole period came from the district
of the Belfast Board of Guardians irrespective of the condition of trade. I
would not be in order in criticising that
elected body, but the Minister of Labour
will know that it was largely due to the
mercenary manner in which they have
treated applications for outdoor relief
through the whole of the years of this
present century, that he had to make an
appeal to them to put a Section of the
Local Government Act of 1898 into force
in this area so as to alleviate the sufferings of the unemployed who had been
turned off the Labour Exchange.
It was because of the meagre reply
given to the destitute poor who were desirous of keeping their homes intact and
avoiding the possibility of having the
stigma of pauperism flung at their children by being told in the public thoroughfare that their father was a pauper and
was once in the workhouse. Yesterday
morning I was speaking to a man who,
for over 40 years, has been a stalwart
supporter of the Government, and he said,
"I am now unemployed, but if I am to
die in the gutter I will never make application for anything connected with the
poor law in Belfast for myself or my
family."
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: He
is getting soft in the head.
He
Mr. DORMAN: He may be getting
soft in the head, but I do not think so.
Perhaps there are many of your supporters who are soft in the head. I speak as
one who aspired to enter into that august
assembly, and during the week I endeavoured to get elected to it there was
an ex-Service man living close by me.
had a wife and five children. He made
application for outdoor relief, and was
rewarded with the amount of £1 per
week, his rent being 6s. I 'phoned to
the Dogs' Home, and asked them what
was their charge for putting up a dog for
mẹ for a fortnight, and they told me 6s,
and that the charge was 3s for a cat.
asked if I sent half-a-dozen dogs would
they make any rebate, and they said no.
It would cost me £2 2s to house and feed
seven dogs for a week, and this body to
whom, either directly or indirectly, the
administration of the needs benefit is to
I
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
be referred is the body that thought £1
sufficient for seven Christians. I do not
wish to enter exhaustively into that, but
last week it cost the Poor-law Guardians
only 7s 44d for the upkeep of every inmate,
or 9s 8d and a fraction of a penny for
establishment expenses. They were purchasing the food at wholesale prices.
that rate it would take £2 10s a week to
keep a similar number of individuals outside as free self-respecting citizens.
At
I want to ask the right hon. Gentleman
a question? How is this needs test to be
applied? Assume a young man of 26
years of age, who, perhaps for six or
eight years has stinted himself in order
to lay by the necessary funds to establish
a home, is turned off transitional benefit.
Will the investigation on the part of this
Committee into his needs be such that
they will conclude that while he has that
sum of, say, £20, for the establishment
of a home intact he is not entitled to
obtain this needs benefit in the transition
period? Or take the case of the father of
a family who is 34 or 36 years of age, and
who has been zealously laying by sufficient
to pass his children through a secondary
school or otherwise to give them a superior
education, and with that object in view
as succeeded in accumulating £150 or
£200. When the Committee investigate
his needs will they be authorised and enitled to say to the man, "Until you
absorb and spend this £150 or £200 which
you have gathered together for the purpose of the future welfare of your
offspring you can get no relief”?
Are
such persons to be told they must first
exhaust the provision which they made.
for their families before they will receive
the recommendation of this committee?
What we have to consider is that the
Poor-law Board has not been established
for the purpose of dealing with the
question of poverty. It only recognises
destitution, and the mentality of all those
connected with the poor law, north, south,
east and west, in this country is that no
person who has any property-in fact I
think it is the legal view-is entitled to
receive relief as a destitute citizen. I
believe this Measure is going to inflict a
grievous injury upon the most helpless.
part of the community, who, for the past
ten years, have made enormous sacrifices
in reductions of wages, and I express my
regret that the Government has seen its
way to relinquish its Parliamentary position and hand it over to a committee of
the Privy Council or any other body. I
do not know how the subordinate lodges
of the Orange Order, who glory in our
Constitution, will support the Government in this reactionary step by which it
is usurping to itself not the divine right
of kings but the divine right of the Privy
Council.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: The
only criticism which I desire to offer at
this stage has reference to that one Clause
in the Bill which deals with the setting
up of committees, and which refers to
local authorities. I know that this Bill
has been drafted from the English Bill,
but I wish to point out to my right hon.
Friend (Mr. Andrews) that the positions
are somewhat different. In England you
have the public assistance committee,
which is really a committee of the local
authority, but here in Belfast, say, the
Poor Law Guardians are no more a local
authority than the Harbour Board, the
Water Board, or the Belfast Corporation,
which have been referred to by my hon.
Friend (Mr. Joseph Cunningham).
I want an assurance from my right hon.
Friend that the Bill will exclude authorities such as borough councils, county
councils, or rural district councils. I want
the Government to make a definite pronouncement as to who is to be the local
authority and who is to carry out this
work. I understand that it is to be the
Board of Guardians. If it is to be the
Board of Guardians, why not say so definitely? It does not say so in the Bill,
and so leaves an uncertainty, which to
my mind ought to be cleared up in order
to prevent any misunderstanding in the
future. If my right hon. Friend can give
me the undertaking that this refers only
to the boards of guardians, and will make
that definite, then I am perfectly satisfied,
but if not, I will be forced to move
an amendment at a later stage.
Mr. LESLIE: It had been my intention
when I entered this House this afternoon
to preface my remarks on the Bill before
us by a few words of welcome addressed
to the Measure in question. Since then.
the Bill has been attacked by the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) on
my left, and by another hon. Senator
(Mr. Dorman), whom I do not at the
moment see in his place. In spite of those
attacks, I can see no reason to depart
from my original intention of welcoming
this Bill. It may be that I should have
felt myself deflected from that course
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
25 SEPTEMBER 1931
had either of these hon. Senators to whom
I have referred-and both of whom, I
may mention, admitted the existence of a
crisis-offered any constructive alternative
to that which is before us now.
Both hon. Senators referred to the
people who were to be oppressed by the
reduction of the unemployment benefit as
the poorest of the poor. Living, as I do,
in a rural district, where the average
wage of the agricultural labourer does not
amount either to 27s 3d or to 34s, and
where the wage of that labourer is calculated without the least regard to the fact
that he is married, and has or has not this
or that number of children, I cannot
admit that a man receiving 27s 3d per
week is of the poorest of the poor. Again,
in listening to the same hon. Senators, I
failed to detect any recognition of the
fact that wages, after all, are nothing but
the counters of production and of supply,
and are very often of far less importance
to the worker than are other conditions of
employment.
Consequently, as I have said, I welcome
this Bill as an Economy Bill in the truest
sense of the word. We might, indeed,
call it a Further Economy Bill, because
the noble Viscount who introduced the
Bill reminded us that certain economies
now being started across the water were
set on foot on this side when our Budget
was before the appropriate assembly in
the month of May. But economy means
-and we have an excellent example of
it in the Bill before us-the ordering of
our household, whether our private household or the household of the State, and
the Measures to be taken now are to be
undertaken in order to avoid greater
disaster overtaking the State, a disaster
similar apparently in character, though
more far-reaching in results, to that
which, according to my hon. Friend on
my left, has already overtaken one if not
more of the trade union councils.
The intention under the Bill is that
borrowing should stop. The exact amount
of borrowing which we have incurred
during recent times is, I think, given in
the White Paper to which reference has
been made. It certainly was given in
another place by the Minister of Finance
(Mr. Pollock). As far as I remember
he said that arrangements had been made
for reducing the old debt of, I think,
£3,434,000, and that these arrangements
had been SO far effective that only
£2,800,000 is now due on that account.
But he also directed our attention to
another side of the question, that is the
new debt which has been accumulated,
and which, I think, amounts at the
moment to very nearly £2,500,000; that
is to say, it is rapidly approaching the
limit of the advances for that purpose
which were set by Parliament some few
months ago.
That borrowing is to stop, and the
immediate expenditure which will be entailed in supplying the deficit due to the
cessation of borrowing is, as I understand, expected to be met partly by the
reduction of expenditure and partly from
the proceeds of new taxes imposed under
our financial system by the Imperial
Parliament and not by ourselves, namely,
increased taxes on petrol, beer, and
tobacco, and variations adverse to the taxpayers in the case of both income tax and
surtax. Those additional taxes will
impose great sacrifices on many people.
We have heard much of the sacrifices of
the insured persons. We have not heard
much this afternoon, or during the past
few days, of the heavy sacrifices which
will be entailed on the small person who
has hitherto been accustomed, if a married
man, to enjoy £250 of his income free of
tax through the reduction of that sum to
£150. We have not heard much about
that. There are other matters also in
which pressure will bear more heavily
than it has done before. These are great
sacrifices and we hope that these new
taxes are going to provide us with the
£300,000 necessary to bring columns I
and III of the last sheet of the White
Paper into consonance.
The purposes of this Bill are most
clearly defined. Economy is mentioned
in the first Clause of the Bill. I believe
that the financial arrangements I have
mentioned will be sufficient to balance
matters, and to put things straight for the
financial year 1931-32. But I look forward almost with trepidation to the position in 1932-33. We have not to cross
that bridge this afternoon, but I feel that
before I sit down I ought to say a few
words about it, though I will postpone
speaking on that subject for a few more
minutes as there are other matters to
which I wish to direct attention.
It has been said-not very much in
this House but to a great extent outsidethat the procedure which the Bill suggests should be adopted is entirely unusual, and I am afraid some people have
gone so far as to say it is unconstitutional.
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I refer to the proposed procedure by
means of Order in Council rather than
by direct statutory enactment in Parliament. Admittedly the course which is
to be taken is an abnormal course, and is
not a course which I should myself favour
in ordinary circumstances. It is almost
worth while to spend a minute or SO
examining what an Order in Council
really is. An Order in Council is simply
a not ill-defined channel of conveying to
the people the wishes or the commands
of the Sovereign in respect of matters
which, whether by common law or by
statutory enactment, have been committed
to the executive authority of the Crown.
What the Government in this Bill are
asking Parliament to do is to give definite
statutory authority to put certain matters
under the executive authority. That is
all there is to it. It differs very little
from a not uncommon procedure in Acts
of Parliament, that is to say, the very
common provision that this or that Act
of Parliament shall not come into operation until the appointed day, which shall
be such day as may be provided by his
Grace in Council. It is necessary that
Parliament should be acquainted of the
procedure which is contemplated before
it consents to a matter like this.
has been done here.
That
There is, therefore, no difficulty whatever, to my mind, in accepting this proposal which seems to me an entirely
different proposal from that form of legislation by proclamation to which I think
the hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) on my
left referred, which
occurred in the reign of his late Majesty
King Henry VIII in a benighted
country across the water and which
-of course it was not definitely
stated in the statute-did not apply
to Ireland, and which was afterwards repealed. That, at all events,
is a different matter. There is precedent
both in Ireland and in England for the
action that is to be taken. It will be
seen that the control of Parliament is
most adequately to be maintained if hon.
Senators, or those who object to the Bill,
will take the trouble to study the Bill and
to reflect that Parliament does not vote
money save from year to year. There is
not really any new principle. Parliament
will have complete control over whatever
steps are taken under this law.
Passing for a moment to the details of
the Bill, I approve of the slight reduction
in the cost of health insurance. I still
am rather afraid that, owing to not very
far distant legislation, we must expend
still more money in the matter of health
insurance in the Province. However, I
think I can claim to have no responsibility
in that regard, but I do think that on
this occasion we should be ready to thank
the doctors for the ready way in which
they have fallen in with the proposals
made to assist in the national emergency.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
It
I approve also of the paragraph to
which attention has already been drawn,
paragraph (b), conferring power on the
local authorities. It seems to me that on
this occasion no more apt phrase could
possibly have been inserted in the Bill
than that phrase, local authorities.
is an expression which some may remember I have occasionally had to attack for
various reasons, but here it has advantages. The Board of Guardians is most
undoubtedly a local authority; there is
no question whatever about that.
point is that the power should be kept as
wide as possible. The expression "Board
of Guardians" does not occur in the Bill
at all, and the intention is, as I understand, to follow what are called Public
Assistance Committees in England. I
think the Lord Mayor referred to that
point.
reason,
The
Public Assistance Committees usually
consist of Boards of Guardians with coopted members. The expression "Public
Assistance Committee" is now in use in
England, but the Public Assistance Committee is a body hitherto unknown in
Scotland. There seems no
I
think, why if such procedure is likely to
be effective in Scotland it should not be
equally effective over here. I do not
myself believe that you could get the work
better done than by people connected
with the people in the locality, and therefore I support the expression as it stands.
I also support the proposal contained in
the White Paper-it is not in the Bill—
to have a test of means.
The money
available is not unlimited. I hope everyone will recognise that there is only a
comparatively limited amount available,
and that Parliament must pause before it
votes sums which it could not find; but
at all events a means test seems to provide the best practical method of ensuring that what money there is goes to
wherever it is most required.
There may be troubles here and there.
Administrative problems will arise. Let
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
25 SEPTEMBER 1931
us call them problems rather than difficulties. We must depend for success on the
goodwill of those who work the scheme,
and I believe that that goodwill will not
be wanting. I think the Minister of
Labour will bear me out when I say that
the Belfast Board of Guardians, the local
authority most concerned in this matter,
has already given the most reliable and
satisfactory assurance of its intention to
assist to the utmost of its power. I also
agree with the proposals to
anomalies, some of which, perhaps, have
not been untainted with fraud.
remove
I dislike the increased contributions,
but I cannot in the circumstances object
to them. The House may remember that
when unemployment insurance was first
started in Great Britain, in the year 1911,
the contribution of the employed contributor was 2d per week and that of the
employer the same. The contribution of
the State was one-third of the combined
contribution, that is to say, the State
contribution twenty years ago was limited
to 2s 3d, and now it is proposed to raise it
to 10d. As regards the employer, the
immediate rise is 2d, and we must recognise that that will impose a further handicap on our employers in competing with
foreign manufacturers.
I earnestly hope, as we all do, that the
figures in the last column of the last sheet
of the White Paper will-I cannot say
be falsified by events, because I know
they have been prepared as honestly as
they could have been prepared, but that
they will not be borne out by events, that
is to say, we must hope that the rate of
unemployment will fall below 30 per cent.
If it does not, and if the expenditure is
maintained on the level contemplated by
the Bill before us, it will be necessary
for us to find some £900,000 in the next
financial year, and, as I make out, we
shall only receive some £62,000 from Great
Britain towards equalisation. One cannot regard with equanimity the possibility
of this Province having to find an
additional £900,000.
I am afraid that if the day comes when
that has to be done we shall have to
admit that the day has at last come, the
day against which we have all fought to
our utmost, in particular the Minister of
Finance, .that day when we must say that
whatever they may be able to do in
England we cannot afford to keep in line.
It is the last thing anybody wants to do,
but it may be that we shall find ourselves
in such a position that we cannot carry on
It
at the rates paid across the water.
will be a most lamentable thing; we shall
all regret it, and I am sure we shall do
all we can to avoid it, but it may be that
the figures will be too strong for us. It is
not easy trying to live on borrowed
money when people cannot be found to
lend the money, and we must remember that the returns show that our taxable capacity is only about 47 per cent.
per head of that in Great Britain. I
hope that the day will never come when
we will have to make the change, but I
am afraid I must say that we cannot dismiss from our minds the possibility that
we may be driven irresistibly in that
direction. For now, at all events, I
accept the Bill. I believe it will be in
the interests of the Province and I may
say that the Bill is most perfectly worded
and adapted to meet the conditions that
exist, and with which it purports to deal.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I think it would
be desirable to contrast the methods
adopted by this Government and the
methods adopted by the British Government when this crisis occurred.
The British Government, notwithstanding its tremendous resources and
its vast population, felt that the
proper thing to do and the only practical
thing to do was to secure the active cooperation of all interests concerned in
England, and a National Government
was formed for the purpose, composed of
all shades of political thought in England.
I have always held two predominating
opinions with regard to Northern Ireland
since I came into public life, and one was
that in this small area, with a population
of only a million and a quarter people,
we could never maintain our position
without the whole-hearted support and
assistance of all the people in Northern
Ireland. The other was the necessity of
promoting the farming industry, which is
our basic industry.
What does the Northern Government
do in this crisis? They profess nominally
to adopt the same system as was adopted
in England, of delegating the powers of
Parliament to the Privy Council, but
instead of endeavouring to secure the cooperation of the minority, of one-third
of the people in Northern Ireland, they
shut them out from having a voice in
legislation. We are not represented in
this House by the numbers that our population entitles us to. But what happens
now? There is no method by which the
minority of one-third of the population
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
you
want
in Northern Ireland can voice their grievances, protect their interests, and point
out their requirements. I think that is
a very serious step to take. If
to promote Communism, if you want to
play into the hands of the agitator, if
you want to stifle the voice of Parliament,
stifle the voice of the moderate man,
create suspicion in moderate minds, and
then you will give the best assistance you
can to the promotion of Communism and
extremism in Northern Ireland and
elsewhere.
I do say that it is a very unfortunate
thing that the Government should have
adopted this means. There is another
aspect of this matter that I should like
to draw attention to before I sit down.
I protest against this proposal to hand
over to the Poor-law Guardians the
administration that should be carried on
by Parliament. I quite admit that I
have a grave suspicion that this is a deliberate attempt on the part of the Government to evade an unpleasant and an
unpopular duty that might have farreaching consequences to them when they
appealed to the country. Why should
not the Government with their own resources and with their own staff carry out
this work? No reason that I have seen
has been given either here or elsewhere
why the Government should delegate this
one unpopular power to the local authorities, who, I am afraid, are foolish
enough and simple enough to take up a
duty which the Government themselves
do not want to discharge.
I should like to hear from the Minister
of Labour, whom we are all glad to see,
some explanation as to why the Government say they cannot discharge this
duty. They will undertake to pay the
money, but the Board of Guardians have
to say when the money cannot be paid.
I am quite earnest and frank in this
matter when I say this is a duty which
the Government should not delegate and
shirk; this is a duty they should have
carried out themselves. It has been said
in another place that there is no brand of
pauperism about this, and that the unemployed should not resent this manner
of investigating their means because income-tax payers are subjected to the same
kind of investigation. I submit with
perfect confidence that there is no
analogy. In the one case the official,
unknown to all business men, and interested in nothing except how much
money he can get out of a man, makes
the investigation. He is probably a man
of the strictest probity, but, on the other
hand, you may have perhaps an unfriendly
neighbour, a man who may have some
prejudice or bias, and he goes round and
investigates the circumstances of his
neighbours. That is casting the brand of
pauperism on these people, and to that I
strongly object.
There is another class of the community
that requires very great sympathy, and
that is the class the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) has referred to.
After all, the middle or lower middle
classes have always had to bear the greater
part of the burden of taxation, and I am
afraid that in consequence of these
economies an additional burden will be
put upon their shoulders which in a great
many instances they will not be able to
bear. In my opinion some of this new
taxation, instead of assisting to balance
the Budget, will decrease the purchasing
power of the community, and there will
be a falling off in revenue instead of an
increase. I know that that opinion is
shared by those who have more experience
and a better knowledge of how these new
taxes are likely to operate than I have.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I have no doubt that
we all deplore the necessity for the earlier
calling together of the Senate. A great
national crisis has been averted. If the
crisis had not been speedily dealt with
there would have been no unemployment
benefit cut, for there would have
been no unemployment benefit at
all.
We have been asked to-day
to agree to the unemployment benefit
cut, and while I much regret that this
deduction has to be made, I do not see
that we can do otherwise in the very exceptional circumstances, and considering
the gravity of the situation that has
arisen.
Indeed, I believe that a great
many of the unemployed and employed
working people fully realise and understand that the cut in unemployment
benefit could not be avoided. I believe
the working people approve of the steps
that have been taken to save the country
from financial disaster, and recognise that
the action of the Government is in their
interest.
The hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) in his speech, which I listened
to most attentively, referred to the great
hardship that will have to be borne by
the working people. I agree with him,
Economy Bill—
Second Reading
25 SEPTEMBER 1931
but he never said one word about the
middle class or any other class that are
bearing the burden at the present time
and will do so to an even greater extent
in the future.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I expressly referred
to the middle classes.
Mr. QUIGLEY: We are interested in
all classes of the people. One would
think from Senator Campbell's speech
that we had an unlimited reservoir of
wealth to pay unemployment benefit,
and at all times. We have not got such
a reservoir, and the hon. and learned
Senator has not yet told us where
we will get it. It is not possible
for us to pay that which we have
not got, but we are doing the
very
best it is possible to do for the people
in the circumstances that exist at present.
We regret very much that this cut has
got to be made, and I think it is a great
pity that the people should not be left
alone and uninfluenced, because I am
satisfied there are a great many people in
the Province who are quite agreeable to
the cuts in the special circumstances that
prevail. Senator Dorman referred to all
classes of politicians being concerned in
this matter. I agree with him, but I
would also point out to him that a great
many of his own class approved of many
of the cuts that have been made. Some
of them gave their approval at a particular time, but afterwards ran away and
left the task to other men who stuck to
their guns nobly, irrespective of the consequences.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): If I may I would like in the
first place to congratulate hon. Members
who have spoken on the reasonable,
eloquent, and impersonal way in which
they each and all have expressed their
opinions to-day. A great many statements have been made, of course, with
which I cannot be expected to agree, but
I do appreciate more than I can say the
moderate way in which these views have
been expressed. I think I may say that
we all regret the necessity for cutting unemployment benefit. We all regret
having to impose more strict conditions
for the receipt of that benefit, and I
think that, as reasonable men, if we look
upon the world's affairs as well as our
own nation's affairs, we must all realise
that economies are inevitable if disaster is
not to befall us. It is upon that foundation that the National Government has.
proceeded, and it is upon that foundation
that we have followed the National Government.
ways,
In connection with unemployment
insurance it was inevitable that we
should follow because of our reinsurance agreement. In this world
we cannot have things both
and we have got either to support
our re-insurance agreement and express
approval of it or to condemn it and have
the courage to say it was a mistake bringing it into existence, and it ought to be
discontinued. I asked hon. Members in
the other House yesterday if one of them
would come forward and say our re-insurance agreement should be discontinued.
I gave them the opportunity to rise in
their places and express their views on
that matter and on that matter alone, and
not one of them did it. What follows
from that? If we maintain the reinsurance agreement, which has brought
approximately six millions to our Unemployment Fund in the last few years, we
must conform to the conditions of that
agreement, which are just and reasonable
as between the citizens of the United
Kingdom.
Those conditions are that there should
be parity both as regards legislation and
administration. Therefore all this talk
about the wrong-doing of the Government
in conforming to the British conditions is
mere camouflage if we are of opinion
that it is in the best interests of our working people, as I believe it to be, that we
should maintain this all-important agreement. I venture to say that is a complete
answer as to why these cuts are inevitable.
I should like to refer to the procedure
that has been alluded to by several Members. I have been told that it is unconstitutional, that it is taking away the
powers of Parliament, and I have been
told a great many other things more
extreme still. Some members in another
place went so far as to say it was establishing a Dictatorship. It is not a form
of procedure that one cares very much
for, but in face of a National emergency
-and everybody admits there is a
National emergency-you cannot always
choose your own way; you have got to
take the way that is best adapted to the
circumstances that exist at the moment.
But I maintain it is not taking away the
powers of Parliament, and for this reason.
It is quite true that this Bill gives the
Government very wide powers, but when
it is read in conjunction with the White
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Andrews]
Paper, in which the Government's intentions are expressed in the clearest possible
way, it does not give the wide powers
which some people imagine it does. After
all, the Government is responsible for its
actions, and if these Orders in Council
were made in a way different from that
laid down in the White Paper, Parliament could call the Government to account
for a breach of its stewardship. The very
fact that in the other House we debated
this Bill and this White Paper for three
days, and that we are debating it here
in detail this afternoon, shows very clearly
that every hon. Member knows what is
intended. Therefore it cannot be said
that the Government is going behind the
backs of the representatives of the people.
There is one matter in this connection
which has been raised by the Lord Mayor
of Belfast, and I should like to clear it
up. It is true that in its reference to
local authorities the Bill gives very wide
scope to the Government, but I am quite
willing to assure the hon. Senator that
the Government is not going to take advantage of that latitude to any considerable extent beyond what is stated in the
White Paper. If hon. Members will
refer to page 5, line 5, of the White
Paper, they will read these words: --
Under the procedure referred to the
Exchange will request the Board of
Guardians to assess their needs.
That is the intention of the Government, and I hope, therefore, the Lord
Mayor of Belfast will not consider it
necessary, having got that promise from
me on behalf of the Government, to proceed with his Amendment. Now the
precedure of handing over this matter of
assessment or needs test to the Guardians
or to the local authority has been greatly
criticised and it may have objectionable
features, but on the other hand I can
assure you there is no intention on the
part of the Government that there should
be any degree of pauperism associated
with the administration of transitional
benefit.
I greatly regret that hon. Members in
this House as well as in the other House
have overshadowed it to some extent
by their remarks. May I say just
exactly what the procedure is going to be?
At the end of the 26 weeks' benefit which,
remember, is standard benefit which the
applicant will be entitled to receive, and
for which he has paid contributions,
when he asks for something for which he
has not really paid that view is sometimes
lost sight of when he asks for something
he requires his claim will be re-considered.
First of all, he will make his application
to the Exchange in the ordinary way.
There is nothing pauperising about that.
His title to benefit apart from his need
will be considered by the machinery that
works at present at the Exchange. There
is nothing pauperising about that.
It is only when his need has to be
assessed that the Guardians are going to
be asked to act. Now, why are they
being asked? It has been suggested here
to-day that my Department has all the
machinery necessary. Well, I am entitled
to say we have not, and never had, because this will necessarily be a personal
investigation, and I would require to have
the authority of Parliament for the
appointing of a great many more officials
to carry on these investigations, and then
it would be argued by some that the need
was being assessed by officials who had no
local knowledge, and who were impressed
only by the official view. Now what are
we doing? We are handing this duty
over to the Guardians, the elected representatives of the people, men with local
knowledge, men who can be held accountable for their actions when the next
election comes round, and we are saying,
"You who have local knowledge can be
trusted to do this thing perfectly fairly
and we believe you will."
Now I do venture to hope that that
candid explanation which I have endeavoured to give will remove from the minds
of hon. Senators any idea that there
is any intention on the Government's
part to do wrong to our unemployed.
What is the motive in assessing this
need? In the first place we have
got to follow Great Britain, whether
we like it or not. But as regards the
motive of the British Government for
assessing this test it is this, and it is ours,
that the money not being unlimited it
should go to those who need it most. Now
I asked hon. Members opposite me yesterday, some of whom criticised me personally most severely, if they would find any
fault with that formula, that the money
being limited it should go to those who
need it most, and there was not one of them
could speak, because it was so obviously
correct. Now I think that that deals in
a reasonable way with that aspect of the
case.
Other matters have been raised here
to-day, but may I say before I pass from
Economy Bill-
Second Reading
25 SEPTEMBER 1931
this point that we are asking the
Guardians and those who will be associated with the Guardians in this matter to
do so under the name of public assistance
committees just as they are acting under
that name in Great Britain. Here again
the desire of the Government is to remove
the pauper taint, any pauper taint that
anyone might think is connected with
the Guardians' administration of outdoor
relief. Therefore we are arranging as
far as possible that the meetings will be
held on what I may call neutral premises.
Now when the agitation was raised
against this I felt beyond a shadow of a
doubt that it was raised and fanned and
inflamed because of the fact that the
public really did not know or understand
what was intended. I hope that hon.
Members of this House, as well as of the
other House, who have now had the truth,
the whole truth and nothing but the
truth, will use their influence to tell the
public exactly what the position is, and
if they do I personally have no fear of
the result.
Now hon. Senator Dorman raised a
great many matters and covered a very
wide field-international finance, foreign
exchange, the crimes of limited liability
companies and, by the way, very few
limited liability companies are paying any
dividend at all-and the National
Government, who, he said, were made up
of odds and ends. Well I have heard
from my youth that in the multitude of
counsellors there is wisdom, so that, may
be, there is wisdom amongst those associated with the National Government.
However, these subjects are too wide for
me to deal with at this hour, as I
do not want to occupy the time of
this House longer than is absolutely
necessary. If I have omitted in my reply
to give any information which I was
asked to give, if any hon. Member would
like to ask me the same question again I
will be pleased to answer, as there is
nothing I wish to avoid giving an
planation about, because I feel that
explanation can be readily given, and the
more this whole scheme is understood the
better I personally will be pleased.
ex-
My hon. and learned Friend, Senator
Leslie, I think, made the most important
remark of all to-day. He referred to the
White Paper, and he referred to a much
larger sum being necessary next year on
the estimates that have been prepared
than is required this year. The estimate
does show that. The estimate has been
prepared, no doubt, in all good faith.
Personally I think and I hope it has been
prepared on a very conservative basis.
It will be seen that unemployment for
next year has been estimated on a 30 per
cent. basis. Well we all know it is impossible to tell with any reasonable degree
of accuracy what unemployment will be
a year or a year and a half hence, or what
business conditions are going to be so far
ahead. In fact amongst other things I
am a business man myself, and
wish I could discover what the business position or what the financial
position or the exchange position is
going to be to-morrow. We will have
to wait and see. Besides that the hon.
Senator will see that a very small sum is
estimated to come under the Insurance
I
Agreement to us next year. Well sometimes it is wise to judge the future by
the past, and I am correct in saying we
have received on the average over
£500,000 a year from Great Britain as
well as a considerable sum from our own
Treasury through this Insurance Agreement, and it may be that next year a
very much larger sum will come. Of
course the amount we get is ascertained
not only in relation to unemployment here
but also in relation to unemployment in
Great Britain, and again, to be quite
frank, the estimate is more or less necessarily guesswork.
The hon. Senator went on to throw out
a suggestion which made me feel rather
uncomfortable. He said the time might
come when we could not keep in step with
Great Britain, because we could not afford
to do it. Well I take a different view
from the hon. and learned Senator of this
matter, and for this reason. We are
citizens of the United Kingdom still.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) And as
long as we are citizens of the United
Kingdom-and we intend to remain soour working people here are entitled to
receive the same conditions as their fellow
workers get in Great Britain. And further,
successive British Governments have
already admitted that, and our Re-insurance Agreement itself is an admission.
And if a Government in Great Britain
was to arise which would-either Whig,
Tory or Socialist-want to go back on
that, then-it is a bad thing in public
life to burn your boats, but I am going
to burn mine now-if that took place I
would no longer be Minister of Labour.
I thank the House for the patience with
which they have listened to me.
Economy Bill—
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. CAMPBELL: After the very full
statement of the right hon. Gentleman
and in response to the invitation which
he has just given us,
there is one
point I wish to clear up. I understood
the Minister to say that the investigation
of the means test, which is to be primarily
the work of the Board of Guardians, will
be conducted on neutral ground. I do
not quite know the meaning of neutral
ground. Does it mean the workhouse
buildings or the labour exchange or some
other place?
Mr. ANDREWS: I do not think we
could call the workhouse neutral ground.
In fact, I feel we could not. I am doubtful whether we could call the labour exchange neutral ground or not. But if
neither is considered neutral ground we
will try to obtain other premises. At any
rate, these inquiries will not take place on
workhouse premises.
Mr. DORMAN: The right hon. Gentleman asked for questions. I would like
to reiterate a question already put.
In
the event of a young man having saved a
hundred pounds to complete the education of his children, will this amount be
taken into consideration by these committees? The tendency is under the poor
law not to give relief of any kind unless
the person is completely destitute.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Clark, Sir G. S.
Cunningha 1, Mr. Joseph
I

CONTENT
know numbers of people of that description who may next week be discharged by
their employers and have exhausted
statutory benefit, and I think it would be
unfair if this saving for a formal purpose
had to be expended.
Mr. ANDREWS: I think this House
will agree it would be very wrong of me
to lay down a definite formula for the guidance of the Guardians. After all, we have
to trust people in this world to perform
responsible public duty, and you must in
a measure give them a free hand, at any
rate, until you see how they discharge
their duty. I would not wish to lay down
a condition in this matter, because if I
did I would obviously have to lay down
other conditions.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH :
After the explanaion given by my right
hon. Friend I am perfectly satisfied and
will not proceed with the Amendment I
had sent in. I am also pleased to learn
that he is calling the Committees the
Public Assistance Committees. That, I
think, gets over a great deal of the
difficulty that arose here.
