1 OCTOBER 1931
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 1st October, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PAPER PRESENTED.
Ministry of Finance:
Statement of Guarantees which the
Ministry of Finance have undertaken to
give up to 30th September, 1931.
Presented pursuant to Act, and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Questions to Minister.
COST OF TRAINING COLLEGES.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Minister
of Education whether in respect of the
three Training Colleges for elementary
school teachers Stranmillis, St. Mary's,
Middlesex, and St. Mary's, Belfast-the
Government, in addition to the capitation
grant per student, defray the entire overhead charges of Stranmillis in the way of
buildings, interest, maintenance and repairs; whether these charges amount to
about £20 a year per student, or a total
of over £5,000 a year whether the
Government defray any overhead charges
of St. Mary's, Middlesex, or any overhead charges of St. Mary's, Belfast;
whether the Government are under
liability to liquidate till the year 1935 a
financial responsibility to St. Mary's
College, Belfast, undertaken by the
Imperial Government in 1911 and by
them transmitted to this Government in
1921; whether the Government employ
and pay clergymen of the Presbyterian,
Episcopalian and Methodist Churches to
give religious catechetical instruction to
the students at Stranmillis and conduct
religious services there at special times
during the day; whether the Government
make any corresponding provision in the
case of the other two Colleges; whether it
is, or may be, the duty of the Principal
of Stranmillis to give simple Bible teaching; whether the Committee of management of eight at Stranmillis was exclusively Protestant; whether five of its
members were members of the Presbyterian Board of Education; and whether
the Government contribute any sum in
aid of the grant made by the Irish Free
State Government each year towards the
training of Protestant teachers in Kildare
Place Training College, Dublin.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): The Government of Northern
Ireland does not incur any charge in
respect of Stranmillis Training College
other than that for capitation grants,
which are at present paid at the rate of
£70 per annum for men students and
£62 10s per annum for women students.
The premises were provided out of grants
made by the Imperial Government, and
the maintenance charges necessary for
their upkeep are defrayed out of the
College funds, consisting of the abovementioned capitation grants and students'
fees. No actual payment is made in
respect of capital cost of the premises.
This capital cost to date amounts to
£179,025.
The maintenance charges in respect of
the St. Mary's Training College, Strawberry Hill, Middlesex, and the St. Mary's
Training College, Belfast, are similarly
defrayed out of the revenues of the
colleges in question; the only payments
made by the Ministry are capitation
grants, together with, in the case of St.
Mary's Belfast, a terminable annuity of
£1,000 per annum towards meeting the
original capital cost of the college. This
annuity, which was instituted by the
Imperial Government in 1911, and which
necessarily devolved on the Government
of Northern Ireland at the transfer of
services in 1922. will cease in 1935.
Three chaplains of the denominations
mentioned are attached to Stranmillis
Training College and are paid salaries out
of the college funds. These chaplains
give Bible and catechetical instruction to
the members of their respective denominations. The religious services are not
conducted by the chaplains. It is understood that a chaplain is attached to St.
Mary's College, Belfast, and receives a
salary from the funds of that college. The
Ministry has no information as to the
arrangements for similar services at St.
Mary's Training College, Strawberry
Hill.
It is no part of the specific duties for
which the principal of Stranmillis Training College was appointed, that he should
give Bible instruction.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
I do not think that the question of the
religious persuasions of the gentlemen
who were members of the Committee for
the training of teachers is relevant, and
I have no information whether some of
them were also members of the Presbyterian Board of Education.
The Government of Northern Ireland
does not contribute any sum in aid of the
grants made by the Irish Free State
Government each year towards the training of Protestant teachers in Kildare
Place Training College, Dublin.
THE TRANSFER OF ELEMENTARY
SCHOOLS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Minister
of Education whether he can state how
many elementary schools were transferred
to education authorities between the passing of the Education Act, 1923, and the
passing of the Education Act, 1930;
how many schools have been transferred
or are in course of transfer since the
passing of the 1930 Act; whether any such
schools were under Catholic management
before transfer; and whether such transferred schools are now maintained wholly
at the public charge through remaining
largely under the management of the
transferors; whether "simple Bible teaching" is given daily in these schools by the
teachers as part of their duties, and
whether any other form of religious
teaching is given by the teachers or any
other person in these schools.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Four hundred and forty-five public elementary
schools were transferred to the local
authorities between the passing of the
Education Act, 1923, and the passing of
the Education Act, 1930. The number
of schools transferred or in process of
transfer since the passing of the 1930 Act
is 127. None of the schools so transferred
was under Roman Catholic management
prior to transfer.
The cost of maintenance of all transferred schools falls on the education
authority concerned, and forms portion.
of the approved expenditure of the
education committee for the purpose of
grants from the Ministry.
With the exception of a few cases in
which there is no definite evidence available, the Ministry is in a position to state
that simple Bible instruction is given in
ali transferred schools by the teachers
in these schools. It does not, however,
necessarily follow that all the teachers in
a particular school give such instruction.
There is no obligation on the teachers
to give catechetical instruction in transferred schools, but some of them do so
voluntarily where the parents of the
children desire it. In a certain number
of transferred schools the local clergy give
some religious instruction.
COST TO THE STATE OF NEW AND
RECONDITIONED SCHOOLS.
more
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Minister
of Education whether he was correctly
reported in the Belfast Press as having
stated, on 13th July, at Castlecaulfield,
that "of provided schools for Protestant
children 34 have been built at the cost to
the State of nearly £403,000, while on
transferred schools which have been reconditioned at a cost of £100 or
£161,232 have been spent by the State";
what is the total to date of the loans by
the Ministry of Finance to education committees for these purposes, and the total
of the loans raised by these authorities
from other sources for these purposes;
what is the total to date of the public
moneys made available for the building
and reconditioning of Catholic voluntary
schools under individual management; (1)
between the passing of the Education
Act, 1923, and the passing of the Education Act, 1930 and (2) since the passing
of the Education Act, 1930.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Since the
passing of the Education Act (Northern
Ireland), 1923, 34 new provided schools
were erected by the Local Education
Authorities, at a cost of approximately
£403,000, while a further expenditure of
approximately £161,000 was incurred in
the reconditioning of existing schools
transferred to these authorities. These
schools are open to pupils of all religious
persuasions.
The cost of the erection of these new
schools, and to a considerable extent of
the reconditioning of the existing schools,
was defrayed not by the State but by
means of loans obtained by the local
authorities either from the Ministry of
Finance or from other sources, but the
annual charge in repayment of principal
1 OCTOBER 1931
Oral Answers

Oral Answers
EXTENT AND PRICE OF SITES FOR
and interest forms portion of the approved
expenditure of such authorities towards
which the Ministry makes grants.
The amount of the loans obtained for
the building and reconditioning of schools
completed or in hand from the Ministry
of Finance to date is approximately
£323,000, and from other
£504,000.
sources
Prior to the passing of the Education
Act, 1930, the Ministry of Education
made grants amounting to £21,130 for the
erection and equipment of new voluntary
schools under Roman Catholic management, and £5,036 for the enlargement,
reconstruction and equipment of existing
voluntary schools under such management. These were direct grants made to
the managers of these schools out of funds
provided by Parliament for the purpose,
and were in fulfilment of obligations taken
over from the former Commissioners of
National Education on the transfer of the
educational services to the Ministry. In
addition, grants amounting to £7,370
have been made by local education authorities towards the building or reconstruction of four voluntary public elementary
schools, the applicants for which were
Roman Catholic clergy. These grants
were made under the provisions of Section
16 of the Education Act of 1923, one of
the conditions of which is that the schools
so aided shall be under the management
of a committee appointed by the Ministry.
Section 10 of the Act of 1930 empowers
the Ministry to make direct grants for the
erection and equipping of new voluntary
schools or the improvement, etc., of existing voluntary schools. No grants have
yet been made, but the Ministry hopes to
be in a position shortly to sanction at least
three substantial grants for the erection
of new schools under Roman Catholic
management, two of which will be in the
city of Belfast and one in the city of
Derry.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask the
Minister of Education if the result of the
figures shows that the ratio is-less than
5 per cent. of this public money goes to
the schools of the minority, and more than
95 per cent. goes to the schools of the
majority in this Province.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to have an opportunity of going into
the figures before answering the question.
SCHOOLS IN BELFAST.
Mr. DORMAN asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the area
of land within the County Borough of
Belfast acquired by the public authority
as sites for public elementary schools since
the passing of the Education Act, 1923;
the price per acre paid for such land, and
its rateable value (if any) at the time
of purchase.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will make
inquiries in regard to the information.
asked for by the hon. Member, but it will
take some time to do it, for I have to get
the information from the Belfast education authority. I think if the hon.
Member would kindly renew the question
in about a week or perhaps a little more,
it would be better.

Loans Guarantee Acts 512
Loans Guarantee Acts
THE SENATE
The Loans Guarantee Acts.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That in the opinion of this House the
state of industry and trade and of public
finances makes it imperative on the
Government to take into consideration
without delay the expediency of underwriting further guarantees under the
Loans Guarantee Acts beyond the
guarantees for over £11,000,000 already
given, predominantly in favour of shipowning concerns, of which the sum of over
£9,000,000 is outstanding."
I should like to approach this question
if it is possible in a non-party spirit, and
I should be glad if it could be received
and discussed in a non-party spirit. Ι
submit to the House that the policy of
the Government in pledging the credit of
the taxpayers of Northern Ireland under
the Loans Guarantee Acts has been unwise policy and the Government in their
administration of the Acts have played
their cards badly. The only justification
attempted for the vast commitments of
the Government under these Acts is the
unconvincing statement that employment in shipbuilding has been thereby
stimulated. We have no proof of this
statement and a large body of the public
are frankly sceptical about it.
The first Trade Facilities Act of the
Parliament at Westminster was passed in
the year 1921, the last in 1926. The
British Parliament experimented for five
years on these lines. At the beginning
of 1926 the then British Prime Minister
announced in Parliament that the general
desire was that proceedings under these
Acts should be brought to a close gradually and as occasion might offer. The
last Imperial Trade Facilities Act of
1926 fixed the limit of the amount of
lcans which might be guaranteed by the
British Treasury at £75,000,000. Did
Northern Ireland get any share of the
guarantees under those Acts? Was the
Province cold-shouldered? Those Acts
extended not only to Great Britain but
also to Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland as part of the United Kingdom must
share whatever ultimate financial responsibility may be entailed by the operation
of those Acts. Whence came the necessity
for the Government here carrying through
a separate series of Trade Facilities or
Loans Guarantee Acts for this small area
and casting upon the Province what may
prove to be a very serious financial
burden.
The first Loans Guarantee Act in
Northern Ireland passed in 1922 fixed the
total capital amount that could be
guaranteed as not more than £2,000,000.
In 1926, the very year when the Westminster Parliament in its wisdom closed
its account under its Trade Facilities Acts,
our Government took a fresh spurt and
got this Parliament to pass two such Acts
raising the maximum limit of the loans
which might be guaranteed from six
million to nine million pounds. Westminster had gained by its experience.
Apparently Belfast had not. So from year
to year for nine years Parliament here
has gone on piling Act on Act till now the
Government guarantees can soar
thirteen millions. The loans guaranteed
so far amount to over eleven millions, the
guarantees being made altogether, I believe, in favour of shipowning concerns,
almost £6,000,000 going to the benefit of
the Kylsant group of companies.
losses to the British Treasury on British
transactions were estimated in 1926 at
about £41,699, subject to any possible
recoveries later. I do not know the
ultimate figures. Many taxpayers in
this area think the Government should
call a halt to their progress along this
broad and easy slope. Perhaps like
Macbeth the Government is
""
Stepped in so far that should it
Iwade no more,
to
The
Returning were as tedious as go o'er."
The
The policy of these Acts has been to
guarantee loans as to principal and
interest raised in the open market by
public bodies or private firms embarking
upon capital expenditure which in the
opinion of the Government would provide
employment in Northern Ireland.
loans guaranteed have been loans of shipowning companies. The Government say
its guarantees have promoted employment.
How much of this claim is romance—
how much reality? They say this policy
has been wise. Has it been? From 1920
to 1931 shipping has been doing badly.
Much of the shipping of the world has
been unable to find remunerative employment. There have been far more ships
than were necessary for the carriage of
the goods available between country and
country. At the close of the War and
immediately afterwards the shipping and
shipbuilding trades were busy constructing
castles in the air. Fancy finance became
fashionable. Financial accommodation
was hard to come by. 1920 was the last
Loans Guarantee Acts
1 OCTOBER 1931
Loans Guarantee Acts
of the golden years of shipbuilding. 1921
was the first of the black years.
The facts regarding one leading shipowning company-one of the Kylsant
group-as revealed lately to the public,
showed that this company was not earning
any profit from the year 1921 to 1927,
and was kept going not by current earnings but by withdrawals from reserves,
mainly piled up during the War, of the
sum of £5,000,000 for dividends. During
those six years the company earned no
profits not a shilling. In 1921 its
working showed a deficit of £900,000, and
even after bringing in aid interest on investments there was still a deficit of
£450,000. The next four years were
years of shattering losses. In 1926
£900,000 was taken for dividends from
reserves of non-recurring items. In 1927
£512,000 was so taken. Did the Government make inquiries from time to time
as to the stability of favoured concerns.
An immediate effect of the British and
Northern guarantees may have been the
production of some more ships than the
traffic of the world justified. Vessels not
required may have been produced by the
instrumentality of these loans, produced
ahead of demands in the natural course
of business. Under normal circumstances
a company orders a ship just ahead of
requirements, but when an order is given.
for a ship under guarantees, the order is
being executed in advance of the usual
programme. The stimulus to shipbuilding
is artificial, delays the orders which would
flow normally into the yards, postpones
the prosperity of shipbuilding, and by
fostering unremunerative tonnage tends
to aggravate unemployment in the future.
More than that, these guarantees do not
increase employment in the aggregate.
You may by their means increase employment for a time in one direction, but
you
diminish it in another. You may galvanise some industries into spasmodic
activity at the expense of others-here
shipbuilding at the expense of farming or
linen. Has shipbuilding a longer, stronger
political pull than linen or farming?
These guarantees have not, I submit,
given employment to any appreciable
greater number than would have got employment if there had been no guarantees
whatever. Have the companies for whose
benefit guarantees were given kept a record as far as possible of the amount of
employment created by the works carried
out by the proceeds of loans? Has the
Government sought any share in the
management or control of those concerns
in return for its guarantees? Are the
main securities for these guarantees ships,
whether laid up idle or sailing the seas,
the value of which is certainly not appreciating with the flight of years.
These
A tremendous sum has been guaranteed
-tremendous for this small area. On the
present basis the annual revenue of Northern Ireland is about £10,500,000. Of this
the Westminster Government controls,
levies, and takes about 75 per cent. The
sums guaranteed represent more than a
whole year's revenue. The public debt of
Northern Ireland is about £5,500,000.
The public liability under these guarantees is twice the public debt.
guarantees are still being underwritten
five years after Great Britain has given
them up.
If they are to go on Parliament should be instructed by a Parliamentary Committee which can grasp the
situation as a whole, and inquire into the
broad question of these State facilities to
industry. The system in vogue is unsound in finance and unwise in principle.
Let us always bethink ourselves of thisthe taxpayers of Ulster alone, unaided by
the British Treasury, will be responsible
in the last resource for the loss in these
transactions.
Mr. MCALLISTER: In listening to the
speech of my colleague, the one thing that
impressed itself upon me was the perfectly
clear evidence of the preferential treatment of the city of Belfast in connection
with these guaranteed loans. We have
nine million pounds already guaranteed,
with liberty to the Government to
guarantee a further two million pounds,
and practically all to one industry in this
city of Belfast. No doubt an infinitesimal amount of this money has been
guaranteed for textile works in Northern
Ireland, but practically the whole of this.
colossal sum has been appropriated to the
city of Belfast. About the time the last
Loans Guarantee Act was being passed,
in 1929, an Act was passed here undertaking to lend the farmers £250,000;
but, as I have said before, I think it is
now perfectly clear that that was merely
picturesque window-dressing done
order to placate the hostility of Northern
Ireland farmers on account of the preferential treatment accorded to Belfast.
It is a significant fact that, owing to the
cumbersome, humiliating, and impossible
regulations made in the Government
J
in
Loans Guarantee Acts
Loans Guarantee Acts
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
office, of that £250,000 which it was
agreed to lend to the farmers of Northern
Ireland, £21,000 was applied for and
only £7,000 granted as a loan to the
farmers. Now I think it is beyond all
dispute that if this £9,000,000 had been
expended on the basic industry of Northern Ireland, the one that employs the
most labour, handles the most money, and
is peopled by most people in Northern
Ireland-if a fraction of that £9,000,000
had been appropriated to the organising
of marketing facilities for farmers, it
would not have had the tragic and deplorable result that this mishandled transacthe life of
tion undoubtedly has on
Northern Ireland.
It is a strange contrast to the foresight
of the people of England. When they
were cutting down these loans that is the
time this Government took to plunge
headlong into them. I would like the
noble Lord the Leader of the House when
he comes to reply to give us some concrete
idea of the effect that £9,000,000 already
guaranteed has had on the people of
Northern Ireland as a whole. I would
also like the noble Lord when replying
to say, do the Government in face of
what has taken place in regard to the
various companies concerned say they are
justified in what they have done in this
particular transaction. That is a very
simple question, and it is one to which
I would very much like to have a definite
reply.
Mr. DORMAN: There is one aspect of
this question that was brought before the
Government, I understand, in the year
1926. The Government was, no doubt,
advised by very competent gentlemen
before they granted these guarantees with
a view to providing employment in the
area. But the question was put up then
as to the advisability of the Government
seeking representation on the boards of
the various companies for whom they
guaranteed these loans. It is necessary
in my humble opinion in order to safeguard and protect the interests of the
taxpayers of Northern Ireland that the
Government should have some voice and
some knowledge of the proceedings of the
various companies for whom these loans
are guaranteed. Such companies may b
pursuing a wasteful and destructful policy
which may ultimately end
in
winding up of such companies, and under
these circumstances I think it is necessary,
the
at least if the Government continue to
guarantee further loans, they should seek
representation on the boards of companies
for whom the loans are guaranteed.
The Gov-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
ernment is asked by this Resolution
to reconsider its policy of giving guarantees under the Loans Guarantee Acts on
the ground that the risk involved in the
giving of guarantees under current conditions of industry has now become too
great in view of the state of the public
finances. This legislation is admittedly
temporary in nature, as evidenced by the
system of yearly statutory extensions, and
each the Cabinet has considered very
year
carefully the policy of continuing these
facilities, having regard to the degree of
unemployment in our shipyards. Admittedly, the Act has had the effect of saving
the Unemployment Fund and indirectly
the Exchequer large sums of money,
owing to the fact that from 6,000 to 8,000
men have been continuously kept in employment over a number of years.
Fifty-four ships have been constructed
involving this number of employees, and
but for these guarantees the orders would
not have materialised. It follows that,
under the changed incidence whereby the
cost of unemployment is now to fall more
heavily upon the Exchequer, the situation
demands that any machinery which can
assist the employment position without
undue risk of loss ought not to be lightly
discarded. I would say that, in my
opinion the Government has been entirely
justified in the action it has taken in
regard to these loans up to the present.
I should like to add also that Great
Britain is guaranteeing part of the Northern Ireland ship contracts which are the
subject of our guarantees. Up to 1926
approximately 40 per cent. of each contract involved a British guarantee.
The policy of the Government is to
retain the statutory powers and to treat
applications on their merits as before with
regard to their economic soundness and the
nature of the security offered.
There are
in existence shipping companies which
have not, for one reason or another, been
affected to the same extent by world trade
depression, and which are financially so
sound that, if the enterprise of our shipyards can prevail upon them to take the
long view despite the troubles at the
moment, it is desirable that the Government should be in a position to co-operate
by the grant of these credits. The House
517 Expiring Laws Bill-

1 OCTOBER 1931
Second Reading
may not have realised that the securing
of a
contract of the value of, say,
£1,000,000 will effect a direct saving to
the Northern Ireland Exchequer of a sum
in the region of £225,000. This circumstance is certainly a factor in considering
whether the Government would be justified, in the present appalling conditions
of unemployment, in repealing the Loans
Guarantee Acts.
I should like to refer to the question
raised by the hon. Senator who spoke last.
If he will kindly raise the matter again
at a future date I shall be pleased to give
him an answer.
On Question, Motion negatived.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This is the usual annual
Bill which continues temporarily certain
enactments which may be regarded as
being still experimental or liable to revision; the date fixed for the continuance
of all of them is the same, namely, 31st
December, 1932.
The Bill fits in with the corresponding
Imperial Bill in so far as the latter deals
with any enactment applicable to Northern Ireland in regard to which our
Parliament here has not the power to
make laws. This Bill now before us
accordingly supplements the provisions of
the Imperial Bill by dealing with enactments relating to subjects over which our
Parliament has legislative power, and the
two Measures taken together deal with all
temporary laws applicable to Northern
Ireland.
In regard to the Schedule of the Bill,
which, of course, contains the matter of
real importance, Part I does not call for
One enactment which
any comment.
appeared in the Schedule of previous
years has been omitted in this part,
namely, the Coal Mines Minimum Wage
Act, 1912; it is not thought necessary to
continue this legislation for our area as
it has no practical application in Northern Ireland. Part II of the Schedule is
identical with that of last year and does
not, therefore, call for explanation.
beg to move.
Mr. MCALLISTER:
I
I rise to oppose
this Bill on the ground principally that
it includes amongst the various Acts the
Local Government Emergency Powers
Act, 1921. That Act is described as an
emergency Act, and it is an Act to
provide that the local authorities should
be superseded by commissions. I suggest
to the House that this is a serious reflection on the people of Northern Ireland,
because these local authorities
popularly elected bodies elected by the
people. There is a want of confidence in
the people, in the first instance, and a
want of confidence in the chosen representatives of the people. That is an undesirable state of affairs.
are
If a Motion like that were coming from
myself or my colleagues here I could
understand it. But the idea of the Government of Northern Ireland libelling by
this Bill the whole people and the whole
public representatives of Northern Ireland
is I think, a position that would be very
hard to justify. As a matter of fact,
myself and my colleagues are here to
defend the reputation of the people when
it is assailed and violently assailed and
libelled like this by the Government.
Therefore, I suggest in the interests of
Northern Ireland and of the name,
reputation, character, and standing of
Northern Ireland, the Act slandering the
people should be deleted from this
Schedule.
Mr. LONG: The Act in the Schedule
to which the hon. Senator has referred
was passed in December, 1921, in order to
enable recalcitrant local bodies to be replaced temporarily by an official administrator. It is not deemed desirable that
the power should be suffered to expire at
the present time, and from information I
have obtained from Government sources it
would be highly injudicious to do so.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Planning and Housing Bill.
Returned from the House of Commons
with Amendments made by the Senate
agreed to without any Amendment.
Education Bill.
Returned from the House of Commons
agreed to without any Amendment.
Adjournment-
Business of the House
THE SENATE
Adjournment.
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday." I should like on Tuesday
if this House agrees with me to suspend
Standing Orders so as to take the First
and Second Reading of the following
Bills:-Unemployment Insurance, Workmen's Compensation, and Widows' and
Orphans' and Old Age Contributory
Pensions. I will only do it if this
House approves, but I think it is doubtful
policy to call the House together just
entirely for First Readings. Copies of
the Bills in question will be circulated
during the week-end. That is what I
would like to be decided. If anybody
has any objection let him say so now or
for ever after hold his peace.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before we adjourn
until Tuesday next, there is a matter of
extreme public importance to which
attention should be directed. It is obvious
to anyone following the newspaper Press
that there is a tendency to advance prices
in this area, and it will soon be strong
generally. Prices apparently will go up,
and the one pound note and the ten
shilling Treasury note will buy less than
they do to-day. Prices have lately been
lower than at any period since 1914, and
they are lower than they are likely to be
for some considerable time.
My suggestion to the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, is that before
Parliament adjourns the
adjourns the Government
should take steps to pass a Bill, which
might be acceptable to all parties in
Parliament, taking powers to prevent
exploitation in the prices of foodstuffs
A corresponding Bill will, I believe, be
laid before the Imperial Parliament in the
next few days. The matter is just as
urgent in our own area as it is in Great
Britain. There has been, I have read
during the present week, an increase of
something like 20 per cent. in some ports
overseas in the prices of goods destined
for these islands. I think this is a
matter that will commend itself to all
parties, irrespective of other questions on
which we may differ.
Mr. LESLIE: We seem to have rather
a variety of subjects before us at the
moment, but I rather want to go back to
the noble Viscount's proposal as regards
Tuesday next. I want to ask him
whether, if we are able to stretch such
elasticity as we possess in the matter indicated on Tuesday, it is proposed we
should sit also on Wednesday.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: On Thursday.
Mr. LESLIE: If it is proposed to take
the First and Second Readings of circulated Bills on Tuesday, and not have to
deal with them again until Thursday,
there need not be any objection to the
proposal of the noble Viscount, but if we
have to go to the next stage on the following day, I think we should be worked a
little unduly long.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is proposed that we should adjourn from Tuesday until Thursday. I would like to
postpone answering the suggestion made
by the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) until Tuesday, and in the
meantime I will find out what the intentions of the Government are in the
matter.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
five minutes after Four
o'clock until Tuesday next.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
6 OCTOBER 1931
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 6th October, 1931.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
Memorandum explanatory of the
Measures proposed to be taken under the
Economy Bill.
Presented (by Command) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Ministry of Commerce:
Report on the Mining and Quarrying
Industries in Northern Ireland for the
year ended 31st December, 1930.
Ministry of Finance:
The
Registrar-General's
Review
of
Vital Statistics of Northern Ireland and
Life Tables (1926).
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
Resolved, "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting
for the purpose of taking the Second
Reading of such Bills as come from the
House of Commons."-(Viscount Charlemont.)
Questions to Minister.
REGULATION OF FOOD PRICES.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether, seeing that during
the recess of Parliament the depreciation
in sterling must lead to rises in the wholesale prices of foodstuffs from abroad and
that retail prices will inevitably rise in
sympathy, the Government will ask
Parliament to vest in the Ministry of
Home Affairs or in a Food Council, full
powers of investigation and regulation of
food prices till Parliament meets again;
and whether he will inform the House
what are the existing powers of the Government in this respect.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): My right hon. Friend the
Minister of Commerce is keeping in close
touch with the Northern Ireland wholesale and retail trade organisations in
regard to this matter. Whilst, as the
Deputy-Leader of the House has already
stated, the Government will not hesitate,
should circumstances so require, to take
whatever steps may be necessary to prevent any undue increase in the price of
foodstuffs taking place, the information
in the possession of the Government at
present does not indicate the necessity for
legislation along the lines suggested by
the hon. and learned Senator. As regards
the existing powers of the Government, I
am advised that these would not extend
to regulatory or punitive action in circumstances such as the present, and that
legislation would, therefore, be necessary
before action of that nature could be
taken.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
would like to ask the Leader of the House
whether he has noticed that a very strong
Measure dealing with this matter has been
introduced in the Imperial Parliament,
and is almost certain to be passed into
law, and whether it would not be possible
for the Northern Government to pass a
similar Measure here?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I hope the
Imperial Bill to which the right hon. and
gallant Member has referred will have
such a salutary effect that it will not be
necessary to bring in similar legislation
in the Northern Parliament. At the
same time, I will repeat the assurance
previously given that if circumstances
demand it the Government will
hesitate to do so.
TRADE BOARD RATES.
not
Mr. S. CUNNINGHAM asked the
Leader of the House if he will state what
are the existing Trade Board Rates, and
what reductions have taken place since
1921 in view of the fall in the cost of
living.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the
answer involves a considerable number of
figures, I propose, with the right hon.
Member's permission, to circulate it in
the Official Report.
The following is the answer referred to:

Message from the Commons.
The Commons agree to the Ministry of
Home Affairs Provisional Order Confirmation (Banbridge Union Borrowing)
Bill, as reported from the Joint Committee, without amendment; they have
passed the Unemployment Insurance (No.
2) Bill, and the Workmen's Compensation
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Unemployment Insurance (No. 2) Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
""
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read a Second time.
I do not propose to detain the House very
long with the details of this Bill. The
First Clause is entirely a money question,
and proposes to increase the borrowing
powers for Unemployment Insurance by
£100,000 up to a final maximum limit of
£2,600,000. It will be remembered that
there is to be no more borrowing for
Unemployment Benefit, and this final sum
is fixed as being the corresponding amount
to the limit which is placed to borrowing
in Great Britain for the British Unemployment Fund. The whole of this extra
amount of £100,000 may not be necessary,
but it is considered desirable to have a
little in hand, as certain adjustments may
have to be made annually.
The Second Clause of the Bill extends
the transitional period for benefit from
forty-two months to forty-eight months.
This is following similar arrangements
already made in Great Britain.
Unemployment Insurance
Workmen's Compensation Bill
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I imagine that the
House will accept the Bill before it as
being part of what I may call the clearing
up process which has been necessitated
partly by the march of events, and partly,
perhaps, by the Economy Bill which has
recently received the Royal Assent. I
think one may perhaps use the opportunity of the Second Reading to ask the
Parliamentary Secretary what arrangements, if any, have been made for the
repayment of this sum of £2,600,000, and
whether any provision for repayment is
included in the figures recently given us
in the White Paper circulated when the
Economy Bill was under discussion. As
we all know, the burdens imposed by the
present system are heavy, so heavy that
we have had to alter them and decline to
borrow further money beyond this comparatively trifling sum necessary for
clearing up, but I think we are naturally
anxious to know as much as we can be
told about what measures are to be taken
to repay it.
Passing from that to the second Clause
of the Bill, I should like to be assured
that I am correct in my reading of the
report of what has been called the
Gregory Committee, to the effect that the
extension of the transitional period to
forty-eight months means really that
claims may arise within that extended
period, and does not mean that transitional benefit or uncovenanted benefit, or
whatever you may call it, will necessarily
cease at the expiration of that period.
That is to say that although, as the law
stands, if I understand it correctly,
claims cannot arise beyond that period,
all claims received in that period will
extend for their normal duration of, I
think, twelve months. Although I am
asking the question now, I do not think
it is necessary that an answer should be
given at the moment. The Parliamentary
Secretary might very possibly prefer to
reply at more length at a later stage of
the Bill.
Mr. LONG: The hon. and learned
Senator is correct in his surmise that the
£100,000 is required in connection with
the clearing up process. With regard to
the question of the repayment of the
money, and whether the transitional
benefit would cease at the expiration of
the forty-eight months. I shall take the
opportunity of dealing with those matters
at a later stage of the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday.
Workmen's Compensation Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, That this Bill be now read a
Second time." This Bill follows a
Measure which was passed through the
Imperial Parliament by agreement between all parties. The primary object of
the Bill is to re-state the intention of
Section 9 (4) of our Act of 1927. This
Section-o rather the corresponding
Section in the English Act-was construed
by the House of Lords, on appeal, to
mean that full compensation could not
be paid to a workman who was unable to
follow his original occupation and who
was unable to obtain other work which
he
was capable of performing. The
Measure, therefore, provides that total
compensation may be paid if it is clear
that the man would be likely to have
continued to find employment at his
original occupation or if, by reason
of his injury, it is impossible for him to
obtain employment for which he is capable
in an alternative occupation.
and
A further provision is that the direct
onus of proof that he has taken all
reasonable steps to obtain and has
failed to obtain employment,
that his failure to so obtain employment is a consequence wholly or mainly of
the injury is removed from the person
concerned. An order for compensation
may be made if it appears to the judge
that these conditions have been satisfied.
Provision is also made to apply the
Measure to any accident which happened
on or after the 1st January, 1924, the
retrospective date to which the Act of
1927 applied.
As I have said, this Measure should be
entirely non-contentious, and I do not
propose to enter into any more detail at
this stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Adjournment-
Business of the House
6 OCTOBER 1931
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[VISCOUNT BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Continuance of Acts in
Schedule) and 2 (Short title and construction) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Schedule agreed to.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Moved: "That this report be now received.”—(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
"That this House do now adjourn until
Thursday," may I say that under normal
circumstances we should take the Committee stages of the Bills we have had
to-day. At the same time I should like,
if possible, that the House would assume
that they were financial Bills which need
not go through Committee, so as to avoid
the Committee stage, and then we should
be able to do the Third Reading and
Passing without suspending Standing
Orders. Otherwise I would move
suspend Standing Orders and take the
to
Committee stage, Third Reading and
Passing on that day. I should like to
hear whether anybody opposes the idea
that they should be regarded as financial
Bills.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I do not
think this House ought to be a party in
any way to extending the restrictions on
its powers to deal with Bills. I do not,
of course, deal with the question of those
two Bills before us in any detail. I do
think they do present characteristics
somewhat different from strictly financial
Measure. Therefore, I am afraid I do
not think it would be quite in accordance
with the best traditions of this House to
abrogate the Committee stage even if it
proved a convenience.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I put the
question really because I perfectly realise
the Bills in question are on the border
line. I shall not press the matter. But
I will give notice to suspend Standing
Orders in order to take these Bills in
time for the Royal Assent next week.
There is also a Measure which I had
hoped would come here from another place
to-day, dealing with the inclusion of
widows and orphans in benefit. I am not
entitled, without the Bill in front of me,
to give the shortest possible résumé.
can only say the purpose of the Bill is to
include certain persons not included
before. It would be very advantageous
for us and for the widows and orphans if
it is not opposed-and the Bill is noncontentious to pass it through all its
stages on Thursday. If not I will take it
after the Royal Assent is given on Tuesday
to the other Bills. However, I will give
notice to suspend Standing Orders. In
the meantime I beg to move "That this
House do now adjourn."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Four o'clock,
until Thursday next.
531 Suspension of Standing Orders
Suspension of Standing Orders 532
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 8th October, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Robinson's Divorce Bill.
The following report was made from
the Joint Standing Committee on Divorce
Bills: that they had considered the said
Bill and that the averments in the
Preamble had been proved to their
satisfaction, and that they had gone
through the said Bill and made Amendment thereto.
Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Order Confirmation (Banbridge
Union Borrowing) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved,
"That this Bill be now read
the Third time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate had
agreed to the said Bill as reported from
the Joint Committee without any Amendment.
Suspension of Standing Orders.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
"That
the House): I beg to move,
Standing Order No. XXV be suspended
for this day's sitting for the purpose of
taking the remaining stages of Bills on
the Order Paper, and such Bills as may
come from the House of Commons."
Mr. LESLIE: I think that perhaps the
noble Viscount is asking a little more than
this House ought to be prepared to give.
I am sure we will all agree, after what
transpired at the last sitting, that the
suspension of Standing Orders for the
purpose of taking the remaining stages of
the Bills on the Order Paper is justifiable,
but if we are to go further and take the
First and Second Readings and the Committee Stage and Third Reading of the
Widows', Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Pensions Bill, which I understand
has passed through another place, and has
not yet been received officially in this
House, I think that is asking, perhaps,
a little more than is right. I have
obtained a copy of the Bill as, I understand, it was when it left another place,
and I can only say the Bill appears to be
of a somewhat complicated character and
a Bill that we ought not to pass without
a breathing space between the stages.
Therefore, I ask the noble Viscount to
accept an Amendment to the Motion by
inserting after the word " and " in the
third line" the First and Second Readings of "
such Bills. I think that is as
far as we ought to go. I do not know
whether Senators desire any time to consider that particular Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I never
like to force through the suspension of
Standing Orders unless there is a case of
real necessity if any Members of this
House object, so that I am quite willing
to accept the Amendment moved by the
hon. and learned Senator. As regards
the Widows', Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions Bill we have to
suspend Standing Orders so as to take the
First and Second Readings now and the
rest of the stages after the Royal Assent
to the other Bills.
Mr. DORMAN: I understand it is the
intention of the Government to date back
the coming into operation of this Bill.
Will this Amendment delay or interfere
in any way with the commencement of
benefits?
Mr. SPEAKER: Perhaps the hon.
Senator will speak up.
Mr. DORMAN: I understand it is the
intention of the Government to date back
the coming into operation of this Bill. I
should like to ask will this postponement
interfere with the Government's intention
in that respect.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: All arrears
will be paid.
Amendment agreed to.
On Question, Motion ("That Standing Order No. XXV be suspended for this
day's sitting for the purpose of taking
the remaining stages of Bills on the Order
Paper, and the First and Second Readings of such Bills as may come from the
House of Commons ") agreed to.
Oral Answers
Wild Birds Bill
8 OCTOBER 1931
Questions to Minister.
FREE MEALS FOR NECESSITOUS
SCHOOL CHILDREN.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that the Education Authorities outside
Belfast have not made use of the powers
given them by the Education Acts of providing at least one free meal daily to
necessitous school children in their areas;
whether resultant ill-effects on the health
of children have come to the knowledge of
the Ministry; and whether he will take
steps to save this enactment from becoming a dead letter outside Belfast.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Lisburn
and Belfast Regional Education Committee daily provide a bread and milk
meal to 60 pupils in the Lisburn Free
Public Elementary School, and also make
a grant of £25 to the Whiteabbey Nursing
Society in aid of the supply of bread and
milk to pupils attending public elementary
schools in that district. The estimated
cost of this service in the present financial
year is £120.
The County Down Regional Education
Committee, also, are in course of arranging
for the supply of a free midday meal for
some 90 children attending public elementary schools in Newry, who, it has been
found upon careful investigation
ducted at the charge of the Education
Committee, are suffering from malnutrition.
con-
No other Regional Education Committees, so far as the Ministry is aware,
are at present making provision for free
meals to public elementary school
children; but the Ministry has every
reason to believe that there is no neglect
of their duties by these Committees. At
present it is apparent that voluntary
agencies outside Belfast are usually
sufficient to cope with individual cases of
distress. The hon. and learned Senator
may be assured that the Ministry will
always be willing to direct the attention
of any Regional Education Committee
should this prove necessary to its duties
under the Section of the Education Act
which makes certair provision for the
feeding of necessitous school children at
the public expense; but I should like to
say that the Ministry has always found
these Committees humane and generous
in their anxiety to relieve distress and
suffering of the children of the poor in the
areas under their supervision.
ACQUISITION OF SITES FOR
SCHOOLS IN BELFAST.
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can state
the area of land within the County
Borough of Belfast acquired by the Public
Authority as sites for public elementary
schools since the passing of the Education
Act, 1923; the price per acre paid for
such land, and its rateable value (if any)
at the time of purchase.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The answer
to the hon. Member's question is in the
form of a tabulated statement, which I
would like, with the hon. Member's permission, to circulate with the official
report. I will also send a copy to him
to-morrow. I regret I cannot do so today, as there has been a slight mistake in
typing.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons agree to the Wild Birds
Protection Bill with some Amendments,
to which they desire the concurrence of
this House. They have also passed the
Widows', Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Pensions Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
Wild Birds Protection Bill.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move
"That this House proceed to the consideration of the Commons' Amendments
to the said Bill."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The first Amendment to which the Commons desire concurrence is Clause 2, page 2, line 30, to
leave out the words "or water" and insert
the words "water or air." The object of
this Amendment is perfectly clear, and I
think it is a real improvement to the Bill.
I beg to move, "That this House do agree
with the Commons in the said Amendment."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The next Amendment to which the Commons desire concurrence is in Clause 6, page 4, line 13,
to leave out the words "killed or taken.'
This Amendment is consequential upon
the Amendment which follows, page 4,
""
Wild Birds Bill-
Commons Amendments
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
line 14, to leave out the words "taken in
contravention of this section," and insert
the words "at a time when a bird, egg or
nest in the same category is protected."
I beg to move, "That this House do
agree with the Commons in said Amendments.*
Mr. LESLIE: It does not seem to me
to make very much difference whether the
first Amendment is passed, but the second
one seems to raise a rather important
question. I am not quite sure that if we
accept this Amendment we shall not be
consenting to something not before us at
the time. It seems to me the effect of
the alteration-I am speaking of the
second Amendment-would be to render
it illegal to have a wild bird's egg in your
possession at any time, and therefore it
would prevent the comparatively common
practice being followed of collecting wild
ducks' eggs from outlying nests and
hatching them under hens. That practice
has been followed for many years by preservers, and I do not think it is desirable
that it should be interfered with.
thought myself that such a procedure was
covered by paragraph (b) of the Clause.
But I am afraid the effect of accepting
this Amendment would render it illegal.
I
There is another point. If we insert
the words "bird, egg or nest in the same
category is protected" it would seem a
very open question whether possession of
a wild duck during the season would not
be illegal, because the eggs and nests are
protected all the year round.
We are
therefore brought up against a somewhat
difficult question as to whether we can
separate the words, and the words "bird,
egg or nest'' be read as though they were
"such bird, egg or nest as the case may
be." I venture to think that is the intention of the Commons' Amendment, but
I am afraid it is left an open question as
to whether that intention is carried out.
Therefore, in order to make it clear, I am
afraid I must oppose the motion of the
hon. and learned Senator.
Mr. CAMPBELL: If this Amendment
is incorporated in the Bill I think there
is very little danger on the score apprehended by the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie). The first part of Clause 6
is divided into three paragraphs. The
first paragraph prohibits the killing of
birds at a time when they are protected;
the second prohibits the wilful disturbance
of any egg or nest at a time when it is
protected; and then follows almost consequentially on the first two paragraphs
a paragraph making it illegal to sell or
to have in one's control or possession, or
to wilfully transport any bird or any
part of any bird or any egg or nest at a
time when a bird, egg or nest of
that class is protected. Each of those
three matters is dealt with singly
and separately. Under this Bill some
birds are protected all the year round,
and some are protected only for a specified period. The law at present is that
the wild duck is protected from 1st March
to 1st August. This Bill extends the
period of protection from 1st March to
12th August, the twelve days added being
very necessary. What is clearly provided
against is selling or possessing the bird
or egg or nest during its protected season,
be it twelve months or six months.
This
One thing we must always remember
in connection with this Bill is this. If
there is complaint in regard to any
class of bird the Ministry of Home
Affairs have complete power to deal with
it. They can classify a bird under any
category they please, and they can at any
time modify the provisions of the Bill as
to eggs and nests. I do not
think there is much reality in the
point raised by Senator Leslie.
House has already taken a great deal of
trouble in considering this Bill in its
various stages, and I personally am grateful to the House for all the trouble it has
I hope that in its present form
gone to.
the Bill will secure the utmost protection
for birds, the utmost benefit for
agriculture and the least likelihood of
widespread evasion of the law. After the
meticulous care this House has taken with
the Bill I think it would be a great pity
if, as a result of any technical objection
such as that taken by Senator Leslie, the
passage of the Measure should be
jeopardised. Another place will meet for
a formal sitting on Tuesday and then
adjourn until 24th November, and if this
House disagreed with the Amendment
made by the Commons the Bill might be
in great danger. I am sure that is not
the wish of any Member of this House,
and for these reasons I hope the hon.
and learned Senator will not press his
objection.
Amendment agreed to.
537 Contributory Pensions Bill-

8 OCTOBER 1931
Second Reading
Commons Amendment:
Clause 15, page 25, leave out the words
"guilty or."

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this House do agree with the Commons in the said Amendment. This is
purely a verbal Amendment to which I
think there can be no objection from any
quarter.
Amendment agreed to.
Commons Amendment:
Clause 20, page 13, lines 7 and 8, leave
out the words "thirty-first day of July,
nineteen hundred and thirty-one," and
insert the words "first day of January,
nineteen hundred and thirty-two."
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this House do agree with the Commons in the said Amendment." This
Amendment provides for an alteration in
the date when the Bill shall come into
operation. The Bill left this House in
May and we thought it might make
speedy progress in another place.
Originally the Bill was to come into
operation on 31st July last, but it did not
come before the Commons until quite recently, and therefore it is now necessary
to alter the date of its coming into operation to 1st January, 1932.
Amendment agreed to.
Widows', Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Pensions Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time." The last occasion that
legislation on the subject of Contributory
Pensions was discussed by hon. Members
was in 1929, and the House was then
asked to give its consent to the extension
of widows' pensions to certain classes of
widows, namely, the widows over 55 years
of age of men who had definitely followed
insurable work as a means of livelihood,
but on whose widows it had not been
found possible to confer a title to pension
at the time the Act of 1925 was passed.
The conditions of the title to pension of
those widows for whom provision was
made in the Act of 1929 was laid down
in Section 1 of that Act, and comprised
several alternative tests, only one of which
concerns us to-day. That test is the
condition requiring a widow to prove that
her husband had at some time within
three years of his death a normal occupation that was of an insurable character.
In the course of the day to day administration of the scheme cases arose where,
owing to illness or genuine unemployment,
the man on whose industrial record the
title depended had been prevented from
doing any work, and the Pension Authorities here took the view that, in these
circumstances, they were entitled to look
back to the period immediately before
sickness or unemployment began, and if
they then found a normal occupation of
an insurable character to regard that
normal occupation as having persisted up
to the date of death. That was, undoubtedly, the intention in the minds of
the Government when the Act of 1929 was
drawn. A similar view of the law was
taken by the administrative departments
in Great Britain.
Some months ago, however, a case was
decided in the High Court in England
in the course of which there was an expression of opinion from the Court which
did not entirely support the view hitherto
held by the departments. Consequently
it has been considered desirable to define
more clearly the meaning of the original
words in Section 1 of the Act of 1929,
and this is done by Clause 1, sub-Section
(1) paragraph (a) of the Bill now in the
hands of hon. Members. Paragraph (b)
of the same sub-Section puts right an
accidental omission from the Act of 1929
that resulted in a very small class of
widows being excluded from pension on
a technicality.
Hon. Members will notice that by Subsection (2), Clause 1, it is proposed to
make the whole Clause operative from
the 2nd January, 1930, which is the date
from which pensions would have been
payable if the Act of 1929 had stated
more definitely the terms of title.
My right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Labour, has informed me that his Department estimates that about eighty free
pensions will become payable under the
provisions of paragraph (a) of Sub-section
(1) and about twenty under paragraph
(b). The cost of these pensions will be
borne in the Pensions Account until the
widows reach 70 when they become auto-
539 Contributory Pensions Bill-
THE SENATE

Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
matically entitled to old-age pensions
without means tests and payable from the
Exchequer. As, however, the bulk of
these widows would, in any event, qualify
for ordinary means pensions at age 70,
the additional Exchequer charge as a consequence of this Clause will not exceed at
the point of its maximum incidence two
or three hundred pounds per annum. No
fresh Exchequer charge is imposed by any
of the other Clauses of the Bill.
I have dealt with Clause 1 at some
length as it constitutes the main purpose
of the Bill. At the same time, the
opportunity is being taken to clear up
certain difficulties and obscurities in
existing pensions law on such divergent
subjects as adopted children, pauper
lunatics, and in Clause 4 the effect on
title to pensions of reciprocal arrangements for health insurance purposes with
the Irish Free State.
We propose in Clause 2 to extend the
title to a child's allowance so that it
should be payable in respect of a child
who was de facto adopted prior to the
husband's death although a statutory
adoption order has not been taken out
by the widow until after her husband's
death. My right hon. Friend assures me
that the limited period during which this
Clause will operate will have the effect of
securing that only bona fide cases will be
dealt with. About forty children are
affected.
Clause 3 merely clarifies the law in regard to a class of pensioner which, I am
happy to say, is not numerous, namely,
pauper lunatics. No new principle of law
is enunciated, and this Amendment of
the original Section 12 of the Act of 1929
is designed to meet difficulties which have
been experienced in administration.
Clause 4 is best explained if I say that
owing to the close connection between the
health insurance and pensions schemes
doubts have arisen as to whether the
reciprocal arrangements between the
United Kingdom and the Irish Free State
with regard to national health insurance
do not operate, on the one hand, so as to
enable purely Free State health insurance
to assist in establishing a title to a contributory pension under the Northern
Ireland scheme, and, on the other hand,
SO as to deprive persons of rights to
pension acquired on purely Northern Ireland Pensions Insurance. I can say with
conviction that it was never our intention
that there should be such illogical features
in our pensions scheme, and we are
advised that this Clause will remove them.
I have now, I hope, given the House
an adequate explanation of the purposes
of this Bill. It breaks no new ground,
and while I hesitate to describe it as a
Measure to remove anomalies, I feel that
we cannot inappropriately look upon it as
an effort to adjust the Contributory
Pensions Acts in these few respects where
a big legislative experiment has failed to
stand the test of administrative practicability.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the House
will be glad to hear from the Noble
Viscount that the maximum cost of this
Bill is not likely to exceed £300 a year.
That is not an unimportant matter at the
moment, when our resources are strained
as we know they are strained.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Perhaps I
might be allowed to interrupt the hon.
and learned Member. I omitted to give
the cost of the eighty additional allowances to be granted under Clause 2. They
will amount to a total maximum additional charge of about £1,000, a sum
which is not of sufficient magnitude to
upset the balance of the scheme, and it is
payable from the Pensions Fund and not
from the Exchequer.
Mr.
LESLIE : I think with that
further explanation we will pass from that
aspect and come to the other details in the
Bill. Perhaps I may be permitted to
indicate some points that seem to me to
arise about which I should like information, either now or on the Committee
stage if that is considered more convenient, as indeed it very well might be.
The bulk of the Bill before us is based
on the Imperial Measure which received
the Royal Assent on 6th December, 1929.
I think that must be so.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That is so.
Mr. LESLIE: Therefore, looking at it
from that point of view, one would like to
be assured that in the first sub-clause the
interests of those who served in the late
War will not be neglected. Those who
are familiar with the Imperial Statute
will remember that special mention is
made of those who served in the Navy.
Reflection will remind us that it is not
practicable for this House, or even for
this Parliament, to legislate in any way
as regards war pensions. I think we
541 Contributory Pensions Bill
Unemployment Insurance 542
8 OCTOBER 1931
might perhaps at a later date be told.
what effect, if any, this Measure will have
on persons who appeared to be covered.
under the Imperial Act but are not
specially and separately covered by the
Bill before us.
As regards the second sub-clause of the
first Clause, I think that the noble
Viscount has already explained in answer
to a question raised on the Motion to
suspend Standing Orders, that repayment
will be made back to the date, the 2nd
January, and I presume that the cost of
that repayment, or rather it is really a
payment of arrears, is included in the
figures which the noble Viscount gave us.
Passing to Clause 2, the adoption
clause, if I remember rightly, when
one of the earlier Bills was before
us attention was called to the possible
anomalies and inconveniences which
might arise owing to the difference
in legislation between the two countries.
at the moment, and that I imagine
is entirely passing away, but I observe
there is inserted here
kind
a
of
time limit in the second paragraph of
Clause 2. I think the noble Viscount
referred to that. He suggested that the
time limit had been put in deliberately,
but I do not recognise it as being taken
from the Imperial Statute. I do not
know that there is anything more to be
told about that.
As regards the provisions dealing with.
certain persons of unsound mind, I see
that follows the wording of the English
Statute exactly, and although it may be
urged that the local authority which has
to maintain what is now called the Public
Mental Hospital, gets nothing out of the
pension, the pension is apparently to be
spent partly on additional comforts for
the unhappy patient, and if there is a
balance it goes to the benefit of the dependents of the patient. It is only fair
to point out that under the law as it
stands at the moment they get nothing.
There is, therefore, no change in that
respect, and I imagine that it is the
deliberate intention of Parliament there
should not be. I understand also that
any regulations made for the purpose of
the section will require the concurrence
of the Ministry of Finance, although that
is not expressed in the Bill. I think I
should be informed if that necessarily
follows from the provision in the section
of the principal Act to which reference
is made.
The noble Viscount spoke specially of
Clause 4 as being an attempt to prevent
unreasonable claims arising. I am not
sure, but I seem to remember that Section
20 of the Act referred to made some provision as regards seamen and people at
sea. I am not sure whether that was the
Section, but I should like to be assured
in the case of seamen, at all events, that
there will be no alteration made by this
Clause, if it is enacted, which would be
to the detriment of the persons concerned.
No doubt information on all these points
can be given better, I think perhaps, on
the Committee Stage than now, but I
thought it might save time to mention
the matters now. I think everybody
would wish the various anomalies and
discrepancies cleared away, and probably
this Bill as a whole is a suitable and inexpensive method of doing so.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will give
the information required at a later stage.
Motion, "That this Bill be now read
a Second time," put and agreed to.
Unemployment Insurance (No. 2) Bill.

Bill." (Mr. Long).

[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Increase of limit on outstanding advances).
The
Mr. LONG: Senator Leslie asked
what arrangements have been made for
the repayment of the Unemployment
Fund debt which can be increased to
£2,600,000 under the present Bill.
answer is that no special arrangements
are required as, under the re-insurance
scheme, the debt on the Northern Irish
Fund will be reduced in consonance with
the debt on the Unemployment Fund of
Great Britain. Further borrowing is now
being stopped so the debt will not increase. Under the re-insurance arrangements the debt now on the Northern
Ireland Unemployment Insurance Fund
moves correspondingly with the debt on
the Fund in Great Britain.
543 Unemployment Insurance

THE SENATE
Adjournment
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Further amendment of
s. 18 (2) of 18 and 19 Geo. 5, c. 3).
Mr. LONG: Senator Leslie asked
whether any transitional benefit will be
payable after the period of 48 months
mentioned in the Bill. The answer is
"Yes," but only to claimants who have
made claims before the period has expired
and for these claims only for twelve
months from the date of claim. Under
existing legislation transitional benefit
begins to expire on 17th instant and disappears on 16th October, 1932. The Bill
extends each of these dates by six months.
Mr. LESLIE: I thank the Parliamentary Secretary for eliciting that
information. The fact is, I think, that
our Bill will be exactly in accordance with
the English Act, and with the interim
report to which I referred on the Second
Reading.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

ordered to stand part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 1 (Amendments in s. 1 of 20
Geo. 5, c. 17.)
Mr. LESLIE: I asked a small question
on Clause 1 at an earlier stage having
regard to the provisions for those who
served in the late war. I think, perhaps,
the right hon. Gentleman the Minister of
Labour is proposing to answer, if not on
this Clause, perhaps on Third Reading.
Mr. ANDREWS: The Act of 1929 both
at Westminster and here made certain
concessions to those who served in his
Majesty's forces.
I think, perhaps, my
hon. and learned Friend is confusing, to
some extent, the Act of 1931 and the 1929
Act. Our Acts are exactly the same.
Mr. LESLIE: The effect is that nothing in this Bill will in any way
prejudice cases of this class?
Mr. ANDREWS: That is quite correct.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

in the administration of the Contributory

part of the Bill.

Tuesday, 24th November, 1931.
The Senate which had stood adjourned since Tuesday, 13th Octobermet at a Quarter after Three of the
Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.

during the Recess :
Ministry of Agriculture:

for the year 1930 on the Sea and Inland

School Committee of Management.

1930.

Accounts.

of the Civil Authorities (Special Powers)

:-
That they had considered the said Bill,
and examined the allegations thereof,
which were found to be true; that the
Committee had gone through the Bill,
and directed the same to be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Order Confirmation (Coleraine
Waterworks) Bill.
