Bills Presented
Business of the House
21 NOVEMBER 1931
Royal Assent.
Mr. LONG: I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to inform
the Senate that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the
following Bill:-
Widows', Orphans', and Old Age
Contributory Pensions.
BILLS PRESENTED.
Assurance Companies (Underwriters)
Bill,
"to extend the provisions of the Assurance Companies Act, 1909, in its
application to underwriters," was presented by Mr. Long, read a First time,
and ordered to be printed. (Bill No. 6).
Poultry Diseases Bill,
"to empower the Ministry of Agriculture to apply the Diseases of Animals
Acts, and to make Orders thereunder,
for the purpose of preventing contagious
disease in poultry," was presented by
Mr. Long, read a First time, and ordered
to be printed. (Bill No. 7).
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, “That this
House do now adjourn until Tuesday
next." The intention is that we should
meet on Tuesday and Wednesday of next
week.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I
wish to ask the Deputy-Leader of the
House two questions. Before doing so I
am sure I am interpreting the wishes of
the House when I say how sorry we are
to learn that the Leader of the House
(Viscount Charlemont) is not very well.
We hope he will speedily be restored to
his usual good health. The first question
I want to ask is whether, when the
Valuation Bill, of which there is so much
talk, leaves the other House it will come
before the Senate in the ordinary course,
or whether it will be regarded as a
purely financial Measure. The second
question is whether the Deputy-Leader
of the House can inform us of the
probable date of the end of this Session.
Mr. LONG: I should like to have
notice of the first question. With regard to the second question, the probable
date of the end of this Session is not yet
sufficiently clear to enable me to give a
definite answer to the right hon. and
gallant Member.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty-five minutes after
Three o'clock until Tuesday next.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 1st December, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Labour:
The Contributory Pensions (Oversea
Claims) Regulations (Northern Ireland),
1931..
The Unemployment Insurance (Insurance Industry Special Scheme) (Amendment), Special Order, 1931.
The National Health Insurance (Duration of Insurance) Regulations, 1931.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Private Business.
Robinson's Divorce Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee were considered and agreed
to.
Question to Minister.
CONTROL OF DANCE HALLS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether in view of
the danger to public morals resulting
from want of effective public control in
dance halls, particularly in rural areas,
the Government will consider favourably
such amendment of the law as will secure
that the licensing and supervision of these
halls will be brought at least up to the
standard obtaining in England and Scotland.
May I say that I am sure hon. Members in all parts of the House will
welcome the noble Lord, the Leader of
the House, to his place again after his
recent illness. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
:
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House) I am much obliged to hon.
Members for their kind expression of
sympathy.
The Government has no evidence indicating that the present position in regard
to the control of dance halls in Northern
Ireland is such as to constitute a danger to
public morals, or that the existing provisions of law relating to the licensing
and supervision of such halls have proved
to be inadequate.
The whole matter of the control of
such halls is governed by Part IV of the
Public Health Acts Amendment Act,
1890, which applies generally both to
England and Ireland, under which provision is made for the licensing by the
licensing justices, subject to such conditions and restrictions as they may see
fit to impose, of places ordinarily used
for public dancing or music, and under
which it is made an offence to keep or
use any house or other place for the purpose unless so licensed.
Part IV of the Act is adoptive only
and is primarily applicable to urban
authorities who are mainly concerned.
By Section 5 of the Act, however, power
is given enabling any rural authority to be
invested with all the powers of an urban
authority, and although this Section only
applies to England, a corresponding provision, giving similar power in Ireland,
will be found in Section 1 of the Public
Health (Ireland) Act, 1896.
It will, therefore, be seen that the law
in Northern Ireland in regard to the
control of such halls is similar to that
prevailing in England, and there is no
reason to suppose that the justices in
Ireland in granting such licences are less
careful in imposing adequate conditions
and restrictions than justices in England.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Arising out of the
answer given by the noble Viscount, may I
say that I was particularly struck with the
hon. and learned Senator's reference in
his question to his satisfaction with the
state of the law in England and Scotland.
What a pity that he and the party which
he represents did not always think so.
Instead, they were always decrying and
slandering Great Britain. It is well, however, that at long last they have found
out their mistake, and that they do now
admit there is something good even in
England and Scotland. I agree that
there should be proper control over dance
halls, but surely the hon. and learned
Member must admit that the supervision
in Ulster is much better and much more
effective than it is in the Irish Free State
1 DECEMBER 1931 Grants for Elementary Schools 558
Oral Answers
where, I understand, the dance halls are
conducted in such a way as to have provoked the strong condemnation of the
heads of the Roman Catholic Church.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Arising out of
the speech of Senator Quigley, may I say
that he must be very easily satisfied when
he quotes us as approving of the fact
that the Government in England is capable of running a dance. He has stated
that the party to which we belong have
for a long time been finding fault with
England and ridiculing everything England has done. He went on to say that
he was glad to see we were satisfied now.
But I would point out to the hon. Member that we have at least been consistent,
because we have always held the one view
throughout our careers. On the other
hand the Prime Minister of Northern
Ireland, although for years and years
he spent himself in the services of Great
Britain, has since declared that at no
price would he go back under the British
Parliament now.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask if the
same condemnation Senator Quigley has
referred to was not also extended to dance
halls in Northern Ireland? Can the noble
Viscount state if any rural authorities in
Northern Ireland have adopted that very
salutary provision of the Public Health
Act?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: With regard to Senator Campbell's supplementary question, as to whether any part of
these Public Health Acts have been
adopted by rural authorities in Northern
Ireland, I am not in a position to give
him a definite answer now, but I will do
so at a future sitting.
A CATHOLIC PUPIL'S SCHOLAR-
SHIP.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether in May,
1931, Richard P. Thornton, a Catholic
pupil of an elementary school, sat with
about 40 others for a junior elementary
scholarship under the scheme of the Coleraine Regional Committee, taking second
place; whether in the notification of his
success he was directed to attend Coleraine
Academical Institution, a non-Catholic
school; whether the Institution is the only
secondary school in the Coleraine area;
whether the lad's father requested the
Committee to allow the boy to go to St.
Columb's College, Derry, where he could
get the subjects he wished to study, and
for which no provision existed in the
Institution; whether the scheme of the
Committee provides that consideration
shall be given to the wishes of the candidate; whether the Committee have refused
to allow the boy the benefit of the scholarship so long as he attends St. Columb's
College; and whether the Ministry will
take action to secure to the boy the
scholarship won by him.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The facts
of the case are as stated by the hon. and
learned Senator.
In reply to the last part of his question,
the difficulties which have arisen in regard
to the award of this boy's scholarship
have been engaging the attention of the
Ministry of Education, and correspondence with the Regional Education Committee has been proceeding for some time.
A letter on the subject was addressed to
the Committee so late as yesterday, and
the hon. and learned Senator may rest
assured that the Ministry will keep the
matter under its most careful scrutiny.
Mr. DORMAN: I am sure it is the
desire of every Member of this House
that if any injustice has been done to any
child of any citizen in Northern Ireland
calculated to retard his progress in education it should be remedied at the earliest
opportunity.
Grants for Elementary Schools.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move:
That this House views with grave
concern the great disproportion in the
distribution of public money on the denominational elementary schools of the
province, £564,000 having been expended from
the
from the passing of
Education Act, 1923, till October,
1931, on the building or reconditioning
of 34 provided and 572 transferred
schools belonging to the majority in
Northern Ireland now wholly maintained at the public charge, while,
apart from the liquidation of
some obligations transmitted by the
Imperial Government ten years ago, no
public money has been expended on the
559 Grants for Elementary Schools
Grants for Elementary Schools 560
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
building or reconditioning of the 700
schools of the minority in Northern Ireland with the exception of £7,370 on
four 4 and 2 committee schools; and
that all public money for the urgent
necessary repairs of the schools of the
minority attended by 72,000 children
has been inequitably denied.
Somewhat over £2,000,000 was voted
by Parliament to meet the expenditure of
the Ministry of Education on primary,
secondary, and university education in the
year 1930-31. The cost of the primary
education of the 2,000,000 children
in the Province was about £8 10s per
child, or about £1 8s per head of our
total population. In Great Britain the
yearly cost is about £1 per head of the
population; in Canada £3 per head; in
New Zealand £2 15s; in Australia £1 10s
and in France 9s. The highest cost in
the world is in the Transvaal, where it
is about £5 per head of the European
population. An analysis reveals how deep
and wide is the channel through which
public money flows one way, and how
shallow and narrow the channel through
which it flows another way.
For every
£1 given for the building and reconditioning of the schools of the minority between.
the years 1923 and 1931 £75 was given
to the majority.
The public elementary schools are to
be, as during the last 100 years, denominational. The Education Act of
1923 foreshadowed a secular system. In
that respect it has become a museum
piece. The Catholic children in the
primary schools number over 72,000 out
of 200,000. They are the Cinderellas of
your primary education. The Methodist
children number some 6,700-about 3 per
cent. of the total. You are diligent about
their interests. In the year 1925 there
existed only two provided and ten transferred schools. In October, 1931, there
were 34 provided and 572 transferred
schools accommodating, perhaps, over
80,000 pupils. Transferred schools and
provided schools give simple Bible instruction. Transferred and provided
schools are Protestant denominational
schools in truth and in fact. They are
maintained wholly at the public charge.
The tendency towards the transfer of the
Protestant elementary schools is plainly
manifest. If the drift continues we shall
see the schools of the Protestant denominations under the euphemistic name of
provided and transferred schools, altogether a charge on the State and
altogether denominational.
""
A clerical speaker at the Armagh
Protestant Synod on 2nd October last,
spoke of these schools as being "all
Protestant with one or two exceptions.'
The Protestant Primate at the same
synod said Roman Catholics would not
use the provided schools. You will say
these provided and transferred schools are
open to all denominations. So are the
Catholic voluntary schools. Their doors
are barred against none. Probably twothirds of the Protestant pupils of this
Province are now taught in schoolhouses,
which have been built or are maintained
altogether at the expense of the general
public.
The Act of 1930 smoothed the path of
the transfer of Protestant voluntary
schools to the local authorities by ensuring to the former managers of these
schools the continuance in a large measure
of their control. When a school is transferred to a Regional Committee or when
a school is built in the place of a transferred school the ex-managers carry forward their authority into such school, and
the appointments in the school are determined according to the denominational
representation of the school management
committee, three-fourths of whom are the
managers and parents of children at the
school.
But the Act of 1930, which made the
transfer of Protestant schools so easy and
advantageous, put one further difficulty
in the way of the transfer of Catholic
schools by enacting that the parents of
any ten children in a Transferred school
can insist on "simple Bible instruction"
being imparted in that school. Under
that system the teacher reads the Bible
and makes his running explanation of the
text. If he is a Presbyterian his comment must of a necessity have a Presbyterian tinge. If he is an Episcopalian it
will not have a Presbyterian tinge. If
he is a Unitarian it will have neither an
Episcopalian nor a Presbyterian tinge.
If he is an Agnostic I do not know what
tinge it will have. No man can teach in
religion what he does not believe. The
Education Act of 1930 expressly excludes from "Bible instruction" "instruction as to any tenet which is
distinctive of any particular religious
561 Grants for Elementary Schools
Grants for Elementary Schools 562
1 DECEMBER 1931
denomination." On which I content myself with only quoting some words of your
great leader, Mr. Disraeli, whose features
are still flaunted on your banners in July.
Speaking of the Education Bill of 1870,
which set up "simple Bible instruction"
in England, he said:—
con-
Although no creed or catechism of
any denomination is to be introduced
the schoolmaster is to have the power
of teaching, enforcing, and explaining
the Holy Scriptures when he reads.
Now he cannot do that without drawing inferences and conclusions, and
what will those inferences and
clusions be but dogmas? You will not
entrust the priest or Presbyter with the
privilege of expounding the Holy
Scriptures, but you are inventing and
establishing a new sacerdotal class.
What then, shall we have the Bible
without note or comment? Not so, for
there are but few of us who read chapters
in either the Old or the New Testament who do not require to have considerable comment upon them."
Was Mr. Disraeli right? The Fourand-Two Committee School is a vanishing type. On 31st December, 1929,
there were 93 such schools-less than 5
per cent. of the schools of the Province.
In Belfast 25 of such schools existed in
the year 1928. In 1929 there were only
16. From the first planting of it here
the Four-and-Two Committee has been a
sickly exotic. Why not cut it down?
Why cumbers it to the ground?
In regard to the other voluntary
schools you will not sanction any public
money helping to pay for the current repairs to the schools, and you will not
suffer them to get more than the
niggardly fraction of half of the
cost of lighting, heating and cleansing.
Any money given to repairs would
go exclusively to increase the efficiency
of the schools. These costs hang
like a millstone round the neck of
the friends of these schools. We should
be glad to keep these schools as up-to-date
as the provided and transferred schools.
were, both sets on the same financial
footing. We must cut our coat according to our cloth. The minority, not by
any means rich in this world's goods,
have made heavy, almost heroic sacrifices for their schools. The hope that
these schools would gradually fade out
by closure or transfer to local authorities
is vain.
These authorities are almost
without exception hostile.
What is the condition of not a few of
the voluntary schools as seen by the
inspectors in 1929-30. I quote from the
Ministry's report:-
"There is no perceptible general
improvement of the voluntary schools
to enable them to keep pace with their
more fortunate fellows, nor is any
general improvement to be hoped for.
Conditions in them remain as before.
It would seem reasonable to require
schools of this kind to be put into a
satisfactory condition if they are to
remain in operation and to receive the
Ministry's grants, and the problem.
which they must present to their
managers, who are almost without
exception persons of culture and
humane dispositions, must be harassing
to a degree. It is difficult to understand why the problem persists, and
its solution is not impossible.”
No, by no means impossible. You
can easily solve it. In the case of
numerous voluntary schools this picture
has no application, but in the case of
too many others it is not overdrawn.
Thousands of children are thus penalised
because their parents cannot in conscience
accept the type of teaching which recommends itself to you, because they hold
fast to the Christian type of education.
These children are being well grounded
in secular matters. Their schools are not
one whit inferior to the council schools in
secular instruction. For confirmation of
my statement I appeal to a witness,
surely never prejudiced in favour of our
case, the Minister of Education, who said
at Castlecaulfield in July:-
In the last eight years something
like 200,000 children have been taught
according to the best modern practice
in secular methods, and not one child
out of these 200,000 but has had
religious teaching as well.
Of this 200,000 the Catholic children
number 72,000 who are "taught
according to the best modern practice in
secular methods." Yet you will not replace a broken pane or a loose slate in
their school rooms. Pause for a moment
or two. Think of the sum saved to the
public pocket by these schools, think of
the deadlock that would arise if the
managers and their people, harassed by
recurring burdens, were to close down
563 Grants for Elementary Schools
Grants for Elementary Schools 564
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
their schools and throw upon the Ministry
or the Regional Committees the huge task
of providing accommodation out of the
rates at a cost of I dare say £30 a head.
If the local authorities had to find schoolhouses for the 72,000 Catholic children
the capital cost of building might run
from 1 million to 2 million pounds,
which might entail in interest a yearly
burden of perhaps £100,000 on the taxpayers. In the actual state of the public
finance this could not be done. A few
weeks ago a provided school was opened
in County Down at a cost of £43,000 for
about 1,200 pupils--about £35 a head.
Through the different stages of these
educational controversies one notes how
the machinery of the Orange Society is at
the critical point brought into action.
The Government and that society are
closely interconnected, acting and reacting upon each other. This inter-action
explains much.
When one thinks of the
influence of that Society in this part of
Ireland to-day one is reminded of Queen
Victoria's words in 1869, as recorded in
Sir Sydney Lee's official life of "King
Edward VII," relative to his visit to Ireland that year as Prince of Wales. Her
Majesty "added her fears (to the Prime
Minister) that the Prince's equerry and
friend, Lord Hamilton, was infecting him
with Orangeism-a factious sentiment
which was a disqualification for any Irish
responsibilities.
"(
""
Tempora Mutantur. To-day it is the
open sesame to Northern Irish responsibilities. It speaks the last word in
educational policy. The Society gives
the command. The Government springs
to attention. In these days of anarchic
forces at work against all religion one
would think that the time has come when
men should sink little prejudices and give
credit to all denominations for doing their
best to mould better citizens. Ours are
reasonable propositions. You reject
them as they have nothing but reason to
recommend them.
Since the passing of the Education Act
of 1930 18 months have gone. Under
that Act the Ministry of Education is
authorised to pay one-half of the expenditure on the provision of equipment
of a new voluntary school or on the
alteration, enlargement, reconstruction,
or equipment of an existing voluntary
school. The gushing stream of public
money, which has richly fertilised the
schools of the majority, still fertilises
them. A thin trickle of a streamlet is
all there is for the schools of the minority.
I doubt if public money is helping the
building of more than three such schools,
or the alteration or enlargement of one
existing voluntary school. I know one
existing school in respect of which a small
grant was asked in February. After
nine months no progress has been made.
If I am in error I shall be glad to be set
right.
One finds it refreshing to turn from
this picture to other pictures. At the
opening of a new Central Catholic School
at Coatbridge last month, Sir H. Keith,
chairman of the Lanarkshire education
authority, said the purpose of that
authority was that every child, without
distinction or creed, should have the very
best facilities that could be afforded, and
the Catholic Archbishop of Glasgow, Dr.
Mackintosh, voiced his congratulations
on the fairness and thoroughness with
which Scotland had solved its scholastic
difficulties where religious differences had
been involved. It was a distinguished
Scotsman, the late Lord Haldane, who
said you must leave people the liberty to
teach the religion desired by the parents.
Scotland is doing that.
Look at this other picture. In
September last the Protestant Archbishop of Dublin, Dr. Gregg, opened a
new school in Offaly, when the Rev. R.
Saunderson Griffin, Rector of Rathangan,
declared that a deep debt of gratitude.
was due to the Free State Minister of
Education for the issuing of the twothirds grant for the building and furnishing of the school, and that without the
timely assistance of the Government it
would have been a difficult task to find
the necessary funds. You must admit
that the Free State Government has
always turned a ready, receptive ear to
the educational claims of the scattered
Protestant communities under its jurisdiction.
Then as to England and the prospect of
the voluntary schools there. You
remember the recent Bill of the Labour
Government for raising the school-leaving
age. A Catholic Labour member moved
and carried an amendment that the Bill
should not come into operation till public
funds were provided to defray the cost
of the extra accommodation thereby
required by the voluntary schools. The
Conservative members, including the
565 Grants for Elementary Schools
1 DECEMBER 1931
Grants for Elementary Schools 566
con-
Ulster members, voted for that
cession to the Catholic and Anglican
schools. The Conservative party is now
in power and will doubtless take up the
cause of the voluntary schools afresh.
Ulster Conservatives will, as a matter of
consistency, vote with their party.
Scotland in this matter deals fairly
with its minority. The Free State deals
fairly with its minority. England, we
may trust, is on the eve of doing tardy
justice to its minority. Is Northern
Ireland to remain the exception? Is the
minority here to be the only minority in
these Islands with a burning sense of the
wrong done by the State in the education
of its poorer children?
Mr. QUIGLEY: I do not see why the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell)
should have cause for any grave concern
over this question of elementary
education. It is a well-known fact that
when our Northern Government came
into power our school accommodation was
in a deplorable state. After the most
careful consideration it was decided to
remodel the system of education so far
as control was concerned, and to place
it on a basis which would put some
measure of control in the hands of those
who had to provide the money.
Protestant schools have come within that
system, and accordingly they qualify for
the necessary grants.
The
For some reasons best known to themselves, those in charge of the Roman
Catholic schools have declined to conform to the conditions which would entitle them to receive similar grants. In
other words, they refuse to allow their
children to be taught secular subjects
side by side with Protestant children.
That is a peculiar attitude to take up
considering that in after life all classes
and creeds must work together for the
common good. It is a thousand pities
that the Roman Catholic Church should
enforce this separation, because it is my
belief that if children were taught in the
same classroom they would become much
more friendly and would grow up to be
much more tolerant towards each other
in after life.
Full facilities are offered in the
schools for religious teaching, and surely
that should satisfy the desires of any
donomination. It is my opinion that a
repetition of many of the divisions and
much of the strife of the past would be
avoided in the future if the children of
the different denominations associated
together more than they have been
allowed to do in the past, and in my
view the best time to bring them together is when they are young and their
minds impressionable. The friendships
formed early in life are those which are
usually lasting.
If the Roman Catholic schools do not
receive as much money from the Government as the hon. and learned Senator
thinks they are entitled to, the remedy
is in their own hands. Let him use his
great influence with his Church to have
these schools brought within popular
control, and then they will receive as
much public assistance as other schools.
I think the schools which have been
erected under the present system are a
credit to the Government and to the
whole community. They are spacious,
well lighted, and healthy, and in a short
time the children will be as well provided
for in that respect as the children in
any part of the United Kingdom.
In addition to the many new schools
which have been built many old buildings have been remodelled and brought
up-to-date. The teachers are loyally and
efficiently carrying out their duties, and
on the whole our system of education
has made great progress since it came
under the control of our own Government. There is no necessity whatever
for any grave concern, and if any grave
concern does exist in the mind of the
hon. and learned Senator there is a very
simple way of removing it. But the
remedy lies in the hands of the Church
of which the hon. and learned Member
is a member, and not in the hands of
the Government. For these reasons I
hope the House will reject the Motion.
Col. SHARMAN CRAWFORD: If
I am not out of order may I first of all

just happened in this House.
early Parliamentary days-and I believe

of the Mover of the Motion before the
House, and that of the last speaker
(Mr. Quigley) was to my mind out of

be allowed to continue in this House.

are being brought up to hate each other.
May I ask the noble Leader of the House
if it is not a fact that in England, where
hon.

and learned
my

am utterly opposed to the Motion, and I

CO-
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In raising
once again this question of building grants
for voluntary schools the hon. and learned
Member has gone over ground which has
been frequently traversed in this House
and in another place, and I do not think
he has added anything fresh to the
arguments we have heard so often before
on this difficult question. He has told us
of the large sums which have been spent
on the building and reconditioning of
provided and transferred schools by the
education authorities since the passing of
the Education Act of 1923, and in doing
so he has borne testimony to the great
success of that legislation, a success on
which I think the people of the Province
are to be very greatly congratulated. But
the burden of his complaint is that those
managers who have not been willing to
place their schools under public control
have not been able to secure public funds
to the same extent for building purposes
and have suffered therefore, as the hon.
Member thinks, an unfair handicap in
their educational advantages.
Now, I do not propose to go over again
a recital of the reasons why this Government, in
common with every other
Government in the British Isles and in
every part of the British Empire, has
found it impossible to grant the entire
cost of the provision of school buildings
to private individuals or religious bodies,
to be held by the latter as their private
property. It has been shown in debates
on this subject more than once that the
grants available to voluntary school
managers for building purposes in Northern Ireland are given on conditions
more favourable to denominational bodies
than in any part of the British Empire,
except possibly in the Irish Free State.
It is well known that the hon. and
learned Member's co-religionists in
England would be
be gratified beyond
belief if building grants were open to
them there on the same conditions as in
this country. The Government of Northern Ireland has made every reasonable
effort to meet the difficulties of voluntary
managers, both in the legislation on this
subject passed last year and previously,
and we cannot admit for one moment
that our attitude has been other than
sympathetic.
We recognise our obligation to provide
the best educational facilities for all
sections of the community, and I would
remind the hon. and learned Member
that the voluntary schools share as fully
as the others in all direct grants from the
Ministry towards the salaries of teachers
and in other ways. But if the whole
cost of the provision and maintenance of
school buildings is to be borne on public
funds, the schools must be transferred to
the local education authorities and become the property of the public. There
is no other way of doing it, and the safeguards to secure the appointments of
teachers acceptable to the parents
established in last year's Act render it
possible for every denomination, and I do
not exclude Roman Catholics, to transfer
their schools and retain teachers of their
own faith and secure a religious education
in conformity with their own religious
beliefs.
If voluntary managers cannot accept
their views and refuse to transfer their
schools, they cannot expect to secure the
same facilities for new buildings as are
available in the case of schools handed
over to public ownership and control.
With regard to the reference of the hon.
and learned Senator to the opening of a
new school at Coatbridge, I think this
Roman
school, though attended by
Catholic pupils, must have been provided by the education authority.
There are no voluntary schools in Scotland; they are
they are all either transferred
or provided, a system which is unacceptable to the Roman Catholic Church
in this country.
But I should like to remind the House
that the whole educational policy of the
Government since its establishment in
569 Grants for Elementary Schools
1 DECEMBER 1931
Grants for Elementary Schools 570
1921 has recognised the existence of the
voluntary schools and the need of provision for new buildings under voluntary
management. The Motion we are discussing reminds us that even before the
Education Act of 1923 was passed into
law the Ministry of Education honoured
the obligations of the former administration in the matter of voluntary school
building grants, and moneys were freely
voted by Parliament for this
in
purpose
every case where there was
reason to
believe that a promise of a grant had been
made by the old National Board. I am
informed on good authority that no legal
obligation to meet these claims rested
upon our Government, and the
tinuance of the grant
grant system
sanctioned through a desire to be
scrupulously fair in this matter and
owing to the pressing need of securing
better accommodation for the pupils.
conwas
Then came the 1923 Act, and generous
provision for new voluntary school buildings was made, as the House knows, in
Section 16 of that Act. This Section
enables education authorities to contribute towards the provision or reconstruction of voluntary schools, subject,
it is true, to the condition of setting up
a committee for the management of the
school, and it was confidently believed
by the promoters of the Act that this
Section would fully meet the claims of
voluntary school managers. We are now
told that the condition of control by a
"four and two" committee makes it impossible for Roman Catholics to work the
Section, but, seeing that this same condition is freely accepted by Roman
Catholics in England, the Ministry had
no reason in 1923 to suppose that it
would be an impassable barrier to
managers to accept grants.
Indeed, the hon. and learned Member
knows as well as I do that several Roman
Catholic managers accepted the "four and
two" committee and built schools under
the arrangement in Section 16, and I can
tell him that notwithstanding the opposition to the Section and the passing of the
Amending Act last year, the Ministry
has recently approved further proposals
on the part of local education authorities
to make grants for new schools under
Roman Catholic management in accordance with the procedure set forth in the
Section. If managers have availed themselves of this provision of the 1923 Act
only to the extent of £7,370, it is
certainly not the fault of the Government,
who had no means of knowing in 1923
that so many managers had this fear of
sharing their authority with a committee
largely made up of persons nominated by
themselves.
I now come to the Act of 1930, passed
in circumstances still in the recollection
of the House. I need not remind the
House that a Government Amendment
was moved during the Committee Stage
in another place to meet the appeals
made by the Opposition on behalf of the
voluntary schools, and under Section 10
of the Act grants to the extent of 50
per cent. of the cost can now be made
by my Ministry direct, for the building
of new voluntary schools and the alteration, enlargement, reconstruction and
equipment of existing voluntary schools.
under individual management without
the necessity of setting up a management committee. The Act passed into
law in June of last year, and steps were
immediately taken in the Ministry of
Education to frame the necessary detailed regulations for the administration
of Section 10, and appropriate Statutory
Rules were placed before Parliament as
soon as the House resumed at the beginning of last winter. Since then the
Ministry has had a considerable number
of applications for grants under the
Section and the examination and investigation of these have been proceeding as
speedily as circumstances permit.
It must not be forgotten that the
building of a new school entails a great
deal of preliminary work in regard to
the choosing of a suitable site, the
preparation of plans and specifications,
and the legal formalities necessary for
the vesting of the school plot in trustees,
and all these things have to be carefully
examined in the Ministry and this takes
time, and often more time than is
realised by a layman. A school building
has to be erected to stand a hundred
years and even longer, and it is
absolutely essential that the greatest possible attention should be paid beforehand to the preparation of the plans and
specifications. Progress is however being
made, and I am able to inform the
House that the Ministry has received
proposals for new buildings and for reconstructions of existing schools which
would involve a gross expenditure of
£130,000, half of which sum would fall
upon the Government's funds. Of this
substantial amount the Ministry has up
571 Grants for Elementary Schools
Grants for Elementary Schools 572
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
to the present given its approval to an
expenditure of £64,000, which is to
provide for two large and fully equipped
schools in the City of Belfast and one
large school in Londonderry. The
Ministry's share of this cost is £32,000.
The remaining applications, involving a
further estimated gross expenditure of
£66,000, are being dealt with in the
Ministry with all convenient speed.
I think it will be evident to the House
that the Government's action in making
these substantial grants to voluntary
school managers cannot justly be described as ungenerous or inequitable, and
I am sorry that the hon. and learned
Member cannot see this in a more favourable light. I think, however, that an
impartial observer looking at the legislation, both of 1923 and of 1930, on
this matter will be bound to admit that
the Government has gone to the utmost
practicable limit to meet the claims of
the voluntary school managers, and has
shown a very real regard for the conscientious convictions of those of the
latter who are unwilling to transfer
their schools to the education authorities.
The last part of the Motion raises the
question of repairs to existing buildings.
Even in the days of the former Commissioners of National Education there
were no funds available from public
sources for repairs to privately owned
schools. The only cases in which the
Board of Works maintained the buildings were those in which the ownership
was vested in the Commissioners, a procedure which as a general rule did not
commend itself to the Roman Catholic
authorities. In the 1923 Act there was
provision for the payment of a moiety by
the local education authorities for necessary repairs to voluntary schools, provided that their management was
trusted to a "four and two" committee.
This was the utmost limit to which the
Government could go, and it is quite
impossible to expect that either the local
authority or my Ministry could make
maintenance grants to schools which remain entirely under private management.
The concession of grants for capital expenditure in the 1930 Act for this class
of school is the maximum assistance
which can be made and I venture to
say should be made by the Government.
en-
In conclusion, I would just like to
point out that the use of the terms
"majority" and "minority" in the
Motion is, I think, apt to be misleading.
There are a good many schools belonging
to what the hon. and learned Member
calls the "majority" which still remain
under voluntary management and which,
therefore, remain in exactly the same
category as the schools of the "minority"
for which he speaks. The distinction is
rather between those which accept the
principle of public control and those
which remain under private management.
The Protestant managers who, for whatever reason, do not wish to transfer their
buildings and there is still a considerable
number of them are accorded exactly
the same treatment as Roman Catholic
managers of voluntary schools, and it will
be seen, therefore, that the distinction
does not follow denominational or
political lines.
I confidently appeal to the House to
reject this Motion, the more so, as I am
convinced that the voluntary schools are
and will be accorded fair and even
generous treatment in this question of
grants from public funds.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I entirely
associate myself with the manner in
which my hon. and learned colleague
(Mr. Campbell) has introduced this
Motion. Let me say for my colleague and
for all those whom we represent, that we
would be glad to see the greatest possible
facilities and the most advantageous
system of education provided for all denominations, provided the same facilities
and advantages were afforded to those
whom we represent. I do hope this debate will have one effect, and that is that
it will clear the air and expose the
fallacy of the statement made from time
to time by responsible persons who are
opposed to our religious beliefs, to the
effect that our schools have received preferential treatment. When it is realised
that the schools of their denomination
have received in grants £564,000, and
that the schools managed by those we represent have received only £7,330, surely
any honest person who differs from us on
this question is bound to admit that we
have received anything but preferential
treatment.
For our conscience sake we have had to
make terrific sacrifices.
At the present
time money is the greatest test of all, and
I think even the Government must admit
that in this respect Catholic schools have
suffered a great hardship. When one
573 Grants for Elementary Schools 1 DECEMBER 1931
Grants for Elementary Schools 574
ground so fully and effectively I will
content myself with associating myself
with his remarks.
ponders over the fact that we are onethird of the population and contribute
our proportion of the rates and taxes, it
is appalling to think that for educational
buildings we receive only £1 for every
£75 received by those who differ from us.
I have been surprised to read the speeches
on this question of those who ought to be
regarded as responsible spokesmen. They
have referred favourably to the Government's treatment of Roman Catholic
schools by representing that these schools
receive preferential treatment. I have
no doubt that many of these people are
perfectly honest in their opinion, but
they have been neglectful in not making
themselves acquainted with the true state
of affairs.
The noble Viscount, in his reply, has
stated that applications have been received by his Ministry for grants for
building purposes amounting to £130,000,
and that the Government have provisionally approved of an expenditure of
£32,000 in grants. I believe the Government have turned down a further scheme
involving an expenditure of about
£40,000. I do not think it can be contended that that is a fair contribution
from the Government and the rates in
view of the sacrifices which the minority
have, undoubtedly, made for the sake of
their conscience since the passing of the
1930 Act.
He
I think there is one thing the House
will be agreed upon to-day, and that is
that England has been singularly unfortunate in the champion she has chosen
in the person of Senator Quigley.
has told us that the English have paid
every attention to the supervision of
dance halls in that country, but that
when the Northern Government took
over their mis-managed schools they
were in a deplorable condition. That is,
I think, a fair and just interpretation
of the two speeches made to-day by
Senator Quigley. He said the remedy
for our grievance is in our own hands.
In other words, he suggested that those
whom we represent should be prepared
to sacrifice their conscience and to do
what we conscientiously believe is not the
proper thing to do in order to obtain
money to build and equip our schools.
That is a thing which I do not think
any decent or respectable section of the
community, or any man, should be asked
to do. After all, a man's conscience
should not be purchasable with money.
Since my colleague has covered the

Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
do not wish to delay the House for any

full statement given by the noble Lord,

on
They still insist
having the
Romanist schools under their own control, and they have exactly the same
benefits as Protestant schools in a similar
position.
Then, on the other hand,
while the Church people, the Presbyterians, the Methodists, and all the
other sections are compelled to accept
training in one building, we are
compelled to
for
provide specially
the training of Roman Catholics
in another building and another place
altogether. So that really I believe the
Northern Government has done everything that could be done to satisfy a
people it is almost impossible to satisfy.
For instance, they were given that 50
per cent. grant. The 1930 Act gave 50
per cent. for those who desired assistance
for building new schools, and everybody
knows without doubt that was for no
other body but the Roman Catholic
people. There is no disguising the fact
they are the only people who wanted the
grant, and wanted to maintain control
over their schools. That surely was a
great concession and shows that the Northern Government were desirous of
doing everything that was fair and
reasonable.
I think they should have some little
decency and say "Thank you" for such
concessions continuously given. The
statement was made that they gave two-
575 Grants for Elementary Schools
Grants for Elementary Schools 576
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
thirds the amount in the Free State for
the building of new schools. It is quite
possible the Free State may have some
control-
Mr. McHUGH: They have not.
if
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
not, they certainly are.
very liberal.
Surely it must be admitted when we
give 50 per cent. it is a very fair amount
to give to new schools controlled entirely
by private or religious bodies outside the
reach of the Government.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The noble Lord,
the Leader of the House, will never
want an apologist so long as Senator
Cunningham is in this House, and I
think I might congratulate Senator
Quigley on being a very good runner-up.
The apologist of the Government says his
own fellow countrymen-I hope he will
admit they are human beings and his
fellow countrymen-have neither modesty
nor decency, because we say that our
friends, the majority, have got £75 for
the past ten years for every £1 we have
got. We think that is hardly fair. Is
it modest to say that, and decent to
say that? If my hon. Friend was Leader
of this House we would be no longer
suffered to say that.
But two facts emerge from this discussion, one is that the figures set forth
in the Motion are not controverted and
that from 1921 to 1931 this Government,
which is a paragon of all the virtues,
gave to one party-the majority of the
North of Ireland, for schools £600,000,
and gave to the minority £7,000. They
say that having done that they have done
all that a Government is bound to do.
It also emerges that out of 200,000
pupils in Northern Ireland 72,000
are Catholic children, and the repairs to their schools will not be
paid for. The winter blast may blow
through the windows, the rain may fall
through the roof, and you say: "We will
not allow one penny to go in repairs."
You say that is our fault. Is it our fault
that the fathers and mothers of 72,000
children have deep convictions? You do
not respect them. It matters nothing to
vou. At the present time the result is
this: You have 34 provided and 572
transferred schools. You have over
80.000 school children in buildings which
are either built or repaired entirely at
the public expense of the Catholic
and Protestant population of the North
of Ireland. You will not allow one penny
of expenditure to repair a broken window
of a Catholic school. You say that is
fair and that it is not modest or decent
to protest against that.
You talk about public control. What
is this public control we hear so much
about? When did the old Tory Party
become enamoured of public control. The
phrase is used here because it is thought it
will hit us. Public control! Why you
control every lesson in the schools. You
can send an inspector any day of the year,
any day of the month, or any day of
the week. The curriculum of the schools
is prescribed by the Ministry. We must
follow it to the letter. We dare not
deviate. Everything is controlled. That
is the most powerful form of public control, and yet our children are penalised
and our schools must go to rack and ruin
because this vague term of public control
is introduced. It has no reality. There
is no
more effective control than the
control exercised by the noble Lord and
his officials.
We are told by Senator Quigley that
facilities are given for religious teaching.
But he knows perfectly well that any
father of ten children-there are very
few fathers nowadays of ten childrenbut any two fathers of ten children can
insist on Bible instruction being taught
in transferred schools. We cannot
accept that. Senator Quigley says we
ought to, but we cannot. We have our
convictions. We prefer to go to the
hedges again if you insist on Bible instruction with all its anomalies and
dangers, which even your own people
recognise. Then he says: "Why are the
children not brought together?", Why
do Protestant children not come to our
schools?
Mr. QUIGLEY: Is that what you
want?
Mr. CAMPBELL: Why are we to go
to their schools? Why do they not join
us? You know perfectly well they would
not be permitted.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: There
is a case in York Street.
Mr. CAMPBELL: It must be a very
remarkable case.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
largest number of pupils are Protestant.
577 Grants for Elementary Schools
1 DECEMBER 1931 Grants for Elementary Schools 578
Mr. CAMPBELL: I know nothing
about the facts. You must admit it is
not typical in the North of Ireland.
Then the right hon. Colonel Sharman-
Crawford made a speech. I can only say
that in this discussion I have tried as far
as possible to keep to relevant points. I
know it is not a popular topic. It needs
some little firmness to bring it forward
in this House, and for the right hon.
Gentleman to say it is utterly irrelevant
is a matter I enter a very emphatic
protest against. I am not responsible for
his intelligence. I think this matter is
relevant, as relevant as I could make it.
I have endeavoured to avoid anything
irrelevant, and if there had been anything irrelevant, I am very certain the
Speaker would have called my attention
to it. I have given attendance here
when the right hon. Gentleman was far
away in distant fields, and his visits here
are few and far between. I can only say
if his speech to-day is typical, I shall be
glad if his visits are fewer and farther
between. I leave the matter in the
hands of the House. If you think this
grievance should not be redressed, and
that we are dealt fairly and justly with,
then by all means reject the Motion.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before the
House actually gives its decision I would
like to protest as much as I can on behalf
of the Ministry of Education against it
being said in this House or outside that
we give preferential treatment to any
schools. When an application for a
grant comes we treat voluntary schools
alike, transferred schools alike, and provided schools alike. No difference is
made by the Ministry between Roman
Catholic and Protestant. But
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Clark, Sir G. S.
Crawford, Colonel, The Right Honourable
R. G. Sharmanthe
CONTENT.
| McAllister, Mr. T. S.
learned Senator who spoke last, and
also his Colleague, spoke about the conscientious objections of Roman Catholics.
to transfer. No one would wish that
they should abjure their religious convictions for the sake of getting a grant.
But in this matter of four-and-two committees there is one question always
present. Governments when they legislate cannot possibly forecast the
behaviour or feeling of any section of the
population. They can only go on precedents which have happened elsewhere.
Now, one of the boasts of the Roman
Catholic Church has been that it is the
same yesterday, to-day, and for ever,
and the same everywhere; whereas Protestants, as we know, are unfortunately
divided. Now, Roman Catholics in
England with voluntary schools have to
accept the principle of the four-and-two
committees to get any grants at all, any
recognition, I mean-they do not get
building grants in any circumstancesand yet Roman Catholics, as a body, in
England have so far as we know never
objected to the principle of the fourand-two committee. They only object to
the fact that as voluntary schools they
cannot get any building grants whatever, much less 50 per cent.
That, I think, is all I can say in the
matter. These conditions are acceptable
to Roman Catholics in England, and I
cannot see where the Government are at
fault for believing they would
acceptable here.

Assurance Companies (Underwriters)
Bill (S.).
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The short
Measure to which I wish to direct the
attention of the House is designed to
clarify the position of underwriters in
relation to assurance business, and more
particularly in relation to the insurance
of motor vehicles in Northern Ireland.
Under Section 7 of the Motor Vehicles
and Road Traffic Act (Northern Ireland),
1930, users of motor cars in Northern
Ireland are required to have in force
policies of insurance in respect of thirdparty risks. Such policies are to be
issued by a person who is an "authorised
insurer, " and the term "authorised insurer' is defined as "an assurance
company or an underwriter in whose case
the requirements of the Assurance
Companies Act, 1909, as amended by this
part of this Act, with respect to deposits
by assurance companies and deposits and
guarantees by underwriters are complied
with."
It appears that a number of policies of
insurance in respect of third-party risks,
arising from the use of motor vehicles in
Northern Ireland, have been issued by
Lloyd's underwriters. Doubts have been
raised as to whether a Lloyd's underwriter is an "authorised insurer" within
the meaning of Section 7 (2) of the Motor
Vehicles and Road Traffic Act (Northern
Ireland), 1930, if the underwriter or
insurer has complied with the Act of
1909 as applying in Great Britain but
not as applying within Northern Ireland.
The whole question has been under
consideration by the legal advisers of the
Government and, as a result of the
advice received, this Measure is put forward as the best means of resolving any
doubts as to the position of underwriters
in this matter. The Bill provides that
any underwriter carrying on, or proposing
to carry on, business in Northern Ireland
who furnishes proof to the satisfaction
of the Ministry of Commerce that he has
complied in Great Britain with the requirements of the law there, that is to
say that he has satisfied the conditions
laid down in the eighth Schedule of the
Assurance Companies Act, 1909, and has
thus become entitled to carry on business
in Great Britain, such underwriter shall
be entitled to transact business in Northern Ireland.
There is precedent for the adoption of
this course in our Assurance Companies
Act (Northern Ireland), 1924, which
authorises assurance companies which
have satisfied the requirements in Great
Britain to carry on business in Northern
Ireland without any obligation to make
a fresh deposit of money in Northern
Ireland. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

at

present.

can be

Architects Act
Architects Act
3 DECEMBER 1931
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 3rd December, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Architects (Registration) Act, 1931.
The Commons Message of December
1st was (according to Order) taken into
consideration :-
:-
That a humble Address be presented to
His Grace the Governor of Northern
Ireland as follows:-
Your Grace :
--
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects the Commons of Northern Ireland in Parliament assembled,
do by this humble Address pray that
it may please His Majesty to make provision by Order in Council under subsection (2) of Section 18 of the
Architects (Registration) Act, 1931,
that the said Act shall extend to Northern Ireland, subject to such adaptations in its application to Northern
Ireland as may appear to His Majesty
in Council to be necessary.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): In presenting this Resolution which has come to us from another
place, I beg to move the following:-
"That this House do agree with the
House of Commons in the Address to
His Grace and do insert the words
'Senators and before the word
'Commons' in the said Address."
The effect of this Amendment is, of
course, to identify the Senate as well
as the House of Commons with the
Resolution. The Resolution as amended,
it will be observed, prays that an Order
in Council may be made to extend the
Architects (Registration) Act, 1931, to
Northern Ireland, subject to such adaptations in its application to Northern Ireland as may appear to be necessary. This
contingency is already provided for in
Sub-Section (2) of Section 18 of the Act
itself. The Act which we seek to adopt
was passed last summer by the Parliament of the United Kingdom. At the
moment it does not apply to Northern
Ireland, but, as I have explained, it will
automatically so apply if and when the
necessary Order in Council is made in
pursuance of a Resolution passed by both
Houses of our Parliament here, and it is
for this purpose that I now submit the
Resolution to the House.
re-
Although the Act comes before us in
its finished form, I may say that when
it was under consideration of a Standing
Committee of the House of Commons in
Westminster, Northern Ireland was
presented on that Committee by Colonel
Sinclair and Mr. Stewart. It then
received long and careful consideration,
and the Measure eventually secured the
general agreement of all the parties concerned. When the Bill was going
through the Imperial Parliament, our
Government here and the Ulster Society
of Architects followed the matter closely
and the latter body is willing and anxious
to see the provisions extended to
Northern Ireland.
As hon. Member's are aware, there is
at present no statutory authority or register for the architect's profession, such as
exists for doctors and dentists. This has
been felt to be a weakness as, of course,
the professional education of qualified
architects is on much the same footing as
for the other professions, and it is felt
that there should be some standard by
which the general public might be satisfied that they are employing competent
people with proper qualifications.
of the important provisions of the Act
is that a Council is to be set up to be
called "The Architects'
Architects' Registration
Council," which will maintain a register
to be called the "Register of Registered
Architects."
One
It will be observed that there will be
no prohibition on the use of the term
"architect" by
by unregistered persons.
Even though they do not get on the register they may continue to style themselves
and to practise as "architects." It will
not, however, be possible for them to
make use of the title "registered
architect." The Council which is to be
set up will include a representative of the
Ulster Society of Architects and a person
nominated by His Grace the Governor of
Northern Ireland, the latter not to be
appointed until the Act has come into
operation in our area.
Agricultural Research Bill 588
Architects Act
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Before the name of any person can be
placed on the register, the Registration
Council must be satisfied eitherthat he is an architect member of
the Royal Academy or of the Royal
Scottish Academy, or
that his application for registration
was made within two years from the
commencement of the Act, and that at
the commencement of the Act he was
or had been practising as an architect
in the United Kingdom, or
that he has passed any examination
in architecture which is for the time
being recognised by the Registration
Council, or
that he possesses qualifications prescribed by the Registration Council in
regulations made under the Act.
It will be seen from these alternatives
that existing architects who have not
academic qualifications can be placed on
the register, provided they are actually
in practice and make their application |
within two years.
There are also two subsidiary bodies
which are set up under this Act and
which will advise the Registration
Council in matters relating to architectural education and the qualifications for
admission to the register. These are,
firstly, the Board of Architectural
Education, which will contain a representative of the Ulster Society of Architects, and, secondly, the Admission
Committee, which will consider applications for registration and report to the
Council as to whether each applicant is
duly qualified.
There are also the usual kind of provisions whereby the names of persons
convicted of a criminal offence and of
professional misconduct can be removed
from the register. Where the case of a
member of a body, such as the Ulster
Society of Architects, is being considered
by the Discipline Committee, the Committee will contain a representative of
that body.
There is the right of appeal on the part
of a person whose name is removed from
the register in this way, the appeal being
to the High Court. The High Court, I
may say so far as Northern Ireland is
concerned, will be our own High Court
here.
Penalties are provided for the unauthorised use of the title "Registered
Architect." Any person who contravenes this provision will be liable on
summary conviction to a fine not exceeding £50 for the first offence, and £100
for every subsequent offence.
I may perhaps say a word in regard to
the procedure which is being adopted in
this matter, namely, by taking over an
English Act and applying it to ourselves
by Order in Council rather than introducing a special Bill of our own. Although
the control of the profession of architects
is a matter which is entirely within the
powers of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland, the reason for the adoption of
the present procedure is that it is desired
that there shall be one common register
for Great Britain and Northern Ireland.
The existence of such a common register
will give to architects in Northern Ireland precisely the same privileges as will
be afforded under the Act to architects
practising in the other parts of the United
Kingdom. Northern Ireland architects
who become registered will be enabled to
use the term "Registered Architect" not
only in this country but also in Great
Britain, and those registered architects in
Northern Ireland who may be seeking
appointments in the British Dominions
or abroad will have precisely the same
professional status as their brethren in
England and Scotland.
The setting up of a separate register
in Northern Ireland would not afford
similar advantages to architects here even
if arrangements could be made for complete reciprocity between the British.
register and the Northern Ireland register.
There might be considerable difficulties
in giving effect to such reciprocity and,
therefore, in all the circumstances the
setting up of a common register for Great
Britain and Northern Ireland is, so far
as Northern Ireland is concerned, by far
the most advantageous arrangement for
registration that could be devised.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Address.
and have inserted the words, "Senator
and" before the word "Commons" in the
said Address.
Agricultural Research Station Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
short Bill dealing with a small matter in
589 Agricultural Research Bill—

3 DECEMBER 1931
Second Reading
connection with our Agricultural Research Station at Hillsborough. Hon.
Members may remember that the Act was
passed in 1927 which constituted the
Agricultural Research Institute of Northern Ireland and which permitted the
Institution to occupy about 485 acres of
the Great Park at Hillsborough. There
are also in the Great Park certain other
portions which are used for forestry purposes by the Ministry of Agriculture.
The object of the present Bill is to
exchange portion of the land occupied by
the Institution for a portion now used for
forestry purposes. The exchange is desirable on every ground as the land now
in the forestry portion is suitable for
tillage, and the land in the Institute
section is only suitable for forestry. The
Bill confirms the indenture which was
made last year to give this effect and the
indenture, which is a Schedule to the
Bill, also gives power to the Ministry of
Finance, in consultation with the Ministry of Agriculture, to make any such
minor changes in the future. I beg to
move.
Mr. McHUGH: I should like to say
that this Agricultural Research Station
at Hillsborough is not looked upon very
favourably by the farmers of Northern
Ireland. The reason they are opposed
to this Station is that they have always
found that when any remedy for cattle
diseases is discovered that would be of
benefit to the farmers it is passed on to
the veterinary surgeons, and the farmers
themselves are prevented from receiving
the information unless they pay
exorbitant fee to the veterinary surgeon.
I will give the House an instance to show
what I mean. Some eighteen months or
two years ago the Government experts
found a remedy to prevent abortion
diseases in cattle. The moment they discovered it they handed the remedy over
to the veterinary surgeons on the ground.
that the ordinary farmer was not intelligent enough to use it himself.
an
The
ultimate result was that if you had a cow
infected with abortion disease you had
to bring a veterinary surgeon to your
place and pay him two guineas for administering the remedy. In the case of
the very small farmers it is impossible
for them to pay those fees.
The Minister of Agriculture, when
challenged about this matter, made a very
strange suggestion. He said that if farmers combined together and got four,
six, eight, or ten cows that were affected
with this disease they could have them
all treated for the removal of the disease
at the rate of 10s per cow. You can at
once see the absurdity of that suggestion.
If I have a cow that becomes infected
with the disease I must go round my
neighbours and ask them if they have got
an animal similarly affected, as I am
anxious to have a veterinary surgeon
called but I am not able to pay his fee.
I say it is a most unfair situation to
place farmers in, and particularly small
farmers. There is another disease which,
as we are all aware, created great havoc
The
amongst cattle, and that is fluke.
same demand was made on the farmers
to pay for the remedy for this disease,
but fortunately for themselves the farmers discovered the cure by applying to
the chemist, who supplied them with the
necessary medicine. The farmers used
this remedy successfully without the
assistance of the veterinary surgeons, and
all it cost them for the medicine was 1/6.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am very sorry to
interrupt the hon. Member, but I do
not think his remarks are relevant to anything in this Bill. The object of the Bill
before the House is simply to confirm and
give effect to an agreement relating to an
exchange of two small plots of land at the
Agricultural Research Station at Hillsborough. There is nothing in the Bill
regarding research work about which the
hon. Member is speaking.
Mr. McHUGH: I am only discussing
the action of the Research Institute.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I must
point out that the hon. Member's criticism does not arise on this Bill, because
there is no veterinary research work
carried out at Hillsborough. It may be
carried out elsewhere, but not at Hillsborough.
Mr. McHUGH: This is the only opportunity I have of putting the grievance
of the farmers in this respect before the
House so that they may be brought to
the notice of the Minister of Agriculture.
Mr. LESLIE: I suppose we may take
it that the Agricultural Research Station
requires a certain amount of support
from public funds. Therefore there is
an opportunity elsewhere of discussing
the Estimate for that provision, and there
is also an opportunity for discussing it in
this House on the Appropriation Bill. I
imagine that that is so. Of course, you
591 Assurance Companies Bill-

THE SENATE
Adjournment
[Mr. Leslie
have already dealt with the point that
this is a mere transfer of land from one
purpose to another.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I shall be
very glad if hon. Members who may wish
to discuss research work in general would
raise the matter on the Motion for the
Adjournment, or by giving due notice at
any other time. This Bill is merely concerned with the exchange of one piece of
land for another.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Assurance Companies (Underwriters)

[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSES 1 (Provisions as to the
underwriters carrying on business in
Northern Ireland), and 2 (Short title,
construction, and citation), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
:

Poultry Diseases Bill (S.).
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: I am not in a position to
move the Second Reading of this Bill at
the present time. It is now practically
certain; in fact, it is definitely certain
that the further stages of this Bill will
not be taken during the present Session.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such date as Mr. Speaker shall
direct."
I am taking this course rather
than bring the House together unnecessarily when there is very little work
to do. I would like to give notice now
that I intend to move on the next sitting
day to suspend the Standing Order with
reference to sittings of the House to
enable me to take the First and Second
Readings of certain Bills, on the understanding that they have been in the
hands of hon. Members a sufficient time
beforehand. That procedure will avoid
bringing the House together for what is
really an unnecessary purpose.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I should like to ask
the noble Viscount whether that will
apply to the Valuation Bill when it
reaches this House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It will
apply to all Bills, but only in case they
have been circulated a convenient time
beforehand so as to allow hon. Members
time to become acquainted with their
provisions.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Now that the Valuation Bill has passed
its Second Reading in another place can
the noble Leader of the House give us any
approximate date when it will come
before this House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think it is likely to appear here before
Tuesday, the 15th, or Wednesday, the
16th; that is to say, the week after next.
Mr. McHUGH: I wish to ask the
Leader of the House if he will bring the
matter I referred to on the Second Reading of the Agricultural Research Station
Bill to the notice of the Minister
of Agriculture, because it is a very
important matter for the farmers of
Northern Ireland.
I cannot
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
say that I know very much personally
about abortion disease in cattle, and I
think the best plan would be for the hon.
Member to put down a question on the
subject. If necessary the matter can
then be debated, and I will have an
opportunity of giving the hon. Senator a
full reply.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
before
twenty minutes
Four o'clock, until such

Tuesday, 8th December, 1931.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
