PRIVATE BUSINESS.
Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Order Confirmation (Strangford
Waterworks) Bill.
The following Report was made by the
Joint Committee on the Bill:-
That they had considered the said Bill,
and examined the allegations thereof
which were found to be true; and that the
Committee had gone through the Bill and
made some Amendments thereto.
Gibson's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
with the Amendments made by the Joint
Committee agreed to.
Harper's Divorce Bill.
Brought from the House of Commons
with the Amendments made by the Joint
Committee agreed to.
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That Standing Order No. XXV be suspended for this
day's sitting for the purpose of taking the
Second Reading of the Drainage Bill and
the Government Loans Bill."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Questions to Minister.
REAR "STOP" LIGHTS ON BUSES.
Major DOBBS asked the Leader of the
House whether the Government will consider the advisability of making it obligatory on all motor omnibuses to carry a
rear "stop" light to be brought into
operation whenever the footbrake
depressed.
is
Viscount CHARLEMONT: My right
Hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, does not think that there is any
adequate reason for distinguishing in this
matter between motor omnibuses and
other forms of motor transport such as
lorries and private cars, more particularly
as all his information goes to show that,
taken as a whole, the drivers of the buses
are amongst the most competent and careful of road users.
The circumstances of all accidents to
public service vehicles are reported to the
Ministry, and a careful scrutiny of these
reports does not support the view that the
number of such accidents would be
sensibly affected if the suggestion of the
hon. and gallant Member were adopted,
while the expense of fitting the device to
existing vehicles would be serious.
Perhaps I may be permitted to add
that the record of accidents to omnibuses
continues to show a gratifying improvement, both as regards numbers and
gravity.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Arising out of that answer, I should like
to say that I happen to be the Chairman
of the Advisory Committee on Roads, and
it has for some time been my idea that
the suggestion made in the question of
the hon. and gallant Member (Major
Dobbs) should be adopted. In my opinion
it is a very important suggestion. It was
a very thick night the other night and
we could not see the bus in front of us,
with the result that there was nearly a
bad accident. If there had been a rear
"stop" light on the bus we would have
seen it. I am afraid I do not agree with
the Government's view on this question,
and I shall have to press the suggestion
at a future meeting of the Roads Advisory
Committee.
Oral Answers
Government Loans Bill-
THE SENATE
NUMBER OF ELEMENTARY
SCHOOLS.
Mr. DORMAN asked the Leader of the
House whether he can state the number
of public elementary schools in Northern
Ireland; the number under public control
and denominational control respectively
in connection with the following denominations: Church of Ireland, Church of
Rome, Presbyterian, Methodist, Congregational, Baptist; and the amount of
public grants, if any, for the upkeep,
repair or building of schools under
denominational control in each case.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would be
obliged if the hon. Member would postpone his question for a short time, as the
Ministry have not been able to obtain all
the information asked for in the time at
their disposal.
ROAD FUND CREDIT BALANCE.
-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD
asked the Leader of the House if he can
state what is the credit balance at the
moment of the Road Fund.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The cash
balance standing to the credit of the Road
Fund is £1,428. The hon. and gallant
Member will, of course, realise that the
greater part of the revenue of the Road
Fund is received in the month of January,
but of course the payment of grants goes
on throughout the year. The balance to
the credit of the Fund is, therefore, at
the lowest ebb during the month of
December, just before the revenue for the
last quarter of the financial year begins to
come in.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
May I ask if that is the balance after
taking into account the £150,000 raided
from the Fund by the Government.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I must have notice of that question.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask if the
Government have any fresh predatory
designs upon this Fund.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
discretion is the better part of valour in
this particular matter.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Drainage
Bill and the Government Loans Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Government Loans Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First Time."-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second Time." The purpose of this
Measure is obvious from the text of the
Bill and I do not propose to delay the
House very long in explaining its provisions. It is a formal Measure, which
is required to write off certain debts from
the Government Loans Fund, and legislation is necessary for this purpose under
the requirements of the Government
Loans and Exchequer Provisions Act of
1925.
It will be noticed from the Schedule
that the majority of the amounts are cornparatively small, and the Ministry of
Finance are quite certain that these
amounts are not recoverable. The list in
the first table of the Schedule comprises a
number of fishermen who obtained loans
for boats and gear at a time when priers
were very high, and it is considered that
they have already exerted themselves to
the utmost in endeavouring to repay these
amounts.
The single amount in the second table
is in respect of a loan for fishing nets
which was given by our own Ministry of
Finance, and the outstanding sum is only
a trifling amount. The man concerned is
out of employment and it is considered.
impracticable to recover.
In regard to the third table, the first
item is in respect of a loan given for the
improvement of a dwelling-house in a
backward rural area. It is considered
that the property is unsaleable and the
only course, therefore, appears to be that
the outstanding amounts should be
written off.
The last item is, of course, the largest,
and has excited some attention in another
place. It may be sufficient for me to say
that the Government has given the most
careful consideration to this case and that
it is definitely of the opinion that the best
Government Loans Bill-
Second Reading
8 DECEMBER 1931
course is that the outstanding amount of
£9,500 odd should be written off. The
object of the loan was so that the Ulster
Farmers' Produce Society should be in a
position to help the farmers themselves by
obtaining supplies of seeds and manures,
etc., at the most advantageous prices and
to assist them also in marketing. Hon
Members will appreciate that affairs in
the business world have been in such a
condition that the best of schemes may
prove disappointing, but I would just like
to point out that it will still be open to the
Ministry of Finance, although these loans
are written off, to require payment from
the debtors if at any time their financial
position appears to warrant it.
Mr. LESLIE: I believe that the only
item in the Schedule to the Bill which has
attracted any public attention is the item
in the Third Part of the Schedule relating
to the advance made to the Ulster
Farmers' Produce Society Limited. It
may, therefore, perhaps be of interest to
the House to recall the latest precedent
dealing with Bills of this description in
the Parliament at Westminster. In a
recent Bill which became law on 30th
September last we find that authority was
given for writing off advances made under
the National Debt and Local Loans Fund
Act, 1887, and under the Harbours and
Passing Tolls Act, 1861, and particularly
four amounts advanced under the Agricultural Credits Act which amounted to
£6,000. The Agricultural Credits Act,
it will be remembered, was passed in 1923,
and it bears a very close relation to our
own Exchequer and Financial Provisions
Act of 1923 in so far as loans for agricultural development are concerned.
seemed to me that anybody who is inclined
to carp at the release of a particular loan
might be interested in knowing that there
was a very strong and recent precedent
across the water.
sure
It
the
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am
House is indebted to the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) for drawing our
attention to the fact that quite recently
in the Imperial Parliament a
sum of
£6,000 was written off under the Agricultural Credits Act, 1923. But £6,000 is
a relatively small amount for the Imperial
Exchequer to bear the loss of, and I think
the House ought to have a little more information as regards the last item in the
Third Schedule to the Bill now before the
House. It relates to a loan made to this
Society for the purpose of agricultural
development. It was made under the
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Act,
1923, so that it must have been a recent
transaction. The amount involved is very
considerable, close on £10,000, which has
apparently been lost to the Government
of Northern Ireland.
I do think that the House is entitled
to know why this loan was made, and
why there was not greater foresight in
regard to this matter, and under what
circumstances has this loan been lost to
the extent of two-thirds. We are left
very much in the dark in this matter.
Although we are not concerned much
with finance I do think at a time when
the taxpayer is hit in every way and in
every direction, a loss of close upon
£10,000 in one transaction alone is a
matter that should not pass muster so
easily in this House.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I
agree with
the previous speaker. Anything that
would be for the advantage of farmers I
would not complain of, but this loan of
£15,000 to the Ulster Farmer's Produce
Company was not taken advantage of
solely by farmers. Cattle dealers and
others got the advantage of this advance
of money, and seedsmen in town who are
in the habit of supplying goods to farmers
and are up against very heavy rates had
to suffer while these people competed with
them at this loss.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I can give
a little of the information asked for, but
perhaps if any further information is required it might be asked for at a later
stage of the Bill. The loan was given in
1926, a year of comparative prosperity;
at any rate it was before the present state
of affairs came about. The object of the
loan was to enable the society to continue
its activities in assisting farmers in Northern Ireland, mainly in County Down
and County Antrim, to obtain supplies of
seeds, manures, etc., at the most advantageous prices, and in a minor degree to
dispose of their produce. Repayment was
to be made by instalments of principal,
£750 in March and September of each
year. Interest was also to be paid on
these dates.
During the first two years the society
was to be excused from payment of either
principal or interest, the first payment
falling due in March, 1928. In December,
1929, the Ministry agreed to allow the
payment of interest due in September,
Agricultural Research Station 600
Drainage Bill
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
1929, and March, 1930, to be deferred,
and also agreed to a postponement of repayment of capital until September, 1930.
The principal object of this concession was
to avoid depleting the working capital at
the society's disposal. The tangible
security has depreciated, and the only
security apart from the profits of the
society is that afforded by the guarantors
of the £5,000, and the Government after
a prolonged discussion with the society and
the guarantors has decided to seek
authority to write off portion of the loan.
as a bad debt.
I am afraid loaning money to farming
enterprises is always something of a risky
business in the present and past state of
agriculture. On the other hand if the
Government had not done its best to assist
farmers and farmers' societies they would
have got at least as much criticism. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Drainage Bill (Northern Ireland).
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First Time."-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
of a
Mr. LONG: I beg to move
"That
this Bill be now read a Second time."
It is a short Bill, which proposes
to amend the existing Drainage Acts.
Its main object, which is given in
the first Clause, is, of course,
financial nature and is concerned with
the provision for the funding of certain
expenses of the Bann Drainage Scheme.
In short, the proposal is to meet proportion of the cost by borrowing instead of
from revenue.
The second Clause aims at giving power
to an urban district council to make a contribution direct towards a drainage
scheme of the county council for that part
of the scheme which lies within its own
area.
The third Clause is concerned with the
question of recovery of expenses by a
county council when the council incurs
such expenses in cleansing watercourses.
A judicial decision has been given that
the existing jurisdiction in such matters
rests only with the petty sessions and the
county council have asked that the remedy
should lie either at petty sessions or
at quarter sessions, as the case may be.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

""

I think that Tuesday week will be the

Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
Owing to the approach of Christmas I
should like to put my old question,
whether there is any intention of rushing
the Valuation Bill, or whether the Bill
will be adjourned until after Christmas,
instead of rushing it through at short
notice as apparently they are going to do
in the other House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have no
intention of doing any rushing.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
It is not you, my Lord.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Well, the
Senate will be in a position to say
whether they will be rushed or not. J
hope to get the Second Reading of the
Valuation Bill before Christmas. It is
only a hope and may not happen. The
remaining stages will be adjourned until
after Christmaz.
Mr. LESLIE: Do I understand the
Session will not close until after Christmas? Is that a settled thing?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Yes.

minutes before Four o'clock

Laid before the House (pursuant to

Gibson's Divorce Bill.

Harper's Divorce Bill.

Business of the House.
Moved: "That Standing Order No.
XXV. be suspended for this day's
Sitting for the purpose of taking the
Second Reading of the National Health

Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Questions to Minister.
DISTRIBUTION OF MILK.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Barclay :-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether it is a fact that much of the
milk sold in Northern Ireland is produced and distributed under unsanitary
conditions, and, if so, whether the Government will take steps to have all milk
pasteurised before distribution, and all
milk distributors registered.
Mr. BARCLAY: I may say I have
been prompted to put down this question
in order to draw the attention of the
House and those who are responsible to
the distribution of milk to the poor,
especially in the City of Belfast. I am
given to understand that a great deal of
the milk that comes into the City of
Belfast is produced under conditions that
you or I would not think proper. I do
not say that all the milk is produced
under such conditions, because some of
the farmers produce milk and send it to
Belfast as clean and pure as if it came
from a grade A herd. But there are
others who do not do so. From information I have received I find that in the
case of those farmers who have a grade A
herd a veterinary inspector inspects those
cows every three months. If a cow is found
to be suffering from tuberculosis the
farmer has got to dispense with her. He
takes her to the fair or market or to the
auction in Belfast and she is sold to
either a grade B or grade C producer,
or to a man who has no grade. The
milk of this cow is mixed with other milk,
sent to Belfast, and distributed amongst
the inhabitants of the city, carrying with
it those deadly germs which we all abhor.
As far as the distribution of the milk
is concerned, I am given to understand
that since the milk "war" all kinds of
people have been distributing milk in
Belfast and carrying it round in all sorts
of vehicles, from a bicycle to a little
handcart. I asked, “What about the
inspectors," and the reply was, "They
do it all before the inspector comes out
in a morning." I think all men who are
distributing milk should be licensed and
registered, and if they are not suitable
persons to distribute milk they should be
stopped altogether. There is no use
local authorities going to great expense
in trying to fight this white man's
disease if we are going to allow this kind
of thing to go on. My opinion is that
prevention is better than cure, and I
think the best plan would be to remove
these diseased cattle from the market
altogether.
I was wondering if it would not be
possible for the Government to bring in
a Bill to enable farmers to insure cattle
against disease, and then if one of them
was found to be suffering from disease
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
15 DECEMBER 1931
let the insurance company take it and
pay the farmer for it.
It would prevent
it being put on the market, and would
save the health of the people.
a
Mr. LONG: I realise the importance
of the question which Senator Barclay
has asked. While there has been
marked improvement in recent years in
the standard of cleanliness in the production and distribution of the milk supply,
it is true that in some cases the hygienic
conditions leave much to be desired, but
on the whole my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Home Affairs, is satisfied that
a fair standard is observed by the farmers
and dairymen in the handling of milk.
Registration of milk vendors is already
in force, and all cowsheds and dairies
where milk is produced or distributed for
sale are subject to supervision by local
authorities. Generally speaking, these
bodies give a considerable amount of
attention to this matter.
It would not be feasible to require all
milk produced and sold to be pasteurised,
because it is practicable to apply pasteurisation only where milk is handled in
large quantities. In considering this.
matter it must be borne in mind
that, while pasteurisation has distinct
advantages, it will not turn dirty milk
into clean milk, and unless scrupulous
care is exercised during the process it may
have the effect of turning clean milk into
dirty milk.
I should like to add that in the district
with which I am familiar the District
Council requires the inspection of milk,
and instead of a quarterly report, to which
the hon. Senator has referred, we get
a very careful report from our veterinary
inspector every month regarding the
health of each particular cow and the
state of the byre as to cleanliness.
With
regard to Senator Barclay's suggestion
about the rejection of cows suffering from
tuberculosis, it is a very large question
indeed, and there is a big difference of
opinion about that matter. As a matter
of fact, I think I was the first in Northern Ireland to suggest that a cow that
was rejected by a veterinary surgeon as
suffering from tuberculosis should be
Under Local Education
Authorities.
Provided. Transferred.

branded on the horn or in some other
way so that the purchaser would know
exactly what he was buying.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I am glad the
deputy leader of the House has given the
information which those of us who are
engaged in the business already possess
that pasteurisation will not take liquid
filth out of milk. It may destroy the
germs, but it does not take out any liquid
filth. If the people want clean, pure
milk for their children they must get
grade A milk, which comes to them direct
and in a pure state, without any of its
good qualities having been taken from it.
ELEMENTARY SCHOOLS-NUM-
BER AND GRANTS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Dorman:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
he can give statistics of the total number
of public elementary schools in Northern
Ireland under the following categoriesunder the control of local education
authorities; under four and two committees; under clerical managers of the
respective religious denominations; under
lay management; and whether he will
state the amounts of public grants made
by the Government of Northern Ireland
for the building, upkeep and repair of
each of these various classes of schools.
other than those under the control of
local education authorities.
Mr. DORMAN: In asking this question
I am sure I am expressing the opinion of
the House as a whole, when I say how
much we regret the absence of the noble
Lord, the Leader of the House (Viscount
Charlemont), owing to illness.
Mr. LONG: I am sure the sentiment
expressed by the hon. Member, which I
know is shared by the House, will be
gratifying to the noble Lord, the Leader
of the House, to whom I shall have great
pleasure in conveying the message.
On the 31st December, 1930, there
were 1,893 public elementary schools in
Northern Ireland. These are divided
into the following categories:-
Clerical Management.
Lay
Manage- TOTAL.
Meth. Others. ment.
Under
4 & 2
Cmtes.
R.C.
C. of I. Pres.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
Grants amounting to £6,816 (including
a sum of £1,600 for rebuilding) have been
made by local education authorities towards the erection of schools, under fourand-two committees. In addition, grants
amounting to £1,792 were made by these
authorities towards the cost of improving
and reconditioning existing schools under
this form of management. These authorities also give annual grants towards
expenditure on repairs, renewals and
general upkeep of such schools, of onehalf of the approved estimates, but the
Ministry has no record of the amounts so
granted.
Grants are not made from public funds
towards the repair and upkeep of schools
under voluntary management, other than
those under four-and-two committees, but
in the case of 115 schools which are
vested in the Ministry, the work of repair and maintenance (for which the
Ministry is responsible under the terms
of the lease) is undertaken by the Ministry of Finance.
Since the Government of Northern
Ireland took over control of the
educational services, ten schools under
Roman Catholic management have
been built with the aid of grants
amounting to £21,130, two schools
under Church of Ireland management
with the aid of grants amounting to
£3,735, two schools under Presbyterian
management with the aid of grants
amounting to £2,531, and one school
under joint Church of Ireland and Presbyterian management, the grant being
£6,000. In addition, one school under
Methodist management was considerably
enlarged by means of a grant amounting
to £1,667. These grants were made by
the Ministry in continuation of the practice which was in operation under the
former Commissioners of National Education.
No new schools have so far been built
under the system of grants set up under
Section 10 of the Education Act of 1930,
but the Ministry has recently sanctioned
grants under this Section amounting to
£35,500 towards the erection of four proposed new schools, two to be in Belfast,
one in the city of Londonderry, and one
in Portadown. These grants represent
fifty per cent. of the cost, as at present
ascertained, of these schools, which will
all be under Roman Catholic clerical
management.
Mr. CAMPBELL: As I understand
the reply, there have, in fact, been no
grants paid to denominational schools mentioned by the hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman)
under the Act of 1930. The money referred to as having been actually paid
was in continuation of obligations transferred to the Government of Northern
Ireland from the Imperial Government
in 1921. The question as it appears on
the Paper has undergone a transformation
since it first appeared. It then made inquiries about schools under what was
called popular control, and schools under
denominational control, indicating that
there were two forms of control. But I
submit to the House that in actual fact,
going behind the mere appearance of
things, there is really only one form of
control in Northern Ireland of these
public elementary schools, and that is
denominational control.
In its present form the question refers
to schools under the control of the local
education authorities. I submit that it
would be more correct to speak of these
schools as being financed by local education authorities, and in regard to that
aspect of the matter I think it is very
likely that, since the 1923 Act was passed
the expenditure on that class of school
must be coming very near the figure of
three-quarters of a million.
Then the question speaks of the number of schools under clerical management.
Again I submit to the House that, looking
at the reality of things in Northern
Ireland, the schools are, practically
everywhere in our area, under clerical
A little while ago the
management.
Leader of the House told us the number
of transferred schools and the number of
provided schools then existing. Well, now
each of these schools-I think the
Deputy Leader of the House will agree
that I am correct-there is a school
management committee.
in
The school management committee
manages the school and nominates the
teachers who are to be appointed by the
local education authorities, and each
school management committee in charge
of each transferred school and each provided school is, as to three-fourths of its
membership, denominational, because it
consists of the former managers, who are
mainly clerical, and representatives of the
parents of the children attending the
school. These schools may be in law
undenominational, but in fact they are
denominational schools. The local edu-
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
15 DECEMBER 1931
cation authorities themselves correspond
to the Regional Education Committees,
and these committees are, for all practical purposes, denominational. We have
eighteen in our area and 351 in membership, and I believe that out of the 351
there are less than twenty belonging to
the minority. I think it would be an
advantage if, in this matter of education,
we could see the facts as they are and try
to get a right perspective.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
understand, from the reply of the Deputy
Leader of the House, that grants amounting to £21,130 have been made for the
building of Roman Catholic schools. Am
I correct in thinking that these schools.
are not in any way controlled by the
Government, and that they are purely
under clerical control?
Mr. LESLIE: Further arising out of
the answer given by the Deputy Leader
of the House, may I inquire whether it
is not the fact that until Section 10 of the
1930 Act came into operation there was
no statutory authority whatever for contributing from public funds that is,
from the Exchequer-to the building,
upkeep, and repair of any public elementary school whatever which was under
the control of a religious denomination,
or under any control other than that of
a public body.
Mr. LONG: The answer to Senator
Cunningham's query is in the affirmative.
I would suggest that, considering the
comparatively short time the Act of 1930
has been in operation, the fact that grants
amounting to £35,500 have been made
augurs well for the successful operation of
the Act in the future.
CONTROL OF DANCE HALLS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can now
give the information asked for in
supplemental question put on 1st instant
relating to public control of dance halls.
a
Mr. LONG: A number of urban
district councils have in fact adopted
Part IV. of the Public Health Acts
Amendment Act, 1890, but so far as I
have been able to ascertain, no rural
district councils have deemed it necessary
to seek to be vested with the powers
under the provisions of the Public Health
(Ireland) Act, 1896, enabling them to
adopt part of the Act of 1890 referred
to. I may add that, in connection with
the preparation of the proposed Measure
dealing with the reform of local government services in Northern Ireland, the
local authorities have been asked for
their views in regard to the question,
inter alia, of making the adoptive provisions of the Public Health Acts
mandatory.
PROSECUTIONS FOR
PERSONATION.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Ballot
Act, 1872, cast on the presiding officer at
Local Government elections the duty of
initiating prosecutions against persons
believed to have been guilty of personation; whether, since the passing of the
Parliamentary and Local Government
Elections Act (Northern Ireland), 1930,
his duty in that respect has ceased except
on the complaint of a polling agent;
whether in the absence of a polling agent,
the presiding officer or his clerks have
now any statutory duty or authority to
take proceedings against persons detected
in personation at these elections.
Mr. LONG: The presiding officer has
no statutory duty or authority to take
proceedings against a person whom he has
reason to believe is committing personation, but by the Parliamentary and
Local Government Elections Act, 1930,
personation was made a felony and it is
the duty of the presiding officer in such
circumstances to draw the attention of
the constable on duty in the station to
the matter and proceedings can then be
taken by the police.
I may perhaps add that this matter
was fully debated in Parliament when
this Act was under discussion.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask whether
the duty to which the Deputy Leader of
the House has referred has been cast on
the presiding officer by any Statute?
Mr. LONG: I am advised that under
the Parliamentary and Local Government Elections Act, 1930, that is so.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons agree to the Amendments made by the Joint Committee to
611 Health Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
First Reading
to
the Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Order Confirmation (Strangford Waterworks) Bill without Amendment; they
agree
the Assurance Companies
(Underwriters) Bill without Amendment;
they have passed the National Health
Insurance (No. 2) Bill, the Appropriation
(No. 2) Bill, and the Finance Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
National Health Insurance (No. 2)
Bill.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read a Second time."
The main purpose of the present Bill
is to continue for a further year the
special Measures for the maintenance of
insurance and benefit rights under the
National Health Insurance Acts of persons who have been genuinely unemployed for a considerable period and who
satisfy the conditions set out in Subsection (3) of Section 3 of the National
Health Insurance Act, 1924, and the
Regulations made thereunder. Existing
legislation provides for the continued insurance of these persons until the end of
the present year, and Clause 1 of the Bill
proposes to extend the period until 31st
December, 1932. Power is sought also
to continue by Regulations the operation.
of the Act to 31st March, 1933, if
necessary, in order to provide for the
contingency of a further extension being
authorised next year by the Imperial
Parliament at a time when this Parliament might not be sitting.
Clause 2 of the Bill provides that an
Exchequer Grant equivalent to 36 contributions will be made to Approved
Societies for each person whose insurance
is prolonged by the Bill. This is based
on an estimate by the Actuaries of
the amount required on the averlage to meet the cost of continuing insurance and benefits under the
conditions of the Bill. The cost to the
Exchequer is estimated at £2,400 in
respect of contributions, and at £500 in
respect of State Grant on related benefits.
Clause 3 of the Bill has been inserted
at the request of the Ministry of Pensions
to enable Medical Benefit to be granted
to certain disabled ex-Servicemen who
are not entitled to it as insured persons.
The cost will be met entirely by the
Ministry of Pensions.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I cannot
claim to be an enthusiastic supporter of
the Bill, the Second Reading of which has
just been moved. On the other hand, I
do not propose to oppose the Second
Reading of the Bill, for a reason which
will, I think, be apparent to the House
in general. The Bill is, as I understand,
or at least so far as the First and Second
Clauses are concerned, a counterpart of
the Bill which was introduced in the
Imperial Parliament and which received
the Royal Assent as recently as the 11th
of this month. If, therefore, the House
passes this Bill, as I have no doubt it
will, it will be proceeding on exactly the
same lines as the English Parliament in
dealing with this matter. That being so,
I presume I am correct in assuming that
when the accounts of Northern Ireland
come to be compared and adjusted by the
Joint Exchequer Board, or whatever the
appropriate authority may be under the
circumstances, Northern Ireland will, so
to speak, get credit for this expenditure
on this head, on the ground that it is proceeding on the same lines as in Great
Britain, and therefore is entitled to the
usual consideration.
The objection to the course which the
Bill takes seems to me to be that it is
another step-not the first step, because
this Bill is a reproduction of an earlie
Measure which would expire normally on
31st December, but it is another step
down that slippery slope of giving something for nothing, which has, I think,
led to a great deal of financial trouble.
The effect of the Bill is to prolong or
keep in insurance certain unfortunate
people who would, but for its assistance,
fall out of insurance and would, therefore, have been left to the tender mercies
of the poor-law as regards the provision
of bread, and to the dispensary system,
which has not yet been abolished, so far
as medical benefit is concerned. There
would be those alternatives. But the
State is proceeding to step in and pay
the cost of keeping these people in insurance, and in doing that the State has to
incur an expenditure estimated at, I
think, £2,900 for the coming year. That
sum of £2,900 will wipe out more than
one of the small economies which we have
recently introduced over here in order to
keep our heads financially above water.
I understand also, that if these unfortunate people, to whom I have alluded,
were allowed to fall out of insurance, the
question of their rights under the Pen-
613 Health Insurance Bill-

15 DECEMBER 1931
Third Reading
sions Act might be at stake.
If the State
is to interfere again to protect those pension rights, it seems to me the effect is
ultimately to reduce what was intended
to be a whole life insurance, or so much
of it as goes to sixty five to an insurance
for some shorter period, but that is a very
serious matter. We all hope that the
Bill is, as drawn, intended to be of temporary operation, and that the Government will not again have to come to
Parliament and ask for what is, in effect,
a renewal of these provisions. If that is
not so, if it is found that this prolongation is to become a permanent feature of
our financial arrangements, the question
will arise as to whether part of this
liability, which it is very desirable should
be incurred for the sake of these unfortunate people, and which I will call the
poor-law part, should not be undertaken
by the poor-law authorities for the relief
of the Exchequer, and whether the medical benefit part should be continued as
part of the medical benefit system, and
finally, whether the whole matter ought
not to be dealt with by the Approved
societies concerned in the way of additional benefit. If the Approved Societies
have funds, this is an expenditure which
those funds ought to bear, and it ought
not to be cast on the general exchequer.
So much for Clauses 1 and 2.
Against Clause 3 nothing can be said.
It may be remembered that, when we
were discussing the Medical Benefit Bill,
the position of certain discharged soldiers
was alluded to in this House. As I
understand it, the effect of Clause 3 will
be simply to give certain people who
served in the late War, and who, in consequence of their service, were not able to
be accepted as members of an Approved
Society the object of the Clause will be
to give those people, very deserving
people, the rights which they might otherwise have been able to enjoy. So far as
that goes, although the cost will not fall
directly on our Exchequer at all, I think
it is obvious we should all desire to comply
with the suggestion made. That is a
mere matter of detail, on the basis that
no man who served in the War should be
in any worse position as regards claims on
the public than he would have been if he
had not served. I support the Clause,
not only in principle but in detail, but
as regards the first two Clauses, I hope
we shall not be asked again to further
extend the period.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I rise to say that
these Insurance Bills are as intricate and
puzzling as the Maze at Hampton Court
Palace. I take it that this Bill is intended for ordinary plain people and not
for experts, for the people who benefit
by these Acts, but I defy any ordinary
citizen in this jurisdiction to understand
this Bill. It would take the most experienced official of the Ministry of
Labour to find out what it means. The
object of the Bill is
"To amend Sub-section (3) of Section
3 of the National Health Insurance
Act, 1924."
We are not told what that Sub-section is.
Then it goes on to say
"and to make financial provision in
connection with such amendment, and
to amend Section 12 of the said Act."
What is Section 12? Who knows what
Section 12 is unless he has read it in the
library? It continues
"as that Section applies in Northern
Ireland by virtue of Section 3 of the
National Health Insurance Act (Northern Ireland), 1930."
There is the scope and ambit of this Bill,
and at the present moment to threequarters of the Members of this House
it is a perfect puzzle.
Mr. DORMAN: I support this Bill,
because I believe that many individuals
who, during the War, were promised that
a grateful country would never forget
them and that they would come back to a
country fit for heroes will benefit by the
provisions of this Measure.
I took a very
extensive part in endeavouring to get
Servicemen to come under the Health
Insurance Acts of 1911 and 1912, and I
was amazed at the lack of appreciation of
the rank and file of the advantages that
would accrue to them when they finished
their service under the Colours, by their
being members, of an Approved Society,
instead of having to be cast off as voluntary contributors and only entitled to
draw the amount of contributions they
had paid under the Act. I believe there
are many ex-Servicemen who left the
Service in good health and would not
come under the category of those described
in Clause 3, who will derive advantage
from this Bill. They left the Service in
apparently good health, and being voluntary contributors many of them found it
difficult to get into an Approved Society,
Finance Bill
First Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr, Dorman]
Their benefits are now exhausted, and the
only regret I have is that this Measure
does not go far enough. But on the
principal that half a loaf is better than
no bread for these unfortunate men,
many of whom risked their lives for their
country, I have great pleasure in supporting the Bill.
Mr. LONG: With regard to the very
proper point raised by Senator Leslie, I
am advised and feel certain that the Joint
Exchequer Board will make an allowance
to Northern Ireland in respect of the
expenditure under this Bill. On the
question of economy, I would point out
that the National Government, even in
these difficult times, believed that the
money that was involved, in this case,
was money well spent. With regard to
Senator Leslie's question as to whether a
similar Bill will be presented in the
future, I am afraid I must have notice of
that.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second Time," agreed to.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First Time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read a Second Time.”
This is the usual type of money Bill,
which I need only formally move. Its
object can be clearly seen from the
Schedule.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, That this Bill be now committed.'-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Finance Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First Time."-(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this Bill be now read a Second Time."
As will be seen from the arrangement of
the various Clauses, this Bill deals with a
number of matters about which I propose to say a few words.
The first two Clauses deal with certain
matters in connection with Death Duties.
The first Clause is designed to remedy a
technical deficiency in the present legislation in connection with annuities which
have been surrendered during the life
of the person whose property is under
consideration. The Clause will put
these annuities on the same basis as an
ordinary life interest for the purpose of
Death Duties and will thus remedy a
technical flaw which was discovered when
a case was taken to the courts.
The second Clause of the Bill is of
some general importance in connection
with the bequeathing of historical or
National objects of interest. The Clause
makes some technical Amendments in the
existing legislation and otherwise consolidates the previous enactments. Hon.
Members can rest assured if this becomes
law that the State will deal as gently as
possible in the matter of Death Duties
with legacies which they may wish to
make to public museums or libraries and
so forth. I may say that the general
effect of the legislation should be to assist
in retaining important collections at
home instead of seeing them go abroad to
other countries which have, perhaps,
more funds available to pay for them.
The next part of the Bill is concerned
with Stamp Duty. Clause 3 is designed
to stop a practice which has come into
vogue in regard to leases whereby there
is some evasion at present through the
giving of a guarantee instead of a properly
stamped lease.
In Clause 4 it is proposed to follow the
concession made in the British Finance
Act of 1931, whereby stockbrokers in the
provinces can get the benefit of a concession in the matter of Stamp Duty on
certain transactions which was formerly
not available.
a
Clause 5 deals with Excise Licence
Duty and gives power to enable
warrant obtained by a county council or
their officers to be executed in any part
of Northern Ireland, even though it be
outside their own county.
Clause 6 deals with a purely administrative and financial matter, and Clause
7 implements a proposal made in another
place in the Budget of the current year
to transfer £120,000 from the Road
Fund to the Exchequer. This, as hon.
Members will realise, is an abnormal
proposal to meet an abnormal financial
situation. Clause 8 gives the Ministry
of Finance power to make temporary
advances to the Road Fund during the
year as, of course, it will be realised that
Finance Bill
First Reading
15 DECEMBER 1931
the great bulk of the money which is
raised by taxation on motors accrues
during the first three months of the year
and it may be necessary to borrow for
the Road Fund to carry it over until the
revenue begins to come in more heavily.
The last part of the Bill deals with
Ulster Savings Certificates, and proposes
that holders of the first issue of the
certificates can have the date of final redemption extended for a further period.
My right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Finance, considers that the holders will
be glad to have this privilege.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure this Bill will
be received with a good deal of interest
by a a variety of persons. Speaking
generally, of course the Bill is based on
action which has already been taken at
Westminster, and I need not go into
that. Most of us will recognise the first
Clause, for instance, as being a reproduction of a Section in the Imperial Act
of 1930, and so on. There are one or two
questions which I desire to put to which
I do not propose to press for an answer
this afternoon, because I shall not be
moving any Amendment to the Bill, and
therefore the answers can stand over,
even to the Third Reading. I begin with
Clause 2, dealing with Death Duties,
objects of art, and so on. Clause 2, Sub-
Clause 2, paragraph 2, provides that
Death Duties shall not become chargeable
if the sale is to any museum, art gallery,
or similar institution. I
the
presume
Government is satisfied that the
pression used here includes library.
should not myself have had the smallest
doubt about it, because "museum" means
a temple of the Muses, but I think that
of late years literature has been attributed
to the Muses. They did not possess it
originally, but we have got to take that
view. The reason this presented a slight
difficulty was a comparison of this paragraph with the corresponding paragraph
in the English Act.
ex-
I
In the Imperial Act the limitation,
instead of being "any such museum, art
gallery,. or similar institution," is "the
National Gallery, the British Museum, or
any other similar National institution."
In addition, later on the same exemption
is given to any University, County
Council, or Municipal Corporation in
Great Britain, or the National Art
Collections Fund. To sum up what I
have to do is to ask the Government
whether they are sure this is the best we
can do, and whether they are quite
satisfied it fulfils the intention to
which the Parliamentary Secretary gave
utterance, the intention to encourage
people to leave these things which they
can not take with them to some public
institution for the benefit of a somewhat
larger circle than can enjoy them during
the life-time of the donor.
Coming to Clause 3, dealing with
Stamp Duties, I should like to know
whether the difficulty referred to is a
difficulty in Northern Ireland alone, or
whether the same difficulty has been experienced across the water. I could not
myself find-although, no doubt, my
search was hurried and perhaps incomplete any corresponding provision in
any English Statute which I could lay
my hands on. In Clause 4 we recognise
we are taking advantage of an opportunity presented to us in the Imperial
Act of 1931. I think the Parliamentary
Secretary alluded to that.
The remaining questions are questions
on the Road Fund. I am sure that if
our right hon. and gallant Friend, the
former Chairman of the Down County
Council (Colonel Sharman Crawford)
were able to be with us this afternoon he
would have addressed the House at some
length on the question of the raiding of
the Road Fund. I am afraid I cannot
embark on any definite crusade on the
subject in this House, because it seems
to me that the matter has been settled
elsewhere and has become an appropriation with which have really no
concern. But I may be permitted,
perhaps, to express the hope, which I am
sure will be shared by the Chairman of
the Londonderry County Council (Mr.
Long), if not by the Parliamentary
Secretary (Mr. Long), that these raids
will not be repeated.
we
The only other point is to draw
attention, in the Ulster Savings Certificate Clause, to the fact that that
currency may be extended to 1943. If I
am right, the corresponding extension in
Great Britain is limited to expire in 1940.
If that be so it will, as I think has already
been said, be regarded as a distinct
advantage to existing holders of those
certificates that they can prolong or
rather agree to the prolongation of their
security for a further period. I do not
imagine there is anything more to be said
about that, unless it should be the desire
to elaborate the situation in any way. On
Government Loans Bill 620
Finance Bill -
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie
the whole, I do not see any reason or any
justification for opposing the Bill in any
way.
Mr. MCALLISTER: If I am right in
my interpretation of Clause 3, I wish to
protest against the introduction of a new
precedent for raising revenue in Northern Ireland. As I understand this
Clause, it simply means demanding two
forms of duty upon the one instrument.
I do not think the Government can
logically justify such a proposition. The
effect of this Clause seems to me to be
that if a man takes out a lease, say for
five years at a rent of £55, that lease is
liable for a duty of something like 15/-.
If the man signs a guarantee the
document is liable to a duty of 5/-.
Mr. LONG: Sixpence.
Mr. MCALLISTER: At all events, I
object to the principle of trying to establish a
new precedent in Northern Ireland, a precedent which the hon. and
learned Member (Mr. Leslie) says he
cannot find in any English Act.
As one
who daily comes in contact with the
tremendously increased duties on all descriptions of legal documents I know the
hardship it inflicts on many people. I
think the situation of Clauses 7 and 8
is distinctly amusing. In the first place
I protest, as I have always protested,
against the raiding of the Road Fund for
a purpose for which it was never intended.
It amounts to a misappropriation by the
Government of money that was contributed for a specific purpose. That money
has been misappropriated in a most immoral way for another and an entirely
different purpose.
But what is the
situation of these two Clauses? It simply means that the Government rob the
Road Fund in one Clause and in the
next Clause they ask liberty to make a
temporary advance to the man they have
robbed. That is the logical effect of those
two Clauses, which, in my judgment, are
perfectly irreconcilable. I protest in the
strongest possible manner against the
raiding of the Road Fund, and against
the principle introduced in this Bill for
the first time of imposing two different
stamp duties upon one legal document.
Mr. LONG: I should like to point out
that there will be no such thing as two
duties being charged in respect of one
legal document. Duty will not be charged
twice, but the Government are strongly
of the opinion that the practice which
has come into vogue recently of substituting guarantees for properly stamped
leases should be put an end to. It is
merely an evasion of the law.
Mr. McALLISTER: With regard to
the proviso in Clause 3-
Mr. SPEAKER: Is the hon. Member
putting this in the form of a question,
because otherwise he is out of order.
The pro-
Mr. McALLISTER: Yes.
viso to Clause 3 says:-
Provided that where the Ministry of
Finance is satisfied that lease duty has
been duly paid upon some instrument
relating to the same letting as that to
which a guarantee relates, such guarantee shall be charged only with such
duty, other than lease duty, as it may
be liable to.
I should like to ask if that does not
bear the interpretation that there are two
duties chargeable, and if so, whether the
Deputy Leader of the House will take
steps to have the wording of that proviso
altered.
Mr. LONG: I am advised by the law
officers that what I have previously stated
is correct. However, I will inquire further into the matter and see if there is
any reality in the surmise of Senator
McAllister.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time," agreed to.

15 DECEMBER 1931
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Moved, "That this Report be now received." (Mr. Long).

Drainage Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved, "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill.-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Provision for funding of
certain expenses of Bann drainage
scheme).
Mr. LESLIE: It may be remembered
that the Act of 1929 provided for part
of the expenses in connection with the
Bann drainage improvement works
being met by the local authorities. Hon.
Members will find that in Section 14 and
15 of the Act to which I have referred,
I should like to hear from the Parliamentary Secretary that the new proposal as
to funding the Exchequer portion will not
in any way react on the local authorities'
contribution, either to advantage or to disadvantage. The point perhaps acquires
some little importance, because amongst
the expenses which the Ministry of
Finance will now be given power to fund
would seem to be the advances which
they can make in respect of expenditure
which will ultimately-that is, as I understand it, at the conclusion of the work
-be recovered from the County Councils.
But I presume that everything is in
order and that the local authorities are
not in any way affected, and consequently
it will not be necessary to amend the Bill
by leaving out the words "Section 13 of"
in line 13, on page 2 of this Clause, in
order that the old Act should become
subject to the provisions of the Bill now
before us. I imagine that that is understood and that the Bill is quite right as it
stands, but I should like to have that
assurance.
Mr. LONG: I am much obliged to the
learned Senator for giving me private
notice of this matter, as otherwise I could
not have given him the definite assurance
I am
now able to give him-that the
local authorities will be unaffected by
the change.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Contributions by urban
district councils to drainage works)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Power of county council
to recover expenses of cleansing watercourses).
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Clause 3, page 3, after Sub-section (2)
insert the following new Sub-section:
"(3) The said Section twelve shall
have effect subject to the addition
thereto of the following Sub-section-
"(4) For the purposes of this Section the expression 'scour out and
cleanse' shall be deemed to include the
cutting, topping or removal of any
bush, shrub or tree causing, or contributing to the causing of, a material
obstruction to the flow of any watercourse."
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry it was not
practicable to give longer notice of the
Amendment which I am about to move
here. It was represented to me yesterday
morning that the County Council of
Tyrone desired a slight Amendment on
the lines of that which appears on the
Paper, in order to give them rather more
power in the matter of ensuring the proper cleansing and scouring of water courses,
and not only water courses, but they
wanted arrangements to make that scouring and cleansing thoroughly efficient. They
found in practice and I have since heard
that the same difficulty is experienced in
County Derry and other places that the
powers given by the Act of 1929 are not
sufficient, because watercourses get
blocked, owing to roots, trees and bushes
growing on the banks or protruding from
the banks into the stream.
It seems somewhat doubtful whether
the county councils at present have the
necessary powers to insist on the removal
of such roots, trees and bushes, and I
think perhaps the whole Committee-
Drainage Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
particularly those of us who live in districts where the cleansing of watercourses
is a matter of everyday requirement, if
not occurrence-will agree that this is
a power which ought to be given if it
does not already exist. Accordingly I
put down the words on the Paper, but
since then it has been represented to me
that what I desire to effect can be more
readily attained by moving the following
Amendment instead of that on the Paper.
Therefore the Amendment I now move is:
Clause 3, page 3, after line 3, insert the
following new Sub-section:
:-
"(3) The said section twelve shall
have effect as if the following subsection were added thereto
"(6) For the purpose of this
Section the expression Scour out
and cleanse shall be deemed to
include the cutting, topping or removal of any bush, shrub or tree
causing, or likely to cause, or contributing to the causing of, a material
obstruction to the flow of any watercourse."
The only differences between
the
Amendment I have moved and that on
the Paper are the slight re-arrangement
of the expression relating to the following
Sub-section. I am now following what
I am informed is the more ordinary
custom. The introduction in the second
part of the sub-section of the expression
"or likely to cause" is rather a material
point. It would also be more convenient to
re-number it as (6) instead of 4a, because
that will make two foot-notes do the work
of three when we come to deal with the
revision of the Statutes. Section 12, to
which I am alluding, is Section 12 of the
Act of 1925, which in its existing form
will be found by reference to Section 22
of the Act of 1929. I beg to move.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I support the
Amendment, but I would like to see
"rock" included in the expression "bush,
shrub, or tree." Sometimes there is a
little bit of rock which it would be very
advisable to remove. Perhaps it is
included in the words "material
obstruction.”
Mr. LESLIE: Perhaps I might add
that it occurs to me that all that can
properly be done in the way of removing
any of these objectionable projections of
rock can be done under the power already
existing of widening the waters. Having
regard to the possibilities of rocks
stretching indefinitely shorewards, it
would perhaps not be wise for us to insist
on any more in this particular case.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I think the use
of the words "material obstruction"
may cause great difficulty in interpretation and the word "material" will be a
source of costly litigation. I was wondering whether the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) could suggest some
term other than "material" which would
be a more clear and better defined
expression.
Mr. LESLIE: The word "material" is
the best word I can find, having regard
to the right of appeal given by the
Section. If a man is required to remove
his bush, shrub, or tree, he has the right
of appeal. We might leave out the
words and simply say "cause obstruction,"
but that would expose us to the same
difficulty. I think it would be well to
leave the words as they are.
Mr. LONG: I should like to commend
the Amendment to the Senate. As one
who has been engaged in endeavouring
to administer the Drainage Acts in the
County of Londonderry, I feel that this
would be a very useful provision to have.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Short title and construction) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered That the Bill be reported,
with Amendment, to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
i
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendment.

CLAUSE 1 (Prolongation of periods.
of insurance in the case of certain employed contributors).
Mr. LESLIE: Clause 1 seems perhaps
a suitable occasion for something in the
It
nature of a personal explanation.
may be remembered that we discussed this
somewhat complicated Bill at some little
length yesterday, and it will be observed
from this morning's Belfast newspapers
that the report of our proceedings was
about as complete a hash as could well be
imagined. In particular, according to
the papers, remarks of mine relating to
the proper incidence
on the approved
societies of certain charges were made to
relate to Clause 3 of the Bill whereas they
could not possibly relate to that Clause,
but they did relate to Clauses 1 and 2.
More important was a matter in which
I am interested but not so immediately
concerned as the Parliamentary Secretary.
I asked him on Clause 1 whether the
finances of Northern Ireland would get
credit from the appropriate Board in
Great Britain for expenditure incurred in
pursuance of this Clause. The Parliamentary Secretary assured me, so I understood, that our finances would get credit.
The newspapers attribute to the Parliamentary Secretary the directly contrary
statement, that is, that we should not
get credit. That is a matter of some
little importance because people sometimes, I regret to say, read the Parliamentary reports of the newspapers rather
than our own debates, so I am afraid I
must ask the Parliamentary Secretary to
confirm what I am pretty certain he told
me, though the newspapers say he did
not tell me.
Mr. LONG: My recollection of the
remarks of Senator Leslie agree with
what he has just stated, and with
regard to the question as to whether the
Joint Exchequer Board would make allowance to Northern Ireland for the cost of
the services to which he referred my
reply was in the affirmative. I should
perhaps also add that the indistinctness.
of my speech was, I regret very much to
say, probably due to a sore throat, because
as a general rule the reports of the Press
-in 999 cases out of 1,000--put a better
construction on my remarks than they
deserve, and I am deeply indebted to the
Press in many instances for editing my
remarks. There is one other matter which
I notice was in the report of my reply
to Senator Barclay. I am reported as
having said the pasteurisation of large
quantities of milk would be impracticable.
What I did say was that pasteurisation of
small quantities would be impracticable.

Mr. CAMPBELL: Arising out of the
remarks of the hon. and learned Senator
and the Deputy Leader of the House, I
am very glad to know that we in this
House do not affect the ignorance that
is affected in some headquarters of English
politics that we do not read the newspapers. I do re-echo what the Deputy
Leader has said, that we are deeply indebted to the newspapers not only for
editing but for sub-editing our speeches.
We sometimes say things we ought not to
say, and they report them as we ought
to have said them.
I
These are the only remarks I intended
to make, but I myself may have a grievance to ventilate because I put a question
to the Deputy Leader of the House in
regard to personation at elections.
asked him whether or not the presiding
officer has statutory authority to prosecute a person detected in personation.
He said that there was such a duty imposed upon him by the Act of 1930. The
newspapers say he answered there was
no such duty, which I think is perfectly
right.

Mr. LONG: On Clause 2 Senator
Leslie inquired whether the term
"museum" would include library. The
answer is in the affirmative if the Ministry of Finance, acting under their
powers under the proviso, prescribe a
public library as being a suitable place
for the reception of such objects as are
mentioned in sub-section (3) of the
Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
Parliamentary Secretary. I am quite
sure that the power given to the Ministry of Finance will be sufficient to bring
our Measure into line with the English
Measure if the particular circumstances.
arise. I am thinking, of course, of circumstances which apparently are
templated in the English Measure and
which are not likely to arise over here.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
con-
CLAUSE 3 (Stamp duty on guarantee
for payment of rent).
Mr. LONG: The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) inquired whether this
Clause referred only to local conditions.
The Clause was inserted in the Bill in
order to deal with a situation which has
arisen in Northern Ireland, and which
has resulted in an evasion of payment
of the heavier stamp duties on leases.
I am not aware whether a similar practice is in vogue elsewhere, but if such
be the case, doubtless this Clause will
form a suitable precedent for legislation
in other jurisdictions.
Finance Bill-
Government Loans Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
Senator McAllister inquired whether
the Clause would not permit of a demand
for the payment of double lease duty.
This is not the case. The Clause does
not enable lease duty to be charged on
a guarantee where lease duty is paid on
an instrument relating to the same transaction. In such a case the guarantee
would only be subject to the usual duty
of 6d.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Notwithstanding
the answer of the Parliamentary Secretary, am I right in saying where a lease
is drawn up by the person securing the
rent, the guarantor is a party to the
lease and duty will not be charged on
the lease and also additional duty on
the guarantee although the guarantee is
included in the lease. Both transactions
are embodied in one document. Am I
not correct in stating that, whilst the
duty on an agreement under hand as
guarantee is 6d, if the guarantee is under
seal the duty will be 10s. Therefore,
there is 10s additional duty although
they are embodied in one single document.
Mr. LONG: It would be only in very
rare cases at least the cases would not
be very frequent in which a guarantee
is required. It would only be in cases
where security is demanded by a land-.
lord.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 4 (Amendment of s. 42 of

CLAUSE 8 (Power to make temporary
advances to Road Fund).
Mr. LESLIE: The newspapers again.
I was made to express the hope that
these loans would not recur. The pious
aspirations I endeavoured to express were
that these raids on the Road Fund would
not recur, because a raid on the Road
Fund is one thing I dislike. I expressed
no such hope in regard to the loan from
the Exchequer to the Road Fund because that is quite another thing, and I
think we all agree it is the best way out
of an unfortunate situation.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

stand part of the Bill.

years 1925 and 1926 to the years 1930 and
1931.

permission I shall circulate the reply in

necessary to give an adequate explanation
of the position. I am also sending the
hon. Member a copy.

17 DECEMBER 1931
Finance Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
Bill be now read the Third time."
Mr. MCALLISTER: Regarding the
question I raised yesterday about the
stamp duty on a lease, I am still convinced that if it is the intention of the
Government to charge only one duty on a
particular document then the Clause dealing with the matter in the Bill is not
sufficient to give effect to that. The case
I put to the Deputy Leader of the House
was this. Suppose a lease is prepared
between a landlord of the first part, a
tenant of the second part, and a guarantor
of the third part, I submit that it is
open to doubt that on that lease the
ordinary lease duty would have to be
paid, and an additional 10/- that is not
now chargeable upon a lease. I think
that is perfectly clear, and I ask the
Deputy Leader if it is the intention of
the Government that on any one document two duties should be charged. If
it is not, I hope he will give that assurance
to the House.
I
Mr. LONG: There is no intention to
increase the stamp duty which is at present chargeable by law upon a lease.
understand it is only in cases in which a
limited liability company becomes surety
that this would be required, but there is
no intention whatever to charge a second
duty.
