Belfast's Milk Supply.
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That this
House do now adjourn until Tuesday
next at 12-15 p.m.' We hope to receive
the Royal Assent to Bills, and to take
the First Reading of the Valuation Bill.

Mr. CAMPBELL: Before the House
adjourns to-day, which is almost the last
day before we meet next year, I would
like to call attention to a matter of direct
and definite importance to the health of
Belfast, and that is the question very
properly raised a few days ago by
Senator Barclay with regard to the absence of all control by the municipality
of Belfast of milk which reaches the city
from outside sources. The milk supply
which comes in this way perhaps constitutes the major portion of the city's milk
supply. The city of Belfast has spent
millions of pounds in endeavouring to
secure for its citizens supplies of water
free from contamination at the source.
Pure water is essential to the health of
the citizens, but pure supplies of milk
are no less essential to the health of the
people. The danger is not so much, I
think, of the character apprehended by
Senator Barclay in the case of what he
calls milk which is not clean, but in my
humble judgment the danger would
rather be tuberculosis from the consumption of milk containing tubercle bacilli.
That is a very real danger, and it is also
a very terrible danger. More than that,
it is a preventable danger.
It came as a great surprise to me when
I listened to the discussion arising out of
Senator Barclay's question to learn that
the tragedy is that, as the law stands at
present, the evil is not preventible in
Belfast. I think competent authorities
recognise that the milk supply from
sources outside Belfast, though it shows
signs of improvement, is not satisfactory,
and that the milk supplied from the dairies
and other places inside the municipal
boundaries is reasonably safe and clean.
There are, I believe, three classes of
milk. There is the T.T. milk, the A
grade I believe it is called, which is quite.
Adjournment-
Belfast's Milk Supply.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
safe; there is the pasteurised milk, the
second class, which is also quite safe--
Mr. HILL DICKSON: No.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am instructed
that when it is pasteurised, although it
takes away some of the vitamines, the
milk is safe for all practical purposes.
Senator Hill Dickson controverts that. I
dare say he speaks with greater knowledge
and experience than I can, but it is the
opinion of many conversant with the
subject that the pasteurisation of milk
does remove any serious danger, though
it may diminish the strength and vitamines in the milk. Then there is the
third class, the ordinary milk, which is
the milk in most common use in Belfast.
It constitutes by far the largest supply
of milk consumed by the citizens. I believe that the distinguished Medical
Superintendent Officer of Health for Belfast has stated that out of 500 specimens
of ordinary Belfast milk examined in the
year 1930, about 50 per cent. were found
to contain germs traceable to dirt somewhere, probably in the handling of the
milk. That is bad enough, but what is
more serious and more disquieting is the
fact revealed by the same distinguished
authority's statement that out of about
700 examinations in 1930 for tubercle
bacilli 8 per cent. were found to contain
this bacillus.
When you link this fact with the further fact ascertained by medical science
that 60 per cent. of cases of surgical
tuberculosis in children were caused by
drinking milk containing this bacillus it
gives one cause for serious reflection.
Taking the area of Belfast, I do not
know what the ratio of non-tubercular
to tubercular cows would be, but I hope
it is not high. I am told that a cow
which is rejected by a veterinary inspector
in the establishment of a T.T. dairyman
can be sold and transferred to the dairy
of an ordinary dairyman who may continue to supply milk from that cow to
the City of Belfast. If that is so it is a
very strange and remarkable fact.
I am
very glad that this matter has been raised
in this House, because it discloses that
the great necessity for a more satisfactory
control over the milk supplies of this city
is very real and very urgent.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: In connection
with the matter which the hon. and
learned Senator has raised, I would remind him of the fact that most of the
rural districts in Northern Ireland have
adopted the Dairies and Cowsheds Order,
and consequently they have a veterinary
inspector to inspect their dairies. The
rural districts object to a double inspection. For instance, when a man in my
area gets a certificate that his byre is
kept in good condition and a veterinary
surgeon inspects it, he does not want a
man coming from Belfast to condemn it.
We have quite enough inspection without
having a double inspection of this kind.
I admit that when milk is pasteurised
it may be free from germs, but it is not
of the same quality as pure milk direct
from a tubercle-free herd of cattle. It
is most iniquitous that when a cow has
been condemned in one place it can be
brought into the market and sold to
another customer. However, if we begin
to slaughter all the cows suffering from
tuberculosis in this country 75 per cent.
of them would have to go.
Mr. CAMPBELL: So high a percentage?
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I think so,
and there would be practically no milk
left for anyone.
However, it is a very
moot question if a cow that be suffering from tuberculosis will react to a
tubercular test. If her udder is free
her milk may be all right.
may
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: 1
am not going to reply to the lurid speech
delivered by the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell), but speaking for my own
county of Down, which supplies a great
deal of milk, I can say that for many
years now tuberculosis in cattle has been
on the decline.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I think the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) has
rendered a public service in drawing
attention to this matter at the present
time, and I should like to support
practically all the statements he has
made in the House to-day. If the ideal
could be attained, and speedily attained,
of having our daily milk supplied from
cows that were free from tuberculosis
then all might be well, and on the
principle of prevention being better than
cure, that is the ideal we ought to aim
at. But it seems that that ideal is not
immediately practicable, so we ought to
direct our attention to getting the next
best thing. If conditions are such as
Adjournment-
Belfast's Milk Supply.
17 DECEMBER 1931
Senator Barclay pointed out, that quite
a large number of the dairies outside our
jurisdiction, that is the jurisdiction of the
urban authorities, are really very far
from being clean and sanitary, the next
step would be to insist on pasteurisation.
Different views are held as regards the
advantages of pasteurisation, but if it is
done properly and in sufficient quantity
and for a sufficient length of time, and
the temperature is kept up to 140 or 150
for a good half hour, we have a guarantee.
that, although we may lose a little in
vitamines we have at any rate a safe milk
for public consumption.
It should be borne in mind as a plea
for the further adoption of pasteurisation
that milk may contain two germs that
perhaps do not come quite so prominently
before our minds as tubercle bacillus. I
refer to the germs of diphtheria and
scarlatina. These germs are inhibited by
pasteurisation, and it is more or less a
subsidiary advantage to know that while
tubercle bacilli are inhibited, if not
absolutely destroyed by pasteurisation,
these other two germs that occasionally
occur and are disseminated in our milk
supply are at any rate sufficiently held
in check. The chief anxiety seems to
hinge on group three milk, owing to the
vast number of small dairies and sources
of supply where the milk is not even free
from the coarser contamination by dirt,
which human digestive organs can dispose
of to a great extent. There is also a great
possibility of these smaller sources of
supply being served by cattle that have
been discarded by T.T. dairymen and sold
to others who go on supplying milk of a
tubercular character to the public. It is
of the utmost importance that children
under the age of six, at any rate, should
be supplied with absolutely pure milk or
non-infected milk so far as germs are
concerned, and in directing public
attention to this matter, as hon. Senators
have done, they are entitled to emphasise
the point that all irregular suppliers of
milk should be immediately inspected
and, if necessary, stopped from retailing
milk, or at any rate rendered amenable
to the ordinary regulations, so that the
citizens of Belfast as well as people in the
country can have a guarantee that the
milk supplied to them is reasonably safe
for children, particularly up to six years
of age.
I support very strongly the contentions
of the hon. and learned Senator opposite
(Mr. Campbell), and I think the matter
is not one that should be allowed to pass
without any action being taken. It may
be that many people cannot afford the
price of T.T. milk, but the large number
of people who cannot should at least, as
taxpayers, have the right to be defended
against infection from sources of that
kind that are get-atable and ascertainable
as regards their purity. I heartily
support the contention that something
should be done.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
think I should say on behalf of the
Belfast Corporation that this is a very
old and a very vexed question which has
given the Belfast Corporation very serious
thought for a considerable time. I am
sure it is the general desire that none
but pure milk should come into the City
of Belfast, but unfortunately the Belfast
Corporation has got to administer the
law as it is. There is no doubt that our
Superintendent Medical Officer of Health
is doing all in his power to ensure that
only pure milk reaches the city, but it
has become quite clear that further legislation in this respect is necessary.
You
may take it from me, speaking on behalf
of the Belfast Corporation, that any further legislation dealing with this question
will be welcomed by the Corporation, who
will endeavour to see that it is carried
out so far as they are concerned.
Mr. McCORKELL: I also support
what has been said by the previous
speakers. At the same time I think I
can perhaps give the House a hint as
to how some of these tubercular cows can
be got rid of. It does seem a scandalous
thing that a man who has in his herd a
cow that is infected with tuberculosis can
sell that cow elsewhere. Under the law
to-day an animal declared to be suffering
from the disease must be slaughtered
is
and compensation paid to the
owner of the cow. That scheme is a
very good one, but unfortunately the
amount of compensation paid is such that
it puts a premium on dishonesty. Speaking from memory, I think the effect of
the words in the Order dealing with the
slaughter of such a cow is that the price
paid in compensation shall be the same
as the price the animal would fetch in
the open market, the purchaser not being
aware that the cow was diseased.
practice, that has not proved to be the
case. There are several grades of disease,
L
In
Adjournment--
Belfast's Milk Supply.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McCorkell]
and a proportionate amount of compensation is paid according to the state the
animal is found to be in on slaughter.
I know of a case where a cow which
had cost £26 was declared to be tubercular. She was slaughtered at the expense of the owner, who also paid the
veterinary surgeon's fee. The animal
having been slaughtered, the owner was
given a perfectly clean bill of health in
respect of the cow. In other words, there
had been a mistake, and of course everyone is not infallible. On a post-mortem
examination it was found that this cow
was perfectly healthy, but compensation
of only £11 was paid for an animal that
had cost £26. I suggest that if the department concerned would pay, not a
good price, but a fair price for the
animals slaughtered it would go a long
way towards collecting these diseased
animals and getting them done away with.
The only argument put up to me when
I suggested this was that there were
dealers in Northern Ireland who would
go round collecting all the tubercular cows
they could find, get them condemned and
slaughtered, and receive compensation
for them.
Even if that proved to be so I contend
that this would be the most simple and
the quickest way of getting rid of them.
at one fell swoop. So long as we go on
paying a man a quarter of the value of
an animal dishonest people, when they
suspect that a cow is tubercular, will pass
it on to someone else before the disease
progresses any further.
Of course,
the
ravages of the disease spread, and the
milk of that cow is supplied to the public. If a man had confidence that he
would receive a fair price for a cow that
he declared to be tubercular we should
get rid of them very much more quickly
than we are doing at present. As it is.
the present system of paying compensation is merely putting a premium on
dishonesty.
Mr. BARCLAY: I should like to ask
the hon. and gallant Senator (Colonel
Sinclair) whether it is possible to have
diphtheria and scarlatina germs in grade
A milk. If so, it would be necessary
that it should also be pasteurised.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
Although I do not know very much about
this subject, there is one remedy that
suggests itself to me. At the present
time inspectors examine milk to ascertain
if water has been added, and if at the
same time it was examined for germs it
might be an advantage.
If milk was
found to be infected and the source of
supply was stopped, the producers would
very soon realise that it was in their own
interests to dispose of diseased cattle.
Colonel SINCLAIR: In reply to the
question asked by Senator Barclay, 1
would say that scarlatina and diphtheria
germs can be in grade A or T.T. milk,
but it is extremely improbable. A dairy
supplying grade A milk is so admirably
managed and has such perfect sanitary
and inspection arrangements that any
outbreak of scarlatina or diphtheria.
would, I think, be at once reported, and
the milk would be protected. Therefore,
while it is theoretically possible for that
very pure milk to become infected in that
way, the possibility is extremely remote
and very unlikely, because the owners
in their Own interests will take the
greatest possible care. They have the
best scientific instruction at their disposal,
and Senator Barclay may rest assured
that it is improbable that such
possibility would occur. As I have said,
it is theoretically possible, but practically
not very likely.
a
Mr. McALLISTER: I am sure the
House is very grateful to the hon. and
gallant Senator (Colonel Sinclair), who
can speak with such authority on this
subject. I think I am expressing the
sentiment of every Member of the Senate
when I say that his speeches in this House
are all too few. In view of the speech
made by the Lord Mayor of Belfast I
should like to say a few words. He has
indicated that in his opinion, speaking as
the head of the Belfast Corporation, the
law as it stands at present is not satisfactory. I would like to say that if there
is any intention of introducing any new
legislation in this respect, the interests
of the farmers, the milk producers,
should be protected. I think the one
oustanding fact that emerged from the
recent milk "war," a stunning fact to
those who did not know, was the very low
price paid by the distributors to the
producers compared with the price
charged to the consumers. Therefore, it
is an undoubted fact that the farmers
cannot afford to bear any part of the loss
that may be incurred through the
slaughter of tubercular animals, or any
part of the cost of any other amelioration
of the present conditions.
6-16
Adjournment-
Belfast's Milk Supply.
17 DECEMBER 1931
I was rather struck by the remark of
Senator Hill Dickson that 75 per cent. of
the cattle in Northern Ireland were
suffering from tuberculosis. I hope he
has exaggerated the facts in this respect.
I think the best way to stamp out this
disease is to see that protective measures
are taken. The farmer should be encouraged to have his animals inspected,
and the best and only way to encourage
him is to give him full value for a cow
that is slaughtered. I understand that
at the present time, if an animal is
slaughtered and no disease is found, the
owner gets the full market value as fixed
by a veterinary inspector, but if the
animal is in an advanced stage of the
disease he gets only one-third of the
value. I think that in cases where a
farmer is an honest victim of the purchase of a diseased cow he should be paid
a fair value for the animal when it is
slaughtered.
Mr. LONG: I should like to direct
the attention of Members of the Senate
to a report of a debate on this subject
which appeared in Hansard about two
years ago. We had a very full and informative discussion on that occasion and
then, as to-day, we had a very valuable
contribution from the hon. and gallant
Member (Colonel Sinclair). I think it
would be an advantage if hon. Members
referred to the report of the previous
debate.
It is a fact that local authorities generally
have an efficient system of inspection, but
there are some districts in which inspection is not carried out. At present the
Dairies and Cowsheds Order is not mandatory, but the Government will, in a
Bill which they hope to introduce in the
near future, invite the opinion of the
Members of this House as to whether in
all the circumstances this Dairies and
Cowsheds Order should be made mandatory. I know that in many districts
inspection is carried out very efficiently
indeed, but I will bring the remarks of
Senator Campbell and other speakers to
the attention of the appropriate Government Department. The matter is being
carefully considered, and will continue to
engage their attention.
On Question, Motion, "That this
House do now adjourn until Tuesday at
12-15 p.m.," agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
minutes before Four o'clock
until Tuesday next at a
Quarter after Twelve o'clock.

I have received your Address praying
that it may please His Majesty to
make an Order in Council under Subsection 2 of Section 18 of the Architects

Death of Sir William Coates, Bart.
Mr. LONG: Before proceeding with our
business this afternoon. I think that it
would be fitting that some reference should
be made to the loss of a prominent and
distinguished member of the community.
I refer to the death of Sir William Coates.
as
Sir William was not a Member of this
House in recent months, but his long
connection with it, both in his ex-officio
capacity as Lord Mayor of Belfast and
an elected Senator, justifies me, I
think, in paying a tribute to his memory.
He was actually its first Member, and
was the first to take the Oath and the
Chair at the initial meeting in June,
1921. He was always jealous of its
dignity, and was a forceful contributor
to its debates. In all that he did he
was actuated solely by a sense of public
duty and by the wish to represent in a
conscientious and worthy manner the city
of Belfast, of which he was so long the
chief magistrate. I am sure that I am
expressing the wishes of hon. Members
in offering our sympathy to his widow
and to the members of his family in their
very great loss.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish to associate
my colleagues and myself with the graceful tribute which has just been paid by
the Deputy Leader of the House to the
memory of Sir William Coates. During
his occupancy of the office of Lord Mayor
of this city he was a Member of this
House. He was constant in his attendance at our sittings, he took a very keen
and a very intelligent 'interest in our
deliberations, and in his many-sided
public career he displayed a rare
of duty, a rare sense of the conscientious
discharge of his duty. He was a great
public servant, and the community, including the Members of this House,
deplore his passing as a heavy loss.
wish to tender to his widow, his son, and
other relatives our deep sense of the
great loss they have sustained by his
passing.
sense
We
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
26 JANUARY 1932
Questions to Minister.
TUBERCULAR MILCH COWS.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:-
To ask the Leader of the House if he
can state the estimate of the Ministry
of Agriculture as to the percentage of
milch cows in Northern Ireland suffering
from tuberculosis; whether that Ministry's
estimate that only one per cent. of the
milch cows are giving diseased milk is
based on actual tests; whether in the year
1930 out of about 700 tests of milk by
the civic authorities in Belfast 8 per cent.
were found to contain tubercle bacilli;
and what were the figures for 1931.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In putting this
question may I be permitted to express
what I am sure is the general and genuine
regret of the Members of the House at
the absence of the noble Leader of the
House (Viscount Charlemont), through
indisposition, and our earnest hope that
he will speedily be restored to health, and
that we shall soon see him back again in
his accustomed place in the Senate. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. LONG: There are no data available either in Great Britain or Northern
Ireland on which to base a reliable estimate of the percentage of milch cows
suffering from tuberculosis. The figures
for Great Britain have been variously
stated as between 30 per cent. and 50 per
cent., but no official estimate is possible.
The Ministry's estimate that not more
than 1 per cent. of the milch cows in
Northern Ireland are affected with tuberculosis of the udder was not the result
of actual tests, but was based mainly on
data obtained from the Report of the
Belfast Medical Officer of Health for the
year 1930, confirmed by figures for com-
Area.
(a) Total payments
on account of Elementary Education
in year ended 31st
March, 1931, out of
Parliamentary
Funds and Local
Rates.
The
parable areas in Great Britain.
figure of 8 per cent. mentioned by the
hon. Senator apparently refers 'almost
exclusively to mixed milks collected in
the streets, at milk shops and railway
stations, and not to the milk from individual cows, and, accordingly,
inference can be drawn therefrom as to
the percentage of the cow population that
is secreting tubercle organisms in the
milk. I do not know if the comparable
figures for 1931 have yet been published.
