THE SENATE
NOTES.
1. The audit of the accounts of the
Local Education Authorities for the year
ended 31st March, 1931, is not yet complete, and, consequently, the figures
given above, whilst substantially accurate,
are not final figures.
2. Payments in respect of cost of
administration, care and education of
afflicted children, the training of teachers,
the superannuation of teachers, personal
allowances to certain ex-model school
teachers, and relief towards repayment of
loans on teachers' residences, are not included in the figures given above.
3. In addition to the figures given
above, net expenditure, amounting to
about £650, fell to be borne by the
Ministry of Education in respect of the
cost of repairing, etc., certain public
elementary schools which were vested in
the Ministry.
PORK, POTATO AND OATMEAL
PRICES.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if he can state how
the average prices of pork, potatoes, and
oatmeal in Northern Ireland to-day compared with the prices ruling three months
ago.
Mr. LONG: According to the returns
of prices of agricultural produce compiled
by the Ministry of Agriculture, the average price of pork in the week ending 16th
January was 51s 10d per cwt., and in the
week ending 17th October, 1931, 48s 6d
per cwt.
In the case of potatoes, the
average price was 8s 04d in the week
ending 16th January, and 5s 6d in the
week ending 17th October.
With regard to the price of oatmeal, it
is understood that during the past three
months the price of medium oatmeal has
advanced from 13s 6d to 15s per cwt., and
the price of flake oatmeal from 30s to 31s
per cwt.
Prices of white oats sold at markets in
Northern Ireland have advanced from
6s 1d per cwt, in the week ending 17th
October to 8s 3d per cwt. in the week
ending 16th January.
RAVAGES BY RATS AND MICE.
Mr. S. CUNNINGHAM: I beg to ask
the Leader of the House whether he can
state the approximate loss to farmers in
Northern Ireland resulting from the
ravages of rats and mice.
I cannot help feeling that there must
be a tremendous loss incurred throughout
the country owing to the large amount of
grain and produce that is destroyed by
rats and mice, not to speak of the amount
of loss that is similarly sustained in the
stores in the city. It has been brought to
my notice that within recent years there
has, perhaps, been a greater increase in
these vermin than ever before, and that
the rat and mice population of Northern
Ireland is greater to-day than ever it was.
These vermin also constitute a great danger
as disease carriers, and the reason I would
like to have this information is with the
object of considering whether anything
can be done to reduce the losses caused by
these vermin.
Mr. CAMPBELL: As this question has
been put by a Member of the House who
happens to be a Privy Councillor, may I
that some of us, in fact all of us,
say
would like to congratulate the father of
the House (Mr. Armstrong), who has been
appointed a Privy Councillor since our
last meeting. He has the prescriptive
right-it is a kind of unwritten lawalthough he has been a long time on the
back bench, to take his seat on the front
bench beside other hon. Members who
happen to be Privy Councillors. I hope
that in a very short time he will be sitting near the Nationalist Members, and
that he will think he is in a very good
neighbourhood and in good company.
It
I am very glad that this question of
the destruction caused by rats and mice
has been raised, and for one reason.
is not generally known that the harbouring of rats and mice by any person is
an offence punishable by law. There is an
Act of Parliament known as the Rats
and Mice Destruction Act, 1919, under
which any person who is found harbouring
rats and mice, and who does not take
reasonably practical steps to get rid of
them is liable to certain sharp penalties
at the instance of the competent authorities, the county council, or the county
borough council, or the port sanitary
authority, as the case may be, and if they
make default in their obligations the
governing body of all, in Northern Ireland
the Ministry of Home Affairs, can in
their default step in and execute the duty.
The reason I mention this is because I
quite agree with the right hon. Senator,
26 JANUARY 1932
Oral Answers

Oral Answers
matter as it concerns farmers, the whole
that this nuisance is on the increase in
Northern Ireland. It constitutes a very
serious menace to the community at large,
a menace that is not really appreciated,
because these animals are not only a dangerous foe to mankind in the way of
destroying food and spoiling the food.
they do not destroy, and otherwise causing
destruction by boring and gnawing, but
they are also formidable carriers of
disease, and I believe the extent of their
disease carrying is increasing.
I was
I would ask hon. Members, in considering this question, to bear in mind that
mere isolated action is useless.
It is no
good one man killing rats if his neighbour
will not do the same. The Act of 1919
aims at concerted action by communities
and members of the community.
very glad to see recently in Derry, where
they are always alive to matters of public
importance, that the town clerk there
reported that the merchants in Derry are
desirous to join in a scheme for the
destruction of rats in that quarter of the
world. I think that is very gratifying,
and perhaps other ports as well as Derry
might be kept clear of these pests.
believe that in England the London
sanitary authority and other authorities,
as well as the railway companies, are
doing their utmost by concerted action to
get rid of these vermin. I am glad the
I
hon. Member raised the question, as it is
one of very considerable importance.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I do not know
whether it would be in order for me to
make a remark in extension or amplification of the remarks that fell from the hon.
and learned Senator opposite. It is in
reference to disease. It may be of interest
to Members of the Senate and the general
public to know that an estimate of the
total rat population of the United Kingdom is 45,000,000; that is to say, a rat
for every human being in the country, and
their depredations have been estimated,
I understand, as carefully as an estimate
of that kind can be framed, at about
£1,000,000 a week, or an annual loss of
about £50,000,000.
It is also of interest in a biological sense
to know that a couple of rats under
favourable conditions may account for a
progeny of 880 rats in one year, so that
the prolific character or the vermin may
very well be determined in that way.
do not wish to detain the Senate with any
further remarks, but while the question
is mainly addressed to one aspect of the
I
community is concerned. The great
plague, the Black Death of medieval
times, was due to a rat-borne disease. We
are still liable to plague-bubonic plague,
as it is called-owing to incursions of
affected rats from the East, and though
the vigilance of the sanitary authorities
guards against it, still it breaks out
occasionally, and it is worthy of attention.
It is a rat-borne disease. Fleas infest the
rats, and they leave the dead rats and
proceed to other rats or human beings and
bite in the poison of the disease. Therefore, one possible source of danger has
been drawn attention to by the question
of the right hon. Gentleman.
There is also the measly porker disease,
trichinosis, which accounted for a great
many deaths in the past, also a form of
dysentery produced mainly by a form of
sewer rat. I should mention also one
form of tape worm, I think the intermediate stage is due to this source, the
worm being found in the rat. These
things I mention-perhaps it is an intrusion-only to show there is another
aspect besides that which concerns farmers, namely the general safety and wellbeing of the people of these islands, and
it would be a further inducement to
exterminate the vermin or the superabundance of the rat population, to know
the morbific influence which rats are well
known to exercise.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
do not know whether I am in order, as
one who has been in foreign and oriental
countries, in making a suggestion to the
Government. When I was in India my
compound was very full of rats. I got a
mongoose, and within a month there was
not a rat in the compound. I suggest,
therefore, the Government should get a
shipload of mongooses and let them out.
(Laughter.)
Mr. LONG: I regret that I am not in
a position to supply any precise information in regard to the monetary loss experienced by farmers in Northern Ireland
from the ravages of rats and mice.
It is undoubted, however, that considerable damage is done by these rodents
to potatoes in pits and stores, to grain in
stacks and stores, and to feeding stuffs.
At the same time these rodents constitute
a nuisance to the farmer in many other
ways, such as through attacks on young
chickens, the stealing of eggs, and damage
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
to wooden poultry houses and feeding
appliances. In agricultural districts
farmers and poultry-breeders are waging
a continuous war against rats.
In reference to the remarks of the hon.
and learned Senator Campbell, I wish to
say that Belfast County Borough Council
and the Belfast Port Sanitary Authority
are the only local authorities at
present exercising powers conferred
on them by the Rats and Mice
Destruction Act, 1919. However, 1
understand that Londonderry County
Borough Council, as the hon. and learned
Senator indicated, has the matter under
consideration. No county councils have
so far used their power in this matter.
Far from the hon. Senator's (Colonel
Sinclair's) contribution to our discussion being an intrusion, I feel, and I am
sure other members also feel, that it was
a valuable contribution to our debates.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Valuation Acts Amendment Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This Bill, it will be seen,
contains 26 clauses and three schedules,
but the essence of the Measure is to be
found in Clause 1, which enacts that there
shall be a general revaluation throughout
Northern Ireland as soon as may be after
the Bill is passed, and that subsequent
general revaluations shall be at intervals
of five years.
In regard to the first of
these proposals, we have to remember that
revaluation is long overdue. The last
general valuation in the country as a
whole was made as long ago as 1852, and
in Belfast there was a revaluation between
1900 and 1906. It is inevitable that
anomalies should have arisen in the intervening period.
I am not saying whether these anomalies
are greater in volume in the city here or
outside it, but they undoubtedly exist,
and I could, if need be, give lists of cases
to show that they are absolutely indefensible. I think that it is a reasonable
proposition that there should now be a
general revaluation, and that everyone
should be brought back into a proper
relationship with his neighbour in regard
to what he ought to pay, whether it be
rates or income tax. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) It may be of interest to
hon. Members to mention that the clause
preserves the "Griffiths" basis of 1852 as
the basis upon which hereditaments are
to be valued. It is expected that the
new valuation lists will come into operation in the financial year 1935-36.
On the second point, namely, that the
subsequent general valuations shall be at
five-year intervals, I do not think that
this need cause any apprehension. From
the point of view of expense, it would
probably be cheaper to do it in this way
than if the intervals were longer. For
the comparatively short period of five
years it will not be necessary for the
Commissioner of Valuation to undertake
on each occasion a full inspection of
property, which would, of course, require
the engagement of a large specialised staff
on each occasion, and which we might be
obliged to employ if the interval were
greater.
Valuation, as I am sure every hon.
Member is aware, is the basis of two
general charges-local rates and income
tax. I have already said that an effect
of the Bill should be to remove anomalies.
No one can-or at all events ought to
-complain of that. So far as rates are
concerned, a general increase in valuation if this were to be the result, should
make no difference, as obviously it should
follow that the necessary rate to be struck
should be automatically decreased. Local
authorities who require a certain fixed
sum will not of course need to fix so high
a poundage if the valuation becomes.
higher than it is at present.
In regard to income tax, there is no
doubt that an increase in valuation will
yield a higher amount, but this is hardly
an injustice when we consider that the
valuation will be based on the proper
value of the property. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) It is not fair that an
owner who derives an income of, say,
£400 a year where the valuation is only
£100 should pay income tax on a basis
of £100, while his neighbour who has a
corresponding income derived from other
sources pays on the full amount.
If any
one thinks that he is aggrieved by the
revised valuation he will have the full
rights of appeal which exist at present
and which are continued under Clause 3
of the Bill.
Regarding the revaluation costs, estimated at £120,000, the Northern Ireland
Government's part of the expenditure,
estimated at £90,000, must, of course,
be met from the Northern Ireland Exchequer. When, however, the amount
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
26 JANUARY 1932
of the Imperial contribution to be paid.
by Northern Ireland comes to be assessed
by the Joint Exchequer Board, it will
be open to that Board to allow Northern
Ireland's expenditure on revaluation to
rank as an allowable deduction before
the contribution figure is reached.
I
In Clause 2 of the Bill, read in conjunction with the First Schedule, there
is the important provision that agricultural land and farm buildings are not to
be revalued. The amounts to be entered
in the revaluation lists are to be the
same as they are at present.
Of course,
these properties have been derated and
derating is not in any way affected.
would like to emphasise this for the benefit of those outside who may have been
listening to idle rumours and to people
who are entirely misinformed-(Hon.
Members: Hear, hear)—as to the effect
of this Bill on the farming community.
Dwelling-houses are of course subject to
payment of rates at present, and farmers
will have to submit to revaluation in that
respect in the same way as other houseowners, but there is no provision whatever in the Bill by which the farmers
can be asked to pay rates on land or
agricultural buildings.
I
I now pass on to the question of the
effect of this Bill on the finances of local
authorities. Clause 16 prescribes the
contributions to be received from the Six
Counties and the two County Boroughs
towards the cost of the annual revisions
carried out at their request. The
amount recoverable by the Exchequer
under existing law was fixed in 1874 and
represents one-half of the cost of annual
revision in each area at that date.
submit that the provisions of Clause 16
provide a fair assessment, and are reasonable in that they relate the charge to
present costs. In Clause 17 it is proposed that in regard to each general revaluation there is to be a contribution
of one-fourth of the total cost from the
counties and county boroughs, subject
however to a maximum of £30,000.
vision is contained in the Bill for the
payment of sums on account as from 1st
April, 1933, and it is expected that the
payments will be spread over three years
from that date. The burden in respect
of any one year should not therefore be
excessive.
Pro-
In addition to these two specific contributions under Clauses 16 and 17 a point
was raised during the passage of the Bill
elsewhere, that as a result of revaluation
there might be a considerable reduction
in the amount of derating grants at
present payable from the Exchequer to
the authorities, and that an undue proportion of local expenditure might, therefore, be thrown upon the underated hereditaments. An assurance has been given
in another place that, lest any injustice
should thereby be caused, a full inquiry
will take place when the results of revaluation are known. This assurance has
now been given statutory form in Clause
24 of the Bill, which enacts that provision
shall be made for a review of the capacity
of the local rates based on the new valuation to bear the expenditure required by
the law then in force to be defrayed out
of those rates. It is thus ensured that
nothing will be done to impose any unjust burden on a county council or other
rating authority as a consequence of revaluation.
Since the Bill passed through another
place, representations have been received
that insufficient time is allowed for the
lodgment of appeals against new valuations. The Government propose to introduce an amendment in Committee which,
it is hoped, will meet this point.
I have now, I think, dealt with the
main principles and matters in the Bill;
there are, of course, a number of points
of detail included in the various clauses,
which can, if necessary, be explained at a
later stage.
We have nere this afternoon
my right hon. Friend the Minister of
Finance (Mr. Pollock)-(Hon. Members:
Hear, hear)-who will be available to
answer the various questions which may
arise in the course of the debate.
I shall conclude, therefore, by saying
that in promoting this Bill the Government is endeavouring to secure that every
individual shall pay his fair share, and
not one penny more than his fair share,
of the common burdens of the Province.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). I beg to
move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am sure we are
all delighted to see the Minister of
Finance here in our midst this evening.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). His visits
to this House are few and far between,
just as angels' visits are. I am especially
pleased to see him here, because in a sense
he in Northern Irelana has taken the
place that a great British Minister used
to hold in England-Mr. Gladstone,
whose Budgets in Victorian times were
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
marvellous specimens of statesmanship
and finance. That great statesman was
referred to as the wizard of finance in
England. Well, if the Minister of
Finance (Mr. Pollock) is the wizard of
finance in Northern Ireland, he has
accomplished a marvellous feat as the
wizard of this Bill, because he has conjured up a Bill for the raising of revenue
in the spectral shape of a Valuation Bill.
That operation was foreshadowed in the
Report of the Committee on the Financial
Relations between the central and local
authorities, which recommended
valuation of buildings
a re-
"to ensure that the yield of taxation in
Northern Ireland is a closer reflection
of the true taxable capacity of the
area,"
but the same Committee put on emphatic
record its view that
"the total amount of money which can
in equity be extracted from the payers
of rates is at the present moment at or
very near its maximum,"
and that the limit of taxable capacity in
Northern Ireland has been reached.
This Bill has been brought in at the
dictation of the bureaucracy in Whitehall,
and is a fresh proof of the great and
growing power of bureaucracy in Whitehall and Northern Ireland. Many years
ago, in the British Parliament, a resolution was passed that when a certain power
was great and growing it ought to be
checked, and I hope that some day by the
unanimous assent of both Houses of
Parliament, both here and elsewhere, a
similar principle will commend itself to
legislators. We are told by Ministers that
if this Bill does not pass, if this Valuation
Bill is not carried here, revaluation will
be forced upon this area by order of
Whitehall. We are told that this is a
subordinate Parliament, that we are not
our
own masters. I think in obeying
that dictation Ministers have shown a
want of backbone, and a little touch of the
spirit of John Hampden would have been
very useful in this Province at the present
time.
This is a Bill to test inquisitorially the
value of property in Northern Ireland,
but it will also test the value of this
Parliament; it will test whether this
Parliament is worthy of the name;
whether this Parliament has really control
of its own fiscal affairs, and whether it is
a Parliament worthy to endure. At the
present time, when by universal admission
the taxpayers and ratepayers of Northern
Ireland are being touched and grieved by
taxes, the Government come along with a
Bill which will bring a large extra revenue
directly to the Exchequer at Whitehall, on
a conservative estimate some £500,000 a
year 5 per cent. of the revenue of Northern Ireland-it may rise to £1,000,000and indirectly, on an adjustment of
accounts between the two Exchequers,
to the Northern Exchequer, which will
save the Northern Exchequer an annual
sum estimated at from £50,000 to £70,000
per annum out of the grants payable to
local authorities under the Derating Act,
1928, and the machinery of which will cost
£120,000. And these diminished grants
will have to be made good by a rate on
the underated occupiers of houses.
The Imperial masters say to Northern
Ireland, whose contribution this year to
the Imperial services is £70,000 provided
at the expense of the Sinking Fund and
the Road Fund, "You do not contribute a
penny to the load of taxation under which
the British people are beggared and bowed
to the ground. Our Treasury owes to our
own citizens 7,000 million pounds, and to
foreigners 1,000 million pounds, now 1,400
millions owing to the depreciation in the
£. You do nothing to lighten this
burden. You pay next to nothing towards
the British Navy, Army, Air Force and
Civil Service. We have given £4,000,000
to help your unemployed. We do not
interfere in your internal affairs.
interfere in ours. Your game of 'heads-
I-win, tails-you-lose' is too clever to last."
Northern Ireland's contribution to the
Imperial services last year was £507,000,
but Northern Ireland got from the Imperial Government £801,000, and has in
every year since 1921 kept the land purchase annuities amounting to £666,000which annuities the Free State still pays
to London-making an Imperial set-off
of almost £1,500,000.
You
Now the taxable capacity of Northern
Ireland per head of the population is
markedly below that of Great Britain.
The average annual revenue of Great
Britain for the three years 1927-28 to
1929-30 was £825,000,000, or £18 12s per
head of the population. The annual
revenue of Northern Ireland was approximately during those years £11,000,000, or
£8 15s per head of the population. Great
Britain disbursed £6 2s per head of its
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
26 JANUARY 1932
revenue on domestic expenditure against
Northern Ireland's £7 16s. Our social
services, wages, and standards of life are
on a level with our near neighbours, but
our yield of rates per head is about half
of the yield in Great Britain.
Small wonder the predominant partner
is dissatisfied. After all, the basis of a
partnership is a sharing of losses as well
as profits. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Yes, "Hear, hear," but mark you, if
Northern Ireland is to go in for a policy
of paying pound for pound per head with
Great Britain it will be an orientation
without precedent in Irish financial history. Since the Act of Union in 1800
to the setting up of Northern Ireland as
an entity Ireland had differential treatment from Great Britain in sundry ways
in finance. It is recognised that Ireland
is a much poorer country. If the Antrim
farmer's capacity as a taxpaying unit is
to be assimilated to the Yorkshire farmer's
capacity, and the Belfast business man's
capacity to that of his compeer in
Birmingham, we will have the step by
step policy, but it will be a quick step
at the start, and it may be the Dead
March at the end.
I would like to quote from the Report
of the Committee on Financial Relations.
I quote from page 32:-
"The crippling effect upon our
Northern Ireland industries of the present excessive taxation is common
knowledge'
We are going to increase that by this Bill.
Colonel
No.
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
Mr. CAMPBELL: Yes, beyond a doubt
we are going to increase it. This talk
about anomalies is the merest, thinnest,
threadbare pretext. This is a Bill to
raise taxation and not to remove anomalies in valuation. It was foreshadowed
as such. This idea of removing anomalies
is an afterthought. It goes down well
with the rank and file of the party, perhaps. The Report proceeds:
"The present situation arises, in our
judgment, from the fact that the
Imperial Government, during years of
increasing financial depression, and
moving in directions which we shall not
here particularise, has, so to speak,
lengthened its stride, and Northern
Ireland is no longer able to keep up."
Now we are trying to keep up with the
length of the stride of Great Britain. The
Report also says:
"Nothing has been more clearly
demonstrated in the course of our investigation than the great disparity
which exists in wealth (per head of
population) between Great Britain and
Northern Ireland.'
Hon. MEMBERS: Hear, hear.

Mr. CAMPBELL: Hon. Members may
say Hear, hear" in this House, but
the rating authorities and the trade and
industry organisations outside this House
are also saying "Hear, hear," but they
are saying it to a very different set of
sentiments, and they are almost Unionist
to a man. The public authorities certainly
are so; you saw to that ten years ago
when the areas were so mapped out that
they remain Unionist to the present day
despite the proportions of the population.
According to the Ministerial view, they
are either fools or knaves. The premier
rating authority in this Province, the
Belfast Corporation, protested, except for
one still, small voice, against this Bill.
The Corporation know how much the
prosperity of Belfast is due to its low
valuation. The Corporation are the
elected custodians of the finances of
Belfast, and what do they say? They say
this Bill will have a most injurious effect
upon the financial standing of the whole
area of Northern Ireland.
-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Wait and see.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Is there nobody but
those on the Opposition side of the House
to say a word for the Corporation? In
this matter the Opposition, I think, have
given the only articulate expression of the
mind of the people. This Bill provides
for a general revaluation of the rateable
hereditaments and a general valuation
every five years thereafter. The British
Treasury say the city has not been revalued since 1906 and the area in general
since Griffith's valuation in 1852. The
Deputy-Leader of the House says that
anomalies have arisen by reason of the
long spell of disregard of conditions of
valuation.
The Treasury are the first to raise a
storm over anomalies—a storm in a teapot. They demand revaluation at a time
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
when the £ is rocking and reeling. All
values are in the melting-pot and will stay
there until the £ is stabilised. Though
this Bill will not come into operation for
two, or perhaps three, years, I submit to
the House that revaluation in existing
circumstances with the £ in its present
fluid condition is practically worthless,
and it will remain worthless until the £
is stabilised. I say again, and I desire to
emphasise it, that I do believe this talk
of anomalies is very much an afterthought; in short, it is a thin, transparent
pretext. Are there one hundred anomalies in the city of Belfast?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Yes.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The hon. Senator
says there are. I do not know what his
knowledge of anomalies is, but I doubt if
there are 100 in Belfast.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFQRD:
Thousands.
Mr. CAMPBELL: If so, what have
the Valuation Office been doing all this
time? It is not asleep. It is not situate
in Sleepy Hollow. Except in the case of
small old dwellings there cannot be many
buildings which retain to-day the valuation fixed in 1852. In Belfast, out of a
total of 106,145 ratings, 49,275 revised
valuations and new ratings have been
issued by the Valuation Office since 1919.
In the centre of Belfast 90 per cent. of
all ratings have been revised. In Donegall
Place all buildings but ten have been revised, and in Royal Avenue and other
central streets all but a few.
And yet
we are told the anomalies are widespread.
I
Agricultural land and office buildings
are exempted from this valuation.
think there is craft in that; there is
cunning in it. Farmers have been lulled
to sleep by the idea that they need not
bother about this Revaluation Bill, that
it is no concern of theirs, that their
withers are unwrung, as it only affects
the townsmen. Therefore, the farmers
have been somnolent and quiet under
these impressions. Land in many places
is hardly worth one-third of what it was
worth ten years ago. A revaluation of
land would give a poor yield. So the
Government seek a valuation of things
worth while. They are aiming at something substantial. The annual valuation
of agricultural hereditaments in the Six
Counties is about £1,500,000, and of
non-agricultural hereditaments about
£3,500,000. I think income tax to the
amount of about £3,000,000 is collected
each year in Northern Ireland overwhelmingly from the non-agricultural classes;
that is, from the town folk. What will the
income tax harvest be when the new
valuations are struck. The reaper will
be our own reapers, I am told.
The valuation for poor rate is in the
main the basis of the income tax (Schedule A) in respect of lands and buildings.
Valuations of buildings in general have
not remained static for 80 years. It is
said that buildings which had appreciated
in value apart from structural alterations
or additions have escaped revision of
valuation. There have been successive
revisions of valuation, removals of anomalies and inequalities. The total net
annual valuation of Northern Ireland is
about £5,260,000, of which total Belfast
has £1,858,000. It is said that some
landlords have valuations one-third or
one-fourth of their rentals, and so escape
income tax. The Deputy Leader to-day
figured a landlord whose rent was £400
and whose valuation was £100. I wonder
who he is. He may exist-I do not know.
Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
In the town, not in the country.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The right hon. and
gallant Senator says there is no such thing
in the country, that the landlords congregate in the towns. I take it that
landlords in the towns are spoken of. It
used to be in the country that the landlords were the great subject of animadversion, now it is in the towns. There
are landlords in Belfast, and may I say
Derry also, whose property is in Belfast
or Derry, but whose personality is in
the city of London-absentee landlords
springing up again in our cities.
Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
That is terrible.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am sure it must
be very gruesome. This is to be a battle
between the town and the country, and
you take the side of the country.
Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
Not a bit of it.
Mr. CAMPBELL: It is said the landlords escape their proper share of income
tax. I say that from a landlord Govern-
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
26 JANUARY 1932
ment and a landlord party that plea
sounds strangely hollow. If such inequalities do exist here and there let them be
straightened out. If the machinery is
not ready to hand, if the Valuation Office,
with its staff of 38, and its annual cost
of almost £16,000, have not the power
to equalise these matters let Parliament
give the power, and no opposition will
come from any party.
The method of valuation of a building
under this Bill will be the existing
method. The annual value will be the
annual net rent clear of all charges and
expenses for which one year with another
the landlord might reasonably expect to
let it. This net value will be higher than
the net rent, because it makes no allowance for ground rent or agents' fees.
Let
Which classes will be hit by the revaluations? Will the farmers stand where they
stand now? They are led to believe that
they need not break their sleep over the
Bill, because it leaves them untouched
and concerns only the townsfolk.
the farmers not be too sure. The Government may keep the word of promise to
their ear but break it to their hope.
About 120,000 of the farmers of Ulster
have holdings under 20 acres. They are
a poor but proud peasantry. Derating
saved £900,000 a year to agriculture, and
£75,000 a year to manufacturers. Derating was acclaimed as manna from
heaven, as the untasted spring of the
desert. If the entire sum was given to
these small farmers alone it would mean
only £7 10s Od per head per year, or less
than 3s a week for each. Under the Derating Act two-thirds of the value of a
farmer's holding is, if I err not, apportioned as the value of the dwelling-house.
If the valuation of the farm is £21, the
home will be £14. The valuation of his
land and farm offices remains unchanged.
That is to say, only one-third of his holding escapes, two-thirds of the value of his
holding will fall for revaluation. The
farmer is by no means on velvet. It is a
sham to say SO. Moreover his farm
offices will be liable to revision in the
ordinary course of annual revision.
Then it is only agricultural land of
which the existing value is to be stereotyped. Pastoral land seems to be outside
the purview of the Bill. Agricultural land
is defined as mainly farm land demesne,
market gardens, nurseries, orchards.
Pastoral land is nowhere mentioned in
the Bill. The types mentioned are
types of land in which cultivation is
annual or periodic. The Bill thus ropes
in large areas of purely milk and dairy
holdings and grazing lands. The taxes
which the Imperial Government will
gather from the Ulster farmers will be
poorly compensated by the tariff
oranges, asparagus, and wheat, or by a
wheat quota.
on
Let us consider the case of some classes
of town dwellers. A very numerous and
very thrifty class is the owner-occupiers
of small houses. They are a class who
make careful provision for the future and
bank on having the security of a little.
home for the close of their lives. They
put their savings-not much as a rulebut like the widow's mite it is sometimes
their all-into the purchase of such a
home, and its valuation is one of the main
considerations they take into account.
They may have pledged their title deeds.
with the bank for the overdraft which
helps them to pay the purchase price.
The present annual valuation of a house
of this type may be £20, but if such a
house can be reasonably let by an owner
to an occupier at £40 a year net, the
owner-occupier may find his valuation
jump from £20 to £40.
Then will tenanted houses valued at
£20 and under be affected? Some of
these houses are old. Others are new
subsidy houses.
Under the Corporation
Acts the owner instead of occupier
is rated to the consolidated rate where
the annual value of property does not
exceed £8, or where premises are let to
weekly or monthly tenants and their
annual value is under £20. The owner
of a house valued at £8 or less gets a
discount for prompt payment of the rates.
If the valuation is raised above £20 has
the owner any liability whatever to be
rated? If not, then many tenants may find
themselves rated who were never rated
before. If the owners can transfer the
rates to the occupiers under the new
valuation they will assuredly pass on the
burden. The Corporation can seek an
Act no doubt to regularise this matterthe Corporation of whom the Government
hold no very exalted opinion; whom they
have flouted and gibed at. But the Corporation may let the Government stew in
its own juice.
A subsidy house in Belfast let at 13s
a week gives a gross rent of £33 16s a
year. Its present day value may be £15.
Its new valuation may be in the
region of £24-entailing perhaps £5 or
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
£6 a year more in rates. How much
more income tax will the Corporation be
liable for as landlord of such houses?
How much more as owners of trams, gas
works or electricity works? The extra
hill may run into thousands of pounds a
year, and the citizens of Belfast will have
to foot the bill.
Private enterprise will not and cannot
build houses at rents within the means of
the working classes. This Bill will
render the building of such dwellings
unless backed by an impossible State
subsidy, an absolutely impracticable
proposition. The Planning and Housing Act will become a piece of
legislative futility. The slums will
remain. As regards decontrolled houses,
the landlords who have to pay more
income tax will recompense themselves by higher rents. The Valuation
Act of 1852 visualised a general revaluation possibly every 14 years, but did not
make it compulsory. This Bill proposes
a compulsory quinquennial revaluation.
Why repeat this inquisition every five
vears? Because they do so in England?
Is that the best reason? If the Government think 10 years too long, why not
compromise on 7 years? That would he
a reasonable respite, and would mean
that the Imperial Authorities could only
give the screw a turn every seven years,
instead of every five years. I hope that
the House will note the drastic powers
given to the Commissioners of Valuation
of compelling owners and occupiers to
furnish returns and particulars under
drastic penalties.
I have endeavoured to avoid all
temptation to exaggeration in
my
statement, yet what an ugly vista
this Bill opens up.
The opposition
to this Bill has been spoken of as a ramp
by outside parties. That is strong
language. The Ministerialists are good
judges of a ramp. The opposition by
public authorities and representative
organisations is neither a ramp nor a
machined opposition. The Ministerial
machine has not been able to galvanise
more than one or two public authorities
throughout the Six Counties into approval
of the Bill. I dare vouch that the
opposition in Parliament has been an
honest opposition and has voiced the real
minds of the people. The opposition outside Parliament is the opposition of a
public struggling to be free from party
bonds.
This Bill sanctions the fleecing of the
Ulster sheep by the shearers of Whitehall,
and that fleecing process will be repeated
every five years under the Bill as it now
stands. It is, I submit, a Bill of evil
precedent, and imposes insufferable and
unsupportable burdens upon our thrifty
poor people, on the working classes, on
petty traders, and harassed ratepayers.
It has been introduced at a time when the
wheels of trade and industry are slowing
down like the wheels of a rusty watch.
I have given many votes for and against
measures in this House, but I have never
given and I shall never give a vote with
more satisfaction that I am right and that
my vote is just and reasonable in every
sense than the vote which I shall give
against this great surrender to Whitehall.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to thank the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) for singling out
my name as being that of the only one in
the City Council who voted rightly when
that Resolution was before the Council.
They did pass the Resolution there, but I
understand that the City Council were
more concerned about an increase of the
rates. They put forward a Resolution.
objecting to the Valuation Bill because it
would increase the rates. The Deputy
Leader of the House (Mr. Long) has
explained to-day that, no matter how
valuations rise or fall, the City Council
will get exactly as much money as they
want and they will not get any more.
They cannot use any more, and will not
be allowed to get any more. So that if
the valuation of property is increased to
twice as much, and if the rate is 2s in the
pound, they are bound to reduce that, because they could not take 2s in the pound.
which would give them twice as much as
they are getting at the present time. It
stands to sense that they cannot increase
the rates.
But I think everyone will admit that
there is likely to be an increase in the
property tax. We know perfectly well
that in Northern Ireland valuation is
nothing like what it is across the water.
There has been no valuation of property
here since 1852, and in that time property has improved and increased to a
tremendous extent. At the present time
in the city of Belfast there are a considerable number of small shops and business
premises where the valuation of the property is only about £25, and yet people
are drawing rents of £200 for that
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
26 JANUARY 1932
property. I am now giving something
which I know to be perfectly correct,
because I have looked at the rate books
of the Belfast City Council. Surely to
goodness no one would attempt to say
that a revaluation scheme was not necessary when such anomalies as that exist.
After all we must admit that those people
who are paying their fair portion towards
the upkeep of the State have just reason
to complain if someone else is escaping
paying his just proportion. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) Nothing makes one
more annoyed or more angry than to know
that he is paying his share while his next
door neighbour is escaping with about a
quarter of what he should be paying.
I have not come across anyone yet who
does not admit that there are considerable
anomalies, and it is to rectify those
anomalies that this Valuation Bill is being
passed. It is all right to talk about what
it will increase. I even saw that some
officials in the City Hall have been
prompted to say that the valuation of the
city would be raised to such and such an
amount. I do not believe that anyone
knows exactly what standard will be set
for valuation in Northern Ireland. No
fool would think that we are going to
put the same valuation on Royal Avenue
in Belfast as is put on Oxford Street in
London. But that is what is being told
to the poorer business people. As I
understand it there is going to be a
valuation in accordance with the valuations in the cities and towns throughout
Northern Ireland. Who is going to complain about that?
Our people in Northern Ireland like to
maintain their close connection with
England. They decided that years ago,
and under no consideration would they
think of separating themselves from England. I do not believe that our people
are desirous to maintain that connection,
to take all the benefits that are to be
obtained by that connection, and then to
refuse to pay their fair share towards the
upkeep of the State. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) I do not believe they are so
miserably mean as that. The hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) a few
minutes ago said that Ireland had always
received preferential treatment, that they
had never been asked to pay as much as
England. We know perfectly well that
they always pleaded poverty and always
did get preferential treatment. They
were never asked to pay anything like
what the people of England, Scotland, or
Wales had to pay. But Northern Ireland
did not always says that.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I said differential
treatment, not preferential treatment.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Perhaps I made a mistake, but nevertheless
it was preferential treatment even if it
were differential. (Laughter.) But Northern Ireland never asked for that preferential treatment. She always stood
out and said she was an integral part of
and directly connected with Great Britain
and would not allow herself to be severed
from her. Furthermore, we always
pointed to the splendid industries we had
in Northern Ireland. No one in Northern Ireland complained of poverty or
asked for any preferential treatment, and
I do not believe now that if the ordinary
people in the street were asked they would
have any objection to this Valuation Bill.
I have taken notice of the resolutions
coming from the councils throughout Northern Ireland. Every one of them is alike.
They were not drawn up by the councils
who sent them out but by some individual
who engineered this opposition.
Mr. McCORKELL: Not all of them.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I did
not notice any difference in them. I have
for a long time taken an interest in public
affairs and the affairs of the people, and
I never saw any resolution sent out to a
body of men so foolish-looking as that
sent to hon. Members of this House.
Surely those people must have a very poor
opinion of the intelligence of this House
when they ask us to vote against this
Valuation Bill simply because another
House voted against the Measures of the
House of Commons at some time or other
during the past generation or two. The
thing is ridiculous. They do not even put
up a reasonable excuse or give a decent
reason for rejecting this Measure. It is
something like the small boy at school used
Tommy Jones did such and
such a thing and you are afraid to do the
same." That is what it amounts to. They
send along this resolution and say, "You
are afraid to do what other people did."
They do not use any argument why you
should do so.
to say:
Mr. CAMPBELL: Who are they?
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
not saying who they are. (Laughter.)
But it is a remarkable thing that on this
occasion the hon. and learned Gentleman
(Mr. Campbell) tells us that he is looking
after the interests of the property owners.
That is something remarkable. Of course
I can understand the hon. and learned
Senator making a change, but he is
always "agin the Government." (Laughter.)
Mr. CAMPBELL: I have said many
things, but I am sure I never said that.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
mistaken if the hon. and learned Senator
was not championing the cause of the property owners, because he told us here that
the property owners were going to suffer.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Sarcastically.
Mr. JOSEPII CUNNINGHAM: In
case too much notice is taken of that
resolution from the City Council I want
the House to bear in mind that that resolution was submitted to the Council before the Second Reading speech had been
made in the House of Commons, before
members of the Council knew exactly
what the Bill was intended to do, or in
what way it affected the financial arrangement between the local authorities and
the Government. While the resolution
was under discussion several members of
the Corporation had the Blue Book dealing with the financial relationship
between local authorities and the Government before them, and it was from that
that they quoted. The members of the
City Council were really in a fog and
knew nothing about what the Valuation
Bill was intended to do, how it was to be
carried out, or anything else.
Mr. McHUGH: Did they rescind it
afterwards?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: No,
but several members of the Corporation
expressed to me the view that they had
been foolishly led when they voted for it,
and, remarkable to say, one of your own
friends was among them. I do not think
there is anything more I have to say,
except this, and I want to speak in
serious vein. The British Government
to-day are in a difficult financial position.
Only recently our own Government had
to pass a Bill asking the poor unemployed
to suffer a reduction of 3s in their weekly
allowances on acount of the financial
state of the country. Some of those unemployed men, I am sure, had cause to
grumble, because it meant denying themselves the actual necessities of life. It
was a sacrifice for them. There is no doubt
about that. Now the Imperial Government have drawn our Government's
attention to the fact that there must be
a considerable number of people in
Northern Ireland who are escaping from
paying their fair share towards the taxation of the country. Surely to goodness
no one can blame our Government, after
applauding them for taking 3s from the
unemployed, if they come along now and
ask those people who are in good financial
positions to disgorge and to pay what
they are entitled to pay to the State.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). Surely
there is nothing hard about that. If the
unemployed could suffer and could sacrifice surely other men have every reason
to pay up and look pleasant. But I must
say that the unemployed workers of
Northern Ireland did not growl or squeal
nearly as much as the people who are
asked to pay up on this occasion.
The property owners are complaining
most, and are endeavouring to stir up
discontent amongst the tenants to whom
the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) referred a minute or two ago,
the poorer people who are paying weekly
rents. They have been told that their
rents are going to be increased. Yet it
was from these very property owners, who
are so concerned now about the poor
workers, that the workers had to be protected by the Government through a Bill
to control rents. And if there was a gap
or if there was a means of escape from that
legislation the property owners were not
backward in increasing rents by 100 per
cent. on the poor people about whom they
are now so much concerned.
Mr. DORMAN: 300 per cent.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Yes,
300 per cent. in some cases.
It is anything but creditable to some of our people
in Northern Ireland. I thought there
was nothing that could be asked of them
that would be too much to show their
good patriotism and loyalty to the Crown
and their country, but I see now that
there are a good number of them who
stagger at loyalty when it touches their
pockets. It is unfortunate, but I must
say I am surprised at some of our people.
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
26 JANUARY 1932
in Northern Ireland. I do not believe
the workers would create as much trouble
if they were called upon, as they have
been, to make similar sacrifices for the
good of the State, as these people to whom
I have referred have done. I intend to
vote for the Bill even if, in the words
of Senator Campbell, it is the only right
vote I have given. I was in the minority
when this matter was before the Belfast
Corporation, but I am satisfied I did the
right thing.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
think it wise at this stage that I should
say a few words with reference to the
attitude of the Belfast Corporation on
this question of revaluation. Let me say
at the outset that anything I say here
to-day I say with a full sense of responsibility, because I am here by virtue of
my office as Lord Mayor of the City of
Belfast, and I will endeavour to put the
Corporation's views frankly and moderately before this House. Apart from the
Belfast Corporation, other municipalities
and local authorities have given expression
to their opinions on this Bill, but I will
not deal with them because the conditions
in Belfast, as far as valuations are concerned, are somewhat different to those
in other areas. As regards the question.
of the inclusion of agricultural land in
this Bill, in my opinion it does not matter
whether such land is included in the
Measure or not, because I do not think
the valuation of land would be increased.
If it were it would make no difference
to the farmers, because the Government
are paying the rates through derating.
Consequently it is to the advantage of
the Government not to have the valuation
of agricultural land increased, because if
it is the Government will have to pay
more under the derating scheme. That is
undoubtedly a fact.
Mr. POLLOCK: Hear, hear.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: If
the valuation of land is raised the Government who have to pay the rates, will
have to pay more, so that in my opinion
land should be left out of the question
altogether. We come to the question of
the city, and I am now speaking for
Belfast. The position of Belfast is somewhat different to that of any other local
authority in Northern Ireland. The
whole of Ulster was re-valued in 1853,
and on that occasion there was a certain
standard of value set up. But Belfast,
for some reason or other, I do not know
why, was re-valued in 1900-1906.
Mr. POLLOCK: Because the Corporation desired it.
It
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: A
very extraordinary thing took place then.
That was that although the valuation of
the city increased by £350,000, owing to
a lawsuit plus appeals, it took seven
years to put that new valuation into
operation. It started in 1900, and it did
not come into operation until 1st April,
1906. I think the system in Belfast
applies to the rest of Northern Ireland,
although I am not sure about that. After
the new valuation came into operation in
1906 the only property on which a revaluation took place was on either new
premises or reconstructed premises.
worked in this way, and this is what has
caused the anomalies that exist to-day.
If an individual who was progressive in
his business rebuilt, reconstructed, or
altered his premises he had to submit a
plan to the City Surveyor. He automatically sent it on to the Superintendent
of Rates, who passed it on to the Commissioner of Valuation, and the premises
were revalued. You will find, as I have
myself found, a progressive business man
who is paying on a modern valuation,
while his neighbour in the same street,
perhaps next door, who has not reconstructed or altered his premises, was
assessed on his old valuation.
That has gone on, and I can point to
many business people to-day who have not
made any alteration to their premises
simply because they knew their valuations
would be raised. They are still sitting
under the valuation of 1906. In 1924 the
Government realised this state of affairs,
and they introduced a Bill to deal with
the matter, and I wish to give them credit
for doing so. Under that Bill it was
decreed that so far as altered premises
were concerned they were to be rated on
the pre-war valuation, plus 15 per cent.
That was a good thing, but at the same
time anomalies still exist to-day which
were created by the system I have referred
to. Those anomalies I say could easily be
remedied and all valuations put on the
same basis.
The hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) referred to the effect of this
Bill on private houses. You may talk
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Sir C. McCullagh]
about business premises, but, after all, as
far as I can understand this Bill, the one
individual above all others who is chiefly
to be affected by this Bill is the private
householder. There is not one word said
about him, and he is sitting quietly at
home thinking he will be all right. He
will not be all right, far from it. I do
not think the figures that were given by
the hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) were quite correct. I obtained some
figures to-day which are typical and are
not selected. If you take a house costing,
say £900, the rent and rates would be £60
a year, or if a man bought it at £900 the
valuation of that house is £19. Consider
the position of the man who bought such
a house, and he is the man I have sympathy for. I was informed to-day by
Mr. Alexander, the Superintendent of the
Rates Department of the Belfast Corporation, that for years, almost without exception when a man has bought a house,
almost the first thing he has done has been
to go to the City Hall to find out the
valuation of the house.
In the case of the type of house I have
mentioned, he would discover the valuation was £19. That type of individual
has been encouraged by the Government
and by the Corporation to become his own
landlord with the object of making him a
better and more responsible citizen. He
finds that he has to pay rates and income
tax on £19, and he scrapes up every
shilling he has, and very often borrows, in
order to purchase that house. What
position is that man going to be in under
the provisions of this Bill? If you take the
Bill as it is at present, if he is going to be
valued on the basis of rent instead of on
his valuation of £19 he will certainly have
to pay on £50 or thereabouts. I do not
think there is anyone here who can tell
exactly how much he will have to pay.
This is the class of man who is being overlooked.
If you take a house rented at £44 a year
the valuation is only £16, and the same
thing applies to other types. The valuation of a house rented at £80 is £29, and
that of a house rented at £70 is £25.
This is what was in the minds of the
members of the Belfast Corporation when
they passed their resolution against this
Bill. The members were quite serious in
coming to their decision, because the
matter was discussed by the Finance Committee, and later at a public meeting of
the Corporation, and I must say that I
never presided over a more harmonious
meeting in my life. It was free from any
ill-will towards the Government, and the
members were there with the sole desire
to represent the views of the people and to
do their best for them. That was one of
the reasons why they spoke so strongly
against this Measure.
Another reason, and I agree with it,
was that the time is not opportune for a
general revaluation. There is no doubt
that every branch of industry and business
has passed through very trying times. Today there is a ray of hope for better times
ahead, and I am delighted there is a
prospect of trade and industry improving.
It is at this time, when things are improving, that the Government see fit to
introduce this Bill. Would it not be far
better to encourage an increase in revenue
through increased prosperity rather than
by putting a damper on trade and industry through the medium of this Bill.
I say there are only two ways of dealing
with this matter. The Government should
either bring in a Bill to deal with anomalies in valuation or they should face the
music, and let the public know what they
are up against. If the Government are
going to face the music they must go to
the public and tell them what is the real
position of affairs, having regard to the
figures I have quoted to-day. If they do
that, that is all right. I would have
liked to support the Bill if I could, but
apart from the criticisms I have already
offered there are several other matters in
the Measue that require adjusting. However, if the Government and the Corporation came together there is no reason why
those difficulties should not disappear.
The Government do not want anything
from the Corporation and the Corporation
do not want anything from the Government.
I
Reference has been made to the question
of derating. There is no doubt, in my
opinion, that derating is responsible for a
great deal of the present trouble.
sympathise with the Government, because
they are faced with the difficulty that they
have got dangerously near to the point
when ends will not meet. That is the
position as I visualise it, and every day I
feel more and more thankful that we did
not decide to pay a settled annual contribution to the Imperial Government. If
we had settled the contribution on the
basis of the figure we paid in the early
Valuation Bill-
Second Reading
26 JANUARY 1932
•
years of the existence of this Government,
I do not know what we should have done
now. From the first I took the view which
has turned out to be the correct view,
that to decide on a fixed contribution at
that period would be entirely wrong, and
that it should be fixed from year to year
on the basis of the ability of the Province
to pay. I would be very sorry to see the day
arrive when Ulster could not pay its way,
but it would not worry me a bit if we
could not pay our Imperial contribution
for a year or two. Why should it? We
are a young country and we are doing the
best we can. The machinery may be too
elaborate, but I do not see how it can be
reduced. I know the Government have
been economising since they started their
economy campaign more than a year ago,
but would it not be better to pay our way
for a year or two even if we did not give
one penny towards an Imperial contribution.
I look upon derating as being like the
dole. You have introduced it and I do
not think you are going to get rid of it.
And in my opinion nobody would object
to paying rates for a struggling industry
that is going to be wiped out. We all
desire to keep industries in existence so
as to cause no further unemployment.
No one could object to paying for the
farmer who was not doing very well and
losing money, but I tell you what the
man in the street objects to. There are
many firms to-day, prosperous firms paying big dividends, wealthy firms that
have been derated, and I venture to suggest that there are many farmers who are
well-to-do and making money, and the
Government are paying their rates.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. McHUGH: Very few.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: It
is not fair. It is not right. It should
be looked into.
Mr. POLLOCK: It would be very inquisitorial, worse than the income tax.
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
can tell you of plenty of firms to-day who
have their rates paid for them, and they
have plenty of money.
In 1920-it is a very important point
-when the values of the houses went up
at such an alarming rate, the Corporation
approached the then Local Government
Board in Dublin to fix the values of the
new houses on the old low valuation. We
actually went to Dublin to ask them to
Why? Because we wanted to
let the houses or sell them as cheaply as
we could. How could the Corporation go
to-day, in view of that, and say "We
want to increase it." I do not see how
we could. One other point. I would
make a suggestion in all sincerity to my
right hon. Friend if he can see his way
-and I hope he will-to agree to it. It
took six years to put the 1906 Act into
operation.
I have heard it said—I
think my right hon. Friend said it-that
this will come into operation in 1935.
Mr. POLLOCK: 1935-36.
I
Sir CRAWFORD MCCULLAGH: I
would make an appeal to-day. I think
it might get over the difficulty for all
concerned. It would certainly let
security be established and let industries
get an opportunity of going ahead.
make an appeal to the right hon. Gentleman to add at least another couple of
years to that and make it 1937 and he
will find me as strong a supporter as he
has of the Bill, subject of course to some
little amendment, which, I say, should
not be of any serious difficulty as between
the Corporation and the Government. I
have endeavoured in these few words to
put the case before you, as it appears to
me, on behalf of the Corporation.
think I have stated it exactly and fairly,
and stated exactly what took place.
I
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise with a sense of
responsibility, because I fully realise
what the effect of the Bill will be to the
Ulster people. Like other Members of
the Senate, I have received many
resolutions and letters from public and
other bodies protesting against the proposed revaluation, but while my sympathies are with those who have to pay
out more money we must all admit that
a revaluation of property is long overdue.
It is some 80 years since such a valuation
was carried out in Northern Ireland, and
some 30 years since one was taken in
Belfast. Of course newly erected buildings have been valued as well as additions
and alterations year after year. But
while some people think that that is
sufficient, we should not forget that the
British Government is greatly dissatisfied.
They have been pressing our Northern
Government from time to time, until
there is no alternative to what is now
proposed. If the Ulster Government
Valuation Bill-
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Quigley]
does not do the work you may be sure
that the British Government will not
hesitate to carry it out for income-tax
purposes.
Mr. POLLOCK: Hear, hear.
Mr. QUIGLEY: Is it not far better
that our own valuers should undertake
the work since it must be carried out?
The revaluation will not be completed
before 1935, and let us hope that by that
time trade and industry generally will be
in a satisfactory and prosperous state.
We have been generously assisted by the
Imperial Government in regard to our
social services. We have always claimed
equal rights and equal services with the
rest of the United Kingdom, and surely
we cannot logically repudiate Great
Britain's right to claim from Northern
Ireland the same conditions in respect of
revaluation, as obtain in Great Britain.
I, therefore, think that our Government
is justified in putting forward this Bill
and should not be blamed.
No one can deny that serious anomalies
and injustices exist, and it is time they
were removed. To me the speech of the
Minister of Finance in the other House,
advocating a revaluation, was convincing.
May I quote one extract from it. Dealing with income tax, Mr. Pollock gave a
short list, extracted at random, of
properties in the City of Belfast and their
valuation. He says:
In
"The first one I see is £115 valuation, rent £400. In other words, the
owner of that property escapes paying
income tax on nearly £300. The next
is valuation £345, rent £1,175, on
£800 of which no income tax is paid by
the owner of that property.
another case the valuation is £69, and
the rental £340, giving £250 on which
the owner pays no income tax. Other
instances are valuation £1,242, rental
£2,750. Valuation £736, rental
£2,300. Valuation £184, rental £800.
Valuation £2,702, rental £4,500, or
nearly £2,000 on which the owner pays
no income tax, while of course his
neighbours have to pay. Valuation
£333, rental £850. Valuation £368,
rental £950. Valuation £83, rental
400, and so on.
Surely the hon. and learned Senator,
Mr. Campbell, cannot say that anomalies
are a myth. Those figures are a revelation to me, and show that some people are
escaping at the expense of others. How
can they be justified?
But while in favour of the revaluation.
I am strongly opposed to any increase in
the rents of houses under £8 valuation,
and from £8 to £26 valuation, and i
should like to have an assurance from the
Government on this point. It would b
most unfair, in fact a hardship, if the
rents of such dwellings were raised in
consequence of anything that is now being
done.
No doubt the question of rates will come
up, but if our public bodies exercise
economy, and do not go in for extravagance, there is no reason why the rates
should not be lowered in accordai.ce with
the rise in valuation. Under all the
circumstances, I feel it is my duty to
support the Second Reading of the Bili.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move the
adjournment of the debate.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: If
this debate is likely to last over to-morrow
I wonder if it is not possible that this
House should meet a little bit earlier than
we have been doing. (Hon. Members:
No, no.)
Mr. LONG: The debate will finish in
good time to-morrow if we meet at the
ordinary time, and we hope to have the
Committee stage on this day week.
Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
You are very sanguine.
On Question, Motion ("That the debate
be now adjourned") agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at a
Quarter after Five o'clock.

Second Reading
